# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder:** KTU 2024 Scheme Part-A = 3 marks each, Part-B = 14 marks (with internal choice). Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Examiner's valuation strictly follows **CO mapping** and **Bloom's Level** (L1–L6).

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Differentiate between file system approach and database approach. List any four limitations of the file system approach.** *(3 Marks)*
`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Bloom's Level:** L2 (Understand)

**Model Answer (Structured for 3 marks):**

The **file system approach** is a traditional method of storing data where each application defines and maintains its own data files independently, with no centralized control. In contrast, the **database approach** uses a unified DBMS to centrally store, manage, and share data among multiple applications.

**Four limitations of file system approach:**

1. **Data Redundancy:** Same data (e.g., student name) is duplicated across multiple files, wasting storage and risking inconsistency.
2. **Data Isolation:** Data is scattered in different formats and locations, making integrated querying difficult.
3. **Integrity Problems:** Constraints like *"marks must be between 0 and 100"* must be hard-coded in every application program.
4. **Concurrent Access Anomalies:** When multiple users simultaneously update the same file, changes can be lost or corrupted (lost-update problem).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write *advantages of DBMS* instead of *limitations of file system*. Always frame your answer as a *problem-solution* duo. Also, listing *more than four* items for a 3-mark question wastes time and dilutes focus.

---

### **Q2. Define the three-schema architecture. Name the mappings between the levels.** *(3 Marks)*
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Bloom's Level:** L1 (Remember)

**Model Answer:**

The **three-schema architecture** (also called the **ANSI/SPARC architecture**) is a framework proposed by the ANSI/SPARC committee in 1975 to provide data abstraction and data independence. It consists of three levels:

1. **Internal Schema (Physical Level):** Describes the physical storage structure of the database—files, indexes, disk blocks.
2. **Conceptual Schema (Logical Level):** Describes the logical structure of the entire database—entities, relationships, constraints—hidden from physical details.
3. **External Schema (View Level):** Describes only the portion of the database relevant to a particular user or application.

**The two mappings are:**
- **External/Conceptual Mapping** ($M_{EC}$): Translates user-level view queries into conceptual-schema operations.
- **Conceptual/Internal Mapping** ($M_{CI}$): Translates conceptual operations into physical storage access.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Do NOT confuse the **three-schema architecture** with the **three-tier architecture**. They are different—one is a *logical abstraction model*, the other is a *physical deployment model*. KTU frequently tests this distinction in Part A.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — Complete Model Question (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the characteristics of the database approach and contrast it with the file system approach. Highlight at least 6 differences. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the three-tier architecture of a DBMS. Explain how it differs from the two-tier architecture. *(7 Marks)*

`[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]`
**Bloom's Levels:** L3 (Apply) for (a), L2 + L4 (Analyze) for (b)

---

### **Model Solution for Q(a) — Database vs File System (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**
- *File system approach:* Each application owns its data files, processed via conventional file primitives (`fopen`, `fread`).
- *Database approach:* A unified DBMS software centrally defines, stores, and shares data, enforcing constraints, security, and concurrency.

**Step 2: Six Contrasting Characteristics (6 × 1 mark each)**

| # | Characteristic | File System | Database Approach |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data Redundancy** | High; same data in multiple files | Minimized via centralized schema |
| 2 | **Data Inconsistency** | Common (partial updates) | Eliminated (atomic transactions) |
| 3 | **Data Sharing** | Difficult, file-format dependent | Easy via SQL, multiple users |
| 4 | **Data Integrity** | Enforced in application code | Enforced via integrity constraints in DBMS |
| 5 | **Security** | Fragmented, app-specific | Centralized authentication & authorization |
| 6 | **Concurrent Access** | Risky (lost updates) | Handled via locking & transaction management |

**Step 3: Concluding Statement (0.5 marks)**
The database approach provides superior *data abstraction*, *integrity*, and *sharing*, making it the *de facto* standard for enterprise applications.

**Step 4: Real-World Tie-In (0.5 marks)**
KTU's own examination system uses an Oracle-backed DBMS to manage results for ~150,000 students, precisely because file-based access would collapse under concurrent load.

---

### **Model Solution for Q(b) — Three-Tier Architecture (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**
The **three-tier architecture** is a client-server deployment model where the database application is logically divided into three layers: **Presentation**, **Application Logic**, and **Data Management**.

**Step 2: Diagram (3 marks)**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph tier1A ["TIER 1 - PRESENTATION LAYER"]
        p1["Web Browser"]
        p2["Mobile App"]
    end

    subgraph tier2A ["TIER 2 - APPLICATION LAYER"]
        a1["Web Server: Apache, Nginx"]
        a2["App Server: Node.js, Django"]
        a3["Business Logic & Validation"]
    end

    subgraph tier3A ["TIER 3 - DATA LAYER"]
        d1["DBMS Server: MySQL, Oracle"]
        d2["Stored Procedures & Triggers"]
    end

    p1 --> a1
    p2 --> a1
    a1 --> a2
    a2 --> a3
    a3 --> d1
    d1 --> d2
```

**Step 3: Layer Description (2 marks)**

- **Tier 1 (Presentation Layer):** Renders UI; collects user input.
- **Tier 2 (Application Layer):** Hosts business rules, processes requests, validates data, calls the DB tier.
- **Tier 3 (Data Layer):** DBMS server stores data, executes queries, ensures ACID properties.

**Step 4: Difference from Two-Tier (1 mark)**

| Feature | Two-Tier | Three-Tier |
|---|---|---|
| **Layers** | Client + Database | Client + App Server + Database |
| **DB Exposure** | Direct client-to-DB connection (security risk) | DB is firewalled behind app server |
| **Scalability** | Limited (DB bottleneck) | High (scale app tier independently) |
| **Load Balancing** | Difficult | Easy via multiple app servers |
| **Example** | MS Access with local DB | Amazon.com, IRCTC, GPay |

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** Describe the three-schema architecture in detail with a neat diagram. Explain the concept of **data independence** and the two types of mappings. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Define the **Entity-Relationship Model**. Explain the concepts of **entity types, entity sets, attributes,** and their **types** with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)*

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]`
**Bloom's Levels:** L2 + L3 for (a), L1 + L2 for (b)

---

### **Model Solution for Q(a) — Three-Schema Architecture & Data Independence (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**
The **three-schema architecture** (ANSI/SPARC, 1975) separates the database into three abstraction levels to provide **data independence**—the ability to change one level without affecting others.

**Step 2: Diagram (3 marks)**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph extB ["EXTERNAL SCHEMA - View Level"]
        v1["View 1: Student Marks"]
        v2["View 2: Fee Receipts"]
    end

    subgraph conB ["CONCEPTUAL SCHEMA - Logical Level"]
        c1["Entities, Attributes, Relationships"]
        c2["Integrity Constraints"]
    end

    subgraph intB ["INTERNAL SCHEMA - Physical Level"]
        i1["File Organizations - Heap, B+ Tree"]
        i2["Indexing and Disk Block Layout"]
    end

    v1 -- "M_EC Mapping" --> c1
    v2 -- "M_EC Mapping" --> c1
    c1 -- "M_CI Mapping" --> i1
    c2 --> c1
    i1 --> i2
```

**Step 3: Three Levels (1.5 marks)**

- **Internal Schema:** Physical storage details (files, indexes, record placement).
- **Conceptual Schema:** Unified logical view—entities like `STUDENT`, `COURSE`, constraints like `PRIMARY KEY`.
- **External Schema:** Per-user views, e.g., a *Faculty* sees only their assigned class marks.

**Step 4: Data Independence & Mappings (1.5 marks)**

- **Physical Data Independence:** $M_{CI}$ shields the conceptual schema from physical changes (e.g., adding an index). Achieved easily.
- **Logical Data Independence:** $M_{EC}$ shields external schemas from logical changes (e.g., adding a new column). Harder to achieve.

---

### **Model Solution for Q(b) — Entity-Relationship Model (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: ER Model Definition (1 mark)**
The **Entity-Relationship (ER) Model** is a *high-level conceptual data model* proposed by **Peter Chen in 1976** for designing databases. It uses *entities, attributes,* and *relationships* to describe the structure of a database in a way that closely mirrors real-world perception.

**Step 2: Entity Types and Entity Sets (2 marks)**
- **Entity:** A real-world object with an *independent existence* that can be uniquely identified (e.g., a particular student named *"Rahul"*).
- **Entity Type:** A *category* of entities sharing the same properties (e.g., `STUDENT`).
- **Entity Set:** The *collection* of all entities of a given type at a point in time (e.g., the set of all 10,000 students currently enrolled in KTU).

**Step 3: Attribute Types (3.5 marks)**

| Attribute Type | Definition | Example for STUDENT |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple (Atomic)** | Cannot be subdivided meaningfully | `RollNo`, `Gender`, `DOB` |
| **Composite** | Can be broken into smaller sub-parts | `Address` = `{House, Street, City, Pincode}` |
| **Single-Valued** | Has exactly one value per entity | `Name`, `DOB` |
| **Multi-Valued** | Can have multiple values per entity | `PhoneNumbers` (a student may have 2-3 phones) |
| **Derived** | Computed from another attribute | `Age` (derived from `DOB` and current date) |
| **Key (Identifier)** | Uniquely identifies each entity | `RollNo` |
| **NULL** | Attribute value is unknown or undefined | `MiddleName` for a student with no middle name |

**Step 4: Concluding Example (0.5 mark)**
For the `STUDENT` entity type: `RollNo` (Key, Simple, Single-Valued), `Name` (Simple, Single-Valued), `Address` (Composite, Single-Valued), `PhoneNumbers` (Multi-Valued), `Age` (Derived from `DOB`).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Box Before Your Exam!**

- **DBMS** = general-purpose software for defining, constructing, manipulating, and sharing databases.
- **File System Limitations** (memorize 4–6): redundancy, isolation, integrity issues, atomicity issues, security holes, no concurrent access, no data abstraction, program-data dependence.
- **Three-Schema Architecture (ANSI/SPARC 1975)** has 3 levels: **External, Conceptual, Internal** with **2 mappings: M_EC and M_CI**.
- **Physical Data Independence** is easier to achieve than **Logical Data Independence**.
- **Three-Tier Architecture** = Presentation + Application + Data tiers. *It is a deployment model, NOT an abstraction model.*
- **Entity** = real-world distinguishable object.
- **Entity Type** = blueprint/category; **Entity Set** = collection of all entities of a type.
- **Attribute Types** = Simple, Composite, Single-Valued, Multi-Valued, Derived, Key, NULL.
- **Key Attribute** is *mandatory* for any ER design (e.g., `RollNo` for STUDENT).
- **Derived Attribute** is *computed*, not stored (e.g., `Age` from `DOB`).
- **Multi-Valued Attribute** requires a *separate table* when converting ER to relational schema.
- **Composite Attribute** is broken into its *constituent simple attributes* during mapping to tables.
- **Schema vs Instance:** Schema is *intension* (structure, stable); Instance is *extension* (data, changes over time).
- **ER Model was proposed by Peter Chen in 1976**—a 1-mark KTU favorite.
- **ACID** properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) are guaranteed by the DBMS but NOT by file systems.
- For 14-mark questions, always draw the **diagram first**, then label mappings, then explain—never the reverse.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Keys, Relationships and Types, Cardinality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Keys, Relationships and Types, Cardinality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Keys in a Relational Database?

A **Key** in a relational database is an attribute (or a set of attributes) used to uniquely identify a tuple (row) in a relation (table), or to establish a logical relationship between two relations. Keys enforce **Entity Integrity** and **Referential Integrity** — two of the most fundamental pillars of the relational model proposed by **E.F. Codd (1970)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> A *Key* is formally defined as a minimal set of attributes that uniquely identifies a row in a relation and cannot be reduced further without losing its uniqueness property.

**Real-World Analogy (Library System):**
Imagine a University Library. Every book has a unique **Accession Number** (e.g., `LIB/2024/CS/00142`). Even if two books share the same title, author, and year, the Accession Number guarantees uniqueness. In DBMS terms, the Accession Number is the **Primary Key** of the BOOK relation. If we also consider the combination of (Title, Author, Publisher, Edition), that may also uniquely identify a book — this is a **Super Key**. The smallest such combination is the **Candidate Key**.

---

### 1.2 Hierarchy of Keys — A Quick Overview

| Key Type | Purpose | Allows NULL? | Unique Values? |
|----------|---------|--------------|----------------|
| **Super Key** | Any set of attributes that uniquely identifies a row | Allowed (in some) | Yes |
| **Candidate Key** | Minimal Super Key | Conditional | Yes |
| **Primary Key** | Chosen Candidate Key | Not allowed | Yes (one per table) |
| **Alternate Key** | Unchosen Candidate Keys | Conditional | Yes |
| **Foreign Key** | References Primary Key of another table | Allowed | Not required |
| **Composite Key** | Key made of two or more attributes | Conditional | Yes |
| **Unique Key** | Enforces uniqueness, not used as identifier | One NULL allowed (most RDBMS) | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout — Super Key:**
> A Super Key is any set of attributes `K` of a relation schema `R` such that for any two tuples `t1` and `t2` in any legal relation state of `r(R)`, `t1[K] ≠ t2[K]`. In simple words: **Super Key = Super Set that Uniquely Identifies.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout — Candidate Key:**
> A Candidate Key is a **minimal** Super Key. Minimality means that removing any attribute from the key destroys the uniqueness property.

---

### 1.3 What is a Relationship?

A **Relationship** in the Entity-Relationship (ER) model is an association among two or more entities. Mathematically, if `E1, E2, ..., En` are entity sets, then a relationship set `R` is a subset of the Cartesian product:

$$R \subseteq E_1 \times E_2 \times \ldots \times E_n$$

**Real-World Analogy (College System):**
Consider a relation **ENROLLS** between STUDENT and COURSE. A student named *Arjun* is enrolled in *DBMS*. Here, `ENROLLS` is a relationship. Many such tuples together form the *relationship set*.

---

### 1.4 What is Cardinality?

**Cardinality** defines the **numerical mapping** (count) between the instances of one entity set and the instances of another entity set via a relationship. In simple words: *How many of entity A relate to how many of entity B?*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cardinality vs. Participation — A common confusion:**
> - **Cardinality** (this topic) = *How many* instances participate.
> - **Participation** = *Whether all or only some* instances participate (Total vs. Partial). Participation is covered in detail in Module 2.

**Real-World Analogy:**
- **Aadhaar ↔ Citizen:** Each citizen has exactly **one** Aadhaar number, and each Aadhaar number maps to **one** citizen → **1:1 Cardinality**.
- **Classroom ↔ Student:** A classroom has **many** students, but each student belongs to **one** classroom (assuming no shared classes) → **1:N Cardinality**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Key Types

#### A. Super Key
- A set of attributes `K` such that `K → R` (K functionally determines all other attributes of R).
- **Property:** Removing any attribute may or may not preserve uniqueness — hence "super" (larger, not minimal).
- **Example:** In `STUDENT(RegNo, Name, DOB, Phone, Email)`, both `{RegNo}` and `{RegNo, Name}` are Super Keys.

#### B. Candidate Key
- A **minimal** Super Key.
- No proper subset of a Candidate Key is a Super Key.
- A relation can have **multiple** Candidate Keys.
- **Example:** If `{RegNo}` and `{Email}` both uniquely identify students, then both are Candidate Keys.

#### C. Primary Key
- One Candidate Key is **chosen** by the database designer to be the Primary Key.
- Properties: **NOT NULL**, **UNIQUE**, **IMMUTABLE** (ideally).
- **Example:** Out of `{RegNo}` and `{Email}`, the designer chooses `RegNo` as the Primary Key.

#### D. Alternate Key
- Candidate Keys that are **not** selected as the Primary Key.
- Often enforced using `UNIQUE` constraint.
- **Example:** In the above, `Email` becomes the Alternate Key.

#### E. Foreign Key
- An attribute (or set of attributes) in a relation that **references** the Primary Key of another (or sometimes the same) relation.
- Enforces **Referential Integrity**.
- **Example:** In `ENROLLMENT(RegNo, CourseID, Grade)`, `RegNo` is a Foreign Key referencing `STUDENT(RegNo)`.

#### F. Composite Key
- A Candidate Key made up of **two or more** attributes.
- No single attribute alone can uniquely identify a row.
- **Example:** In `ENROLLMENT(RegNo, CourseID, Grade)`, the combination `{RegNo, CourseID}` is a Composite Key.

#### G. Unique Key
- Similar to Primary Key in uniqueness but **allows one NULL** (in most RDBMS like MySQL, SQL Server).
- A table can have **multiple** Unique Keys.
- Used for columns that must be unique but are not the primary identifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Multiple Key Types?**
> Database designers use different keys to balance **uniqueness**, **integrity**, **performance** (indexing), and **business logic** (e.g., Aadhaar must be unique but email is optional).

---

### 2.2 Relationship Types — A Structured View

| Relationship Type | Symbol | Example |
|-------------------|--------|---------|
| One-to-One (1:1) | `1 : 1` | Person ↔ Passport |
| One-to-Many (1:N) | `1 : N` | Department ↔ Employees |
| Many-to-One (N:1) | `N : 1` | Employees ↔ Department |
| Many-to-Many (M:N) | `M : N` | Students ↔ Courses |
| Recursive (Unary) | Self-loop | Employee ↔ Manages (Employee) |
| Ternary (n-ary) | `R(A,B,C)` | Doctor ↔ Patient ↔ Medicine |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes / Unit |
|---------|----------------|--------------|
| Number of Super Keys from n Candidate Keys | $\text{SuperKeys} = 2^{n} - 1$ | All non-empty subsets of Candidate Keys |
| Cartesian Product of two sets A, B | $\vert A \times B \vert = \vert A \vert \times \vert B \vert$ | Used to find maximum tuples in a relationship |
| Maximum tuples in M:N relationship | $\vert A \vert \times \vert B \vert$ | If no participation constraint |
| Maximum tuples in 1:N relationship | $\max(\vert A \vert, \vert B \vert)$ | One-side can have at most $\vert A \vert$ tuples |
| Maximum tuples in 1:1 relationship | $\min(\vert A \vert, \vert B \vert)$ | Limited by the smaller side |
| Closure of attribute set X | $X^{+} = X \cup \{\text{all derivable attributes}\}$ | Used to test if Y is in X+ |
| Functional Dependency | $X \rightarrow Y$ | X is determinant, Y is dependent |
| Membership Test for FD | Is $Y \subseteq X^{+}$ ? | If yes, FD holds in F |
| Trivial FD | $X \rightarrow Y$ where $Y \subseteq X$ | Always true |
| Non-Trivial FD | $X \rightarrow Y$ where $Y \cap X = \emptyset$ | Holds only when X is known |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> These rules are used daily by **DBAs** in schema design, by **backend developers** in ORM mapping (Hibernate, Entity Framework), and by **data engineers** in ETL pipeline design to ensure lossless joins and referential consistency.

---

### 2.4 Cardinality in ER Modeling — Mapping Strengths

When mapping an ER diagram to a relational schema, cardinality governs the foreign key placement:

| Cardinality | Foreign Key Placement | Example |
|-------------|----------------------|---------|
| **1:1** | FK in **either** table (not both) | PERSON has FK to PASSPORT |
| **1:N** | FK placed on the **"many"** side | EMPLOYEE has FK to DEPARTMENT |
| **M:N** | Create a **new junction/bridge table** | ENROLLMENT links STUDENT and COURSE |
| **Recursive 1:N** | FK in the same table, referencing its own PK | EMPLOYEE(MgrID FK → EmpID) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cardinality in UML vs. ER:**
> In **UML class diagrams**, multiplicity `1..*` means "one or more". In **ER (Chen's) notation**, a single line means 1, and a crow's foot (>) means "many". In **Barker's notation**, the crow's foot appears on the side where many instances exist.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Finding All Candidate Keys from Functional Dependencies

This is one of the most frequently asked derivations in KTU University Examinations. Let us walk through the **exhaustive step-by-step method**.

**Given Relation:**
$$R(A, B, C, D, E, F)$$

**Functional Dependencies (F):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F = \{\; &AB \rightarrow C,\; C \rightarrow D,\; D \rightarrow E,\; E \rightarrow F,\; F \rightarrow A \;\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Goal:** Find all Candidate Keys of R.

---

#### Step 1 — Identify attributes that NEVER appear on the Right-Hand Side (RHS)

RHS attributes across all FDs: `{A, C, D, E, F}`.

LHS attributes (determinants): `{A, B, C, D, E, F}`.

Attributes **not on RHS** = `{B}`.

> **Conclusion:** `B` **must** be present in every Candidate Key (it is essential).

#### Step 2 — Identify attributes that NEVER appear on the Left-Hand Side (LHS)

LHS determinants: `{A, B, C, D, E, F}`.

Attributes **not on LHS** = `{B}` only (others appear at least once on LHS).

> Since `B` is the only such attribute, it is the **starting point** of our closure computation.

#### Step 3 — Compute the closure of {B}, written as `{B}+`

We will iteratively add attributes that can be derived.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{B\}^{+} &= \{B\} \\
&\text{No FD has B alone as LHS.} \\
&\text{Hence } \{B\}^{+} = \{B\} \quad \text{(closure stops here)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

> `{B}` alone is **not** a Candidate Key because `B+ ≠ R`.

#### Step 4 — Try {B} combined with one other attribute at a time

Compute closures of `{B, A}+`, `{B, C}+`, etc.

**For {B, A}+:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\{B, A\}^{+} &= \{A, B\} \\
&\text{Apply } AB \rightarrow C: \text{adds } C. \\
&= \{A, B, C\} \\
&\text{Apply } C \rightarrow D: \text{adds } D. \\
&= \{A, B, C, D\} \\
&\text{Apply } D \rightarrow E: \text{adds } E. \\
&= \{A, B, C, D, E\} \\
&\text{Apply } E \rightarrow F: \text{adds } F. \\
&= \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} = R
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** `{B, A}+ = R` ⇒ `{A, B}` is a Candidate Key.

**For {B, C}+:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\{B, C\}^{+} &= \{B, C\} \\
&\text{Apply } C \rightarrow D: \text{adds } D. \\
&= \{B, C, D\} \\
&\text{Apply } D \rightarrow E: \text{adds } E. \\
&= \{B, C, D, E\} \\
&\text{Apply } E \rightarrow F: \text{adds } F. \\
&= \{B, C, D, E, F\} \\
&\text{Apply } F \rightarrow A: \text{adds } A. \\
&= \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} = R
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** `{B, C}+ = R` ⇒ `{B, C}` is a Candidate Key.

**For {B, D}+:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\{B, D\}^{+} &= \{B, D\} \\
&\text{Apply } D \rightarrow E: \text{adds } E. \\
&= \{B, D, E\} \\
&\text{Apply } E \rightarrow F: \text{adds } F. \\
&= \{B, D, E, F\} \\
&\text{Apply } F \rightarrow A: \text{adds } A. \\
&= \{A, B, D, E, F\} \\
&\text{Apply } AB \rightarrow C \text{ (now A, B available)}: \text{adds } C. \\
&= \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} = R
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** `{B, D}+ = R` ⇒ `{B, D}` is a Candidate Key.

**For {B, E}+, {B, F}+:**

By similar logic, `{B, E}+ = R` and `{B, F}+ = R` (the chain E→F→A→...C works).

#### Step 5 — Check Super Sets

Any 2-element subset of `{A, B, C, D, E, F}` containing `B` will close to `R`. There are **5** such subsets: `{A,B}, {B,C}, {B,D}, {B,E}, {B,F}`.

> **All Five are Candidate Keys of R.**

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Number of Tuples in Relationships

Suppose we have two entity sets:
- `STUDENT` with `|S| = 1000` tuples
- `COURSE` with `|C| = 50` tuples

| Cardinality | Maximum Tuples in Relationship | Minimum Tuples | Notes |
|-------------|-------------------------------|----------------|-------|
| 1:1 (one student — one course ID card) | $\min(1000, 50) = 50$ | 0 | At most 50 students get an ID card |
| 1:N (one dept — many students) | $\max(1000, 50) = 1000$ | 0 | All students can belong to departments |
| M:N (many students — many courses) | $1000 \times 50 = 50{,}000$ | 0 | Each student can take every course |
| N:1 (many employees — one manager) | 1000 | 0 | Each employee has one manager |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:**
> In a normalized (3NF or BCNF) database, M:N relationships always require a **junction table** (also called *associative entity* or *bridge table*). In SQL, this translates to a separate table with two Foreign Keys and possibly additional attributes (e.g., Grade, EnrollDate).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Verifying if an FD belongs to F+ (FD Closure)

**Given:** $F = \{ AB \rightarrow C,\; C \rightarrow D,\; D \rightarrow E \}$

**Question:** Does $AB \rightarrow E$ hold in `F+`?

**Step-by-step:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\{A, B\}^{+} &= \{A, B\} \quad \text{(start)} \\
\text{Apply } AB \rightarrow C: \quad &\rightarrow \{A, B, C\} \\
\text{Apply } C \rightarrow D: \quad &\rightarrow \{A, B, C, D\} \\
\text{Apply } D \rightarrow E: \quad &\rightarrow \{A, B, C, D, E\} \\
\text{Is } E \in \{A,B\}^{+}? \quad &\textbf{Yes.} \\
\therefore \; AB \rightarrow E &\in F^{+}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The FD `AB → E` is **implied** by the given set F, and is therefore a member of the FD closure `F+`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid ER Diagram — Cardinality in a College Database

```mermaid
erDiagram
    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ STUDENT : "offers admission to"
    STUDENT ||--o{ ENROLLMENT : "registers for"
    COURSE ||--o{ ENROLLMENT : "is taken in"
    FACULTY ||--o{ COURSE : "teaches"
    FACULTY }o--o{ COURSE : "research collaboration"

    DEPARTMENT {
        string DeptID PK
        string DeptName
        string HOD
    }
    STUDENT {
        string RegNo PK
        string Name
        string Email UK
        string DeptID FK
    }
    COURSE {
        string CourseID PK
        string CourseName
        int Credits
        string DeptID FK
    }
    ENROLLMENT {
        string RegNo FK
        string CourseID FK
        date EnrollDate
        string Grade
    }
    FACULTY {
        string FacultyID PK
        string FacultyName
        string DeptID FK
    }
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- `||--o{` = Exactly one on left, zero or many on right (**1:N**).
- `}o--o{` = Zero or many on both sides (**M:N**).
- `PK` = Primary Key, `FK` = Foreign Key, `UK` = Unique Key.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Key Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Super Key] --> B[Candidate Key]
    B --> C[Primary Key]
    B --> D[Alternate Key]
    A --> E[Foreign Key]
    A --> F[Composite Key]
    A --> G[Unique Key]
    A --> H[Partial Key - weak entity]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Interpretation:** Every Candidate Key, Primary Key, and Alternate Key is also a Super Key. A Foreign Key may or may not be a Super Key (in the referencing table, it usually isn't, but it must match the PK of the referenced table).

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cardinality Mapping Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAPPING[Cardinality Mapping to Relational Schema]
        direction TB
        M1[1:1 Relationship] --> N1[Add FK in either table]
        M2[1:N Relationship] --> N2[Add FK in N-side table]
        M3[M:N Relationship] --> N3[Create new junction table with 2 FKs]
        M4[Recursive 1:N] --> N4[FK in same table referencing own PK]
        M5[Ternary Relationship] --> N5[New table with n FKs]
    end

    style M1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style M2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style M3 fill:#FFE4B5
    style M4 fill:#FFE4B5
    style M5 fill:#FFE4B5
    style N1 fill:#E0FFFF
    style N2 fill:#E0FFFF
    style N3 fill:#E0FFFF
    style N4 fill:#E0FFFF
    style N5 fill:#E0FFFF
```

---

### 4.4 Worked Example — Visualizing 1:N, M:N, 1:1

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DEPARTMENT] ---|1:N| B1[STUDENT Alice]
    A ---|1:N| B2[STUDENT Bob]
    A ---|1:N| B3[STUDENT Carol]

    C[STUDENT] ---|M:N| D1[COURSE DBMS]
    C ---|M:N| D2[COURSE OS]
    C ---|M:N| D3[COURSE CN]

    E[PERSON] ---|1:1| F1[PASSPORT P1]
    E2[PERSON 2] ---|1:1| F2[PASSPORT P2]

    style A fill:#FFB6C1
    style C fill:#87CEEB
    style E fill:#90EE90
    style E2 fill:#90EE90
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define the following with an example each:**
**(i) Super Key, (ii) Candidate Key, (iii) Primary Key.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(i) Super Key:** A Super Key is any set of attributes that uniquely identifies each tuple in a relation. Formally, K is a Super Key of R if for all tuples t1, t2 in r(R), t1[K] ≠ t2[K].  
*Example:* In STUDENT(RegNo, Name, Phone, Email), both `{RegNo}` and `{RegNo, Name}` are Super Keys.

**(ii) Candidate Key:** A Candidate Key is a minimal Super Key — no proper subset of it is a Super Key.  
*Example:* In STUDENT(RegNo, Name, Phone, Email), if `{RegNo}` and `{Email}` both uniquely identify students, then both are Candidate Keys.

**(iii) Primary Key:** The Candidate Key chosen by the DBA to uniquely identify tuples. It must be NOT NULL and UNIQUE.  
*Example:* `RegNo` is chosen as the Primary Key of STUDENT; `Email` becomes the Alternate Key.

*(1 mark each for definition + example = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is cardinality? Explain 1:1, 1:N, and M:N relationships with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Cardinality** specifies the number of entity instances in one entity set that can be associated with instances in another entity set via a relationship.

- **1:1 (One-to-One):** Each entity in A is related to at most one entity in B and vice versa.  
  *Example:* Each citizen has exactly one Aadhaar card; each Aadhaar card belongs to one citizen.

- **1:N (One-to-Many):** One entity in A can be related to many entities in B, but each entity in B relates to only one entity in A.  
  *Example:* One Department has many Students, but each Student belongs to one Department.

- **M:N (Many-to-Many):** Many entities in A relate to many entities in B.  
  *Example:* Many Students enroll in many Courses.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for three types + 0.5 mark for examples = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the concept of a Key in DBMS. Explain in detail the different types of keys (Super Key, Candidate Key, Primary Key, Alternate Key, Foreign Key, Composite Key, Unique Key) with suitable examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of a Key (1 Mark):**  
A Key is an attribute or a set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple (row) in a relation and is used to establish relationships between tables while maintaining integrity.

**Types of Keys (6 × 1 Mark = 6 Marks):**

Consider a sample schema:
`STUDENT(RegNo, Aadhaar, Name, DOB, Email, Phone)`

1. **Super Key:** Any attribute set that uniquely identifies a row.  
   *Examples:* `{RegNo}`, `{RegNo, Name}`, `{Aadhaar, Phone}` — all are Super Keys.

2. **Candidate Key:** Minimal Super Keys. No proper subset is a Super Key.  
   *Examples:* `{RegNo}`, `{Aadhaar}`, `{Email}` are all Candidate Keys (assuming each is unique).

3. **Primary Key:** The chosen Candidate Key. NOT NULL, UNIQUE.  
   *Example:* DBA selects `RegNo` as the Primary Key.

4. **Alternate Key:** Candidate Keys that are not selected as Primary.  
   *Example:* `Aadhaar` and `Email` become Alternate Keys.

5. **Foreign Key:** An attribute in a table that references the Primary Key of another table.  
   *Example:* In `ENROLLMENT(RegNo, CourseID, Grade)`, `RegNo` is a Foreign Key referencing `STUDENT(RegNo)`.

6. **Composite Key:** A Candidate Key formed by two or more attributes.  
   *Example:* In `ENROLLMENT`, the combination `{RegNo, CourseID}` is a Composite Key.

7. **Unique Key:** Enforces uniqueness but allows one NULL; multiple allowed per table.  
   *Example:* `Email` can be declared as a Unique Key, allowing one row to have NULL email.

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Explain the different types of relationships in DBMS with examples. Discuss how M:N relationships are mapped to a relational schema. Illustrate with an ER diagram.**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Relationships (3 Marks):**

1. **One-to-One (1:1):** Each entity in set A is associated with at most one entity in set B.  
   *Example:* PERSON — PASSPORT. One person has one passport, and one passport belongs to one person.

2. **One-to-Many (1:N):** One entity in A associates with many in B, but each in B associates with only one in A.  
   *Example:* DEPARTMENT — STUDENT. One department has many students; each student belongs to one department.

3. **Many-to-One (N:1):** Reverse perspective of 1:N. Many employees work in one department.

4. **Many-to-Many (M:N):** Many entities in A relate to many in B.  
   *Example:* STUDENT — COURSE. A student takes many courses; a course has many students.

5. **Recursive (Unary) Relationship:** An entity relates to itself.  
   *Example:* EMPLOYEE manages EMPLOYEE.

6. **Ternary Relationship:** Involves three entity sets.  
   *Example:* DOCTOR prescribes MEDICINE to PATIENT.

**Mapping M:N to Relational Schema (3 Marks):**

To map a Many-to-Many relationship, we create a **new junction/bridge table** with two Foreign Keys that together form a Composite Primary Key. Additional attributes of the relationship are also included.

**Example — Mapping STUDENT ↔ COURSE (M:N):**

Create a new table ENROLLMENT:
- `ENROLLMENT(RegNo, CourseID, EnrollDate, Grade)`
- `RegNo` is a Foreign Key → STUDENT(RegNo)
- `CourseID` is a Foreign Key → COURSE(CourseID)
- `{RegNo, CourseID}` is the Composite Primary Key.
- `EnrollDate` and `Grade` are descriptive attributes of the relationship.

**ER Diagram (1 Mark):**

```mermaid
erDiagram
    STUDENT ||--o{ ENROLLMENT : ""
    COURSE ||--o{ ENROLLMENT : ""
    STUDENT {
        string RegNo PK
        string Name
    }
    COURSE {
        string CourseID PK
        string CourseName
    }
    ENROLLMENT {
        string RegNo FK
        string CourseID FK
        date EnrollDate
        string Grade
    }
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**With reference to a relation R(A, B, C, D, E) and the FDs `F = {A → B, B → C, C → D, D → E}`, determine all the candidate keys. Show the closure computation step-by-step.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Identify attributes never on RHS.  
- RHS: `{B, C, D, E}`.  
- Attribute not on RHS: **A**.  
- Therefore, A must be in every Candidate Key.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute `{A}+$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\{A\}^{+} &= \{A\} \\
\text{Apply } A \rightarrow B: &= \{A, B\} \\
\text{Apply } B \rightarrow C: &= \{A, B, C\} \\
\text{Apply } C \rightarrow D: &= \{A, B, C, D\} \\
\text{Apply } D \rightarrow E: &= \{A, B, C, D, E\} = R
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Since `{A}+ = R`, the single attribute `A` is a Candidate Key. Hence it is the **only Candidate Key**.

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Verify by closure of other singletons:
- `{B}+ = {B, C, D, E} \neq R$ (A is missing).
- `{C}+ = {C, D, E} \neq R$.
- `{D}+ = {D, E} \neq R$.
- `{E}+ = \{E\} \neq R$.

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Verification using superset logic:
Since only `A` is essential, any superset of `{A}` (e.g., `{A,B}`, `{A,C}`) is a Super Key, but these are not minimal → not Candidate Keys.

**Final Answer (1 Mark):** The only **Candidate Key** of R is `{A}`.  
**Primary Key:** `A`.  
**Super Keys:** All supersets of `{A}` containing at least A, including `{A}, {A,B}, {A,C}, \ldots, {A,B,C,D,E}$.

**Total Super Keys = $2^{5-1} = 2^{4} = 16$** (since A must be in all of them, remaining 4 attributes can be optionally included).

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Explain the concept of Cardinality in ER modeling. Discuss the four main types of cardinality (1:1, 1:N, M:1, M:N) with real-world examples and show how each is converted to a relational schema.**

**Model Solution:**

**Cardinality Definition (1 Mark):**  
Cardinality in ER modeling defines the **number of instances** of one entity that can be associated with the number of instances of another entity through a relationship. It captures the *count* aspect of associations.

**Four Cardinality Types (4 × 1 Mark = 4 Marks):**

1. **1:1 — One-to-One:**  
   - *Example:* PERSON ↔ DRIVING_LICENSE. Each person has one license; each license is held by one person.  
   - *Schema:* PERSON(PersonID PK, Name, LicenseID FK UNIQUE) and DRIVING_LICENSE(LicenseID PK, IssueDate).

2. **1:N — One-to-Many:**  
   - *Example:* DEPARTMENT ↔ STUDENT. One department has many students; each student belongs to one department.  
   - *Schema:* DEPARTMENT(DeptID PK, DeptName); STUDENT(RegNo PK, Name, DeptID FK).

3. **M:1 — Many-to-One:**  
   - *Example:* STUDENT ↔ ADVISOR. Many students have one advisor. (Reverse of 1:N.)  
   - *Schema:* ADVISOR(AdvisorID PK, Name); STUDENT(RegNo PK, AdvisorID FK).

4. **M:N — Many-to-Many:**  
   - *Example:* STUDENT ↔ COURSE. Many students enroll in many courses.  
   - *Schema:* Requires junction table ENROLLMENT(RegNo FK, CourseID FK, Grade), Composite PK = `{RegNo, CourseID}`.

**Mapping Rules Table (2 Marks):**

| Cardinality | Mapping Strategy |
|-------------|------------------|
| 1:1 | Add FK in either table (the one with **Total Participation** is preferred) |
| 1:N or N:1 | Add FK on the **"many"** side |
| M:N | Create a **new junction table** with both FKs |
| Unary/Recursive | FK in the same table referencing its own PK |

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Cardinality with Participation:** Students write "Total/Partial" under cardinality questions → **−2 marks**. Cardinality is about *count*; participation is about *existence* (all or some).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Junction Table in M:N:** When asked to map M:N to a schema, students often put a Foreign Key on both sides directly → **−3 marks**. Always create a **separate bridge table**.
>
> 3. **Confusing Primary Key with Unique Key:** Writing "Primary Key allows NULL" or "a table can have many Primary Keys" → **−2 marks**. Primary Key = NOT NULL, only ONE per table.
>
> 4. **Skipping Closure Steps in Key-Finding:** Writing only the final answer without showing iterative `+` computation → **−3 to −4 marks**. KTU examiners award step-marks for each FD application.
>
> 5. **Wrong cardinality notation:** Writing "1:N" as "N:1" or omitting the direction → **−1 mark**. The *left side* always refers to the **first entity** mentioned.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to state minimality** when defining Candidate Key → **−1 mark**. "Minimal" is the keyword that distinguishes it from a Super Key.
>
> 7. **Writing a Primary Key as a Foreign Key in the same table** without explanation (allowed only if it is a recursive relationship) → **−2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Super Key** = Any set of attributes that uniquely identifies a row. **Candidate Key** = *Minimal* Super Key. **Primary Key** = The *chosen* Candidate Key. **Alternate Key** = The *unchosen* Candidate Key.
- 🔑 **Foreign Key** = Attribute in one table that **references** the Primary Key of another table → enforces **Referential Integrity**.
- 🔑 **Composite Key** = Candidate Key made of **two or more** attributes. **Unique Key** = Like Primary Key but **allows one NULL**; **multiple allowed per table**.
- 🔗 **Relationships in ER:** Define associations between entity sets; cardinality specifies *how many*.
- 🔢 **Four Cardinality Types:** **1:1** (Person–Passport), **1:N** (Dept–Student), **M:1** (Student–Advisor), **M:N** (Student–Course).
- 🛠️ **Mapping Cardinality to Schema:**  
  • 1:1 → FK in either table.  
  • 1:N / N:1 → FK on the "many" side.  
  • M:N → **New junction table** with two FKs forming a Composite Key.
- 🧮 **Key Counting Formula:** If a relation has `n` Candidate Keys, the number of Super Keys = $\mathbf{2^{n} - 1}$ (excluding the empty set).
- 🧮 **Closure Rule:** A set X is a Super Key if `X+ = R` (closure equals the full relation). A set X is a Candidate Key if additionally, for every proper subset `Y ⊂ X`, `Y+ ≠ R`.
- ⚠️ **Cardinality ≠ Participation:** Cardinality = *count* (1, N, M); Participation = *existence* (Total vs. Partial) — **Don't mix them in KTU answers**.
- 📐 **Min/Max Tuples:** `1:1 → min(|A|,|B|)`, `1:N → max(|A|,|B|)`, `M:N → |A| × |B|`.
- 🎯 **High-Frequency KTU Question:** *"Find all Candidate Keys of R given a set of FDs"* — always show **closure computation step-by-step**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Participation, Structural Constraints, Conventional and Min-Max Notations, Drawing and Interpreting ER Diagrams, Relational Model Concepts: Structure of Relational Databases, Domains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Participation, Structural Constraints, Conventional and Min-Max Notations, Drawing and Interpreting ER Diagrams, Relational Model Concepts: Structure of Relational Databases, Domains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Participation Constraint (Total vs Partial Participation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Participation Constraint** specifies whether the existence of an entity in a relationship set depends on it being linked to another entity via the relationship set. It is a **structural constraint** defined on a relationship type, separately from cardinality ratio.

There are two distinct kinds:

### (a) Total Participation (Mandatory / Existence Dependency)
An entity **E** in entity set **A** is said to have **total participation** in relationship **R** if **every entity in A must participate in at least one relationship in R**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Notation:** Total participation is drawn as a **double line** connecting the entity set to the relationship diamond in an ER diagram. It is also sometimes called **existence dependency**, since the entity's existence is dependent on the relationship.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a **Department** and a **Professor**. Every Department *must* have at least one Professor (Head/Coordinator) — a Department cannot exist in isolation from having some Professor associated with it. This is *total participation* of the Department entity in the relationship.

### (b) Partial Participation (Optional)
An entity in **A** participates *partially* in relationship **R** if **some entities of A may not participate in R**.

**Analogy:** A **Student** *may or may not* enroll in an elective course. The participation of a Student in the *Elective* relationship is *partial* — some students choose not to enroll.

| Feature | Total Participation | Partial Participation |
|---|---|---|
| Notation | Double line | Single line |
| Also called | Mandatory, Existence-dependent | Optional |
| Real-world meaning | Entity cannot exist without the relationship | Entity can exist independently |

---

## 1.2 Structural Constraint — Mapping Cardinality / Cardinality Ratio

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **Mapping Cardinality** (also called **Cardinality Ratio** or **Structural Constraint**) expresses the *number of relationship instances* an entity can/should participate in, with respect to another entity in a binary relationship. For a binary relationship R between entity sets A and B, the cardinality is one of: **1:1, 1:N, M:N**.

These two constraints (participation + cardinality ratio) together form the **structural constraints** of an ER schema, as distinguished from **key constraints** (used in alternative notations).

---

## 1.3 Conventional Notation (1:1, 1:N, M:N)

The **conventional notation** places an arrow or a label on the connecting line:

| Relationship Type | Meaning | Conventional Drawing Convention |
|---|---|---|
| **One-to-One (1:1)** | One A relates to at most one B, and vice versa | Arrow → on *both* sides, or label "1" on both sides |
| **One-to-Many (1:N)** | One A relates to many B, but each B relates to one A | Arrow → on the "many" side, or label "1" and "N" |
| **Many-to-Many (M:N)** | Many A relate to many B (no restriction) | No arrows, or labels "M" and "N" on both sides |

**Intuition:** Think of a *Person–Passport* relationship (1:1) — every person has exactly one passport and every passport belongs to one person. *Department–Employee* (1:N) — one department has many employees, but each employee belongs to exactly one department. *Student–Course* (M:N) — students take many courses and each course has many students.

---

## 1.4 Min-Max Notation (Look-Across / (min, max) Pairs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Min-Max Notation** is the *alternate (Chen's look-across)* style: each entity's participation is described by a pair of integers **(min, max)** on the edge connecting it to the relationship diamond.
> - **min** = minimum number of relationship instances each entity instance must participate in (0 → partial, ≥1 → total)
> - **max** = maximum number of relationship instances an entity instance can participate in

| Constraint | Min-Max Form (A-side, B-side) | Conventional Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Each A maps to exactly one B; each B to exactly one A | (1,1) on both sides | 1:1 |
| Each A maps to many B; each B to one A | (0,N) on A-side, (1,1) on B-side | 1:N |
| Many-to-many | (0,N) on both sides | M:N |

---

## 1.5 Drawing and Interpreting ER Diagrams

**Drawing rules (KTU Convention):**
- **Rectangle** → Entity set
- **Diamond** → Relationship set
- **Ellipse** → Attribute
- **Underlined ellipse** → Key attribute
- **Double ellipse** → Multivalued attribute
- **Dashed ellipse** → Derived attribute
- **Double line** → Total participation
- **Arrow →** → "exactly one" side in conventional notation

**Interpreting rules:** Read each binary relationship as a *sentence*: *"Each [Entity A] is related to [cardinality] [Entity B]."*

---

## 1.6 Relational Model — Structure of Relational Databases

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **Relational Model** (proposed by **E.F. Codd, 1970**) represents a database as a collection of **relations**. A **relation** is a mathematical *set of tuples* having the same *attributes* drawn from *domains*. In tabular form, a relation corresponds to a **table**.

### Fundamental Structural Terms

| Term | Formal Definition | Tabular Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Relation** | A subset of the Cartesian product of domains D₁ × D₂ × … × Dₙ | Table |
| **Tuple** | An ordered set of values (d₁, d₂, …, dₙ) where each dᵢ ∈ Dᵢ | Row / Record |
| **Attribute** | A named role played by some domain D | Column / Field |
| **Schema of Relation** | R = (A₁, A₂, …, Aₙ) where Aᵢ are attribute names | Table header |
| **Relation Schema** | R(A₁:D₁, A₂:D₂, …, Aₙ:Dₙ) | Header with type info |
| **Relation Instance** | The current set of tuples at a given time | Current rows of a table |
| **Degree (arity)** | Number of attributes n | Number of columns |
| **Cardinality** | Number of tuples at a time | Number of rows |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instance vs. Schema:** The *schema* (intension) is the structure — it rarely changes. The *instance* (extension) is the snapshot of data — it changes with every update.

---

## 1.7 Domains

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Domain** D is a **set of atomic (indivisible) values** of the same type. Every attribute of a relation is defined on a domain. By definition, every value of an attribute Aᵢ must be an element of its declared domain Dᵢ, and **NULL** is a special value meaning *unknown / not applicable* (it is technically not a domain element, but a permitted placeholder).

### Properties of Domains
1. **Atomicity:** All domain values are *indivisible* (1NF requirement).
2. **Named:** Each domain is given a *name* (e.g., `Phone_Number`, `Salary_USD`).
3. **Constraint-bearing:** A domain may carry a *data type* and *format* (e.g., `Date = {YYYY-MM-DD}`).
4. **Source-of-meaning:** The domain is the *semantic pool* from which attribute values are drawn.

### KTU Standard Domain Examples

| Domain Name | Logical Definition | Example Values |
|---|---|---|
| `USA_phone_numbers` | 10-digit phone numbers | `{650-123-4567, …}` |
| `Social_Security` | 9-digit SSNs | `{123-45-6789, …}` |
| `Names` | Character strings | `{'John', 'Mary', …}` |
| `Ages` | Integers from 0 to 120 | `{0, 1, 2, …, 120}` |
| `Genders` | Enumerated | `{'M', 'F'}` |
| `Salary_Rupees` | Positive integers/floats | `{15000.00, 50000.00, …}` |

### Domain Notation in Schema
For a relation schema `Employee(EmpID, EmpName, Salary, DeptID)`:
```
Employee(
  EmpID   : INTEGER_DOMAIN,
  EmpName : STRING_DOMAIN,
  Salary  : SALARY_RUPEES_DOMAIN,
  DeptID  : INTEGER_DOMAIN
)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **logical definition** of a domain (its set of allowed values) is *separate from* its physical representation (data type). This is a key KTU exam point: domains describe *what the values mean*, not just *how they are stored*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Participation Constraint

### Step-by-step Logical Flow

1. **Identify the two entity sets** in a binary relationship: A (left), B (right).
2. **Examine each entity instance** in set A. Ask: *"Does every A-instance have at least one relationship instance linking it to some B-instance?"*
3. If **YES** for all A-instances → A has **total participation** in R.
4. If **NO** (some A-instances are unlinked) → A has **partial participation** in R.
5. Repeat independently for B.

### Why it Matters (Engineering Utility)
- Drives **referential integrity** rules in the mapped relational schema (foreign keys must NOT be NULL on the totally participating side).
- Determines whether **NULL values** are forbidden in the foreign-key column of the relation representing the entity with total participation.

### Real-World Utility
In an **HR System**, the relation `Department` must have at least one `Employee` (HOD). So the *Employee* relation has a `DeptID` foreign key that is **NOT NULL** — a direct consequence of total participation.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Mapping Cardinality

### Cardinality Decision Procedure

1. Pick any single instance *a* from entity set A.
2. Count the number of related instances of B. Call this *k*.
3. Repeat for many instances to find the pattern: *k* is *always 1*? *Many*? *Variable*?
4. Combine the A-side pattern with the B-side pattern.

### Mapping Cardinality Outcomes (Binary Relationship R between A and B)

| A → B Mapping | B → A Mapping | Cardinality Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| One | One | **1 : 1** |
| One | Many | **1 : N** (or N : 1) |
| Many | One | **N : 1** |
| Many | Many | **M : N** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A relationship is **M:N** *unless proven otherwise*. This is the most general case in ER modeling and has the richest constraint structure in SQL.

### Real-World Utility
- In a **University Database**, mapping cardinality determines whether `Enrolls` becomes one table (M:N) or two tables with a foreign key (1:N).
- In a **Hospital DB**, the *Patient–Room* relationship (1:N) becomes a simple foreign key `RoomID` in the Patient relation.

---

## 2.3 Min-Max Notation — Deep Rules

### Rules for Reading (min, max) Pairs
- **(0, 1)**: Each entity instance relates to *at most one* other instance (optional, unique).
- **(1, 1)**: Each entity instance relates to *exactly one* other instance (mandatory, unique).
- **(0, N)**: Each entity instance relates to *zero or many* others (optional, multiple).
- **(1, N)**: Each entity instance relates to *one or many* others (mandatory, multiple).

> [!NOTE]
> **min = 0 ↔ Partial Participation** for that entity.
> **min ≥ 1 ↔ Total Participation** for that entity.

### Worked Mapping Table

| Relationship | A-side (min,max) | B-side (min,max) | Conventional |
|---|---|---|---|
| Professor *manages* Department (1:1) | (1, 1) | (0, 1) — only one professor manages a dept, but a prof may not manage any | 1:1 |
| Department *employs* Employee (1:N) | (1, N) | (1, 1) | 1:N |
| Student *takes* Course (M:N) | (0, N) | (0, N) | M:N |
| Person *holds* Passport (1:1) | (1, 1) | (1, 1) | 1:1 |

---

## 2.4 The Relational Model — Formal Structure

### Relational Schema Definition

Let R be a relation schema defined as:

$$R(A_1 : D_1,\ A_2 : D_2,\ \dots,\ A_n : D_n)$$

where:
- $R$ = relation (table) name
- $A_i$ = attribute (column) name
- $D_i$ = domain of attribute $A_i$

A **relation instance** $r(R)$ is a *finite set* of tuples:

$$r(R) \subseteq D_1 \times D_2 \times \dots \times D_n$$

### Properties of Tuples in a Relation
1. **Unordered:** Rows have no inherent sequence.
2. **Unique:** No two tuples are identical (this is the set property).
3. **Atomic:** Every value at the intersection of row and column is atomic (1NF).
4. **Attribute-value pairs:** Each value carries its *attribute name* (positional equivalence holds only by convention).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Notation Sheet

| Concept | Symbolic / Notational Form | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Total Participation of A in R | $\forall a \in A,\ \exists r \in R : a \in r$ | Every A-instance must relate |
| Partial Participation of A in R | $\exists a \in A : \forall r \in R,\ a \notin r$ | Some A-instance may be unlinked |
| 1:1 Cardinality | $\vert \pi_A(r) \vert = \vert A \vert$ and $\vert \pi_B(r) \vert = \vert B \vert$ | One-to-one mapping |
| 1:N Cardinality | For each $b \in B$, related A is *unique*; for each $a \in A$, many B allowed | Foreign key on N-side |
| M:N Cardinality | No restriction on either side | New relation required |
| Relation Instance | $r(R) \subseteq D_1 \times D_2 \times \dots \times D_n$ | Subset of Cartesian product |
| Domain | $D = \{v \mid v \text{ satisfies predicate } P_D\}$ | Atomic, named, constrained |
| Tuple | $t = \langle v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n \rangle,\ v_i \in D_i$ | Element of $r(R)$ |
| Schema (Intension) | $R(A_1,\dots,A_n)$ | Static structure |
| Instance (Extension) | $r(R)$ at time $t$ | Dynamic data |
| Degree | $n = \vert \text{schema attributes of } R \vert$ | Number of columns |
| Cardinality | $\vert r(R) \vert$ at time $t$ | Number of rows |

---

## 2.6 Engineering / Industrial Utility

| Field | Application of Structural Constraints |
|---|---|
| **Banking** | Account–Customer (M:N) joint accounts; Branch–ATM (1:N); Customer–Loan (M:N) |
| **E-Commerce** | Order–Product (M:N), Order–Customer (N:1) — drives cart and order history schema |
| **Healthcare** | Patient–Doctor (M:N), Department–Ward (1:N) |
| **University ERP** | Student–Course (M:N), Department–Faculty (1:N), Faculty–Course (M:N) |
| **Social Networks** | User–User (M:N) friends, User–Post (1:N), Post–Comment (1:N) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mastery Tip for KTU:** Always *convert ER-to-Relational* using the rules: 1:1 → merge or FK; 1:N → FK on N-side; M:N → new relation with both as composite PK.

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cardinality Ratio from a Real ER Scenario

**Scenario:** Consider the **Department–Employee** relationship in a company.

### Given
- Let D = {d₁, d₂, d₃} be the set of Department instances.
- Let E = {e₁, e₂, e₃, e₄, e₅} be the set of Employee instances.
- The relationship **WORKS_FOR** is defined as:
  $R = \{(d_1, e_1),\ (d_1, e_2),\ (d_2, e_3),\ (d_2, e_4),\ (d_2, e_5),\ (d_3, \text{none})\}$

Wait — observe $d_3$ has no employees. We need to consider whether $d_3$ is *allowed* to have none.

### Step 1 — Determine B-Side Mapping (Department → Employees)
For each $d_i$, count employees related:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Count}(d_1) &= 2 \\
\text{Count}(d_2) &= 3 \\
\text{Count}(d_3) &= 0 \text{ or } 1
\end{aligned}$$

If the *maximum* count across all departments is **> 1**, then a single department relates to *many* employees. This implies **Department : Employee = 1 : N**.

### Step 2 — Determine A-Side Mapping (Employee → Department)
For each $e_i$, count departments related:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Count}(e_1) &= 1 \quad (\text{works in } d_1) \\
\text{Count}(e_2) &= 1 \quad (\text{works in } d_1) \\
\text{Count}(e_3) &= 1 \quad (\text{works in } d_2) \\
\text{Count}(e_4) &= 1 \quad (\text{works in } d_2) \\
\text{Count}(e_5) &= 1 \quad (\text{works in } d_2)
\end{aligned}$$

The *maximum* count is exactly **1**, so each employee works in *at most one* department.

### Step 3 — Conclude Cardinality

$$\text{Cardinality} = \mathbf{1 : N} \quad \text{(Department : Employee)}$$

where **1** is on the Department side (one department hosts many employees) and **N** is on the Employee side (each employee belongs to one department).

### Step 4 — Translate to Min-Max Notation

Reading the (min, max) pairs:
- For **Department**: min = 1 (must have at least one employee — total participation) or min = 0 if $d_3$ is allowed. Take min = 1 here, max = N. So **(1, N)**.
- For **Employee**: min = 1 (must belong to a department — total participation), max = 1. So **(1, 1)**.

$$\boxed{\text{Department: (1, N)} \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Employee: (1, 1)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Conventional ↔ Min-Max Mapping (Conversion Table)

### Rule of Conversion

For a binary relationship R between A and B:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Conventional } 1{:}1 &\equiv A:(1,1),\ B:(0,1) \text{ or } B:(1,1) \\
\text{Conventional } 1{:}N &\equiv A:(0,N) \text{ or } A:(1,N),\ B:(1,1) \\
\text{Conventional } M{:}N &\equiv A:(0,N),\ B:(0,N)
\end{aligned}$$

### Detailed Derivation of the M:N → Min-Max Case
In M:N:
- A can relate to **zero** (partial) → min = 0.
- A can relate to **many** → max = N.
- Same logic applies to B.

Therefore both sides get **(0, N)**.

### Detailed Derivation of the 1:1 Case
In 1:1:
- A can relate to **at most one** B → max = 1.
- If we say every A *must* relate to one B, min = 1; if optional, min = 0. Conventionally (KTU), one side is *mandatory* (the "owner") and the other *optional* (the "owned").

Hence the **1:1 asymmetric** form is $A:(1,1),\ B:(0,1)$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Constraint Types at a Glance

| Relationship Type | Cardinality (Conventional) | A (min,max) | B (min,max) | A Participation | B Participation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Person ↔ Passport | 1:1 | (1,1) | (1,1) | Total | Total |
| Person ↔ Spouse (optional) | 1:1 | (0,1) | (0,1) | Partial | Partial |
| Department → Employee | 1:N | (1,N) | (1,1) | Total | Total |
| Team → Member (optional) | 1:N | (0,N) | (1,1) | Partial | Total |
| Student ↔ Course | M:N | (0,N) | (0,N) | Partial | Partial |
| Mandatory Enrollment | M:N | (1,N) | (0,N) | Total | Partial |
| Library Book ↔ Borrower | 1:1 | (0,1) | (1,1) | Partial | Total |
| Author ↔ Book | M:N | (0,N) | (0,N) | Partial | Partial |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: From ER Diagram to Relational Schema

### Scenario
ER has entities **STUDENT(RegNo, Name, DOB)** and **COURSE(CourseID, Title, Credits)** with M:N relationship **ENROLLS** with attributes *Grade* and *Semester*.

### Step 1 — Identify Cardinality
**ENROLLS** is **M:N** because a student takes many courses and a course has many students.

### Step 2 — Create a New Relation
For M:N, always create a new relation:

$$R_{\text{ENROLLS}} = \text{ENROLLMENT}(\text{RegNo},\ \text{CourseID},\ \text{Grade},\ \text{Semester})$$

### Step 3 — Form Composite Primary Key

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PK of ENROLLMENT} &= \text{RegNo} \oplus \text{CourseID} \\
&\text{(composite, derived from the participating PKs)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Declare Foreign Keys

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FK}_1 &: \text{ENROLLMENT.RegNo} \rightarrow \text{STUDENT.RegNo} \\
\text{FK}_2 &: \text{ENROLLMENT.CourseID} \rightarrow \text{COURSE.CourseID}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Check Participation Constraints
If **STUDENT** has total participation in ENROLLS (every student must enroll in ≥ 1 course), then `RegNo` is **NOT NULL** in ENROLLMENT.

---

## 3.5 Domain — Derivation of Constraint Compatibility

### Definition Recap
A domain D is a set of allowed atomic values:

$$D = \{v \in \text{BaseType} \mid \text{Predicate}(v) = \text{true}\}$$

### Example — KTU Standard Domain
**Domain `INDIAN_MOBILE_NUMBERS`:**
$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{mobile}} &= \{ v \in \text{STRING} : \text{len}(v)=10,\ v[0] \in \{6,7,8,9\}, \\
&\qquad\qquad\quad v[i] \in \{0..9\}\ \forall i=1..9\}
\end{aligned}$$

### Property Verification
1. **Atomicity:** Each value is a single 10-digit string — indivisible.
2. **Named:** Domain has a name `INDIAN_MOBILE_NUMBERS`.
3. **Constrained:** Predicate restricts length and first digit.
4. **Source-of-meaning:** Any attribute using this domain semantically *means* an Indian mobile number, not just any string.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Difference (Domain vs. Data Type):** A *data type* (INTEGER, VARCHAR) is a **physical/storage** classification. A *domain* is a **logical/semantic** classification. Two attributes of the same data type may belong to **different domains** (e.g., `Age` and `Salary` both INTEGER but semantically distinct domains).

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ER Diagram Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Real-World Entities] --> B[Define Entity Sets with PK]
    B --> C[Identify Relationships between Entities]
    C --> D[Determine Mapping Cardinality 1:1 or 1:N or M:N]
    D --> E[Determine Participation Total or Partial]
    E --> F[Assign Attributes to Entities or Relationships]
    F --> G[Derive Keys Constraints]
    G --> H[Final ER Diagram with Double Lines and Arrows]
    H --> I[Convert to Relational Schema using Mapping Rules]
    I --> J[Final Tables with PK FK and NOT NULL constraints]
    nodeA[Stage 1: Conceptual]
    nodeB[Stage 2: Logical]
    nodeC[Stage 3: Physical]
    A --> nodeA
    F --> nodeA
    H --> nodeB
    I --> nodeB
    J --> nodeC
```

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Participation Constraint in an ER Model. Differentiate between Total and Partial Participation with an example.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A *Participation Constraint* specifies whether every entity instance of an entity set must participate in at least one relationship instance of a relationship set.

**Total Participation (1 mark):**
Total participation means every entity instance must be related to at least one instance of the other entity via the relationship. In an ER diagram, it is shown by a **double line** connecting the entity to the relationship. 

*Example:* Every *Department* in a university must have at least one *Faculty* member as its Head — Department has total participation in the *HEADS* relationship.

**Partial Participation (1 mark):**
Partial participation allows some entity instances to be unrelated. It is shown by a **single line**. 

*Example:* A *Student* may or may not participate in an *Elective Course*; therefore, Student has partial participation in *Enrolls_Elective*.

---

### Question 2: Define a Domain in the Relational Model. Why are domains important?
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A *Domain* D is a set of atomic, named values of the same type from which attribute values are drawn. Formally, an attribute Aᵢ of a relation R is defined on a domain Dᵢ, and every value of Aᵢ must be an element of Dᵢ.

**Importance (1.5 marks):**
1. **Constraint Enforcement:** Domains enforce valid values (e.g., salary must be positive).
2. **Semantic Clarity:** The domain describes *what the value means*, not just storage type.
3. **Reusability:** A domain (e.g., `INDIAN_MOBILE`) can be reused across multiple attributes.
4. **Consistency:** Ensures that `Age` and `Salary` (both integers) are not erroneously interchanged.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1

**Question A (14 marks):**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Mapping Cardinality / Structural Constraint** in an ER model. Illustrate each of the three types (1:1, 1:N, M:N) using suitable examples and conventional notation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Min-Max (look-across) notation** with an example for each cardinality type. Also explain how participation is reflected in min-max pairs.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Mapping Cardinality [7 marks]

**Definition [2 marks]:**
Mapping cardinality, also called cardinality ratio or structural constraint, is the number of relationship instances that one entity can (or must) participate in with respect to another entity. For a binary relationship R between entity sets A and B, the cardinality ratio expresses how many B-entities relate to one A-entity and vice versa.

**Types [5 marks — 1.5 marks each + 0.5 marks example]:**

**(i) One-to-One (1:1) [1.5 marks]:**
A single A relates to at most one B and vice versa.
- *Example:* Person ↔ Passport. Each person has exactly one passport, and each passport is held by exactly one person.
- *Conventional Notation:* Arrow on both sides.

```
PERSON ──→ HAS ──→ PASSPORT
```

**(ii) One-to-Many (1:N) [1.5 marks]:**
A single A relates to many B, but each B relates to exactly one A.
- *Example:* Department → Employee. One department has many employees, but each employee belongs to one department.
- *Conventional Notation:* Arrow on the "many" side only.

```
DEPARTMENT ──→ EMPLOYS ──→ EMPLOYEE
```

**(iii) Many-to-Many (M:N) [2 marks]:**
Many A relate to many B with no restriction.
- *Example:* Student ↔ Course. Each student takes many courses, each course has many students.
- *Conventional Notation:* No arrows (or labels M and N).

```
STUDENT ─── ENROLLS ─── COURSE
```

---

#### Part (b) — Min-Max Notation [7 marks]

**Definition [2 marks]:**
The Min-Max notation (proposed by Chen, often called "look-across") represents each entity's participation by a pair **(min, max)** placed on the edge connecting it to the relationship diamond.

- **min** = minimum number of relationships an entity instance must participate in.
- **max** = maximum number of relationships an entity instance can participate in.

**Mapping [5 marks — 1.25 marks each]:**

| Type | A-side (min,max) | B-side (min,max) | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1:1 | (1, 1) | (0, 1) | Person ↔ Passport |
| 1:N | (0, N) or (1, N) | (1, 1) | Department → Employee |
| M:N | (0, N) | (0, N) | Student ↔ Course |

**Participation Rule [2 marks]:**
- *min = 0* ⇒ Partial participation (optional).
- *min ≥ 1* ⇒ Total participation (mandatory).

For example, in a *mandatory 1:1 Department ↔ HOD* relationship, the Department side is **(1, 1)** (total) and the HOD side is **(0, 1)** (partial — a professor may not head a department yet).

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**Question B (14 marks):**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw an ER diagram for a **Hospital Management System** with entities *Patient, Doctor, Ward, Treatment* and relationships showing all three mapping cardinalities. Identify primary keys and total/partial participation for each relationship.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **Structure of Relational Databases** with the formal definition of relation, tuple, attribute, schema, instance, and domain. Illustrate with one example schema and its instance.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Hospital ER Diagram [7 marks]

**Entities and PKs [1.5 marks]:**
- PATIENT(**PatientID**, Name, DOB, Gender)
- DOCTOR(**DoctorID**, Name, Specialization, Salary)
- WARD(**WardID**, WardType, FloorNo)
- TREATMENT(**TreatmentID**, Description, Cost)

**Relationships [4 marks]:**

1. **ADMITTED_TO** between PATIENT and WARD: **M:N** (a patient may move wards; a ward has many patients). Total participation on Patient side (a patient must be admitted).

```
PATIENT }o--o{ WARD : "ADMITTED_TO"
```

2. **TREATS** between DOCTOR and PATIENT: **M:N** (a doctor treats many patients; a patient may be seen by multiple doctors).

```
DOCTOR }o--o{ PATIENT : "TREATS"
```

3. **SUPERVISES** between DOCTOR and WARD: **1:N** (one doctor supervises one ward; a ward has one supervising doctor).

```
DOCTOR ||--o{ WARD : "SUPERVISES"
```

4. **UNDERGOES** between PATIENT and TREATMENT: **M:N** with descriptive attribute *Date*.

```
PATIENT }o--o{ TREATMENT : "UNDERGOES"
```

**Participation Summary [1.5 marks]:**

| Relationship | A-side | B-side | Cardinality |
|---|---|---|---|
| ADMITTED_TO | Total (Patient) | Partial (Ward) | M:N |
| TREATS | Partial (Doctor) | Partial (Patient) | M:N |
| SUPERVISES | Total (Ward) | Partial (Doctor) | 1:N |
| UNDERGOES | Total (Patient) | Partial (Treatment) | M:N |

---

#### Part (b) — Relational Structure [7 marks]

**Formal Definitions [5 marks]:**

1. **Domain D (1 mark):** A set of atomic, named values. Example: `D_Salary = {10000, 20000, …, 500000}`.

2. **Relation Schema R (1 mark):** A named set of attributes, each with a domain. Example: `DOCTOR(DoctorID: INTEGER, Name: STRING, Specialization: STRING, Salary: SALARY_DOMAIN)`.

3. **Relation Instance r(R) (1 mark):** A finite set of tuples conforming to R. Each tuple t = ⟨101, 'Dr. Roy', 'Cardiology', 85000⟩.

4. **Tuple (1 mark):** An ordered list of values, one per attribute, each drawn from its domain.

5. **Attribute (1 mark):** A named role played by a domain; the column header.

**Example Instance [2 marks]:**

DOCTOR relation:
| DoctorID | Name | Specialization | Salary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | Dr. Roy | Cardiology | 85000 |
| 102 | Dr. Sen | Neurology | 90000 |
| 103 | Dr. Iyer | Pediatrics | 75000 |

Here, **Schema** = `DOCTOR(DoctorID, Name, Specialization, Salary)`, **Degree** = 4 (four attributes), **Cardinality** = 3 (three tuples).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Mixing up Cardinality Ratio vs. Participation Constraint (–2 marks):** Students often confuse 1:1/1:N/M:N (cardinality) with Total/Partial (participation). They are **independent** constraints; both must be specified separately.
> 2. **Forgetting the Double Line Notation (–1 mark):** Total participation is shown by a *double line*, not a thicker line or a dashed line. Examiners strictly check the symbol.
> 3. **Drawing M:N without an arrow anywhere (–1 mark):** For 1:1 and 1:N, the arrow direction *must* indicate the "exactly one" side. M:N has *no arrows*.
> 4. **Confusing Domain with Data Type (–2 marks):** Domain is *semantic*, data type is *physical*. Always say *what values are allowed* and *why*, not just *INTEGER* or *VARCHAR*.
> 5. **Tuple vs. Row (–1 mark):** A *tuple* has no order; a *row* is a tabular display where order matters by convention. Use the formal term "tuple" in KTU answers.
> 6. **Missing the Composite PK in M:N Conversion (–2 marks):** When converting M:N to a new relation, the composite PK must include **both** participating entity PKs. Forgetting this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 7. **Not Labeling Min/Max Pairs (–1 mark):** In Min-Max notation, both numbers must be explicitly written. Writing only "N" loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Checklist

- [ ] **Participation** has two types: **Total (double line)** and **Partial (single line)**.
- [ ] **Mapping Cardinality** has three types: **1:1, 1:N, M:N**.
- [ ] **1:1**: Both sides unique — use arrows on both sides in conventional notation.
- [ ] **1:N**: One side unique, other side many — arrow points to the "one" side.
- [ ] **M:N**: No arrows; both sides free.
- [ ] **Min-Max Notation**: A-side (minA, maxA), B-side (minB, maxB).
- [ ] **min = 0** → Partial; **min ≥ 1** → Total participation.
- [ ] **max = 1** → At most one; **max = N** → Many.
- [ ] **Conversion to Relational Schema**:
   - 1:1 → Foreign key on either side, marked UNIQUE NOT NULL.
   - 1:N → Foreign key on N-side, may be NOT NULL if total participation.
   - M:N → New relation with composite PK = participating PKs + descriptive attributes.
- [ ] **Relation Schema R(A₁:D₁, ..., Aₙ:Dₙ)** declares structure.
- [ ] **Relation Instance r(R)** is the actual data at time t.
- [ ] **Tuple** = row (no ordering).
- [ ] **Attribute** = column.
- [ ] **Domain** = atomic pool of named values.
- [ ] **Degree** = number of attributes.
- [ ] **Cardinality** = number of tuples.
- [ ] **NULL** is not a domain value but a permitted special marker.
- [ ] **Domain ≠ Data Type**: Domain is logical/semantic; data type is physical/storage.
- [ ] **Instance (Extension)** changes over time; **Schema (Intension)** is fixed.
- [ ] Always draw ER diagrams with **PK underlined** in the entity rectangles.
- [ ] **Multivalued attribute** → double ellipse; **Derived attribute** → dashed ellipse; **Composite attribute** → ellipse with sub-ellipses.

### High-Yield Mnemonic

> **"T-P-M"** = **T**otal vs. **P**artial (Participation), **M**apping (Cardinality).

### Formula Flashcard

$$\text{Participation:}\ \forall a \in A,\ \exists r \in R : a \in r\ \text{(Total)}$$

$$\text{Cardinality:}\ \text{Rel}(a) = \{b \in B \mid (a,b) \in R\};\ \vert \text{Rel}(a) \vert \in \{1, N\}$$

$$\text{Relation Instance:}\ r(R) \subseteq D_1 \times D_2 \times \dots \times D_n$$

$$\text{Schema:}\ R(A_1 : D_1,\ A_2 : D_2,\ \dots,\ A_n : D_n)$$

$$\text{Degree:}\ \text{deg}(R) = n;\quad \text{Cardinality:}\ \text{card}(r(R)) = m$$

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In any ER question, *always* state BOTH the cardinality ratio AND the participation constraint for each entity. Saying only "1:N" without specifying total/partial is incomplete and loses marks. Likewise, in a relational schema question, *always* list domains explicitly — never write only data types.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

In the **Entity-Relationship (ER) Model**, an **Attribute** is a named property or characteristic that describes an **Entity** or a **Relationship**. Formally, for an entity type $E$ with attribute set $A = \{A_1, A_2, A_3, \dots, A_n\}$, each attribute $A_i$ maps every entity instance $e \in E$ to a value drawn from its permissible **Domain** (also called Value Set).

Mathematically, an attribute is a function:

$$A_i : E \rightarrow \text{Dom}(A_i)$$

where $\text{Dom}(A_i)$ represents the set of all legal atomic values the attribute can take (e.g., integers, strings, dates, booleans).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Attributes are the *descriptive* elements of the ER Model. Without attributes, an entity would be an anonymous, meaningless object. Attributes carry the *semantic information* that we eventually store as columns (fields) in a relational table.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Student ID Card**. The card itself represents the **Entity** (Student). The fields printed on the card — *Name*, *Roll Number*, *Date of Birth*, *Blood Group*, *Branch*, *Photograph* — are all **Attributes**.

- Just as the card cannot exist without telling you *who* it belongs to and *what* details matter, an entity in a database cannot exist without attributes describing it.
- The **Domain** is the rulebook: Blood Group must belong to the set $\{\text{A}^+, \text{A}^-, \text{B}^+, \text{B}^-, \text{O}^+, \text{O}^-, \text{AB}^+, \text{AB}^-\}$, while Date of Birth belongs to a valid date range.

In real life, every time you fill a form, you are essentially providing **attribute values** for a specific entity.

## Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard ER Notation:**
> - **Ellipse** → represents an attribute
> - **Underlined attribute name** → denotes a **Key Attribute**
> - **Dashed ellipse** → denotes a **Derived Attribute**
> - **Double ellipse** → denotes a **Multi-Valued Attribute**

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Attribute** | A data item that describes a property of an entity or relationship |
| **Domain** | The set of allowable values for an attribute |
| **Atomic Value** | A value that cannot be further subdivided meaningfully (e.g., Age = 21) |
| **Composite Attribute** | An attribute built from smaller sub-parts (e.g., Address = Street + City + Pincode) |
| **Key Attribute** | An attribute whose value uniquely identifies an entity instance |
| **Null** | A special marker meaning "value unknown / not applicable / does not exist" |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## Classification of Attributes (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Attributes are broadly classified into the following categories based on their structural and semantic behavior:

### 1. Simple / Atomic Attribute
