- Cannot be divided into smaller meaningful sub-parts.
- **Example:** `Age = 25`, `Salary = 50000`.
- These are also called **Single-Valued Simple Attributes** when they hold exactly one atomic value.

### 2. Composite Attribute
- Can be broken down into smaller sub-parts, each representing a more basic attribute with its own meaning.
- **Example:** `Full_Name` → `{First_Name, Middle_Name, Last_Name}`; `Address` → `{House_No, Street, City, State, Pincode}`.
- Useful when applications frequently need to access only a portion of the attribute.

### 3. Single-Valued Attribute
- Holds exactly one value for a given entity instance.
- **Example:** `Date_of_Birth` for a student (each student has only one DOB).

### 4. Multi-Valued Attribute
- Can hold **zero, one, or multiple values** for the same entity instance.
- **Example:** A student can have multiple `Phone_Numbers`, `Email_IDs`, or `Degrees`.
- **KTU Notation:** Drawn with a **double-lined ellipse**.
- These violate **First Normal Form (1NF)** and must be converted to a separate relation during relational mapping.

### 5. Derived Attribute
- Its value can be **computed** or **derived** from other attributes — it is not stored physically.
- **Example:** `Age` can be derived from `Date_of_Birth` and the current date; `Experience` from `Date_of_Joining`.
- **KTU Notation:** Drawn with a **dashed/dotted ellipse**.

### 6. Key Attribute (Primary Identifier)
- Uniquely identifies each entity instance within an entity set.
- **Property:** No two entity instances can share the same key attribute value, and it cannot be `NULL`.
- **Example:** `Roll_Number` for Student, `Employee_ID` for Employee, `ISBN` for Book.
- **KTU Notation:** The attribute name is **underlined** inside its ellipse.

### 7. Descriptive Attribute
- An attribute of a **Relationship** (not of an entity). It describes a property of the relationship between entities.
- **Example:** In the relationship `Enrolls(Student, Course)`, the attribute `Enrollment_Date` and `Grade` are descriptive attributes.

## Attribute Domains

The **Domain** $\text{Dom}(A_i)$ defines the permissible set of values an attribute can assume. Three properties govern domains:

- **Atomicity:** Values are indivisible units.
- **Data Type:** INTEGER, VARCHAR, DATE, BOOLEAN, FLOAT, CHAR.
- **Constraints:** NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK (e.g., `Age BETWEEN 0 AND 150`).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Domains enforce data integrity at the schema level. In industries (banking, healthcare, aviation), domains prevent illegal values (e.g., negative salary, future birth dates) from corrupting the database.

## The NULL Concept

`NULL` is a special marker used in SQL/ER models when:

- **Value Unknown:** A student's `Middle_Name` may genuinely not be recorded.
- **Not Applicable:** A `Spouse_Name` for an unmarried employee.
- **Does Not Exist:** A `Driver_License_No` for someone who has never driven.

> [!WARNING]
> `NULL` is **not zero, not blank, and not an empty string**. It is a true placeholder representing the *absence* of information.

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Attribute Type | ER Notation | Storage Implication | Violates 1NF? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Simple / Atomic** | Plain ellipse | Stored as a single column | No |
| **Composite** | Ellipse with sub-ellipses | Each sub-attribute → separate column | No |
| **Single-Valued** | Plain ellipse | One value per row | No |
| **Multi-Valued** | Double ellipse | Requires a separate relation table | **Yes** |
| **Derived** | Dashed ellipse | Not stored; computed via query/trigger | No |
| **Key** | Underlined ellipse | Becomes PRIMARY KEY constraint | No |
| **Descriptive** | Ellipse on relationship diamond | Stored as foreign-key columns | No |
| **Null** | Implicit | Stored as NULL marker | No |

## Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Composite Attributes** are used in HR systems where addresses are split for regional tax processing.
- **Multi-Valued Attributes** drive NoSQL choices (e.g., MongoDB arrays for storing tags).
- **Derived Attributes** save storage cost — instead of storing both `DOB` and `Age`, only `DOB` is stored.
- **Key Attributes** form the foundation of **Primary Key / Foreign Key** constraints in SQL.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Structural Decomposition

## Analytical Classification Matrix

Because this topic is conceptual rather than algebraic, the "derivation" here is a structured logical decomposition showing how each attribute type maps to real-world data.

### Step 1: Identify the Entity Set
Consider an entity **EMPLOYEE** in an HR database.

### Step 2: Enumerate Candidate Properties
Properties of an employee include: `Emp_ID`, `Name`, `Address`, `Phone`, `Email`, `DOB`, `Age`, `Salary`, `Department`, `Skills`.

### Step 3: Classify Each Property

$$ \text{Attribute}(E) \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Atomic} \\ \text{Composite} \\ \text{Single-Valued} \\ \text{Multi-Valued} \\ \text{Derived} \\ \text{Key} \end{cases} $$

### Step 4: Apply the Classification Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Emp\_ID} &\rightarrow \text{Simple + Single-Valued + Key} \\
\text{Name} &\rightarrow \text{Composite} \; (=\;\text{First} \cup \text{Middle} \cup \text{Last}) \\
\text{Address} &\rightarrow \text{Composite} \; (=\;\text{House} \cup \text{Street} \cup \text{City} \cup \text{Pin}) \\
\text{Phone} &\rightarrow \text{Multi-Valued} \\
\text{Email} &\rightarrow \text{Multi-Valued} \\
\text{DOB} &\rightarrow \text{Simple + Single-Valued} \\
\text{Age} &\rightarrow \text{Derived} \; (=\;\text{CurrentDate} - \text{DOB}) \\
\text{Salary} &\rightarrow \text{Simple + Single-Valued} \\
\text{Department} &\rightarrow \text{Simple + Single-Valued} \\
\text{Skills} &\rightarrow \text{Multi-Valued} \; (\text{e.g., {Java, SQL, AWS}})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Validate the Domain Constraints

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dom(Emp\_ID)} &= \text{VARCHAR(10)},\;\text{NOT NULL},\;\text{UNIQUE} \\
\text{Dom(Salary)} &= \text{NUMERIC(10,2)},\; \text{CHECK (Salary} \geq 0) \\
\text{Dom(DOB)} &= \text{DATE},\; \text{CHECK (DOB} \leq \text{Current\_Date)} \\
\text{Dom(Age)} &= \text{INTEGER},\; \text{CHECK (Age BETWEEN 18 AND 70)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Mapping to Relational Schema

Since multi-valued attributes violate 1NF, we create a separate table:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMPLOYEE}(\underline{\text{Emp\_ID}},\; \text{First\_Name},\; \text{Middle\_Name},\; \text{Last\_Name}, \\
\text{House\_No},\; \text{Street},\; \text{City},\; \text{Pincode},\; \text{DOB},\; \text{Salary},\; \text{Dept\_ID}) \\
\text{EMP\_PHONE}(\underline{\text{Emp\_ID}},\; \underline{\text{Phone\_No}}) \quad \text{[Foreign Key → EMPLOYEE]} \\
\text{EMP\_SKILLS}(\underline{\text{Emp\_ID}},\; \underline{\text{Skill\_Name}})
\end{aligned}
$$

The derived attribute `Age` is **not stored** — it is computed at query time using:

$$ \text{Age} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Current\_Date} - \text{DOB}}{365.25} \right\rfloor $$

## Worked Example: Resolving a Composite Attribute

Given the composite attribute `Name = \text{Dr. Arjun R. Nair}`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Name}_{\text{composite}} &\rightarrow \{\text{Salutation},\; \text{First\_Name},\; \text{Middle\_Initial},\; \text{Last\_Name}\} \\
\text{Salutation} &= \text{Dr.} \\
\text{First\_Name} &= \text{Arjun} \\
\text{Middle\_Initial} &= \text{R.} \\
\text{Last\_Name} &= \text{Nair}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Decomposition:** A composite attribute may be partially stored (only `First_Name` and `Last_Name`) if the application does not need the rest — this is a design decision, not a normalization rule.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Mermaid ER Diagram: Attribute Classification on STUDENT Entity

```mermaid
graph LR
    student["STUDENT ENTITY"]

    student --- empA["Roll_No KEY ATTRIBUTE"]
    student --- empB["Name COMPOSITE"]
    student --- empC["DOB SIMPLE"]
    student --- empD["Age DERIVED"]
    student --- empE["Phone MULTIVALUED"]
    student --- empF["Email MULTIVALUED"]
    student --- empG["Branch SIMPLE"]
    student --- empH["Address COMPOSITE"]

    empB --- empB1["First_Name"]
    empB --- empB2["Middle_Name"]
    empB --- empB3["Last_Name"]

    empH --- empH1["House_No"]
    empH --- empH2["Street"]
    empH --- empH3["City"]
    empH --- empH4["Pincode"]

    empD -. derived from .-> empC
```

## Mermaid Block: Attribute Categorization Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["ATTRIBUTE OF ENTITY E"]

    root --> c1["By Value Cardinality"]
    root --> c2["By Structural Complexity"]
    root --> c3["By Storage Origin"]
    root --> c4["By Uniqueness"]

    c1 --> s1["Single Valued"]
    c1 --> s2["Multi Valued"]

    c2 --> cm1["Simple Atomic"]
    c2 --> cm2["Composite"]

    c3 --> d1["Stored"]
    d1 --> d1a["Persistent"]
    c3 --> d2["Derived"]

    c4 --> k1["Key Primary Identifier"]
    c4 --> k2["Non Key Descriptive"]

    s2 -. violates .-> norm["FIRST NORMAL FORM"]
    cm2 -. splittable into .-> cm1
    d2 -. computed from .-> d1a
```

## Mermaid Relationship Diagram: Descriptive Attributes on Relationships

```mermaid
graph LR
    s["STUDENT"]
    c["COURSE"]

    s --- rel["Enrolls RELATIONSHIP"]
    c --- rel

    rel --- relA["Enrollment_Date DESCRIPTIVE"]
    rel --- relB["Grade DESCRIPTIVE"]
    rel --- relC["Semester DESCRIPTIVE"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Read These Diagrams in the Exam:** Always label attribute ellipses with the attribute name. Underline key attribute names. Use double-line borders for multi-valued and dashed borders for derived. KTU examiners award partial credit for correct notation alone.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define an attribute in the ER model. List and briefly explain any four types of attributes with examples.**

**Model Answer:**
An attribute is a named property that describes a characteristic of an entity or relationship in the ER model. It maps each entity instance to a value from a permitted domain.

Four types of attributes:

1. **Simple Attribute:** Cannot be subdivided (e.g., `Age = 25`).
2. **Composite Attribute:** Can be split into sub-parts (e.g., `Address` → Street, City, Pincode).
3. **Multi-Valued Attribute:** Holds multiple values for one entity (e.g., `Phone_Numbers`).
4. **Derived Attribute:** Computed from other attributes (e.g., `Age` derived from `Date_of_Birth`).

> *(Examiner expects: definition + four types + one example each + ER notation symbol. ~150 words.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is a NULL value? Differentiate between "value unknown" and "value not applicable" with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
A NULL is a special marker indicating the absence of a value. It is *not* zero or an empty string.

- **Value Unknown:** The attribute exists but the value has not been recorded. Example: A customer's `Middle_Name` was never collected during registration.
- **Value Not Applicable:** The attribute does not apply to the entity at all. Example: `Spouse_Name` for an unmarried employee.

> *(Examiner expects: NULL definition + two distinct scenarios + correct usage of examples. Avoid confusing the two cases.)*

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **composite and multi-valued attributes** with suitable examples. Illustrate how these attributes are represented in an **ER diagram** using proper notation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the entity **VEHICLE** with the following properties: `Vehicle_No`, `Engine_No`, `Chassis_No`, `Manufacturer`, `Model`, `Year_of_Purchase`, `Age`, `Owner_Name`, `Address`, `Phone_Numbers`, `Insurance_Policy_No`. **Classify each property** as Simple/Composite, Single/Multi-Valued, Stored/Derived, and identify the **key attribute**. Justify each classification. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a):

A **composite attribute** is one that can be decomposed into smaller meaningful sub-parts. Example: `Address = \{House\_No,\; Street,\; City,\; State,\; Pincode\}$. Each sub-attribute has its own semantic meaning and can be accessed independently by applications.

In an ER diagram, a composite attribute is drawn as an ellipse connected to sub-ellipses representing its components.

A **multi-valued attribute** can hold multiple independent values for the same entity. Example: A `Person` entity may have several `Phone_Numbers` or `Email_IDs`.

In an ER diagram, multi-valued attributes are drawn using a **double-lined ellipse**.

**ER Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    person["PERSON"]
    person --- a1["Phone DOUBLE ELLIPSE MULTIVALUED"]
    person --- a2["Email DOUBLE ELLIPSE MULTIVALUED"]
    person --- a3["Address COMPOSITE"]
    a3 --- a3a["House"]
    a3 --- a3b["Street"]
    a3 --- a3c["City"]
    a3 --- a3d["Pincode"]
```

**Storage Note:** Multi-valued attributes violate 1NF and must be mapped to a separate relation. Composite attributes can either be stored as separate columns or kept as one column depending on application needs.

#### Model Answer for (b):

Classification table for the **VEHICLE** entity:

| Property | Composite? | Single/Multi-Valued | Stored/Derived | Key? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Vehicle_No` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | **Yes (Primary Key)** |
| `Engine_No` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | No (Unique but not chosen) |
| `Chassis_No` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | No |
| `Manufacturer` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | No |
| `Model` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | No |
| `Year_of_Purchase` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | No |
| `Age` | Simple | Single-Valued | **Derived** (from Year) | No |
| `Owner_Name` | Composite (First, Last) | Single-Valued | Stored | No |
| `Address` | Composite (House, City, Pin) | Single-Valued | Stored | No |
| `Phone_Numbers` | Simple | **Multi-Valued** | Stored | No |
| `Insurance_Policy_No` | Simple | Single-Valued | Stored | No |

**Justifications:**

- `Vehicle_No` is unique and non-null — chosen as key attribute.
- `Age` is derived as: `Age = Current\_Year - Year\_of\_Purchase`, hence dashed ellipse.
- `Owner_Name` is composite because in Indian naming conventions `First_Name` and `Last_Name` may be independently queried.
- `Phone_Numbers` is multi-valued since one owner may have multiple contact numbers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Module Internal Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **derived attributes** with two real-world examples. Explain why derived attributes are **not stored** in the database and how their values are computed when needed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design an **ER diagram** for a **LIBRARY** system with entities `BOOK`, `MEMBER`, and `AUTHOR`. Identify the attributes of each entity, classify them properly, specify the **primary key** for each entity, and add at least **two descriptive attributes** to the relationships. Use correct ER notation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a):

A **derived attribute** is one whose value can be computed or calculated from other attributes already present in the database. It is *not* physically stored to avoid redundancy and to save storage space.

**Real-World Examples:**

1. **Age** of an employee: Derived from `Date_of_Birth` and the current system date.

   $$ \text{Age} = \frac{\text{Current\_Date} - \text{DOB}}{365.25} $$

2. **Total_Price** of an order: Derived from `Unit_Price × Quantity - Discount`.

   $$ \text{Total\_Price} = (\text{Unit\_Price} \times \text{Quantity}) - \text{Discount} $$

**Why not stored?**

- Avoids data redundancy.
- Ensures the value is always consistent (no stale data).
- Saves storage space.
- Automatically updates when underlying attributes change.

**How computed when needed:** Using SQL `SELECT` statements, views, computed columns, or triggers in the DBMS. For example:

```
SELECT Name, DATEDIFF(YEAR, DOB, GETDATE()) AS Age FROM Employee;
```

In the ER diagram, derived attributes are represented by **dashed-line ellipses**.

#### Model Answer for (b):

**Entities and Their Attributes:**

- **BOOK:** `ISBN` (Key), `Title` (Composite), `Publisher` (Simple), `Edition` (Simple), `Price` (Simple), `Year_of_Publication` (Simple), `Shelf_Location` (Simple).
- **MEMBER:** `Member_ID` (Key), `Name` (Composite), `Phone` (Multi-Valued), `Email` (Multi-Valued), `Membership_Date` (Simple), `Membership_Age` (Derived).
- **AUTHOR:** `Author_ID` (Key), `Name` (Composite), `Affiliation` (Simple).

**Relationships and Descriptive Attributes:**

- `Borrows` (MEMBER ↔ BOOK): Descriptive attributes → `Borrow_Date`, `Return_Due_Date`.
- `Written_By` (BOOK ↔ AUTHOR): Descriptive attribute → `Royalty_Percentage`.

**ER Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    book["BOOK"]
    member["MEMBER"]
    author["AUTHOR"]

    book --- bKey["ISBN KEY"]
    book --- bTitle["Title COMPOSITE"]
    book --- bPub["Publisher"]
    book --- bPrice["Price"]

    member --- mKey["Member_ID KEY"]
    member --- mName["Name COMPOSITE"]
    member --- mPhone["Phone MULTIVALUED"]
    member --- mEmail["Email MULTIVALUED"]
    member --- mDate["Join_Date"]
    member --- mAge["Tenure DERIVED"]

    author --- aKey["Author_ID KEY"]
    author --- aName["Name COMPOSITE"]

    book ---|Borrows| rel1[" "]
    member ---|Borrows| rel1
    rel1 --- rel1a["Borrow_Date"]
    rel1 --- rel1b["Return_Due_Date"]

    book ---|Written_By| rel2[" "]
    author ---|Written_By| rel2
    rel2 --- rel2a["Royalty_Percentage"]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting ER notation rules** — Underline key attributes, use double ellipse for multi-valued, dashed ellipse for derived. Marks deducted if notation is wrong.
> 2. **Confusing NULL types** — Mixing "value unknown" with "not applicable" is a common error.
> 3. **Storing derived attributes** — Examiners check if you understand *why* they aren't stored. Vague answers like "to save space" alone are insufficient.
> 4. **Treating multi-valued attributes as composite** — These are orthogonal classifications. Multi-valued = *how many values*; Composite = *can be split structurally*.
> 5. **Missing descriptive attributes on relationships** — Always check if the relationship itself needs attributes (e.g., date, grade).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- An **attribute** is a named property of an entity or relationship that maps to a value from a domain.
- **Domain** defines the permissible value set (data type + constraints).
- **Simple (Atomic):** Cannot be subdivided further.
- **Composite:** Made up of sub-attributes (e.g., Name, Address).
- **Single-Valued:** Holds exactly one value per entity.
- **Multi-Valued:** Holds multiple values — drawn with double ellipse, violates 1NF.
- **Derived:** Computed from other attributes — drawn with dashed ellipse.
- **Key Attribute:** Uniquely identifies an entity — drawn with underlined name.
- **Descriptive Attribute:** Belongs to a relationship, not an entity.
- **NULL** ≠ zero ≠ blank — it represents absence of value (unknown / not applicable / does not exist).
- ER notation is **mandatory** in KTU answers; missing notation loses marks even if the explanation is correct.
- During relational mapping, **multi-valued attributes → new table**; **composite attributes → split columns**; **derived attributes → omit from schema**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tuples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tuples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Relational Data Model** as defined by E.F. Codd and adopted in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, a **Tuple** is formally defined as an **ordered set of attribute values** that uniquely identifies a single entity instance within a relation (table). Mathematically, a tuple is an element of a relation, denoted as:

$$ t \in r $$

where **r** represents a relation (table) and **t** represents a single tuple (row). A tuple with **n** attributes is structured as:

$$ t = \langle v_1, v_2, v_3, \ldots, v_n \rangle $$

Here, each $v_i$ corresponds to a value drawn from the domain $D_i$ of the *i-th* attribute, such that $v_i \in D_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A tuple is the smallest unit of meaningful data in the relational model. It is synonymous with the terms **row**, **record**, or **entity instance**, but in rigorous academic and examination contexts, the term **tuple** is the preferred relational-algebra notation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **school attendance register**:

- The **Relation** (table) is the entire register book.
- The **Header** (schema) lists column names: *Roll No*, *Name*, *Class*, *Date of Birth*.
- A **Tuple** is a single horizontal entry for one specific student — say, "101, Anil Kumar, 12-A, 14-Aug-2006".

Just as each student gets exactly one row in the register, each unique real-world entity corresponds to one tuple in a relation. The tuple brings together *all attributes* of that entity into a single, inseparable unit of information.

## 1.3 Core Properties Highlight

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Tuple Properties for Examinations:**
> 1. **Atomicity**: Each attribute value within a tuple must be atomic (indivisible) — per **1NF**.
> 2. **Homogeneity of Structure**: All tuples in a relation share the same schema.
> 3. **Uniqueness**: In a set-theoretic relation, no two tuples are identical.
> 4. **Ordering (In Theory)**: Mathematically, a tuple is an *ordered* set, but in practice, attribute ordering is irrelevant because attributes are referenced by name.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Tuple

A tuple in the relational model can be decomposed into the following structural components:

- **Attribute Position (Index):** The positional reference of an attribute within the schema (e.g., 1st, 2nd, ..., *n*-th attribute).
- **Attribute Value:** The actual data stored, which must conform to the domain $D_i$.
- **Tuple Identifier:** In a base relation, a **Primary Key** ensures each tuple is uniquely identifiable.
- **Domain Constraint:** Enforces that every value $v_i \in D_i$.

## 2.2 Ordered vs. Unordered Tuples

In pure relational algebra, a tuple is **ordered** — that is, swapping the values creates a *different* tuple mathematically:

$$ \langle 101, \text{Anil}, \text{CSE} \rangle \neq \langle 101, \text{CSE}, \text{Anil} \rangle $$

However, in SQL-based implementations, **attribute order is irrelevant** because columns are accessed by name, not position.

## 2.3 Tuple Variables

A **tuple variable** is a temporary, range-bound variable used in relational calculus expressions. It is denoted as:

$$ t \in \text{RELATION\_NAME} $$

**Example Use Case:** "Find the names of all students whose GPA exceeds 8.5":

$$ \{ t.Name \mid t \in \text{STUDENT} \land t.GPA > 8.5 \} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Tuple Variables Matter:** They enable **declarative querying** without modifying the base relation. This concept is foundational for **Relational Calculus**, which is a high-weightage topic in KTU examinations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Notation | Meaning | Usage Context |
|----------|---------|---------------|
| $t \in r$ | Tuple *t* belongs to relation *r* | Set membership test |
| $t[A_i]$ | Value of attribute $A_i$ in tuple *t* | Attribute projection |
| $t[A]$ | Sub-tuple over attribute set *A* | Partial projection |
| $\vert t \vert = n$ | Tuple has *n* attribute values | Arity / degree of tuple |
| $\vert r \vert = m$ | Relation has *m* tuples | Cardinality of relation |
| $t_1 = t_2$ | Tuple equality (component-wise) | Equi-join conditions |
| $D = D_1 \times D_2 \times \ldots \times D_n$ | Cartesian product of domains | Domain of a relation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Tuples underpin **OLTP systems** (banking, e-commerce) and **OLAP data warehousing**, where billions of tuples are processed daily. Efficient tuple handling directly impacts query performance, indexing strategies, and storage optimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Counting the Maximum Number of Tuples in a Relation

**Problem Setup:** Consider a relation $R$ with $n$ attributes, where the $i$-th attribute has a domain of size $\vert D_i \vert$. Derive the **maximum possible cardinality** of $R$.

**Step 1:** A single tuple is formed by selecting one value from each domain:

$$ \text{One tuple} \in D_1 \times D_2 \times \ldots \times D_n $$

**Step 2:** The total number of distinct tuple combinations equals the **Cartesian product** of all domain sizes:

$$ \text{Max Tuples} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert $$

**Step 3:** If all domains are finite and equal to size $k$, this simplifies to:

$$ \text{Max Tuples} = k^n $$

**Step 4:** Apply to a concrete KTU-style problem:

> *Relation STUDENT(RegNo, Branch, Semester) has 1000 register numbers, 5 branches, and 8 semesters.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max Tuples} &= \vert D_{\text{RegNo}} \vert \times \vert D_{\text{Branch}} \vert \times \vert D_{\text{Sem}} \vert \\
&= 1000 \times 5 \times 8 \\
&= 40{,}000 \text{ tuples}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** However, real-world relations contain only **valid combinations**. If only 3 branches actually admit students, the practical cardinality becomes:

$$ \text{Practical Tuples} = 1000 \times 3 \times 8 = 24{,}000 $$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Tuple Operations in Relational Algebra

| Operation | Symbol | Effect on Tuple | Example Notation |
|-----------|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| Selection | $\sigma$ | Filters tuples based on predicate | $\sigma_{\text{GPA}>8}(r)$ |
| Projection | $\pi$ | Creates sub-tuples with fewer attributes | $\pi_{\text{Name, Branch}}(r)$ |
| Union | $\cup$ | Combines tuples from two relations | $r \cup s$ |
| Cartesian Product | $\times$ | Concatenates two tuples into one | $t_1 \circ t_2$ |
| Rename | $\rho$ | Reassigns tuple variable | $\rho_{x}(r)$ |

## 3.3 Derivation: Tuple Concatenation in Natural Join

When two tuples $t_1 \in R$ and $t_2 \in S$ share a common attribute $A$ with equal values, the **Natural Join** merges them:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= \langle a_1, b_1, c_1 \rangle \\
t_2 &= \langle c_1, d_1, e_1 \rangle \\
t_{\text{joined}} &= \langle a_1, b_1, c_1, d_1, e_1 \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that the duplicate common attribute $c_1$ is eliminated, ensuring the result remains a **valid tuple under 1NF**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Tuple within the Relational Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Domain D1] --> E[Cartesian Product D1 x D2 x ... x Dn]
    B[Domain D2] --> E
    C[Domain D3] --> E
    D[Domain Dn] --> E
    E --> F[Relation R = Subset of Cartesian Product]
    F --> G[Tuple t1]
    F --> H[Tuple t2]
    F --> I[Tuple t3]
    F --> J[Tuple tm]
    G --> K[Attribute Value v1]
    G --> L[Attribute Value v2]
    G --> M[Attribute Value vn]
    H --> N[Atomic Constraint 1NF]
    I --> N
    J --> N
    N --> O[Valid Relation Instance]
```

## 4.2 Component Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Base Relation r] --> Q[Schema Definition]
    Q --> R[Attribute Domains]
    R --> S[Tuple Construction]
    S --> T[Primary Key Enforcement]
    T --> U[Unique Tuple Storage]
    U --> V[Query Processing Engine]
    V --> W[Selection sigma]
    V --> X[Projection pi]
    V --> Y[Join Operation]
    W --> Z[Result Set New Relation]
    X --> Z
    Y --> Z
```

## 4.3 Tuple Lifecycle in DBMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[User INSERT Statement] --> B1[Parser Validates Schema]
    B1 --> C1[Domain Check on Each Value]
    C1 --> D1{Constraints Satisfied}
    D1 -- Yes --> E1[Tuple Written to Disk]
    D1 -- No --> F1[Rejection Error]
    E1 --> G1[Indexed for Retrieval]
    G1 --> H1[Available for Queries]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide:** The block diagrams illustrate the *flow of information* from raw domains to a fully validated, query-ready tuple. Each tuple is the atomic unit of data manipulated by every DDL, DML, and DCL operation in the DBMS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define a tuple in the relational model. How is it different from a record?**

**Model Answer:**
A tuple is an ordered set of attribute values that represents a single entity instance in a relation. Formally, if relation $R$ has schema $R(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n)$, then a tuple $t \in R$ is denoted as $t = \langle v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n \rangle$ where each $v_i \in D_i$. The difference between a tuple and a record lies in their mathematical rigor: a **tuple** is a set-theoretic, position-aware construct used in relational algebra, while a **record** is the physical file-system-level storage representation. Practically, both refer to the same row of data.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is a tuple variable? Give an example.**

**Model Answer:**
A tuple variable is a free variable that ranges over tuples of a specific relation, used primarily in relational calculus. **Example:** To retrieve names of all CSE branch students:

$$ \{ t.Name \mid t \in \text{STUDENT} \land t.Branch = \text{'}CSE\text{'} \} $$

Here, $t$ is the tuple variable, and $t.Name$ projects the Name attribute.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Choice Option 1

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of a tuple in detail with a suitable example. Discuss the properties of tuples in a relational database. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A tuple is a fundamental data structure in the relational model, representing one complete description of an entity. Consider the relation:

**STUDENT (RegNo, Name, Branch, GPA)**

A sample tuple is:

$$ t_1 = \langle 101, \text{Anil Kumar}, \text{CSE}, 8.7 \rangle $$

**Properties of Tuples:**
1. **Atomicity:** Each attribute value (e.g., 8.7) is indivisible — adheres to 1NF.
2. **Schema Conformity:** All tuples in STUDENT share the same four-attribute structure.
3. **Uniqueness:** No two tuples are identical (set-theoretic property).
4. **Domain Adherence:** Each value belongs to its predefined domain (e.g., GPA $\in$ [0, 10]).
5. **No Positional Dependence in SQL:** Attributes are referenced by name.

---

**Q: (b)** A relation $R$ has 3 attributes with domain sizes 50, 100, and 200. Compute the maximum number of tuples and the maximum number of attributes in any tuple. If only 60% of the Cartesian product represents valid combinations, how many valid tuples can $R$ contain? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max Tuples} &= \vert D_1 \vert \times \vert D_2 \vert \times \vert D_3 \vert \\
&= 50 \times 100 \times 200 \\
&= 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ tuples}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Maximum attributes per tuple:** The number of attributes is fixed by the schema, hence **3 attributes**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Valid Tuples} &= 0.60 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 600{,}000 \text{ tuples}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum cardinality is $10^6$ tuples, but practical constraints reduce this to $6 \times 10^5$ valid tuples.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Choice Option 2

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structural relationship between domains, Cartesian product, relations, and tuples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The relationship can be visualized as a hierarchical containment structure:

1. **Domains** ($D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n$) are the most fundamental level — sets of allowable atomic values.
2. The **Cartesian Product** $D_1 \times D_2 \times \ldots \times D_n$ generates all possible combinations of values.
3. A **Relation** $R$ is a subset of this Cartesian product, filtering only meaningful, valid tuples.
4. **Tuples** are the individual elements (rows) of this relation.

For example, if $D_{\text{Branch}} = \{\text{CSE, ECE, MECH}\}$ and $D_{\text{Sem}} = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$, the Cartesian product yields $3 \times 4 = 12$ combinations, but a relation may include only those combinations representing active student batches.

---

**Q: (b)** Differentiate between ordered and unordered tuples. Explain with suitable relational algebra examples how tuple ordering affects operations like projection and selection. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Ordered Tuple | Unordered Tuple |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| Mathematical Form | $\langle a, b, c \rangle$ | $\{a, b, c\}$ |
| Position Significance | Yes | No |
| SQL Treatment | Not preserved | Effectively used |
| Equality Check | Position-wise | Value-wise |

In pure relational algebra:

$$ \sigma_{\text{GPA} > 8}(R) = \{ t \mid t \in R \land t[\text{GPA}] > 8 \} $$

This selection is **independent of tuple order**, as it filters based on predicate evaluation.

For projection:

$$ \pi_{\text{Name, Branch}}(R) = \{ t[\text{Name, Branch}] \mid t \in R \} $$

The projection creates **sub-tuples** with reduced arity, preserving the relative order of specified attributes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **tuple arity** (number of attributes) with **relation cardinality** (number of tuples). Always remember: arity = columns, cardinality = rows.
> 2. **Mark Loss Zone:** Forgetting to specify the **domain** when writing tuple notation will cost 1-2 marks. Always write $v_i \in D_i$.
> 3. **Truncation Trap:** In numerical problems, students often compute the *Cartesian product* but forget to apply the **percentage validity factor** given in the question.
> 4. **Notation Error:** Do not write tuples as sets $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ in relational algebra — use angle brackets $\langle \cdot \rangle$ to denote ordered tuples.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A tuple is an ordered collection of attribute values, $t = \langle v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n \rangle$, where each $v_i \in D_i$.
- **Notation:** Membership is denoted as $t \in r$, and attribute access as $t[A_i]$.
- **Arity:** The number of attributes in a tuple equals the **degree** of the relation.
- **Cardinality:** The number of tuples in a relation denotes its **cardinality**.
- **Tuple Variable:** Used in relational calculus; ranges over a relation (e.g., $t \in \text{STUDENT}$).
- **Maximum Tuples Formula:** $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert$.
- **Properties:** Atomicity, uniqueness, schema conformity, domain adherence.
- **Ordered vs. Unordered:** Mathematically ordered; practically unordered in SQL.
- **Operations:** Selection, Projection, Union, Join — all operate on tuples as atomic units.
- **1NF Compliance:** Every tuple value must be atomic — no nested relations or multi-valued attributes.
- **Primary Key:** Ensures tuple uniqueness within a base relation.
- **Natural Join:** Merges tuples by eliminating duplicate common attribute values.
- **Engineering Relevance:** Tuples are the backbone of OLTP transactions, OLAP analytics, and distributed query processing in modern DBMS engines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relations, Types of Keys, Integrity Constraints, Synthesizing ER Diagram to Relational Schema, Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relations, Types of Keys, Integrity Constraints, Synthesizing ER Diagram to Relational Schema, Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Relational Model — Formal Definition

The **Relational Model** of data, introduced by **Dr. Edgar F. Codd** (IBM, 1970), is the dominant data model used in modern commercial Database Management Systems. It represents the database as a **collection of relations**, where each relation is conceptually a **two-dimensional table** consisting of rows and columns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Relation** $R$ on a set of domains $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n$ is a subset of the Cartesian product of these domains:
> $$R \subseteq D_1 \times D_2 \times D_3 \times \dots \times D_n$$
> Where $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n$ are the **domains** (sets of atomic values) and $n$ is the **degree** (arity) of the relation.

**Component Terminology Mapping (Relation ↔ Table):**

| Formal Relational Term | Equivalent Table Term | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Relation | Table | A 2D structure holding data of one entity type |
| Tuple | Row / Record | A single instance of the relation |
| Attribute | Column / Field | A named property describing a tuple |
| Domain | Pool of valid values | The set of allowable atomic values |
| Schema | Table Header / Column Titles | The structural blueprint of the relation |
| Instance | Table Body / Current Rows | The actual data values at a point in time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Degree of a Relation** = the number of attributes (columns).
> **Cardinality of a Relation** = the number of tuples (rows) currently present.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Relation is Like a Spreadsheet

Think of a **Relation** as a single **spreadsheet tab in Excel** dedicated to one concept (e.g., "Students"). Each **column header** is an *attribute* (Roll_No, Name, Branch, CGPA). Each **row** is a *tuple* representing one real-world entity (one student). The **domain** is the rulebook — for example, the *Branch* column can only hold values from {CSE, ECE, ME, CE}. This one-to-one mapping between an Excel-like table and Codd's Relation is the cornerstone of relational thinking.

---

### 1.3 Types of Keys — The "Identity" of Records

Keys are the **backbone of the relational model** — they allow us to uniquely identify rows, link tables, and enforce integrity. The minimum cardinality constraints for a KTU-compliant key system are summarized below:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Definition — Super Key:**
> A **Super Key** is any set of one or more attributes that **uniquely identifies** a tuple in a relation. The set may contain *redundant* attributes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Definition — Candidate Key:**
> A **Candidate Key** is a **minimal** Super Key — removing even one attribute destroys the uniqueness property. Every relation must have **at least one** candidate key.

**Hierarchy of Keys (in descending order of generality):**

1. **Super Key** — Identifies tuples uniquely (may be redundant).
2. **Candidate Key** — A *minimal* super key (no subset is a super key).
3. **Primary Key** — The *chosen* candidate key (cannot be NULL).
4. **Alternate Key** — Candidate keys that were *not* chosen as primary.
5. **Foreign Key** — An attribute in one relation that references the **Primary Key** of another (or the same) relation.
6. **Composite Key** — A key formed by the combination of *two or more* attributes.
7. **Unique Key** — Values must be unique but **NULL is allowed** (one NULL in most DBMS).
8. **Partial Key (Discriminator)** — A set of attributes that *weakly* identifies a tuple in a **weak entity** type.

---

### 1.4 Integrity Constraints — The "Rules of Validity"

Integrity constraints are the **guardians of data correctness** in a relational database. KTU syllabus mandates the following five:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Domain Integrity Constraint:**
> Every attribute value must be an **atomic** value drawn from its declared domain. (E.g., `Age INT CHECK (Age >= 0)`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2. Entity Integrity Constraint:**
> The **Primary Key** attribute(s) of a relation must have a **non-NULL** value for every tuple. This guarantees that every record is uniquely identifiable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3. Referential Integrity Constraint:**
> A **Foreign Key** value in a child relation must either **match an existing Primary Key value** in the parent relation, or be **NULL** (if business rules permit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **4. Key Integrity (Uniqueness) Constraint:**
> The value of any **Candidate Key** (including Primary Key) must be **unique** across all tuples of a relation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5. Null Integrity Constraint:**
> Specifies whether NULL values are permitted for a particular attribute (controlled via `NOT NULL` clauses).

---

### 1.5 Synthesizing ER Diagram → Relational Schema — Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> The process of converting a conceptual **Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagram** into a logical **Relational Schema** is called **ER-to-Relational Mapping**. KTU syllabus mandates the **7-step algorithm** (Elmasri & Navathe style) which we will fully expand in Section 3.

**Intuitive Analogy — ER is the "Blueprint", Relational Schema is the "Final Building":**
The ER diagram is a high-level architectural drawing (concepts: entities, relationships, attributes). The Relational Schema is the engineer’s final list of rooms (tables), doors (foreign keys), and load-bearing walls (primary keys). Mapping is the act of "pouring concrete" — turning abstract concepts into concrete tables.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Properties of a Relation (The 6 Codd’s Rules for Tables)

For a relation to be a *valid* relational structure, it **must** satisfy the following properties (extremely high-yield for KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions):

1. **Atomicity of Values:** Every cell contains an *atomic* (indivisible) value — no repeating groups or arrays. This enforces **First Normal Form (1NF)**.
2. **Unique Tuples:** No two tuples in a relation are identical (guaranteed by the mathematical definition of a *set*).
3. **Unordered Tuples:** The order of rows is *immaterial*. A relation is a *set*, not a *list*.
4. **Unordered Attributes:** The order of columns is *immaterial*; columns are identified by name, not position.
5. **Unique Attribute Names:** Within a relation, every attribute must have a *distinct* name.
6. **Domain Compatibility:** Every value in a column must belong to the *same domain* as declared in the schema.

### 2.2 Detailed Key Analysis — The Why Behind Each Key

**A. Super Key vs Candidate Key — A Worked Explanation**

Consider a `STUDENT` relation with attributes `{Roll_No, Name, DOB, Phone, Email}`.

- `{Roll_No}` is a super key and a candidate key (unique on its own).
- `{Roll_No, Name}` is also a super key but **NOT** a candidate key (Name is *redundant*).
- `{Name, DOB}` might be a candidate key (only minimal identifier we can rely on).
- `{Phone}` may be a candidate key if phone numbers are guaranteed unique.

Hence, *every candidate key is a super key, but the converse is false*.

**B. Primary Key Selection — The Database Designer’s Choice**

A relation may have **multiple** candidate keys. The **Primary Key** is the one *chosen* by the designer to be the principal identifier. Criteria for primary key selection:
- **Stability** (values rarely change).
- **Simplicity** (preferably single-attribute, short).
- **Familiarity** (semantically meaningful to users).
- **Non-nullable by default** (NOT NULL is enforced).

**C. Foreign Key Mechanics**

A Foreign Key $FK$ in relation $R_2$ referencing Primary Key $PK$ of relation $R_1$ enforces:
$$ \text{For every tuple } t_2 \in R_2, \; t_2.FK \; \text{either equals some} \; t_1.PK \in R_1 \; \text{or is NULL.} $$

**D. Composite vs Simple Keys**

- **Simple Key:** A single attribute uniquely identifies a tuple. *Example:* `Roll_No`.
- **Composite Key:** A combination of 2+ attributes is required. *Example:* `{Student_ID, Course_ID}` in an `ENROLLMENT` relation.

**E. Unique Key vs Primary Key — The Critical Distinction**

| Feature | Primary Key | Unique Key |
|---|---|---|
| NULL allowed? | **NO** (NOT NULL enforced) | **YES** (one NULL in most RDBMS) |
| Quantity per table | Exactly **1** | Many (no theoretical limit) |
| Purpose | Principal identifier | Alternate unique identifier |
| Clustered index? | Default in many DBMS | Default non-clustered |

### 2.3 Integrity Constraints — Deep Mechanism

**Entity Integrity in Action:**
If `STUDENT(Roll_No, Name, Branch)` has `Roll_No` as Primary Key, then the system **rejects** any `INSERT` with `Roll_No = NULL`.

**Referential Integrity — The Two Action Choices on Violation:**

When a `DELETE` or `UPDATE` on the parent table would orphan child rows, the DBMS can:

1. **RESTRICT / NO ACTION** — Reject the modification.
2. **CASCADE** — Propagate the change to all matching child rows.
3. **SET NULL** — Set the foreign key in child rows to NULL.
4. **SET DEFAULT** — Set the foreign key to a default value.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| $R(A_1 \colon D_1, \dots, A_n \colon D_n)$ | Relation schema with attributes and domains | $STUDENT(Roll\_No \colon INT, Name \colon STRING)$ |
| $t[A_i]$ | Value of attribute $A_i$ in tuple $t$ | $t[Name] = \text{"Anu"}$ |
| $r(R)$ | Relation state (set of tuples) at a moment | $r(STUDENT) = \{t_1, t_2, \dots\}$ |
| $\vert R \vert$ | Degree (number of attributes) of $R$ | $\vert STUDENT \vert = 4$ |
| $\vert r \vert$ | Cardinality (number of tuples) in current state | $\vert r(STUDENT) \vert = 500$ |
| $D_1 \times D_2$ | Cartesian product (all possible pairings) | $\{a,b\} \times \{1,2\} = \{(a,1),(a,2),(b,1),(b,2)\}$ |
| $PK(R)$ | Primary Key of relation $R$ | $PK(STUDENT) = Roll\_No$ |
| $FK$ referencing $PK$ | Foreign Key relationship | $ENROLLMENT.Roll\_No \rightarrow STUDENT.Roll\_No$ |
| $\rightarrow$ | Referential link (FK to PK) | `Branch_Code` in STUDENT $\rightarrow$ `Code` in BRANCH |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Banking Systems:** Referential integrity guarantees that no transaction can exist without a valid account.
- **Hospital Management:** Entity integrity ensures every patient record is uniquely identified for safety-critical care.
- **E-Commerce:** Foreign keys link Orders → Customers → Addresses → Payment methods.
- **Aerospace & IoT:** Domain integrity prevents sensor values from exceeding safe physical ranges.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The 7-Step ER-to-Relational Mapping Algorithm

This is the **core KTU Module 1 derivation** that frequently appears as a 7-mark or 14-mark question. We will derive it step by step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Input:** A complete ER diagram with entities, attributes (simple, composite, multivalued, derived), relationships (1:1, 1:N, M:N), and participation constraints (total/partial).
> **Output:** A set of normalized relational schemas with Primary Keys, Foreign Keys, and Integrity Constraints clearly identified.

**STEP 1 — Map Strong (Regular) Entity Sets to Relations**

**Logic:** Each strong entity set $E$ becomes a relation $R$ that includes all the *simple* attributes of $E$.

**Derivation Step:**
- For each strong entity set $E$, create a relation $R$ such that:
$$R(\text{simple\_attributes}(E) \cup PK(E))$$
- Choose one of the *key attributes* of $E$ as the Primary Key $PK(E)$.

*Example:* $E = \text{STUDENT}$ with attributes `{Roll_No, Name, DOB, CGPA}`.

$$
R_{STUDENT} \;=\; STUDENT(\underline{Roll\_No},\; Name,\; DOB,\; CGPA)
$$

The underline denotes the **Primary Key**.

**STEP 2 — Map Weak Entity Sets to Relations**

**Logic:** A weak entity set $W$ with owner entity $E$ becomes a relation that includes the *Primary Key of E* as a **Foreign Key**, plus the *partial key* of $W$.

**Derivation Step:**
- For each weak entity set $W$ with owner $E$:
$$R_W(\text{simple\_attributes}(W) \cup \text{partial\_key}(W) \cup PK(E))$$
- The **Primary Key of $R_W$** is the *combination* of $PK(E)$ and the partial key of $W$.

*Example:* $W = \text{DEPENDENT}$ (weak) owned by $E = \text{EMPLOYEE}$ with `Emp_ID` as PK and partial key `Dep_Name`.

$$
R_{DEPENDENT} \;=\; DEPENDENT(\underline{Emp\_ID},\; \underline{Dep\_Name},\; Age,\; Relationship)
$$

$$
\text{Foreign Key: } Emp\_ID \;\rightarrow\; EMPLOYEE(Emp\_ID)
$$

**STEP 3 — Map Multivalued Attributes to Separate Relations**

**Logic:** Since the relational model (1NF) prohibits multivalued attributes, each multivalued attribute $M$ of entity $E$ is split into a new relation.

**Derivation Step:**
- For each multivalued attribute $M$ of entity $E$:
$$R_M(\underline{PK(E)},\; \underline{M})$$
- The Primary Key is the **combination** of $PK(E)$ and $M$.

*Example:* $E = \text{STUDENT}$ has multivalued attribute `Phone_No`.

$$
R_{STUDENT\_PHONE} \;=\; STUDENT\_PHONE(\underline{Roll\_No},\; \underline{Phone\_No})
$$

**STEP 4 — Map Binary 1:1 Relationship Sets**

**Logic:** For a 1:1 relationship $S$ between $E_1$ and $E_2$, the Primary Key of *either* $E_1$ or $E_2$ can be chosen as a Foreign Key in the other relation.

**Derivation Step:**
- Identify the **total participation side** (the entity that *must* participate).
- Place the PK of the *other* entity as a FK in this entity's relation.
- If both are total, you can merge the two relations.

*Example:* 1:1 relationship `MANAGES` between `EMPLOYEE` and `DEPARTMENT` (each dept has exactly one manager, and that manager manages only that dept).

$$
EMPLOYEE(\underline{Emp\_ID},\; Name,\; Mgr\_Start\_Date,\; Dept\_ID)
$$

$$
\text{Foreign Key: } Dept\_ID \;\rightarrow\; DEPARTMENT(\underline{Dept\_ID})
$$

**STEP 5 — Map Binary 1:N Relationship Sets**

**Logic:** For a 1:N relationship $S$ between $E_1$ (1-side) and $E_2$ (N-side), place the **PK of the 1-side** as a **FK in the N-side relation**, plus any descriptive attributes of $S$.

**Derivation Step:**
- For each 1:N relationship $S$ from $E_1$ to $E_2$:
$$R_{E_2}(\text{attrs}(E_2) \cup PK(E_1) \cup \text{descriptive\_attrs}(S))$$
- $PK(E_1)$ becomes a **Foreign Key** in $R_{E_2}$.

*Example:* 1:N relationship `STUDIES` between `DEPARTMENT` (1) and `STUDENT` (N).

$$
STUDENT(\underline{Roll\_No},\; Name,\; Branch,\; CGPA,\; Dept\_Code)
$$

$$
\text{Foreign Key: } Dept\_Code \;\rightarrow\; DEPARTMENT(\underline{Dept\_Code})
$$

**STEP 6 — Map Binary M:N Relationship Sets**

**Logic:** An M:N relationship $S$ between $E_1$ and $E_2$ becomes a **new relation** whose primary key is the *combination* of $PK(E_1)$ and $PK(E_2)$.

**Derivation Step:**
- For each M:N relationship $S$ between $E_1$ and $E_2$:
$$R_S(\underline{PK(E_1)},\; \underline{PK(E_2)},\; \text{descriptive\_attrs}(S))$$
- Both $PK(E_1)$ and $PK(E_2)$ are **Foreign Keys**.

*Example:* M:N relationship `ENROLLS` between `STUDENT` and `COURSE` with descriptive attribute `Grade`.

$$
ENROLLS(\underline{Roll\_No},\; \underline{Course\_ID},\; Grade,\; Semester)
$$

$$
\text{FK: } Roll\_No \;\rightarrow\; STUDENT(Roll\_No)
$$

$$
\text{FK: } Course\_ID \;\rightarrow\; COURSE(Course\_ID)
$$

**STEP 7 — Map N-ary Relationship Sets (n > 2)**

**Logic:** For an n-ary relationship $S$ among $n$ entity sets, create a new relation containing the **Primary Keys of all participating entities** as composite primary key, plus any descriptive attributes.

**Derivation Step:**
- For each n-ary relationship $S$ among $E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n$:
$$R_S(\underline{PK(E_1)},\; \underline{PK(E_2)},\; \dots,\; \underline{PK(E_n)},\; \text{descriptive\_attrs}(S))$$
- All $PK(E_i)$ are Foreign Keys.

*Example:* Ternary relationship `SUPPLIES` between `SUPPLIER`, `PART`, and `PROJECT`.

$$
SUPPLIES(\underline{Supplier\_ID},\; \underline{Part\_ID},\; \underline{Project\_ID},\; Quantity)
$$

### 3.2 Composite Attributes — The Decomposition Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule:** When an entity has a *composite* attribute, only its **simple component attributes** are included in the relation — *not* the composite name itself.

*Example:* Entity `STUDENT` has composite attribute `Address` with sub-parts `{Street, City, State, PIN}`.

**Before decomposition (Conceptual):**
$$
STUDENT(Roll\_No,\; Name,\; Address(Street,\; City,\; State,\; PIN))
$$

**After decomposition (Logical Relational Schema):**
$$
STUDENT(\underline{Roll\_No},\; Name,\; Street,\; City,\; State,\; PIN)
$$

### 3.3 Derived Attributes — The Exclusion Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule:** A *derived* attribute is **NOT** stored in the relation; it is computed at query time using a formula or trigger.

*Example:* `Age` is derived from `DOB`. We store only `DOB`; `Age` is computed as:
$$
Age \;=\; \text{CurrentDate} - DOB
$$

### 3.4 Worked-Out Synthesis Example (University ER → Schema)

**Source ER Diagram Description:**
- **Entities:** `STUDENT(Roll_No, Name, DOB, CGPA)`, `COURSE(Course_ID, Title, Credits)`, `BRANCH(Code, Branch_Name)`.
- **Relationships:**
  - `STUDENT` *belongs to* `BRANCH` (N:1).
  - `STUDENT` *enrolls in* `COURSE` (M:N) with attribute `Grade`.
  - `COURSE` has multivalued attribute `Textbook`.

**Synthesized Relational Schema (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Strong Entities:**
$$
STUDENT(\underline{Roll\_No},\; Name,\; DOB,\; CGPA)
$$

$$
COURSE(\underline{Course\_ID},\; Title,\; Credits)
$$

$$
BRANCH(\underline{Code},\; Branch\_Name)
$$

**Step 2 — Multivalued Attribute (Textbook):**
$$
COURSE\_TEXTBOOK(\underline{Course\_ID},\; \underline{Textbook})
$$

**Step 3 — 1:N Relationship (Student belongs to Branch):**
$$
STUDENT(\underline{Roll\_No},\; Name,\; DOB,\; CGPA,\; Branch\_Code)
$$

$$
\text{FK: } Branch\_Code \;\rightarrow\; BRANCH(Code)
$$

**Step 4 — M:N Relationship (Student enrolls in Course):**
$$
ENROLLS(\underline{Roll\_No},\; \underline{Course\_ID},\; Grade)
$$

$$
\text{FK: } Roll\_No \;\rightarrow\; STUDENT(Roll\_No)
$$

$$
\text{FK: } Course\_ID \;\rightarrow\; COURSE(Course\_ID)
$$

**Final Consolidated Schema (6 Relations):**

| # | Relation | Primary Key | Foreign Key(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | STUDENT | Roll_No | Branch_Code → BRANCH |
| 2 | COURSE | Course_ID | — |
| 3 | BRANCH | Code | — |
| 4 | COURSE_TEXTBOOK | (Course_ID, Textbook) | Course_ID → COURSE |
| 5 | ENROLLS | (Roll_No, Course_ID) | Roll_No → STUDENT, Course_ID → COURSE |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid ER Diagram for the Worked-Out Example (Section 3.4)

The diagram below visualizes the source ER model that was mapped in Section 3.4. It uses **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (prefixed with letters) and **raw uppercase labels** per the Mermaid safeguards.

```mermaid
erDiagram
    STUDENT_ENTITY {
        string Roll_No PK
        string Name
        date DOB
        float CGPA
    }
    COURSE_ENTITY {
        string Course_ID PK
        string Title
        int Credits
    }
    BRANCH_ENTITY {
        string Code PK
        string Branch_Name
    }
    ENROLLS_REL {
        float Grade
    }
    BELONGS_REL
    STUDENT_ENTITY ||--o{ ENROLLS_REL : "enrolls"
    COURSE_ENTITY ||--o{ ENROLLS_REL : "is taken in"
    STUDENT_ENTITY }o--|| BRANCH_ENTITY : "belongs to"
    COURSE_ENTITY ||--o{ COURSE_TEXTBOOK_REL : "has textbook"
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — The 7-Step ER-to-Relational Mapping Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Start: Read ER Diagram"]
    stepB["STEP 1: Map each Strong Entity to a Relation with its PK"]
    stepC["STEP 2: Map each Weak Entity using Owner PK plus Partial Key"]
    stepD["STEP 3: Split each Multivalued Attribute into a new Relation"]
    stepE["STEP 4: For 1:1 Relationship, place PK of one side as FK on the other"]
    stepF["STEP 5: For 1:N Relationship, place PK of 1 side as FK on N side"]
    stepG["STEP 6: For M:N Relationship, create new Relation with combined PK"]
    stepH["STEP 7: For N ary Relationship, create new Relation with all PKs"]
    stepI["Decompose Composite Attributes into simple components"]
    stepJ["Exclude Derived Attributes from storage"]
    stepK["Enforce Entity, Referential, Domain, Key Integrity Constraints"]
    stepL["Output: Final Relational Schema Set"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of the Relational Mapping Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Conceptual ER Diagram"] --> engineB["Mapping Engine Module 1"]
    inputC["Key and Constraint Specifications"] --> engineB
    engineB --> outputD["Relational Schemas List"]
    engineB --> outputE["Foreign Key Reference Graph"]
    engineB --> outputF["Integrity Constraint Catalogue"]
    outputD --> gdbmsG["Target RDBMS Deployment"]
    outputE --> gdbmsG
    outputF --> gdbmsG
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define the following with an example for each: (i) Super Key (ii) Candidate Key (iii) Primary Key.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Super Key** is any set of one or more attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple in a relation. Example: In `STUDENT(Roll_No, Name, DOB, Phone)`, both `{Roll_No}` and `{Roll_No, Name}` are super keys.

A **Candidate Key** is a *minimal* super key, i.e., no proper subset of it is a super key. Example: `{Roll_No}` is a candidate key, but `{Roll_No, Name}` is not (since `Name` is redundant).

A **Primary Key** is the candidate key *selected* by the database designer to uniquely identify tuples. Example: `Roll_No` may be chosen as the primary key of `STUDENT`.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for each definition + example)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Explain Entity Integrity and Referential Integrity Constraints with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Entity Integrity Constraint:** The Primary Key attribute of a relation must always have a non-NULL value. This ensures every tuple is uniquely identifiable.
Example: In `STUDENT(Roll_No, Name)`, an attempt to `INSERT` a row with `Roll_No = NULL` will be rejected.

**Referential Integrity Constraint:** A Foreign Key value in a relation must either match an existing Primary Key value in the referenced relation or be NULL.
Example: If `ENROLLS(Roll_No, Course_ID)` has `Roll_No` as FK referencing `STUDENT(Roll_No)`, then `Roll_No = 101` is valid only if a student with `Roll_No = 101` already exists in `STUDENT`. The system rejects orphan enrollments.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks each constraint with example)*

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often **omit Foreign Key declaration lines** when mapping relationships — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Multivalued attributes are frequently forgotten** in mappings — examiners specifically test Step 3.
> 3. Composite attributes must be *decomposed*; placing the composite name in the relation loses marks.
> 4. Students write the *Primary Key* of a 1:N relationship wrongly on the 1-side instead of the N-side.
> 5. Do not confuse **Candidate Key** with **Super Key**; always justify *minimality*.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the different types of keys used in the relational model with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The relational model uses several key types to identify tuples and link relations:

1. **Super Key:** A set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple (may include redundant attributes). Example: In `EMPLOYEE(Emp_ID, Name, DOB, Phone)`, `{Emp_ID, Name}` is a super key.

2. **Candidate Key:** A *minimal* super key from which no attribute can be removed without losing uniqueness. Example: `{Emp_ID}` alone is a candidate key, but `{Emp_ID, Name}` is not.

3. **Primary Key:** The candidate key chosen by the designer as the principal identifier. It must be unique and non-NULL. Example: `Emp_ID` is selected as the PK.

4. **Alternate Key:** Candidate keys not chosen as primary. Example: If `Phone` is also a candidate key but not selected, it becomes the alternate key.

5. **Foreign Key:** An attribute in one relation referencing the PK of another. Example: `Dept_ID` in `EMPLOYEE` referencing `Dept_ID` in `DEPARTMENT`.

6. **Composite Key:** A key with two or more attributes. Example: `{Student_ID, Course_ID}` in `ENROLLMENT`.

7. **Unique Key:** A non-primary candidate key that allows one NULL. Example: `Email` field in `STUDENT`.

*(7 marks — 1 mark per key type with example)*

**(b)** Map the following ER diagram to a relational schema. Entities: `EMPLOYEE(Emp_ID, Ename, Salary, DOB)` and `DEPARTMENT(Dept_ID, Dname, Location)`. Relationship: `WORKS_FOR` (1:N from DEPARTMENT to EMPLOYEE) with attribute `Joining_Date`. Also, `DEPARTMENT` has a multivalued attribute `Phone_No`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Map Strong Entities:**

$$
EMPLOYEE(\underline{Emp\_ID},\; Ename,\; Salary,\; DOB)
$$

$$
DEPARTMENT(\underline{Dept\_ID},\; Dname,\; Location)
$$

**Step 2 — Map Multivalued Attribute (`Phone_No` of DEPARTMENT):**

$$
DEPT\_PHONE(\underline{Dept\_ID},\; \underline{Phone\_No})
$$

$$
\text{FK: } Dept\_ID \;\rightarrow\; DEPARTMENT(Dept\_ID)
$$

**Step 3 — Map 1:N Relationship `WORKS_FOR` (Dept → Employee):**

Place `Dept_ID` (PK of DEPARTMENT) as a Foreign Key in EMPLOYEE, along with the descriptive attribute `Joining_Date`:

$$
EMPLOYEE(\underline{Emp\_ID},\; Ename,\; Salary,\; DOB,\; Dept\_ID,\; Joining\_Date)
$$

$$
\text{FK: } Dept\_ID \;\rightarrow\; DEPARTMENT(Dept\_ID)
$$

**Final Consolidated Schema (3 Relations):**

| Relation | Primary Key | Foreign Key(s) |
|---|---|---|
| DEPARTMENT | Dept_ID | — |
| DEPT_PHONE | (Dept_ID, Phone_No) | Dept_ID → DEPARTMENT |
| EMPLOYEE | Emp_ID | Dept_ID → DEPARTMENT |

*(7 marks — 2 marks for each step + 1 mark for FK declaration)*

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a)** Describe the five integrity constraints in the relational model with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The five integrity constraints that safeguard the consistency of a relational database are:

1. **Domain Integrity:** Every attribute value must be atomic and belong to its declared domain. Example: `Marks INT CHECK (Marks BETWEEN 0 AND 100)`.

2. **Entity Integrity:** The Primary Key of a relation cannot be NULL. Example: `STUDENT(Roll_No PK, Name)` — inserting `Roll_No = NULL` is rejected.

3. **Referential Integrity:** A Foreign Key must match an existing PK in the parent table or be NULL. Example: `ENROLLS.Roll_No` must match a valid `STUDENT.Roll_No`.

4. **Key Integrity (Uniqueness):** Every Candidate Key value must be unique across the table. Example: Two students cannot share the same `Roll_No`.

5. **Null Integrity:** Specifies whether attributes accept NULL values. Example: `Phone_No VARCHAR(15) NULL` is allowed to be missing.

*(7 marks — 1.4 marks each)*

**(b)** Consider an ER model for a Library Management System with entities `BOOK(Book_ID, Title, Author, Price)`, `MEMBER(Member_ID, MName, Address)`, and a M:N relationship `BORROWS` with attribute `Borrow_Date`. Map this to a relational schema. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Map Strong Entities:**

$$
BOOK(\underline{Book\_ID},\; Title,\; Author,\; Price)
$$

$$
MEMBER(\underline{Member\_ID},\; MName,\; Address)
$$

**Step 2 — Map M:N Relationship `BORROWS`:**

Since `BORROWS` is M:N, a new relation is created with the *combined* PK and the descriptive attribute `Borrow_Date`:

$$
BORROWS(\underline{Book\_ID},\; \underline{Member\_ID},\; Borrow\_Date)
$$

$$
\text{FK: } Book\_ID \;\rightarrow\; BOOK(Book\_ID)
$$

$$
\text{FK: } Member\_ID \;\rightarrow\; MEMBER(Member\_ID)
$$

**Final Schema (3 Relations):**

| Relation | Primary Key | Foreign Key(s) |
|---|---|---|
| BOOK | Book_ID | — |
| MEMBER | Member_ID | — |
| BORROWS | (Book_ID, Member_ID) | Book_ID → BOOK, Member_ID → MEMBER |

*(7 marks — 2 marks for entity mapping + 3 marks for M:N mapping + 2 marks for FK declaration)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Relation** = 2D table; **Tuple** = row; **Attribute** = column; **Domain** = allowed values.
- **Degree** = number of attributes; **Cardinality** = number of tuples.
- **Super Key ⊇ Candidate Key ⊇ Primary Key** (set inclusion hierarchy).
- **Alternate Key** = unchosen candidate key; **Unique Key** = allows one NULL.
- **Composite Key** requires 2+ attributes; **Foreign Key** links child → parent.
- **5 Integrity Constraints:** Domain, Entity, Referential, Key, Null.
- **Entity Integrity** says PK ≠ NULL; **Referential Integrity** says FK ⊆ PK(Parent) ∪ {NULL}.
- **7-Step Mapping Algorithm:** Strong entity → Weak entity → Multivalued → 1:1 → 1:N → M:N → N-ary.
- **Multivalued attributes** always become *separate* relations (1NF rule).
- **Composite attributes** are *decomposed* into simple sub-parts in the relation.
- **Derived attributes** are *excluded* from storage and computed dynamically.
- **1:1 relationship:** Place FK on the *total participation* side.
- **1:N relationship:** Place FK of the 1-side on the N-side (most common pattern).
- **M:N relationship:** Always create a *new junction* relation.
- **N-ary relationship:** New relation with combined PK of all participating entities.
- On DELETE/UPDATE of parent PK, choose: **RESTRICT, CASCADE, SET NULL, SET DEFAULT**.
- Always declare **Primary Key** and **Foreign Keys** explicitly — examiners deduct marks otherwise.
- The relational model is **mathematically founded on set theory** (Cartesian product of domains).
- **First Normal Form (1NF)** is satisfied by the atomicity property of relations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Select
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Select

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS)** and the **Structured Query Language (SQL)**, the **`SELECT`** statement is the **Data Query Language (DQL)** command used to retrieve a subset of rows and columns (a *result set*) from one or more tables, views, or materialized views in a relational schema. Formally, the SELECT statement implements the **relational algebra projection (π)** and **selection (σ)** operations, augmented with extended operations such as **aggregation (𝒢)**, **renaming (ρ)**, **sorting**, and **set-theoretic joins**.

According to the **ANSI/ISO SQL:2016** standard (which aligns with KTU 2024 syllabus terminology), a SELECT statement has six logical processing phases executed in a strict sequence by the query optimizer:

$$\text{FROM} \rightarrow \text{WHERE} \rightarrow \text{GROUP BY} \rightarrow \text{HAVING} \rightarrow \text{SELECT} \rightarrow \text{ORDER BY}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2:**
> The `SELECT` statement is the **cornerstone of SQL Data Retrieval**. Mastery of single-table retrieval, multi-table retrieval (JOINs), nested subqueries, and aggregate functions is mandatory for KTU End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **library** with **millions of books (rows)** arranged across **thousands of shelves (tables)**. You walk up to the librarian and say:

> *"I want only the **blue-covered books (filter condition)** published after **2020 (date filter)**, but I only need the **title and author name (column projection)** — and sort them **alphabetically (ORDER BY)**."*

The librarian (i.e., the **Database Engine**) translates your request into the formal `SELECT` statement, scans the indexed shelves (uses B+ Tree indexes), and hands you a neatly printed list (the **result set / cursor**). The `SELECT` statement is precisely this **"request form"** — a declarative contract where *you specify WHAT you want*, not *HOW to fetch it*. The query optimizer decides the HOW.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Constants & Metrics)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Result Set** | The virtual table returned by `SELECT`. It is **not** a stored table unless materialized via `INTO` or `CREATE TABLE AS`. |
| **Projection (π)** | Choosing which **columns** appear in output. |
| **Selection (σ)** | Choosing which **rows** appear based on a predicate. |
| **Predicate** | A Boolean expression evaluating to TRUE, FALSE, or UNKNOWN (3-valued logic). |
| **NULL** | SQL's representation for **missing / unknown / inapplicable** values. |
| **Cardinality** | Number of **rows** in the result set. |
| **Degree / Arity** | Number of **columns** in the result set. |

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Valued Logic (3VL):** SQL uses **TRUE**, **FALSE**, and **UNKNOWN**. A comparison with `NULL` (e.g., `salary = NULL`) returns **UNKNOWN**, not FALSE. Use `IS NULL` / `IS NOT NULL` operators instead.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical `SELECT` Syntax

The full ANSI-grammar for a single-table SELECT statement (as per SQL:2016):

```sql
SELECT [DISTINCT | ALL] select_list
FROM   table_reference
[WHERE  search_condition]
[GROUP  BY grouping_columns]
[HAVING group_condition]
[ORDER  BY sort_columns [ASC | DESC]]
[LIMIT  n] [OFFSET m];
```

### 2.1.1 Clause-by-Clause Decomposition (The "WHY" and "HOW")

- **`SELECT [DISTINCT | ALL] select_list`** → Projects columns. `DISTINCT` eliminates duplicate tuples; `ALL` (default) retains duplicates. Internally implemented as **Hash Aggregate** or **Sort + Unique** operations.

- **`FROM table_reference`** → Identifies the **source relation(s)**. Can be a base table, view, joined tables, or a subquery (derived table). The DBMS builds an initial **Cartesian product** here if multiple tables exist.

- **`WHERE search_condition`** → Applies row-wise filtering (σ-selection). Uses operators: `=`, `<>`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`, `BETWEEN`, `IN`, `LIKE`, `IS NULL`, logical `AND/OR/NOT`. Indexed columns in WHERE enable **B+ Tree / Hash index scans**.

- **`GROUP BY grouping_columns`** → Partitions rows into groups with identical column values. Required before aggregate functions can collapse multiple rows into one per group.

- **`HAVING group_condition`** → Filters **groups** (not rows). Evaluated **after** aggregation, unlike WHERE.

- **`ORDER BY sort_columns [ASC | DESC]`** → Sorts the final result. Default is **ASC**. Uses external merge sort when result exceeds memory.

- **`LIMIT n OFFSET m`** → Returns at most `n` rows starting from the `m`-th row (0-indexed). Implements **pagination**.

---

## 2.2 Aggregate Functions (Built-in Column Reducers)

| Aggregate Function | Returns | NULL Handling |
|---|---|---|
| `COUNT(*)` | Number of rows in group | Counts rows **including** NULLs |
| `COUNT(column)` | Number of non-NULL values in column | **Ignores** NULLs |
| `SUM(column)` | Total of non-NULL numeric values | Ignores NULLs |
| `AVG(column)` | Arithmetic mean = `SUM / COUNT(non-NULL)` | Ignores NULLs |
| `MIN(column)` | Smallest non-NULL value | Ignores NULLs |
| `MAX(column)` | Largest non-NULL value | Ignores NULLs |

$$\text{AVG}(A) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i}{\text{COUNT}(A \neq \text{NULL})}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every** critical SQL SELECT construct required for KTU ESE. Memorize this verbatim.

| # | Construct | Syntax Pattern | Returns / Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Projection | `SELECT col1, col2 FROM T;` | All rows of chosen cols |
| 2 | All Columns | `SELECT * FROM T;` | Full table |
| 3 | Distinct | `SELECT DISTINCT col FROM T;` | Unique values only |
| 4 | Alias | `SELECT col AS "Name" FROM T;` | Renamed output column |
| 5 | Computed Col | `SELECT price*qty AS total FROM T;` | Arithmetic expression |
| 6 | Concatenation | `SELECT first_name \|\| ' ' \|\| last_name FROM T;` | String merge |
| 7 | Range Filter | `WHERE sal BETWEEN 1000 AND 5000` | Inclusive range |
| 8 | Set Filter | `WHERE dept IN ('CS','IT')` | Membership test |
| 9 | Pattern Match | `WHERE name LIKE 'A%'` | `%`=any string, `_`=one char |
| 10 | NULL Test | `WHERE phone IS NULL` | Three-valued logic |
| 11 | Grouping | `GROUP BY dept` | Collapses rows |
| 12 | Group Filter | `HAVING COUNT(*) > 5` | Post-aggregation filter |
| 13 | Sorting | `ORDER BY sal DESC, name ASC` | Final ordering |
| 14 | Pagination | `LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20` | Rows 21–30 |
| 15 | Set Union | `UNION` / `UNION ALL` | Removes / keeps dupes |
| 16 | Set Intersect | `INTERSECT` | Common rows |
| 17 | Set Difference | `EXCEPT` (or `MINUS` in Oracle) | Rows in A not in B |
| 18 | Subquery | `(SELECT ...)` in WHERE/FROM | Nested query |

> **Engineering Utility:** The `SELECT` statement underpins virtually every **OLAP dashboard, business intelligence report, and CRUD backend endpoint** in modern software (banking, e-commerce, healthcare, IoT telemetry pipelines). Mastering it is non-negotiable for any software engineer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> We will use the canonical **university schema** universally adopted in KTU question papers.

## 3.1 Reference Schema (Running Example)

```text
STUDENT(sid INT PK, sname VARCHAR(50), age INT, dept VARCHAR(10), gpa FLOAT)
COURSE(cid INT PK, cname VARCHAR(50), credits INT, dept VARCHAR(10))
ENROLL(sid INT FK, cid INT FK, grade CHAR(2), sem INT)
```

---

## 3.2 Derivative 1 — Query with WHERE, BETWEEN, LIKE, ORDER BY

**Question:** *Find the names and GPAs of all CS-department students whose GPA is between 8.0 and 9.5 (inclusive), whose name starts with 'A', sorted by GPA descending.*

```sql
SELECT sname, gpa
FROM   STUDENT
WHERE  dept = 'CS'
  AND  gpa BETWEEN 8.0 AND 9.5
  AND  sname LIKE 'A%'
ORDER  BY gpa DESC;
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Evaluation

**Step 1 — FROM clause:**
The DBMS accesses the **STUDENT** base table. Let the cardinality be $N$ rows.

$$\text{Intermediate Result}_0 = \sigma_{\text{TRUE}}(\text{STUDENT}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad |R_0| = N$$

**Step 2 — WHERE clause (sequential predicate evaluation):**

Predicate P1: `dept = 'CS'` → reduces cardinality using index on `dept` if available.

$$R_1 = \sigma_{\text{dept} = \text{'CS'}}(R_0) \quad \Rightarrow \quad |R_1| \approx N \cdot p_1$$

where $p_1$ is the selectivity (fraction of matching rows).

Predicate P2: `gpa BETWEEN 8.0 AND 9.5` → range scan on indexed `gpa` column.

$$R_2 = \sigma_{8.0 \le \text{gpa} \le 9.5}(R_1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad |R_2| \approx |R_1| \cdot p_2$$

Predicate P3: `sname LIKE 'A%'` → prefix match; uses B+ Tree on `sname`.

$$R_3 = \sigma_{\text{sname LIKE 'A\%'}}(R_2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad |R_3| \approx |R_2| \cdot p_3$$

**Step 3 — SELECT clause (Projection):**

$$\pi_{\text{sname, gpa}}(R_3)$$

Only two columns are emitted; vertical I/O is reduced.

**Step 4 — ORDER BY clause:**

The optimizer performs an **in-memory or external merge sort** on the projected 2-column result set:

$$R_{\text{final}} = \tau_{\text{gpa DESC}}(\pi_{\text{sname, gpa}}(R_3))$$

---

## 3.3 Derivative 2 — GROUP BY with HAVING (Aggregation Pipeline)

**Question:** *For each department, display the department name and the average GPA of students aged 20 or above, but only show departments where the average GPA exceeds 8.5 and there are at least 3 such students.*

```sql
SELECT   dept, AVG(gpa) AS avg_gpa, COUNT(*) AS num_students
FROM     STUDENT
WHERE    age >= 20
GROUP BY dept
HAVING   AVG(gpa) > 8.5 AND COUNT(*) >= 3
ORDER BY avg_gpa DESC;
```

### Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply WHERE (pre-aggregation filter):**

$$R_1 = \sigma_{\text{age} \ge 20}(\text{STUDENT})$$

**Step 2 — Partition by `dept` (GROUP BY):**

$$\mathcal{G}_{\text{dept}}(R_1) = \bigcup_{d \in D} \text{Group}_d$$

where each group is:

$$\text{Group}_d = \{ t \in R_1 \mid t.\text{dept} = d \}$$

**Step 3 — Apply aggregate functions per group:**

For each group $d$, compute:

$$\text{avg\_gpa}_d = \frac{\sum_{t \in \text{Group}_d} t.\text{gpa}}{|\text{Group}_d|}$$

$$\text{num\_students}_d = \vert \text{Group}_d \vert$$

**Step 4 — Apply HAVING (post-aggregation filter):**

$$R_{\text{filtered}} = \{ d \in D \mid \text{avg\_gpa}_d > 8.5 \ \land \ \text{num\_students}_d \ge 3 \}$$

**Step 5 — Sort output:**

$$\text{Final Result} = \tau_{\text{avg\_gpa DESC}}(R_{\text{filtered}})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** Columns in the `SELECT` list that are **not** inside an aggregate function **must** appear in the `GROUP BY` clause. Otherwise, the query is non-deterministic and violates SQL standard.

---

## 3.4 Derivative 3 — Subquery with `IN`, `EXISTS`, and Scalar Subquery

**Question:** *Find names of students who have enrolled in at least one course offered by the 'CS' department.*

```sql
-- Approach 1: IN with subquery
SELECT sname
FROM   STUDENT
WHERE  sid IN (
    SELECT E.sid
    FROM   ENROLL E JOIN COURSE C ON E.cid = C.cid
    WHERE  C.dept = 'CS'
);

-- Approach 2: EXISTS with correlated subquery
SELECT sname
FROM   STUDENT S
WHERE  EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM   ENROLL E JOIN COURSE C ON E.cid = C.cid
    WHERE  E.sid = S.sid AND C.dept = 'CS'
);
```

### Logical Equivalence

$$\{ s \in \text{STUDENT} \mid \exists e \in \text{ENROLL}: e.\text{sid} = s.\text{sid} \land \exists c \in \text{COURSE}: c.\text{cid} = e.\text{cid} \land c.\text{dept} = \text{'CS'} \}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — WHERE vs HAVING (THE Most Tested Distinction)

| Criterion | WHERE | HAVING |
|---|---|---|
| Filters | **Individual rows** | **Groups** (after aggregation) |
| Position | Before GROUP BY | After GROUP BY |
| Aggregate Allowed? | ❌ **NO** (except nested subquery) | ✅ **YES** |
| Index Usable? | ✅ Yes (single-row predicates) | ❌ Generally no (full group scan) |
| Example | `WHERE age >= 20` | `HAVING AVG(gpa) > 8.5` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logical Processing Order of a SELECT Statement

> [!NOTE]
> The **written order** of SQL clauses differs from the **logical execution order**. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Query Received] --> B[1. FROM Clause]
    B --> C[Build Cartesian Product of Source Tables]
    C --> D[2. ON / USING - Apply Join Predicates]
    D --> E[3. WHERE Clause]
    E --> F[Filter Individual Rows]
    F --> G[4. GROUP BY Clause]
    G --> H[Partition Rows into Groups]
    H --> I[5. HAVING Clause]
    I --> J[Filter Groups via Aggregates]
    J --> K[6. SELECT Clause]
    K --> L[Project Columns and Compute Expressions]
    L --> M[7. DISTINCT - Remove Duplicate Tuples]
    M --> N[8. ORDER BY Clause]
    N --> O[Sort Final Result Set ASC or DESC]
    O --> P[9. LIMIT and OFFSET]
    P --> Q[Paginate Final Output]
    Q --> R[End: Return Result Set to Client]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
