```mermaid
    style G fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style N fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: SELECT Execution Pipeline in a DBMS Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER["User Layer"]
        U1[SQL Text: SELECT Statement]
    end

    subgraph PARSER["Parser Layer"]
        P1[Lexical Analyzer]
        P2[Syntax Analyzer]
        P3[Semantic Analyzer]
        P4[Parse Tree]
    end

    subgraph OPTIMIZER["Query Optimizer Layer"]
        Q1[Query Rewriter]
        Q2[Cost-Based Optimizer]
        Q3[Execution Plan]
    end

    subgraph EXECUTOR["Execution Engine Layer"]
        E1[Plan Operator: TableScan]
        E2[Plan Operator: IndexScan]
        E3[Plan Operator: NestedLoopJoin]
        E4[Plan Operator: HashAgg]
        E5[Plan Operator: Sort]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE["Storage Layer"]
        S1[Buffer Pool]
        S2[Disk: Heap Files]
        S3[Disk: B+ Tree Indexes]
    end

    U1 --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> E1
    Q3 --> E2
    Q3 --> E3
    Q3 --> E4
    Q3 --> E5
    E1 --> S1
    E2 --> S3
    E3 --> S1
    E4 --> S1
    E5 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S1
    S3 --> S2
    S1 --> OUT[Result Set Returned to Client]

    style U1 fill:#17a2b8,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style OUT fill:#28a745,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style PARSER fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#000
    style OPTIMIZER fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#000
    style EXECUTOR fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#000
    style STORAGE fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Concept Map: SELECT Statement Components

```mermaid
mindmap

    Clauses
      FROM
      WHERE
      GROUP BY
      HAVING
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between the WHERE clause and the HAVING clause in SQL with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| WHERE | HAVING |
|---|---|
| Filters **individual rows** before grouping. | Filters **groups** after aggregation. |
| Cannot contain aggregate functions directly. | Can contain aggregate functions (e.g., `AVG`, `COUNT`). |
| Executed **before** GROUP BY. | Executed **after** GROUP BY. |
| Example: `WHERE age > 20` | Example: `HAVING AVG(gpa) > 8.5` |

**Example query:**

```sql
SELECT dept, AVG(gpa)
FROM   STUDENT
WHERE  age >= 20          -- row-level filter
GROUP  BY dept
HAVING AVG(gpa) > 8.5;    -- group-level filter
```

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the difference between `UNION` and `UNION ALL`? Write one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`UNION`** combines the result sets of two or more `SELECT` statements and **removes duplicate rows** (performs implicit `DISTINCT`). It is therefore **slower** (requires a sort/hash to dedup).
- **`UNION ALL`** simply **concatenates** all rows including duplicates — **faster**.

**Example:**

```sql
SELECT sid FROM STUDENT WHERE dept = 'CS'
UNION
SELECT sid FROM STUDENT WHERE gpa > 9.0;
```

> Both `SELECT` statements must have the **same number of columns** and **compatible data types** in corresponding positions.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Pattern: Each module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Consider the following schema:**
- `STUDENT(roll_no PK, name, age, dept, gpa)`
- `COURSE(course_id PK, course_name, credits, dept)`
- `ENROLL(roll_no FK, course_id FK, grade, semester)`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write SQL queries for the following:
1. Find the names of all students in the 'CS' department who have a GPA greater than 8.5.
2. Find the distinct list of course names taken by students named 'Rahul'.
3. For each department, display the department name and the number of students. Sort by student count in descending order.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write SQL queries for the following using **subqueries and GROUP BY + HAVING**:
4. Find the names of students who have enrolled in **more than 3 courses**.
5. Find the course names where the **average grade points** of enrolled students is greater than 8.0 (assume grade mapping: A=10, B=8, C=6, D=4).
6. Find the **top 5 students** by GPA across all departments.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-A

**(a.1) CS dept students with GPA > 8.5**

```sql
SELECT name
FROM   STUDENT
WHERE  dept = 'CS' AND gpa > 8.5;
```

**(a.2) Distinct course names taken by 'Rahul'**

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT C.course_name
FROM   COURSE C
       JOIN ENROLL E ON C.course_id = E.course_id
       JOIN STUDENT S ON E.roll_no  = S.roll_no
WHERE  S.name = 'Rahul';
```

**(a.3) Department-wise student count, sorted DESC**

```sql
SELECT   dept, COUNT(*) AS num_students
FROM     STUDENT
GROUP BY dept
ORDER BY num_students DESC;
```

**(b.4) Students enrolled in more than 3 courses**

```sql
SELECT name
FROM   STUDENT
WHERE  roll_no IN (
    SELECT roll_no
    FROM   ENROLL
    GROUP BY roll_no
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 3
);
```

**(b.5) Courses where avg grade > 8.0** (using `CASE` for grade mapping)

```sql
SELECT C.course_name
FROM   COURSE C
       JOIN ENROLL E ON C.course_id = E.course_id
GROUP BY C.course_name
HAVING AVG(CASE E.grade
              WHEN 'A' THEN 10
              WHEN 'B' THEN 8
              WHEN 'C' THEN 6
              WHEN 'D' THEN 4
              ELSE 0
           END) > 8.0;
```

**(b.6) Top 5 students by GPA**

```sql
SELECT name, gpa
FROM   STUDENT
ORDER BY gpa DESC
LIMIT  5;
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider schema:
- `EMPLOYEE(eid PK, ename, salary, dept_id, manager_id)`
- `DEPARTMENT(dept_id PK, dname, location)`

Write queries to:
1. Display the employee names and their department names (use JOIN).
2. Find employees who earn more than the **average salary** of their department.
3. Find the **second-highest salary** in the company (without using LIMIT).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write queries involving **set operations and nested subqueries**:
4. List employee names who work in either 'HR' or 'Finance' department.
5. Find departments that have **no employees** assigned.
6. Find employees whose salary is greater than **every** employee in the 'IT' department.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-B

**(b.1) Employee names with department names**

```sql
SELECT E.ename, D.dname
FROM   EMPLOYEE E
       JOIN DEPARTMENT D ON E.dept_id = D.dept_id;
```

**(b.2) Employees earning more than their dept's average**

```sql
SELECT ename, salary
FROM   EMPLOYEE E1
WHERE  salary > (
    SELECT AVG(salary)
    FROM   EMPLOYEE E2
    WHERE  E2.dept_id = E1.dept_id
);
```

**(b.3) Second-highest salary (without LIMIT)**

```sql
SELECT MAX(salary) AS second_highest
FROM   EMPLOYEE
WHERE  salary < (
    SELECT MAX(salary) FROM EMPLOYEE
);
```

Algebraically:

$$\text{2nd Highest} = \max\{s \in \text{Salary} \mid s < \max(\text{Salary})\}$$

**(b.4) Employees in HR or Finance**

```sql
SELECT ename
FROM   EMPLOYEE E JOIN DEPARTMENT D ON E.dept_id = D.dept_id
WHERE  D.dname IN ('HR', 'Finance');
```

**(b.5) Departments with no employees**

```sql
SELECT dname
FROM   DEPARTMENT D
WHERE  NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM EMPLOYEE E WHERE E.dept_id = D.dept_id
);
```

**(b.6) Salary greater than ALL IT-department salaries**

```sql
SELECT ename, salary
FROM   EMPLOYEE
WHERE  salary > ALL (
    SELECT salary
    FROM   EMPLOYEE E JOIN DEPARTMENT D ON E.dept_id = D.dept_id
    WHERE  D.dname = 'IT'
);
```

Algebraically:

$$\{ e \in \text{EMP} \mid \forall i \in \text{IT\_EMP}: e.\text{salary} > i.\text{salary} \}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `GROUP BY`** when mixing aggregates with non-aggregated columns → **2 marks deducted**.
> 2. **Using `WHERE` instead of `HAVING`** for aggregate filters (or vice versa) → **1–2 marks deducted**.
> 3. **Writing `= NULL`** instead of **`IS NULL`** → query returns 0 rows; **3 marks deducted** as it is a logical error.
> 4. **Using `LIMIT` in Oracle-style questions** where `ROWNUM` is expected → check the DBMS specified in question.
> 5. **Subquery returning multiple rows** used with `=` instead of `IN` → runtime error; **2 marks deducted**.
> 6. **Forgetting `DISTINCT`** when question explicitly asks for *distinct* values → **1 mark deducted**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ `SELECT` is a **DQL (Data Query Language)** command — it is **non-destructive**; data is never modified.
- ✅ The **logical execution order** is `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT`, **not** the textual order.
- ✅ **`WHERE` filters rows; `HAVING` filters groups.** This is the #1 tested distinction.
- ✅ **NULL** requires `IS NULL` / `IS NOT NULL`. Comparisons with `NULL` return `UNKNOWN`.
- ✅ `COUNT(*)` counts rows including NULLs; `COUNT(col)` ignores NULLs.
- ✅ `DISTINCT` applies to the **entire row tuple**, not individual columns.
- ✅ **Subqueries** can appear in `WHERE`, `FROM` (as derived tables), and `SELECT` (as scalar subqueries).
- ✅ **`EXISTS`** is generally faster than `IN` for correlated subqueries on large datasets.
- ✅ **Set operations** (`UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT`) require column-compatible result sets.
- ✅ The `SELECT` statement is **declarative** — you specify *what*, not *how*; the optimizer decides access paths (index scan, full scan, hash join, etc.).
- ✅ Master the **reference schema** and at least 5 standard query patterns: projection, selection, join, aggregate, nested subquery.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Project

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In Relational Algebra, the **PROJECT** operation (denoted by the Greek capital letter **π**, pronounced "pi") is a **unary** operation that takes a single relation $R$ as input and produces a new relation containing **only a specified subset of attributes (columns)** of $R$, with all resulting duplicate tuples automatically eliminated.

Formally, for a relation schema $R(A_1, A_2, A_3, \ldots, A_n)$ and a chosen attribute subset $\{A_{i_1}, A_{i_2}, \ldots, A_{i_k}\}$ where $k \le n$:

$$\pi_{A_{i_1}, A_{i_2}, \ldots, A_{i_k}}(R)$$

The resulting relation is a **vertical subset** (a slice through the columns) of the original relation. The degree (arity) of the output relation equals $k$, the number of projected attributes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PROJECT operation is one of the **five fundamental relational algebra operations** (alongside SELECT $\sigma$, UNION $\cup$, SET DIFFERENCE $-$, CARTESIAN PRODUCT $\times$, and RENAME $\rho$). Mastery of π is mandatory for both KTU ESE and continuous assessment evaluations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **large spreadsheet of every student in your college** containing 15 columns: Roll Number, Name, Date of Birth, Address, Phone, Email, Department, Semester, GPA, Attendance, Blood Group, Guardian Name, Aadhaar, Bank Account, and Fee Status.

Now suppose the placement cell asks you: *"Give us a list containing ONLY the Name, Department, and GPA of all students."*

What you actually do is:

1. **Slide a vertical shutter** that hides every column except Name, Department, and GPA.
2. **Erase any duplicate rows** (because if two students have the exact same Name + Department + GPA, the placement cell doesn't want them listed twice).

That is **exactly what PROJECT does** — it is like a "vertical projector" (hence the symbol π, resembling a projector's beam) that displays only the columns you select and collapses duplicates.

| Real-World Action | Mathematical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hiding unwanted columns | Selecting an attribute subset |
| Erasing duplicate rows | Set-based duplicate elimination |
| Reordering the columns | Permutation of projected attributes |
| Renaming a column header | Combined use of $\pi$ with $\rho$ |

---

## 1.3 Physical Properties & Standard Metrics

- **Symbol:** $\pi$ (Greek capital pi)
- **Type:** **Unary** operation (operates on exactly one relation)
- **Arity:** $k$ (number of attributes chosen)
- **Cardinality:** $\vert \pi_{A_1,\ldots,A_k}(R) \vert \;\le\; \vert R \vert$ where $\vert R \vert$ denotes the original tuple count. The inequality becomes strict when duplicates exist in the projected column combination.
- **Commutativity (Partial):** $\pi_{A_1}(\pi_{A_1, A_2}(R)) \;=\; \pi_{A_1}(R)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Idempotency Property:** Unlike SELECT, PROJECT is *idempotent* only in the outer-to-inner direction. Nested projections collapse to the outermost projection: $\pi_{A_1}(\pi_{A_1,A_2}(\pi_{A_1,A_2,A_3}(R))) = \pi_{A_1}(R)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The PROJECT operation executes in **three deterministic phases**:

1. **Phase 1 — Column Selection (Vertical Cut):**
   For each tuple $t \in R$, a new tuple $t'$ is constructed such that:
   
   $$t'[A_{i_j}] \;=\; t[A_{i_j}] \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, k$$

2. **Phase 2 — Duplicate Elimination (Set Semantics):**
   All tuples that have identical values across every retained attribute are collapsed into a single representative tuple. The result is therefore a **mathematical set**, not a multiset (bag).
   
   $$\pi_{\vec{A}}(R) \;=\; \{\, t' \mid t \in R,\; t' = t[\vec{A}] \,\}$$

3. **Phase 3 — Optional Column Renaming (via $\rho$):**
   If the user wishes to relabel any projected column, a RENAME operation may be composed:
   
   $$\rho_{S(\,B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_k\,)}\!\bigl(\,\pi_{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_k}(R)\,\bigr)$$

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Phase

| Phase | Why it Exists | How the DBMS Executes It |
|---|---|---|
| Column Selection | The user is rarely interested in *all* attributes — projection minimizes I/O. | A scan over each tuple extracts only the requested attribute offsets. |
| Duplicate Elimination | Relational algebra requires the result to be a valid relation (no duplicate tuples). | Either **sort-based** (sort then adjacent-compare) or **hash-based** (partition then compare) elimination. |
| Renaming | Enables using the same relation twice in queries (e.g., self-joins) and customizing output headers. | Metadata-level attribute substitution before output. |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU ESE — Memorize the Properties Below.**

| # | Property Name | Mathematical Statement | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Notation** | $\pi_{A_1,\ldots,A_k}(R)$ | Selects columns $A_1$ through $A_k$ from $R$ |
| 2 | **Cardinality Bound** | $0 \le \vert \pi_{\vec{A}}(R) \vert \le \vert R \vert$ | Used in cost estimation and query optimization |
| 3 | **Idempotence (Outer)** | $\pi_{\vec{A}}(\pi_{\vec{A},\vec{B}}(R)) = \pi_{\vec{A}}(R)$ | Query optimizer rule — eliminate redundant projections |
| 4 | **Empty Projection** | $\pi_{\emptyset}(R)$ is **undefined** in standard algebra | Avoid using empty attribute lists |
| 5 | **Full Projection** | $\pi_{\text{all}}(R) = R$ after duplicate removal | Identity-like behavior |
| 6 | **Distribution over Union** | $\pi_{\vec{A}}(R \cup S) = \pi_{\vec{A}}(R) \cup \pi_{\vec{A}}(S)$ | Used in distributed query processing |
| 7 | **Distribution over Set Difference** | $\pi_{\vec{A}}(R - S) \subseteq \pi_{\vec{A}}(R) - \pi_{\vec{A}}(S)$ | **Subset relation, NOT equality** — common exam trap |
| 8 | **Composition with Select** | $\pi_{\vec{A}}(\sigma_{\theta}(R)) = \sigma_{\theta}(\pi_{\vec{A}}(R))$ **only if** $\theta$ uses only $\vec{A}$ | **Push-down rule** — projection before selection saves I/O |
| 9 | **Arithmetic Expression** | $\pi_{A, \;B*1.1 \;\text{as} \; B_{\text{adj}}}(R)$ | Allows per-row computation (extended projection) |
| 10 | **Composed with Rename** | $\rho_{X(\vec{C})}(\pi_{\vec{A}}(R))$ | Decouples input and output schemas |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

In real-world Database Management Systems such as **PostgreSQL, Oracle, MySQL, and SQL Server**, the PROJECT operation is the foundational equivalent of the SQL `SELECT` clause (with the `DISTINCT` keyword implied in pure relational algebra, since the result must be a set). Every time a front-end dashboard fetches *"only the columns the user has permission to view"*, it is invoking the projection principle.

- **Data Privacy:** Column-level access control uses PROJECT to expose only authorized attributes.
- **Bandwidth Optimization:** In distributed systems, PROJECT reduces the volume of data shipped across the network.
- **Index-Only Scans:** A covering index allows the DBMS to answer a query using *only* the projected columns without touching the base table.
- **Columnar Databases** (e.g., Apache Parquet, Amazon Redshift): Designed around PROJECT — they store columns separately so projection is blazingly fast.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Canonical Example Schema

Throughout this section, we will use the following sample relation **STUDENT** which is consistent with KTU paper conventions:

**STUDENT Relation Schema:**

$$STUDENT(\underline{SID},\; Name,\; Dept,\; Age,\; GPA)$$

**Sample Instance (R):**

| SID | Name | Dept | Age | GPA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S01 | Arun | CSE | 20 | 9.2 |
| S02 | Bhavya | ECE | 21 | 8.5 |
| S03 | Chandan | CSE | 22 | 9.2 |
| S04 | Divya | MECH | 20 | 7.8 |
| S05 | Esha | CSE | 21 | 9.2 |
| S06 | Farhan | ECE | 22 | 8.5 |

The cardinality of this instance is $\vert R \vert = 6$ tuples.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Single Attribute Projection

**Query:** *"Display the department of every student."*

### Step 1: Identify the Attribute Subset

The single attribute requested is **Dept**. Therefore $\vec{A} = \{\text{Dept}\}$ and $k = 1$.

### Step 2: Formally Express the Operation

$$\pi_{\text{Dept}}(STUDENT)$$

### Step 3: Compute the Result via Vertical Cut

We slice the **Dept** column from each tuple:

$$\{ \text{CSE},\; \text{ECE},\; \text{CSE},\; \text{MECH},\; \text{CSE},\; \text{ECE} \}$$

### Step 4: Eliminate Duplicates (Set Semantics)

$$\{ \text{CSE},\; \text{ECE},\; \text{MECH} \}$$

### Step 5: Present the Final Relation

| Dept |
|---|
| CSE |
| ECE |
| MECH |

The cardinality is $\vert \pi_{\text{Dept}}(STUDENT) \vert = 3$, which satisfies the bound $3 \le 6$. ✔

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Multi-Attribute Projection

**Query:** *"Display each student's name and GPA."*

### Step 1: Formally Express the Operation

$$\pi_{\text{Name, GPA}}(STUDENT)$$

### Step 2: Vertical Cut

$$\{ (Arun,\, 9.2),\; (Bhavya,\, 8.5),\; (Chandan,\, 9.2),\; (Divya,\, 7.8),\; (Esha,\, 9.2),\; (Farhan,\, 8.5) \}$$

### Step 3: Eliminate Duplicates

All six pairs are distinct in this instance.

### Step 4: Final Output

| Name | GPA |
|---|---|
| Arun | 9.2 |
| Bhavya | 8.5 |
| Chandan | 9.2 |
| Divya | 7.8 |
| Esha | 9.2 |
| Farhan | 8.5 |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Projection with Duplicate Collapse (High-Yield KTU Scenario)

**Query:** *"Find all unique (Dept, GPA) pairs across the student body."*

### Step 1: Formally Express

$$\pi_{\text{Dept, GPA}}(STUDENT)$$

### Step 2: Vertical Cut (Raw Tuples)

$$\{ (\text{CSE}, 9.2),\; (\text{ECE}, 8.5),\; (\text{CSE}, 9.2),\; (\text{MECH}, 7.8),\; (\text{CSE}, 9.2),\; (\text{ECE}, 8.5) \}$$

### Step 3: Eliminate Duplicates

Notice that $(CSE, 9.2)$ appears three times and $(ECE, 8.5)$ appears twice. After deduplication:

$$\{ (\text{CSE}, 9.2),\; (\text{ECE}, 8.5),\; (\text{MECH}, 7.8) \}$$

### Step 4: Final Output

| Dept | GPA |
|---|---|
| CSE | 9.2 |
| ECE | 8.5 |
| MECH | 7.8 |

**Cardinality observation:** $\vert \pi_{\text{Dept, GPA}}(STUDENT) \vert = 3 \le 6 = \vert STUDENT \vert$. The inequality is strict because duplicate (Dept, GPA) pairs existed. ✔

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Extended Projection with Arithmetic Expression

In the *extended* form of PROJECT (used in modern DBMSs), arithmetic computations are allowed within the projection list.

**Query:** *"Display each student's name, current GPA, and a 'boosted GPA' which is 10% higher."*

### Step 1: Formally Express

$$\pi_{\text{Name},\; \text{GPA},\; \text{GPA} \times 1.1 \; \text{as} \; \text{GPA}_{\text{adj}}}(STUDENT)$$

### Step 2: Per-Tuple Computation

For the first tuple $(Arun,\, 9.2)$:
$$\text{GPA}_{\text{adj}} = 9.2 \times 1.1 = 10.12$$

For the second tuple $(Bhavya,\, 8.5)$:
$$\text{GPA}_{\text{adj}} = 8.5 \times 1.1 = 9.35$$

### Step 3: Final Output

| Name | GPA | GPA_adj |
|---|---|---|
| Arun | 9.2 | 10.12 |
| Bhavya | 8.5 | 9.35 |
| Chandan | 9.2 | 10.12 |
| Divya | 7.8 | 8.58 |
| Esha | 9.2 | 10.12 |
| Farhan | 8.5 | 9.35 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In standard relational algebra, the result relation must still be a valid set. If two tuples after the arithmetic operation happen to be identical, duplicates must still be removed. In the example above, since Name differs, all six tuples remain.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Composition of SELECT and PROJECT (Push-Down Demonstration)

**Query:** *"List the names of CSE students with GPA greater than 9.0."*

### Step 1: Formally Express (Approach A — Select First)

$$\pi_{\text{Name}}\!\Bigl(\sigma_{(\text{Dept}=\text{'CSE'}) \,\land\, (\text{GPA} > 9.0)}(STUDENT)\Bigr)$$

### Step 2: Apply SELECT First

The SELECT first yields tuples satisfying both conditions:

| SID | Name | Dept | Age | GPA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S01 | Arun | CSE | 20 | 9.2 |
| S03 | Chandan | CSE | 22 | 9.2 |
| S05 | Esha | CSE | 21 | 9.2 |

### Step 3: Apply PROJECT on the Reduced Relation

$$\pi_{\text{Name}}(\text{above})$$

$$\{ (\text{Arun}),\; (\text{Chandan}),\; (\text{Esha}) \}$$

### Step 4: Equivalent Approach B — Project First, Then Select

$$\sigma_{(\text{Dept}=\text{'CSE'}) \,\land\, (\text{GPA} > 9.0)}\!\Bigl(\pi_{\text{Name, Dept, GPA}}(STUDENT)\Bigr)$$

Both yield identical results, confirming the **commutativity under the constraint** that the SELECT condition $\theta$ uses only attributes retained by the PROJECT.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Summary Table

| Example | Projection List | Input Tuples | Output Tuples | Duplicate Removal? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\{Dept\}$ | 6 | 3 | Yes |
| 2 | $\{Name, GPA\}$ | 6 | 6 | No duplicates |
| 3 | $\{Dept, GPA\}$ | 6 | 3 | Yes (intensive) |
| 4 | $\{Name, GPA, GPA \times 1.1\}$ | 6 | 6 | No (Name differs) |
| 5 | $\{Name\}$ after $\sigma$ | 3 | 3 | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the PROJECT Operation

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **internal pipeline** of how a Database Engine executes a PROJECT operation. Every stage is annotated with its input, transformation, and output schema.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Relation R with schema A1, A2, ..., An] --> B[Attribute Selector]
    B -->|Extracts requested columns Ai1 through Aik| C[Tuple Stream t1, t2, ..., tm]
    C --> D{Duplicate Detection Enabled?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Hash-Based Dedup or Sort-Merge Dedup]
    D -->|No| F[Bag Semantics Pass-Through]
    E --> G[Result Set pi of k columns]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Column Rename Required?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Rename Module rho applied]
    H -->|No| J[Final Output Relation]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Consumer: SELECT SQL / Query Optimizer / Front-End]
```

**How to read this diagram:**

1. **Input Relation R** enters the pipeline.
2. **Attribute Selector** performs the vertical cut, retaining only the columns specified in the projection list.
3. The resulting **Tuple Stream** is forwarded to a decision branch.
4. If duplicate elimination is enabled (which is **always true** in pure relational algebra), the tuples flow through a **deduplication engine** — either hash-based or sort-merge based.
5. The optional **rename stage** allows renaming of attributes before the final output is produced.
6. The **final output relation** is delivered to the consumer.

---

## 4.2 Schema-Level View of the Vertical Cut

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STUDENT
    S1[SID]
    S2[Name]
    S3[Dept]
    S4[Age]
    S5[GPA]
    end
    subgraph pi_Dept_Name
    P1[Dept]
    P2[Name]
    end
    S3 --> P1
    S2 --> P2
```

This schematic shows that the attributes **Dept** and **Name** are pulled out of the STUDENT relation, while SID, Age, and GPA are discarded by the projection.

---

## 4.3 Comparison Block: PROJECT vs SELECT

| Dimension | PROJECT (π) | SELECT (σ) |
|---|---|---|
| Symbol | Greek capital pi | Greek lowercase sigma |
| Operates On | Columns (attributes) | Rows (tuples) |
| Arity of Operator | Unary | Unary |
| Reduces | Degree (number of columns) | Cardinality (number of rows) |
| Removes Duplicates | **Yes (always)** | No |
| Composed With | Often followed by $\sigma$ | Often followed by $\pi$ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define the PROJECT operation in relational algebra. Write its formal notation and explain the significance of duplicate elimination with an example.

**Model Answer:**

The **PROJECT** operation, denoted by the Greek symbol **π**, is a unary relational algebra operation that selects a subset of attributes (columns) from a relation. Its formal notation is:

$$\pi_{A_{i_1}, A_{i_2}, \ldots, A_{i_k}}(R)$$

where $R$ is a relation and $A_{i_1}, A_{i_2}, \ldots, A_{i_k}$ is a subset of attributes of $R$.

**Significance of Duplicate Elimination:**

After selecting the requested columns, the operation automatically **removes duplicate tuples** so that the result satisfies the set-based definition of a relation (no two tuples can be identical). For example, given a relation STUDENT with attributes $\{Name, Dept\}$, the expression $\pi_{\text{Dept}}(STUDENT)$ returns only the *distinct* department names. If three students belong to 'CSE', 'CSE' appears only **once** in the output, not three times.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Students often forget to mention duplicate elimination — full 3 marks require defining π, providing notation, *and* explaining duplicate semantics.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Differentiate between PROJECT (π) and SELECT (σ) operations in relational algebra.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PROJECT (π) | SELECT (σ) |
|---|---|---|
| Operation Type | Unary | Unary |
| Greek Symbol | Capital pi (π) | Lowercase sigma (σ) |
| Acts On | Columns (attributes) | Rows (tuples) |
| Notation | $\pi_{A_1, A_2}(R)$ | $\sigma_{\text{condition}}(R)$ |
| Effect on Degree | Reduces the degree (number of attributes) | Degree remains the same |
| Effect on Cardinality | May reduce cardinality via duplicate removal | May reduce cardinality via filtering |
| Removes Duplicates? | **Yes, always** | No |
| Example | $\pi_{\text{Name, GPA}}(STUDENT)$ | $\sigma_{\text{GPA} > 8.0}(STUDENT)$ |

The PROJECT operation is a **vertical** operation (works on columns), while SELECT is a **horizontal** operation (works on rows).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the PROJECT operation in relational algebra with suitable notation and example. Discuss its properties. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following relations:

$$EMPLOYEE(\underline{\text{EmpID}},\; \text{Name},\; \text{DeptID},\; \text{Salary},\; \text{JoinDate})$$

$$DEPARTMENT(\underline{\text{DeptID}},\; \text{DeptName},\; \text{Location})$$

Write relational algebra expressions using PROJECT (and other necessary operations) for the following queries: **(7 Marks)**

1. Display the names and salaries of all employees.
2. Display the distinct department locations.
3. Display the names of employees along with their department names (use cartesian product and selection, no join symbol).

---

### Model Answer for Question A

**(a) Explanation (7 Marks):**

The **PROJECT** operation, denoted by π, is a unary operation in relational algebra used to extract specific columns from a relation.

**Notation:**

$$\pi_{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_k}(R)$$

where $R$ is the input relation and $\{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_k\}$ is the chosen attribute subset.

**Example:** Given STUDENT(SID, Name, Dept, GPA), the expression $\pi_{\text{Name, Dept}}(STUDENT)$ returns a relation with two columns containing all distinct (Name, Dept) combinations.

**Properties of PROJECT:**

1. **Unary Operation:** Operates on a single relation.
2. **Duplicate Elimination:** The output is always a set (no duplicates).
3. **Idempotence (Outer-to-Inner):** $\pi_{\vec{A}}(\pi_{\vec{A},\vec{B}}(R)) = \pi_{\vec{A}}(R)$
4. **Cardinality Bound:** $\vert \pi_{\vec{A}}(R) \vert \le \vert R \vert$
5. **Distribution over Union:** $\pi_{\vec{A}}(R \cup S) = \pi_{\vec{A}}(R) \cup \pi_{\vec{A}}(S)$
6. **Commutativity with SELECT** (conditional): When the selection predicate involves only the projected attributes, the two operations can be reordered for optimization.
7. **Arity Reduction:** The degree of the result equals the number of projected attributes.

**(b) Relational Algebra Queries (7 Marks — 2.33 marks each):**

**Query 1: Names and salaries of all employees.**

$$\pi_{\text{Name, Salary}}(EMPLOYEE)$$

**Query 2: Distinct department locations.**

$$\pi_{\text{Location}}(DEPARTMENT)$$

**Query 3: Names of employees along with their department names (using cartesian product).**

$$\pi_{\text{Name, DeptName}}\!\Bigl(\sigma_{\text{EMPLOYEE.DeptID} = \text{DEPARTMENT.DeptID}}(EMPLOYEE \times DEPARTMENT)\Bigr)$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define PROJECT and SELECT operations. Given the relation:

$$BOOK(\underline{\text{ISBN}},\; \text{Title},\; \text{Author},\; \text{Publisher},\; \text{Price},\; \text{Year})$$

Write relational algebra expressions for: **(7 Marks)**

1. Display all titles published after 2020.
2. Display distinct authors and publishers.
3. Display titles and prices of books costing more than ₹500.

**(b)** Explain the significance of duplicate elimination in the PROJECT operation. State and prove (with a counter-example if needed) the property that PROJECT distributes over UNION. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question B

**(a) Definitions and Queries (7 Marks):**

- **PROJECT (π):** A unary operation that selects specified columns from a relation and removes duplicate tuples.
- **SELECT (σ):** A unary operation that selects tuples (rows) satisfying a given predicate, without affecting the schema degree.

**Query 1: All titles published after 2020.**

$$\pi_{\text{Title}}\!\Bigl(\sigma_{\text{Year} > 2020}(BOOK)\Bigr)$$

**Query 2: Distinct authors and publishers.**

$$\pi_{\text{Author, Publisher}}(BOOK)$$

**Query 3: Titles and prices of books costing more than ₹500.**

$$\pi_{\text{Title, Price}}\!\Bigl(\sigma_{\text{Price} > 500}(BOOK)\Bigr)$$

**(b) Duplicate Elimination and Distribution over UNION (7 Marks):**

**Significance of Duplicate Elimination:**

The PROJECT operation must produce a valid relation, which by definition is a **set of tuples** (no duplicates allowed). After performing the vertical cut, the intermediate result may contain duplicates. These must be collapsed so that the output is mathematically well-defined and adheres to first-order relational semantics. Without duplicate elimination, the output would be a **multiset (bag)**, which is not a true relation.

**Property — Distribution over UNION:**

$$\pi_{\vec{A}}(R \cup S) = \pi_{\vec{A}}(R) \cup \pi_{\vec{A}}(S)$$

**Proof Sketch:**

Let $t$ be an arbitrary tuple in $\pi_{\vec{A}}(R \cup S)$. Then $t$ is a projection of some tuple in $R \cup S$, meaning $t \in \pi_{\vec{A}}(R)$ or $t \in \pi_{\vec{A}}(S)$. Hence $t \in \pi_{\vec{A}}(R) \cup \pi_{\vec{A}}(S)$. The reverse inclusion follows symmetrically.

**Counter-example for Set Difference:** Unlike UNION, PROJECT does **not** distribute over set difference:

$$\pi_{\vec{A}}(R - S) \;\ne\; \pi_{\vec{A}}(R) - \pi_{\vec{A}}(S)$$

For instance, consider $R = \{(a, 1), (a, 2)\}$ and $S = \{(b, 3)\}$. Then $R - S = R$, so $\pi_A(R-S) = \{a\}$. But $\pi_A(R) - \pi_A(S) = \{a\} - \emptyset = \{a\}$. They match here. A true counterexample: $R = \{(1, a), (2, a)\}$, $S = \{(1, b)\}$. Then $\pi_{A_2}(R - S) = \{a\}$, but $\pi_{A_2}(R) - \pi_{A_2}(S) = \{a\} - \{b\} = \{a\}$. Still matches. A sharper case: when an element exists in both $R$ and $S$ via different other-attribute values — its projection may survive in the left side but vanish on the right.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Duplicate Elimination:** Students write `π Dept (STUDENT)` and list *all six* departments including duplicates. **Loss: up to 1.5 marks per instance.**
> 2. **Confusing π with σ:** Writing `π_GPA > 8.0(STUDENT)` instead of `σ_GPA > 8.0(STUDENT)`. PROJECT works on **columns**, SELECT on **rows**. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 3. **Incorrect Notation for Multi-Attribute Lists:** Using commas inside the projection attribute list is correct (`π_{A, B}`), but the comma must be a *comma*, not a logical AND.
> 4. **Asserting Equality over Set Difference:** Claiming $\pi(R - S) = \pi(R) - \pi(S)$. This is **false** in general. Only **subset** inclusion holds.
> 5. **Writing SQL Instead of Relational Algebra:** KTU explicitly asks for "relational algebra expression." Writing `SELECT Name FROM STUDENT` earns partial credit at best.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PROJECT Operation (π)**

- 🔹 **Symbol:** π (Greek capital pi, looks like a projector beam)
- 🔹 **Type:** Unary operation
- 🔹 **Purpose:** Vertical subset — selects specific columns
- 🔹 **Always removes duplicate tuples** (set semantics)
- 🔹 **Reduces the degree** (number of attributes) of the relation
- 🔹 **May reduce the cardinality** if duplicates exist in the projected columns
- 🔹 **Cardinality Bound:** $0 \le \vert \pi_{\vec{A}}(R) \vert \le \vert R \vert$
- 🔹 **Idempotence:** $\pi_{\vec{A}}(\pi_{\vec{A}, \vec{B}}(R)) = \pi_{\vec{A}}(R)$ — outermost π wins
- 🔹 **Distribution over UNION:** $\pi_{\vec{A}}(R \cup S) = \pi_{\vec{A}}(R) \cup \pi_{\vec{A}}(S)$ ✔
- 🔹 **Distribution over Set Difference:** Only $\subseteq$, **NOT** $=$ ✘
- 🔹 **Commutes with SELECT** only when the selection predicate references *only* the projected attributes
- 🔹 **Extended projection** allows arithmetic expressions: `π_{A, B*1.1 as C}(R)`
- 🔹 **Combines with RENAME (ρ)** to rename output attributes
- 🔹 **SQL equivalent:** `SELECT DISTINCT col1, col2 FROM table;`
- 🔹 **Engineering Applications:** Columnar databases, data privacy (column-level access control), index-only scans, network bandwidth reduction in distributed systems
- 🔹 **Common Mistake:** Confusing with SELECT (σ); remember π = columns, σ = rows

---

*End of Module 2 — Topic: PROJECT Operation Notes*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rename
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Rename # 📘 Module 2: Relational Algebra — The RENAME Operation (ρ) > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > The **Rename** operation is a unary operator in Relational Algebra, denoted by the Greek letter **ρ (rho)**. It is used to assign a new name to a relation and/or rename one or more of its attributes without altering the underlying tuples. The general syntax is: > $$\rho_{S(B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n)}(R)$$ > where **R** is the source relation, **S** is the new relation name, and **B₁, B₂, …, Bₙ** are the new attribute names. ## 🔍 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain English Analogy:** > Imagine you have a personal phone contact named *"Rahul"* who is *also* your classmate and *also* your project teammate. Same person, **three different roles**. To avoid confusion, you might call him *"Rahul (Classmate)"*, *"Rahul (Project Lead)"*, and *"Rahul (Friend)"* in your mental notepad. **You are not creating a new person** — you are simply assigning **logical aliases** to refer to the same individual in different contexts. > > The **Rename operation (ρ)** does exactly this for relations in a database. It allows the DBMS engine (and the query writer) to treat the **same relation** under **different names** so that operations like joins, unions, and set differences remain unambiguous. The tuples and data remain untouched — only the **label** changes. ## 🎯 Why Is Rename Critical in DBMS? | Scenario | Problem Without Rename | Solution Using ρ | |----------|------------------------|------------------| | **Self-Join** (e.g., finding employee-manager pairs) | Joining `EMPLOYEE` with itself causes attribute name collisions | ρ gives a temporary alias like `E1` and `E2` | | **Set Difference (R − R)** | Subtracting a relation from itself requires referring to it twice | ρ renames one copy to differentiate | | **Cartesian Product with Common Attributes** | Attribute ambiguity in result schema | ρ clarifies attribute origins | | **Query Readability** | Long queries become hard to trace | ρ adds semantic clarity | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** Rename is the **silent workhorse** behind every non-trivial relational algebra expression. Most KTU exam questions on joins and set operations will require you to **explicitly invoke ρ** to avoid losing marks for "ambiguous schema." ## 🏛️ Formal Specification The Rename operation is formally a **unary operator** (operates on one relation). It produces a new relation **identical in content** to the original, differing only in: 1. **Relation Name** (optional) 2. **Attribute Names** (optional) 3. **Both** (most common usage) The Rename operator is **idempotent in content** but **distinct in identity** — two renamed copies of the same relation are treated as separate schemas by the relational engine. # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🧩 Three Canonical Forms of the Rename Operator ### **Form 1: Rename the Relation Only** $$\rho_{S}(R)$$ This creates a relation **S** that is structurally identical to **R** but with a different name. All attribute names are preserved. **Example:** $$\rho_{\text{EmpTemp}}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$$ Result: A relation called `EmpTemp` with the same schema as `EMPLOYEE`. --- ### **Form 2: Rename Attributes Only** $$\rho_{(B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n)}(R)$$ This keeps the original relation name but **renames the attributes** in order. The new attribute names are mapped positionally: **B₁** replaces the first attribute, **B₂** replaces the second, and so on. **Example:** If `EMPLOYEE(SSN, Name, Salary)` and we write: $$\rho_{(\text{ID}, \text{EmpName}, \text{Sal})}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$$ The result has attributes: `ID`, `EmpName`, `Sal`. --- ### **Form 3: Rename Both Relation and Attributes** ⭐ *(Most Exam-Relevant)* $$\rho_{S(B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n)}(R)$$ This is the **complete form** used in 90% of KTU problems. It creates a new relation **S** with renamed attributes **B₁, B₂, …, Bₙ**. **Example:** $$\rho_{\text{E1}(\text{EmpID}, \text{EmpName}, \text{Dept})}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$$ --- ## 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes every variant of ρ you must memorize for the KTU Board Exam. | **Syntax** | **Renames** | **Use Case** | **KTU Frequency** | |------------|-------------|--------------|-------------------| | $\rho_{S}(R)$ | Relation only | Duplicate relation for set ops | ⭐⭐ | | $\rho_{(B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n)}(R)$ | Attributes only | Schema renaming for clarity | ⭐⭐ | | $\rho_{S(B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n)}(R)$ | Both relation & attributes | Self-joins, disambiguation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | $\rho_{S}(R) \bowtie \rho_{T}(R)$ | Self-join pattern | Employee-manager, course-prerequisite | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | $\rho_{S}(R) - \rho_{T}(R)$ | Self-difference pattern | Find records not matching themselves | ⭐⭐⭐ | --- ## ⚙️ Operational Properties of Rename | **Property** | **Description** | **Exam Relevance** | |--------------|-----------------|-------------------| | **Unary** | Operates on exactly one input relation | Definition question | | **Schema-preserving in degree** | Same number of attributes (arity unchanged) | True/False traps | | **Tuple-preserving** | No rows are added, removed, or modified | Conceptual clarity | | **Non-destructive** | Original relation R remains unchanged | Important for nested expressions | | **Combinable** | Can be nested: $\rho_{S}(\rho_{T}(R))$ | Order of nesting | --- ## 🏗️ Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why DBMS Engineers Care About Rename:** > - **Query Optimizers** use renaming to create intermediate logical representations of queries before physical execution. > - **View Definitions** in SQL often rely on renaming for clarity (analogous to SQL aliases). > - **Distributed Databases** use renaming to reconcile schema mismatches across heterogeneous nodes. > - **Data Warehousing & ETL pipelines** frequently rename columns to maintain consistency across sources. --- ## 🧠 The "Why" Behind Rename — KTU's Favorite Conceptual Trap > [!WARNING] > **Common Misconception:** "Rename changes the data." > **Reality:** Rename is a **purely logical / metadata operation**. It does NOT touch the tuples. The relational engine treats the renamed relation as a **separate schema object** for the duration of the query, but the underlying storage is untouched. --- ## 🔗 Rename in Composite Expressions Rename is almost never used in isolation in KTU problems. It appears as a **sub-expression** within larger relational algebra queries: ### **Pattern 1: Self-Join** $$\rho_{E1}(\text{EMPLOYEE}) \bowtie_{\text{E1.SSN = E2.MgrSSN}} \rho_{E2}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$$ This finds every employee paired with their manager. ### **Pattern 2: Set Difference After Rename** $$\rho_{A}(R) - \rho_{B}(R)$$ This subtracts the renamed copy, useful in finding records that violate constraints. ### **Pattern 3: Projection with Rename** $$\pi_{\text{NewName}}(\rho_{S(B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n)}(R))$$ Renaming before projection allows clean column selection in the final output. # 📊 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🧪 Worked Example 1: Self-Join Using Rename (Full Derivation) > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** We have the relation `EMPLOYEE(SSN, Name, Salary, MgrSSN)` where `MgrSSN` is the SSN of the employee's manager. Find the names of all employees who earn **more than their manager**. ### **Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:** **Step 1 — Identify the Need for Rename:** We need to compare each employee's salary with their manager's salary. Since manager is also an employee stored in the **same** `EMPLOYEE` relation, we must treat it as **two separate logical copies**. **Step 2 — Apply Rename to Create Two Copies:** $$\rho_{E1(\text{ESSN}, \text{EName}, \text{ESal}, \text{EMgrSSN})}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$$ $$\rho_{E2(\text{MSSN}, \text{MName}, \text{MSal}, \text{MMgrSSN})}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$$ > Here, **E1** represents the employee view and **E2** represents the manager view. Attributes are renamed to avoid ambiguity after the join. **Step 3 — Perform the Join:** $$\text{Temp} = \rho_{E1} \bowtie_{\text{E1.EMgrSSN} = \text{E2.MSSN}} \rho_{E2}$$ The join condition `E1.EMgrSSN = E2.MSSN` ensures we pair each employee with their **correct manager**. **Step 4 — Apply Selection (σ):** $$\text{Temp2} = \sigma_{\text{E1.ESal} > \text{E2.MSal}}(\text{Temp})$$ This filters tuples where employee's salary exceeds manager's salary. **Step 5 — Project the Required Attribute:** $$\text{Result} = \pi_{\text{E1.EName}}(\text{Temp2})$$ **Final Combined Expression:** $$\boxed{\pi_{\text{E1.EName}}\left(\sigma_{\text{E1.ESal} > \text{E2.MSal}}\left(\rho_{E1}(\text{EMPLOYEE}) \bowtie_{\text{E1.EMgrSSN} = \text{E2.MSSN}} \rho_{E2}(\text{EMPLOYEE})\right)\right)}$$ --- ## 🧮 Worked Example 2: Attribute-Only Renaming (Schema Transformation) > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** Given `STUDENT(RollNo, Name, Marks)`, rename the attributes to `StudentID`, `StudentName`, `Score` without changing the relation name. ### **Derivation:** **Step 1 — Original Schema:** $$\text{STUDENT}(\text{RollNo}, \text{Name}, \text{Marks})$$ **Step 2 — Apply Rename (Attributes Only Form):** $$\rho_{(\text{StudentID}, \text{StudentName}, \text{Score})}(\text{STUDENT})$$ **Step 3 — Verify Positional Mapping:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{RollNo} &\rightarrow \text{StudentID} \\ \text{Name} &\rightarrow \text{StudentName} \\ \text{Marks} &\rightarrow \text{Score} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Sample Tuple Transformation:** | **Original STUDENT** | **After Rename** | |----------------------|------------------| | `(101, "Anu", 95)` | `(101, "Anu", 95)` | | `(102, "Rahul", 88)` | `(102, "Rahul", 88)` | Notice: **Data is identical**, only the **column headers** (metadata) change. --- ## 🧮 Worked Example 3: Set Difference Using Rename > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** Given `BOOKS(ISBN, Title)`, find books that are in the catalog but were never ordered. Let `ORDERS(ISBN, OrderID)` be the orders table. ### **Derivation:** **Step 1 — Rename BOOKS for Clarity:** $$R_1 = \rho_{B1(\text{BookISBN}, \text{BookTitle})}(\text{BOOKS})$$ **Step 2 — Rename ORDERS for Clarity:** $$R_2 = \rho_{O1(\text{OrdISBN}, \text{OrdID})}(\text{ORDERS})$$ **Step 3 — Project ISBN from Orders:** $$R_3 = \pi_{\text{OrdISBN}}(R_2)$$ **Step 4 — Rename to Match Schemas:** $$R_4 = \rho_{B2(\text{BookISBN}, \text{BookTitle})}(R_3 \times \text{BOOKS})$$ > *Note: In practice, this is simplified by projecting BOOKS first:* $$R_4 = \pi_{\text{BookISBN}, \text{BookTitle}}(R_1) - \pi_{\text{BookISBN}, \text{BookTitle}}(\text{BOOKS} \bowtie \text{ORDERS})$$ **Step 5 — Final Expression:** $$\boxed{\pi_{B1.\text{BookISBN}, B1.\text{BookTitle}}\left(\rho_{B1}(\text{BOOKS})\right) - \pi_{B1.\text{BookISBN}, B1.\text{BookTitle}}\left(\rho_{B1}(\text{BOOKS}) \bowtie \rho_{O1}(\text{ORDERS})\right)}$$ --- ## 📋 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Form | **Question Requirement** | **Rename Form to Use** | **Justification** | |--------------------------|------------------------|------------------| | Compare rows within the same relation | $\rho_{S(B_1,\dots,B_n)}(R) \bowtie \rho_{T(C_1,\dots,C_n)}(R)$ | Two copies needed | | Find anomalies between current and historical versions | $\rho_{A}(R) - \rho_{B}(R)$ | Set difference on same relation | | Make query output readable for end-user | $\rho_{(\text{new attrs})}(R)$ | Attribute clarity | | Combine with aggregation | $\rho_{S}(R)$ followed by aggregate functions | Intermediate aliasing | | Implement recursive queries (course prerequisites) | Nested $\rho$ in UNION | Self-reference | # 🎨 Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 🗺️ Diagram 1: Rename Operation Flow (Conceptual Pipeline) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Source Relation R
Attributes A1, A2, A3, A4"] --> B["Rho Operator
rho"] B --> C["Renamed Relation S
Attributes B1, B2, B3, B4"] B --> D["Metadata Change
Names only"] B --> E["Tuple Data
Unchanged"] D --> C E --> C C --> F["Used in Downstream
Operations: Join, Union, Difference, Projection"] ``` --- ## 🗺️ Diagram 2: Self-Join Architecture Using Rename ```mermaid flowchart TD R["EMPLOYEE Relation
SSN, Name, Salary, MgrSSN"] R --> R1["Rho 1
rho E1"] R --> R2["Rho 2
rho E2"] R1 --> J["Join Operation
E1.MgrSSN = E2.SSN"] R2 --> J J --> S["Selection
E1.Salary greater than E2.Salary"] S --> P["Projection
E1.Name"] P --> OUT["Final Result
Names of qualifying employees"] ``` --- ## 🗺️ Diagram 3: Rename Variants Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Need to Apply Rename?"] --> Q1{"Renaming the
relation name?"} Q1 -->|Yes, only relation| F1["Form 1
rho S of R"] Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Renaming the
attributes?"} Q2 -->|Yes, only attributes| F2["Form 2
rho B1 B2 Bn of R"] Q2 -->|Both relation and attributes| F3["Form 3
rho S B1 B2 Bn of R"] F1 --> End["Apply in Query"] F2 --> End F3 --> End ``` --- ## 🗺️ Diagram 4: Block-Level Architecture of Rename in Query Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IN["Input Layer"] I1["Relation R
Logical Schema"] end subgraph RE["Rename Engine Block"] B1["Parser Block
Tokenizes rho notation"] B2["Metadata Mapper
Attribute substitution table"] B3["Schema Rebuilder
Constructs new relation header"] B1 --> B2 --> B3 end subgraph OUT["Output Layer"] O1["Renamed Relation S
Same tuples, new schema label"] end I1 --> B1 B3 --> O1 ``` --- ## 🗺️ Diagram 5: Self-Difference Pattern Using Rename ```mermaid flowchart LR R["Original Relation R"] --> A["Rho A
rho A of R"] R --> B["Rho B
rho B of R"] A --> D["Set Difference
A minus B"] B --> D D --> RES["Result
Tuples in A but not in B"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** In all the above diagrams, note that the **arrow into Rename** and the **arrow out of Rename** carry the **same tuple data** — only the **schema label** (relation name and/or attribute names) changes. This is the core conceptual takeaway for KTU Board Exams. # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the Rename operation in Relational Algebra. List its three syntactic forms with one example each.** #### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks): > The **Rename operation** is a unary relational algebra operator denoted by the Greek letter **ρ (rho)**. It is used to assign a new name to a relation and/or its attributes **without modifying any tuple data**. It is essential for disambiguating self-joins, set operations on the same relation, and improving query readability. **Three syntactic forms:** | **Form** | **Syntax** | **Example** | |----------|------------|-------------| | 1. Rename relation only | $\rho_{S}(R)$ | $\rho_{\text{EmpCopy}}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$ | | 2. Rename attributes only | $\rho_{(B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n)}(R)$ | $\rho_{(\text{EID}, \text{EName})}(\text{EMP})$ | | 3. Rename both | $\rho_{S(B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n)}(R)$ | $\rho_{\text{E1(EID, EName, ESal})}(\text{EMP})$ | --- ### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why is the Rename operation necessary in Relational Algebra? Illustrate with the self-join scenario.** #### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks): > The Rename operation (ρ) is necessary because it allows the **same relation to be referenced multiple times** in a single relational algebra expression without causing schema ambiguity. > > **Illustration — Self-Join Scenario:** To find employees who earn more than their manager, the `EMPLOYEE` relation must be **logically duplicated**: one copy (say, `E1`) represents the employee, and another copy (`E2`) represents the manager. Without ρ, the join `EMPLOYEE ⨝ EMPLOYEE` would create **attribute name collisions** (e.g., two `SSN` columns). Rename resolves this by assigning distinct prefixes, enabling the join condition `E1.MgrSSN = E2.SSN` to execute unambiguously. --- ## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has **two sub-parts** of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **ONE full question** (with both sub-parts) out of the internal choice provided. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Given the following schema:** - `STUDENT(SID, SName, Dept, GPA)` - `ENROLLMENT(SID, CourseID, Grade)` #### **(a)** Write a relational algebra expression using **Rename (ρ)** to find the names of all students who are enrolled in **both 'CS101' and 'CS102'**. **(7 Marks)** #### **(b)** Explain why **Rename is essential** in this query and demonstrate what happens if Rename is omitted. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution: ##### **Part (a) — Relational Algebra Expression:** **Step 1:** Identify students enrolled in CS101. $$A = \pi_{\text{SID}}\left(\sigma_{\text{CourseID} = \text{'CS101'}}(\text{ENROLLMENT})\right)$$ **Step 2:** Identify students enrolled in CS102. $$B = \pi_{\text{SID}}\left(\sigma_{\text{CourseID} = \text{'CS102'}}(\text{ENROLLMENT})\right)$$ **Step 3:** Rename for clarity (apply ρ to both intermediate results to make schema explicit). $$A' = \rho_{\text{CS101\_Enrolled}(\text{StudentID})}(A)$$ $$B' = \rho_{\text{CS102\_Enrolled}(\text{StudentID})}(B)$$ **Step 4:** Intersection to find common students. $$C = A' \cap B'$$ **Step 5:** Join with STUDENT to get names. $$\text{Result} = \pi_{\text{SName}}\left(\text{STUDENT} \bowtie_{\text{SID} = C.\text{StudentID}} C\right)$$ **Final Combined Expression:** $$\boxed{\pi_{\text{SName}}\left(\text{STUDENT} \bowtie_{\text{SID} = \text{StudentID}}\left(\rho_{\text{CS101\_Enrolled}(\text{StudentID})}\left(\pi_{\text{SID}}\left(\sigma_{\text{CourseID} = \text{'CS101'}}(\text{ENROLLMENT})\right)\right) \cap \rho_{\text{CS102\_Enrolled}(\text{StudentID})}\left(\pi_{\text{SID}}\left(\sigma_{\text{CourseID} = \text{'CS102'}}(\text{ENROLLMENT})\right)\right)\right)\right)}$$ ##### **Part (b) — Why Rename is Essential:** | **Without Rename** | **With Rename (ρ)** | |---------------------|---------------------| | Both `A` and `B` have attribute `SID`, causing ambiguity during intersection `A ∩ B` | Renamed to `CS101_Enrolled(StudentID)` and `CS102_Enrolled(StudentID)` — schemas are **distinct** | | The final join condition `SID = SID` is **ambiguous** | The join condition `SID = CS101_Enrolled.StudentID` is **explicit** | | Query optimizer struggles to resolve attribute references | Attribute resolution is **unambiguous and tractable** | > **Conclusion:** Rename provides **schema-level disambiguation** and **logical separation** of identical relations used multiple times in one expression. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Given the following schema:** - `EMPLOYEE(EID, EName, Salary, DeptID, MgrEID)` #### **(a)** Using **Rename (ρ)**, write a relational algebra expression to find the **names of all employees who have the same manager as employee 'E101'**. **(7 Marks)** #### **(b)** Rewrite the expression from part (a) **without using Rename**, and explain why this leads to **incorrect or ambiguous results**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution: ##### **Part (a) — Expression Using Rename:** **Step 1:** Find the manager of E101. $$\text{MgrOfE101} = \rho_{M1(\text{MgrEID\_of\_E101})}\left(\pi_{\text{MgrEID}}\left(\sigma_{\text{EID} = \text{'E101'}}(\text{EMPLOYEE})\right)\right)$$ **Step 2:** Create two logical copies of EMPLOYEE. $$E_A = \rho_{\text{EmpA}(\text{EID\_A}, \text{EName\_A}, \text{Sal\_A}, \text{DeptID\_A}, \text{MgrEID\_A})}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$$ $$E_B = \rho_{\text{EmpB}(\text{EID\_B}, \text{EName\_B}, \text{Sal\_B}, \text{DeptID\_B}, \text{MgrEID\_B})}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$$ **Step 3:** Filter employees whose manager matches E101's manager. $$\text{Final} = \pi_{\text{EName\_A}}\left(\sigma_{\text{EmpA.MgrEID\_A} = \text{MgrOfE101.MgrEID\_of\_E101}} \left(E_A \bowtie E_B\right)\right)$$ **Final Combined Expression:** $$\boxed{\pi_{\text{EName\_A}}\left(\sigma_{\text{EmpA.MgrEID\_A} = \text{M1.MgrEID\_of\_E101}}\left(\rho_{\text{EmpA}}(\text{EMPLOYEE}) \bowtie \rho_{\text{M1}}\left(\pi_{\text{MgrEID}}\left(\sigma_{\text{EID} = \text{'E101'}}(\text{EMPLOYEE})\right)\right)\right)\right)}$$ ##### **Part (b) — Without Rename — Why It Fails:** **Naive (Incorrect) Expression:** $$\pi_{\text{EName}}\left(\sigma_{\text{EMPLOYEE.MgrEID} = \text{Temp.MgrEID}}\left(\text{EMPLOYEE} \bowtie \pi_{\text{MgrEID}}\left(\sigma_{\text{EID} = \text{'E101'}}(\text{EMPLOYEE})\right)\right)\right)$$ **Problems:** | **Issue** | **Explanation** | |-----------|-----------------| | **Self-join without rename** | `EMPLOYEE ⨝ EMPLOYEE` is attempted, creating two identical attribute sets — the join condition is **syntactically invalid** in standard relational algebra | | **Attribute collision** | Both sides of the join expose `EID`, `EName`, `MgrEID` — the system cannot determine which `MgrEID` to compare | | **E101 included in output** | Without filtering out E101, E101 himself satisfies the condition (since his MgrEID matches his own MgrEID) | | **Optimizer ambiguity** | The query plan cannot be generated without unique attribute references | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write the rename operator as **ρ** but forget to specify the **new attribute names** when both forms are needed. **Always specify both relation name AND attributes** in self-join scenarios. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **ρ (Rename)** with **π (Projection)** in handwriting. KTU examiners **strictly deduct 1 mark** for miswritten Greek symbols. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Using Rename in a way that **changes the degree (number of attributes)** — this is **incorrect**. Rename preserves arity. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting to apply Rename on **BOTH copies** of a self-referenced relation. If only one is renamed, the join condition is still ambiguous. > - **Common Mistake #5:** Including the reference employee (e.g., E101) in the final result when the query implies "other employees." Add a condition `EID ≠ 'E101'`. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Rename (ρ) Operation** - 🔹 **Symbol:** Greek letter **ρ (rho)** — memorize the spelling and symbol clearly. - 🔹 **Type:** **Unary operator** — operates on exactly **one** relation. - 🔹 **Three Forms:** Relation-only, Attributes-only, Both — Form 3 is **most frequently tested**. - 🔹 **Syntax:** $\rho_{S(B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n)}(R)$ — S is new relation name, B's are new attribute names. - 🔹 **Positional Mapping:** Attribute renaming is **positional** — B₁ replaces 1st attribute, B₂ replaces 2nd, etc. - 🔹 **Data Integrity:** Rename does **NOT modify tuples** — it is a **pure metadata operation**. - 🔹 **Arity Preservation:** The number of attributes (degree) **remains unchanged**. - 🔹 **Primary Use Case 1:** **Self-Joins** — joining a relation with itself (e.g., employee-manager). - 🔹 **Primary Use Case 2:** **Set Operations on Same Relation** — `ρ(R) − ρ(R)`, `ρ(R) ∪ ρ(R)`. - 🔹 **Primary Use Case 3:** **Query Readability** — making nested expressions traceable. - 🔹 **SQL Equivalent:** The `AS` keyword in SQL (e.g., `SELECT E1.Name FROM Employee AS E1 JOIN Employee AS E2 ON E1.MgrID = E2.ID`). - 🔹 **Exam Tip:** Whenever you see a query about "employees and their managers," "courses and prerequisites," or "find pairs within the same table" — **immediately think of ρ**. - 🔹 **Trap to Avoid:** Rename does **NOT** filter, project, join, or alter data. It is **purely renamative**. - 🔹 **KTU Board Pattern:** Self-join questions using ρ appear in **almost every KTU Module 2 exam paper** — practice drawing the rename-then-join pipeline.

topics.title: Union
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Union

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Relational Algebra** of Database Management Systems, the **UNION** operation (denoted by the symbol **∪**) is a **binary set operation** that produces a new relation containing all tuples that appear in either of two source relations (or in both). The result relation automatically enforces **set semantics**, meaning that **duplicate tuples are eliminated** in the final output, retaining only one copy of each unique tuple.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The Union operation on two relations R and S is defined as:
> $$R \cup S = \{ t \mid t \in R \lor t \in S \}$$
> where the result relation has the same **schema (attribute structure)** as both R and S.

### 1.2 Union Compatibility — The Cardinal Pre-Requisite

Two relations R and S are said to be **union-compatible** if and only if they satisfy **all three** of the following conditions:

| S.No. | Condition | Description |
|:-----:|:----------|:------------|
| 1 | **Same Degree** | Both relations must have the **same number of attributes (arity)**. |
| 2 | **Same Domains** | The corresponding attributes (in the order they appear) must be drawn from the **same domain**. |
| 3 | **Meaningful Correspondence** | The attribute names (or positional correspondence) must represent **semantically comparable** values. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A relation `STUDENT(SID, SName, Branch)` and `FACULTY(FID, FName, Department)` are **NOT** union-compatible because the first is about students and the second is about faculty, even if column counts match. A more compatible example would be `CS_STUDENT(RollNo, Name, Branch)` and `IT_STUDENT(RollNo, Name, Branch)`.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Baskets of Fruits" 🍎🍊

Imagine you have **two baskets of fruits**:
- **Basket R** contains: Apple, Banana, Mango
- **Basket S** contains: Banana, Grapes, Orange

When you **UNION** the two baskets, you take **every fruit that is present in EITHER basket (or both)** and place them into a single big basket. The result will be: **{Apple, Banana, Mango, Grapes, Orange}** — note that **Banana appears only once** even though it was in both baskets, because we are performing a **set operation**, not a **multiset (bag) operation**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Mnemonic:** "UNION = OR logic." A tuple qualifies for the result if it belongs to **R OR S** (or both), just like the logical OR operator. This is fundamentally different from INTERSECTION (AND logic) and DIFFERENCE (NOT IN logic).

### 1.4 Cardinality (Row Count) Metric

The maximum and minimum number of tuples in the union result can be formally stated as:

$$|R| + |S| - |R \cap S| \;\leq\; \vert R \cup S \vert \;\leq\; |R| + |S|$$

- **Maximum bound:** Achieved when R and S share **no common tuples** (i.e., R ∩ S = ∅). In this case, the result has exactly $|R| + |S|$ tuples.
- **Minimum bound:** Achieved when **S is a subset of R** (i.e., S ⊆ R). In this case, the result has exactly $|R|$ tuples.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

The UNION operation executes in **four distinct logical phases** within the query engine:

- **Phase 1 — Schema Validation:** The DBMS first verifies that the two operand relations are **union-compatible** (same arity, compatible domains). If not, the system returns a **type error** and aborts the operation.
- **Phase 2 — Tuple Streaming:** All tuples from relation R are streamed into a temporary working buffer. Then all tuples from relation S are appended to this buffer.
- **Phase 3 — Duplicate Elimination (Dedup):** A sort-based or hash-based deduplication algorithm is applied to ensure that no two identical tuples appear in the final output. This is the most **computationally expensive** step.
- **Phase 4 — Result Materialization:** The deduplicated tuples are projected into a new relation with the schema of R (which equals the schema of S).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is duplicate elimination necessary?**
> Relational Algebra is grounded in **set theory**, which mandates that every element in a set is **unique**. Therefore, UNION enforces set semantics by default. However, in practical SQL implementations, the **UNION ALL** operator can be used to preserve duplicates (multiset semantics), which is significantly faster because it skips the expensive dedup phase.

## 2.2 Algebraic Properties of UNION

| Property | Mathematical Form | Intuitive Meaning |
|:---------|:------------------|:------------------|
| **Commutativity** | $R \cup S = S \cup R$ | The order of operands does not matter. |
| **Associativity** | $R \cup (S \cup T) = (R \cup S) \cup T$ | Grouping of operations does not matter. |
| **Idempotence** | $R \cup R = R$ | Unioning a relation with itself returns the same relation. |
| **Identity Element** | $R \cup \emptyset = R$ | The empty set is the identity for union. |
| **Absorption** | $R \cup (R \cap S) = R$ | Absorption law of set theory. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Questions on **commutativity and associativity** of relational operators are extremely common in 3-mark and 14-mark questions. Always remember that **UNION, INTERSECTION, and JOIN are commutative**, but **MINUS (Set Difference) is NOT commutative**.

## 2.3 UNION vs UNION ALL — A Critical Distinction

| Feature | UNION (Set Semantics) | UNION ALL (Bag Semantics) |
|:--------|:----------------------|:---------------------------|
| **Duplicate Handling** | Removes duplicate tuples | Retains all duplicate tuples |
| **Result Ordering** | May sort tuples internally for dedup | No implicit sorting required |
| **Performance** | Slower (requires sort/hash) | Faster (no dedup overhead) |
| **SQL Standard** | ANSI SQL compliant | ANSI SQL compliant |
| **Use Case** | When unique records are needed | When duplicates carry meaning (e.g., transactions) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Name | Definition | Cardinality Rule |
|:------------------|:-----|:-----------|:-----------------|
| $R \cup S$ | Union | $R \cup S = \{t \mid t \in R \lor t \in S\}$ | $\vert R \cup S \vert = \vert R \vert + \vert S \vert - \vert R \cap S \vert$ |
| $R \cap S$ | Intersection | $R \cap S = \{t \mid t \in R \land t \in S\}$ | $\vert R \cap S \vert = \vert R \vert + \vert S \vert - \vert R \cup S \vert$ |
| $R - S$ | Difference | $R - S = \{t \mid t \in R \land t \notin S\}$ | $0 \leq \vert R - S \vert \leq \vert R \vert$ |
| $R \times S$ | Cartesian Product | $R \times S = \{t \mid t = \text{concat}(r, s), r \in R, s \in S\}$ | $\vert R \times S \vert = \vert R \vert \times \vert S \vert$ |
| $\emptyset$ | Empty Set | Contains no tuples | $\vert \emptyset \vert = 0$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Application:** In real-world enterprise systems, UNION is used extensively in **data warehousing** for combining partitioned data across multiple nodes, in **federated databases** for merging results from heterogeneous sources, and in **reporting tools** for consolidating reports from different business units.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Detailed Relational Algebra Execution

### Given Relations:

**Relation R — `CSE_STUDENT`:**

| RollNo | Name | Branch |
|:-------|:-----|:-------|
| 101 | Ananya | CSE |
| 102 | Bharat | CSE |
| 103 | Chitra | CSE |

**Relation S — `IT_STUDENT`:**

| RollNo | Name | Branch |
|:-------|:-----|:-------|
| 102 | Bharat | IT |
| 104 | Deepa | IT |
| 105 | Eshaan | IT |

### Step 1: Check Union Compatibility

- R has **3 attributes** (RollNo, Name, Branch) ✓
- S has **3 attributes** (RollNo, Name, Branch) ✓
- Corresponding attributes have the **same domain** (Integer, String, String) ✓
- **Conclusion:** R and S are **union-compatible**. ✅

### Step 2: Stream All Tuples into a Working Buffer

We concatenate all tuples from R followed by all tuples from S:

$$\text{Buffer} = \{ (101, \text{Ananya}, \text{CSE}), (102, \text{Bharat}, \text{CSE}), (103, \text{Chitra}, \text{CSE}), (102, \text{Bharat}, \text{IT}), (104, \text{Deepa}, \text{IT}), (105, \text{Eshaan}, \text{IT}) \}$$

### Step 3: Apply Duplicate Elimination

We compare every pair of tuples to identify duplicates. Note that the tuple **(102, Bharat, CSE)** and **(102, Bharat, IT)** are **NOT identical** because the **Branch** value differs. Therefore, **no duplicates exist** in this example.

$$\text{Unique Tuples} = \text{Buffer} \text{ (no change, since all tuples are distinct)}$$

### Step 4: Materialize the Result Relation

**Result: $R \cup S$ — `ALL_STUDENTS`:**

| RollNo | Name | Branch |
|:-------|:-----|:-------|
| 101 | Ananya | CSE |
| 102 | Bharat | CSE |
| 102 | Bharat | IT |
| 103 | Chitra | CSE |
| 104 | Deepa | IT |
| 105 | Eshaan | IT |

### Cardinality Verification:

$$\vert R \cup S \vert = \vert R \vert + \vert S \vert - \vert R \cap S \vert$$

$$\vert R \cup S \vert = 3 + 3 - 0 = 6$$

Since $R \cap S = \emptyset$ (no tuple is identical across both relations), the result has **6 tuples**. This matches our computed result. ✅

## 3.2 Case Where Duplicates Arise

**Modified Relation R (after re-takes):**

| RollNo | Name | Branch |
|:-------|:-----|:-------|
| 101 | Ananya | CSE |
| 102 | Bharat | CSE |
| 102 | Bharat | CSE |
| 103 | Chitra | CSE |

Here, the tuple **(102, Bharat, CSE)** appears **twice** in R.

**Step 1 — Stream:** Buffer has 7 tuples total (4 from R + 3 from S).

**Step 2 — Dedup:** The tuple (102, Bharat, CSE) is identified as appearing more than once. The second occurrence is removed.

**Step 3 — Result:**

| RollNo | Name | Branch |
|:-------|:-----|:-------|
| 101 | Ananya | CSE |
| 102 | Bharat | CSE |
| 102 | Bharat | IT |
| 103 | Chitra | CSE |
| 104 | Deepa | IT |
| 105 | Eshaan | IT |

**Cardinality Verification:**

$$\vert R \cup S \vert = 4 + 3 - 1 = 6$$

Here, $\vert R \cap S \vert = 1$ because the tuple **(102, Bharat, CSE)** is technically a member of both R (appears twice but counted once as a set element) and S does NOT contain it. Wait — let me correct this:

Actually, since S does not contain (102, Bharat, CSE) at all, $R \cap S = \emptyset$, so:

$$\vert R \cup S \vert = 4 + 3 - 0 = 7 \text{ (in multiset semantics)}$$

But with **set semantics** (which UNION enforces), the result has **6 tuples** because the duplicate within R is eliminated. This demonstrates the critical role of **deduplication** in UNION.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use UNION

| Scenario | Recommended Operation | Reason |
|:---------|:----------------------|:-------|
| Combining customer lists from two different regions with no overlap expected | **UNION ALL** | No duplicates expected; UNNECESSARY dedup wastes time. |
| Merging two product catalogs where the same product may be listed in both | **UNION** | Duplicate removal is semantically important. |
| Aggregating transaction logs from multiple servers | **UNION ALL** | Every transaction is unique and meaningful; duplicates should be preserved. |
| Creating a master employee list from acquired companies | **UNION** | Duplicate employees must be merged. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Many students confuse UNION with JOIN. Remember — **UNION combines rows vertically** (more rows, same columns), whereas **JOIN combines columns horizontally** (more columns, same rows). This is a classic 3-mark question!

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The UNION Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Relation R"] --> C["Schema Compatibility Check"]
    B["Input Relation S"] --> C
    C --> D{"Union Compatible?"}
    D -- "No" --> E["Return Type Error"]
    D -- "Yes" --> F["Stream Tuples from R"]
    F --> G["Stream Tuples from S"]
    G --> H["Combined Buffer with Duplicates"]
    H --> I["Sort Based Dedup"]
    H --> J["Hash Based Dedup"]
    I --> K["Unique Tuple Set"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Output Relation R Union S"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — UNION vs UNION ALL Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UNION["UNION Path - Set Semantics"]
        R1["Relation R"] --> M1["Merge All Tuples"]
        S1["Relation S"] --> M1
        M1 --> D1["Deduplication Engine"]
        D1 --> O1["Unique Output Set"]
    end
    subgraph UNIONALL["UNION ALL Path - Bag Semantics"]
        R2["Relation R"] --> M2["Merge All Tuples"]
        S2["Relation S"] --> M2
        M2 --> O2["Complete Output with Duplicates"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Venn Diagram Representation of UNION

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Universe["Universe of All Tuples"]
        subgraph Rcircle["Relation R"]
            A1["Tuple 1 in R"]
            A2["Tuple 2 in R"]
            A3["Shared Tuple 3"]
        end
        subgraph Scircle["Relation S"]
            B1["Shared Tuple 3"]
            B2["Tuple 4 in S"]
            B3["Tuple 5 in S"]
        end
    end
    A1 --> ResultA["Result R Union S includes all tuples from both R and S with duplicates removed"]
    A2 --> ResultA
    A3 --> ResultA
    B1 --> ResultA
    B2 --> ResultA
    B3 --> ResultA
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — SQL Execution Pipeline for UNION

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["First SELECT Query"] --> V["Schema Matching Stage"]
    Q2["Second SELECT Query"] --> V
    V --> W{"Same Number of Columns?"}
    W -- "No" --> X["SQL Compilation Error"]
    W -- "Yes" --> Y["Execute Query 1 and Collect Result Set"]
    Y --> Z["Execute Query 2 and Append to Result Set"]
    Z --> AA{"UNION or UNION ALL?"}
    AA -- "UNION ALL" --> BB["Direct Output without Sorting"]
    AA -- "UNION" --> CC["Sort and Remove Duplicates"]
    CC --> DD["Final Unique Result Set"]
    BB --> DD
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Aid:** In the Venn diagram, the UNION corresponds to the **entire shaded region of both circles combined**. Only the **overlapping (intersection) region** would be selected if we were performing an INTERSECTION operation. The **exclusively R region** minus the overlap would be R − S (set difference).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define the UNION operation in Relational Algebra. State the two essential conditions for two relations to be union-compatible.**

**Model Answer:**

The **UNION** operation in relational algebra is a binary set operation that combines tuples from two relations R and S to produce a new relation containing all distinct tuples that belong to **at least one** of the two relations. It is denoted by the symbol **∪** and is formally defined as:

$$R \cup S = \{ t \mid t \in R \lor t \in S \}$$

The **two essential conditions** for two relations to be union-compatible are:
1. Both relations must have the **same number of attributes** (same degree or arity).
2. The corresponding attributes (in the same positional order) must be drawn from the **same domain** of values.

If these conditions are satisfied, the result of R ∪ S will have the **same schema** as R and S, and will contain **all unique tuples** from both relations (duplicates are automatically removed).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between UNION and UNION ALL in SQL with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | UNION | UNION ALL |
|:-------|:------|:----------|
| **Duplicate Handling** | Eliminates duplicate rows from the result. | Retains all duplicate rows. |
| **Performance** | Slower due to internal sort/hash-based deduplication. | Faster as no deduplication is performed. |
| **Semantics** | Follows set theory (unique elements). | Follows multiset/bag theory. |

**Example:**

```sql
-- Table A: {1, 2, 3}, Table B: {2, 3, 4}

SELECT val FROM TableA
UNION
SELECT val FROM TableB;
-- Result: {1, 2, 3, 4}   (4 unique rows)

SELECT val FROM TableA
UNION ALL
SELECT val FROM TableB;
-- Result: {1, 2, 3, 2, 3, 4}   (6 rows with duplicates)
```

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is **14 marks** with a **Module Internal Choice**. The question is split into two sub-parts: **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Consider the following two relations:

**STUDENT_CSE:**

| RollNo | Name | CGPA |
|:-------|:-----|:-----|
| 101 | Anil | 8.5 |
| 102 | Babita | 9.1 |
| 103 | Chetan | 7.8 |
| 104 | Deepa | 8.9 |

**STUDENT_IT:**

| RollNo | Name | CGPA |
|:-------|:-----|:-----|
| 103 | Chetan | 7.8 |
| 105 | Esha | 8.2 |
| 106 | Farhan | 9.0 |
| 102 | Babita | 9.1 |

Compute the result of **STUDENT_CSE ∪ STUDENT_IT**. Also write the equivalent SQL query. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove the following identity using set theory and explain its significance in query optimization: **R ∪ (S ∩ T) = (R ∪ S) ∩ (R ∪ T)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Solution:

**Step 1 — Check Union Compatibility:**
- Both relations have **3 attributes**: (RollNo, Name, CGPA) ✓
- Corresponding attributes have the **same domains** (Integer, String, Decimal) ✓
- **Result:** The relations are union-compatible. ✅

**Step 2 — List all tuples from both relations:**

From STUDENT_CSE: { (101, Anil, 8.5), (102, Babita, 9.1), (103, Chetan, 7.8), (104, Deepa, 8.9) }

From STUDENT_IT: { (103, Chetan, 7.8), (105, Esha, 8.2), (106, Farhan, 9.0), (102, Babita, 9.1) }

**Step 3 — Eliminate Duplicates:**
The tuples (102, Babita, 9.1) and (103, Chetan, 7.8) appear in **both** relations. These duplicates are removed in set semantics.

**Result: STUDENT_CSE ∪ STUDENT_IT:**

| RollNo | Name | CGPA |
|:-------|:-----|:-----|
| 101 | Anil | 8.5 |
| 102 | Babita | 9.1 |
| 103 | Chetan | 7.8 |
| 104 | Deepa | 8.9 |
| 105 | Esha | 8.2 |
| 106 | Farhan | 9.0 |

**Total tuples in result:** 6

**Cardinality Verification:**

$$\vert R \cup S \vert = \vert R \vert + \vert S \vert - \vert R \cap S \vert = 4 + 4 - 2 = 6 \; ✓$$

**Equivalent SQL Query:**

```sql
SELECT RollNo, Name, CGPA FROM STUDENT_CSE
UNION
SELECT RollNo, Name, CGPA FROM STUDENT_IT;
```

**Note:** In the SQL version, `UNION` automatically removes duplicates. To keep them, replace with `UNION ALL`.

---

#### Part (b) — Proof of the Distributive Law:

**Statement:** $R \cup (S \cap T) = (R \cup S) \cap (R \cup T)$

**Proof using Set Theory (Element-wise approach):**

Let $t$ be an arbitrary tuple. We need to show that $t \in [R \cup (S \cap T)] \iff t \in [(R \cup S) \cap (R \cup T)]$.

**Forward Direction (⊆):**
Assume $t \in R \cup (S \cap T)$.

This means $t \in R$ **OR** $t \in (S \cap T)$.

**Case 1:** If $t \in R$, then:
- $t \in R \Rightarrow t \in (R \cup S)$ ✓
- $t \in R \Rightarrow t \in (R \cup T)$ ✓
- Therefore, $t \in (R \cup S) \cap (R \cup T)$ ✓

**Case 2:** If $t \in (S \cap T)$, then:
- $t \in S \Rightarrow t \in (R \cup S)$ ✓
- $t \in T \Rightarrow t \in (R \cup T)$ ✓
- Therefore, $t \in (R \cup S) \cap (R \cup T)$ ✓

In both cases, $t \in (R \cup S) \cap (R \cup T)$. Hence, $R \cup (S \cap T) \subseteq (R \cup S) \cap (R \cup T)$. ✓

**Reverse Direction (⊇):**
Assume $t \in (R \cup S) \cap (R \cup T)$.

This means $t \in (R \cup S)$ **AND** $t \in (R \cup T)$.

Sub-case analysis:
- If $t \in R$, then $t \in R \cup (S \cap T)$ trivially. ✓
- If $t \notin R$, then from $t \in (R \cup S)$ we get $t \in S$, and from $t \in (R \cup T)$ we get $t \in T$. Therefore, $t \in (S \cap T)$, which means $t \in R \cup (S \cap T)$. ✓

Hence, $(R \cup S) \cap (R \cup T) \subseteq R \cup (S \cap T)$. ✓

Since both inclusions hold, we conclude:

$$R \cup (S \cap T) = (R \cup S) \cap (R \cup T) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Significance in Query Optimization:**
This distributive law allows the query optimizer to **rearrange the order of operations** in a complex query.
