### 1.3.3 Date and Time Data Types

| ANSI | MySQL | Oracle | SQL Server |
|---|---|---|---|
| `DATE` | `DATE` | `DATE` | `DATE` |
| `TIME` | `TIME` | — (use `INTERVAL`) | `TIME` |
| `TIMESTAMP` | `TIMESTAMP` | `TIMESTAMP` | `DATETIME` |
| — | `DATETIME` | `DATE` | `DATETIME2` |
| — | `YEAR` | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Format:** `DATE` is conventionally stored as **`YYYY-MM-DD`** (ISO 8601). KTU questions often test format conversions using `TO_CHAR()` / `TO_DATE()` (Oracle) or `DATE_FORMAT()` (MySQL).

### 1.3.4 Other Specialized Types

- `ENUM('M','F','O')` — restricts column to a predefined list.
- `JSON` — stores JSON documents (MySQL 5.7+, PostgreSQL, SQL Server 2016+).
- `UUID` — stores 128-bit universally unique identifiers.
- `XML`, `GEOMETRY`, `SPATIAL` — vendor-specific extensions.

---

## 1.4 Data Definition Language (DDL) — The `CREATE` Family

The `CREATE` statement in DDL is used to construct **database objects**. The most frequently tested objects in KTU are:

1. `CREATE DATABASE`
2. `CREATE TABLE`
3. `CREATE VIEW`
4. `CREATE INDEX`
5. `CREATE SCHEMA`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auto-Commit Property:** DDL commands in most RDBMS (Oracle, MySQL) are **auto-committed**. Once you run `CREATE TABLE`, the schema is permanently saved — you cannot roll it back with a `ROLLBACK` command. This is a frequently tested conceptual question.

---

## 1.5 `CREATE DATABASE` — Syntax & Semantics

**Syntax (ANSI Standard):**

```sql
CREATE DATABASE database_name;
```

**Examples:**

```sql
CREATE DATABASE KTU_University;
```

**With explicit character set and collation (MySQL):**

```sql
CREATE DATABASE KTU_University
  CHARACTER SET utf8mb4
  COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci;
```

> [!NOTE]
> `COLLATE` defines the **sort order and character comparison rules** (e.g., case-insensitive vs case-sensitive). For Kerala university student name records, `utf8mb4_unicode_ci` is recommended for full Unicode support (including Malayalam script).

---

## 1.6 `CREATE TABLE` — The Heart of DDL

This is the **most heavily tested DDL command** in KTU board examinations. The complete general syntax is:

```sql
CREATE TABLE table_name (
    column1 datatype [column_constraint],
    column2 datatype [column_constraint],
    ...
    [table_constraint]
);
```

### Worked Example — University Student Database

```sql
CREATE TABLE Student (
    RegNo        VARCHAR(15)    PRIMARY KEY,
    Name         VARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,
    Branch       CHAR(4)        NOT NULL,
    DOB          DATE,
    CGPA         DECIMAL(4,2)   CHECK (CGPA BETWEEN 0 AND 10),
    Email        VARCHAR(100)   UNIQUE,
    AdmissionDt  DATE           DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE
);
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the schema above:** A `Student` has a registration number (PK, ≤15 chars), full name (required, ≤50 chars), branch code (exactly 4 chars — e.g., `CSEB`, `ECSA`), date of birth (optional), CGPA (decimal with up to 2 decimal places, must be 0–10), unique email, and an admission date that defaults to today if not supplied.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

---

## 2.1 SQL Command Classification — The Five Pillars

A KTU favorite question is: *"Classify the following commands: `CREATE`, `INSERT`, `SELECT`, `GRANT`, `COMMIT`."*

| Sub-Language | Abbreviation | Purpose | Commands | Auto-Commit? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data Definition Language | DDL | Define schema | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`, `RENAME` | ✅ Yes |
| Data Manipulation Language | DML | Manipulate rows | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`, `MERGE` | ❌ No (Transactional) |
| Data Query Language | DQL | Retrieve data | `SELECT` | ❌ No |
| Data Control Language | DCL | Permissions | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` | ✅ Yes |
| Transaction Control Language | TCL | Transaction boundaries | `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`, `SET TRANSACTION` | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU valuation:** If you mark `SELECT` as a DML command (because it "manipulates output"), you lose 1 mark. The correct classification is **DQL** (a sub-branch of DML in older textbooks, but standalone in modern KTU 2024 syllabus).

---

## 2.2 The Six SQL Constraints — Complete Reference

Constraints enforce **business rules at the schema level** (i.e., before any data is even inserted). The KTU board loves testing these in 7-mark sub-parts.

| # | Constraint | Syntax Placement | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `NOT NULL` | Column-level | Disallows empty values | `Name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL` |
| 2 | `UNIQUE` | Both | All values must differ (allows multiple NULLs in ANSI) | `Email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE` |
| 3 | `PRIMARY KEY` | Both | `NOT NULL` + `UNIQUE`; only one per table | `RegNo VARCHAR(15) PRIMARY KEY` |
| 4 | `FOREIGN KEY` | Both | Enforces referential integrity | `FOREIGN KEY (DeptID) REFERENCES Dept(ID)` |
| 5 | `CHECK` | Both | Custom boolean condition | `CHECK (CGPA >= 0 AND CGPA <= 10)` |
| 6 | `DEFAULT` | Column-level | Auto-fills value if none given | `Status CHAR(1) DEFAULT 'A'` |

### 2.2.1 Column-Level vs Table-Level Constraints

A **column-level constraint** is declared inline with the column definition:

```sql
CREATE TABLE Dept (
    ID    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name  VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL UNIQUE
);
```

A **table-level constraint** is declared after all columns:

```sql
CREATE TABLE Course (
    CCode   CHAR(6),
    DeptID  INT,
    Title   VARCHAR(100),
    PRIMARY KEY (CCode),
    FOREIGN KEY (DeptID) REFERENCES Dept(ID)
);
```

> [!WARNING]
> **When MUST you use table-level form?** When the constraint involves **two or more columns** (a *composite key*). For example, an enrollment table where `(RegNo, CourseCode, Sem)` together form the primary key — you cannot declare this at column level.

---

## 2.3 Referential Integrity & Foreign Keys — The Deep Dive

A **Foreign Key (FK)** in table `R` (the *referencing* or *child* table) must match an existing value in the **Primary Key (or UNIQUE column)** of table `S` (the *referenced* or *parent* table), OR be `NULL` (subject to constraint definition).

**Cascading Actions (MySQL/Oracle/SQL Server):**

| Action | Behavior on Parent UPDATE/DELETE |
|---|---|
| `ON DELETE CASCADE` | Child rows are **automatically deleted** |
| `ON DELETE SET NULL` | Child FK is **set to NULL** |
| `ON DELETE RESTRICT` | **Refuses** the parent change (default in most DBs) |
| `ON DELETE NO ACTION` | Same as `RESTRICT` (deferred check in ANSI) |
| `ON UPDATE CASCADE` | Child FK value **automatically updated** |

**Example:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Enrollment (
    EnrollID    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    RegNo       VARCHAR(15),
    CourseCode  CHAR(6),
    Sem         INT,
    Grade       CHAR(2),
    FOREIGN KEY (RegNo) REFERENCES Student(RegNo)
        ON DELETE CASCADE
        ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (CourseCode) REFERENCES Course(CCode)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT
);
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world KTU application:** If a student is deleted from the `Student` table, all their `Enrollment` records are automatically removed (CASCADE). But if you try to delete a `Course` that has enrollments, the DB blocks it (RESTRICT) — preventing data loss.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Data Types & DDL Syntax

### 📋 Quick Reference Card

| Concept | Syntax Template | Mandatory Exam Points |
|---|---|---|
| Create DB | `CREATE DATABASE db_name;` | Auto-committed, cannot rollback |
| Create Table | `CREATE TABLE t (col dtype [constr]);` | Constraints enforce data integrity |
| CHAR vs VARCHAR | `CHAR(n)` fixed, `VARCHAR(n)` variable | Use CHAR for fixed-length codes (e.g., state codes) |
| DECIMAL precision | `DECIMAL(p,s)` | p = total digits, s = digits after decimal |
| Primary Key | `PRIMARY KEY` | Implicit `NOT NULL` + `UNIQUE`, one per table |
| Foreign Key | `FOREIGN KEY (col) REFERENCES parent(col)` | Must match parent PK or UNIQUE value |
| CHECK | `CHECK (condition)` | Rejected if condition is FALSE; NULL passes |
| DEFAULT | `DEFAULT value` | Used when INSERT omits the column |
| Composite PK | `PRIMARY KEY (col1, col2)` | **Must** be table-level form |
| DROP vs TRUNCATE | `DROP` removes structure; `TRUNCATE` empties data | TRUNCATE is faster, resets auto-increment |

### 📋 Common Error Codes (KTU Practical Exam Favorite)

| SQLSTATE | Error | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| `23000` | Integrity constraint violation | Violates UNIQUE, FK, or CHECK |
| `42000` | Syntax error | Malformed SQL |
| `42S02` | Base table not found | Typo in table name |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems:** `DECIMAL(15,2)` for currency to avoid floating-point errors.
- **IoT Sensor Logs:** `TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` to auto-stamp readings.
- **University ERPs (KTU Project Context):** `VARCHAR` for names, `CHAR(4)` for branch codes, composite PK for enrollment junction tables.
- **E-Commerce:** `ENUM` for order status (`'PLACED'`, `'SHIPPED'`, `'DELIVERED'`).
- **Healthcare:** `CHECK` constraints on medical parameters (`CHECK (BP_systolic BETWEEN 50 AND 250)`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Choosing the **wrong data type** is the #1 cause of performance issues in production databases. A `VARCHAR(4000)` where `VARCHAR(50)` suffices wastes disk, slows index B-tree traversal, and increases I/O. *Measure twice, declare once.*

# 🔬 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Schema Design from a Business Specification

> **Problem Statement (typical KTU 7-mark Part-A):**
> *"Design a database schema for a Library Management System. The system must track books, members, and issue/return transactions. A member can issue multiple books; each book can be issued by only one member at a time. Each book has a unique ISBN. Members have a library card number. Issue records must preserve the issue date and a return date (which can be NULL until returned)."*

### Step 1 — Identify Entities

> An *entity* is a real-world object with independent existence. From the specification:
> - **Book** (the library's inventory)
> - **Member** (the patrons)
> - **Issue** (the transaction linking Book ↔ Member)

### Step 2 — Identify Attributes & Keys

| Entity | Attributes | Primary Key | Foreign Keys |
|---|---|---|---|
| Book | ISBN (PK), Title, Author, Publisher, YearPublished, CopiesTotal | `ISBN` | — |
| Member | CardNo (PK), Name, Phone, Email, JoinDate | `CardNo` | — |
| Issue | IssueID (PK), ISBN, CardNo, IssueDate, ReturnDate | `IssueID` | `ISBN → Book`, `CardNo → Member` |

### Step 3 — Determine Data Types

| Column | Chosen Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `ISBN` | `CHAR(13)` | ISBN-13 is always exactly 13 digits — **fixed length is optimal** |
| `Title` | `VARCHAR(200)` | Book titles vary widely; some are short ("It"), some long |
| `YearPublished` | `INT` | Integer years (e.g., 2024) |
| `Phone` | `VARCHAR(15)` | Country codes, leading zeros, hyphens — never numeric |
| `Email` | `VARCHAR(100)` | Variable length, mostly short |
| `IssueDate` | `DATE` | Day-level granularity suffices |
| `ReturnDate` | `DATE` (nullable) | NULL until book is returned |
| `IssueID` | `INT AUTO_INCREMENT` | Surrogate key, auto-generated |

### Step 4 — Identify Constraints

- `ISBN` → `PRIMARY KEY` (uniqueness)
- `CardNo` → `PRIMARY KEY` (uniqueness)
- `Email` → `UNIQUE NOT NULL`
- `Phone` → `NOT NULL`
- `YearPublished` → `CHECK (YearPublished >= 1450 AND YearPublished <= YEAR(CURDATE()))` (Gutenberg press year to current year)
- `CopiesTotal` → `CHECK (CopiesTotal >= 1)`
- `ReturnDate` → `CHECK (ReturnDate IS NULL OR ReturnDate >= IssueDate)`

### Step 5 — Write the Complete DDL

```sql
CREATE TABLE Book (
    ISBN           CHAR(13)       PRIMARY KEY,
    Title          VARCHAR(200)   NOT NULL,
    Author         VARCHAR(100)   NOT NULL,
    Publisher      VARCHAR(100),
    YearPublished  INT            CHECK (YearPublished >= 1450),
    CopiesTotal    INT            DEFAULT 1  CHECK (CopiesTotal >= 1)
);

CREATE TABLE Member (
    CardNo     VARCHAR(10)    PRIMARY KEY,
    Name       VARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,
    Phone      VARCHAR(15)    NOT NULL,
    Email      VARCHAR(100)   NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    JoinDate   DATE           DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE)
);

CREATE TABLE Issue (
    IssueID      INT           PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    ISBN         CHAR(13)      NOT NULL,
    CardNo       VARCHAR(10)   NOT NULL,
    IssueDate    DATE          NOT NULL DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    ReturnDate   DATE,
    FOREIGN KEY (ISBN)   REFERENCES Book(ISBN)     ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    FOREIGN KEY (CardNo) REFERENCES Member(CardNo) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    CHECK (ReturnDate IS NULL OR ReturnDate >= IssueDate)
);
```

### Step 6 — Verification (Self-Check)

- ✅ All entities present? Yes — Book, Member, Issue.
- ✅ All relationships encoded? Yes — Issue FKs to both Book and Member.
- ✅ Cardinality captured? Yes — a single Issue row ties ONE book to ONE member.
- ✅ Business rules enforced? Yes — YearPublished range, ReturnDate logic.
- ✅ NULL handling? Yes — ReturnDate and Publisher are nullable.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Storage Size Calculation

> **Problem (KTU 3-mark type):** *"Calculate the maximum storage size (in bytes) required for one row of the following table on a MySQL InnoDB system with `utf8mb4` (4 bytes/char):"*
>
> ```sql
> CREATE TABLE Exam (
>     RegNo     CHAR(10),
>     Name      VARCHAR(50),
>     Branch    CHAR(4),
>     Marks     INT,
>     Grade     CHAR(2)
> );
> ```

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Identify per-column size:**

| Column | Type | Length (chars) | Bytes/char (utf8mb4) | Inline bytes | Variable-length prefix |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `RegNo` | `CHAR(10)` | 10 | 4 | $10 \times 4 = 40$ | 0 (fixed) |
| `Name` | `VARCHAR(50)` | up to 50 | 4 | max $50 \times 4 = 200$ | 1 or 2 bytes (length) |
| `Branch` | `CHAR(4)` | 4 | 4 | $4 \times 4 = 16$ | 0 (fixed) |
| `Marks` | `INT` | — | — | **4 bytes** | 0 |
| `Grade` | `CHAR(2)` | 2 | 4 | $2 \times 4 = 8$ | 0 (fixed) |

**Step 2 — Sum fixed-size columns (no variable-length prefix):**

$$S_{fixed} = 40 + 16 + 4 + 8 = 68 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Add variable-length column max size:**

$$S_{variable} = 200 \text{ bytes (Name) } + 2 \text{ bytes (length prefix for > 255 chars would be 2; for ≤255, 1 byte)}$$

$$S_{variable} = 200 + 1 = 201 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Total maximum row size:**

$$S_{max} = S_{fixed} + S_{variable} = 68 + 201 = 269 \text{ bytes}$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$\boxed{S_{max} = 269 \text{ bytes per row}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Note:** KTU typically asks for *maximum* size in such problems. If asked for *typical* size, you must assume an average Name length (e.g., 20 chars) and recalculate.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Constraint Failure Diagnosis

> **Problem (KTU 4-mark type):** *"The following INSERT fails. Identify the violated constraint(s) and rewrite the correct statement."*
>
> ```sql
> CREATE TABLE Product (
>     PID    INT PRIMARY KEY,
>     Pname  VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
>     Price  DECIMAL(6,2) CHECK (Price > 0),
>     Qty    INT DEFAULT 0
> );
> ```
>
> ```sql
> INSERT INTO Product VALUES (101, 'Pen', -50.00, 10);
> ```

### Step-by-Step Diagnosis

**Step 1 — Parse the INSERT:** Values provided: `(101, 'Pen', -50.00, 10)`.

**Step 2 — Check each column against its constraint:**

| Column | Value | Constraint | Pass? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `PID` | `101` | PRIMARY KEY (unique, not null) | ✅ First row, passes |
| `Pname` | `'Pen'` | NOT NULL | ✅ Not null |
| `Price` | `-50.00` | `CHECK (Price > 0)` | ❌ **FAILS** — negative value |
| `Qty` | `10` | DEFAULT 0 | ✅ Positive integer |

**Step 3 — Identify the violated constraint:** The `CHECK` constraint on `Price`.

**Step 4 — Correct the statement:**

```sql
INSERT INTO Product VALUES (101, 'Pen', 50.00, 10);
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Constraint violations are *intentional* — they protect data integrity at the schema level, *before* application logic runs. This is a key advantage of declarative SQL over imperative code.

# 🎯 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between `CHAR` and `VARCHAR` data types in SQL. When would you prefer `CHAR` over `VARCHAR`?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `CHAR(n)` | `VARCHAR(n)` |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | **Fixed length** — always `n` characters | **Variable length** — only as many characters as stored |
| Padding | Pads with blanks to fill `n` | No padding |
| Performance | Slightly faster for fixed-size data | Slightly slower due to length prefix |
| Example | `CHAR(10)` storing "KOTTAYAM" → uses 10 bytes | `VARCHAR(10)` storing "KOTTAYAM" → uses 8 bytes |

**When to prefer `CHAR`:** For columns where all values have **uniform length**, such as:
- Branch codes (`'CSEB'`, `'ECSA'`) → `CHAR(4)`
- State codes (`'KER'`, `'TN'`) → `CHAR(3)`
- ISO country codes (`'IN'`, `'US'`) → `CHAR(2)`
- ISBN-13 numbers → `CHAR(13)`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Allocation:** 1.5 marks for the difference table + 1.5 marks for the application example.

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Choice

### **Question A (Option 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Design the schema for a **Hospital Management System** with entities `Patient`, `Doctor`, and `Appointment`. Write the complete `CREATE TABLE` statements with appropriate data types, primary keys, foreign keys, and at least one `CHECK` and one `UNIQUE` constraint. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between `DROP TABLE`, `TRUNCATE TABLE`, and `DELETE FROM` statements. Mention which is a DDL command and which is a DML command. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Entities & Attributes:**

| Entity | Attributes |
|---|---|
| Patient | `PID` (PK), `Name`, `Age`, `Gender`, `Phone`, `Disease` |
| Doctor | `DID` (PK), `Name`, `Specialization`, `Salary`, `Email` |
| Appointment | `AppID` (PK), `PID` (FK), `DID` (FK), `AppDate`, `Fee` |

**Step 2 — DDL Statements:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Patient (
    PID      INT            PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    Name     VARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,
    Age      INT            CHECK (Age > 0 AND Age < 130),
    Gender   CHAR(1)        CHECK (Gender IN ('M','F','O')),
    Phone    VARCHAR(15)    NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    Disease  VARCHAR(100)
);

CREATE TABLE Doctor (
    DID            INT           PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    Name           VARCHAR(50)   NOT NULL,
    Specialization VARCHAR(50)   NOT NULL,
    Salary         DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (Salary > 0),
    Email          VARCHAR(100)  UNIQUE
);

CREATE TABLE Appointment (
    AppID   INT         PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    PID     INT         NOT NULL,
    DID     INT         NOT NULL,
    AppDate DATE        NOT NULL,
    Fee     DECIMAL(8,2) CHECK (Fee >= 0),
    FOREIGN KEY (PID) REFERENCES Patient(PID) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (DID) REFERENCES Doctor(DID)  ON DELETE RESTRICT
);
```

**Justification of choices:**
- `AUTO_INCREMENT` for surrogate keys → avoids manual ID management.
- `CHECK (Age > 0)` → prevents invalid negative ages.
- `CHECK (Gender IN ('M','F','O'))` → enforces valid categories.
- `UNIQUE` on `Phone` and `Email` → prevents duplicate contact records.
- `ON DELETE CASCADE` for Patient → if a patient record is removed, their appointments are auto-cleaned.
- `ON DELETE RESTRICT` for Doctor → cannot remove a doctor who has appointments (data preservation).

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

| Feature | `DROP TABLE` | `TRUNCATE TABLE` | `DELETE FROM` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | **DDL** | **DDL** | **DML** |
| **Effect** | Removes **structure + data** permanently | Removes **all rows** but keeps structure | Removes **specific rows** (or all if no WHERE) |
| **WHERE clause** | ❌ Not allowed | ❌ Not allowed | ✅ Allowed |
| **Rollback?** | ❌ Cannot rollback | ❌ Cannot rollback (in most RDBMS) | ✅ Can rollback |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Very fast | Slower (row-by-row) |
| **Triggers fired?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Resets AUTO_INCREMENT?** | — | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Frees disk space?** | ✅ Fully | ✅ Fully | ❌ Marks as deleted only |

**Examples:**

```sql
DROP TABLE Appointment;          -- Removes the table entirely
TRUNCATE TABLE Appointment;      -- Empties the table, keeps structure
DELETE FROM Appointment;         -- Deletes all rows, logs each deletion
DELETE FROM Appointment 
       WHERE AppDate < '2020-01-01';  -- Conditional delete
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly classify `TRUNCATE` as a DML command because it deals with data. The correct answer is **DDL** — it modifies the schema (resets the high-water mark and auto-increment counter), not just the row data. Losing this distinction costs **1.5 marks**.

---

### **Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the following constraints in SQL: `PRIMARY KEY`, `FOREIGN KEY`, `UNIQUE`, `CHECK`, `NOT NULL`, and `DEFAULT`. Mention which can be declared only at the table level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following two tables. Write SQL `CREATE` statements for both and identify the foreign key relationship. Explain the referential integrity violations that could occur if the constraints are not properly enforced. **(7 marks)**

- **Department** (`DeptID` INT, `DeptName` VARCHAR(50), `HOD` VARCHAR(50))
- **Employee** (`EmpID` INT, `EmpName` VARCHAR(50), `Salary` DECIMAL(10,2), `DeptID` INT)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Complete constraint table with examples:**

```sql
-- 1. PRIMARY KEY (NOT NULL + UNIQUE, only ONE per table)
CREATE TABLE Dept (
    DeptID   INT         PRIMARY KEY,
    DeptName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

-- 2. FOREIGN KEY (Referential integrity)
CREATE TABLE Emp (
    EmpID  INT PRIMARY KEY,
    DeptID INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (DeptID) REFERENCES Dept(DeptID)
);

-- 3. UNIQUE (No duplicates, allows multiple NULLs)
CREATE TABLE Users (
    UserID  INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Email   VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE
);

-- 4. CHECK (Custom boolean validation)
CREATE TABLE Course (
    Code    CHAR(6) PRIMARY KEY,
    Credits INT CHECK (Credits BETWEEN 1 AND 5)
);

-- 5. NOT NULL (Mandatory value)
CREATE TABLE Book (
    ISBN  CHAR(13) PRIMARY KEY,
    Title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL
);

-- 6. DEFAULT (Auto-fills when omitted)
CREATE TABLE Orders (
    OrderID    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    OrderDate  DATE DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    Status     CHAR(1) DEFAULT 'P'
);
```

**Constraints that MUST be declared at table-level:**

| Constraint | Reason for Table-Level Only |
|---|---|
| **Composite PRIMARY KEY** | Spans multiple columns: `PRIMARY KEY (Col1, Col2)` |
| **Composite FOREIGN KEY** | References a composite key: `FOREIGN KEY (A, B) REFERENCES T(X, Y)` |
| **Composite UNIQUE** | Multi-column uniqueness: `UNIQUE (FirstName, LastName, DOB)` |
| **Constraint with multiple columns** | Any constraint involving more than one column |

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**DDL Statements:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Department (
    DeptID    INT          PRIMARY KEY,
    DeptName  VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    HOD       VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE Employee (
    EmpID    INT            PRIMARY KEY,
    EmpName  VARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,
    Salary   DECIMAL(10,2)  CHECK (Salary > 0),
    DeptID   INT            NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (DeptID) REFERENCES Department(DeptID)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT
        ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
```

**Referential Integrity Violations Prevented:**

| Violation Type | Scenario | Without FK | With FK |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Orphan Insert** | Inserting an employee with `DeptID = 999` (non-existent) | Allowed → dangling reference | ❌ Blocked with error 23000 |
| **Parent Deletion** | Deleting a Department that has 5 employees | Allowed → orphans remain | ❌ Blocked by `RESTRICT` |
| **Parent Update** | Renumbering a `DeptID` from 10 → 20 | Child rows keep old `DeptID = 10` → inconsistency | ✅ `CASCADE` updates all children automatically |
| **NULL FK** | Employee with no department | Allowed if no constraint | Allowed only if FK is nullable |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** When asked about referential integrity, students often mention only the *orphan insert* case. They forget the *parent update* and *parent delete* cases. All three are part of referential integrity. **2 marks are reserved for this comprehensive coverage** in 7-mark questions.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section. Read it 5 minutes before the exam.**

### ✅ Conceptual Mastery Checklist

- [ ] SQL has **5 sub-languages**: DDL, DML, DQL, DCL, TCL — and I can classify any command correctly.
- [ ] `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE` are **DDL** (auto-committed).
- [ ] `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` are **DML** (transactional, can be rolled back).
- [ ] `SELECT` is **DQL** (not DML, in modern KTU syllabus).
- [ ] `CHAR(n)` is fixed-length; `VARCHAR(n)` is variable-length — use `CHAR` for codes, `VARCHAR` for names.
- [ ] `DECIMAL(p,s)` — `p` is total digits, `s` is decimal digits.
- [ ] `PRIMARY KEY` = `NOT NULL` + `UNIQUE`; only one per table.
- [ ] `FOREIGN KEY` enforces referential integrity between two tables.
- [ ] `CHECK` constraint validates a boolean condition; `NULL` values bypass it.
- [ ] `DEFAULT` provides a fallback value during INSERT.
- [ ] Composite keys (multi-column) **must** be table-level constraints.
- [ ] Cascading actions: `CASCADE`, `SET NULL`, `RESTRICT`, `NO ACTION`.
- [ ] `DROP` removes structure; `TRUNCATE` removes data but keeps structure; `DELETE` removes rows conditionally.

### ✅ Syntax Templates to Memorize

```sql
-- Database
CREATE DATABASE db_name;

-- Table
CREATE TABLE table_name (
    col1 datatype [constraint],
    col2 datatype [constraint],
    [PRIMARY KEY (col1, col2)],
    [FOREIGN KEY (col) REFERENCES parent(col)],
    [CHECK (condition)]
);

-- Common Constraints
NOT NULL | UNIQUE | PRIMARY KEY | 
FOREIGN KEY ... REFERENCES ... |
CHECK (...) | DEFAULT value
```

### ✅ Common KTU Traps to Avoid

| Trap | Correct Understanding |
|---|---|
| "`SELECT` is DML" | **No — it's DQL** in the 2024 syllabus |
| "`TRUNCATE` is DML" | **No — it's DDL** (resets high-water mark) |
| "`CHAR(50)` uses less storage than `VARCHAR(50)`" | **For values shorter than 50 — yes. For values at 50 — same.** |
| "You can have multiple PRIMARY KEYs" | **No — only ONE per table** (it can be composite) |
| "FOREIGN KEY must reference a PRIMARY KEY" | **No — it can reference any UNIQUE column** |
| "CHECK constraint blocks NULL" | **No — NULL passes CHECK (treated as UNKNOWN)** |
| "`DROP TABLE` can be rolled back" | **No — DDL is auto-committed** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Wisdom:** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *application-based* SQL questions (e.g., "design a schema for XYZ system") over pure syntax memorization. Master the **six constraints**, the **cascading rules**, and the **data type selection logic**, and you can answer virtually any Module 2 question.

---

*End of Module 2 Topic Notes — SQL Data Types & DDL CREATE*
*Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned | Outcome-Based Education*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Alter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **ALTER** is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** command in Structured Query Language (SQL) used to **modify the schema (structure)** of an existing database object such as a table, view, index, or database itself, **without destroying the existing data** stored within that object. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, ALTER falls under DDL operations that alter the *intension* (schema) while preserving the *extension* (current tuples/rows) of the relation.

The SQL `ALTER` statement is issued to perform structural modifications on a **relation schema** $R(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n)$ producing a new schema $R'(A_1, \ldots, A_{n+k})$ or $R'(A_1, \ldots, A_{n-1})$ through column additions, deletions, modifications, renaming, or constraint manipulation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of ALTER as RENOVATING an existing house.**
>
> - Your **DATABASE** = The entire colony / township
> - Your **TABLE** = A single house in that colony
> - Your **COLUMNS** = Rooms inside the house
> - Your **ROWS** = Furniture and belongings inside the rooms
> - **CREATE** = Building a brand new house from scratch
> - **ALTER** = Renovating an already-built house (add a new room, demolish an old room, rename the door, install a new lock)
> - **DROP** = Demolishing the entire house

When you use `ALTER`, you are **NOT demolishing the house (DROP)** and **NOT building from scratch (CREATE)**. You are **modifying the structure** while the family (data) continues to live inside.

### 1.3 Classification of ALTER Operations in SQL

```text
ALTER
 ├── ALTER TABLE (Most Frequently Used)
 │     ├── ADD column
 │     ├── DROP COLUMN
 │     ├── MODIFY / ALTER COLUMN
 │     ├── RENAME COLUMN / TABLE
 │     ├── ADD CONSTRAINT
 │     └── DROP CONSTRAINT
 ├── ALTER DATABASE
 ├── ALTER VIEW
 └── ALTER INDEX
```

### 1.4 Why ALTER Matters in Real-World Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> In enterprise systems (banking, e-commerce, healthcare), requirements evolve continuously. A customer table that started with 5 columns may need 2 new columns next quarter for regulatory compliance. `ALTER TABLE` allows live schema evolution without service downtime or data loss, making it indispensable for **agile database development**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Syntax Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Decomposition of ALTER TABLE

The `ALTER TABLE` statement can be understood as a **schema transformation operator** $\phi$ applied to a relation schema:

$$\phi: R(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n) \longrightarrow R'(A_1, \ldots, A_{n \pm k}, C_{new})$$

where:
- $R$ is the original relation (table)
- $A_i$ represents the existing attributes (columns)
- $A_{n \pm k}$ represents the addition or removal of $k$ attributes
- $C_{new}$ represents newly added constraints

### 2.2 The Six Core ALTER TABLE Sub-Operations

| Operation | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **ADD** | Introduce a new column or constraint into existing table | Very High |
| **DROP COLUMN** | Permanently remove a column and all its data | High |
| **MODIFY / ALTER COLUMN** | Change data type or size of existing column | High |
| **RENAME** | Change the name of a column or the table itself | Medium |
| **ADD CONSTRAINT** | Enforce a new integrity rule (PK, FK, UNIQUE, CHECK) | Very High |
| **DROP CONSTRAINT** | Remove an existing integrity rule | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** The most frequently tested operations are `ADD COLUMN`, `DROP COLUMN`, `MODIFY COLUMN`, `ADD CONSTRAINT`, and `DROP CONSTRAINT`. Renaming has minor weightage.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Operation — Engineering Rationale

- **Why ADD COLUMN?** To accommodate new business requirements (e.g., adding `email_verified BOOLEAN` to a users table).
- **Why DROP COLUMN?** To remove obsolete features and reclaim storage space.
- **Why MODIFY COLUMN?** To widen a field (e.g., changing `phone VARCHAR(10)` to `phone VARCHAR(15)` for international numbers).
- **Why ADD CONSTRAINT?** To enforce business rules that were previously un-enforced (e.g., making `email` UNIQUE after registration redesign).
- **Why DROP CONSTRAINT?** To relax a rule that was over-restrictive or causing application bugs.

### 2.4 Auto-Commit Nature of DDL Commands

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** `ALTER` is a **DDL command**, and therefore it is **auto-committed** in virtually all RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, SQL Server). This means **once executed, it CANNOT be rolled back using ROLLBACK**. Students frequently lose marks by mentioning ROLLBACK as a recovery option for ALTER.

### 2.5 Syntax Reference Cheat Sheet (Engine-Neutral)

| Operation | Generic SQL Syntax |
|---|---|
| Add Column | `ALTER TABLE table_name ADD column_name datatype [constraint];` |
| Drop Column | `ALTER TABLE table_name DROP COLUMN column_name;` |
| Modify Column (Standard SQL) | `ALTER TABLE table_name ALTER COLUMN column_name TYPE new_datatype;` |
| Modify Column (MySQL/Oracle flavor) | `ALTER TABLE table_name MODIFY column_name new_datatype;` |
| Rename Column | `ALTER TABLE table_name RENAME COLUMN old_name TO new_name;` |
| Rename Table | `ALTER TABLE old_table_name RENAME TO new_table_name;` |
| Add Constraint | `ALTER TABLE table_name ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name definition;` |
| Drop Constraint | `ALTER TABLE table_name DROP CONSTRAINT constraint_name;` |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Syntax Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU DBMS Module 2, "derivations" refer to exhaustive syntax breakdowns, semantic analysis, and effect-tracing on sample tables. Each ALTER statement is broken into its lexical tokens and analyzed for its effect on the schema and stored data.**

### 3.1 Worked Example Setup — Sample Base Table

Let us define a working relation `STUDENT` that we will use throughout this section.

```sql
CREATE TABLE STUDENT (
    roll_no   INTEGER       PRIMARY KEY,
    name      VARCHAR(50)   NOT NULL,
    age       INTEGER,
    dept      VARCHAR(20)
);
```

Initial schema state:
$$R_{STUDENT} = \{\text{roll\_no}, \text{name}, \text{age}, \text{dept}\}$$

### 3.2 DERIVATION 1 — Adding a New Column

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE STUDENT ADD email VARCHAR(100);
```

**Token-by-Token Analysis:**

```text
ALTER TABLE  →  DDL verb specifying schema modification on a table
STUDENT      →  Target relation (must already exist in the database)
ADD          →  Sub-operation keyword meaning "append new element"
email        →  New attribute name being introduced
VARCHAR(100) →  Data type with maximum length constraint
;            →  Statement terminator
```

**Effect on Schema:**
$$R_{STUDENT}^{new} = R_{STUDENT} \cup \{\text{email}\}$$

**Effect on Existing Rows:**
All previously stored rows will have the new `email` column populated with `NULL` by default, since no DEFAULT clause was specified.

**Mathematical Representation:**
$$\forall t \in \text{STUDENT}^{old}, \quad t^{new} = t \cup \{\langle \text{email}, \text{NULL} \rangle\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** If the `NOT NULL` constraint is added along with the new column, **the command will FAIL** if the table already contains rows, because NULL is the only default value and it violates NOT NULL. Students must specify a DEFAULT in such cases.

### 3.3 DERIVATION 2 — Adding a Column with DEFAULT Value

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE STUDENT ADD status VARCHAR(10) DEFAULT 'ACTIVE';
```

**Effect:** Every existing row now has `status = 'ACTIVE'`. New inserts will also default to `'ACTIVE'` unless overridden.

$$\forall t^{new}: t^{new}.\text{status} = \text{'ACTIVE'}$$

### 3.4 DERIVATION 3 — Dropping a Column

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE STUDENT DROP COLUMN age;
```

**Effect on Schema:**
$$R_{STUDENT}^{new} = R_{STUDENT} - \{\text{age}\}$$

**Effect on Data:** All values stored in the `age` column are **permanently erased** from the database.

> [!WARNING]
> **Irreversible Operation:** Most RDBMS do NOT support rollback of DDL. Always **backup data** before dropping columns in production.

### 3.5 DERIVATION 4 — Modifying an Existing Column's Data Type

**SQL Statement (Standard SQL / PostgreSQL style):**
```sql
ALTER TABLE STUDENT ALTER COLUMN name TYPE VARCHAR(100);
```

**SQL Statement (MySQL style):**
```sql
ALTER TABLE STUDENT MODIFY name VARCHAR(100);
```

**SQL Statement (Oracle style):**
```sql
ALTER TABLE STUDENT MODIFY (name VARCHAR(100));
```

**Effect:** The maximum allowed length of the `name` column expands from 50 to 100 characters. If existing data already exceeds the new limit, the command will **fail with a truncation error**.

**Condition for Successful Execution:**
$$\forall t \in \text{STUDENT}, \quad \text{LENGTH}(t.\text{name}) \leq 100$$

If this condition is violated, the RDBMS raises an error and the schema change is aborted.

### 3.6 DERIVATION 5 — Adding a PRIMARY KEY Constraint via ALTER

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE STUDENT ADD CONSTRAINT pk_student PRIMARY KEY (roll_no);
```

**Token Analysis:**

```text
ADD CONSTRAINT    →  Introduce a named integrity rule
pk_student        →  Constraint name (used later for DROP CONSTRAINT)
PRIMARY KEY       →  Integrity constraint type
(roll_no)         →  Column(s) participating in the constraint
```

**Validation Performed by RDBMS:**

1. Column `roll_no` must **not already** be PRIMARY KEY (else: duplicate constraint error).
2. All values in `roll_no` must be **NOT NULL** (else: violation).
3. All values in `roll_no` must be **unique** (else: violation).

$$\forall t_i, t_j \in \text{STUDENT} \text{ with } i \neq j: t_i.\text{roll\_no} \neq t_j.\text{roll\_no}$$

### 3.7 DERIVATION 6 — Adding a FOREIGN KEY Constraint via ALTER

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE ENROLLMENT
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_student
FOREIGN KEY (roll_no) REFERENCES STUDENT(roll_no);
```

**Effect:** Creates a referential integrity link. Future INSERTs into ENROLLMENT with a `roll_no` not present in STUDENT will be **rejected** by the RDBMS.

$$\forall t \in \text{ENROLLMENT}: \exists s \in \text{STUDENT} \text{ such that } t.\text{roll\_no} = s.\text{roll\_no}$$

### 3.8 DERIVATION 7 — Dropping a Constraint

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE STUDENT DROP CONSTRAINT pk_student;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **MySQL Pitfall:** MySQL does NOT support `DROP CONSTRAINT` directly for PRIMARY KEY. The correct MySQL syntax is: `ALTER TABLE STUDENT DROP PRIMARY KEY;`

### 3.9 DERIVATION 8 — Renaming a Column

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE STUDENT RENAME COLUMN dept TO department;
```

**Effect:** The attribute name `dept` is globally renamed to `department` across the schema. All stored rows are untouched in terms of data, only their header label changes.

### 3.10 DERIVATION 9 — Renaming a Table

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE STUDENT RENAME TO STUDENT_MASTER;
```

**Effect:** The relation `STUDENT` is renamed to `STUDENT_MASTER`. All foreign keys, views, and stored procedures referencing `STUDENT` may need to be updated depending on the RDBMS.

### 3.11 Complete Effect-Trace Matrix

| Operation | Schema Changed | Data Preserved | Auto-Commit | Reversible via ROLLBACK |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ADD column | Yes | Yes (with NULL/DEFAULT) | Yes | No |
| DROP COLUMN | Yes | Partial (that column lost) | Yes | No |
| MODIFY COLUMN | Yes | Yes (if compatible) | Yes | No |
| RENAME | Yes (name only) | Yes | Yes | No |
| ADD CONSTRAINT | Yes | Validated first | Yes | No |
| DROP CONSTRAINT | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of ALTER Command

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode["ALTER Command Family"]
    rootNode --> tabNode["ALTER TABLE"]
    rootNode --> dbNode["ALTER DATABASE"]
    rootNode --> vwNode["ALTER VIEW"]
    rootNode --> ixNode["ALTER INDEX"]

    tabNode --> addCol["ADD COLUMN"]
    tabNode --> dropCol["DROP COLUMN"]
    tabNode --> modCol["MODIFY / ALTER COLUMN"]
    tabNode --> renCol["RENAME COLUMN"]
    tabNode --> renTab["RENAME TABLE"]
    tabNode --> addCon["ADD CONSTRAINT"]
    tabNode --> dropCon["DROP CONSTRAINT"]

    addCon --> addPK["PRIMARY KEY"]
    addCon --> addFK["FOREIGN KEY"]
    addCon --> addUQ["UNIQUE"]
    addCon --> addCK["CHECK"]
    addCon --> addNN["NOT NULL"]
```

### 4.2 Execution Flow of ALTER TABLE Statement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["SQL Parser receives ALTER TABLE statement"]
    parseNode["Lexical and Syntax Analysis"]
    semNode["Semantic Check: Does target table exist?"]
    lockNode["Acquire Exclusive Lock on Table"]
    valNode["Validate Against Existing Data"]
    schemaNode["Modify System Catalog / Data Dictionary"]
    dataNode["Apply Changes to Base Table and Existing Rows"]
    logNode["Write to Transaction Log Auto-Commit"]
    doneNode["Success: Schema Updated"]

    startNode --> parseNode
    parseNode --> semNode
    semNode -- Yes --> lockNode
    semNode -- No --> errNode["Error: Table not found"]
    lockNode --> valNode
    valNode -- Valid --> schemaNode
    valNode -- Invalid --> failNode["Error: Constraint Violation"]
    schemaNode --> dataNode
    dataNode --> logNode
    logNode --> doneNode
```

### 4.3 Schema Evolution Visualization Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["STUDENT Schema V1: roll_no, name, age, dept"]
    s2["ALTER ADD email"]
    s3["STUDENT Schema V2: roll_no, name, age, dept, email"]
    s4["ALTER DROP age"]
    s5["STUDENT Schema V3: roll_no, name, dept, email"]
    s6["ALTER MODIFY name TYPE VARCHAR 100"]
    s7["STUDENT Schema V4: roll_no, name, dept, email with widened name"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6 --> s7
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the purpose of the ALTER TABLE command in SQL? List any four sub-operations of ALTER TABLE with one-line descriptions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `ALTER TABLE` command is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** statement used to modify the structure of an existing table in a database without losing the stored data. It allows schema evolution such as adding new columns, removing obsolete columns, changing data types, and enforcing new integrity constraints. Unlike `CREATE TABLE`, it does not create a new table; unlike `DROP TABLE`, it does not destroy existing data.

Four sub-operations are:

1. **ADD** — Adds a new column or constraint to the existing table.
2. **DROP COLUMN** — Removes an existing column permanently from the table.
3. **MODIFY / ALTER COLUMN** — Changes the data type or size of an existing column.
4. **ADD CONSTRAINT** — Enforces a new integrity rule such as PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, UNIQUE, or CHECK on the table.

*(Plus optional 5th: RENAME COLUMN / TABLE for extra credit)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the ALTER command classified as a DDL statement? Is it auto-committed? Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The ALTER command is classified as a **DDL (Data Definition Language)** statement because it operates on the **schema (structure)** of database objects rather than on the data (rows) within them. It modifies the **intension** of a relation — that is, the table definition stored in the system catalog or data dictionary.

Yes, ALTER is **auto-committed** in virtually all standard RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, SQL Server). This means that once an ALTER statement is successfully executed, the change is **permanently written to the database** and **cannot be rolled back** using the `ROLLBACK` command. This is because DDL operations implicitly trigger a `COMMIT` before and after execution to maintain schema-data consistency.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q. (a)** Consider the following `EMPLOYEE` table:

```sql
CREATE TABLE EMPLOYEE (
    emp_id     INTEGER,
    ename      VARCHAR(30),
    salary     DECIMAL(10,2),
    dept_no    INTEGER
);
```

Write SQL `ALTER TABLE` statements to perform the following modifications **step by step** in the order given:

1. Add a new column `email VARCHAR(100)`.
2. Add a NOT NULL constraint on `ename`.
3. Make `emp_id` the PRIMARY KEY.
4. Add a FOREIGN KEY constraint on `dept_no` referencing `DEPARTMENT(dept_no)`.
5. Modify the `ename` column to width `VARCHAR(60)`.
6. Drop the `salary` column.
7. Rename the table to `EMP_MASTER`.

Explain each statement briefly. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** Discuss the difference between `DROP TABLE`, `TRUNCATE TABLE`, and `ALTER TABLE DROP COLUMN` with respect to:
(i) Effect on schema, (ii) Effect on data, (iii) Recoverability, (iv) Auto-commit nature. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step ALTER Statements:**

**(1) Add new column `email`:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE EMPLOYEE ADD email VARCHAR(100);
```
*Effect:* A new attribute `email` is appended. All existing rows receive `NULL` in this column.

**(2) Add NOT NULL constraint on `ename`:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE EMPLOYEE MODIFY ename VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL;
```
*Effect:* Future inserts must provide a value for `ename`. If existing rows have NULL in `ename`, the command will **fail**.

**(3) Make `emp_id` the PRIMARY KEY:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE EMPLOYEE ADD CONSTRAINT pk_emp PRIMARY KEY (emp_id);
```
*Effect:* Uniqueness and NOT NULL are enforced on `emp_id`.

**(4) Add FOREIGN KEY on `dept_no`:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE EMPLOYEE ADD CONSTRAINT fk_dept
FOREIGN KEY (dept_no) REFERENCES DEPARTMENT(dept_no);
```
*Effect:* Every `dept_no` value in EMPLOYEE must already exist in DEPARTMENT.

**(5) Modify `ename` to VARCHAR(60):**
```sql
ALTER TABLE EMPLOYEE MODIFY ename VARCHAR(60);
```
*Effect:* Maximum length of ename expands from 30 to 60 characters.

**(6) Drop the `salary` column:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE EMPLOYEE DROP COLUMN salary;
```
*Effect:* The salary column and all its data are permanently removed.

**(7) Rename table to `EMP_MASTER`:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE EMPLOYEE RENAME TO EMP_MASTER;
```
*Effect:* The table is renamed. All its data, constraints, and indexes remain intact.

---

**Part (b) — Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | DROP TABLE | TRUNCATE TABLE | ALTER TABLE DROP COLUMN |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Schema** | Table definition removed entirely from catalog | Schema preserved | Column definition removed from schema |
| **(ii) Data** | All rows permanently deleted | All rows permanently deleted | Only that column's data removed; other columns preserved |
| **(iii) Recoverability** | Irrecoverable without backup | Irrecoverable without backup (in most RDBMS) | Irrecoverable without backup |
| **(iv) Auto-commit** | Yes (DDL) | Yes (DDL in many RDBMS) | Yes (DDL) |

---

#### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of schema modification using the `ALTER TABLE` command. With a suitable example, demonstrate how a column can be:
(i) Added with a DEFAULT value
(ii) Added with NOT NULL constraint
(iii) Modified to change its data type
(iv) Renamed
What happens to the existing rows in each case? *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** A library database has the following table:
```sql
CREATE TABLE BOOK (
    book_id   INTEGER,
    title     VARCHAR(50),
    price     DECIMAL(8,2),
    author    VARCHAR(40)
);
```
Write ALTER TABLE statements to:
1. Add a `published_year INTEGER` column with default 2024.
2. Add a UNIQUE constraint on `title`.
3. Add a CHECK constraint ensuring `price > 0`.
4. Drop the UNIQUE constraint on `title`.
5. Drop the CHECK constraint.
6. Drop the `author` column.

State the prerequisites (if any) for each statement to execute successfully. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

**Part (a) — Schema Modification Demonstration:**

Schema modification refers to **changing the structure (intension)** of an existing table while keeping its stored data (extension) intact. The `ALTER TABLE` command provides this capability.

**Sample Base Table:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE PRODUCT (
    pid   INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    pname VARCHAR(30)
);
```

**(i) Add column with DEFAULT:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE PRODUCT ADD stock INTEGER DEFAULT 100;
```
**Effect on existing rows:** All existing rows get `stock = 100`.

**(ii) Add column with NOT NULL:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE PRODUCT ADD category VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'GENERAL';
```
**Effect on existing rows:** All existing rows get `category = 'GENERAL'`. The DEFAULT clause is **mandatory** here, otherwise the command fails on existing rows because NULL violates NOT NULL.

**(iii) Modify data type:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE PRODUCT MODIFY pname VARCHAR(100);
```
**Effect:** Length of pname expanded. Existing data unchanged. The command fails if any existing pname exceeds 100 characters.

**(iv) Rename column:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE PRODUCT RENAME COLUMN pname TO product_name;
```
**Effect:** Column header changes from `pname` to `product_name`. All row values remain in place under the new name.

---

**Part (b) — ALTER Statements for BOOK Table:**

**(1)** Add `published_year` with default 2024:
```sql
ALTER TABLE BOOK ADD published_year INTEGER DEFAULT 2024;
```
*Prerequisite:* None. Existing rows get 2024.

**(2)** Add UNIQUE on `title`:
```sql
ALTER TABLE BOOK ADD CONSTRAINT uq_title UNIQUE (title);
```
*Prerequisite:* All existing values in `title` must already be distinct. If any duplicates exist, the command fails.

**(3)** Add CHECK constraint on `price > 0`:
```sql
ALTER TABLE BOOK ADD CONSTRAINT chk_price CHECK (price > 0);
```
*Prerequisite:* All existing rows must already have `price > 0`. Otherwise, the constraint cannot be retroactively enforced.

**(4)** Drop the UNIQUE constraint:
```sql
ALTER TABLE BOOK DROP CONSTRAINT uq_title;
```
*Prerequisite:* None. Existing duplicate titles (if any introduced after step 2 was removed) would now be allowed.

**(5)** Drop the CHECK constraint:
```sql
ALTER TABLE BOOK DROP CONSTRAINT chk_price;
```
*Prerequisite:* None. Negative or zero prices can now be inserted.

**(6)** Drop the `author` column:
```sql
ALTER TABLE BOOK DROP COLUMN author;
```
*Prerequisite:* No other table should reference `author` as a FOREIGN KEY. If so, the FK must be dropped first.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in ALTER TABLE questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that ALTER is auto-committed** — Writing "we can use ROLLBACK to undo" loses full marks. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 2. **Confusing `MODIFY` (MySQL/Oracle) with `ALTER COLUMN` (Standard SQL/PostgreSQL)** — Examiner expects engine-specific awareness. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 3. **Not mentioning that DROP COLUMN permanently destroys data** — Always write "data in that column is permanently lost." *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 4. **Missing prerequisites** — For `ADD CONSTRAINT`, students forget to mention that **existing data must already satisfy the constraint** for the command to succeed. *(Lose 2 marks)*
> 5. **Confusing `DROP TABLE` with `DROP COLUMN`** — These are entirely different operations. *(Lose 2 marks)*

---

### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for ALTER Command:**
>
> - ✅ ALTER is a **DDL command**, used for **schema modification**.
> - ✅ ALTER changes the **structure**, not the **stored rows**.
> - ✅ ALTER is **auto-committed** — **CANNOT be rolled back**.
> - ✅ Common sub-operations: `ADD`, `DROP`, `MODIFY`, `RENAME`, `ADD CONSTRAINT`, `DROP CONSTRAINT`.
> - ✅ `ADD COLUMN` populates existing rows with `NULL` by default; use `DEFAULT` clause to override.
> - ✅ Adding `NOT NULL` to existing column **fails** if rows already contain NULL.
> - ✅ `DROP COLUMN` permanently removes the column AND its data.
> - ✅ `MODIFY` changes data type/size; fails if existing data violates new type.
> - ✅ `ADD CONSTRAINT` requires **existing data to be valid** against the new constraint.
> - ✅ MySQL uses `MODIFY`; PostgreSQL/Standard SQL uses `ALTER COLUMN ... TYPE`; Oracle uses `MODIFY (...)`.
> - ✅ `RENAME COLUMN` and `RENAME TO` change names without altering data.
> - ✅ DROP COLUMN is irreversible — always backup before executing in production.
> - ✅ ALTER TABLE acquires an **exclusive lock** during execution, blocking other DML operations.
> - ✅ System catalog / data dictionary is automatically updated after successful ALTER.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Drop

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **`DROP`** statement in SQL is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** command belonging to the ISO/IEC 9075 standard (the formal SQL specification). It is used to **permanently and irreversibly remove** a schema object from the database catalog. The objects that can be dropped include, but are not limited to: **DATABASE**, **SCHEMA**, **TABLE**, **VIEW**, **INDEX**, **SEQUENCE**, **TYPE**, **TRIGGER**, **DOMAIN**, **COLLATION**, and **CONSTRAINT**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – DDL Constructs):**
> The DROP command is a core DDL operation. Unlike DML commands (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), DROP operations are **auto-committed** in most RDBMS engines (Oracle, MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL), meaning the change is final and **cannot be rolled back** without a pre-existing backup.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **filing cabinet** in a library office.

- **`CREATE TABLE`** is like installing a **new drawer** in the cabinet with a label (e.g., "STUDENT RECORDS").
- **`DROP TABLE`** is like **ripping that entire drawer out of the cabinet and incinerating it** — including all folders, papers, and the label itself.
- **`DELETE FROM table`** is like **emptying the drawer** (taking out all papers but keeping the drawer).
- **`TRUNCATE TABLE`** is like **shredding all papers in the drawer** (emptying contents efficiently, but the drawer structure remains).

This distinction is **critical** for KTU exams — examiners frequently test whether students understand the difference between *emptying* and *destroying*.

### 1.3 Core Properties of the DROP Command

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Properties (Board-Relevant):**
> 1. **Irreversibility**: DROP is a DDL operation; in nearly all RDBMS it triggers an implicit **COMMIT**. No rollback is possible.
> 2. **Cascade Control**: The optional `CASCADE` and `RESTRICT` keywords govern dependent-object behavior.
> 3. **Catalog Update**: The DROP statement removes the object's definition from the **Data Dictionary / System Catalog** (e.g., `INFORMATION_SCHEMA` in MySQL/PostgreSQL, `DATA_DICTIONARY` in Oracle).
> 4. **Privilege Required**: The user must own the object or hold `DROP` privilege (e.g., `DROP ANY TABLE` in Oracle).
> 5. **Storage Reclamation**: Under most storage engines (InnoDB, PostgreSQL), the underlying data files and indexes are released back to the tablespace, though physical disk blocks may not be zeroed immediately.

### 1.4 Standard Syntax (ISO SQL)

The general syntactic form, as per the SQL standard, is:

```
DROP <object_type> [IF EXISTS] <object_name> [CASCADE | RESTRICT];
```

The clauses operate as follows:

| Clause | Function |
|---|---|
| `<object_type>` | Specifies kind: TABLE, VIEW, INDEX, SCHEMA, DATABASE, etc. |
| `IF EXISTS` | Prevents error when the object is absent (suppresses "object not found" exception). |
| `CASCADE` | Automatically drops all dependent objects (foreign-key referencing tables, views, constraints). |
| `RESTRICT` (default) | Refuses to drop if any dependent object exists. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categorization of DROP Targets

The DROP operation is polymorphic — it adapts to the type of schema object being targeted. Below is the systematic classification expected by KTU examiners:

**A. DROP DATABASE / DROP SCHEMA**
- Removes the entire database or named schema.
- In MySQL, `DROP DATABASE` is the canonical form.
- In PostgreSQL and ISO SQL, `DROP SCHEMA` is the normative term; "database" and "schema" are treated as distinct concepts.
- All tables, views, triggers, and stored procedures inside are eliminated.
- The underlying physical files on disk are deleted.

**B. DROP TABLE**
- Removes the table definition **and** all its rows.
- Frees allocated extents/pages.
- Removes all associated indexes (in MySQL InnoDB, indexes are dropped with the table; in Oracle, indexes are marked as unusable unless explicitly dropped beforehand).

**C. DROP VIEW**
- Removes only the **view definition** (the stored query).
- Underlying base tables are **unaffected**.
- If `CASCADE` is used, other views dependent on this view are also dropped.

**D. DROP INDEX**
- Removes the index data structure (B-Tree, Hash, Bitmap, etc.).
- The base table's data and other indexes remain intact.

**E. DROP CONSTRAINT**
- Removes a named integrity constraint (PRIMARY KEY, UNIQUE, FOREIGN KEY, CHECK).
- Table structure remains; only the rule is eliminated.

### 2.2 The CASCADE vs RESTRICT Decision Matrix

This is one of the **highest-yield topics** in KTU Module 2 evaluations.

| Scenario | CASCADE | RESTRICT (Default) |
|---|---|---|
| Drop Table T1; T2 has FK referencing T1 | T1 and T2 both dropped | Statement **fails** with referential-integrity error |
| Drop View V1; V2 is built on V1 | Both V1 and V2 dropped | Statement **fails** |
| Drop Table with no dependents | Table dropped | Table dropped (identical result) |
| `IF EXISTS` + object missing | Silent no-op (if used) | Error raised |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:**
> In **MySQL**, if you do not specify `CASCADE` or `RESTRICT`, the older versions default to `RESTRICT`. In **PostgreSQL**, the default is `RESTRICT`. In **Oracle**, the `CASCADE CONSTRAINTS` clause is used (older Oracle syntax) to drop referential integrity constraints that reference the table being dropped.

### 2.3 DROP vs DELETE vs TRUNCATE — The Triad of Destruction

This comparison table is the **single most frequently asked question** in KTU Module 2 university exams. Memorize it verbatim.

| Property | DROP | TRUNCATE | DELETE |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SQL Category** | DDL | DDL (technically DDL in most engines) | DML |
| **Auto-Commit** | Yes | Yes | No (transactional) |
| **Rollback Possible?** | No | No (most engines) | Yes |
| **Removes Structure?** | **Yes — entire object gone** | No — structure preserved | No — structure preserved |
| **Removes Data?** | Yes (implicitly) | Yes (all rows) | Yes (optionally filtered via WHERE) |
| **Fires Triggers?** | No | No | Yes (per row) |
| **WHERE Clause?** | No | No | Yes |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Very Fast (deallocates extents) | Slowest (row-by-row logging) |
| **Resets Identity / AUTO_INCREMENT?** | N/A (object gone) | **Yes** | No |
| **Storage Reclaim** | Full | Full (deallocates pages) | Partial (uses High Water Mark concept) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Syntax Element | Symbolic Representation | Boundary Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|
| Standard DROP syntax | `DROP OBJECT [IF EXISTS] name [CASCADE ∣ RESTRICT]` | `CASCADE` and `RESTRICT` are **mutually exclusive** |
| Dependency failure probability | $P_{\text{fail}} = 1 - P_{\text{no\_deps}}$ | RESTRICT aborts when $\exists$ dependent object |
| Drop with no dependents | Equivalent for both modes | Both succeed silently |
| Effect on storage | $S_{\text{after}} = 0$ for the object | $S_{\text{reclaimed}} = S_{\text{before}}$ |
| Identity counter after TRUNCATE | $I_{\text{next}} = 1$ | (Listed for DROP contrast) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> In production database administration (DBA), the DROP command is exercised during **schema migrations**, **decommissioning legacy modules**, **cleaning up temporary staging tables**, and during **GDPR / data-purge operations**. A single misplaced DROP TABLE statement without a backup has historically caused catastrophic data loss incidents in enterprises — which is why `IF EXISTS` and transaction-wrapped DDL (introduced in PostgreSQL and recent MySQL versions) are best-practice safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: DROP TABLE with Foreign-Key Dependencies

Consider the following schema (classic KTU textbook scenario):

```sql
CREATE TABLE Department (
    dept_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE Student (
    roll_no   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    s_name    VARCHAR(50),
    dept_id   INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
);
```

**Case 1: Drop Student (no dependents exist)**

The Student table is the **referencing** table; nothing depends on it. Both `CASCADE` and `RESTRICT` succeed.

```sql
DROP TABLE Student;
-- OR
DROP TABLE Student RESTRICT;
-- OR
DROP TABLE Student CASCADE;
-- Result: Student table and its definition removed.
```

**Case 2: Drop Department with RESTRICT (default)**

The Department table is the **referenced** (parent) table. Student contains a foreign key referencing `Department(dept_id)`.

The semantic decision tree executed by the RDBMS engine is:

$$
\text{DROP Department} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Check } \exists \text{ FK in dependent tables}
$$

$$
\exists \text{ FK in Student} \Rightarrow \text{Engine consults CASCADE / RESTRICT flag}
$$

$$
\text{Flag} = \text{RESTRICT} \Rightarrow \text{Operation ABORTED, error raised}
$$

The error returned (syntax varies by RDBMS):

- MySQL: `Error 3730: Cannot drop table 'Department' referenced by a foreign key constraint 'student_ibfk_1'...`
- PostgreSQL: `ERROR: cannot drop table department because other objects depend on it`
- Oracle: `ORA-02449: unique/primary keys in table referenced by foreign keys`

**Case 3: Drop Department with CASCADE**

```sql
DROP TABLE Department CASCADE;
```

Semantic evaluation:

$$
\text{DROP Department CASCADE} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Recursively drop all dependent objects}
$$

$$
\text{Dependent of Department} = \{\text{Student, FK constraint on Student.dept\_id}\}
$$

$$
\text{After CASCADE} \Rightarrow \text{Department} = \emptyset, \quad \text{Student} = \emptyset
$$

Both the parent and the child (referencing) table are removed in a single operation.

### 3.2 Worked Example: DROP DATABASE — Complete Removal

```sql
DROP DATABASE UniversityDB;
```

The RDBMS performs the following internal operations (ordered):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Acquire EXCLUSIVE lock on the database catalog entry} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Iterate through } \text{INFORMATION\_SCHEMA.TABLES} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{For each table } T_i \text{: drop } T_i \text{ with CASCADE} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{For each view } V_j \text{: drop } V_j \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{For each trigger, procedure, function: drop} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Delete database files from disk (ibd, MYD, MYI, etc.)} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{Remove catalog entry from } \text{SCHEMATA} \text{ metadata table} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{COMMIT the transaction (implicit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example: IF EXISTS — Idempotent Behavior

Without `IF EXISTS`:

```sql
DROP TABLE NonExistent;
-- MySQL: ERROR 1051 (42S02): Unknown table 'universitydb.nonexistent'
```

With `IF EXISTS`:

```sql
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS NonExistent;
-- MySQL: Query OK, 0 rows affected, 1 warning (Warning: Unknown table)
```

Logical equivalence:

$$
\text{DROP TABLE IF EXISTS } X \;\equiv\; \text{IF } (\exists X) \text{ THEN } \text{DROP } X \text{ ELSE no-op}
$$

This makes DROP statements **idempotent**, which is invaluable in deployment scripts where repeated execution must not fail.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: DROP Variants Across RDBMS Engines

| DROP Operation | MySQL Syntax | PostgreSQL Syntax | Oracle Syntax |
|---|---|---|---|
| Remove database | `DROP DATABASE db;` | `DROP DATABASE db;` (since v13) | N/A — uses schemas |
| Remove schema | N/A (uses databases) | `DROP SCHEMA s CASCADE;` | `DROP USER s CASCADE;` (drops schema) |
| Remove table with FK | `DROP TABLE t CASCADE;` | `DROP TABLE t CASCADE;` | `DROP TABLE t CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;` |
| Drop view with dependents | `DROP VIEW v CASCADE;` | `DROP VIEW v CASCADE;` | `DROP VIEW v CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Engine Nuance (often tested):**
> The Oracle syntax `CASCADE CONSTRAINTS` is **not** the same as MySQL/PostgreSQL `CASCADE`. In Oracle, `CASCADE CONSTRAINTS` only drops referential integrity constraints (FKs) that reference the table being dropped — it does **not** drop the referencing tables themselves. In contrast, PostgreSQL's `CASCADE` recursively drops both.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DROP Resolution

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates how the RDBMS engine processes a `DROP TABLE` statement, particularly when dependency checks are involved.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["User Issues DROP TABLE statement"] --> nodeB{"Object Exists?"}
    nodeB -- No --> nodeC{"IF EXISTS specified?"}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeD{"Dependent Objects Exist?"}
    nodeC -- No --> nodeE["Raise ERROR object not found"]
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeF["Silent Success no op"]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeG["Drop Object Immediately"]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeH{"CASCADE specified?"}
    nodeH -- No, RESTRICT --> nodeI["Raise ERROR referential integrity violation"]
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeJ["Enumerate All Dependent Objects"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Recursively Drop Each Dependent Object"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Drop the Original Target Object"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Update System Catalog Metadata"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Release Storage Back to Tablespace"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Implicit COMMIT Operation Final"]
    nodeG --> nodeM
```

### 4.2 Diagram: CASCADE Propagation Across Multi-Level Dependencies

Consider a chain: Table A → View B (depends on A) → View C (depends on B). The following diagram shows the dependency graph and how `DROP VIEW B CASCADE` propagates.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    baseTbl["Table A Base Entity"] --> viewB["View B Derived from A"]
    viewB --> viewC["View C Derived from B"]
    viewB --> viewD["View D Also derived from B"]
    dropOp["DROP VIEW B CASCADE"] --> viewB
    dropOp -. cascades to .-> viewC
    dropOp -. cascades to .-> viewD
    baseTbl -. unaffected .-> unaffectedNote["Table A remains intact"]
```

### 4.3 Diagram: The Lifecycle Position of DROP Among SQL Sublanguages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sqlRoot["SQL Language"] --> ddlBranch["DDL Data Definition Language"]
    sqlRoot --> dmlBranch["DML Data Manipulation Language"]
    sqlRoot --> dclBranch["DCL Data Control Language"]
    sqlRoot --> tclBranch["TCL Transaction Control Language"]
    ddlBranch --> cmd1["CREATE"]
    ddlBranch --> cmd2["ALTER"]
    ddlBranch --> cmd3["DROP"]
    ddlBranch --> cmd4["TRUNCATE"]
    ddlBranch --> cmd5["RENAME"]
    ddlBranch -- operates on --> obj1["Tables, Views, Indexes"]
    ddlBranch -- operates on --> obj2["Schemas, Databases"]
    ddlBranch -- operates on --> obj3["Constraints, Triggers"]
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Storage State Before and After DROP

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph beforeDrop["Before DROP TABLE Student"]
        tblStruct1["Table Definition in Catalog"]
        rowSet1["Rows R1 R2 R3 ... Rn"]
        idxStruct1["Index Structures B Tree"]
    end
    subgraph afterDrop["After DROP TABLE Student"]
        tblStruct2["Catalog Entry Removed"]
        rowSet2["Storage Pages Returned to Tablespace"]
        idxStruct2["Index Pages Also Returned"]
    end
    beforeDrop ==> dropAction["DROP TABLE Student"] ==> afterDrop
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**What is the difference between the DROP and DELETE commands in SQL?**

**Model Answer:**

The `DROP` command is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** operation that removes the entire table structure along with all its data from the database. It is irreversible and auto-committed. In contrast, the `DELETE` command is a **Data Manipulation Language (DML)** operation that removes specific rows from a table based on a `WHERE` clause, but preserves the table structure. DELETE is transactional and can be rolled back.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Explain the purpose of the CASCADE option in the DROP statement.**

**Model Answer:**

The `CASCADE` option in a DROP statement instructs the RDBMS to **automatically drop all dependent objects** along with the target object. For example, when dropping a parent table that is referenced by a foreign key in a child table, specifying `CASCADE` will drop both the parent and child tables (and any dependent views, constraints, or triggers). Without `CASCADE` (i.e., under default `RESTRICT` behavior), the DROP operation would fail with a referential-integrity error.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the various DROP commands in SQL with suitable examples. Discuss the role of the `IF EXISTS` clause and the difference between `CASCADE` and `RESTRICT`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`, and `DELETE` commands in SQL with respect to their functional category, reversibility, speed, trigger behavior, and impact on auto-increment counters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The SQL `DROP` command is a DDL statement used to permanently remove schema objects. The various forms are:

1. **DROP DATABASE** — Removes an entire database along with all contained tables, views, and stored procedures.

```sql
DROP DATABASE UniversityDB;
```

2. **DROP SCHEMA** — Removes a named schema (used in PostgreSQL/Oracle).

```sql
DROP SCHEMA Library CASCADE;
```

3. **DROP TABLE** — Removes a table definition and its data.

```sql
DROP TABLE Employee;
```

4. **DROP VIEW** — Removes a view definition; underlying tables remain.

```sql
DROP VIEW HighSalaryEmployees;
```

5. **DROP INDEX** — Removes an index structure.

```sql
DROP INDEX idx_emp_name;
```

The **IF EXISTS** clause prevents the statement from raising an error when the target object does not exist:

```sql
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS TempTable;
```

This makes the operation **idempotent** — repeated execution produces the same final state without errors.

The **CASCADE** option causes the engine to recursively drop all dependent objects (foreign-key referencing tables, dependent views, constraints). The **RESTRICT** option (default in most RDBMS) refuses to drop the object if any dependent objects exist.

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Property | DROP | TRUNCATE | DELETE |
|---|---|---|---|
| Category | DDL | DDL | DML |
| Removes structure | Yes | No | No |
| Removes data | Yes | Yes (all) | Yes (filtered by WHERE) |
| Transactional | Auto-commit | Auto-commit | Within transaction |
| Rollback possible | No | No | Yes |
| Fires triggers | No | No | Yes |
| Resets auto-increment | N/A | Yes | No |
| Speed | Fastest | Very fast | Slowest (row-by-row) |

**DROP** removes the entire object (structure + data). **TRUNCATE** empties the table but retains structure and resets identity counters. **DELETE** removes rows one at a time and supports the `WHERE` clause for selective deletion, but it preserves identity counters.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Consider two tables `DEPT(dept_id PK, dept_name)` and `EMP(emp_id PK, emp_name, dept_id FK)`. Explain with step-by-step execution what happens when you issue:
   - (i) `DROP TABLE DEPT;`
   - (ii) `DROP TABLE DEPT CASCADE;`
   - (iii) `DROP TABLE DEPT CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;` (Oracle syntax) **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a schema? Describe the `DROP SCHEMA` command with examples. How does the cascade behavior differ between MySQL and PostgreSQL when dropping a schema containing tables with foreign-key relationships? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

(i) `DROP TABLE DEPT;` — The engine checks `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.REFERENTIAL_CONSTRAINTS` and finds that EMP.dept_id references DEPT.dept_id. Since `RESTRICT` is the default, the operation **fails** with an error such as: *Error 3730: Cannot drop table 'DEPT' referenced by a foreign key constraint*.

(ii) `DROP TABLE DEPT CASCADE;` — In MySQL and PostgreSQL, the engine recursively drops both the parent table DEPT and the child table EMP (along with the FK constraint). After execution, neither table exists.

(iii) `DROP TABLE DEPT CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;` (Oracle-specific) — In Oracle, this drops the DEPT table and also drops the foreign-key constraint defined in EMP. However, the EMP table itself **remains intact**; only the referential rule linking it to DEPT is removed. This is a key syntactic difference between Oracle's `CASCADE CONSTRAINTS` and PostgreSQL/MySQL's `CASCADE`.

**Model Solution for (b):**

A **schema** is a logical namespace within a database that groups related tables, views, indexes, and other objects. It functions as a container for database objects owned by a single user (or role).

The `DROP SCHEMA` command removes a schema and (optionally) all its contents:

```sql
DROP SCHEMA Library CASCADE;
```

In **MySQL**, the concept of "schema" is synonymous with "database," so `DROP DATABASE` is the operative command. A `DROP DATABASE` in MySQL removes all tables without any need for explicit `CASCADE`, but it will fail if any tables are locked or if other connections hold active references.

In **PostgreSQL**, schemas are distinct from databases. `DROP SCHEMA Library CASCADE;` will recursively drop all tables, views, and functions inside the `Library` schema, including those with foreign-key dependencies among them. Without `CASCADE`, the operation fails if any object inside the schema has dependents.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing DROP with DELETE/TRUNCATE** — Examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically to the DDL-vs-DML distinction. Memorize the comparison table.
> 2. **Ignoring CASCADE semantics** — Many students write `CASCADE` and assume it works identically across RDBMS. The Oracle `CASCADE CONSTRAINTS` vs PostgreSQL `CASCADE` distinction is a frequent trap question.
> 3. **Forgetting IF EXISTS** — When asked about idempotent DDL, students often omit `IF EXISTS`. Always include it when the operation may be re-run.
> 4. **Auto-commit oversight** — DROP cannot be rolled back in most engines. Mentioning this earns additional credit.
> 5. **Schema vs Database confusion** — MySQL treats them as synonyms; PostgreSQL does not. Cite the correct RDBMS context.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DROP is DDL**, permanently removes the entire schema object (structure + data + metadata + dependent indexes).
- **Auto-committed** in nearly all RDBMS — **no rollback** without external backup.
- **`IF EXISTS`** clause provides idempotency and suppresses "object not found" errors.
- **`CASCADE`** recursively drops dependent objects (foreign-key referencing tables, dependent views, constraints).
- **`RESTRICT`** (default) refuses to drop when dependents exist; operation aborts with referential-integrity error.
- **DROP vs TRUNCATE vs DELETE** — the triad comparison is the single most-tested concept:
  - DROP removes structure.
  - TRUNCATE removes all data but keeps structure; resets identity.
  - DELETE removes filtered rows; is transactional; does not reset identity.
- **Oracle distinction**: `CASCADE CONSTRAINTS` only removes FK rules; tables remain. PostgreSQL/MySQL `CASCADE` removes the entire dependent table.
- **DROP DATABASE** removes the entire database file set from disk; all objects inside are gone.
- **DROP VIEW** only removes the view definition; base tables are unaffected.
- **Privileges required**: user must own the object or hold the corresponding DROP privilege (e.g., `DROP ANY TABLE` in Oracle).
- **System catalog update** happens automatically — the object's row in `INFORMATION_SCHEMA` (or `DBA_OBJECTS` in Oracle) is deleted.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Truncate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **SQL (Structured Query Language)** under **DDL (Data Definition Language)** operations, **TRUNCATE** is a command used to remove **all rows** from a table **instantaneously** while preserving the table's structure, schema, indexes, constraints, and column definitions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> `TRUNCATE TABLE` is a Data Definition Language (DDL) statement in SQL that is used to remove **all the records/tuples** from a table at once. It performs an implicit `COMMIT` before and after execution, meaning the operation is **auto-committed** and the transaction log is released immediately.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **whiteboard** in a classroom:

- The **whiteboard frame, the stand, and the surface** = the **table structure** (columns, data types, constraints).
- The **written text/notes on the whiteboard** = the **rows/records** stored in the table.

Now, when you want to erase the notes:
- **`DELETE` command** = erasing each line one by one using a duster (slow, logs each deletion, can be undone).
- **`TRUNCATE` command** = using a big cloth to **wipe the entire whiteboard clean in one swift motion**. The board itself remains intact — you can still write on it again, but **everything that was on it is gone**.

> [!NOTE]
> The whiteboard (table) still exists, but all the chalk marks (data) are removed **permanently and instantly**.

### 1.3 Standard Syntax in SQL

The standard SQL syntax for the TRUNCATE command is:

```sql
TRUNCATE TABLE table_name;
```

- `TABLE` keyword is **optional in MySQL** but **mandatory in Oracle and PostgreSQL**.
- Some databases (like **PostgreSQL**) allow `TRUNCATE` on **multiple tables simultaneously** using a comma-separated list.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Metrics to Highlight

| Metric | Standard Value / Behavior |
|--------|--------------------------|
| **Category** | **DDL (Data Definition Language)** |
| **Rollback capability** | **Cannot be rolled back** in most RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle) |
| **Trigger firing** | **Does NOT fire DELETE triggers** |
| **Identity/Auto-increment reset** | **Resets** to seed value |
| **Speed** | **Faster** than DELETE without WHERE clause |
| **WHERE clause support** | **NOT supported** |
| **Transaction log usage** | **Minimal** — logs page deallocation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown of TRUNCATE

The `TRUNCATE` command operates in the following structured sequence:

1. **Schema Lock Acquisition** — The database engine acquires an **exclusive lock** on the target table to prevent concurrent DML operations during the truncation.
2. **Page Deallocation** — Instead of deleting each row, the engine **deallocates the data pages** that hold the table's rows. This is the key reason TRUNCATE is **extremely fast**.
3. **Identity Counter Reset** — Any `AUTO_INCREMENT`, `IDENTITY`, or `SERIAL` columns are **reset to their initial seed value**.
