4. **Implicit COMMIT** — Since TRUNCATE is a DDL command, it issues an **implicit COMMIT** both **before** and **after** execution.
5. **Trigger Bypass** — Any `DELETE` triggers associated with the table are **not executed**.
6. **Storage Reclamation** — The freed data pages are returned to the **tablespace** for reuse.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Happens | How It Benefits Performance |
|------|---------------|----------------------------|
| Page Deallocation | Removes storage blocks, not rows | Eliminates row-by-row processing |
| Identity Reset | Table is treated as "fresh" | New inserts start from original seed |
| Implicit Commit | DDL cannot be transactional | Ensures atomicity automatically |
| Trigger Bypass | TRUNCATE is not DELETE | Saves trigger execution overhead |
| Minimal Logging | Only page deallocations logged | Reduces WAL (Write-Ahead Log) size |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | TRUNCATE | DELETE (without WHERE) | DROP TABLE |
|----------|----------|------------------------|------------|
| **Command Type** | **DDL** | **DML** | **DDL** |
| **Removes Rows** | ✅ Yes (all) | ✅ Yes (all) | ❌ Removes table entirely |
| **Removes Structure** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **WHERE clause** | ❌ Not allowed | ✅ Allowed | ❌ Not allowed |
| **Rollback possible** | ❌ No (usually) | ✅ Yes (in transaction) | ❌ No |
| **Fires Triggers** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Resets Identity** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Speed** | **Fastest** | Slow (row-by-row) | Fast |
| **Logs rows individually** | ❌ No (minimal logging) | ✅ Yes (full logging) | ❌ No |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Data Warehousing**: ETL pipelines use `TRUNCATE` to clear staging tables before fresh data loads — it is **significantly faster** than `DELETE` on millions of rows.
- **Testing Environments**: Developers frequently truncate test tables between test runs to ensure **clean state** without re-creating schema.
- **Log Management**: Old log tables are periodically truncated to manage **storage growth**.
- **Performance Tuning**: When a table needs to be emptied and refilled, `TRUNCATE + INSERT` is **orders of magnitude faster** than `DELETE + INSERT`.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A common 3-mark KTU question is *"Differentiate between DELETE and TRUNCATE."* Memorize the comparison table above — it is the most frequently asked topic under this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Conceptual Derivation: How TRUNCATE Achieves Its Speed

Let us analytically derive why `TRUNCATE` outperforms `DELETE` for full-table removal.

### Step 1: DELETE Operation Cost Analysis

When a `DELETE FROM table_name` (without WHERE) is executed, the database engine performs the following:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{DELETE}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( T_{\text{read}} + T_{\text{log}} + T_{\text{index}} + T_{\text{trigger}} + T_{\text{lock}} \right)
$$

Where:

- $n$ = total number of rows in the table
- $T_{\text{read}}$ = time to read each row
- $T_{\text{log}}$ = time to write each row deletion to the transaction log
- $T_{\text{index}}$ = time to update each index entry
- $T_{\text{trigger}}$ = time to fire DELETE triggers
- $T_{\text{lock}}$ = time to acquire row-level locks

This is an **O(n)** operation — the cost scales **linearly** with the number of rows.

### Step 2: TRUNCATE Operation Cost Analysis

When `TRUNCATE TABLE table_name` is executed:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{TRUNCATE}} = T_{\text{lock}} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} T_{\text{dealloc}} + T_{\text{commit}}
$$

Where:

- $p$ = total number of **data pages** (not rows) allocated to the table
- $T_{\text{dealloc}}$ = time to deallocate a single data page
- $T_{\text{commit}}$ = time for the implicit COMMIT
- $p \ll n$ because each page contains **multiple rows**

### Step 3: Comparative Ratio

The performance advantage can be expressed as:

$$
\frac{\text{Cost}_{\text{DELETE}}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{TRUNCATE}}} = \frac{n \cdot (T_{\text{read}} + T_{\text{log}} + T_{\text{index}} + T_{\text{trigger}})}{p \cdot T_{\text{dealloc}}}
$$

Since each data page typically holds **hundreds to thousands of rows**, we have $n \gg p$, making TRUNCATE **orders of magnitude faster**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** TRUNCATE's efficiency comes from operating at the **storage layer (pages)** rather than the **logical layer (rows)**. This is a classic example of **bulk operation optimization** in database engine design.

## 3.2 Worked SQL Example with Step-by-Step Analysis

Consider a table `Student` with the following initial state:

```sql
CREATE TABLE Student (
    roll_no INT IDENTITY(1,1) PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(50),
    marks INT
);

INSERT INTO Student (name, marks) VALUES 
('Arjun', 85),
('Meera', 92),
('Rahul', 78);
```

**Initial State:**

$$
\text{Student} = \{(\text{roll\_no}: 1, \text{name}: \text{'Arjun'}), \ldots, (\text{roll\_no}: 3, \text{name}: \text{'Rahul'})\}
$$

### Step-by-Step Execution:

**Step 1:** Execute TRUNCATE command

```sql
TRUNCATE TABLE Student;
```

**Step 2:** Database engine acquires **exclusive table lock** on `Student`.

**Step 3:** Engine **deallocates all data pages** belonging to `Student` from the heap.

**Step 4:** **Identity counter is reset** — next insert will start from `roll_no = 1`.

**Step 5:** **Implicit COMMIT** is issued — transaction is finalized.

**Step 6:** Lock is released.

### Verification Query:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Student;
```

**Expected Output:**

| COUNT(*) |
|----------|
| 0        |

### Now Insert a New Row:

```sql
INSERT INTO Student (name, marks) VALUES ('Sneha', 88);

SELECT * FROM Student;
```

**Expected Output:**

| roll_no | name | marks |
|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Sneha | 88 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Notice that the new row has `roll_no = 1`, **not** `roll_no = 4`. This confirms that **TRUNCATE resets the identity/auto-increment counter**. This is a frequently tested KTU concept.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TRUNCATE Operation Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User Issues TRUNCATE TABLE command"] --> B["Parse SQL Statement"]
    B --> C["Acquire Exclusive Table Lock"]
    C --> D{"Table Referenced by FK?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Block Operation in Standard Mode"]
    D -- "No" --> F["Identify Data Pages"]
    F --> G["Deallocate All Data Pages"]
    G --> H["Reset Identity or Auto Increment"]
    H --> I["Bypass DELETE Triggers"]
    I --> J["Write Minimal Log Entry"]
    J --> K["Issue Implicit COMMIT"]
    K --> L["Release Table Lock"]
    L --> M["Table Empty Structure Retained"]
    
    E --> N["CASCADE Option Required"]
    N --> F
```

## 4.2 TRUNCATE vs DELETE — Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DELETE["DELETE Command DML"]
        D1["Acquire Row Locks"] --> D2["Read Each Row"]
        D2 --> D3["Log Each Deletion"]
        D3 --> D4["Update Each Index"]
        D4 --> D5["Fire DELETE Triggers"]
        D5 --> D6["Wait for COMMIT or Rollback"]
    end
    
    subgraph TRUNCATE["TRUNCATE Command DDL"]
        T1["Acquire Table Lock"] --> T2["Locate Data Pages"]
        T2 --> T3["Deallocate Pages in Bulk"]
        T3 --> T4["Reset Identity Counter"]
        T4 --> T5["Implicit COMMIT Auto"]
        T5 --> T6["Operation Complete"]
    end
    
    DELETE ~~~ TRUNCATE
```

## 4.3 Table State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TableWithData : Initial State with n rows
    TableWithData --> TruncationInProgress : TRUNCATE TABLE executed
    TruncationInProgress --> EmptyTablePreserved : Pages deallocated identity reset
    EmptyTablePreserved --> TableWithNewData : New INSERT after truncate
    TableWithNewData --> [*]
    
    note right of TruncationInProgress
        DDL Operation
        Cannot be rolled back
        No triggers fired
        Minimal logging
    end note
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw or explain TRUNCATE behavior, always mention: **(a) Table structure is retained**, **(b) All rows are removed**, **(c) Identity resets**, and **(d) Operation is auto-committed**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is the TRUNCATE command in SQL? Mention its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `TRUNCATE` command is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** statement used in SQL to remove **all rows** from a table quickly and efficiently.

Key characteristics:

1. It is a **DDL command**, not a DML command.
2. It **removes all rows** in a single operation and **cannot** use a `WHERE` clause.
3. It is **faster** than the `DELETE` command because it deallocates entire data pages rather than deleting rows individually.
4. It **resets identity / auto-increment** columns to their original seed value.
5. It performs an **implicit COMMIT** and generally **cannot be rolled back**.
6. It **does not fire DELETE triggers** associated with the table.
7. The **table structure, columns, indexes, and constraints are preserved**.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between DELETE and TRUNCATE commands in SQL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | DELETE | TRUNCATE |
|-------|--------|----------|
| **Command type** | DML | DDL |
| **WHERE clause** | Supported | Not supported |
| **Rollback** | Possible within transaction | Not possible |
| **Speed** | Slower (row-by-row) | Faster (page-level) |
| **Identity reset** | No | Yes |
| **Trigger firing** | Yes | No |

DELETE removes specific rows (or all if no WHERE clause) and logs each deletion. TRUNCATE removes all rows instantly by deallocating data pages.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a)** Explain the TRUNCATE command in SQL with its syntax. Discuss any **five** differences between DELETE and TRUNCATE. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `TRUNCATE` command in SQL is a DDL statement that removes all rows from a table while preserving its schema. The standard syntax is:

```sql
TRUNCATE TABLE table_name;
```

**Five key differences between DELETE and TRUNCATE:**

1. **Command Category:** DELETE is a DML command; TRUNCATE is a DDL command.

2. **Row Selection:** DELETE supports a `WHERE` clause to remove specific rows; TRUNCATE always removes **all rows** and does not support `WHERE`.

3. **Transaction Behavior:** DELETE operations can be rolled back if executed inside a transaction before COMMIT; TRUNCATE performs an implicit COMMIT and **cannot be rolled back** in most RDBMS.

4. **Performance:** DELETE processes rows individually, generating one log entry per row; TRUNCATE deallocates entire data pages, making it **significantly faster** on large tables.

5. **Identity Reset:** DELETE does not reset the auto-increment counter; TRUNCATE **resets it** to the original seed value.

6. **Trigger Execution:** DELETE fires any associated DELETE triggers; TRUNCATE **does not fire** triggers.

**Q (b)** Consider the following `Employee` table:

```sql
Employee(emp_id INT IDENTITY(100,1), name VARCHAR(40), salary DECIMAL(10,2))
```

The table currently contains 50 rows. Write the SQL command to **(i)** remove all rows from the table using TRUNCATE, **(ii)** insert a new employee named "Kiran" with salary 55000, and **(iii)** verify the emp_id assigned to Kiran. Explain why the resulting emp_id is **100** and not **151**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i)** Remove all rows:

```sql
TRUNCATE TABLE Employee;
```

**(ii)** Insert new employee:

```sql
INSERT INTO Employee (name, salary) VALUES ('Kiran', 55000.00);
```

**(iii)** Verify emp_id:

```sql
SELECT emp_id, name, salary FROM Employee WHERE name = 'Kiran';
```

**Expected Output:**

| emp_id | name | salary |
|--------|------|--------|
| 100 | Kiran | 55000.00 |

**Explanation of emp_id = 100:**

The `IDENTITY(100, 1)` specification defines the **seed value as 100** and **increment as 1**. Although the table had 50 rows before truncation (with emp_ids ranging from 100 to 149), the `TRUNCATE` operation **resets the identity counter back to the original seed value of 100**. Therefore, the next inserted row receives emp_id = 100, **not 151** (which would have been the case if `DELETE` had been used instead, as DELETE does not reset the identity counter).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **internal working of the TRUNCATE command** in a relational database management system. Mention at least **four** internal steps performed by the DBMS engine. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

When a user issues `TRUNCATE TABLE table_name;`, the DBMS engine executes the following internal sequence:

**Step 1 — Lock Acquisition:**
The engine acquires an **exclusive table-level lock** to prevent any concurrent DML or DDL operations on the target table during truncation.

**Step 2 — Foreign Key Validation:**
The engine checks whether the table is referenced by any **foreign key constraints** from other tables. If yes, the operation is blocked (unless `CASCADE` is specified).

**Step 3 — Data Page Deallocation:**
The engine locates all data pages (the smallest unit of storage) belonging to the table and **deallocates them** from the heap. This is the primary reason for TRUNCATE's speed — it does not iterate over rows.

**Step 4 — Identity Counter Reset:**
Any `IDENTITY`, `AUTO_INCREMENT`, or `SERIAL` columns are **reset to their original seed values**.

**Step 5 — Trigger Bypass:**
Unlike DELETE, TRUNCATE **does not execute DELETE triggers** defined on the table.

**Step 6 — Minimal Logging:**
Only the **page deallocation event** is written to the transaction log, not individual row deletions.

**Step 7 — Implicit COMMIT:**
Since TRUNCATE is a DDL command, it performs an **automatic COMMIT** both before and after the operation.

**Step 8 — Lock Release:**
The exclusive lock is released, and the table is now empty but structurally intact.

**Flow Diagram:**

```
TRUNCATE issued → Lock → FK check → Page dealloc → Identity reset → 
Trigger skip → Minimal log → Implicit COMMIT → Lock release
```

**Q (b)** A company maintains a `Sales` table with **10 million rows**. The DBA needs to clear this table weekly before loading new sales data. Compare the performance and behavior of using **DELETE vs TRUNCATE** for this scenario. Justify which command should be used and why. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Scenario Analysis:**

The `Sales` table has 10 million rows and is cleared weekly. Two options are:

**Option 1 — Using DELETE:**
```sql
DELETE FROM Sales;
COMMIT;
```

**Option 2 — Using TRUNCATE:**
```sql
TRUNCATE TABLE Sales;
```

**Performance Comparison:**

For 10 million rows:

- **DELETE** must process each row individually:
  - Reads every row
  - Writes a log entry for each deletion
  - Updates every index entry
  - Fires any DELETE triggers
  - Acquires and releases row locks

  Approximate cost: $O(n)$ where $n = 10{,}000{,}000$

- **TRUNCATE** deallocates entire data pages:
  - Locates all data pages (typically a few thousand pages)
  - Deallocates them in bulk
  - Writes minimal log entries
  - No triggers fired
  - No per-row processing

  Approximate cost: $O(p)$ where $p \ll n$

**Estimated Time:**

$$
\frac{\text{Time}_{\text{DELETE}}}{\text{Time}_{\text{TRUNCATE}}} \approx \frac{n}{p} \approx \frac{10^7}{10^3} = 10^4
$$

DELETE may take **several minutes to hours**; TRUNCATE completes in **milliseconds to seconds**.

**Behavioral Differences:**

- DELETE can be rolled back (if inside a transaction before COMMIT).
- TRUNCATE cannot be rolled back.
- DELETE does NOT reset identity; TRUNCATE DOES reset identity.
- DELETE fires triggers; TRUNCATE does NOT.

**Recommendation:**

For this scenario, **TRUNCATE should be used** because:

1. The entire table needs to be cleared (no WHERE clause needed).
2. The operation is performed frequently (weekly), so **performance is critical**.
3. There is **no need for rollback** — the old data is meant to be discarded permanently.
4. There is **no need for trigger execution** during clearing.
5. The auto-increment counter should be reset weekly for clean tracking.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently **lose marks** on TRUNCATE questions due to the following mistakes:
>
> - **Wrongly classifying** TRUNCATE as a DML command instead of DDL. (Lose 1 mark)
> - **Claiming TRUNCATE can be rolled back** — in most RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle), it cannot. (Lose 1 mark)
> - **Forgetting that TRUNCATE resets the identity counter** — this is a high-weight point. (Lose 1 mark)
> - **Writing TRUNCATE with a WHERE clause** — this is a syntax error; TRUNCATE does not support WHERE. (Lose 1 mark)
> - **Confusing DROP, TRUNCATE, and DELETE** — remember: DROP removes structure, TRUNCATE removes all rows, DELETE removes specific or all rows with full logging.
>
> Always state explicitly that TRUNCATE is **auto-committed**, **non-rollbackable**, **page-level**, and **identity-resetting**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TRUNCATE is a DDL command** — it auto-commits and cannot be rolled back in most RDBMS.
- **Syntax:** `TRUNCATE TABLE table_name;`
- **Removes ALL rows** — does NOT support a `WHERE` clause.
- **Preserves table structure** — columns, indexes, constraints remain intact.
- **Resets identity / auto-increment** columns to their original seed value.
- **Does NOT fire DELETE triggers** defined on the table.
- **Uses minimal logging** — only page deallocations are logged, not individual rows.
- **Significantly faster than DELETE** because it operates at the **page level**, not the row level.
- **Cannot be used on tables referenced by foreign keys** (unless CASCADE is specified).
- **Difference mnemonic:** "**TRUNCATE = Total Removal, Undoing Not Allowed, No Conditions Allowed, Cannot be Traced, Auto-commit, Trigger skip, Entity reset**".
- **Compare carefully with DELETE (DML) and DROP (DDL removes structure)** — these are the three most confused commands in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rename
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rename

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Relational Algebra (a procedural query language forming the theoretical foundation of SQL), the **Rename** operation is a unary operator denoted by the Greek letter **ρ (rho)**. It is used to assign a new name to the result of a relational algebra expression, either by renaming the relation itself, renaming its attributes, or both simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Rename operation ρ produces a new relation that is identical to the original relation except for its name and/or the names of some of its attributes. It does not alter the tuples or the schema values — it only changes the *label* by which the relation/attribute is referred to.

The Rename operator has **two standard syntactic forms** as prescribed in the KTU DBMS syllabus:

$$\rho_{R}(E)$$

$$\rho_{S(B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n)}(E)$$

Where:
- **E** is an arbitrary relational algebra expression that yields a relation.
- **R** is the new name of the resulting relation.
- **S** is the new relation name, and **B₁, B₂, …, Bₙ** are the new attribute names for the *n* attributes of E (in the same left-to-right order as they appear in the schema of E).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are a teacher who maintains a register of students. The original register is called "Class10A_Chemistry_Marks". For a parent–teacher meeting, you photocopy that same register but stick a new label on the cover that says "Mr. Sharma's Ward Report", and you also re-label the column "Total Marks" as "Final Score". The *data inside has not changed one bit*, but the way you *refer to it* has. This is exactly what the Rename operation does in a database.

Think of ρ as a **"label sticker"** — it never moves, deletes, or modifies a tuple. It simply lets you call the same table by a different name when you need to reuse it in a query (especially in joins where the same table is referenced twice).

### 1.3 Why Rename is Necessary — The Motivation

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** In Relational Algebra, every intermediate result is a relation and must have a name. Without Rename, you would have no systematic way to refer back to that intermediate result when composing queries or performing operations like self-joins.

The three primary engineering motivations for using ρ are:

1. **Self-Join Support** — When a query needs to compare rows of the same relation with other rows of that *same* relation, two distinct logical copies are required. Rename gives those copies distinct identities.
2. **Attribute Ambiguity Resolution** — When two relations in a Cartesian product or join share an attribute name, Rename disambiguates which one is being referenced.
3. **Compositional Query Clarity** — Nested expressions can become unreadable; renaming makes them traceable.

### 1.4 Properties of the Rename Operator

| Property | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Operator Type | **Unary** |
| Symbol | **ρ (rho)** |
| Arity (Inputs) | **1** expression E |
| Modifies Data? | **No** |
| Modifies Schema? | **Only labels (names)** |
| Result Cardinality | **Same as E** |
| Result Degree | **Same as E** |
| Standard Reference | Elmasri & Navathe, *Fundamentals of Database Systems* |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Formal Syntax Decomposition

The Rename operation can be broken into three distinct logical layers:

**Layer 1 — Pure Relation Rename:**
$$\rho_{R}(E)$$
- The resulting relation is named **R**.
- The attribute names are inherited unchanged from E.
- Use case: When you only need a different relation name (e.g., to reference the expression later).

**Layer 2 — Pure Attribute Rename:**
$$\rho_{(B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n)}(E)$$
- The relation name remains unchanged.
- The **i-th** attribute of E becomes **Bᵢ**.
- Critical rule: The number *n* must exactly equal the **arity (degree)** of E.

**Layer 3 — Combined Relation and Attribute Rename:**
$$\rho_{S(B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n)}(E)$$
- Both relation name and attribute labels are changed.
- This is the most commonly tested form in KTU examinations.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

When the DBMS engine evaluates a Rename expression, it executes the following logical sequence:

1. **Evaluate E first** — Compute the resulting relation from the inner expression. This is the standard "inside-out" evaluation rule of relational algebra.
2. **Verify arity match** — If attribute names are being changed, confirm that the number of new labels *n* equals the number of attributes produced by E.
3. **Apply new labels** — Strip the old attribute names from the schema of E and substitute them with the new names **B₁, …, Bₙ** in the same positional order.
4. **Bind the new relation name** — Associate the new relation name **R** (or **S**) with this relabeled result.
5. **Preserve all tuples** — Every tuple (t₁, t₂, …, tₙ) of E is carried over unchanged into the renamed relation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table captures every formula, rule, and boundary condition a KTU 2024 student is expected to memorize for the Rename operation.

| Concept | Formal Expression / Rule | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Relation Rename | $\rho_{R}(E)$ | Change only the relation's name |
| Attribute Rename | $\rho_{(B_1,\ldots,B_n)}(E)$ | Change only attribute names |
| Combined Rename | $\rho_{S(B_1,\ldots,B_n)}(E)$ | Change both relation and attribute names |
| Arity Constraint | $n = \text{arity}(E)$ | Number of new names must match degree of E |
| Cardinality | $\vert \rho(E) \vert = \vert E \vert$ | Tuple count is preserved |
| Degree | $\text{deg}(\rho(E)) = \text{deg}(E) = n$ | Attribute count is preserved |
| Order Preservation | $i\text{-th attribute of } \rho \equiv i\text{-th attribute of } E$ | Positional mapping is left-to-right |
| Tuple Preservation | $\forall t \in E, \, t \in \rho(E)$ | No tuple is added or removed |
| Self-Join Pattern | $E \bowtie \rho_{R'}(E)$ | Two logical copies of E joined |
| Theta-Join with Rename | $\sigma_{\theta}(R \times \rho_{S}(T))$ | Resolve attribute name conflicts |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Rename

The Rename operation is the silent workhorse behind nearly every practical database query. Its real-world utilities include:

- **SQL Self-Joins** — When you write `SELECT A.name, B.name FROM Employee A JOIN Employee B ON A.mgr_id = B.id`, the aliasing `A` and `B` are the direct SQL counterpart of relational algebra's ρ.
- **Data Warehousing** — Staging tables are often renamed before being loaded into star/snowflake schemas to follow naming conventions.
- **ETL Pipelines** — During Extract-Transform-Load, intermediate datasets are renamed to maintain traceability across stages.
- **View Definition** — In creating database views, Rename is implicitly used to provide clean column names to end users without altering the base tables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

### 3.1 Foundational Example — Simple Relation Rename

**Problem Setup:**
Let there be a relation:
$$\text{Student}(\text{SID}, \text{Name}, \text{Marks})$$
With tuples: {(101, Anu, 85), (102, Binu, 92), (103, Chinu, 78)}.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Express the rename operation:
$$\rho_{\text{Result}}(Student)$$

Step 2 — Evaluate the inner expression. Since *Student* is already a base relation, the evaluation simply yields the *Student* relation with its existing 3 tuples.

Step 3 — Apply the new relation name "Result" while keeping the attribute names (SID, Name, Marks) untouched.

Step 4 — Final result relation:
$$\text{Result}(\text{SID}, \text{Name}, \text{Marks}) = \{(101,\text{Anu},85),(102,\text{Binu},92),(103,\text{Chinu},78)\}$$

**Conclusion:** The Rename operation here produces a relation that is **structurally and data-wise identical** to Student but addressable by the new name "Result".

---

### 3.2 Attribute-Only Rename — Worked Derivation

**Problem Setup:**
Consider the expression:
$$\rho_{(\text{StudentID}, \text{FullName}, \text{Score})}(\text{Student})$$

Step 1 — The base relation Student has arity = **3** with attributes (SID, Name, Marks).

Step 2 — The rename specifies *n* = **3** new attribute names. Verify that *n* = arity(Student) = 3. ✓ (Constraint satisfied.)

Step 3 — Apply positional renaming:
- Position 1: SID → **StudentID**
- Position 2: Name → **FullName**
- Position 3: Marks → **Score**

Step 4 — The resulting relation has schema (StudentID, FullName, Score) but identical tuples.

**Verification of Cardinality and Degree:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert \rho_{(StudentID, FullName, Score)}(Student) \vert &= \vert Student \vert = 3 \\
\text{deg}\big(\rho_{(StudentID, FullName, Score)}(Student)\big) &= \text{deg}(Student) = 3
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Self-Join Application — The Classic KTU Question Pattern

**Problem Setup:**
Schema: **Employee(EID, Name, Salary, ManagerID)**

Question: *Find the names of all employees who earn more than their manager.*

**Full Analytical Derivation:**

Step 1 — We need to compare each employee with their manager. Since both are in the **same** relation, we need **two logical copies** of Employee. This is exactly where Rename is used.

Step 2 — Create two logical copies using ρ:
$$E_1 = \rho_{\text{Emp}}(EID, \text{EName}, \text{Sal}, \text{MgrID})(\text{Employee})$$
$$E_2 = \rho_{\text{Mgr}}(EID, \text{MName}, \text{MSal}, \text{MMgrID})(\text{Employee})$$

Step 3 — Apply the condition that E₁'s ManagerID must equal E₂'s EID (linking employee to manager):
$$\sigma_{\text{MgrID = EID}}(E_1 \times E_2)$$

Step 4 — Apply the salary condition (employee earns more than manager):
$$\sigma_{\text{Sal > MSal}}\big(\sigma_{\text{MgrID = EID}}(E_1 \times E_2)\big)$$

Step 5 — Project only the employee name:
$$\pi_{\text{EName}}\Big(\sigma_{\text{Sal > MSal}}\big(\sigma_{\text{MgrID = EID}}(E_1 \times E_2)\big)\big)$$

**Final Composed Expression:**

$$\pi_{\text{EName}}\Big(\sigma_{\text{Sal > MSal}}\big(\sigma_{\text{MgrID = EID}}\big(\rho_{\text{Emp}}\rho_{\text{Mgr}}(\text{Employee}) \times \rho_{\text{Mgr}}(\text{Employee})\big)\big)\Big)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without Rename, this query cannot be expressed.** Two distinct aliases of the same relation are mandatory to disambiguate the "employee" role from the "manager" role.

---

### 3.4 Verification of Preservation Laws

The Rename operator satisfies three core algebraic laws that KTU examiners frequently test:

**Law 1 — Idempotence of Rename:**
$$\rho_{R}\big(\rho_{S}(E)\big) = \rho_{R}(E)$$
Renaming an already-renamed relation to yet another name simply adopts the latest name; tuples remain untouched.

**Law 2 — Tuple Count Preservation:**
$$\forall E, \quad \vert \rho_{R}(E) \vert = \vert E \vert$$
Every tuple of E survives exactly once in ρ_R(E).

**Law 3 — Schema-Projection Compatibility:**
$$\pi_{A_1, \ldots, A_k}\big(\rho_{S(B_1, \ldots, B_n)}(E)\big) = \pi_{A'_1, \ldots, A'_k}(E)$$
where each A'ᵢ is the original-name counterpart of Aᵢ.

**Proof Sketch for Law 2 (Tuple Preservation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } T_E &= \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_m\} \quad \text{be the tuple set of } E. \\
\text{Apply } \rho_{R}: \quad T_{\rho(E)} &= \{t \mid t \in T_E\} \\
\therefore \quad \vert T_{\rho(E)} \vert &= m = \vert T_E \vert \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Operational Flow of the Rename Operation

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the exact decision flow executed by a DBMS engine when processing a Rename expression.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive rho Expression] --> B[Parse Expression rho R or rho S B1..Bn E]
    B --> C[Evaluate Inner Expression E]
    C --> D{Is E a Valid Relation?}
    D -- No --> E[Error: Invalid Inner Expression]
    D -- Yes --> F[Determine Arity of E = n]
    F --> G{Is Attribute Rename Requested?}
    G -- No --> H[Keep Original Attribute Names]
    G -- Yes --> I{Does Number of New Names Equal n?}
    I -- No --> J[Error: Arity Mismatch]
    I -- Yes --> K[Apply Positional Rename B1 to attr1, B2 to attr2, ..., Bn to attrn]
    H --> L[Bind New Relation Name R]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Preserve All Tuples from E]
    M --> N[Output: Renamed Relation with Same Data, New Labels]
    N --> O[End]
```

### 4.2 Self-Join Architecture Using Rename

This block diagram shows how Rename is used to create two logical copies of a relation for self-join operations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Employee Relation] --> B[Copy 1: rho Emp]
    A --> C[Copy 2: rho Mgr]
    B --> D[Cartesian Product]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Selection: MgrID equals EID]
    E --> F[Selection: Sal greater than MSal]
    F --> G[Projection: EName]
    G --> H[Final Result: Names of Employees Earning More Than Manager]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Rename as a Label Sticker

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputLayer [Input Layer]
        A1[Original Relation R with Attributes A1, A2, ..., An]
    end
    subgraph RenameLayer [Rename Processing]
        B1[Apply rho Operator]
        B2[Check Arity Constraint]
        B3[Map Old Labels to New Labels Positionally]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer [Output Layer]
        C1[New Relation S]
        C2[New Attributes B1, B2, ..., Bn]
        C3[Same Tuples Preserved Verbatim]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Form of Rename

| Scenario | Recommended ρ Form | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Self-join required | $\rho_{S}(E)$ with attribute rename | Both copies need distinct names + attributes |
| Only relation name needed | $\rho_{R}(E)$ | Minimal change, attribute clarity retained |
| Column labels confusing | $\rho_{(B_1,\ldots,B_n)}(E)$ | Improves readability of projection output |
| View creation | $\rho_{S(B_1,\ldots,B_n)}(E)$ | Exposes clean names to consumers |
| Nested query composition | $\rho_{R}(E)$ | Provides anchor name for downstream ops |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define the Rename operation in relational algebra. Mention its significance.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The Rename operation, denoted by ρ (rho), is a unary relational algebra operator used to assign a new name to the result of a relational algebra expression. Its two forms are $\rho_{R}(E)$ for renaming the relation only, and $\rho_{S(B_1,B_2,\ldots,B_n)}(E)$ for renaming both the relation and its attributes. Its significance lies in enabling self-joins, resolving attribute name ambiguities in joins and Cartesian products, and improving query composition clarity. The operation preserves both the tuple set and the arity of the original expression.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]**

**Q2. Why is the Rename operation essential for expressing self-joins in relational algebra?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A self-join requires comparing rows of a relation with other rows of the **same** relation. In relational algebra, every operand must be a distinct referenceable relation; therefore, two logical copies of the same relation must exist simultaneously. The Rename operation ρ provides these two copies with distinct names (and distinct attribute labels), enabling the DBMS to differentiate between, for example, an "employee" role and a "manager" role when both originate from the Employee table. Without ρ, the expression $E \bowtie E$ is syntactically ambiguous and semantically invalid.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Main Attempt]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the Rename operation in relational algebra with its syntax. Discuss the two syntactic forms with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Given the schema **Employee(EmpID, EmpName, Dept, Salary)**, write a relational algebra expression using Rename to find the names of all employees who work in the same department as 'Anu'. Show each step of derivation. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q A(a):

**Definition:**
The Rename operation ρ is a unary operator that produces a new relation identical to its input expression except for its relation name and/or attribute labels.

**Syntax Form 1 — Relation-only rename:**
$$\rho_{R}(E)$$
*Example:* $\rho_{\text{EmpTable}}(\text{Employee})$ renames the Employee relation to EmpTable.

**Syntax Form 2 — Combined relation and attribute rename:**
$$\rho_{S(B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n)}(E)$$
*Example:* $\rho_{\text{E}(ID, Name, Dept, Sal)}(\text{Employee})$ renames the relation to E and the attributes to ID, Name, Dept, Sal respectively.

**Key Properties:**

- Tuple preservation: $\vert \rho(E) \vert = \vert E \vert$
- Arity preservation: $\text{deg}(\rho(E)) = \text{deg}(E)$
- Positional mapping: i-th attribute of E becomes i-th new label Bᵢ.

#### Model Solution for Q A(b):

**Step 1:** Identify that we need to first find Anu's department.
$$\sigma_{\text{EmpName = 'Anu'}}(\text{Employee})$$
This yields Anu's record.

**Step 2:** Extract only the Dept attribute.
$$\pi_{\text{Dept}}(\sigma_{\text{EmpName = 'Anu'}}(\text{Employee}))$$
Call this intermediate result **D**.

**Step 3:** Rename D for clarity (optional but good practice):
$$\rho_{\text{AnuDept}}(\pi_{\text{Dept}}(\sigma_{\text{EmpName = 'Anu'}}(\text{Employee})))$$

**Step 4:** Now join Employee with AnuDept on matching Dept values:
$$\rho_{\text{AnuDept}}(\pi_{\text{Dept}}(\sigma_{\text{EmpName = 'Anu'}}(\text{Employee}))) \bowtie_{\text{Dept = Dept}} \text{Employee}$$

**Step 5:** Project the employee name from the result:
$$\pi_{\text{EmpName}}\Big(\rho_{\text{AnuDept}}(\pi_{\text{Dept}}(\sigma_{\text{EmpName = 'Anu'}}(\text{Employee}))) \bowtie_{\text{Dept = Dept}} \text{Employee}\Big)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently forget to apply Rename to disambiguate the common attribute 'Dept' appearing on both sides of the join. The result relation must have a clear schema before projection. Also, never include 'Anu' herself in the final output — add an additional selection condition `EmpName ≠ 'Anu'` to filter that out, otherwise marks are deducted.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** What is the need for the Rename operation in relational algebra? Illustrate with the self-join use case. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Consider the schema **Project(PID, PName, Budget, DeptID)** and **Department(DID, DName, Location)**. Write a relational algebra expression using Rename to find pairs of projects in the same department where the first project has a higher budget than the second. Show all steps. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q B(a):

**Need for Rename:**
The Rename operation addresses three critical engineering needs:

1. **Self-Join Enablement** — Allows a relation to be referenced multiple times in the same query with distinct logical identities.
2. **Attribute Disambiguation** — Resolves naming conflicts during natural joins, theta joins, and Cartesian products.
3. **Compositional Traceability** — Provides meaningful names to intermediate results in nested queries.

**Self-Join Illustration:**
Given *Employee(EID, Name, Salary, ManagerID)*, to find employees earning more than their manager:

- Without Rename: Cannot express because Employee cannot be both left and right operand simultaneously.
- With Rename:
$$\pi_{\text{Name}}\Big(\sigma_{\text{Sal > MSal}}\big(\sigma_{\text{MgrID = MEID}}\big(\rho_{\text{E1}}(\text{Employee}) \times \rho_{\text{E2}}(\text{Employee})\big)\big)\Big)$$

The two ρ operations create distinct logical copies E1 (the employee) and E2 (the manager) for the comparison.

#### Model Solution for Q B(b):

**Step 1:** Recognize this is a self-join problem on the Project relation. We need two copies of Project.

**Step 2:** Apply Rename to create two copies with distinct attribute names:
$$P_1 = \rho_{\text{P1}}(\text{PID}_1, \text{PName}_1, \text{Budget}_1, \text{DeptID}_1)(\text{Project})$$
$$P_2 = \rho_{\text{P2}}(\text{PID}_2, \text{PName}_2, \text{Budget}_2, \text{DeptID}_2)(\text{Project})$$

**Step 3:** Cartesian product of the two copies:
$$P_1 \times P_2$$

**Step 4:** Apply selection — same department AND first budget greater:
$$\sigma_{\text{DeptID}_1 = \text{DeptID}_2 \land \text{Budget}_1 > \text{Budget}_2 \land \text{PID}_1 \neq \text{PID}_2}(P_1 \times P_2)$$

**Step 5:** Project the relevant pair attributes:
$$\pi_{\text{PName}_1, \text{PName}_2}\Big(\sigma_{\text{DeptID}_1 = \text{DeptID}_2 \land \text{Budget}_1 > \text{Budget}_2 \land \text{PID}_1 \neq \text{PID}_2}\big(\rho_{\text{P1}}(\text{Project}) \times \rho_{\text{P2}}(\text{Project})\big)\Big)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Two common errors cost students heavy marks here:
> 1. **Missing the PID ≠ PID condition** — Without this, the same project pairs with itself and pollutes the result. Examiners specifically check this.
> 2. **Forgetting Rename on both copies** — If only one copy is renamed, attribute ambiguity in the Cartesian product makes the selection condition unresolvable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exams:**

- ☐ Rename is denoted by **ρ (rho)** and is a **unary** operator.
- ☐ Two syntactic forms: $\rho_{R}(E)$ and $\rho_{S(B_1,\ldots,B_n)}(E)$.
- ☐ Rename does **not** change data — it only changes **labels**.
- ☐ Cardinality preserved: $\vert \rho(E) \vert = \vert E \vert$.
- ☐ Degree preserved: $\text{deg}(\rho(E)) = \text{deg}(E) = n$.
- ☐ **Arity rule:** Number of new attribute names **must equal** arity of E.
- ☐ Attribute mapping is **positional and left-to-right** (i-th old attribute → i-th new label).
- ☐ **Primary use case:** Self-joins (same relation joined to itself).
- ☐ **Secondary use case:** Resolving attribute name conflicts in joins and products.
- ☐ **Tertiary use case:** Naming intermediate results for compositional queries.
- ☐ In SQL, Rename is implemented via **table aliases** and **column aliases** (`AS` keyword).
- ☐ The operation is **idempotent** under nested application: $\rho_{R}(\rho_{S}(E)) = \rho_{R}(E)$.
- ☐ When drawing relational algebra expression trees for the exam, always place ρ **closest to the leaf relation** it is renaming.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Manipulation Language (DML): Select
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Manipulation Language (DML): Select

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DML (Data Manipulation Language)** is the subset of SQL used to **retrieve, insert, update, and delete** data stored in relational database objects (tables/views). It operates on the *data* (rows/tuples) of the database, in contrast to DDL which manipulates the *schema* (structure).

The **`SELECT`** statement is the **read-only / retrieval** member of the DML quartet (alongside `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`). According to the **ISO/IEC 9075 (SQL standard)** and KTU 2024 syllabus, the canonical form of a query expression is:

$$
\text{SELECT } \langle \text{select\_list} \rangle \;\; \text{FROM } \langle \text{table\_source(s)} \rangle \;\; [\text{WHERE } \langle \text{predicate} \rangle] \;\; [\text{GROUP BY } \dots] \;\; [\text{HAVING } \dots] \;\; [\text{ORDER BY } \dots]
$$

A `SELECT` statement does **NOT** modify data. It produces a **result set** (a virtual, derived table) computed lazily by the query engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 2 — DML):** Students must master single-table retrieval, filtering, aggregation, grouping, ordering, aliases, and the conceptual *logical query processing phases*. Set operations and joins are typically covered in the adjoining module and form the foundation for advanced retrieval.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu"

Imagine a giant library of recipes (the **database**). The kitchen (DBMS engine) holds the raw ingredients in storerooms (**tables**).

- A **`SELECT`** is your *order slip* given to the waiter (the query processor).
- You specify **which columns** you want (the dish name) — *projection*.
- You specify **which rows** you want (ingredients available *today*) — *selection / filtering*.
- You may ask the chef to **group** dishes by cuisine and **count** them — *aggregation*.
- You may ask to **sort** the menu by price — *ordering*.
- The kitchen never alters its stockroom to fulfil your order; the dish is freshly prepared each time.

This mirrors the **declarative nature** of SQL: you state *what* you want, not *how* to fetch it.

## 1.3 Sub-Categories Within DML

| DML Command | Purpose | KTU 2024 Weight |
|---|---|---|
| `SELECT` | **Read / retrieve** data | **Highest (this topic)** |
| `INSERT` | Add new rows | Covered in adjacent topic |
| `UPDATE` | Modify existing rows | Covered in adjacent topic |
| `DELETE` | Remove rows | Covered in adjacent topic |

> [!TIP]
> The acronym **CRUD** (Create, Read, Update, Delete) maps perfectly: `SELECT` = **R**ead. In relational theory, `SELECT` corresponds to the **relational algebra** operations of *projection (π)*, *selection (σ)*, *rename (ρ)*, *union (∪)*, *intersection (∩)*, *difference (−)*, *cartesian product (×)*, and various *joins (⋈)*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Logical Clauses of a `SELECT` (Operational Decomposition)

A KTU examiner expects you to know each clause, its purpose, and **the order in which the engine logically processes them** (this is *not* the order they are written).

### Clause 1 — `SELECT <select_list>` (Projection)
- Specifies **which columns** (or computed expressions) appear in the output.
- Accepts column names, literals, expressions, aggregate calls, and the wildcard `*`.
- Supports **`DISTINCT`** to eliminate duplicate rows from the result.

### Clause 2 — `FROM <table_source>`
- Identifies the **source relation(s)** — tables, views, joined tables, subqueries.
- Supports **table aliases** (`FROM Employee E`) for brevity and self-joins.

### Clause 3 — `WHERE <search_condition>` (Row filter)
- Filters **rows *before* aggregation**. Works on raw column values (no aggregates allowed here).
- Predicates use comparison operators (`=`, `<>`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`), logical operators (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`), and special operators (`BETWEEN`, `IN`, `LIKE`, `IS NULL`, `EXISTS`).

### Clause 4 — `GROUP BY <grouping_columns>`
- Partitions the filtered rows into **groups** sharing identical values in the grouping columns.
- After `GROUP BY`, every column in the `SELECT` list must either be in the `GROUP BY` clause or be wrapped in an aggregate function.

### Clause 5 — `HAVING <group_filter>` (Group filter)
- Filters **groups *after* aggregation**. This is the *only* clause where aggregate functions (`COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`) are allowed in a predicate.
- Functionally similar to `WHERE` but operates on the *group level*.

### Clause 6 — `ORDER BY <sort_keys> [ASC | DESC]`
- Sorts the **final result set** (not the underlying table).
- Default order = **Ascending (ASC)**. `NULL`s are sorted as the lowest value (in ASC) in standard SQL.
- Column positions (e.g., `ORDER BY 2`) or aliases may be used — both are KTU-favoured shortcuts.

## 2.2 Aggregate (Set) Functions

| Function | Meaning | Returns `NULL` when… |
|---|---|---|
| `COUNT(*)` | Number of rows in the group | Never (counts rows, including `NULL`s) |
| `COUNT(expr)` | Number of non-NULL values of `expr` | When `expr` is `NULL` |
| `SUM(expr)` | Sum of `expr` | When group is empty (returns `NULL`) |
| `AVG(expr)` | Arithmetic mean of `expr` | When group is empty (returns `NULL`) |
| `MIN(expr)` | Smallest value of `expr` | When group is empty (returns `NULL`) |
| `MAX(expr)` | Largest value of `expr` | When group is empty (returns `NULL`) |

> [!NOTE]
> `COUNT(*)`, `COUNT(1)`, and `COUNT(<constant>)` all count rows and are semantically identical in standard SQL.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Special Operators

| Operator | Meaning | Example | KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| `BETWEEN a AND b` | Inclusive range | `salary BETWEEN 30000 AND 50000` | Boundaries are **inclusive** |
| `IN (v1, v2, ...)` | Membership in a list | `dept IN ('CS','IT')` | Equivalent to `= ANY(...)` |
| `LIKE 'pattern'` | Pattern match with `%` (any string) and `_` (one char) | `name LIKE 'A%'` | `%` matches **zero or more** characters |
| `IS NULL` / `IS NOT NULL` | Tests for unknown / missing value | `mgr IS NULL` | **Never** use `= NULL` — it is undefined |
| `EXISTS (subquery)` | True if subquery returns at least one row | `WHERE EXISTS (...)` | Common in correlated subqueries |
| `DISTINCT` | Eliminates duplicate rows | `SELECT DISTINCT dept` | Applied **across all** select-list columns |

## 2.4 Logical Query Processing Order (CRAOWH-S)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **logical processing order** differs from the **lexical (written) order**. KTU 2024 questions on "what is executed first?" test this exact distinction.

$$
\text{Written Order} \neq \text{Logical Processing Order}
$$

$$
\text{Written: } \;\text{SELECT} \rightarrow \text{FROM} \rightarrow \text{WHERE} \rightarrow \text{GROUP BY} \rightarrow \text{HAVING} \rightarrow \text{ORDER BY}
$$

$$
\text{Logical: } \;\text{FROM} \rightarrow \text{WHERE} \rightarrow \text{GROUP BY} \rightarrow \text{HAVING} \rightarrow \text{SELECT} \rightarrow \text{ORDER BY}
$$

| # | Phase | Operates on | Can use aggregates? | Can use column aliases? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `FROM` | Cartesian product + joins | No | No |
| 2 | `WHERE` | Rows | **No** | No |
| 3 | `GROUP BY` | Rows → groups | No | No |
| 4 | `HAVING` | Groups | **Yes** | No |
| 5 | `SELECT` | Final projection, `DISTINCT` | Yes (if grouped) | **Yes** (for `ORDER BY`) |
| 6 | `ORDER BY` | Result set rows | Yes | **Yes** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Information systems:** Reports, dashboards, MIS — every `SELECT` powers a business report.
- **Data science pipelines:** `SELECT` forms the extraction (`E`) step of **ETL** (Extract, Transform, Load).
- **Web applications:** ORM frameworks (Hibernate, Django ORM) ultimately generate `SELECT` statements to hydrate objects.
- **Telecom / IoT:** Time-series analytics — `SELECT AVG(signal) FROM sensor WHERE ts BETWEEN ...`.
- **Banking:** Fraud detection uses windowed `SELECT` (analytical functions) over transaction logs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section **decomposes** representative `SELECT` queries clause-by-clause, mirroring the logical processing order. Master this flow — it is the single most-tested KTU concept under DML.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Single-Table Retrieval with Filter

**Schema (running example used throughout the notes):**

$$
\text{Employee}(\underline{\text{emp\_id}},\; \text{ename},\; \text{dept},\; \text{salary},\; \text{hire\_date},\; \text{mgr\_id})
$$

**Question:** *List the name, department, and salary of all employees in the 'CS' department earning more than 50,000, ordered by salary descending.*

**SQL Solution (written order):**

```sql
SELECT ename, dept, salary
  FROM Employee
 WHERE dept = 'CS'
   AND salary > 50000
 ORDER BY salary DESC;
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Evaluation

**Step 1 — `FROM Employee`:**  
Identify the source table. The engine reads the physical pages of `Employee` and produces an initial virtual table **VT1** containing **all rows and all columns**.

**Step 2 — `WHERE dept = 'CS' AND salary > 50000`:**  
For each row in **VT1**, evaluate the predicate.  
- Row passes only if `dept` equals the string literal `'CS'` **AND** `salary` is strictly greater than `50000`.  
- Result: filtered virtual table **VT2** (a strict subset of **VT1**).

**Step 3 — `SELECT ename, dept, salary`:**  
Project only the three named columns. The result table **VT3** has the same row count as **VT2** but only 3 columns.

**Step 4 — `ORDER BY salary DESC`:**  
Sort **VT3** by the `salary` column in descending order. This is the **final result set** returned to the user.

> [!NOTE]
> **Derived Predicate:** The conjunction `p1 AND p2` is true only when both `p1` and `p2` are true. Its truth table has exactly one `T` row out of four.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= (\text{dept} = \text{'CS'}) \\
p_2 &= (\text{salary} > 50000) \\
\phi &= p_1 \;\wedge\; p_2
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Aggregation with `GROUP BY` and `HAVING`

**Question:** *For each department having more than 2 employees, display the department name and the average salary. Sort the result by average salary descending.*

```sql
  SELECT dept, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
    FROM Employee
GROUP BY dept
  HAVING COUNT(*) > 2
ORDER BY avg_sal DESC;
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Evaluation

**Step 1 — `FROM Employee` → VT1:** All rows, all columns.

**Step 2 — `WHERE` (none here) → VT2 = VT1:** No filtering applied.

**Step 3 — `GROUP BY dept` → VT3:** Partition **VT2** into buckets where all rows share the same `dept` value. Suppose the departments are `{CS, IT, HR, FIN}` — we get four groups.

**Step 4 — `HAVING COUNT(*) > 2` → VT4:** For each group, compute `COUNT(*)`. Discard groups whose count ≤ 2. The remaining groups form **VT4**.

**Step 5 — `SELECT dept, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal` → VT5:**  
For each surviving group, compute the mean of `salary` and project two columns: the `dept` value and the computed average. The alias `avg_sal` is **assigned here** — it is **not** available earlier in the pipeline.

**Step 6 — `ORDER BY avg_sal DESC` → VT6:** Sort **VT5** using the alias `avg_sal` (allowed because `ORDER BY` runs *after* `SELECT`).

### Aggregate Algebra (Formal Derivation)

For a relation $R$ partitioned by attribute $A$ into groups $G_1, G_2, \dots, G_k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_i &= \sigma_{A = a_i}(R) \\
\text{avg\_sal}_i &= \frac{1}{\vert G_i \vert} \sum_{t \in G_i} t.\text{salary} \\
\text{VT3} &= \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} \big\{(a_i,\; \text{avg\_sal}_i)\big\} \\
\text{VT4} &= \sigma_{\vert G_i \vert > 2}(\text{VT3})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** You **cannot** use a column alias defined in `SELECT` inside `WHERE` or `GROUP BY`. Example: `WHERE avg_sal > 50000` will throw *Invalid column name*. Use the underlying expression: `WHERE AVG(salary) > 50000` is also invalid in `WHERE` — you must use `HAVING` for that.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Pattern Matching with `LIKE`

**Question:** *Find all employees whose name starts with 'A' and ends with 'n', works in any of ('CS','IT','ECE'), and was hired between 2018 and 2024.*

```sql
SELECT emp_id, ename, hire_date
  FROM Employee
 WHERE ename LIKE 'A%n'
   AND dept IN ('CS','IT','ECE')
   AND hire_date BETWEEN '2018-01-01' AND '2024-12-31';
```

### Pattern Mechanics

| Pattern | Matches | Does NOT match |
|---|---|---|
| `'A%n'` | "An", "Aman", "Arun" | "aMan" (case sensitive by default in most DBs) |
| `'A_n'` | "Aan", "Abn" | "Ann", "Arjun" (≥2 chars between) |
| `'%@%.com'` | "a@b.com", "x@y.z.com" | "abc.com" |
| `'___'` | Any string of length 3 | "ab", "abcd" |

The predicate `hire_date BETWEEN '2018-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'` is fully equivalent to:

$$
(\text{hire\_date} \geq \text{'2018-01-01'}) \;\wedge\; (\text{hire\_date} \leq \text{'2024-12-31'})
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — `NULL` Semantics (Three-Valued Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SQL uses **three-valued logic (3VL)**: `TRUE`, `FALSE`, `UNKNOWN`. Any predicate involving `NULL` evaluates to `UNKNOWN` — and rows with `UNKNOWN` are **filtered out** by `WHERE` / `HAVING`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\text{NULL} = 5) &\Rightarrow \text{UNKNOWN} \\
(\text{NULL} <> 5) &\Rightarrow \text{UNKNOWN} \\
(\text{NULL} \;\text{IS NULL}) &\Rightarrow \text{TRUE} \\
(\text{NULL} \;\text{IS NOT NULL}) &\Rightarrow \text{FALSE} \\
\text{TRUE} \;\wedge\; \text{UNKNOWN} &\Rightarrow \text{UNKNOWN} \\
\text{FALSE} \;\vee\; \text{UNKNOWN} &\Rightarrow \text{UNKNOWN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**KTU Trick Question:** "Find employees who do NOT earn 50000." — writing `WHERE salary <> 50000` will **silently exclude** rows where `salary IS NULL`. The correct form is:

```sql
WHERE salary <> 50000 OR salary IS NULL;
```

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Clause × Allowed Elements

| Clause | Column references | Aggregate functions | Aliases defined in `SELECT` | Subqueries |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `SELECT` | ✅ | ✅ (if grouped or no grouping needed) | — (defines them) | ✅ (scalar) |
| `FROM` | ✅ (table columns) | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (derived tables) |
| `WHERE` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| `GROUP BY` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `HAVING` | ✅ (grouped cols) | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| `ORDER BY` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (rare) |

## 3.6 KTU 2024 Style — Convert English to SQL (Practice Algorithm)

When the question gives a natural-language requirement, use this **5-step translation algorithm** to avoid missing clauses:

1. **Identify the source table(s)** → `FROM` clause.
2. **Identify filter conditions on raw rows** → `WHERE` clause.
3. **Identify grouping requirement** (e.g., "for each department", "per category") → `GROUP BY`.
4. **Identify filter conditions on groups** (e.g., "more than 2", "average > X") → `HAVING`.
5. **Identify output columns and order** → `SELECT` and `ORDER BY`.
6. *(Bonus)* If duplicates should be removed → add `DISTINCT` in `SELECT`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logical Query Processing Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

This diagram visualises the **execution order** of a `SELECT` statement — the most-tested KTU concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Parse SQL] --> B[FROM clause - Load source table/s]
    B --> C{Cartesian Product needed?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply JOIN conditions ON]
    C -- No --> E[Single table VT1]
    D --> F[VT2: Joined rows]
    E --> G[WHERE clause - Row filter]
    F --> G
    G --> H{VT3: Filtered rows}
    H --> I{GROUP BY present?}
    I -- No --> K[HAVING skipped]
    I -- Yes --> L[Group rows by column values - VT4]
    L --> M[HAVING clause - Group filter]
    M --> N[VT5: Surviving groups]
    K --> O[SELECT list - Projection and DISTINCT]
    N --> O
    O --> P[VT6: Final projection]
    P --> Q{ORDER BY present?}
    Q -- No --> S[Return result set]
    Q -- Yes --> R[Sort VT6 by sort keys ASC or DESC]
    R --> S[Return sorted result set]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** `WHERE` filters **rows**, `HAVING` filters **groups**, `SELECT` projects **columns**, `ORDER BY` sorts the **final output**.

## 4.2 Clause Dependency Map

This block diagram shows the **input–output data flow** between the six clauses, highlighting that aliases flow forward but never backward.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[(Source Table/s)] --> FROM[FROM]
    FROM --> WHERE[WHERE]
    WHERE --> GROUP[GROUP BY]
    GROUP --> HAVING[HAVING]
    HAVING --> SELECTLIST[SELECT - Column List and DISTINCT]
    SELECTLIST --> ORDER[ORDER BY]
    ORDER --> RESULT[/Final Result Set to Client/]

    A1[Alias AS x] -. defined in .-> SELECTLIST
    A2[Alias x available] -. consumed by .-> ORDER
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping: SQL Clauses ↔ Relational Algebra

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SQL_FROM[SQL FROM clause] --> RA_X[RA Cartesian Product X]
    SQL_WHERE[SQL WHERE clause] --> RA_SIGMA[RA Selection sigma]
    SQL_SELECT[SQL SELECT clause] --> RA_PI[RA Projection pi]
    SQL_ORDER[SQL ORDER BY] --> RA_TAU[RA Sort tau]
    SQL_GROUP[SQL GROUP BY and aggregates] --> RA_SUM[RA Summarization / Extended RA]
    SQL_HAVING[SQL HAVING] --> RA_SIGMA_G[RA Selection on groups]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — "WHERE or HAVING?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Need to filter something?] --> Q1{Is it a row-level condition?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Aggregate function involved?}
    Q2 -- No --> W[Use WHERE clause]
    Q2 -- Yes, e.g. AVG salary > 50000 --> H[Use HAVING clause]
    Q1 -- No, group-level condition --> H2[Use HAVING clause]
```

## 4.5 Schema Block Diagram (Running Example)

```mermaid
erDiagram
    EMPLOYEE {
        int emp_id PK
        varchar ename
        varchar dept
        decimal salary
        date hire_date
        int mgr_id FK
    }
    DEPARTMENT {
        varchar dept_name PK
        varchar location
    }
    EMPLOYEE }o--|| DEPARTMENT : "works in (dept = dept_name)"
    EMPLOYEE }o--o| EMPLOYEE : "manages (mgr_id = emp_id)"
```

> [!NOTE]
> `mgr_id` is a **recursive foreign key** (self-referencing) — common in real schemas for representing hierarchies. Self-joins use this pattern, though joins themselves fall in the next KTU module.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Differentiate between the `WHERE` and `HAVING` clauses in SQL. Can `WHERE` clause contain aggregate functions? Justify.**

**Model Answer (≈3-mark depth):**

| Property | `WHERE` | `HAVING` |
|---|---|---|
| **Filters** | Individual **rows** | **Groups** of rows |
| **Execution phase** | Before `GROUP BY` | After `GROUP BY` |
| **Aggregate functions allowed** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Operates on** | Raw column values | Grouped/aggregated values |
| **Typical use** | Row-level predicates | "Departments having more than 5 employees" |

**Justification:** No, the `WHERE` clause **cannot** contain aggregate functions. This is because `WHERE` is logically evaluated *before* the `GROUP BY` phase, so groups do not yet exist and their aggregates cannot be computed. Any attempt such as `WHERE AVG(salary) > 50000` will be rejected by the SQL engine with a *"Invalid use of group function"* error. The correct placement is `HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000`.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the logical processing order of clauses in a `SELECT` statement. Why is it different from the written order?**

**Model Answer:**

The written (lexical) order of a `SELECT` is `SELECT → FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → ORDER BY`. However, the SQL engine logically processes the clauses in the following sequence:

1. **`FROM`** — Identify and combine source tables (cartesian product, then apply joins).
2. **`WHERE`** — Filter individual rows using row-level predicates.
3. **`GROUP BY`** — Partition surviving rows into groups.
4. **`HAVING`** — Filter groups using aggregate predicates.
5. **`SELECT`** — Project the final column list, evaluate expressions, apply `DISTINCT`.
6. **`ORDER BY`** — Sort the final result set.

**Why the difference?** The order is dictated by *data dependencies*. A row filter (`WHERE`) must run before grouping because groups do not exist beforehand. Aggregates in `HAVING` require groups to exist. Column aliases defined in `SELECT` are only available *after* projection, hence they can be referenced in `ORDER BY` (which runs later) but **not** in `WHERE` (which runs earlier). This is why the logical order is *not* the written order — it is the only order that satisfies the data-availability constraints.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — `SELECT` Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting `GROUP BY` columns** — every non-aggregated column in `SELECT` MUST appear in `GROUP BY`. Marks lost = **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using `=` instead of `IS` for NULL** — `WHERE mgr_id = NULL` is **always false**; you must write `IS NULL`. Marks lost = **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing `WHERE` and `HAVING`** — placing aggregate predicates in `WHERE` causes a *runtime error*. Marks lost = **2 marks**.
> 4. **Writing `LIKE`** patterns incorrectly — `%` matches *zero or more characters*, not exactly one. Use `_` for a single character. Marks lost = **1 mark**.
> 5. **Using `ORDER BY` column aliases in `WHERE`** — alias is unknown at `WHERE` time; this throws an error. Marks lost = **1 mark**.
> 6. **Not applying `DISTINCT`** when the question demands "unique" / "different" / "distinct" values. Marks lost = **1 mark**.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]`

**(a)** Consider the following relational schema: **[7 Marks]**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Employee}(\underline{\text{Eid}}, \;\text{EName},\; \text{Dept},\; \text{Salary},\; \text{HireDate},\; \text{MgrId}) \\
&\text{Department}(\underline{\text{DName}},\; \text{Location},\; \text{Budget})
\end{aligned}
$$

Write SQL `SELECT` statements for the following:

1. **(2 marks)** Display the name, department, and salary of all employees whose name starts with 'S' and who earn between 30,000 and 70,000.

2. **(2 marks)** Find the total salary payout for each department. Rename the computed column as `TotalPayout`.

3. **(3 marks)** Display departments whose average salary is greater than 60,000 and which have at least 3 employees. Sort the result by average salary in descending order.

#### Model Solution

**(1) Name starts with 'S' and salary in [30000, 70000]:**

```sql
SELECT EName, Dept, Salary
  FROM Employee
 WHERE EName LIKE 'S%'
   AND Salary BETWEEN 30000 AND 70000;
```

**Explanation:** `LIKE 'S%'` matches any name beginning with the letter `S` (with any number of following characters, including zero). `BETWEEN 30000 AND 70000` is an inclusive range, equivalent to `Salary >= 30000 AND Salary <= 70000`.

**(2) Total salary per department:**

```sql
SELECT Dept, SUM(Salary) AS TotalPayout
  FROM Employee
GROUP BY Dept;
```

**Explanation:** The `GROUP BY Dept` clause partitions all employees into groups sharing the same `Dept` value. `SUM(Salary)` is computed *per group*. The alias `TotalPayout` is defined in the `SELECT` clause and becomes the column header of the result.

**(3) Departments with average salary > 60,000 and at least 3 employees, sorted by average salary DESC:**

```sql
  SELECT Dept, AVG(Salary) AS AvgSal
    FROM Employee
GROUP BY Dept
  HAVING AVG(Salary) > 60000
     AND COUNT(*) >= 3
ORDER BY AvgSal DESC;
```

**Explanation:** This query demonstrates all six clauses.  
- `FROM Employee` loads the source.  
- `GROUP BY Dept` creates one group per department.  
- `HAVING AVG(Salary) > 60000 AND COUNT(*) >= 3` filters out groups that do not meet *both* conditions — note that aggregate functions can appear in `HAVING` but **never** in `WHERE`.  
- `SELECT Dept, AVG(Salary) AS AvgSal` projects two columns and assigns the alias.  
- `ORDER BY AvgSal DESC` sorts the final output by the alias `AvgSal` (legal because `ORDER BY` runs after `SELECT`).

---

**(b)** Explain the concept of **three-valued logic** in SQL with respect to `NULL`. How does it affect the evaluation of `WHERE` predicates? Provide an example to show why `column = NULL` is incorrect and what the correct form is. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Three-Valued Logic (3VL):**  
Standard boolean logic has two truth values: `TRUE` and `FALSE`. SQL extends this with a third value, **`UNKNOWN`**, primarily to handle `NULL` (which represents *unknown* or *not applicable*). Every comparison involving `NULL` yields `UNKNOWN`, never `TRUE` or `FALSE`.

**Truth table for `AND`:**

| p | q | p AND q |
|---|---|---|
| T | T | T |
| T | F | F |
| T | U | **U** |
| F | T | F |
| F | F | F |
| F | U | F |
| U | T | **U** |
| U | F | F |
| U | U | **U** |

**Effect on `WHERE`:** The `WHERE` clause retains only those rows for which the predicate evaluates to `TRUE`. Rows evaluating to `FALSE` **or** `UNKNOWN` are discarded. This means a `NULL` value effectively makes the row *invisible* to most filter predicates.

**Why `column = NULL` is incorrect:**  
`column = NULL` is itself a comparison involving `NULL`, so it always evaluates to `UNKNOWN`. The row will never be returned. This is a famously common bug.

**Example:** To find employees who do **not** earn exactly 50,000:

```sql
-- WRONG (silently excludes NULL salaries):
SELECT * FROM Employee WHERE Salary <> 50000;

-- CORRECT (includes NULL salaries):
SELECT * FROM Employee WHERE Salary <> 50000 OR Salary IS NULL;
```

**The correct way to test for NULL is:**

```sql
SELECT * FROM Employee WHERE MgrId IS NULL;       -- find employees with no manager
SELECT * FROM Employee WHERE MgrId IS NOT NULL;   -- find employees with a manager
```

**Real-world implication:** Reports that use `WHERE column = value` without explicit `IS NULL` handling can silently undercount data, leading to inaccurate business intelligence. KTU questions frequently test this with the famous "find rows *not matching* a value" trap.

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]`

**(a)** Consider the same `Employee` schema as above. Write SQL `SELECT` statements for each of the following: **[7 Marks]**

1. **(2 marks)** Find the names of distinct departments in the `Employee` table.

2. **(2 marks)** List the top 3 highest-paid employees in the 'CS' department, showing their name, salary, and hire date.

3. **(3 marks)** For each department, display the department name, the number of employees, the minimum salary, and the maximum salary. Show only those departments where the maximum salary exceeds 90,000.

#### Model Solution

**(1) Distinct department names:**

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT Dept
  FROM Employee;
```

**Explanation:** `DISTINCT` removes duplicate rows from the result set. Without `DISTINCT`, the query would return one row per employee. The result is the set of unique department values.

**(2) Top 3 highest-paid CS employees:**

```sql
SELECT TOP 3 EName, Salary, HireDate
  FROM Employee
 WHERE Dept = 'CS'
ORDER BY Salary DESC;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **`TOP` is SQL Server / Sybase syntax.** The KTU 2024 syllabus allows either vendor dialect; the equivalent in **MySQL / PostgreSQL** is `LIMIT 3`, and in **Oracle** it is `FETCH FIRST 3 ROWS ONLY`. All three are accepted forms; the `ORDER BY` is **mandatory** to give "top" meaning.

**Explanation:** The `ORDER BY Salary DESC` sorts the CS employees by salary, highest first. `TOP 3` retains only the first three. The `ORDER BY` clause is essential — without it, "top 3" has no defined meaning.

**(3) Department-level statistics where max salary > 90,000:**

```sql
  SELECT Dept,
         COUNT(*)      AS EmpCount,
         MIN(Salary)   AS MinSal,
         MAX(Salary)   AS MaxSal
    FROM Employee
GROUP BY Dept
  HAVING MAX(Salary) > 90000;
```

**Explanation:**  
- `GROUP BY Dept` creates one group per department.  
- For each group, the `SELECT` clause computes four values: the department name, the row count, the minimum, and the maximum salary.  
- `HAVING MAX(Salary) > 90000` discards groups whose peak salary is 90,000 or below. This filter *cannot* be expressed in `WHERE` because `MAX` is an aggregate function.

---

**(b)** With a neat flowchart/diagram, explain the **logical processing order of the `SELECT` statement**. Why must `WHERE` run before `GROUP BY` and `HAVING` run after `GROUP BY`? What happens if a column alias defined in `SELECT` is used inside `WHERE`? **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Logical Processing Order (text + diagram description):**

The engine processes the clauses in this order:

$$
\text{FROM} \rightarrow \text{WHERE} \rightarrow \text{GROUP BY} \rightarrow \text{HAVING} \rightarrow \text{SELECT} \rightarrow \text{ORDER BY}
$$

```
+----------+      +---------+      +-----------+      +---------+      +-----------+      +----------+
|   FROM   | ---> |  WHERE  | ---> | GROUP BY  | ---> | HAVING  | ---> |  SELECT   | ---> | ORDER BY |
+----------+      +---------+      +-----------+      +---------+      +-----------+      +----------+
   Load              Filter           Partition        Filter           Project &        Sort final
   tables            rows             rows ->          groups           compute           result
                                      groups                           aliases
```

**Why `WHERE` before `GROUP BY`:**  
`WHERE` operates on individual rows and their raw column values. Groups are an artefact of `GROUP BY`; before that phase, the very concept of "group" does not exist. Therefore, row-level predicates must be evaluated first. This is also why aggregate functions like `SUM` or `AVG` are **not allowed** in `WHERE` — there is nothing to aggregate at the row level. Placing a `WHERE Avg(salary) > 50000` is a syntax/runtime error.

**Why `HAVING` after `GROUP BY`:**  
After `GROUP BY`, each group is a single virtual row in the relation. `HAVING` is the only clause in which aggregate functions may legally appear in a predicate, because by that point the groups and their aggregates exist. `HAVING` then filters out entire groups that fail the predicate.

**Column alias in `WHERE` — what happens?**  
A column alias (e.g., `AS AvgSal`) is *defined* in the `SELECT` clause. Since the `SELECT` clause runs **after** `WHERE` in the logical order, the alias is *not yet known* when `WHERE` is evaluated. Attempting to use it in `WHERE` produces the error:

```
Invalid column name 'AvgSal'
```

The correct placement of any predicate that references a computed column or aggregate is **after** the alias is defined — i.e., in `HAVING` (for groups) or `ORDER BY` (for the final output).

**Example of the bug:**

```sql
-- WRONG (error at parse/resolve time):
SELECT Dept, AVG(Salary) AS AvgSal
  FROM Employee
 WHERE AvgSal > 60000          -- alias not yet defined!
GROUP BY Dept;

-- CORRECT (alias moved to HAVING or ORDER BY):
SELECT Dept, AVG(Salary) AS AvgSal
  FROM Employee
GROUP BY Dept
HAVING AVG(Salary) > 60000;     -- uses raw expression
```

The takeaway: **aliases flow forward, not backward** — they are valid only in clauses that execute *after* the `SELECT` clause, namely `ORDER BY`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam Rapid-Revision (last 5 minutes before walking in):**

- ✅ `SELECT` is a **read-only DML** command — it never modifies data.
- ✅ The **logical processing order** is `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY`. **Memorize this** — it is the single most-frequent KTU question on this module.
- ✅ `WHERE` filters **rows**; `HAVING` filters **groups**. They are **not** interchangeable.
- ✅ **Aggregate functions** (`COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`) are **forbidden in `WHERE`**; use them only in `HAVING` or `SELECT`.
- ✅ Every non-aggregated column in `SELECT` must appear in `GROUP BY` (this is the *single value rule*).
- ✅ Use `IS NULL` / `IS NOT NULL` — never `= NULL` or `<> NULL`.
- ✅ `LIKE` wildcards: `%` = any string (incl. empty), `_` = exactly one character.
- ✅ `BETWEEN x AND y` is **inclusive** of both boundaries.
- ✅ `ORDER BY` accepts **column aliases** from `SELECT`; `WHERE` and `GROUP BY` do **not**.
- ✅ `DISTINCT` applies to the **entire** select list (combination of all listed columns).
- ✅ Default sort order in `ORDER BY` is **ASC** (ascending).
- ✅ `NULL` evaluates to **`UNKNOWN`** in 3VL, which is **filtered out** by `WHERE` and `HAVING`.
- ✅ The standard's truth value for `(NULL = NULL)` is **`UNKNOWN`**, not `TRUE`.
- ✅ Set operations (`UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT`) and `JOIN` types are separate sub-topics in the KTU 2024 syllabus — typically Module 3.
- ✅ `TOP n` (SQL Server), `LIMIT n` (MySQL/PostgreSQL), `FETCH FIRST n ROWS ONLY` (Oracle/ISO SQL:2008) are equivalent syntaxes for limiting result rows.
- ✅ Remember: the `SELECT` clause is **last to run among the data-producing clauses** — it does not *find* rows, it merely *shapes* the result that the earlier phases produced.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Insert
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Insert

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Database Management Systems (DBMS)** under the KTU 2024 syllabus, the **INSERT** operation is a core **Data Manipulation Language (DML)** command in **Structured Query Language (SQL)** used to add new rows (tuples) into an existing relational table. It allows the user to populate a relation with either explicit data values, the result of a sub-query, or bulk rows through a single statement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** INSERT is grouped under **DML (Data Manipulation Language)** alongside `SELECT`, `UPDATE`, and `DELETE`. It does **NOT** auto-commit unless the DBMS is in auto-commit mode (e.g., MySQL) or the transaction is explicitly committed using `COMMIT`.

The ANSI SQL standard syntax backbone is:

```sql
INSERT INTO table_name (column_list) VALUES (value_list);
```

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Think of a **relational table** as a **register/ledger book** in an accounts office.

- Each **column** is a *column heading* in the ledger (Name, Register No, Marks, etc.).
- Each **row** is a *new entry* written into the book.
- The **INSERT** statement is the **pen action** — the moment you write a fresh entry into the ledger, you are performing an insertion.

Just as you cannot write a half-entry in a real ledger without filling in at least the required fields, in SQL, you cannot insert a row unless **NOT NULL constraints** are satisfied. Also, if the ledger has a pre-numbered page (an `AUTO_INCREMENT` column), you don't need to write the page number manually — the system fills it for you.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In a University ERP system (used heavily in KTU-affiliated colleges), `INSERT` is used when a student registers for a semester — student details, course enrollments, and fee records are inserted into tables like `STUDENT`, `ENROLLMENT`, and `PAYMENT`.

### 1.3 Categories of INSERT

| Variant | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Single-row INSERT with VALUES** | Insert exactly one row using literal values. |
| **Multi-row INSERT with VALUES** | Insert several rows in a single statement (tuple list). |
| **INSERT with Sub-query (INSERT...SELECT)** | Insert rows derived from another table's query result. |
| **INSERT with DEFAULT VALUES** | Insert a row where unspecified columns take their default values. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "write an INSERT query," always specify the **column list explicitly**. Writing `INSERT INTO Student VALUES (...);` without columns is considered poor practice and may cost marks in valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown

The INSERT command operates on the relational algebra concept of **insertion** $\sigma_{new} \leftarrow R \cup \tau$, where:

- $R$ = the existing relation (target table)
- $\tau$ = the new tuple(s) to be added

For every INSERT, the DBMS engine performs the following sequence of logical checks (constraint enforcement):

1. **Syntax Validation** — Checks whether the statement conforms to the SQL grammar.
2. **Semantic Resolution** — Verifies that the table and columns exist in the catalog.
3. **Type Checking** — Ensures each value matches the declared column data type.
4. **Constraint Enforcement** — Evaluates `PRIMARY KEY` (uniqueness), `UNIQUE`, `NOT NULL`, `CHECK`, and `FOREIGN KEY` rules.
5. **Trigger Activation** — Fires any `BEFORE INSERT` and `AFTER INSERT` triggers.
6. **Write to Buffer/Disk** — Logs the change to the transaction log (for rollback support) and writes the row to the data page.

> [!NOTE]
> If **any** of the above steps fail, the entire INSERT is rolled back atomically — this is the **ACID Atomicity** property that KTU examiners frequently test.

### 2.2 KTU Syntax Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below replaces the conventional "Formula Sheet" because INSERT is a declarative DML operation rather than a numerical formula. All syntax patterns must be memorized verbatim for the KTU ESE.

| # | Syntax Pattern | Purpose / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `INSERT INTO T (c1,c2) VALUES (v1,v2);` | Single-row literal insertion (most common). |
| 2 | `INSERT INTO T VALUES (v1,v2,v3);` | All-column insertion in declared order (risky — avoid). |
| 3 | `INSERT INTO T (c1,c2) VALUES (r1c1,r1c2),(r2c1,r2c2);` | Bulk / multi-row insertion (efficient for batch loading). |
| 4 | `INSERT INTO T1 (c1,c2) SELECT c1,c2 FROM T2 WHERE cond;` | Insertion via sub-query / cross-table copy. |
| 5 | `INSERT INTO T DEFAULT VALUES;` | Insert a row where every column takes its default. |
