| 6 | `INSERT INTO T (c1,c2) VALUES (v1,v2) ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE c2=v2;` | Upsert in MySQL — update on duplicate key conflict. |
| 7 | `INSERT IGNORE INTO T ... ;` | Skip rows that violate uniqueness (MySQL specific). |

### 2.3 Order of Column Provisioning (Column List Rule)

For a row to be inserted legally, the column list must obey:

$$
\text{Provided Columns} \;\cup\; \text{Columns with DEFAULT} \;\cup\; \text{NULL-allowed Columns} \;=\; \text{All Columns}
$$

Equivalently, the *missing* columns must satisfy:

$$
\text{Missing} \subseteq \text{(Columns with DEFAULT)} \cup \text{(Nullable Columns)} \cup \text{(AUTO\_INCREMENT Columns)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Bulk INSERT statements are extensively used in **data warehousing ETL pipelines** (Extract → Transform → Load) where millions of records are moved from staging tables into fact tables during nightly batch jobs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Examples with Model Solutions

#### Example 1 — Single-Row INSERT (Literal Values)

**Given Schema:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Student (
    RegNo      VARCHAR(15) PRIMARY KEY,
    Name       VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    Dept       VARCHAR(5)  DEFAULT 'CSE',
    CGPA       DECIMAL(4,2),
    DOB        DATE
);
```

**Question:** Write an INSERT query to add a student with RegNo `KTU2024CS001`, Name `Anand`, CGPA `8.75`, DOB `2004-05-12`. Dept may take its default.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\text{Step 1: Identify required columns} \Rightarrow \text{RegNo, Name, CGPA, DOB}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Identify omitted columns} \Rightarrow \text{Dept (has DEFAULT 'CSE')}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Match values to columns positionally}
$$

```sql
INSERT INTO Student (RegNo, Name, CGPA, DOB)
VALUES ('KTU2024CS001', 'Anand', 8.75, '2004-05-12');
```

> Dept will automatically be assigned `'CSE'` by the DBMS engine due to the DEFAULT clause.

---

#### Example 2 — Multi-Row INSERT (Bulk Insertion)

**Question:** Insert three students in a single statement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1} &: \;\text{RegNo='KTU2024CS002', Name='Beena', Dept='ECE', CGPA=9.10} \\
\text{Row 2} &: \;\text{RegNo='KTU2024CS003', Name='Chitra', Dept='Mech', CGPA=7.85} \\
\text{Row 3} &: \;\text{RegNo='KTU2024CS004', Name='Deepak', Dept='CSE', CGPA=8.00}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution:**

```sql
INSERT INTO Student (RegNo, Name, Dept, CGPA)
VALUES
    ('KTU2024CS002', 'Beena',  'ECE',   9.10),
    ('KTU2024CS003', 'Chitra', 'Mech',  7.85),
    ('KTU2024CS004', 'Deepak', 'CSE',   8.00);
```

$$
\text{Atomicity Check} \Rightarrow \text{If any one row violates a constraint, ALL rows are rolled back.}
$$

---

#### Example 3 — INSERT via Sub-query (INSERT...SELECT)

**Given:** Another table `TopperList(RegNo, Name, CGPA)` contains top-performing students.

**Question:** Copy all students from `CSE` department having CGPA $\geq 9.0$ from `Student` into `TopperList`.

**Logical Flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step A: Form the inner SELECT query} \\
&\quad \text{SELECT RegNo, Name, CGPA FROM Student WHERE Dept='CSE' AND CGPA >= 9.0 \\
&\text{Step B: Wrap it inside INSERT...SELECT} \\
&\quad \text{INSERT INTO TopperList (RegNo, Name, CGPA) <inner query>}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Query:**

```sql
INSERT INTO TopperList (RegNo, Name, CGPA)
SELECT RegNo, Name, CGPA
FROM   Student
WHERE  Dept = 'CSE'
  AND  CGPA >= 9.0;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Column count must match exactly** between the INSERT column list and the SELECT projection — otherwise a SQL error `1222` (operand mismatch) is raised.

---

#### Example 4 — INSERT with DEFAULT VALUES

**Question:** Insert a row into Student where only RegNo is known; all other columns must take their default values (NULL or DEFAULT).

**Solution:**

```sql
INSERT INTO Student (RegNo) VALUES ('KTU2024CS099');
```

$$
\text{Note: Name must NOT be NULL, so this will FAIL because Name has no DEFAULT.}
$$

**Corrected Version (assuming Name has DEFAULT 'UNKNOWN'):**

```sql
INSERT INTO Student DEFAULT VALUES;
```

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Errors During INSERT

| # | Common Mistake | Resulting DBMS Error | Why It Happens |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mismatched column count vs. value count | `Column count doesn't match value count` | Forgetting to include DEFAULT/NULL columns. |
| 2 | String without quotes | `Unknown column` | `'Anand'` ≠ `Anand`. |
| 3 | Duplicate PRIMARY KEY | `Duplicate entry` | Inserting a row whose PK already exists. |
| 4 | Violating FOREIGN KEY | `Cannot add or update a child row` | Referenced parent row doesn't exist. |
| 5 | Wrong data type | `Incorrect integer value` | `'abc'` into an INT column. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — INSERT Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User Issues INSERT Statement"] --> B["Parser: Syntax Check"]
    B --> C{"Syntax Valid?"}
    C -- "No" --> Z["Return Syntax Error to User"]
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Resolver: Verify Table and Columns Exist"]
    D --> E{"Schema Valid?"}
    E -- "No" --> Z
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Type Checking: Match Value Data Types"]
    F --> G{"Type Match?"}
    G -- "No" --> Z
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Constraint Enforcement: PK, FK, UNIQUE, NOT NULL, CHECK"]
    H --> I{"Constraints Satisfied?"}
    I -- "No" --> Z
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Trigger Activation: BEFORE INSERT"]
    J --> K["Write Row to Data Page Buffer Pool"]
    K --> L["Append Entry to Transaction Log (REDO/UNDO)"]
    L --> M["Trigger Activation: AFTER INSERT"]
    M --> N["Row Successfully Inserted"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — INSERT Variants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SQL["SQL DML Layer"]
        direction TB
        SQ1["INSERT...VALUES Single Row"]
        SQ2["INSERT...VALUES Multi Row"]
        SQ3["INSERT...SELECT Sub Query"]
        SQ4["INSERT DEFAULT VALUES"]
    end

    subgraph ENG["DBMS Engine Layer"]
        direction TB
        EN1["Query Optimizer"]
        EN2["Constraint Validator"]
        EN3["Transaction Manager"]
        EN4["Storage Engine"]
    end

    subgraph STORE["Physical Storage Layer"]
        direction TB
        ST1["Data Pages Heap"]
        ST2["Index Pages B+ Tree"]
        ST3["Redo Undo Log"]
    end

    SQ1 --> EN1
    SQ2 --> EN1
    SQ3 --> EN1
    SQ4 --> EN1
    EN1 --> EN2
    EN2 --> EN3
    EN3 --> EN4
    EN4 --> ST1
    EN4 --> ST2
    EN3 --> ST3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The diagrams above show that INSERT is not just a "write" — it engages the **query optimizer, constraint validator, transaction manager, and storage engine** simultaneously. This is why KTU examiners may ask conceptual questions like *"Which component of the DBMS ensures the integrity of an INSERT statement?"* (Answer: Constraint Validator + Transaction Manager).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between `INSERT...VALUES` and `INSERT...SELECT` statements with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `INSERT...VALUES` | `INSERT...SELECT` |
|---|---|---|
| Source of data | Hard-coded literal values provided by the user. | Data fetched from another table via a `SELECT` query. |
| Use case | Adding one or a few known rows manually. | Bulk copy / migration of rows satisfying a condition. |
| Example | `INSERT INTO Dept(Did,Dname) VALUES (1,'CSE');` | `INSERT INTO Topper SELECT * FROM Student WHERE CGPA>=9;` |
| Sub-query required | No | Yes |

*(1 mark for definition difference + 1 mark for example of each = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three constraints that are validated during an INSERT operation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **NOT NULL** — Ensures the column cannot be empty unless a DEFAULT is provided. *(1 mark)*
2. **PRIMARY KEY / UNIQUE** — Ensures no duplicate values exist in the indexed column. *(1 mark)*
3. **FOREIGN KEY** — Ensures the inserted value matches an existing value in the referenced parent table. *(1 mark)*

*(Alternative valid constraints: CHECK, DEFAULT enforcement, AUTO_INCREMENT trigger.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Consider the following schema for a KTU library database. Write SQL `CREATE TABLE` statements with appropriate constraints and then write INSERT statements to populate at least **three rows** into each table using a **multi-row INSERT** (7 Marks).

```text
BOOK(BookID INT PK, Title VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, Author VARCHAR(50), Price DECIMAL(6,2), PubYear INT CHECK(PubYear>=1900))
MEMBER(MemberID INT PK, MName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, Dept VARCHAR(5) DEFAULT 'CSE', JoinDate DATE)
```

**(b)** Write an `INSERT...SELECT` statement to copy all members who joined after `'2023-01-01'` from the `MEMBER` table into a new table `NEW_MEMBERS_2024` having the same structure. Explain the constraint implications. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Step-by-Step:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE BOOK (
    BookID   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Title    VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    Author   VARCHAR(50),
    Price    DECIMAL(6,2),
    PubYear  INT CHECK (PubYear >= 1900)
);

CREATE TABLE MEMBER (
    MemberID  INT PRIMARY KEY,
    MName     VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    Dept      VARCHAR(5) DEFAULT 'CSE',
    JoinDate  DATE
);
```

*(2 marks for correct CREATE TABLE with all constraints)*

**Multi-row INSERT statements:**

```sql
INSERT INTO BOOK (BookID, Title, Author, Price, PubYear)
VALUES
    (101, 'Database System Concepts', 'Korth', 650.00, 2019),
    (102, 'Operating Systems',       'Silberschatz', 720.50, 2018),
    (103, 'Computer Networks',       'Tanenbaum', 599.99, 2021);

INSERT INTO MEMBER (MemberID, MName, Dept, JoinDate)
VALUES
    (1, 'Anand Kumar',  'CSE',   '2023-08-15'),
    (2, 'Beena Joseph', 'ECE',   '2024-01-10'),
    (3, 'Chitra Nair',  'Mech',  '2024-07-22');
```

*(3 marks for correctly formatted multi-row INSERTs with proper values matching data types)*

**Self-verification step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check 1:} &\;\; \text{Title is NOT NULL} \Rightarrow \text{All 3 rows provide it.} \\
\text{Check 2:} &\;\; \text{PubYear CHECK >= 1900} \Rightarrow \text{2019, 2018, 2021 all pass.} \\
\text{Check 3:} &\;\; \text{Primary Keys unique} \Rightarrow 101 \neq 102 \neq 103. \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for verification / explanation of constraints)*

---

**(b) Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Create the destination table (same structure):**

```sql
CREATE TABLE NEW_MEMBERS_2024 (
    MemberID  INT PRIMARY KEY,
    MName     VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    Dept      VARCHAR(5) DEFAULT 'CSE',
    JoinDate  DATE
);
```

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Perform INSERT...SELECT:**

```sql
INSERT INTO NEW_MEMBERS_2024 (MemberID, MName, Dept, JoinDate)
SELECT MemberID, MName, Dept, JoinDate
FROM   MEMBER
WHERE  JoinDate > '2023-01-01';
```

*(3 marks)*

**Step 3 — Constraint Implications Explanation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Implication 1:} &\;\; \text{PRIMARY KEY uniqueness must hold across both tables.} \\
                      &\;\; \text{If two tables share the same MemberID range, a row will be REJECTED.} \\
\text{Implication 2:} &\;\; \text{NOT NULL on MName means the SELECT must not yield NULLs.} \\
\text{Implication 3:} &\;\; \text{Foreign Keys in related tables referencing MEMBER remain valid since rows are copied, not moved.} \\
\text{Implication 4:} &\;\; \text{The operation is atomic — partial copies do not occur.}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for implications)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain with an example how the `DEFAULT` clause interacts with INSERT operations. Discuss what happens when all columns have defaults and a single row is inserted using `DEFAULT VALUES`. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Consider the schema `PRODUCT(PID PK, PName, Price, StockQty)`. Write:
1. A single-row INSERT inserting one product.
2. A multi-row INSERT inserting three products.
3. An INSERT...SELECT that copies products whose StockQty < 10 into a `LOW_STOCK` table.
4. An INSERT using `ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE` to upsert a product. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Step-by-Step:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Employee (
    EmpID    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    EName    VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    Dept     VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'GENERAL',
    Salary   DECIMAL(10,2) DEFAULT 30000,
    JoinDate DATE DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE)
);
```

*(1 mark)*

**Interaction of DEFAULT with INSERT:**

$$
\text{When a column is OMITTED from the INSERT column list, the DBMS substitutes its DEFAULT value.}
$$

```sql
INSERT INTO Employee (EmpID, EName)
VALUES (1, 'Rahul');
```

Resulting row → `(1, 'Rahul', 'GENERAL', 30000.00, '2024-...')`

*(3 marks for example explanation)*

**INSERT with DEFAULT VALUES:**

```sql
INSERT INTO Employee DEFAULT VALUES;
```

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Result:} &\;\; \text{DBMS attempts to assign DEFAULT to every column.} \\
              &\;\; \text{If any column is NOT NULL without a DEFAULT, this statement FAILS.} \\
\text{In our case:} &\;\; \text{EmpID has no DEFAULT and is PRIMARY KEY — FAILS with "Field 'EmpID' doesn't have a default value".}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks)*

---

**(b) Solutions:**

**1. Single-row INSERT:**

```sql
INSERT INTO PRODUCT (PID, PName, Price, StockQty)
VALUES (501, 'Laptop', 55000.00, 25);
```

**2. Multi-row INSERT:**

```sql
INSERT INTO PRODUCT (PID, PName, Price, StockQty)
VALUES
    (502, 'Mouse',    500.00,  8),
    (503, 'Keyboard', 1200.00, 15),
    (504, 'Monitor',  9500.00, 5);
```

**3. INSERT...SELECT:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE LOW_STOCK (
    PID      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    PName    VARCHAR(50),
    Price    DECIMAL(10,2),
    StockQty INT
);

INSERT INTO LOW_STOCK (PID, PName, Price, StockQty)
SELECT PID, PName, Price, StockQty
FROM   PRODUCT
WHERE  StockQty < 10;
```

**4. UPSERT (MySQL specific):**

```sql
INSERT INTO PRODUCT (PID, PName, Price, StockQty)
VALUES (502, 'Wireless Mouse', 750.00, 30)
ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE
    PName    = 'Wireless Mouse',
    Price    = 750.00,
    StockQty = 30;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Missing Quotes on Strings:** Forgetting single quotes around VARCHAR values (e.g., `Laptop` instead of `'Laptop'`) costs **1 mark**.
> - **Column Count Mismatch:** Inserting 3 values into 4 columns without omitting a NOT-NULL column costs **2 marks**.
> - **Using INSERT without column list in `INSERT...SELECT`:** Column count must match; KTU strictly evaluates this.
> - **Forgetting DEFAULT keyword:** When using `DEFAULT VALUES`, students often write `INSERT VALUES ()` — that is **wrong syntax** in standard SQL.
> - **Mixing up COMMIT:** In transactional contexts (PostgreSQL, Oracle), forgetting `COMMIT;` means the row is **not persisted** across sessions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **INSERT is a DML command**, not DDL — it does not auto-define or modify schema.
- Always prefer **explicit column lists** to avoid ambiguity during schema evolution.
- **Multi-row INSERT** is significantly more efficient than issuing N separate single-row inserts (single parser pass + single transaction).
- **INSERT...SELECT** is the standard mechanism for **data migration between tables**.
- **DEFAULT VALUES** requires every column to either be NULL-able, have a DEFAULT, or be AUTO_INCREMENT.
- Every INSERT must satisfy **ACID Atomicity** — partial failure rolls back the entire statement.
- Constraint checks executed in order: **Type → NOT NULL → UNIQUE → CHECK → FOREIGN KEY**.
- In MySQL, **ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE** implements an **UPSERT** (insert-or-update).
- The new row's **storage location** is determined by the storage engine — InnoDB places it on the correct clustered index page; MyISAM appends to the data file's end.
- KTU frequently tests the difference between **logical INSERT (relational algebra $\cup$ operation)** and **physical INSERT (disk write through buffer pool)**.
- Remember: An INSERT that violates a **FOREIGN KEY** triggers an error, not a warning — be ready to explain cascading rules (`ON DELETE CASCADE`, `ON UPDATE CASCADE`) in viva.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Update
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Update

# SQL UPDATE Operation — KTU 2024 Scheme Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SQL UPDATE (DML Operation)**
> The `UPDATE` statement is a **Data Manipulation Language (DML)** command in Structured Query Language (SQL) used to **modify the values of existing rows (tuples)** in a single relational table or across multiple tables (using joins or subqueries). It changes the data but **does not** change the table's schema (structure). It is governed by SQL standard **ANSI/ISO SQL:2016** and is supported by all major RDBMS engines (Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server).

The general form, as specified in the KTU 2024 syllabus, is:

```sql
UPDATE table_name
SET column1 = value1, column2 = value2, ...
[WHERE condition];
```

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a Register / Ledger Correction**
>
> Imagine you are the class teacher and you have a **paper register** containing student marks. A student named "Arjun" was marked absent during the unit test. Later, he submits a medical certificate, and the teacher wants to **change only Arjun's mark from `AB (Absent)` to `85`**.
>
> - You do **not** create a new register → that is `INSERT`.
> - You do **not** throw away a student's row → that is `DELETE`.
> - You simply **erase the old mark and write a new one** for an existing row → that is exactly what `UPDATE` does!
>
> Key intuition: **`UPDATE` modifies existing data in place. It does not add new rows or remove existing rows.**

### 1.3 Critical Highlights (Syllabus Must-Knows)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Golden Rules of UPDATE (Frequently Asked in KTU)**
>
> 1. **`UPDATE` is a DML command**, not DDL. Hence, it is **transactional** — it can be **rolled back** using `ROLLBACK` (provided auto-commit is OFF).
> 2. **The `WHERE` clause is OPTIONAL but DANGEROUS** — omitting it updates **every single row** in the table.
> 3. **Referential Integrity** must be preserved — you cannot update a primary key value if it is referenced by a foreign key (unless `ON UPDATE CASCADE` is defined).

### 1.4 Classification within SQL Sub-languages

| Sub-language | Commands | Purpose |
|--------------|----------|---------|
| **DDL** (Data Definition Language) | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE` | Defines structure |
| **DML** (Data Manipulation Language) | `INSERT`, **`UPDATE`**, `DELETE`, `MERGE` | Modifies data |
| **DQL** (Data Query Language) | `SELECT` | Retrieves data |
| **DCL** (Data Control Language) | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` | Manages permissions |
| **TCL** (Transaction Control Language) | `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT` | Manages transactions |

The `UPDATE` command belongs firmly to **DML**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Syntax Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the UPDATE Statement — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

Let us deconstruct every keyword and clause in a structured manner:

- **`UPDATE <table_name>`**
  - **Why?** Identifies the target table whose rows must be modified.
  - **How?** The table must exist, and the user must have the `UPDATE` privilege on that table.

- **`SET <column1> = <expression1> [, <column2> = <expression2> , ...]`**
  - **Why?** Specifies which column(s) must receive new value(s).
  - **How?** The expression on the RHS can be:
    - A constant literal (e.g., `5000`)
    - An arithmetic expression (e.g., `salary * 1.10`)
    - A scalar subquery (returns one value)
    - A `DEFAULT` keyword (resets to column default)
    - A `NULL` keyword (sets the value to NULL)

- **`[WHERE <condition>]`**
  - **Why?** Filters which rows are affected. If omitted, **all rows** are updated.
  - **How?** The condition can use comparison operators (`=`, `<>`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`), logical operators (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`), pattern matching (`LIKE`), set membership (`IN`, `NOT IN`), range checks (`BETWEEN`), and existence checks (`EXISTS`).

- **`[ORDER BY ...] [LIMIT n]`** *(MySQL/PostgreSQL extension)*
  - **Why?** Restricts the number of rows updated (e.g., update only the top 5 highest salaries).
  - **How?** Especially useful with `ORDER BY ... LIMIT n` patterns.

### 2.2 Variants of UPDATE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The five official KTU-expected variants are:**
> 1. **Simple UPDATE** (single table, constant values)
> 2. **Arithmetic UPDATE** (using expressions in SET)
> 3. **Conditional UPDATE** (using WHERE with multiple conditions)
> 4. **Subquery UPDATE** (using scalar/correlated subqueries)
> 5. **Joined UPDATE** (updating one table based on another table — vendor-specific syntax)

### 2.3 KTU Syntax / Cheat Sheet Table

> [!NOTE]
> The table below summarizes the **canonical syntax templates** that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce verbatim in answer scripts.

| # | Variant | Canonical Syntax Template | Vendor Standard |
|---|---------|---------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Simple UPDATE | `UPDATE T SET col = val WHERE cond;` | ANSI SQL (all) |
| 2 | Multi-column UPDATE | `UPDATE T SET c1=v1, c2=v2 WHERE cond;` | ANSI SQL (all) |
| 3 | Arithmetic UPDATE | `UPDATE T SET salary = salary * 1.10 WHERE dept='CSE';` | ANSI SQL (all) |
| 4 | Subquery UPDATE (Scalar) | `UPDATE T SET col = (SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ...) WHERE cond;` | ANSI SQL (all) |
| 5 | Correlated Subquery UPDATE | `UPDATE E SET salary = (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM E WHERE dept_no = E.dept_no);` | Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL |
| 6 | Joined UPDATE (MySQL) | `UPDATE T1 JOIN T2 ON T1.id=T2.id SET T1.col = T2.col;` | MySQL, PostgreSQL |
| 7 | Joined UPDATE (Oracle/SQL Server) | `UPDATE T1 SET col = (SELECT col FROM T2 WHERE T2.id=T1.id) WHERE EXISTS (...);` | Oracle, SQL Server |
| 8 | UPDATE with DEFAULT | `UPDATE T SET status = DEFAULT WHERE cond;` | ANSI SQL:2003+ |
| 9 | UPDATE with NULL | `UPDATE T SET manager_id = NULL WHERE cond;` | ANSI SQL (all) |
| 10 | UPDATE ... LIMIT n | `UPDATE T SET col=val ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 5;` | MySQL/PostgreSQL |

### 2.4 Engineering / Industry Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications of UPDATE in Industry:**
> - **Banking Systems:** Daily interest credit to all savings accounts → `UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + (balance * 0.05/365);`
> - **E-Commerce:** Bulk price hike or discount → `UPDATE products SET price = price * 1.15 WHERE category = 'Electronics';`
> - **HR Payroll:** Annual increment → `UPDATE employees SET salary = salary * 1.10 WHERE hire_date < '2023-01-01';`
> - **Order Management:** Status transitions (`PENDING` → `SHIPPED` → `DELIVERED`).
> - **Audit Logging:** Setting `is_active = 0` (soft delete) instead of physical `DELETE`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Schema (used throughout this section):**
> Consider the `employee` table with the following schema:
> `employee(emp_id INT PK, name VARCHAR(50), department VARCHAR(30), salary DECIMAL(10,2), manager_id INT)`
>
> Sample data:
>
> | emp_id | name    | department | salary    | manager_id |
> |--------|---------|------------|-----------|------------|
> | 101    | Arjun   | CSE        | 50000.00  | NULL       |
> | 102    | Meera   | CSE        | 60000.00  | 101        |
> | 103    | Rahul   | ECE        | 55000.00  | 101        |
> | 104    | Sneha   | ECE        | 70000.00  | NULL       |
> | 105    | Vivek   | MECH       | 45000.00  | 104        |

### 3.1 Example 1 — Simple Single-Column UPDATE

**Problem:** Increase the salary of employee with `emp_id = 102` (Meera) to `65000`.

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
UPDATE employee
SET salary = 65000
WHERE emp_id = 102;
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Evaluation:**

- **Step 1 — `UPDATE employee`:** The DBMS engine locks the `employee` table (row-level lock on matched rows).
- **Step 2 — `SET salary = 65000`:** The engine prepares the new value `65000.00` for the `salary` column.
- **Step 3 — `WHERE emp_id = 102`:** The engine scans the table (or uses the index on `emp_id`) and identifies the single row where `emp_id = 102` (Meera).
- **Step 4 — Write Phase:** The salary value of that row is overwritten in the buffer. The change is logged in the **redo log** (for recovery) and **undo log** (for rollback).
- **Step 5 — Commit:** Without explicit `COMMIT`, in Oracle the change is uncommitted; in MySQL with autocommit ON, it is committed immediately.

**Result:**

| emp_id | name    | department | salary    | manager_id |
|--------|---------|------------|-----------|------------|
| 101    | Arjun   | CSE        | 50000.00  | NULL       |
| 102    | Meera   | CSE        | **65000.00** | 101     |
| 103    | Rahul   | ECE        | 55000.00  | 101        |
| 104    | Sneha   | ECE        | 70000.00  | NULL       |
| 105    | Vivek   | MECH       | 45000.00  | 104        |

### 3.2 Example 2 — Bulk Arithmetic UPDATE (All Rows of a Department)

**Problem:** Give a **10% salary hike** to all employees in the `CSE` department.

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
UPDATE employee
SET salary = salary * 1.10
WHERE department = 'CSE';
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Evaluation:**

- **Step 1 — Filter:** The optimizer evaluates `department = 'CSE'` → matches rows 101 (Arjun) and 102 (Meera).
- **Step 2 — Expression Evaluation:** For each matched row, the RHS expression `salary * 1.10` is evaluated using the **OLD value** of `salary` (not the just-updated one — this is **important** in standard SQL, and prevents double-hikes).
  - Arjun: $50000 \times 1.10 = 55000$
  - Meera: $65000 \times 1.10 = 71500$
- **Step 3 — Write:** Both rows' salary fields are updated atomically (within a single transaction).

**Mathematical Generalization:**

For an employee with old salary $S_{old}$, the new salary $S_{new}$ is computed as:

$$
S_{new} = S_{old} \times \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right)
$$

where $p$ is the percentage hike. For $p = 10$:

$$
S_{new} = S_{old} \times 1.10
$$

**Verification for Arjun:**

$$
S_{new} = 50000 \times 1.10 = 55000
$$

**Verification for Meera:**

$$
S_{new} = 65000 \times 1.10 = 71500
$$

### 3.3 Example 3 — Subquery UPDATE (Cross-Table Reference)

**Schema Extension:** Add a `dept_bonus(department VARCHAR, bonus_amt DECIMAL)` table:

| department | bonus_amt |
|------------|-----------|
| CSE        | 5000      |
| ECE        | 3000      |
| MECH       | 2000      |

**Problem:** Add the `bonus_amt` from `dept_bonus` to the `salary` of every matching employee.

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
UPDATE employee
SET salary = salary + (SELECT bonus_amt 
                       FROM dept_bonus 
                       WHERE dept_bonus.department = employee.department)
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 
              FROM dept_bonus 
              WHERE dept_bonus.department = employee.department);
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Evaluation:**

- **Step 1 — Subquery in SET:** For each row in `employee`, the correlated subquery fetches the `bonus_amt` for that employee's department.
  - Arjun (CSE): bonus = 5000 → new salary = $55000 + 5000 = 60000$
  - Meera (CSE): bonus = 5000 → new salary = $71500 + 5000 = 76500$
  - Rahul (ECE): bonus = 3000 → new salary = $55000 + 3000 = 58000$
  - Sneha (ECE): bonus = 3000 → new salary = $70000 + 3000 = 73000$
  - Vivek (MECH): bonus = 2000 → new salary = $45000 + 2000 = 47000$

- **Step 2 — WHERE EXISTS:** Acts as a safeguard to skip employees whose department has no entry in `dept_bonus` (avoids NULL from subquery).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why correlated subquery and not JOIN-based UPDATE?**
> Correlated subqueries are **portable across all RDBMS** (Oracle, MySQL, SQL Server, PostgreSQL), whereas `UPDATE ... JOIN` syntax is **vendor-specific** (supported in MySQL, PostgreSQL, but not in standard Oracle). KTU examiners prefer the **ANSI-portable correlated-subquery form** for full marks.

### 3.4 Example 4 — Joined UPDATE (MySQL Syntax)

**Problem:** Update each employee's `manager_id` to the `manager_id` of their department head from a `dept_head` table.

**dept_head table:**

| department | head_id |
|------------|---------|
| CSE        | 201     |
| ECE        | 202     |
| MECH       | 203     |

**MySQL Syntax:**
```sql
UPDATE employee AS e
JOIN dept_head AS d ON e.department = d.department
SET e.manager_id = d.head_id;
```

**Oracle / SQL Server Equivalent (Correlated Subquery):**
```sql
UPDATE employee e
SET e.manager_id = (SELECT d.head_id 
                    FROM dept_head d 
                    WHERE d.department = e.department)
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 
              FROM dept_head d 
              WHERE d.department = e.department);
```

### 3.5 Example 5 — UPDATE with CASE / Conditional Logic

**Problem:** Set the `department` field to `'AI-ML'` for all CSE employees with salary > 60000, and to `'CORE-CSE'` for others in CSE.

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
UPDATE employee
SET department = CASE
                    WHEN department = 'CSE' AND salary > 60000 THEN 'AI-ML'
                    WHEN department = 'CSE' AND salary <= 60000 THEN 'CORE-CSE'
                    ELSE department
                 END
WHERE department = 'CSE';
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `ELSE department` clause is **critical** — without it, non-CSE employees would have their `department` set to `NULL`. This is a **common KTU pitfall** (see warning in Section 5).

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Errors vs. Correct Forms

| # | Mistake (Common Student Error) | Consequence | Correct Approach |
|---|--------------------------------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | `UPDATE employee SET salary = 65000;` (no WHERE) | **Every row's salary becomes 65000** | Always include `WHERE` unless intentional |
| 2 | `UPDATE employee SET salary = 65000 WHERE name = 'Meera';` (name may repeat) | Multiple rows updated unintentionally | Use **primary key** in WHERE when possible |
| 3 | `UPDATE employee SET emp_id = 999 WHERE emp_id = 102;` | **Foreign key violation** if other tables reference it | Use `ON UPDATE CASCADE` or update FKs first |
| 4 | Forgetting `COMMIT` in Oracle | Other users don't see the change | Always `COMMIT;` after DML in transactions |
| 5 | Using `=` for both assignment and comparison | `SET salary = salary * 1.10` is correct; `SET salary = salary * 1.10` is correct — confusion arises in `WHERE salary = 65000` (comparison) | Remember: `SET` uses `=`, `WHERE` also uses `=` (but for comparison) |
| 6 | Confusing `UPDATE` with `ALTER` | `ALTER` changes **structure**; `UPDATE` changes **data** | Use `ALTER TABLE` for schema; `UPDATE` for rows |
| 7 | Not using `BEGIN TRANSACTION` before bulk UPDATE | Cannot roll back if a mistake happens | Wrap in `BEGIN; ... ROLLBACK;` (or `COMMIT;`) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Execution Flow of an UPDATE Statement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User issues UPDATE statement] --> B[Parser: Syntax Check]
    B --> C{Valid Syntax?}
    C -->|No| D[Return SQL Syntax Error]
    C -->|Yes| E[Optimizer: Build Execution Plan]
    E --> F[Acquire Row-Level Locks on Target Rows]
    F --> G[Evaluate WHERE Clause]
    G --> H{Matches Found?}
    H -->|No| I[0 rows affected - No-op]
    H -->|Yes| J[For each matched row: evaluate SET expressions]
    J --> K[Check Constraints: PK, FK, CHECK, NOT NULL]
    K --> L{Constraints Satisfied?}
    L -->|No| M[Rollback + Return Constraint Error]
    L -->|Yes| N[Write new values to Data Buffers]
    N --> O[Log to Redo Log and Undo Log]
    O --> P{Autocommit ON?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Auto COMMIT - Change Persistent]
    P -->|No| R[Wait for explicit COMMIT or ROLLBACK]
    Q --> S[Return: N rows updated]
    R --> S
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Components of an UPDATE Statement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SQL["SQL UPDATE Statement Structure"]
        direction LR
        KW1[UPDATE keyword] --> TBL[Table Name]
        TBL --> KW2[SET keyword]
        KW2 --> COL1[Column 1]
        COL1 --> EQ1[=]
        EQ1 --> VAL1[Value or Expression]
        VAL1 --> COMMA1[,]
        COMMA1 --> COL2[Column 2]
        COL2 --> EQ2[=]
        EQ2 --> VAL2[Value or Subquery]
        VAL2 --> KW3[WHERE keyword]
        KW3 --> COND[Boolean Condition]
    end

    SQL --> R1[Single-Row Update]
    SQL --> R2[Bulk Update]
    SQL --> R3[Subquery-Based Update]
    SQL --> R4[Join-Based Update]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Transaction Lifecycle of an UPDATE

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Parsing: User submits UPDATE
    Parsing --> SyntaxError: Invalid SQL
    SyntaxError --> [*]
    Parsing --> Executing: Valid SQL
    Executing --> Locked: Acquiring row locks
    Locked --> Evaluated: WHERE clause evaluated
    Evaluated --> Writing: New values written to buffer
    Writing --> Committed: Auto or explicit COMMIT
    Writing --> RolledBack: Explicit ROLLBACK
    Committed --> [*]
    RolledBack --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — UPDATE with Referential Integrity Check

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant E as SQL Engine
    participant L as Lock Manager
    participant RI as Integrity Checker
    participant DB as Data Buffers
    participant LOG as Redo/Undo Log

    U->>E: UPDATE employee SET dept = 'AI' WHERE emp_id = 101
    E->>E: Parse & Validate
    E->>L: Request lock on row emp_id = 101
    L-->>E: Lock acquired
    E->>E: Evaluate WHERE -> 1 row matches
    E->>RI: Check FK constraints (e.g., dept must exist in dept table)
    RI-->>E: OK (or violation)
    E->>DB: Write new dept value to buffer
    E->>LOG: Append redo + undo entry
    E-->>U: 1 row updated
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *Differentiate between the `UPDATE` and `ALTER` commands in SQL. In which sub-language of SQL does each belong, and why is this distinction important?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `UPDATE` | `ALTER` |
|--------|----------|---------|
| **SQL Sub-language** | DML (Data Manipulation Language) | DDL (Data Definition Language) |
| **Purpose** | Modifies the **data** (row values) of an existing table | Modifies the **structure** (schema) of an existing table |
| **Examples** | Change salary, change status | Add a column, drop a column, change data type |
| **Transactional?** | Yes — can be rolled back | No — auto-commits in most RDBMS |
| **Affects** | Rows (data) | Table definition (metadata) |

> The distinction matters because DML operations are **transactional and recoverable**, whereas DDL operations are **auto-committed and non-reversible** (in many databases).

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *What happens if you execute the following SQL statement? Explain with an example.*
```sql
UPDATE employee SET salary = 60000;
```

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

This statement will **update the `salary` column of EVERY row** in the `employee` table to `60000`, because the `WHERE` clause is omitted. The SQL engine interprets the absence of a `WHERE` filter as "all rows match."

For example, if the table had 5 employees with salaries 50000, 65000, 55000, 70000, and 45000 respectively, after this statement, **all 5 employees** will have salary = 60000.

> This is one of the **most dangerous SQL mistakes** in practice. Best practice is to **always run a `SELECT` with the same `WHERE` first** to verify the row count before running `UPDATE`.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the following two tables:

```
employee(emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50), department VARCHAR(30), salary DECIMAL(10,2))
dept_bonus(department VARCHAR(30) PRIMARY KEY, bonus_amt DECIMAL(10,2))
```

Write SQL `UPDATE` statements to perform the following:

1. Give a **15% salary hike** to all employees in the `CSE` department.
2. Add the corresponding `bonus_amt` from `dept_bonus` to the salary of all employees whose department exists in `dept_bonus`.
3. Reset the `department` of all employees with salary less than `30000` to `'INTERN'`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how `UPDATE` interacts with **referential integrity constraints**. What is `ON UPDATE CASCADE`, and how does it differ from `ON UPDATE SET NULL`? Provide an example schema demonstrating both.

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Sub-part (1) — 15% Hike for CSE:**
```sql
UPDATE employee
SET salary = salary * 1.15
WHERE department = 'CSE';
```

**Sub-part (2) — Add Bonus from Another Table (Correlated Subquery):**
```sql
UPDATE employee
SET salary = salary + (SELECT bonus_amt 
                       FROM dept_bonus 
                       WHERE dept_bonus.department = employee.department)
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 
              FROM dept_bonus 
              WHERE dept_bonus.department = employee.department);
```

**Sub-part (3) — Reset Department for Low Salaries:**
```sql
UPDATE employee
SET department = 'INTERN'
WHERE salary < 30000;
```

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

When updating a **primary key** (or a `UNIQUE` key) that is referenced by a **foreign key** in another table, the RDBMS enforces **referential integrity** by default and rejects the `UPDATE` with a constraint violation error.

**`ON UPDATE CASCADE`:**
Automatically propagates the new primary key value to all referencing foreign keys in child tables. Example:

```sql
CREATE TABLE department (
    dept_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_name VARCHAR(50)
);

CREATE TABLE employee (
    emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(50),
    dept_id INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES department(dept_id)
    ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
```

Now, `UPDATE department SET dept_id = 20 WHERE dept_id = 10;` will **automatically update** all employees belonging to department 10 to belong to department 20.

**`ON UPDATE SET NULL`:**
Sets the foreign key in child rows to `NULL` instead of propagating. Example:

```sql
FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES department(dept_id)
ON UPDATE SET NULL
```

After the same UPDATE, employees previously in department 10 will have `dept_id = NULL`.

| Action | ON UPDATE CASCADE | ON UPDATE SET NULL |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Behavior | Propagates new value to FKs | Sets FKs to NULL |
| Data integrity | Maintains linkage | Breaks linkage (soft orphan) |
| Use case | When the relationship must be preserved | When the relationship is optional |

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **correlated subquery** in the context of an `UPDATE` statement? Write a complete SQL `UPDATE` statement that uses a correlated subquery to set the `salary` of every employee in the `employee` table to **5% more than the average salary of their respective department**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **three differences** between `UPDATE` and `MERGE` (or `UPSERT`) statements. When would you prefer one over the other in a real-world ETL pipeline?

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

A **correlated subquery** is a subquery that references one or more columns of the outer (main) `UPDATE` query. It is evaluated **once per row** processed by the outer query. It is the **only ANSI-portable way** to perform a cross-table `UPDATE` in standard SQL.

**SQL Statement:**
```sql
UPDATE employee e
SET salary = salary * 1.05
WHERE salary < (SELECT AVG(salary)
                FROM employee e2
                WHERE e2.department = e.department);
```

*Note:* The condition `salary < dept_avg` is optional — the question asked to set everyone's salary to 5% above dept average. The unconditional form is:

```sql
UPDATE employee e
SET salary = (SELECT AVG(e2.salary) * 1.05
              FROM employee e2
              WHERE e2.department = e.department);
```

**Execution Trace (Conceptual):**

For each row in `employee e`, the engine:
1. Reads `e.department`.
2. Executes the inner subquery to compute the average salary for that department.
3. Assigns the computed value (or expression result) back to `e.salary`.

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

| # | Aspect | UPDATE | MERGE (UPSERT) |
|---|--------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | **Purpose** | Modifies existing rows | Inserts new rows OR updates existing rows in one statement |
| 2 | **Row Existence** | Requires rows to exist (does nothing if not found) | Handles both matched and unmatched cases |
| 3 | **Atomicity** | Single operation (update only) | Combined INSERT + UPDATE in one atomic statement |
| 4 | **SQL Standard** | ANSI SQL:1986+ | ANSI SQL:2003+ (also called UPSERT in PostgreSQL/MySQL) |
| 5 | **Typical Use** | Bulk correction, hike, status change | ETL pipelines, data synchronization between source and target tables |

**Real-World Preference:**
- Use `UPDATE` for **simple in-place corrections** (e.g., "give everyone a 10% hike").
- Use `MERGE` for **data warehouse ETL scenarios** where rows from a staging table must be inserted if new, or updated if they already exist in the target dimension table.

```sql
MERGE INTO employee_target AS t
USING employee_staging AS s
ON t.emp_id = s.emp_id
WHEN MATCHED THEN
    UPDATE SET t.salary = s.salary, t.department = s.department
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
    INSERT (emp_id, name, department, salary)
    VALUES (s.emp_id, s.name, s.department, s.salary);
```

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in UPDATE Questions (Valuator's Perspective)**
>
> 1. **Omitting the `WHERE` clause** in syntax-writing questions — even when the question says "update Meera's salary", students often write `UPDATE employee SET salary = 65000;` → **−1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing `UPDATE` with `ALTER`** — writing `ALTER employee SET salary = ...` → **−1 mark** (syntax error).
> 3. **Using `==` instead of `=`** in SQL — `==` is a C/C++/Java operator; SQL uses single `=` for both assignment and comparison → **−0.5 to −1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting `COMMIT`** in Oracle-flavored answers — examiners look for transaction awareness in 7-mark questions → **−0.5 mark**.
> 5. **Not specifying the correlation variable** in correlated subquery (writing `(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employee WHERE department = department)` instead of `e2.department = e.department`) → **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention transactional behavior** (rollback, undo log) in theory questions about how `UPDATE` works internally → **−1 mark**.
> 7. **Confusing `TRUNCATE` with `UPDATE`** — `TRUNCATE` removes all rows and is DDL/DDL-like; `UPDATE` modifies data in place.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — UPDATE in SQL**
>
> - ✅ `UPDATE` is a **DML** command — transactional, supports `COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK`.
> - ✅ Syntax: `UPDATE table SET col=val [WHERE cond];`
> - ✅ **Always** use `WHERE` unless intentionally updating all rows.
> - ✅ The RHS of `SET` accepts constants, arithmetic expressions, `DEFAULT`, `NULL`, scalar subqueries.
> - ✅ For cross-table updates, the **correlated subquery** form is the **ANSI-portable** standard.
> - ✅ `UPDATE ... JOIN` is **vendor-specific** (MySQL/PostgreSQL), not in Oracle standard.
> - ✅ **Referential integrity** can be enforced via `ON UPDATE CASCADE` or `ON UPDATE SET NULL`.
> - ✅ Always test with a `SELECT` first when using `WHERE` — count the affected rows.
> - ✅ `UPDATE` is **NOT** a substitute for `INSERT` (new rows) or `DELETE` (removed rows).
> - ✅ `UPDATE` changes **data**, not **schema**; `ALTER` changes **schema**, not **data**.
> - ✅ Standard SQL evaluates SET expressions using **old row values** (no double-hike bug in single statement).
> - ✅ Use `BEGIN TRANSACTION; ... ROLLBACK;` pattern for safe experimentation.
> - ✅ `UPDATE` writes to **undo log** (for rollback) and **redo log** (for crash recovery) internally.
> - ✅ `MERGE` (or `UPSERT`) is preferred when both insert and update logic is needed.
> - ✅ Common KTU keywords to memorize: `SET`, `WHERE`, `ORDER BY`, `LIMIT`, `CASE`, `EXISTS`, correlated subquery, referential integrity, ON UPDATE CASCADE, DML, transaction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delete
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Delete

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **SQL DELETE statement** is a **Data Manipulation Language (DML)** command used to permanently remove one or more existing rows (records/tuples) from a relational table, governed by an optional search condition. It manipulates the *data* within a table without altering the table's structure (schema), column definitions, indexes, or constraints.

The American National Standards Institute (ANSI) / ISO SQL standard formally classifies `DELETE` under the **Data Manipulation Language** sub-category of SQL, alongside `INSERT` and `UPDATE`. It is a *row-oriented* destructive operation that respects the **ACID properties** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) of a transaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The DELETE command is examined as a core DML operation. Students must master its syntax, conditional filtering using the `WHERE` clause, cascading integrity behavior, and its critical differences from the DDL commands `DROP` and `TRUNCATE`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Classroom Attendance Register**
>
> Imagine a physical attendance register maintained by a teacher:
>
> - **`DELETE FROM Register;`** → The teacher rips out **every single entry** from the register but keeps the register book itself (with all column headings like *Roll No*, *Name*, *Signature*) intact on the desk.
> - **`DELETE FROM Register WHERE RollNo = 25;`** → The teacher looks specifically for Roll Number 25, draws a line through that one entry, and only that entry vanishes. All other entries remain perfectly intact.
> - **`DROP TABLE Register;`** → The teacher picks up the **entire register book** and throws it into a fire. The book ceases to exist — there is no register anymore.
> - **`TRUNCATE TABLE Register;`** → The teacher takes the register, opens it, and uses a paper shredder to remove all written content in one mechanical sweep — leaving a brand-new blank register.

This analogy is foundational for understanding why the KTU examiner tests the *context* of deletion in every exam cycle.

## 1.3 Standard Categorization

| Property | Classification |
|---|---|
| SQL Category | **DML** (Data Manipulation Language) |
| Autocommit Behavior | Can be **rolled back** (transactional) |
| Trigger Activation | Fires `BEFORE DELETE` and `AFTER DELETE` triggers |
| Affected Object | **Rows (data)** only — schema is untouched |
| Space Reclamation | Does **not** immediately reclaim disk space |
| Standard | **ANSI SQL-92, SQL:1999, SQL:2003, SQL:2006, SQL:2008, SQL:2011, SQL:2016, SQL:2023** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DELETE Syntax — Dissected

The full BNF (Backus-Naur Form) generalized syntax is:

```sql
DELETE FROM <table_name>
[WHERE <search_condition>];
```

Let us dissect every token:

| Token | Function | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|
| `DELETE FROM` | Keyword pair initiating the row-deletion operation | **YES** |
| `<table_name>` | The target table from which rows are removed | **YES** |
| `WHERE <search_condition>` | Boolean predicate; rows satisfying this are deleted | **NO** (but operationally critical) |
| `;` | Statement terminator (standard SQL) | Best practice |

## 2.2 Three Operational Modes of DELETE

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently frame questions around these three modes.

### Mode 1: Unconditional DELETE (The "All Rows" Mode)

```sql
DELETE FROM Employee;
```

**Behavior:** Every single row in the `Employee` table is deleted. The table itself, its columns, constraints, indexes, and views remain intact.

**Transactional Note:** In compliance with **ACID**, this operation is wrapped in an implicit transaction and *can* be rolled back using `ROLLBACK;` until a `COMMIT;` is issued.

### Mode 2: Conditional DELETE (The "Filtered" Mode)

```sql
DELETE FROM Employee WHERE Department = 'HR';
```

**Behavior:** Only those rows whose `Department` column equals the literal string `'HR'` are deleted. All other rows remain untouched.

### Mode 3: Subquery-Driven DELETE (The "Correlated/Non-Correlated" Mode)

```sql
DELETE FROM Employee
WHERE DeptID IN (SELECT DeptID FROM Department WHERE Location = 'Kerala');
```

**Behavior:** Rows in `Employee` are deleted based on a dynamically evaluated set retrieved by a nested `SELECT` statement. In MySQL, self-referencing subqueries on the same table are restricted unless wrapped in a derived table.

## 2.3 The DELETE vs. TRUNCATE vs. DROP Decision Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single highest-weightage comparison in KTU Module 2 SQL examinations.** Memorize the distinctions below with clinical precision.

| Dimension | `DELETE` | `TRUNCATE` | `DROP` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SQL Category** | DML | DDL | DDL |
| **Removes Rows?** | Yes (optionally all) | Yes (all) | Yes (all, with table) |
| **Removes Structure?** | **No** | No | **Yes** |
| **WHERE Clause?** | **Supported** | Not supported | Not applicable |
| **Transaction Rollback?** | **Yes** | No (in most RDBMS) | No |
| **Fires Triggers?** | **Yes** (per row) | No | No |
| **Resets Auto-Increment?** | No | **Yes** | Yes (table is gone) |
| **Speed** | Slowest (row-by-row) | Fastest (deallocates pages) | Fast |
| **Reclaims Disk Space?** | No (marks pages free) | **Yes** | Yes |
| **Permission Required** | `DELETE` privilege | `DROP` privilege (often) | `DROP` privilege |
| **Identity / Sequence** | Preserved | Reset to seed | Removed |

## 2.4 Referential Integrity and Cascading Deletes

When the target table has **Foreign Key** relationships, the DELETE operation interacts with referential actions declared in the `REFERENCES` clause of the child table's `FOREIGN KEY` constraint.

```sql
ALTER TABLE OrderTable
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_customer
FOREIGN KEY (CustomerID) REFERENCES Customer(CustomerID)
ON DELETE CASCADE;
```

| Referential Action | Effect When Parent Row is Deleted |
|---|---|
| `ON DELETE CASCADE` | Child rows are **automatically deleted** |
| `ON DELETE SET NULL` | Foreign key in child rows becomes `NULL` |
| `ON DELETE SET DEFAULT` | Foreign key is set to its default value |
| `ON DELETE RESTRICT` | **DELETE is rejected** if child rows exist |
| `ON DELETE NO ACTION` | Similar to RESTRICT (default in many DBMS) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In an e-commerce platform like *Amazon*, when a customer account is deleted, their orders may need to be purged (CASCADE) or retained for legal records (RESTRICT). DBAs select the action that matches business policy.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Comparison Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic DELETE | `DELETE FROM T WHERE C;` | If `C` is omitted → all rows deleted |
| 2 | Subquery DELETE | `DELETE FROM T WHERE col IN (SELECT col FROM T2 WHERE ...);` | Subquery cannot reference `T` directly in MySQL |
| 3 | Atomicity Rule | `n_rows_affected = Σ(rows where condition = TRUE)` | Either all qualifying rows are deleted, or none |
| 4 | Referential Constraint | `FOREIGN KEY ... ON DELETE {CASCADE ∨ SET NULL ∨ RESTRICT}` | Default = `NO ACTION` |
| 5 | Auto-Increment Preservation | After `DELETE`, `AUTO_INCREMENT` retains last value | `TRUNCATE` resets to `1` |
| 6 | Lock Granularity | Row-level locks during `DELETE` | Escalation possible in high-volume batch deletes |
| 7 | Transaction Log Entries | `2n + 1` log records for `n` deleted rows (approximation) | Depends on RDBMS engine |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Sample Derivations

## 3.1 Sample Schema for Analytical Problems

Consider the following normalized schema (commonly appearing in KTU question papers):

```sql
CREATE TABLE Department (
    DeptID   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    DeptName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    Location VARCHAR(50)
);

CREATE TABLE Employee (
    EmpID    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    EmpName  VARCHAR(50),
    Salary   DECIMAL(10,2),
    DeptID   INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (DeptID) REFERENCES Department(DeptID)
        ON DELETE SET NULL
);
```

**Inserted Data:**

| DeptID | DeptName | Location |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | HR | Kerala |
| 20 | IT | Karnataka |
| 30 | Finance | Tamil Nadu |

| EmpID | EmpName | Salary | DeptID |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arun | 50000 | 10 |
| 2 | Beena | 60000 | 20 |
| 3 | Cyrus | 55000 | 10 |
| 4 | Divya | 70000 | 30 |

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Conditional Deletion

**Query:**
```sql
DELETE FROM Employee WHERE DeptID = 10;
```

**Step-by-step logical evaluation:**

| Step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parser receives the statement | Builds parse tree |
| 2 | Optimizer scans `Employee` table | Reads every row |
| 3 | For each row, evaluate `DeptID = 10` | Row 1 (Arun) → TRUE |
| | | Row 2 (Beena) → FALSE |
| | | Row 3 (Cyrus) → TRUE |
| | | Row 4 (Divya) → FALSE |
| 4 | Mark qualifying rows for deletion | Rows 1 & 3 marked |
| 5 | Apply referential integrity check | No parent deletion occurs → no cascade needed |
| 6 | Write to transaction log | Log records created |
| 7 | Execute physical delete | Rows 1 & 3 removed |
| 8 | Return affected row count | **2 rows deleted** |

**Final Employee Table:**

| EmpID | EmpName | Salary | DeptID |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | Beena | 60000 | 20 |
| 4 | Divya | 70000 | 30 |

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: DELETE with Referential Cascade

**Query:**
```sql
DELETE FROM Department WHERE DeptID = 20;
```

**Logical evaluation chain:**

| Step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scan `Department` table for `DeptID = 20` | Row matching "IT" found |
| 2 | Inspect `Employee.DeptID` foreign key definition | Action = `ON DELETE SET NULL` |
| 3 | Set `Employee.DeptID` to `NULL` where `DeptID = 20` | Row 2 (Beena) → `DeptID = NULL` |
| 4 | Delete the parent row from `Department` | Row 20 removed from Department |
| 5 | Commit transaction | Both changes atomic |

**Final State:**

| DeptID | DeptName | Location |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | HR | Kerala |
| 30 | Finance | Tamil Nadu |

| EmpID | EmpName | Salary | DeptID |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | Beena | 60000 | **NULL** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Had the foreign key been defined with `ON DELETE RESTRICT`, the DELETE would have been **rejected entirely**, preserving data integrity at the cost of flexibility.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Subquery-Driven DELETE

**Query:**
```sql
DELETE FROM Employee
WHERE DeptID IN (
    SELECT DeptID FROM Department WHERE Location = 'Kerala'
);
```

**Algebraic-style representation:**

$$
\text{DELETE}_{\sigma_{\text{Location} = \text{'Kerala'}}}(\text{Employee})
$$

**Equivalent relational algebra expansion:**

$$
\text{Employee}' = \text{Employee} - \sigma_{\text{DeptID} \in \pi_{\text{DeptID}}(\sigma_{\text{Location}=\text{'Kerala'}}(\text{Department}))}(\text{Employee})
$$

**Execution trace:**

| Step | Operation | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Execute inner subquery | `{10}` (DeptID of HR) |
| 2 | Substitute into outer WHERE | `WHERE DeptID IN (10)` |
| 3 | Identify matching rows in Employee | Arun, Cyrus |
| 4 | Delete matching rows | 2 rows deleted |

## 3.5 Worked Example 4: DELETE All Rows (Unconditional)

**Query:**
```sql
DELETE FROM Employee;
```

**Behavioral trace:**

$$
\text{Rows affected} = \vert \text{Employee} \vert = n
$$

Where $n$ is the cardinality of the `Employee` table at execution time.

| Property | Observation |
|---|---|
| Schema | Preserved |
| Constraints | Preserved |
| Indexes | Preserved |
| Views referencing table | Continue to exist (may return empty) |
| Identity column | Last inserted value retained |
| Transactional rollback | Supported |
| Trigger firing | `BEFORE DELETE` and `AFTER DELETE` execute **once per row** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SQL DELETE Statement Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User issues DELETE statement] --> B[SQL Parser]
    B --> C{Syntax Valid?}
    C -- No --> D[Return Syntax Error]
    C -- Yes --> E[Query Optimizer]
    E --> F[Generate Execution Plan]
    F --> G[Acquire Row-Level Locks]
    G --> H[Scan Target Table Rows]
    H --> I{WHERE Clause Present?}
    I -- No --> J[Mark All Rows for Deletion]
    I -- Yes --> K[Evaluate Predicate per Row]
    K --> L{Row Matches Predicate?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Check Referential Integrity]
    L -- No --> H
    M --> N{Integrity Constraint Satisfied?}
    N -- No --> O[Abort and Rollback]
    N -- Yes --> P[Write Transaction Log Entry]
    P --> Q[Execute Physical Row Deletion]
    J --> M
    Q --> R[Fire AFTER DELETE Triggers]
    R --> S[Release Locks]
    S --> T[Return Affected Row Count]
    T --> U[End of Operation]
```

## 4.2 DELETE vs TRUNCATE vs DROP — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to remove data from a table?] --> B{Do you need to keep the table structure?}
    B -- No --> C[Use DROP TABLE]
    B -- Yes --> D{Do you need conditional deletion?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use DELETE with WHERE clause]
    D -- No --> F{Do you need transactional rollback?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use DELETE without WHERE]
    F -- No --> H{Do you need to reset auto-increment?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use TRUNCATE TABLE]
    H -- No --> G
    E --> J[Operation Complete]
    G --> J
    I --> J
    C --> J
```

## 4.3 Referential Integrity Action Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Parent Row DELETE Issued] --> B{Referential Action Specified?}
    B -- CASCADE --> C[Automatically Delete Child Rows]
    B -- SET NULL --> D[Set Child FK to NULL]
    B -- SET DEFAULT --> E[Set Child FK to Default Value]
    B -- RESTRICT --> F[Reject DELETE Operation]
    B -- NO ACTION --> G[Check at Statement End]
    C --> H[Transaction Commits]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> I[Error Returned]
    G --> J{Child Rows Exist?}
    J -- Yes --> I
    J -- No --> H
```

## 4.4 DELETE Lifecycle — Transactional View

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Application
    participant DB as Database Engine
    participant L as Transaction Log
    participant T as Trigger System

    U->>DB: BEGIN TRANSACTION
    U->>DB: DELETE FROM Employee WHERE DeptID = 10
    DB->>L: Write "BEGIN DELETE" record
    DB->>T: Fire BEFORE DELETE trigger
    loop For each qualifying row
        DB->>L: Write "DELETE row" log entry
        DB->>T: Fire per-row BEFORE/AFTER triggers
    end
    DB->>L: Write "END DELETE" record
    U->>DB: COMMIT
    DB->>L: Mark transaction as committed
    DB-->>U: Return affected row count
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between DELETE and TRUNCATE commands in SQL.**

**Model Answer:**

The `DELETE` command is a **Data Manipulation Language (DML)** operation that removes one or more rows from a table based on a specified `WHERE` clause condition. It supports conditional deletion using predicates, fires `BEFORE DELETE` and `AFTER DELETE` triggers for every affected row, and crucially, **can be rolled back** if executed within a transaction. The `AUTO_INCREMENT` counter is preserved after a DELETE operation.

The `TRUNCATE` command is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** operation that removes **all rows** from a table in a single operation by deallocating the data pages used by the table. It does not support a `WHERE` clause, **cannot be rolled back** in most RDBMS implementations (MySQL, PostgreSQL), does not fire row-level triggers, and **resets the auto-increment counter** to its seed value. TRUNCATE is faster than DELETE for removing all rows because it operates at the storage layer rather than evaluating rows individually.

| Aspect | DELETE | TRUNCATE |
|---|---|---|
| Category | DML | DDL |
| WHERE clause | Supported | Not supported |
| Rollback | Possible | Not possible |
| Trigger firing | Yes (per row) | No |
| Auto-increment | Preserved | Reset |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What happens if you execute `DELETE FROM Employee;` without a WHERE clause? Is the table structure affected?**

**Model Answer:**

Executing `DELETE FROM Employee;` without a `WHERE` clause removes **every row** present in the `Employee` table. However, the table **structure is not affected**. The table continues to exist in the database with all its column definitions, data types, constraints (PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, UNIQUE, CHECK, NOT NULL), indexes, and views intact. After the operation, the table is empty but immediately usable for new `INSERT` operations.

Importantly, because DELETE is a DML command, the entire operation is executed within a transaction. If the user issues a `ROLLBACK;` statement before `COMMIT;`, all deleted rows can be recovered. Additionally, `BEFORE DELETE` and `AFTER DELETE` triggers fire for every row that is removed, which can be performance-intensive for large tables. The `AUTO_INCREMENT` counter, if any, retains its last value.

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
*Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: each question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Pattern]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Consider the following two tables:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Dept (
    Dno INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Dname VARCHAR(30)
);

CREATE TABLE Staff (
    Sno INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Sname VARCHAR(30),
    Salary INT,
    Dno INT REFERENCES Dept(Dno) ON DELETE CASCADE
);
```

**The tables contain the following data:**

**Dept:**

| Dno | Dname |
|---|---|
| 1 | HR |
| 2 | Accounts |
| 3 | Sales |

**Staff:**

| Sno | Sname | Salary | Dno |
|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | Anil | 30000 | 1 |
| 102 | Bina | 40000 | 2 |
| 103 | Cijo | 35000 | 1 |
| 104 | Deepa | 45000 | 3 |

**Write and execute the following SQL queries:**
1. Delete the staff record with `Sno = 103`.
2. Delete all staff belonging to department number 2.
3. Delete the entire `Dept` table.
4. Delete the department with `Dno = 1`. What happens to the staff records?

**Model Solution:**

**Query 1: Delete staff with Sno = 103**
```sql
DELETE FROM Staff WHERE Sno = 103;
```
**Execution:** A single row (Cijo) is removed. The `Dept` table is unaffected. After execution, the Staff table contains Anil, Bina, and Deepa.

**Query 2: Delete all staff in Department 2**
```sql
DELETE FROM Staff WHERE Dno = 2;
```
**Execution:** A single row (Bina) is removed because only she belongs to Department 2. After execution, Staff contains Anil (Dno=1) and Deepa (Dno=3).

**Query 3: Delete the entire Dept table**
```sql
DELETE FROM Dept;
```
**Execution:** All rows in the `Dept` table are deleted. However, the **referential integrity action `ON DELETE CASCADE`** causes all corresponding rows in `Staff` (which reference existing Dno values) to be automatically deleted as well. After execution, both tables are empty, but their structures remain intact.

**Query 4: Delete the department with Dno = 1**
```sql
DELETE FROM Dept WHERE Dno = 1;
```
**Execution:** The HR row in Dept is removed. Due to `ON DELETE CASCADE`, the staff rows belonging to Dno=1 (i.e., Anil and Cijo, though Cijo was already removed) are automatically deleted. The final Staff table contains only Deepa (Sno=104).

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Explain with an example the difference between `ON DELETE CASCADE` and `ON DELETE SET NULL`. What would happen if no referential action is specified?**

**Model Solution:**

**`ON DELETE CASCADE`:** When a parent row is deleted, all child rows that reference it through a foreign key are **automatically and recursively deleted**. This maintains referential integrity by removing dependent data in a single atomic transaction.

*Example:*
```sql
CREATE TABLE OrderTable (
    OrderID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    CustID INT REFERENCES Customer(CustID) ON DELETE CASCADE
);
```
If `Customer(CustID = 5)` is deleted, all rows in `OrderTable` with `CustID = 5` are also deleted automatically.

**`ON DELETE SET NULL`:** When a parent row is deleted, the foreign key column in the child rows is set to `NULL`. The child rows themselves are preserved, but their link to the parent is severed.

*Example:*
```sql
CREATE TABLE OrderTable (
    OrderID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    CustID INT REFERENCES Customer(CustID) ON DELETE SET NULL
);
```
If `Customer(CustID = 5)` is deleted, all rows in `OrderTable` with `CustID = 5` will have `CustID` updated to `NULL`.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | ON DELETE CASCADE | ON DELETE SET NULL |
|---|---|---|
| Child rows removed? | Yes | No |
| Child FK value | Deleted entirely | Set to NULL |
| Use case | Dependent data is meaningless without parent | Child can exist independently |
| Storage | Frees space | Retains space |
| Nullable FK required? | No | Yes |

**No Referential Action Specified:** If neither `ON DELETE CASCADE`, `SET NULL`, `SET DEFAULT`, nor `RESTRICT` is specified, the default action in most RDBMS is `NO ACTION` (equivalent to `RESTRICT`). In this case, the DELETE statement on the parent table is **rejected with an integrity constraint violation error** if any child rows reference the parent. The database protects itself from orphan records by refusing the operation.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Pattern]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Explain the SQL DELETE command with its general syntax. Differentiate between the DROP, TRUNCATE, and DELETE commands with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Solution:**

**General Syntax of DELETE:**
```sql
DELETE FROM <table_name>
[WHERE <condition>];
```

The `WHERE` clause is optional. If omitted, all rows of the table are deleted. The operation is transactional and can be rolled back. DELETE fires triggers and respects referential integrity constraints.

**DROP Command:**
```sql
DROP TABLE Employee;
```
DROP is a DDL command that removes the **entire table** including its structure, data, indexes, constraints, and triggers. After execution, the table ceases to exist. It cannot be rolled back, and any view, stored procedure, or synonym referencing the table becomes invalid.

**TRUNCATE Command:**
```sql
TRUNCATE TABLE Employee;
```
TRUNCATE is a DDL command that removes **all rows** from the table while preserving the structure. It is faster than DELETE because it deallocates the data pages rather than removing rows one by one. It resets the AUTO_INCREMENT counter and typically cannot be rolled back.

**DELETE Command:**
```sql
DELETE FROM Employee WHERE Salary < 25000;
```
DELETE is a DML command that removes rows based on a condition. Only those rows satisfying the predicate are removed. It is transactional, supports rollback, and fires triggers.

**Comprehensive Comparison:**

| Feature | DELETE | TRUNCATE | DROP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | DML | DDL | DDL |
| Removes rows | Selected/All | All | All (with table) |
| Removes structure | No | No | Yes |
| WHERE clause | Yes | No | No |
| Rollback | Yes | No | No |
| Triggers fired | Yes | No | No |
| Auto-increment reset | No | Yes | N/A |
| Speed | Slow | Fast | Fast |

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Consider the following table:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Library (
    BookID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Title VARCHAR(50),
    Author VARCHAR(50),
    Price DECIMAL(8,2),
    Category VARCHAR(20)
);
```

**Write SQL DELETE statements for the following scenarios:**
1. Delete the book with BookID = 105.
2. Delete all books priced above 500.
3. Delete all books whose category is "Fiction" and price is below 300.
4. Delete all records from the Library table.

**Model Solution:**

**Scenario 1: Delete a single specific book**
```sql
DELETE FROM Library WHERE BookID = 105;
```
This removes the row where `BookID` equals `105`. At most one row is affected since `BookID` is the primary key.

**Scenario 2: Delete books priced above 500**
```sql
DELETE FROM Library WHERE Price > 500;
```
This removes all rows where the `Price` column exceeds 500. Multiple rows may be affected.

**Scenario 3: Delete Fiction books priced below 300**
```sql
DELETE FROM Library WHERE Category = 'Fiction' AND Price < 300;
```
This combines two predicates using the logical AND operator. Only rows matching *both* conditions are deleted.

**Scenario 4: Delete all records**
```sql
DELETE FROM Library;
```
This removes every row from the `Library` table. The table structure, including all column definitions, primary key constraint, and indexes, remains intact. The operation is wrapped in a transaction and can be rolled back.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting `FROM` keyword:** Writing `DELETE Employee` instead of `DELETE FROM Employee` results in a syntax error.
> 2. **Confusing TRUNCATE with DELETE:** Stating that TRUNCATE can be rolled back is factually incorrect for MySQL/PostgreSQL and costs full marks on comparison questions.
> 3. **Ignoring referential integrity:** Not mentioning `ON DELETE CASCADE` behavior when explaining deletion in related tables leads to incomplete answers.
> 4. **Omitting transaction context:** DELETE's rollback capability is a frequently tested point — students who omit the transactional nature lose marks.
> 5. **Misidentifying SQL category:** Marking DELETE as DDL instead of DML is a critical classification error.
> 6. **Skipping trigger behavior:** Forgetting that DELETE fires `BEFORE`/`AFTER DELETE` triggers per row is a common omission.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for DELETE in KTU Module 2:**

- 🔹 **DELETE** is a **DML** command; **TRUNCATE** and **DROP** are **DDL** commands.
- 🔹 Syntax: `DELETE FROM table_name [WHERE condition];`
- 🔹 Without `WHERE`, **all rows** are deleted but the **table structure remains**.
- 🔹 DELETE supports **transactional rollback**; TRUNCATE typically does not.
- 🔹 DELETE **fires triggers**; TRUNCATE does not.
- 🔹 DELETE is **slower** than TRUNCATE for bulk deletion due to per-row processing.
- 🔹 DELETE **does not reset** auto-increment counters; TRUNCATE does.
- 🔹 Referential actions on DELETE: `CASCADE`, `SET NULL`, `SET DEFAULT`, `RESTRICT`, `NO ACTION`.
- 🔹 DELETE with a **subquery** enables dynamic, correlated row removal.
- 🔹 DELETE respects **foreign key constraints** unless cascade actions are defined.
- 🔹 DELETE returns the **number of affected rows** (row count).
- 🔹 DELETE **does not free disk space** immediately; it marks pages as reusable.
- 🔹 In MySQL, DELETE on a self-referencing subquery requires a **derived table** wrapper.
- 🔹 DELETE can be combined with `LIMIT n` in MySQL to cap affected rows.
- 🔹 DELETE logs every row deletion in the **transaction log** for recovery.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nested Queries (Correlated and Non-Correlated), Aggregation and Grouping, Views, Example SQL Queries on Single and Multiple Tables, Query Practise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nested Queries (Correlated and Non-Correlated), Aggregation and Grouping, Views, Example SQL Queries on Single and Multiple Tables, Query Practise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Nested Queries (Subqueries) — Formal Definition

A **Nested Query** (also called a *subquery* or *inner query*) is a `SELECT` statement embedded within another SQL statement, typically enclosed in parentheses and executed as part of a larger query. KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes nested queries into two distinct execution models:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A subquery is a query that appears inside another query statement. Subqueries can be classified as **non-correlated** (independent of the outer query, evaluated once) and **correlated** (references columns of the outer query, evaluated for each row of the outer query)."*

### Types of Subqueries

| Type | Independence | Execution Frequency | Result Cardinality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-Correlated Subquery** | Independent of outer query | Executed **once** | Returns a single value, list, or table |
| **Correlated Subquery** | References outer query columns | Executed **once per outer row** | Returns a value/table per iteration |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library and the Bookshelf"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> Imagine you are in a library with two rooms — **Room A** (Outer Query) and **Room B** (Inner Query).
> - In a **Non-Correlated** subquery, the librarian in Room B prepares a complete list of all books by a specific author *first*, then brings this finished list into Room A. The list is ready before Room A even starts.
> - In a **Correlated** subquery, Room A tells Room B: *"For every book I show you, tell me whether it's by a specific author."* Room B must re-check **for each book** passed in. It cannot start until Room A gives it a row, and Room A cannot decide without Room B's verdict.

## 1.3 Aggregation and Grouping — Formal Definition

**Aggregation** in SQL refers to the application of *aggregate functions* that operate on a set of rows to produce a single summarized value. **Grouping** is the partitioning of a relation into subsets of tuples sharing common attribute values.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Aggregate Functions:** `COUNT(*)`, `COUNT([DISTINCT] col)`, `SUM(col)`, `AVG(col)`, `MIN(col)`, `MAX(col)`. These are evaluated **after** the `FROM`/`WHERE` clauses and **before** the `HAVING` clause.

## 1.4 Views — Formal Definition

A **View** in SQL is a *virtual relation* defined by a query expression. It does **not** store tuples physically; instead, it stores a **query expression** that is materialized **on demand** when referenced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *"A view is a virtual table whose contents are defined by a query. Unlike base tables, a view does not form part of the physical schema; it is a dynamic window onto one or more underlying base tables."*

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — "The CCTV Monitor"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for Views:** Think of a view as a *CCTV camera* pointed at one or more rooms (tables). The camera doesn't store any video by default — it just shows you a specific angle. If the room rearranges furniture (data changes), the camera shows the new arrangement. Some cameras (updatable views) can even be used to *modify* what's inside the room, but most are read-only.

---

## 1.6 Reference Schema Used Throughout These Notes

To make all examples concrete, we adopt the canonical **Employee–Department schema** standard to KTU board examinations:

**DEPARTMENT** (`DNo`, `DName`, `Mgr_SSN`, `Mgr_Start_Date`)

**EMPLOYEE** (`SSN`, `EName`, `BDate`, `Address`, `Sex`, `Salary`, `Super_SSN`, `DNo`)

**DEPT_LOCATIONS** (`DNo`, `Location`)

**PROJECT** (`PNo`, `PName`, `PLocation`, `DNo`)

**WORKS_ON** (`SSN`, `PNo`, `Hours`)

> [!NOTE]
> Primary keys are **underlined**: `DNo` in DEPARTMENT, `SSN` in EMPLOYEE, `PNo` in PROJECT, and the composite `{DNo, Location}` in DEPT_LOCATIONS, `{SSN, PNo}` in WORKS_ON.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Non-Correlated Subqueries — Operational Logic

A **non-correlated subquery** is evaluated *once* and its result is fed into the outer query. The inner query has **no reference** to the outer query's attributes.

### 2.1.1 Allowed Result Shapes

| Result Shape | Use With | Example Operator |
|---|---|---|
| Single scalar value | `=`, `<>`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` | `WHERE Salary > (SELECT AVG(Salary) ...)` |
| Single-column list | `IN`, `NOT IN`, `ANY`, `ALL` | `WHERE DNo IN (1, 2, 3)` |
| Multi-row multi-column | `EXISTS` only (or FROM-clause) | `WHERE (a,b) IN (...)` |

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Execution Model

1. **Parse** the entire statement.
2. **Evaluate the inner query** in isolation → produces a relation $R_{inner}$.
3. **Substitute** $R_{inner}$ logically into the outer query predicate.
4. **Evaluate** the outer query against the base tables.
5. **Return** the final result set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The optimizer may rewrite this to a *join*, but the **logical** SQL model still follows the above 5-step procedure. KTU examiners expect this logical model in written answers.

## 2.2 Correlated Subqueries — Operational Logic

A **correlated subquery** depends on one or more columns of the outer query. The inner query is *logically re-executed* for each candidate row of the outer query.

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Execution Model

1. **Pick a candidate row** $r$ from the outer table.
2. **Substitute** $r$'s column values into the inner query's correlated references.
3. **Evaluate** the inner query with these substituted values.
4. **Use the boolean** outcome (or returned value) to test the predicate on $r$.
5. **Repeat** for the next outer row until the outer table is exhausted.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Exam Pitfall: Students often write correlated subqueries where a non-correlated one would suffice. This leads to **O(N × M)** complexity. Examiners specifically test this distinction in **3-mark questions**.

## 2.3 Aggregation & Grouping — Logical Phases

SQL query evaluation (specifically with aggregation) follows a **strict phase order** mandated by the SQL standard:

| Phase | Clause | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **P1** | `FROM` | Cartesian product + join |
| **P2** | `WHERE` | Row-level filter |
| **P3** | `GROUP BY` | Partition rows into groups |
| **P4** | `HAVING` | Group-level filter (uses aggregates) |
| **P5** | `SELECT` | Compute output expressions (including aggregates) |
| **P6** | `DISTINCT` | Eliminate duplicate output rows |
| **P7** | `ORDER BY` | Sort final result |
| **P8** | `LIMIT/OFFSET` | Pagination |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregate functions CANNOT appear in `WHERE`** because `WHERE` runs **before** grouping (P2 vs P3). This is one of the most heavily tested KTU concepts.

## 2.4 The `HAVING` vs `WHERE` Distinction

| Feature | `WHERE` | `HAVING` |
|---|---|---|
| Operates on | Individual rows | Groups of rows |
| Aggregate functions allowed? | **NO** | **YES** |
| Executes | **Before** `GROUP BY` | **After** `GROUP BY` |
| Typical use | Filter tuples | Filter summarized groups |

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — SQL High-Yield Reference Box**

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
