| Aggregate `COUNT(*)` | $\text{COUNT}(*) = n$ where $n$ = number of rows | Counts rows, including NULLs |
| Aggregate `COUNT(col)` | $\text{COUNT}(col) = \vert \{ t \in R : t.col \neq \text{NULL} \} \vert$ | Excludes NULLs |
| `SUM` | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i.col$ | Ignores NULLs |
| `AVG` | $\dfrac{\sum_{i} t_i.col}{\vert \{i : t_i.col \neq \text{NULL}\} \vert}$ | NULLs excluded from numerator & denominator |
| `MIN` / `MAX` | $\min, \max$ over non-NULL values | Skip NULLs |
| `GROUP BY` semantic | $R' = \text{Partition}(R, \text{attrs})$ | Each group shares values of grouping attrs |
| `IN` quantifier | $x \in S \iff \exists y \in S : x = y$ | Membership test |
| `EXISTS` | $\exists t \in S : P(t)$ | Returns TRUE if subquery non-empty |
| `NOT EXISTS` | $\lnot \exists t \in S : P(t) \iff \forall t \in S : \lnot P(t)$ | **Set difference** semantic |
| `ANY` (with $>$) | $x > \text{ANY}(S) \iff \exists y \in S : x > y$ |  |
| `ALL` (with $>$) | $x > \text{ALL}(S) \iff \forall y \in S : x > y$ |  |
| View expansion | $\text{View}_V(Q) \rightarrow Q'$ where $Q' = Q$ with subquery substituted | **Query modification** approach |
| Updatable view | Single base table + no `DISTINCT`/`GROUP BY`/aggregates | Required for `INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` |

> [!NOTE]
> Note the use of `\vert` (set cardinality) instead of `|` to maintain clean markdown table rendering. The pipe symbol is reserved for table-cell separation by the markdown parser.

## 2.6 Engineering / Real-World Utility

| Concept | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| **Subqueries** | Reporting dashboards, hierarchical org charts, data auditing |
| **Aggregation** | Payroll computation, sales analytics, OLAP cubes, billing summaries |
| **Views** | Role-based access control, decoupling schema, simplifying complex joins for end-users |
| **Group By + Having** | Top-N-per-group queries (e.g., "highest salary per department") |

---

## 2.7 The SQL Statement Skeleton (Master Template)

```sql
SELECT   [DISTINCT]  select_list              -- P5, P6
FROM     table_list                           -- P1
[WHERE   row_filter]                          -- P2
[GROUP BY group_columns]                      -- P3
[HAVING  group_filter]                        -- P4
[ORDER BY sort_columns [ASC | DESC]]          -- P7
[LIMIT n OFFSET m];                           -- P8
```

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the **phase order P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 → P5 → P6 → P7 → P8** — it is the single most important concept for solving KTU SQL questions and predicting output traces.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates *logical derivation* of result sets and execution traces. Since SQL is a declarative language, "derivation" here means step-by-step transformation of the query into its relational-algebra equivalent and row-by-row execution trace.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Non-Correlated Subquery (Single Value)

### Question
*"Find the names of employees who earn more than the average salary."*

### SQL
```sql
SELECT EName
FROM   EMPLOYEE
WHERE  Salary > (SELECT AVG(Salary) FROM EMPLOYEE);
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

Let $R = \text{EMPLOYEE}$, and let $S_{avg}$ denote the result of the inner query.

**Step 1 — Evaluate Inner Query (Independent):**
$$S_{avg} = \sigma_{\text{aggregate}}(R) = \text{AVG}(R.\text{Salary})$$

Suppose the relation $R$ has salaries $\{30\text{k},\, 40\text{k},\, 50\text{k},\, 60\text{k},\, 70\text{k}\}$. Then:
$$S_{avg} = \frac{30+40+50+60+70}{5} = \frac{250}{5} = 50\text{k}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Outer Query Predicate:**
$$\sigma_{\text{Salary} > S_{avg}}(R) = \sigma_{\text{Salary} > 50\text{k}}(R)$$

**Step 3 — Compute Final Projection:**
$$\pi_{\text{EName}}(\sigma_{\text{Salary} > 50\text{k}}(R)) = \{ \text{'Anu'}, \text{'Ravi'} \}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The inner query was evaluated **exactly once**, producing a scalar. The outer query then used this scalar in its `WHERE` predicate. This is the textbook signature of a **non-correlated** subquery.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Correlated Subquery (Row-by-Row)

### Question
*"Find the names of employees who earn more than the average salary **of their own department**."*

### SQL
```sql
SELECT E1.EName
FROM   EMPLOYEE E1
WHERE  E1.Salary > (
         SELECT AVG(E2.Salary)
         FROM   EMPLOYEE E2
         WHERE  E2.DNo = E1.DNo
       );
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

Here, the inner query references `E1.DNo` from the **outer** query — this is the correlation link.

Let the EMPLOYEE table be:

| SSN | EName  | Salary | DNo |
|-----|--------|--------|-----|
| 1   | Anu    | 50k    | 1   |
| 2   | Ravi   | 70k    | 1   |
| 3   | Meera  | 60k    | 2   |
| 4   | Sajan  | 80k    | 2   |
| 5   | Latha  | 40k    | 1   |

**Iteration 1 — Outer row $E1 = (\text{Anu}, 50\text{k}, 1)$:**
- Inner query with `E2.DNo = 1` retrieves rows $\{50\text{k}, 70\text{k}, 40\text{k}\}$.
- $\text{AVG} = \dfrac{50+70+40}{3} = 53.33\text{k}$.
- Test: $50\text{k} > 53.33\text{k}$? **FALSE** → Anu is **excluded**.

**Iteration 2 — Outer row $E1 = (\text{Ravi}, 70\text{k}, 1)$:**
- Inner AVG (same as above) = $53.33\text{k}$.
- Test: $70\text{k} > 53.33\text{k}$? **TRUE** → Ravi is **included**.

**Iteration 3 — Outer row $E1 = (\text{Meera}, 60\text{k}, 2)$:**
- Inner AVG for DNo=2 = $\dfrac{60+80}{2} = 70\text{k}$.
- Test: $60\text{k} > 70\text{k}$? **FALSE** → Meera is **excluded**.

**Iteration 4 — Outer row $E1 = (\text{Sajan}, 80\text{k}, 2)$:**
- Inner AVG = $70\text{k}$.
- Test: $80\text{k} > 70\text{k}$? **TRUE** → Sajan is **included**.

**Iteration 5 — Outer row $E1 = (\text{Latha}, 40\text{k}, 1)$:**
- Inner AVG = $53.33\text{k}$.
- Test: $40\text{k} > 53.33\text{k}$? **FALSE** → Latha is **excluded**.

**Final Result:** $\{ \text{Ravi}, \text{Sajan} \}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Complexity Analysis for KTU:** Total work = $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert R_i \vert \le n^2$. For $n=10^6$, this is $10^{12}$ operations. The optimizer usually converts this to a *window function* or *join* to avoid the quadratic blowup. KTU questions test both forms.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aggregation with `GROUP BY` and `HAVING`

### Question
*"For each department having more than 2 employees, show the department number and the maximum salary."*

### SQL
```sql
SELECT   DNo, MAX(Salary) AS MaxSal
FROM     EMPLOYEE
GROUP BY DNo
HAVING   COUNT(*) > 2;
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Phase P1 — FROM:** No joins; relation is $R = \text{EMPLOYEE}$ (5 rows from §3.2).

**Phase P2 — WHERE:** None in this query.

**Phase P3 — GROUP BY DNo:** Partition $R$ by `DNo`:

| DNo | Group Tuples (Salary) | Group Size |
|-----|----------------------|------------|
| 1   | $\{50\text{k}, 70\text{k}, 40\text{k}\}$ | 3 |
| 2   | $\{60\text{k}, 80\text{k}\}$ | 2 |

**Phase P4 — HAVING COUNT(*) > 2:**
- Group DNo=1: COUNT = 3 > 2 → **KEEP**
- Group DNo=2: COUNT = 2 > 2 → **FALSE → DROP**

**Phase P5 — SELECT:** Compute `MAX(Salary)` for surviving groups:
- DNo=1: $\max\{50, 70, 40\} = 70\text{k}$.

**Final Result:**

| DNo | MaxSal |
|-----|--------|
| 1   | 70000  |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — `IN` vs `EXISTS` vs `JOIN` (Three Equivalent Forms)

### Question
*"Find the names of employees who work on **any** project."*

### Form A — `IN` (Non-Correlated)
```sql
SELECT EName FROM EMPLOYEE
WHERE  SSN IN (SELECT SSN FROM WORKS_ON);
```

### Form B — `EXISTS` (Correlated)
```sql
SELECT EName FROM EMPLOYEE E
WHERE  EXISTS (SELECT * FROM WORKS_ON W WHERE W.SSN = E.SSN);
```

### Form C — `JOIN`
```sql
SELECT DISTINCT E.EName
FROM   EMPLOYEE E JOIN WORKS_ON W ON E.SSN = W.SSN;
```

### Logical Equivalence

All three forms compute:
$$\pi_{\text{EName}}( \text{EMPLOYEE} \bowtie_{\text{EMPLOYEE.SSN} = \text{WORKS_ON.SSN}} \text{WORKS_ON} )$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 examiners often ask: *"Rewrite the given `IN` query using `EXISTS`."* The trick is to remember that `IN` is **non-correlated** (the inner list is computed first) while `EXISTS` is typically **correlated** (the inner runs per outer row). The result is logically identical; performance differs.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — View Creation and Materialization

### View Definition
```sql
CREATE VIEW HighEarners AS
SELECT EName, Salary, DNo
FROM   EMPLOYEE
WHERE  Salary > 50000;
```

### Query Using the View
```sql
SELECT DNo, COUNT(*) AS NumHigh
FROM   HighEarners
GROUP BY DNo;
```

### View Modification (Logical Expansion)

The DBMS performs **query modification** — it inlines the view's defining query:

**Step 1 — Replace the view reference with its definition:**
```sql
SELECT DNo, COUNT(*) AS NumHigh
FROM   (SELECT EName, Salary, DNo
        FROM   EMPLOYEE
        WHERE  Salary > 50000) AS HighEarners
GROUP BY DNo;
```

**Step 2 — Apply normal phases P1 → P8.**

**Algebraic Representation:**
$$\text{Result} = \pi_{\text{DNo, COUNT(*)}}\big( \gamma_{\text{DNo, COUNT(*)}}(\sigma_{\text{Salary}>50000}(\text{EMPLOYEE})) \big)$$

where $\gamma$ is the `GROUP BY` operator and $\sigma$ is the selection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The view itself is **not** materialized (unless it is a *materialized view* — not in standard SQL:2023 unless explicitly created as one). Every reference re-runs the underlying query. This is a **frequently-asked 3-mark question**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comprehensive Comparison

| Feature | Non-Correlated Subquery | Correlated Subquery | View | Aggregate w/ Group By |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Execution frequency of inner | **1 time** | **N times** (per outer row) | **On demand** per reference | 1 time per query |
| Depends on outer? | No | Yes | N/A | N/A |
| Can use `EXISTS`? | Yes (degenerate) | Yes (canonical) | N/A | N/A |
| Can use `IN`? | Yes (canonical) | Possible but rare | N/A | N/A |
| Aggregates allowed inside? | Yes | Yes | In defining query | **Yes — central** |
| Uses `GROUP BY`? | Optional | Optional | Optional | **Required for grouping** |
| Modifies data? | Only via outer DML | Only via outer DML | If updatable, yes | No |
| Cost model | $O(N) + O(M)$ | $O(N \cdot M)$ | $O(\text{view def. cost})$ | $O(N \log N)$ typical |
| KTU weightage | **High** | **Very High** | **High** | **Very High** |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are rendered as **Mermaid** blocks per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol. Node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. Labels use raw uppercase text only.

---

## 4.1 Execution Flow — Non-Correlated vs Correlated Subquery

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START QUERY] --> B[PARSE BOTH INNER AND OUTER]
    B --> C{INNER REFERENCES OUTER COLUMNS}
    C -- NO --> D1[NON CORRELATED PATH]
    C -- YES --> D2[CORRELATED PATH]

    D1 --> E1[STEP 1 EVALUATE INNER QUERY ONCE]
    E1 --> F1[STEP 2 PRODUCE RESULT SET R INNER]
    F1 --> G1[STEP 3 SUBSTITUTE R INNER INTO OUTER PREDICATE]
    G1 --> H1[STEP 4 EVALUATE OUTER QUERY]
    H1 --> I1[STEP 5 RETURN FINAL RESULT]
    I1 --> Z[END]

    D2 --> E2[STEP 1 PICK FIRST OUTER ROW R]
    E2 --> F2[STEP 2 BIND R COLUMNS INTO INNER QUERY]
    F2 --> G2[STEP 3 EVALUATE INNER QUERY WITH BOUND VALUES]
    G2 --> H2[STEP 4 APPLY OUTER PREDICATE TEST ON R]
    H2 --> I2[STEP 5 INCLUDE OR EXCLUDE R FROM RESULT]
    I2 --> J2{MORE OUTER ROWS REMAINING}
    J2 -- YES --> E2
    J2 -- NO --> K2[RETURN FINAL RESULT]
    K2 --> Z
```

---

## 4.2 SQL Statement Processing Pipeline (8 Phases)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[P1 FROM CARTESIAN AND JOIN] --> P2[P2 WHERE ROW FILTER]
    P2 --> P3[P3 GROUP BY PARTITION INTO GROUPS]
    P3 --> P4[P4 HAVING GROUP FILTER]
    P4 --> P5[P5 SELECT COMPUTE OUTPUT]
    P5 --> P6[P6 DISTINCT REMOVE DUPLICATES]
    P6 --> P7[P7 ORDER BY SORT RESULT]
    P7 --> P8[P8 LIMIT OFFSET PAGINATE]
    P8 --> OUT[FINAL RESULT SET]

    style P1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style P2 fill:#e1f5ff
    style P3 fill:#fff4e1
    style P4 fill:#fff4e1
    style P5 fill:#e8f5e1
    style P6 fill:#e8f5e1
    style P7 fill:#f5e1f5
    style P8 fill:#f5e1f5
```

> [!NOTE]
> `WHERE` runs in **P2**, **before** grouping in P3. Therefore, aggregate functions cannot appear in `WHERE` — they are only valid in P4 (`HAVING`) or P5 (`SELECT`).

---

## 4.3 View Architecture — Logical to Physical

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    USER[USER QUERY REFERENCES VIEW V] --> RESOLVE[DBMS VIEW RESOLVER]
    RESOLVE --> SUB[QUERY MODIFICATION ENGINE]
    SUB --> EXPAND[INLINE VIEW DEFINITION INTO USER QUERY]
    EXPAND --> EXEC[EXECUTE EXPANDED QUERY AGAINST BASE TABLES]
    EXEC --> BT1[BASE TABLE 1 EMPLOYEE]
    EXEC --> BT2[BASE TABLE 2 DEPARTMENT]
    EXEC --> BT3[BASE TABLE 3 PROJECT]
    BT1 --> MERGE[COMBINE PARTIAL RESULTS]
    BT2 --> MERGE
    BT3 --> MERGE
    MERGE --> OUT[RETURN RESULT TO USER]

    subgraph META [DATA DICTIONARY]
        VDEF[VIEW DEFINITION STORED HERE]
        BTDEF1[BASE TABLE SCHEMAS]
    end

    RESOLVE -. LOOKUP .-> VDEF
    EXEC -. LOOKUP .-> BTDEF1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Only the **view definition** (the `SELECT` query) is stored — not the data. The result is computed fresh on every access unless a *materialized view* is explicitly defined.

---

## 4.4 Group By and Having Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[SQL QUERY WITH AGGREGATES] --> Q1{IS AGGREGATE IN WHERE CLAUSE}
    Q1 -- YES --> ERR1[ERROR AGGREGATES NOT ALLOWED IN WHERE]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{ARE AGGREGATES IN SELECT OR HAVING}
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3{IS GROUP BY PRESENT}
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4[IMPLICIT SINGLE GROUP OVER ALL ROWS]
    Q3 -- YES --> Q5[PARTITION ROWS BY GROUP BY COLUMNS]
    Q4 --> Q6[COMPUTE AGGREGATES PER GROUP]
    Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 --> Q7{HAVING CLAUSE PRESENT}
    Q7 -- YES --> Q8[FILTER GROUPS USING HAVING PREDICATE]
    Q7 -- NO --> Q9[RETURN ALL GROUPS]
    Q8 --> SEL[SELECT OUTPUT COLUMNS]
    Q9 --> SEL
    Q4 --> SEL
```

---

## 4.5 Subquery Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SQ[SUBQUERY CLASSIFICATION] --> TYP{RETURNS WHAT}
    TYP -- SINGLE VALUE --> SCALAR[SCALAR SUBQUERY]
    TYP -- SINGLE COLUMN LIST --> LIST[LIST SUBQUERY]
    TYP -- MULTI COLUMN --> TAB[TABLE SUBQUERY]
    TYP -- BOOLEAN --> EX[EXISTS OR NOT EXISTS]

    SQ --> COR{CORRELATED WITH OUTER}
    COR -- NO --> NC[NON CORRELATED]
    COR -- YES --> CR[CORRELATED]

    NC --> USES[USED WITH OPERATORS]
    USES --> OP1[SCALAR COMPARISON EQUAL LESS GREATER]
    USES --> OP2[IN OR NOT IN]
    USES --> OP3[ANY OR ALL]

    CR --> USES2[USED WITH OPERATORS]
    USES2 --> OP4[EXISTS OR NOT EXISTS]
    USES2 --> OP5[CORRELATED SCALAR COMPARISON]
```

---

## 4.6 Conceptual Block Diagram — Multi-Table Query Join Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[EMPLOYEE TABLE] -->|SSN| W[WORKS_ON TABLE]
    P[PROJECT TABLE] -->|PNo| W
    D[DEPARTMENT TABLE] -->|DNo| E
    D -->|DNo| P
    DL[DEPT_LOCATIONS TABLE] -->|DNo| D

    Q[USER QUERY] -->|JOINS| E
    Q -->|JOINS| P
    Q -->|JOINS| W
    Q -->|JOINS| D
    Q -->|JOINS| DL
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram represents the **logical join graph** for our reference schema. Real-world queries traverse this graph using the foreign-key links: `DNo`, `SSN`, `PNo`. KTU questions often require joins across 3+ tables.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are framed to mirror **actual KTU board exam** style under the 2024 Scheme. Each Part A is **3 marks** and each Part B sub-question is **7 marks** (totaling 14 marks per main question with internal choice).

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **correlated** and **non-correlated** subqueries with a suitable example for each.

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

| Aspect | Non-Correlated Subquery | Correlated Subquery |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Inner query is **independent** of the outer query | Inner query **references** one or more columns of the outer query |
| **Execution** | Inner query executed **once**, then its result is used by outer | Inner query executed **once per outer row** |
| **Performance** | Generally **faster** (single execution) | **Slower** (repeated execution) |
| **Typical operators** | `IN`, `=`, `>`, `<`, comparison with scalar | `EXISTS`, correlated scalar comparison |

**Non-Correlated Example** (1.5 marks):
```sql
SELECT EName FROM EMPLOYEE
WHERE  DNo = (SELECT DNo FROM DEPARTMENT WHERE DName = 'Research');
```
The inner query is evaluated once; the resulting `DNo` is used to filter outer.

**Correlated Example** (1 mark):
```sql
SELECT E1.EName FROM EMPLOYEE E1
WHERE  E1.Salary > (SELECT AVG(E2.Salary) FROM EMPLOYEE E2
                    WHERE E2.DNo = E1.DNo);
```
The inner query references `E1.DNo` from the outer query, so it must re-execute for each row of `E1`.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Explain the difference between the `WHERE` and `HAVING` clauses. Why cannot aggregate functions be used in `WHERE`?

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

The `WHERE` and `HAVING` clauses both filter rows, but they operate at **different stages** of SQL evaluation (2 marks):

1. **`WHERE` clause** — applies a predicate to *individual rows* of the base relation **before** any grouping occurs. It executes in **Phase P2** of the SQL pipeline.
2. **`HAVING` clause** — applies a predicate to *entire groups* of rows **after** the `GROUP BY` operation. It executes in **Phase P4**.

Aggregate functions (e.g., `SUM`, `AVG`, `COUNT`) compute a single value from a **set** of rows. They require the rows to already be partitioned into groups before they can be evaluated. Since `WHERE` runs *before* `GROUP BY` (Phase P2), the input set for an aggregate is not yet defined. Hence, the SQL standard **forbids** aggregate functions in `WHERE` (1 mark).

**Example illustrating the difference:**
```sql
SELECT DNo, COUNT(*) FROM EMPLOYEE
WHERE  Sex = 'M'        -- filter individual rows first
GROUP BY DNo
HAVING COUNT(*) > 2;    -- then filter the resulting groups
```

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

Consider the schema:
- **EMPLOYEE** (`SSN`, `EName`, `Salary`, `Super_SSN`, `DNo`)
- **DEPARTMENT** (`DNo`, `DName`, `Mgr_SSN`)
- **WORKS_ON** (`SSN`, `PNo`, `Hours`)

Write SQL queries for the following:

**(a)** Find the names of all employees who work on **every project** controlled by department 5. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For each department, list the department number, number of employees, and average salary, but only include departments where the average salary is greater than the overall average salary of the company. Sort the result by average salary in descending order. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Employees Working on All Projects of Dept 5 *(7 marks)*

This is a classic **universal quantification** problem in SQL. The phrase *"works on every project"* translates to:

$$ \text{Employee } e \text{ works on every project} \iff \lnot \exists\, \text{project } p \in P_5 \text{ such that } e \text{ does not work on } p $$

**Step 1 — Identify projects controlled by department 5 (subquery S1):**
```sql
SELECT PNo FROM PROJECT WHERE DNo = 5
```

**Step 2 — Use double negation with `NOT EXISTS` (correlated subquery):**
```sql
SELECT  E.EName
FROM    EMPLOYEE E
WHERE   NOT EXISTS (
            SELECT PNo
            FROM   PROJECT
            WHERE  DNo = 5
            AND    PNo NOT IN (
                       SELECT PNo
                       FROM   WORKS_ON W
                       WHERE  W.SSN = E.SSN
                   )
        );
```

**Explanation of logic (2 marks):**
- The outer `NOT EXISTS` ensures there is **no** project of dept 5 that the employee fails to work on.
- The inner `NOT IN` finds all projects of dept 5 the employee *does not* work on.
- If the inner returns any project, the `NOT EXISTS` is FALSE, and the employee is excluded.
- This is equivalent to a **set containment** test: $\text{WORKS\_ON}(e) \supseteq P_5$.

**Alternative solution using `EXCEPT` (alternative 3 marks):**
```sql
SELECT  E.EName
FROM    EMPLOYEE E
WHERE  (SELECT PNo FROM PROJECT WHERE DNo = 5)
        EXCEPT
       (SELECT PNo FROM WORKS_ON WHERE SSN = E.SSN)
       IS NULL;   -- empty set means employee works on all
```

**Key takeaway:** In KTU exams, the `NOT EXISTS` form is the **canonical** way to express universal quantification and is preferred by examiners.

### Part (b) — Department-wise Statistics Above Company Average *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Decompose the problem into sub-tasks (2 marks):**
1. Compute overall company average → scalar subquery.
2. Group by department → `GROUP BY DNo`.
3. Compute count and average per group → aggregate functions.
4. Filter groups where local average > company average → `HAVING`.
5. Sort descending → `ORDER BY`.

**Step 2 — Final SQL Query:**
```sql
SELECT   DNo,
         COUNT(*)    AS NumEmployees,
         AVG(Salary) AS AvgSalary
FROM     EMPLOYEE
GROUP BY DNo
HAVING   AVG(Salary) > (SELECT AVG(Salary) FROM EMPLOYEE)
ORDER BY AVG(Salary) DESC;
```

**Execution trace using the reference data (3 marks):**

Suppose EMPLOYEE has 5 rows with DNo and Salary as in §3.2:

| DNo | Salaries | Count | AVG | Overall AVG |
|-----|----------|-------|-----|-------------|
| 1   | 50, 70, 40 | 3 | 53.33 | 50 |
| 2   | 60, 80 | 2 | 70.00 | 50 |

- Inner subquery returns scalar $S_{overall} = 50$.
- Group DNo=1: $\text{AVG} = 53.33 > 50$ → **KEEP**.
- Group DNo=2: $\text{AVG} = 70 > 50$ → **KEEP**.
- Sort by AvgSalary DESC: DNo=2 first, then DNo=1.

**Final Output:**

| DNo | NumEmployees | AvgSalary |
|-----|--------------|-----------|
| 2   | 2            | 70000.00  |
| 1   | 3            | 53333.33  |

**Pitfall callout for part (b):**
> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistake:** Students often write `WHERE AVG(Salary) > ...`. This is a **syntax error** because `WHERE` cannot contain aggregates. Use `HAVING` for aggregate predicates. Examiners deduct **2 full marks** for this.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Consider the same schema as Question A.

**(a)** Write a SQL query to find the **second-highest salary** in the EMPLOYEE table **without** using `LIMIT` or `OFFSET`. Use a nested subquery approach. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Create a view named `DEPT_SUMMARY` that shows, for each department, the department name, manager name, number of employees, and total salary expenditure. Then write a query to **retrieve** all rows from this view for departments having more than 3 employees. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Second Highest Salary without LIMIT/OFFSET *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Logical reasoning (2 marks):**
The second-highest salary is the **maximum** salary that is **strictly less than** the maximum salary overall. Formally:

$$ \text{Salary}_2 = \max\{ s \in R.\text{Salary} : s < \max(R.\text{Salary}) \} $$

**Step 2 — Subquery construction (3 marks):**

Inner subquery S1 finds the **highest** salary:
```sql
SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM EMPLOYEE
```

Outer query finds the highest salary *less than* S1:
```sql
SELECT MAX(Salary) AS SecondHighest
FROM   EMPLOYEE
WHERE  Salary < (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM EMPLOYEE);
```

**Step 3 — Trace with example data (2 marks):**

Using salaries $\{50, 70, 40, 60, 80\}$:
- $S_1 = \max = 80$.
- Outer: $\max\{ s \in \{50, 70, 40, 60, 80\} : s < 80 \} = 70$.
- **Result:** `SecondHighest = 70000`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** `SELECT DISTINCT Salary FROM EMPLOYEE ORDER BY Salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1` is *forbidden* by the problem statement. KTU explicitly tests the subquery form to assess depth of understanding.

**Bonus — handling ties (additional 1 mark if mentioned):**
If two employees share the top salary, the above returns the *next* unique value correctly because of the strict `<` operator. The `DISTINCT` keyword is **not required** here.

### Part (b) — View Creation and Usage *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — View Definition (4 marks):**
```sql
CREATE VIEW DEPT_SUMMARY AS
SELECT   D.DName,
         M.EName       AS ManagerName,
         COUNT(E.SSN)  AS NumEmployees,
         SUM(E.Salary) AS TotalSalary
FROM     DEPARTMENT D
JOIN     EMPLOYEE   M  ON D.Mgr_SSN = M.SSN
LEFT JOIN EMPLOYEE   E  ON E.DNo    = D.DNo
GROUP BY D.DNo, D.DName, M.EName;
```

**Explanation of components (1 mark):**
- **Join 1 (`D JOIN M`)**: links department to its manager.
- **Join 2 (`LEFT JOIN E`)**: counts all employees in that department. `LEFT JOIN` is used so a department with *zero* employees still appears with `NumEmployees = 0`. If `INNER JOIN` is used, such departments vanish.
- **`GROUP BY`**: required because we use `COUNT` and `SUM` (aggregates).
- We group by `D.DNo` (PK of DEPARTMENT) plus the other non-aggregated columns.

**Step 2 — Query on the view (2 marks):**
```sql
SELECT *
FROM   DEPT_SUMMARY
WHERE  NumEmployees > 3;
```

**Trace (bonus 1 mark):** The DBMS expands the view:
```sql
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT D.DName, M.EName AS ManagerName, COUNT(E.SSN) AS NumEmployees, SUM(E.Salary) AS TotalSalary
    FROM   DEPARTMENT D
    JOIN   EMPLOYEE M ON D.Mgr_SSN = M.SSN
    LEFT JOIN EMPLOYEE E ON E.DNo = D.DNo
    GROUP BY D.DNo, D.DName, M.EName
) AS DEPT_SUMMARY
WHERE NumEmployees > 3;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **View Updatability Pitfall:** `DEPT_SUMMARY` is **NOT updatable** because it has aggregations, joins, and `GROUP BY`. Any attempt at `UPDATE DEPT_SUMMARY SET ...` will fail in standard SQL. KTU examiners may ask this as a follow-up: *"Is this view updatable? Justify."*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MISTAKES THAT COST KTU STUDENTS FULL MARKS**
> 1. **Using aggregate functions in `WHERE`** — always use `HAVING`. *(−2 marks per occurrence)*
> 2. **Forgetting correlation variable aliasing** in correlated subqueries (writing `EMPLOYEE` instead of `E1`/`E2`). *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Using `LIMIT` where the question demands subquery logic** — examiners test SQL depth, not just result correctness. *(−2 marks)*
> 4. **Not enclosing view names in proper `CREATE VIEW` syntax** with `AS` keyword. *(−1 mark)*
> 5. **Mixing up `ANY` and `ALL` semantics** — `> ANY` means "greater than at least one", `> ALL` means "greater than every". *(−1.5 marks)*
> 6. **Aliasing conflict in self-joins/correlated subqueries** — forgetting that the inner query cannot reference the outer table without an alias. *(−1 mark)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 2: SQL Queries**

### A. Nested Queries
- ☐ A **subquery** is a `SELECT` inside another SQL statement.
- ☐ **Non-correlated**: inner query is independent, runs **once**; uses `IN`, scalar comparison, `ANY`, `ALL`.
- ☐ **Correlated**: inner query references outer columns, runs **once per outer row**; uses `EXISTS`, correlated scalar comparison.
- ☐ **`EXISTS` returns TRUE** if subquery produces **at least one row**; **does not** care about values.
- ☐ **`NOT EXISTS`** is the SQL idiom for **universal quantification** ("for all").
- ☐ `x > ANY(S)` ≡ $\exists y \in S : x > y$.
- ☐ `x > ALL(S)` ≡ $\forall y \in S : x > y$.
- ☐ The `IN` operator with a subquery is logically equivalent to `= ANY`.

### B. Aggregation and Grouping
- ☐ Five standard aggregate functions: `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`.
- ☐ `NULL`s are **ignored** by all aggregates **except** `COUNT(*)`.
- ☐ The **phase order** is: `FROM` → `WHERE` → `GROUP BY` → `HAVING` → `SELECT` → `DISTINCT` → `ORDER BY` → `LIMIT`.
- ☐ **`WHERE` cannot contain aggregates** — use `HAVING` for group-level filters.
- ☐ Every column in `SELECT` that is **not** an aggregate must appear in `GROUP BY` (with some DBMS extensions, this is relaxed; standard SQL forbids it).
- ☐ `HAVING` is **optional**; if omitted, all groups pass through.
- ☐ `COUNT(*)` counts rows; `COUNT(col)` counts non-NULL values in `col`.

### C. Views
- ☐ A **view** is a **virtual table** defined by a `SELECT` query.
- ☐ Views **do not store data**; they store a **query definition** in the data dictionary.
- ☐ The DBMS uses **query modification** to inline view definitions on access.
- ☐ **Updatable views** must involve exactly **one** base table, no `DISTINCT`, no `GROUP BY`, no aggregates, no `UNION`.
- ☐ **`WITH CHECK OPTION`** prevents updates that would cause rows to *leave* the view.
- ☐ Views provide: (1) **security** (column/row-level access), (2) **simplicity** (hide complex joins), (3) **logical data independence**.

### D. Multi-Table Queries
- ☐ Always declare **table aliases** when joining — they reduce ambiguity and improve readability.
- ☐ The **join graph** is determined by **foreign keys**: `EMPLOYEE.DNo → DEPARTMENT.DNo`, `WORKS_ON.SSN → EMPLOYEE.SSN`, etc.
- ☐ Prefer `INNER JOIN` when unmatched rows are not needed; use `LEFT/RIGHT/FULL OUTER JOIN` when they are.
- ☐ `NATURAL JOIN` is convenient but **risky** — column-name conflicts can silently change results.

### E. KTU Exam Quick-Recall SQL Snippets

| Need | Pattern |
|---|---|
| Top-N per group | `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...)` |
| Find duplicates | `SELECT col FROM t GROUP BY col HAVING COUNT(*) > 1` |
| Second highest | `SELECT MAX(col) FROM t WHERE col < (SELECT MAX(col) FROM t)` |
| "Works on all" | Outer `NOT EXISTS` + inner `NOT IN` / `EXCEPT` |
| Above-average filter | `HAVING AVG(col) > (SELECT AVG(col) FROM t)` |

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to *"convert a subquery to a join"* or vice-versa, always show the **logical equivalence** in relational algebra notation. This demonstrates depth and earns full marks consistently across KTU 2024 Scheme valuation schemes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DB Design Anomalies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DB Design Anomalies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** curriculum for *Database Management Systems*, a **Database Design Anomaly** is formally defined as an *unintended, undesirable side-effect* that occurs during **Insertion, Deletion, or Modification (Update)** operations on a **poorly normalized relational schema**. These anomalies arise primarily due to the presence of **redundant data** within a single, large, un-normalized relation, where attributes that are not functionally dependent on the primary key are forced to coexist in the same table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"A design anomaly is a symptom of a bad relational schema — one that exhibits data redundancy to such a degree that Insertion, Deletion, and Update operations on the relation produce logically inconsistent or semantically meaningless results."*

The three classical categories of anomalies recognized in the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Insertion Anomaly** — inability to insert a new tuple due to missing/partial information.
2. **Deletion Anomaly** — loss of unintended data when deleting a single tuple.
3. **Update (Modification) Anomaly** — inconsistency caused by partial updates of duplicated data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Single Filing Cabinet" Problem

Imagine a small college that maintains **one giant paper register** for *everything* related to a student: their personal details, their enrolled department, the head of that department, the hostel they live in, and the warden of that hostel — all stuffed into **one single A4 sheet per student**.

- When a **new department** is created (say, *Biotechnology*), but no student has been admitted yet — you **cannot record** the department in the register because there is no student row to attach it to. That is an **Insertion Anomaly**.
- When a student **drops out** and you tear out their sheet, you *also* lose the fact that *Dr. X* is the Head of the *Biotechnology* Department (because that information lived only on that student's sheet). That is a **Deletion Anomaly**.
- When *Dr. X* resigns and is replaced by *Dr. Y*, you must update **every single sheet** belonging to a Biotechnology student. If you miss even one sheet, the database now claims the department has *two heads simultaneously*. That is an **Update Anomaly**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fix:** Split the giant filing cabinet into smaller, **logically connected** cabinets (one for Students, one for Departments, one for Hostels). This splitting process is called **Normalization**.

## 1.3 Why KTU Tests This Concept

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (CO), this topic directly maps to:

| CO Code | Course Outcome | Bloom's Level |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| **CO3** | Design normalized relational databases up to a suitable normal form | Apply / Analyze |
| **CO4** | Identify and eliminate update anomalies through functional dependency analysis | Analyze |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Root Cause of Anomalies — The Anatomy of a "Bad" Schema

A relation exhibits anomalies when it violates the principles of a **well-formed relational schema**. The KTU syllabus attributes this to two interrelated root causes:

1. **Data Redundancy** — the same fact (e.g., department name, department head) is stored multiple times across many tuples.
2. **Mixed Functional Dependencies (FDs)** — the relation contains two or more semantically independent entities (e.g., *Student* and *Department*) mashed into a single table, causing FDs of unrelated entities to coexist.

A relation is considered **anomaly-prone** when it is in **Un-normalized Form (UNF)** or **First Normal Form (1NF)** but fails to satisfy the constraints of **Second Normal Form (2NF)** or higher.

## 2.2 The Three Anomaly Types — Detailed Breakdown

### 🔴 Type 1: Insertion Anomaly

- **Operational Definition:** Occurs when a new fact **cannot be inserted into the database** because the primary key value (or some other NOT NULL attribute) is not yet known.
- **Why it happens:** Two distinct real-world entities share a single composite primary key. To record information about Entity A, you must already possess information about Entity B.
- **Example scenario:** A `STUDENT_DEPT` table with attributes `(S_ID, S_NAME, DEPT_ID, DEPT_NAME, DEPT_HEAD)`. If the university wants to create a new department *"Data Science"* before admitting any students, the tuple cannot be inserted because `S_ID` (part of the primary key) is `NULL`.

### 🔴 Type 2: Deletion Anomaly

- **Operational Definition:** Occurs when deleting a single row of data **inadvertently removes other unrelated information** that happened to be stored on that row.
- **Why it happens:** Information about a secondary entity is stored *only* because the primary entity exists. Once the primary entity row is removed, the secondary entity information vanishes.
- **Example scenario:** If the *only* student of the *Mechanical* department graduates and their tuple is deleted, the entire record of the Mechanical department (including its Head of Department) is lost forever.

### 🔴 Type 3: Update (Modification) Anomaly

- **Operational Definition:** Occurs when a logically single-valued fact is **stored in multiple rows**, and updating that fact requires changing *every* row; missing one row creates **data inconsistency**.
- **Why it happens:** Redundant copies of the same attribute value exist across many tuples.
- **Example scenario:** If the Head of the CSE department is stored in 200 student tuples and changes from "Dr. A" to "Dr. B", all 200 tuples must be updated atomically. If only 199 are updated, the database now claims CSE has *two* heads simultaneously.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Property | Mathematical Notation | Boundary Condition / Property | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Functional Dependency | $X \rightarrow Y$ | For a given $X$, $Y$ is uniquely determined | Foundation for detecting redundancy |
| 2 | Full Functional Dependency | $X \xrightarrow{f} Y$ | $Y$ depends on the **whole** of $X$, not on any proper subset | Used to test for **2NF** |
| 3 | Partial Dependency | $X \rightarrow A$ where a proper subset $X' \subset X$ and $X' \rightarrow A$ | A non-prime attribute depends on only part of a composite key | **Direct cause** of anomalies |
| 4 | Transitive Dependency | $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow Z$, therefore $X \rightarrow Z$ | $Y$ must NOT be a candidate key and $X$ must NOT be a superkey | **Direct cause** of anomalies in 3NF |
| 5 | Lossless Join Decomposition | $R_1 \bowtie R_2 = R$ | $\bigl(R_1 \cap R_2\bigr) \rightarrow R_1$ **OR** $\bigl(R_1 \cap R_2\bigr) \rightarrow R_2$ | Guarantees no spurious tuples |
| 6 | Dependency Preservation | $\bigl(F^+ \bigr)_{\pi_{R_1}} \cup \bigl(F^+\bigr)_{\pi_{R_2}} = F^+$ | Local FDs must collectively imply all original FDs | Avoids expensive global joins for constraint checking |
| 7 | Attribute Closure | $\{X\}^+$ | Set of all attributes functionally determined by $X$ | Used to compute candidate keys |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In the ESE (End Semester Exam), questions on anomalies almost always carry a **small relational schema (3–5 attributes)** as a case study. Your answer must (i) **identify** the anomaly, (ii) **explain why** it occurs (cite the violated FD), and (iii) **propose** the decomposed schema.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Database design anomalies are not merely textbook curiosities. In production-grade engineering systems:

- **Banking Systems:** A poorly designed *Customer-Account-Loan* table could allow a customer to be "deleted" while still owing money — a *deletion anomaly* with **catastrophic financial consequences**.
- **E-Commerce Platforms:** Storing product category information redundantly in every order row creates massive *update anomalies* during seasonal re-categorization.
- **Healthcare Information Systems:** An *insertion anomaly* in a *Patient-Doctor-Ward* schema could prevent a newly appointed doctor from being recorded until their first patient is admitted — a **compliance violation** under medical data regulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Case Study — The `EMP_DEPT` Schema

Let us walk through a **canonical KTU-style case study** used in board examinations.

### Original (Un-normalized) Schema

$$
R = \{\text{EID},\ \text{ENAME},\ \text{DEPT\_ID},\ \text{DEPT\_NAME},\ \text{DEPT\_LOC},\ \text{DEPT\_HEAD}\}
$$

$$
\text{Primary Key} = \text{EID}
$$

### Functional Dependencies (FDs) Identified

$$
\text{FD}_1:\ \text{EID} \rightarrow \text{ENAME},\ \text{DEPT\_ID}
$$

$$
\text{FD}_2:\ \text{DEPT\_ID} \rightarrow \text{DEPT\_NAME},\ \text{DEPT\_LOC},\ \text{DEPT\_HEAD}
$$

### Sample Data Instance (to visualize redundancy)

| EID | ENAME | DEPT_ID | DEPT_NAME | DEPT_LOC | DEPT_HEAD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| E01 | Anu | D01 | CSE | Block-A | Dr. X |
| E02 | Binu | D01 | CSE | Block-A | Dr. X |
| E03 | Chitra | D02 | ECE | Block-B | Dr. Y |
| E04 | Deepa | D01 | CSE | Block-A | Dr. X |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Anomaly Identification

### Step 1 — Detect the Transitive Dependency

Observe the chain of FDs:

$$
\text{EID} \rightarrow \text{DEPT\_ID} \rightarrow \{\text{DEPT\_NAME},\ \text{DEPT\_LOC},\ \text{DEPT\_HEAD}\}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{EID} \rightarrow \text{DEPT\_HEAD} \quad (\text{transitively, via DEPT\_ID})
$$

This means non-prime attributes (`DEPT_NAME`, `DEPT_LOC`, `DEPT_HEAD`) are **not directly dependent** on the primary key `EID`. They depend on `DEPT_ID` instead. **This is a transitive dependency — a violation of 3NF and BCNF.**

---

### Step 2 — Insertion Anomaly Demonstration

**Operation:** *"Create a new department D03 (IT) with Dr. Z as its head."*

**SQL Attempt:**
```sql
INSERT INTO EMP_DEPT (EID, DEPT_ID, DEPT_NAME, DEPT_HEAD)
VALUES (NULL, 'D03', 'IT', 'Dr. Z');
```

**Result:** ❌ **Operation rejected** — `EID` is the primary key and cannot be `NULL`.

**Diagnosis:** Two semantically independent entities (*Employee* and *Department*) are fused. To record a department, an employee must exist.

---

### Step 3 — Deletion Anomaly Demonstration

**Operation:** *"Employee E04 (Deepa) leaves the organization."*

**SQL Attempt:**
```sql
DELETE FROM EMP_DEPT WHERE EID = 'E04';
```

**Result:** ❌ **Intentional delete causes data loss** — Although no other employee is in DEPT_ID D02, the *entire information about ECE department* (its name, location, and head Dr. Y) is now **permanently erased** from the database.

---

### Step 4 — Update Anomaly Demonstration

**Operation:** *"Dr. X is replaced by Dr. W as the CSE department head."*

**Required SQL:**
```sql
UPDATE EMP_DEPT SET DEPT_HEAD = 'Dr. W' WHERE DEPT_ID = 'D01';
```

**Failure Mode:** If the `WHERE` clause is mistyped (e.g., `WHERE DEPT_NAME = 'CSE'` instead of `WHERE DEPT_ID = 'D01'`), or if the transaction crashes mid-update, the database enters an **inconsistent state** where some CSE tuples still reference *Dr. X* while others reference *Dr. W*. There is now **no single source of truth**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping FDs to Anomalies

| FD Pattern | Violated Normal Form | Anomaly Type Triggered | Decomposition Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| $X \rightarrow A$ where $X \subsetneq \text{PK}$ (proper subset) | **2NF** | Insertion + Deletion + Update | Decompose on partial dependencies |
| $X \rightarrow Y \rightarrow Z$ where $Y$ is non-prime | **3NF** | Insertion + Deletion + Update | Decompose on transitive dependencies |
| $X \rightarrow Y$ where $X$ is not a superkey and $Y$ is prime | **BCNF (3.5NF)** | Update (mostly) | Decompose on overlapping candidate keys |
| Multi-valued dependency $X \twoheadrightarrow Y$ | **4NF** | Update + Insertion | Decompose on independent multi-valued facts |

---

## 3.4 The Decomposition — Anomaly Elimination Proof

### Decomposed Schema

$$
R_1 = \{\text{EID},\ \text{ENAME},\ \text{DEPT\_ID}\} \quad (\text{Employee information})
$$

$$
R_2 = \{\text{DEPT\_ID},\ \text{DEPT\_NAME},\ \text{DEPT\_LOC},\ \text{DEPT\_HEAD}\} \quad (\text{Department information})
$$

### Verification 1 — Lossless Join

The common attribute is `DEPT_ID`. Since:

$$
\text{DEPT\_ID} \rightarrow \{\text{DEPT\_NAME},\ \text{DEPT\_LOC},\ \text{DEPT\_HEAD}\}
$$

`DEPT_ID` functionally determines all attributes of $R_2$. Therefore:

$$
\bigl(R_1 \cap R_2\bigr) \rightarrow R_2 \quad \checkmark
$$

The decomposition satisfies the **lossless join property** — no spurious tuples will be generated upon natural join.

### Verification 2 — Dependency Preservation

- $\text{FD}_1$ (`EID → ENAME, DEPT_ID`) is preserved in $R_1$.
- $\text{FD}_2$ (`DEPT_ID → DEPT_NAME, DEPT_LOC, DEPT_HEAD`) is preserved in $R_2$.

Both FDs can be checked **locally** without rejoining. The decomposition is **dependency-preserving**. ✓

### Anomaly Re-check on Decomposed Schema

| Operation | Before Decomposition | After Decomposition |
|---|---|---|
| Insert new department D03 | ❌ Cannot insert (EID NULL) | ✅ Insert into $R_2$ only |
| Delete employee E04 | ❌ Loses ECE dept info | ✅ Deletes only from $R_1$; $R_2$ intact |
| Update CSE head | ❌ Risk of inconsistency | ✅ Single row update in $R_2$ |

---

## 3.5 Closure Computation Example (Frequently Asked in KTU)

**Question:** Given $F = \{A \rightarrow B,\ B \rightarrow C,\ C \rightarrow D\}$, compute $\{A\}^+$.

**Solution:**

$$
\{A\}^+ = \{A\}
$$

Using $A \rightarrow B$, add $B$:

$$
\{A\}^+ = \{A, B\}
$$

Using $B \rightarrow C$, add $C$:

$$
\{A\}^+ = \{A, B, C\}
$$

Using $C \rightarrow D$, add $D$:

$$
\{A\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D\}
$$

No more FDs can be applied. Therefore, $A$ is a **candidate key** of the relation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Anomaly Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Relational Schema R] --> stepB[Identify Primary Key PK]
    stepB --> stepC[List All Attributes of R]
    stepC --> stepD{Group Attributes by FD Dependencies}
    stepD --> stepE1[Group 1: Attributes Dependent on PK directly]
    stepD --> stepE2[Group 2: Attributes Dependent on Non-Key attribute]
    stepD --> stepE3[Group 3: Attributes Dependent on Partial Key]
    stepE1 --> stepF[Analyze Each Group for Anomalies]
    stepE2 --> stepF
    stepE3 --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG1{Insertion Anomaly?}
    stepF --> stepG2{Deletion Anomaly?}
    stepF --> stepG3{Update Anomaly?}
    stepG1 -->|YES| stepH[Decompose R into R1 and R2]
    stepG2 -->|YES| stepH
    stepG3 -->|YES| stepH
    stepH --> stepI[Verify Lossless Join]
    stepH --> stepJ[Verify Dependency Preservation]
    stepI --> stepK[Final Normalized Schema]
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepG1 -->|NO| stepL[Schema is Anomaly-Free]
    stepG2 -->|NO| stepL
    stepG3 -->|NO| stepL
```

---

## 4.2 Anomaly Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    root1[Database Design Anomalies] --> n1[Insertion Anomaly]
    root1 --> n2[Deletion Anomaly]
    root1 --> n3[Update Anomaly]
    n1 --> n1a[Cause: Missing PK value for new entity]
    n1 --> n1b[Cause: Mixed entities in single relation]
    n2 --> n2a[Cause: Secondary info stored only on primary entity row]
    n2 --> n2b[Cause: Deleting one fact removes another]
    n3 --> n3a[Cause: Redundant copies of same fact]
    n3 --> n3b[Cause: Partial update leaves inconsistency]
    n1a --> fix1[Decompose Relation]
    n1b --> fix1
    n2a --> fix1
    n2b --> fix1
    n3a --> fix1
    n3b --> fix1
    fix1 --> norm[Apply Normalization 1NF 2NF 3NF BCNF]
```

---

## 4.3 Before-and-After Schema Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE[Before Decomposition BAD Design]
        R0[EMP_DEPT Table]:::bad
    end
    R0 --> a1[EID PK]
    R0 --> a2[ENAME]
    R0 --> a3[DEPT_ID]
    R0 --> a4[DEPT_NAME]
    R0 --> a5[DEPT_LOC]
    R0 --> a6[DEPT_HEAD]
    BEFORE --> ANOM[Anomalies Exist]
    ANOM --> an1[Insertion]
    ANOM --> an2[Deletion]
    ANOM --> an3[Update]

    subgraph AFTER[After Decomposition GOOD Design]
        R1[EMPLOYEE Table]:::good
        R2[DEPARTMENT Table]:::good
    end
    R1 --> b1[EID PK]
    R1 --> b2[ENAME]
    R1 --> b3[DEPT_ID FK]
    R2 --> b4[DEPT_ID PK]
    R2 --> b5[DEPT_NAME]
    R2 --> b6[DEPT_LOC]
    R2 --> b7[DEPT_HEAD]
    AFTER --> NORM[Anomaly-Free]
    NORM --> nf1[Lossless Join]
    NORM --> nf2[Dependency Preserved]

    classDef bad fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#CC0000,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#CCFFCC,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Dependency Chain Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pk[EID Primary Key]:::key --> empname[ENAME]
    pk --> deptid[DEPT_ID]:::fkey
    deptid --> deptname[DEPT_NAME]
    deptid --> deptloc[DEPT_LOC]
    deptid --> depthead[DEPT_HEAD]
    pk -.Transitive Dependency.-> depthead
    classDef key fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef fkey fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted arrow from `EID` to `DEPT_HEAD` represents a **transitive dependency** — the root cause of all three anomaly types in this schema.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the three types of database design anomalies. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Database design anomalies are unintended side-effects that occur during DML operations on poorly normalized schemas:

1. **Insertion Anomaly:** A new fact cannot be inserted because some other required attribute (typically the primary key) is unknown. *Example:* Cannot insert a new department in `EMP_DEPT(EID, ENAME, DEPT_ID, DEPT_NAME)` if no employee exists for it, because `EID` (PK) would be `NULL`.

2. **Deletion Anomaly:** Deleting a row inadvertently removes other unrelated information. *Example:* Deleting the only employee of a department removes the entire department record.

3. **Update Anomaly:** A logically single-valued fact exists in multiple redundant rows; updating only some creates inconsistency. *Example:* Changing the Head of Department requires updating every employee tuple in that department; missing one row causes dual-head inconsistency.

*(Award 1 mark per anomaly definition + example.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of functional dependencies in identifying design anomalies?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Functional dependencies (FDs) act as the **diagnostic tool** for detecting anomalies. The presence of certain FD patterns signals an anomaly-prone schema:

- **Partial Dependencies** ($\text{proper subset of PK} \rightarrow \text{non-prime attribute}$) indicate **2NF violations** and lead to all three anomaly types.
- **Transitive Dependencies** ($\text{PK} \rightarrow X \rightarrow Y$, where $X$ is non-prime) indicate **3NF violations** and cause redundancy-driven update anomalies.
- **Non-superkey determinants** indicate **BCNF violations**.

By computing the **attribute closure** $\{X\}^+$ and identifying these patterns, a database designer can systematically detect and decompose relations to eliminate anomalies.

*(Award 1 mark for FD definition, 1 mark for anomaly-causing patterns, 1 mark for closure-based detection.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Consider the relation $R(A, B, C, D, E, F)$ with functional dependencies:**
$F = \{A \rightarrow B,\ C \rightarrow D,\ E \rightarrow F,\ AC \rightarrow E\}$

#### **Part (a)** [7 Marks]
**Identify the primary key of $R$ and determine which normal form (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF) the relation violates.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the candidate key(s) using attribute closure.**

Try $\{A, C\}^+$:
- Start: $\{A, C\}$
- Apply $A \rightarrow B$: add $B$ → $\{A, B, C\}$
- Apply $C \rightarrow D$: add $D$ → $\{A, B, C, D\}$
- Apply $AC \rightarrow E$: add $E$ → $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$
- Apply $E \rightarrow F$: add $F$ → $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ ✓

Therefore:

$$
\{A, C\}^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} = R
$$

So $(A, C)$ is a **candidate key** and hence the **primary key**.

**Step 2 — Check for partial dependency (2NF test).**

Since the primary key is composite $(A, C)$, check whether any non-prime attribute depends on only a part of the key:

- $A \rightarrow B$ — partial dependency on $A$ alone ❌
- $C \rightarrow D$ — partial dependency on $C$ alone ❌

**Conclusion:** The relation violates **2NF** (and consequently 3NF and BCNF).

*(Award 2 marks for key computation, 3 marks for 2NF violation identification, 2 marks for stating consequences.)*

---

#### **Part (b)** [7 Marks]
**Decompose $R$ into BCNF and verify that the decomposition is lossless and dependency-preserving.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Decompose based on the violating FDs.**

From $A \rightarrow B$:
$$
R_1 = (A, B) \quad \text{with key } A
$$

From $C \rightarrow D$:
$$
R_2 = (C, D) \quad \text{with key } C
$$

The remaining attributes, along with the keys $A, C$ and the dependent $E, F$, give:
$$
R_3 = (A, C, E, F) \quad \text{with FD } AC \rightarrow E \text{ and } E \rightarrow F
$$

Further decompose $R_3$ since $E \rightarrow F$ violates BCNF (as $E$ is not a superkey of $R_3$):
$$
R_4 = (E, F) \quad \text{with key } E
$$

Final decomposed schema:
$$
R_1 = (A, B), \quad R_2 = (C, D), \quad R_3 = (A, C, E), \quad R_4 = (E, F)
$$

**Step 2 — Lossless Join Verification.**

- $(R_1 \cap R_3) = \{A\}$; since $A \rightarrow R_1$ ✓ lossless
- $(R_2 \cap R_3) = \{C\}$; since $C \rightarrow R_2$ ✓ lossless
- $(R_3 \cap R_4) = \{E\}$; since $E \rightarrow R_4$ ✓ lossless

All pairwise intersections preserve losslessness. ✓

**Step 3 — Dependency Preservation.**

- $A \rightarrow B$ preserved in $R_1$ ✓
- $C \rightarrow D$ preserved in $R_2$ ✓
- $AC \rightarrow E$ preserved in $R_3$ ✓
- $E \rightarrow F$ preserved in $R_4$ ✓

All original FDs are locally enforced. ✓

**Conclusion:** The decomposition is in **BCNF**, **lossless**, and **dependency-preserving**.

*(Award 3 marks for decomposition steps, 2 marks for lossless proof, 2 marks for dependency preservation proof.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A university maintains the following relation:**
`STUDENT_COURSE(SID, SNAME, CID, CNAME, INAME, GRADE)`

**with FDs:** $\text{SID} \rightarrow \text{SNAME}$; $\text{CID} \rightarrow \text{CNAME, INAME}$; $\text{SID, CID} \rightarrow \text{GRADE}$.

#### **Part (a)** [7 Marks]
**Demonstrate, with specific operations, how each of the three anomalies can occur in this relation.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the schema and FDs.**

Primary Key: $(SID, CID)$

FDs:
- $\text{SID} \rightarrow \text{SNAME}$ (partial — 2NF violation)
- $\text{CID} \rightarrow \{\text{CNAME}, \text{INAME}\}$ (partial — 2NF violation)
- $\text{SID, CID} \rightarrow \text{GRADE}$ (full — well-formed)

**Step 2 — Demonstrate Insertion Anomaly.**

> *"A new course 'DBMS-501' is created with instructor 'Prof. K', but no student has enrolled yet."*

The tuple would require $\text{SID} = \text{NULL}$, which violates the PK constraint. Hence, course information **cannot be stored** until a student enrolls.

**Step 3 — Demonstrate Deletion Anomaly.**

> *"The last student of course 'CS-101' drops the course."*

Deleting that student's tuple **removes all information** about course CS-101 — its name and instructor Prof. M are lost forever, even though the course still exists.

**Step 4 — Demonstrate Update Anomaly.**

> *"Prof. M is replaced by Prof. N as the instructor of CS-101."*

Since `INAME` is stored in every tuple of every student enrolled in CS-101 (say, 50 students), **all 50 rows** must be updated. A partial update creates inconsistent data: some tuples show Prof. M, others show Prof. N.

*(Award ~2 marks per anomaly: 1 for operation description, 1 for impact explanation.)*

---

#### **Part (b)** [7 Marks]
**Decompose the relation into 3NF. Show that your decomposition preserves dependencies and is lossless.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construct a Minimal Cover.**

The given FDs are already minimal:
- $\text{SID} \rightarrow \text{SNAME}$
- $\text{CID} \rightarrow \text{CNAME}$
- $\text{CID} \rightarrow \text{INAME}$
- $\text{SID, CID} \rightarrow \text{GRADE}$

**Step 2 — 3NF Decomposition Algorithm.**

Step 2a: For each FD, create a relation:
- $R_a = (\text{SID, SNAME})$
- $R_b = (\text{CID, CNAME, INAME})$
- $R_c = (\text{SID, CID, GRADE})$

Step 2b: Since the candidate key $(\text{SID, CID})$ is not contained in any $R_a, R_b, R_c$, add a relation for the key:
- $R_d = (\text{SID, CID})$

But $R_c$ already contains the key, so $R_d$ is redundant.

**Final 3NF Schema:**
$$
R_1 = (\text{SID, SNAME})
$$

$$
R_2 = (\text{CID, CNAME, INAME})
$$

$$
R_3 = (\text{SID, CID, GRADE})
$$

**Step 3 — Lossless Join Verification.**

- $(R_1 \cap R_3) = \{\text{SID}\}$; $\text{SID} \rightarrow R_1$ ✓
- $(R_2 \cap R_3) = \{\text{CID}\}$; $\text{CID} \rightarrow R_2$ ✓
- Cascade lossless on $R_3$ ✓

**Step 4 — Dependency Preservation.**

Each FD is enforced entirely within one relation. ✓

*(Award 2 marks minimal cover, 3 marks decomposition, 1 mark lossless, 1 mark dependency preservation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Normal Form terminology:** Students often write *"the relation is in 3NF"* when it actually violates **2NF**. Remember: **2NF violation ⇒ 3NF violation ⇒ BCNF violation** (the hierarchy is cumulative).
>
> 2. **Skipping the lossless join proof:** KTU examiners allocate **2–3 dedicated marks** for proving losslessness using the intersection rule. Writing *"the decomposition is lossless"* without showing $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1$ or $R_2$ **invariably loses marks**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting dependency preservation:** A decomposition can be lossless yet still violate dependency preservation. Always verify $F^+ = \pi_{R_1}(F^+) \cup \pi_{R_2}(F^+) \cup \dots$
>
> 4. **Misidentifying the candidate key:** The primary key must be derived via **attribute closure computation**, not by intuition. Always show the iterative steps.
>
> 5. **Treating all FDs as violations:** Only FDs where the **determinant is NOT a superkey** (and the dependent is non-prime for 3NF) constitute violations. Prime attributes dependences may be acceptable in 3NF.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Anomaly Definition:** Unintended side-effects of DML operations caused by data redundancy in un-normalized or partially normalized schemas.
- ✅ **Three Types:** **Insertion** (cannot add new fact), **Deletion** (lose unrelated data), **Update** (inconsistency from redundant copies).
- ✅ **Root Cause:** Mixing semantically independent entities in a single relation, leading to **partial** or **transitive** dependencies on the primary key.
- ✅ **FD as Diagnostic Tool:** Use FDs to formally prove the existence of anomalies via attribute closure.
- ✅ **2NF Violation** ↔ Partial Dependency ↔ Composite Key with subset-dependent non-prime attributes.
- ✅ **3NF Violation** ↔ Transitive Dependency ↔ Non-prime attribute determining another non-prime attribute.
- ✅ **BCNF Violation** ↔ Every determinant must be a superkey; prime-attribute dependencies may still violate BCNF.
- ✅ **Lossless Join Test:** $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1$ **OR** $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_2$.
- ✅ **Dependency Preservation:** All original FDs must be enforceable within a single decomposed relation.
- ✅ **Decomposition Hierarchy:** $1NF \subset 2NF \subset 3NF \subset BCNF \subset 4NF \subset 5NF$.
- ✅ **KTU Frequency:** This topic appears in **ESE Part A (3 marks)** and frequently as a **14-mark Part B** with internal choice every semester.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for Anomalies:** **"ICU"** — **I**nsertion, **C**onstraint-violation (deletion), **U**pdate inconsistency.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Dependencies, Armstrong's Axioms
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Functional Dependencies, Armstrong's Axioms

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Functional Dependency (FD) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** *Functional dependency* is the cornerstone of **relational database design**. It is the formal mathematical vehicle used to express *business rules* as constraints on attribute values, and forms the theoretical backbone for **Normalisation**, **Lossless-Join Decomposition**, and **Dependency Preservation** in the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 syllabus.

A **functional dependency**, denoted $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, is a constraint between two sets of attributes $\alpha$ and $\beta$ of a relation schema $R$. It specifies that for any two tuples $t_1$ and $t_2$ in any valid relation state $r$ of $R$:

$$
\text{If } t_1[\alpha] = t_2[\alpha], \text{ then } t_1[\beta] = t_2[\beta]
$$

In plain English: *the value of attribute set $\beta$ is uniquely determined by the value of attribute set $\alpha$*. We say that $\alpha$ **functionally determines** $\beta$.

**Formal Notation:**

$$ \alpha \;\rightarrow\; \beta \quad \text{(alpha functionally determines beta)} $$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "University Roll Number" Intuition

Imagine a KTU university registration system. Each student has a unique **University Register Number (URN)**, say `KER2024CS0042`. From this single number, the university can look up:

- Student Name
- Branch (e.g., CSE)
- Semester
- Section
- Total Credits Registered

So, for the relation `STUDENT(URN, Name, Branch, Sem, Section, Credits)`, we can confidently write:

$$ \text{URN} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Name, Branch, Sem, Section, Credits} $$

Two different students **cannot** share the same URN, so the URN "decides" all other attributes. Conversely, knowing only the `Branch` does **not** determine the `Name`, because many students share the same branch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Board Exams:** A functional dependency is a *property of the relational schema's semantics* — it describes the *meaning* of the data, **not** the current instance. A relation may *satisfy* an FD today and *violate* it tomorrow if the business rule changes. Therefore, FDs are deduced from the *mini-world* (application domain), not from sample data alone.

### 1.3 Terminology — The FDs Vocabulary Sheet

| Term | Meaning | Example (Student Relation) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Determinant** | The LHS attribute set ($\alpha$) | `URN` is the determinant |
| **Dependent** | The RHS attribute set ($\beta$) | `Name` depends on `URN` |
| **Trivial FD** | An FD where $\beta \subseteq \alpha$ | `{URN, Name} → Name` |
| **Non-Trivial FD** | An FD where $\beta \not\subseteq \alpha$ | `URN → Name` |
| **Completely Non-Trivial FD** | $\alpha \cap \beta = \emptyset$ | `URN → Name` |
| **Singleton RHS** | FD with a single attribute on RHS | `URN → Name` |
| **Canonical Form** | Every FD has singleton RHS | `URN → Name; URN → Branch` |
| **Superkey** | $\alpha \rightarrow$ all attributes of $R$ | `URN → {URN, Name, Branch,...}` |

### 1.4 Types of Functional Dependencies (KTU High-Priority Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners frequently test the *types* of FDs. Memorise the table below — it has appeared in **Module-3** questions across multiple KTU University Exam cycles.

**1. Trivial Functional Dependency**
An FD $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ is called **trivial** if the dependent attributes are a subset of the determinant:
$$ \beta \;\subseteq\; \alpha $$
Example: `{StudentID, CourseID} → StudentID` — trivially true.

**2. Non-Trivial Functional Dependency**
An FD is **non-trivial** if at least one attribute in $\beta$ is **not** in $\alpha$:
$$ \beta \;\not\subseteq\; \alpha $$
Example: `{StudentID} → Name` — the `Name` is not part of the determinant.

**3. Completely Non-Trivial Functional Dependency**
A non-trivial FD where the determinant and dependent sets are **disjoint**:
$$ \alpha \;\cap\; \beta \;=\; \emptyset $$
Example: `{StudentID} → Name`.

**4. Multivalued Dependency (MVD)** — *advanced topic, covered in Module 4.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need a Formal Inference System?

A typical real-world database has **hundreds of business rules**, each expressible as an FD. From a small set of explicitly given FDs (called **F**, the *set of FDs*), we can often **derive many more** FDs that *must* also hold. 

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Question:** *"Given a set of FDs $F$, what is the complete set of FDs that logically follow from $F$?"* — The answer is the **closure** of $F$, written $F^+$.

To compute $F^+$ mechanically and prove correctness, we need **sound** (every derived FD is logically valid) and **complete** (every logically valid FD can be derived) inference rules. This is exactly what **Armstrong's Axioms** provide.

## 2.2 Armstrong's Axioms — The Three Primitive Rules (1974)

William W. Armstrong formulated three foundational inference rules in his seminal 1974 paper. Every other FD rule can be derived from these three.

### Axiom 1 — **Reflexivity** (IR1)
If $\beta \subseteq \alpha$, then $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ always holds.
$$ \text{If } \beta \;\subseteq\; \alpha, \text{ then } \alpha \;\rightarrow\; \beta $$

> **Intuition:** If I already *know* your complete address, I automatically know your street name (because the street is part of the address). This is a *trivial* FD — true for *every* relation.

### Axiom 2 — **Augmentation** (IR2)
If $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ holds, then $\alpha \gamma \rightarrow \beta \gamma$ also holds, for any attribute set $\gamma$.
$$ \alpha \;\rightarrow\; \beta \;\Longrightarrow\; \alpha\gamma \;\rightarrow\; \beta\gamma $$

> **Intuition:** Adding the *same* extra information to both sides preserves the functional relationship. If `URN → Name`, then `{URN, Branch} → {Name, Branch}` must also hold.

### Axiom 3 — **Transitivity** (IR3)
If $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\beta \rightarrow \gamma$ hold, then $\alpha \rightarrow \gamma$ also holds.
$$ \alpha \;\rightarrow\; \beta \;\wedge\; \beta \;\rightarrow\; \gamma \;\Longrightarrow\; \alpha \;\rightarrow\; \gamma $$

> **Intuition:** If A decides B, and B decides C, then A transitively decides C. This is the *chained* reasoning that builds long FDs from short ones.

## 2.3 Derived (Secondary) Inference Rules

From the three primary axioms, we can mathematically derive additional useful rules:

| # | Rule | Formal Statement | Why It Matters |
| :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **D1** | **Union / Additivity** | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\alpha \rightarrow \gamma \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma$ | Combine multiple RHS into one FD |
| **D2** | **Decomposition / Projectivity** | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\alpha \rightarrow \gamma$ | Split a multi-attribute RHS |
| **D3** | **Pseudo-transitivity** | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\gamma\beta \rightarrow \delta \Rightarrow \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \delta$ | Generalised transitivity |
| **D4** | **Composition** | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\gamma \rightarrow \delta \Rightarrow \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\delta$ | Combine two independent FDs |
| **D5** | **Accumulation** | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\alpha \rightarrow \gamma \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \beta\cap\gamma$ | Intersection closure |
| **D6** | **Augmentation corollary** | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\gamma \rightarrow \delta \Rightarrow \alpha\delta \rightarrow \beta\delta$ | Useful for key proofs |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** This table is the *single most important* revision artefact for Module 3 FD problems. Print it and stick it on your wall.

| Rule | Symbol | Schema | KTU Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reflexivity | IR1 | $\beta \subseteq \alpha \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \beta$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Augmentation | IR2 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta \Rightarrow \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\gamma$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Transitivity | IR3 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta,\; \beta \rightarrow \gamma \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \gamma$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Union | D1 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta,\; \alpha \rightarrow \gamma \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Decomposition | D2 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma \Rightarrow \alpha \rightarrow \beta$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Pseudo-transitivity | D3 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta,\; \gamma\beta \rightarrow \delta \Rightarrow \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \delta$ | ⭐⭐ |
| Composition | D4 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta,\; \gamma \rightarrow \delta \Rightarrow \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\delta$ | ⭐⭐ |
| Closure $(\alpha)^+$ | — | $\alpha^+$ = set of all attrs functionally determined by $\alpha$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Closure of FD-set $F^+$ | — | $F^+ = \{\alpha \rightarrow \beta \vert \alpha \rightarrow \beta \text{ derivable from } F\}$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Candidate Key | — | $K$ is a key iff $K^+ = R$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Minimal Cover | — | Unique, irreducible, single-RHS canonical cover of $F$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of FD Theory |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Banking Systems (SBI, Federal Bank core-banking)** | Enforcing that *AccountNo* decides *Balance, CustomerID, IFSC*. Prevents update anomalies. |
| **Hospital EMR (Apollo, KIMS)** | `PatientID + TestID → TestResult`. Determines test uniqueness per patient. |
| **E-Commerce (Amazon-style order schema)** | `OrderID → OrderDate, CustomerID, TotalAmount`. Validates invoice integrity. |
| **University MIS (KTU itself!)** | `URN + CourseCode → Grade, Marks, AttemptNo`. |
| **Aerospace & IoT sensor logs** | `SensorID + Timestamp → Reading, BatteryStatus`. |

In every case, **Armstrong's Axioms** let database engineers *prove* that a decomposition is safe, that a proposed schema preserves all business rules, and that no redundant constraints are stored.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proving the Derived Rules from Armstrong's Axioms (Mandatory KTU Derivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Every time you cite a *derived* rule like Union or Decomposition, the examiner expects at least *one* line of proof using Armstrong's axioms. Below are the complete proofs.

### Derivation 1 — Union Rule (D1)

**Given:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\alpha \rightarrow \gamma$.
**To Prove:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \;& \alpha \rightarrow \beta \quad && \text{(Given)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \;& \alpha\alpha \rightarrow \beta\alpha \quad && \text{(IR2: Augmentation with } \gamma = \alpha \text{)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \;& \alpha \rightarrow \beta\alpha \quad && \text{(Simplify: } \alpha\alpha = \alpha \text{)} \\
\text{Step 4:} \;& \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\gamma \quad && \text{(IR2: Augment Step 1 with } \gamma \text{)} \\
\text{Step 5:} \;& \alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma \quad && \text{(IR3: Transitivity on Steps 3 and 4)} \\
\therefore \; & \alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Translation:** Step 1 to Step 5 shows that we can *bulk up* the LHS arbitrarily (using Augmentation twice) and then *collapse* the redundancy via Transitivity to reach the final Union result.

### Derivation 2 — Decomposition Rule (D2)

**Given:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma$.
**To Prove:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ (and similarly $\alpha \rightarrow \gamma$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \;& \alpha \rightarrow \beta\gamma \quad && \text{(Given)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \;& \beta\gamma \rightarrow \beta \quad && \text{(IR1: Reflexivity, since } \beta \subseteq \beta\gamma \text{)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \;& \alpha \rightarrow \beta \quad && \text{(IR3: Transitivity on Steps 1 and 2)} \\
\therefore \; & \alpha \rightarrow \beta \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Translation:** If a single FD tells us about *multiple* attributes on the RHS, each attribute is *separately* determined. This is the foundation of putting FDs into **Canonical Form** (singleton RHS).

### Derivation 3 — Pseudo-Transitivity (D3)

**Given:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\gamma\beta \rightarrow \delta$.
**To Prove:** $\alpha\gamma \rightarrow \delta$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \;& \alpha \rightarrow \beta \quad && \text{(Given)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \;& \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \beta\gamma \quad && \text{(IR2: Augment Step 1 with } \gamma \text{)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \;& \gamma\beta \rightarrow \delta \quad && \text{(Given, equivalent to } \gamma\beta = \beta\gamma \text{)} \\
\text{Step 4:} \;& \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \delta \quad && \text{(IR3: Transitivity on Steps 2 and 3)} \\
\therefore \; & \alpha\gamma \rightarrow \delta \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Algorithm — Computing the Attribute Closure $\alpha^+$

The closure $\alpha^+$ is the *set of all attributes* functionally determined by $\alpha$ under $F$. This is the single most important algorithmic tool in Module 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:** Tracing the closure algorithm step-by-step is a guaranteed 7-mark sub-question. Master this loop.

**Algorithm: Attribute-Closure**

```
Input:  A set of attributes α, and a set of FDs F
Output: The closure α⁺

closure := α                    // Initialise with α itself
repeat
    for each FD β → γ in F do
        if β ⊆ closure then
            closure := closure ∪ γ
        end if
    end for
until (closure did not change in this iteration)
return closure
```

## 3.3 Worked Example — Attribute Closure Computation

**Given Relation:** $R(A, B, C, D, E, F)$
**Given FDs:** $F = \{\; A \rightarrow B,\; E \rightarrow CF,\; B \rightarrow E,\; CD \rightarrow EF \;\}$

**Task:** Compute $(A)^+$ — i.e., determine everything decided by $A$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Init:} \;& \text{closure} = \{A\} \\
\text{Iter 1, FD1: } A \rightarrow B: &\;\; A \subseteq \{A\}, \text{ so add } B. \\
&\;\; \text{closure} = \{A, B\} \\
\text{Iter 1, FD2: } E \rightarrow CF: &\;\; E \not\subseteq \{A,B\}, \text{ skip.} \\
\text{Iter 1, FD3: } B \rightarrow E: &\;\; B \subseteq \{A,B\}, \text{ so add } E. \\
&\;\; \text{closure} = \{A, B, E\} \\
\text{Iter 1, FD4: } CD \rightarrow EF: &\;\; CD \not\subseteq \{A,B,E\}, \text{ skip.} \\
\text{Iter 2, FD2: } E \rightarrow CF: &\;\; E \subseteq \{A,B,E\}, \text{ add } C, F. \\
&\;\; \text{closure} = \{A, B, C, E, F\} \\
\text{Iter 2, FD4: } CD \rightarrow EF: &\;\; CD \subseteq \{A,B,C,E,F\}, \text{ add } E, F \text{ (already present).} \\
&\;\; \text{closure} = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} \\
\text{Iter 3:} \;& \text{No change.} \\
\therefore \; & (A)^+ = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\} = R
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** $A$ is a **superkey** of $R$. In fact, since no proper subset of $\{A\}$ (which has only $A$ itself) gives $R$, $A$ is also a **candidate key**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm-to-Application Mapping

| Step | Action | Justification (Axiom Used) | Effect on closure |
| :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Initialise | closure := $\alpha$ | Trivial base case | $\|\alpha\|$ attributes |
| 1 | Find FD where LHS $\subseteq$ closure | Reflexivity check | No change yet |
| 2 | Add RHS to closure | Augmentation + Decomposition | $\|\text{closure}\| + k$ |
| 3 | Re-scan all FDs | Repeat | More FDs may now apply |
| 4 | Until fixed point | Transitivity exhaustion | $closure = \alpha^+$ |

## 3.5 Candidate Key Identification — Analytical Procedure

A candidate key $K$ is a *minimal* superkey. The algorithm is:

> **Step 1:** Find an attribute set whose closure $= R$.
> **Step 2:** Remove each attribute one at a time; if closure is still $R$, the attribute is **extraneous**. Remove it permanently.
> **Step 3:** Repeat until no attribute can be removed without shrinking the closure. The remaining set is a **candidate key**.

**Re-using the example from §3.3:**

Compute $(AE)^+$:
- Init: $\{A, E\}$
- $A \rightarrow B$: $\{A, B, E\}$
- $E \rightarrow CF$: $\{A, B, C, E, F\}$
- $B \rightarrow E$: no change (already in)
- $CD \rightarrow EF$: still missing $D$, no change.
- Final: $\{A, B, C, E, F\} \neq R$.

So $AE$ is **not** a superkey. Only $A$ is — and $\{A\}$ is irreducible, so it is the sole candidate key.
