| C | Composite | Yes — single attribute | ❌ Violates 2NF | Decompose into (subset, attr) + (full key, remaining) |
| D | Composite | Yes — multiple attributes | ❌ Violates 2NF | Multiple decompositions needed |
| E | Single attribute | N/A but transitive deps exist | ⚠️ In 2NF, but **not in 3NF** | Apply 3NF normalization next |

## 3.3 Derivation — The Mathematical Form of 2NF

The formal definition, expressed in predicate logic for full rigor:

$$
\text{2NF}(R) \iff \text{1NF}(R) \;\land\; \forall A \in \text{NonPrime}(R), \forall S \subseteq K : (S \rightarrow A \implies S = K)
$$

In words: A relation R is in 2NF if and only if R is in 1NF, and for every non-prime attribute A and every subset S of the primary key K, if S functionally determines A, then S must equal K (i.e., A cannot be determined by any *proper* subset of K).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Takeaway for KTU Valuation:** A common mistake is testing only 1NF. Always verify **partial dependencies** on non-prime attributes when the primary key is composite. This is the precise KTU expectation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Normal Form Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Unnormalized Relation] -->|Step 1: Atomic Values| A1[1NF Relation]
    A1 -->|Step 2: Eliminate Partial Dependencies| A2[2NF Relation]
    A2 -->|Step 3: Eliminate Transitive Dependencies| A3[3NF Relation]
    A3 -->|Step 4: Stronger BCNF Rules| A4[BCNF Relation]
    A2 -.->|Check: Partial Dependency Exists| A5[Violation Detected]
    A5 -.->|Decompose into Sub-Relations| A2
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Dependency Flow in STUDENT_COURSE

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FK1[Proper Subset 1]
        K1a[RegNo]
    end
    subgraph FK2[Proper Subset 2]
        K2a[CourseID]
    end
    subgraph FK3[Full Key]
        K3a[RegNo]
        K3b[CourseID]
    end
    K1a -->|Partial FD| NPA1[StudentName]
    K2a -->|Partial FD| NPA2[CourseName]
    K2a -->|Partial FD| NPA3[Credits]
    K3a --> K3b
    K3a -->|Full FD| NPA4[Grade]
    K3b --> NPA4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decomposition Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    ORIG[Original Relation R RegNo CourseID StudentName CourseName Credits Grade] --> DETECT{Composite Key and Partial FDs}
    DETECT -->|Detected| DECOMP[Apply 2NF Decomposition]
    DECOMP --> R1[R1: RegNo, StudentName]
    DECOMP --> R2[R2: CourseID, CourseName, Credits]
    DECOMP --> R3[R3: RegNo, CourseID, Grade]
    R1 --> VAL[Validate Lossless Join and Dependency Preservation]
    R2 --> VAL
    R3 --> VAL
    VAL --> DONE[All Three Relations in 2NF]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for 2NF Test

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start: Examine Relation R] --> Q1{Is R in 1NF?}
    Q1 -->|No| FIX1NF[Normalize to 1NF First]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is the Primary Key Composite?}
    Q2 -->|No, Single Attribute| AUTO[Automatically in 2NF]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Any Non-Prime Attribute Depends on a Proper Subset of the Key?}
    Q3 -->|No| PASS[Relation is in 2NF]
    Q3 -->|Yes| FAIL[Relation Violates 2NF]
    FAIL --> ACT[Decompose into Smaller Relations]
    ACT --> PASS
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. Define Second Normal Form (2NF). State the conditions for a relation to be in 2NF.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A relation R is in **Second Normal Form (2NF)** if and only if:
1. It is in **First Normal Form (1NF)** — all attributes hold atomic values.
2. **Every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on the primary key** — there must be no partial dependency, i.e., no non-prime attribute may be functionally determined by any proper subset of the primary key (especially significant when the primary key is composite).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
### **Q2. What is a partial dependency? How does it violate 2NF?**
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **partial dependency** is a functional dependency $X \rightarrow A$ where $X$ is a **proper subset** of a composite candidate key, and $A$ is a non-prime attribute. It violates 2NF because 2NF requires every non-prime attribute to depend on the **whole** key, not just part of it. Partial dependencies cause redundancy and update, insertion, and deletion anomalies, which 2NF is designed to eliminate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (ESE Module 3 — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Consider the following relation for a publishing company:

`BOOK_PUBLISHER(BookID, AuthorID, BookTitle, AuthorName, AuthorCountry, Publisher, Year)`

with functional dependencies:

- $\{\text{BookID, AuthorID}\} \rightarrow \text{BookTitle, Publisher, Year}$
- $\text{BookID} \rightarrow \text{BookTitle, Publisher, Year}$
- $\text{AuthorID} \rightarrow \text{AuthorName, AuthorCountry}$

Identify the **primary key**, classify attributes as prime/non-prime, and determine whether the relation is in 2NF. Justify with explicit reasoning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the relation is not in 2NF, **decompose it into 2NF relations**. Show all decomposed schemas with their keys underlined, and verify that the decomposition is **lossless** and **dependency-preserving**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Primary Key**

The only candidate key derivable from the given FDs is the **composite key**:

$$
K = \{\text{BookID, AuthorID}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Classify Attributes**

- **Prime attributes:** BookID, AuthorID
- **Non-prime attributes:** BookTitle, AuthorName, AuthorCountry, Publisher, Year

**Step 3 — Test for Partial Dependencies**

Proper subsets of K:

$$
K_1 = \{\text{BookID}\}, \quad K_2 = \{\text{AuthorID}\}
$$

Evaluate each FD:

| FD | RHS Attribute | Partial Dependency? |
|----|---------------|:-------------------:|
| $\text{BookID} \rightarrow \text{BookTitle}$ | BookTitle (non-prime) | ✅ **YES** |
| $\text{BookID} \rightarrow \text{Publisher}$ | Publisher (non-prime) | ✅ **YES** |
| $\text{BookID} \rightarrow \text{Year}$ | Year (non-prime) | ✅ **YES** |
| $\text{AuthorID} \rightarrow \text{AuthorName}$ | AuthorName (non-prime) | ✅ **YES** |
| $\text{AuthorID} \rightarrow \text{AuthorCountry}$ | AuthorCountry (non-prime) | ✅ **YES** |

**Conclusion:** The relation **violates 2NF** due to multiple partial dependencies. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Decomposition Step 1 — Extract partial dependency on BookID**

$$
R_1(\underline{\text{BookID}}, \text{BookTitle, Publisher, Year})
$$

**Decomposition Step 2 — Extract partial dependency on AuthorID**

$$
R_2(\underline{\text{AuthorID}}, \text{AuthorName, AuthorCountry})
$$

**Decomposition Step 3 — Retain the original composite key with remaining fully-dependent attributes**

$$
R_3(\underline{\text{BookID}}, \underline{\text{AuthorID}})
$$

**Final 2NF Schema:**

$$
\boxed{R_1(\underline{\text{BookID}}, \text{BookTitle}, \text{Publisher}, \text{Year}), \quad R_2(\underline{\text{AuthorID}}, \text{AuthorName}, \text{AuthorCountry}), \quad R_3(\underline{\text{BookID}}, \underline{\text{AuthorID}})}
$$

**Lossless Join Verification:**

- $R_1 \cap R_2 = \emptyset$ — handled trivially.
- $R_1 \cap R_3 = \{\text{BookID}\}$ — and `BookID` is a key of $R_1$. ✅
- $R_2 \cap R_3 = \{\text{AuthorID}\}$ — and `AuthorID` is a key of $R_2$. ✅

Decomposition is **lossless**. **(4 marks)**

**Dependency Preservation Verification:**

- $\Pi_{R_1}(F) = \{\text{BookID} \rightarrow \text{BookTitle, Publisher, Year}\}$ ✅
- $\Pi_{R_2}(F) = \{\text{AuthorID} \rightarrow \text{AuthorName, AuthorCountry}\}$ ✅
- $\Pi_{R_3}(F) = \emptyset$ (the composite key FD is preserved by the join) ✅

All original FDs are preserved. **Dependency preservation confirmed.** **(3 marks)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **functional dependency** and differentiate between **full functional dependency** and **partial dependency** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the relation:

`PROJECT(EmpID, ProjectID, EmpName, ProjectName, HoursWorked, DeptID, DeptName)`

with FDs:

- $\text{EmpID} \rightarrow \text{EmpName, DeptID}$
- $\text{DeptID} \rightarrow \text{DeptName}$
- $\text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{ProjectName}$
- $\{\text{EmpID, ProjectID}\} \rightarrow \text{HoursWorked}$

Identify the primary key. Check whether the relation is in 2NF. If not, **normalize it to 2NF** with full justification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

A **functional dependency** $X \rightarrow Y$ states that for any two tuples $t_1$ and $t_2$ in a relation, if $t_1[X] = t_2[X]$, then $t_1[Y] = t_2[Y]$. Here, $X$ is called the **determinant** and $Y$ is the **dependent**.

A **full functional dependency** $X \xrightarrow{f} Y$ exists when $Y$ is functionally dependent on $X$ but **not** on any proper subset of $X$.

**Example:** In `ENROLLMENT(RegNo, CourseID, Grade)`, the FD $\{\text{RegNo, CourseID}\} \rightarrow \text{Grade}$ is a **full** functional dependency, because Grade cannot be determined by RegNo alone or CourseID alone.

A **partial dependency** $X \xrightarrow{p} Y$ exists when $Y$ is functionally dependent on a **proper subset** of $X$ (the candidate key).

**Example:** In `STUDENT(RegNo, CourseID, StudentName, CourseName)`, the FD $\text{RegNo} \rightarrow \text{StudentName}$ is a **partial dependency** because RegNo alone (a proper subset of the composite key) determines StudentName. This is the precise violation that 2NF eliminates. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Primary Key**

The only FD that requires both attributes is $\{\text{EmpID, ProjectID}\} \rightarrow \text{HoursWorked}$. Hence:

$$
K = \{\text{EmpID, ProjectID}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Classify Attributes**

- **Prime attributes:** EmpID, ProjectID
- **Non-prime attributes:** EmpName, ProjectName, HoursWorked, DeptID, DeptName

**Step 3 — Detect Partial Dependencies**

| FD | Partial? |
|----|:--------:|
| $\text{EmpID} \rightarrow \text{EmpName}$ | ✅ Yes |
| $\text{EmpID} \rightarrow \text{DeptID}$ | ✅ Yes |
| $\text{DeptID} \rightarrow \text{DeptName}$ | ⚠️ Transitive (also invalid for 2NF? No — 2NF does not address transitive dependencies; 3NF does) |
| $\text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{ProjectName}$ | ✅ Yes |
| $\{\text{EmpID, ProjectID}\} \rightarrow \text{HoursWorked}$ | ❌ No (full FD) |

**Conclusion:** Relation **violates 2NF** due to three partial dependencies.

**Step 4 — Normalize to 2NF**

$$
R_1(\underline{\text{EmpID}}, \text{EmpName}, \text{DeptID})
$$

$$
R_2(\underline{\text{ProjectID}}, \text{ProjectName})
$$

$$
R_3(\underline{\text{EmpID}}, \underline{\text{ProjectID}}, \text{HoursWorked})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: `DeptID → DeptName` is a **transitive dependency** (non-key → non-key). It does not violate 2NF — it is addressed at the **3NF** stage. For 2NF, we stop here.

**Lossless Join:** The common attributes `EmpID` and `ProjectID` are keys in the respective relations, ensuring lossless decomposition. **(7 marks awarded)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on 2NF Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check if the relation is in 1NF first** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if 1NF verification is omitted.
> 2. **Confusing partial dependency with transitive dependency** — Partial dependency = non-key attribute depends on **part of a candidate key**. Transitive dependency = non-key attribute depends on **another non-key attribute**. They are distinct violations at different normal form levels.
> 3. **Not decomposing fully** — If the relation has **multiple** partial dependencies, each must be extracted into its own sub-relation. Partial credit is given for incomplete decomposition.
> 4. **Omitting lossless-join and dependency-preservation verification** — KTU expects explicit verification statements, not just the decomposed schema.
> 5. **Marking a single-attribute primary-key relation as needing 2NF normalization** — Single-attribute keys **cannot** exhibit partial dependency; 2NF is automatically satisfied. State this clearly to earn full marks.
> 6. **Writing the composite key incorrectly** — Misidentifying the candidate key leads to cascading errors. Always derive the key from the given FDs first.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid-Revision Checklist for 2NF (Print Before Exam)**
>
> - ✅ 2NF applies **only after 1NF** is verified.
> - ✅ 2NF targets **partial dependencies**, not transitive ones.
> - ✅ A relation with a **single-attribute primary key** is **always in 2NF** (no proper subset exists to cause a partial dependency).
> - ✅ 2NF violations arise **only with composite primary keys**.
> - ✅ **Prime attributes** can participate in partial dependencies *without violating 2NF*; only **non-prime attributes** matter.
> - ✅ Decomposition of a 2NF-violating relation must be **lossless** (common key in joined relations) and **dependency-preserving**.
> - ✅ The proper subset of the composite key that causes the partial dependency becomes the **primary key of the new sub-relation**.
> - ✅ 2NF is a **necessary but not sufficient** condition for higher normal forms (3NF, BCNF).
> - ✅ 2NF eliminates **update, insertion, and deletion anomalies** caused by partial-key dependency redundancy.
> - ✅ KTU exam pattern: **definition (3 marks) + decomposition problem (7 marks sub-parts) + verification (lossless + dependency-preserving)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Third Normal Form (3NF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Third Normal Form (3NF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Codd, 1971):** A relation schema $R$ is in **Third Normal Form (3NF)** if and only if it is in **Second Normal Form (2NF)** and **no non-prime attribute** of $R$ is **transitively dependent** on any **candidate key** of $R$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Formal Definition (using FDs):** A relation schema $R$ is in 3NF with respect to a set of functional dependencies $F$ if, for every functional dependency $X \rightarrow A$ in $F^+$ (the closure of $F$), at least **one** of the following conditions holds:
> 1. $X \rightarrow A$ is a **trivial functional dependency** (i.e., $A \subseteq X$).
> 2. $X$ is a **superkey** of $R$.
> 3. $A - X$ (i.e., each attribute in $A - X$) is a **prime attribute** — an attribute that is part of some candidate key of $R$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Notice that the **third condition** (prime attribute on the RHS) is what differentiates 3NF from **Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)**. BCNF requires only conditions 1 and 2, while 3NF relaxes the rule to admit dependencies where the RHS is a prime attribute.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Catalog" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Consider a **University Library** that maintains a catalog of books.

> Imagine a single, giant spreadsheet where every row records: *Book Title → Author → Author's Country of Birth → Author's Birth Year*.

> - The **Book Title** uniquely identifies the **Author** (Book Title → Author).
> - The **Author** uniquely identifies the **Author's Country of Birth** (Author → Country).
> - Therefore, **Book Title transitively determines Country** (Book Title → Author → Country).

> This is a **transitive dependency**. If you update the Author's country, you must find and update *every* row containing books by that author — a classic **update anomaly**.

> **3NF's job** is to split this single spreadsheet into:
> 1. **Table 1:** Book Title, Author (the "fact" table)
> 2. **Table 2:** Author, Country, Birth Year (the "lookup" table)

> Now, when the author's country changes, you update **one row in Table 2**, and the change is automatically reflected everywhere — **anomalies eliminated**.

## 1.3 Why 3NF Matters in Engineering Practice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Significance:**
> - **Data Integrity:** Eliminates update, insertion, and deletion anomalies caused by transitive dependencies.
> - **Storage Efficiency:** Reduces redundant storage of non-key data.
> - **Query Performance:** Smaller, focused relations often lead to better join selectivity.
> - **3NF is the "Sweet Spot":** It is provably always achievable via **dependency-preserving** and **lossless-join** decomposition, unlike BCNF (which may sacrifice dependency preservation).

## 1.4 Position in the Normalization Hierarchy

| Normal Form | Eliminates | Builds On |
|-------------|------------|-----------|
| **1NF** | Multi-valued attributes, repeating groups | Atomic values |
| **2NF** | Partial dependencies on composite keys | 1NF + Full FD on PK |
| **3NF** | Transitive dependencies on non-prime attrs | 2NF + No transitives |
| **BCNF** | All non-trivial FDs from non-superkeys | 3NF + Stricter FD rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy Rule:** Every BCNF relation is in 3NF, but **not** every 3NF relation is in BCNF. The reverse is **not** true.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposing a 2NF Relation into 3NF — The Logic Steps

> [!TIP]
> **When to Apply 3NF Decomposition:**
> Apply when a 2NF relation $R$ contains a transitive dependency of the form:
> $X \rightarrow Y \rightarrow Z$, where $X$ is a candidate key, $Y$ is a non-prime attribute, and $Z$ is a non-prime attribute.

**Step-by-Step Decomposition Logic:**

1. **Step 1 — Identify the transitive chain:** Find all FDs of the form $A \rightarrow B$ where $A$ is non-superkey and $B$ is non-prime.
2. **Step 2 — Isolate the intermediate dependency:** Create a new relation $R_2(A, B)$ containing the determinant and the transitively-dependent attribute(s).
3. **Step 3 — Remove redundant attributes from original:** Create $R_1(X, A)$ where $X$ is the original candidate key and $A$ is the immediate determinant.
4. **Step 4 — Verify Lossless Join:** The common attribute(s) between $R_1$ and $R_2$ must be a **superkey of at least one** of the decomposed relations.
5. **Step 5 — Verify Dependency Preservation:** All original FDs must be representable as FDs over the decomposed relations.

## 2.2 Lossless Join Condition (Heath's Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heath's Theorem (1971):** A decomposition of $R$ into $R_1$ and $R_2$ is **lossless** if and only if the common attributes $R_1 \cap R_2$ form a **superkey of at least one** of $R_1$ or $R_2$.

> Equivalently: $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1$ **OR** $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_2$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Property / Theorem** | **Formula / Condition** | **Purpose** |
|------------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| 3NF Core Test | For every $X \rightarrow A \in F^+$: $X$ is superkey **OR** $A \subseteq X$ **OR** $A$ is prime | Membership test |
| Transitive Dependency | $X \rightarrow Y$, $Y \rightarrow Z$, $Y \nrightarrow X$, $X$ is not superkey | Anomaly indicator |
| Lossless Join (Binary) | $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1$ **OR** $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_2$ | Join correctness |
| Lossless Join (General) | $\left( (R_1 \cap R_2)^+ \cap R_1 \right) \cup R_2 = R_1$ (or $R_2$) | n-ary decomposition |
| Dependency Preservation | $(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \ldots \cup F_n)^+ = F^+$ | FD retainability |
| Attribute Count (Lower Bound) | Minimum relations = Number of canonical covers with non-superkey LHS | Synthesis bound |
| Closure Operator | $X^+ = X \cup \{A \mid X \rightarrow A \text{ derivable from } F\}$ | FD computation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Notation:** The expression $\vert A \vert$ (cardinality) is written as $\lvert A \rvert$ to prevent table syntax breakage. Similarly, set difference $A - B$ must not use vertical pipes.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Applications of 3NF:**
> - **Banking Systems:** Account → Branch → Branch Address decomposition eliminates update anomalies across millions of accounts.
> - **E-Commerce:** Order → Product → Product Category → Category Discount chain split for performance and integrity.
> - **Hospital Management:** Patient → Doctor → Department → Department Head normalization.
> - **OLTP Systems:** 3NF is the **de-facto standard** for transactional database design (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, Oracle default).

## 2.5 The 3NF Synthesis Algorithm (Bernstein, 1976)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** The Synthesis Algorithm guarantees a **lossless-join, dependency-preserving** 3NF decomposition in polynomial time.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Step 1 — Minimal Cover:** Compute a **canonical (minimal) cover** $F_c$ of $F$.
   - Decompose RHS into single attributes.
   - Remove extraneous attributes from LHS.
   - Remove redundant FDs.
2. **Step 2 — Group by LHS:** For each distinct LHS $X$ in $F_c$, create a relation schema $R_i = X \cup \{A_1, A_2, \ldots\}$ where $A_i$ are all attributes appearing on RHS of FDs with LHS $= X$.
3. **Step 3 — Eliminate Redundancy:** If any $R_j \subseteq R_i$, remove $R_j$ (subsumed schemas).
4. **Step 4 — Ensure Key Inclusion:** If no relation contains a candidate key of $R$, add a new relation containing the candidate key.
5. **Step 5 — Output:** The final set of relations is a **dependency-preserving, lossless-join 3NF decomposition**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: 2NF → 3NF Decomposition

> [!NOTE]
> **Given Relation:** `Lending` with attributes and FDs as below.

**Schema:** $R = \{ \text{Branch}, \text{LoanNo}, \text{CustomerID}, \text{CustomerName}, \text{City} \}$

**Functional Dependencies (FDs):**
1. $\text{Branch}, \text{LoanNo} \rightarrow \text{CustomerID}$
2. $\text{CustomerID} \rightarrow \text{CustomerName}$
3. $\text{CustomerName} \rightarrow \text{City}$
4. $\text{Branch} \rightarrow \text{City}$

**Candidate Key:** $\{$ Branch, LoanNo $\}$ (only FD with this LHS determines all attributes)

**Prime Attributes:** Branch, LoanNo (both in the candidate key)
**Non-Prime Attributes:** CustomerID, CustomerName, City

### Step 1 — Detect the Transitive Chain

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Branch}, \text{LoanNo} \rightarrow \text{CustomerID} \rightarrow \text{CustomerName} \rightarrow \text{City} \\
&\text{AND} \\
&\text{Branch} \rightarrow \text{City} \quad \text{(direct FD, also transitive via the key)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Observation:** There exists a transitive dependency:
$$
\text{Branch}, \text{LoanNo} \rightarrow \text{Branch} \rightarrow \text{City}
$$
where **Branch** is non-superkey and **City** is non-prime. ❌ **Violates 3NF.**

### Step 2 — Decomposition Plan

**Relation 1 (Lending_Fact):** $R_1 = \{ \text{Branch}, \text{LoanNo}, \text{CustomerID} \}$

**Relation 2 (Branch_Info):** $R_2 = \{ \text{Branch}, \text{City} \}$

**Relation 3 (Customer_Info):** $R_3 = \{ \text{CustomerID}, \text{CustomerName} \}$

**Relation 4 (City_Resolution):** $R_4 = \{ \text{CustomerName}, \text{City} \}$

### Step 3 — Verify Lossless Join

For $(R_1, R_2)$:
- $R_1 \cap R_2 = \{ \text{Branch} \}$
- $\{ \text{Branch} \}^+ = \{ \text{Branch}, \text{City} \} \supseteq R_2$ ✅ **Lossless**

For $(R_1, R_3)$:
- $R_1 \cap R_3 = \{ \text{CustomerID} \}$
- $\{ \text{CustomerID} \}^+ = \{ \text{CustomerID}, \text{CustomerName} \} \supseteq R_3$ ✅ **Lossless**

For $(R_3, R_4)$:
- $R_3 \cap R_4 = \{ \text{CustomerName} \}$
- $\{ \text{CustomerName} \}^+ = \{ \text{CustomerName}, \text{City} \} \supseteq R_4$ ✅ **Lossless**

### Step 4 — Verify Dependency Preservation

| Original FD | Realizable In |
|-------------|---------------|
| Branch, LoanNo → CustomerID | $R_1$ ✅ |
| CustomerID → CustomerName | $R_3$ ✅ |
| CustomerName → City | $R_4$ ✅ |
| Branch → City | $R_2$ ✅ |

**All FDs preserved.** ✅

### Step 5 — Final 3NF Schema

$$
\boxed{ R_1(\text{Branch}, \text{LoanNo}, \text{CustomerID}), \quad R_2(\text{Branch}, \text{City}), \quad R_3(\text{CustomerID}, \text{CustomerName}), \quad R_4(\text{CustomerName}, \text{City}) }
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Synthesis Algorithm Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** $R = \{ A, B, C, D, E, F \}$ with FDs:
> - $F = \{ AB \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow D, D \rightarrow E, E \rightarrow F, F \rightarrow A \}$

### Step 1 — Compute Canonical Cover $F_c$

**1a. Make RHS singleton:**

$$
F' = \{ AB \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow D, \; D \rightarrow E, \; E \rightarrow F, \; F \rightarrow A \}
$$

(All RHS are already singletons.)

**1b. Remove extraneous LHS attributes:**

- For $AB \rightarrow C$: Check if $A \rightarrow C$ or $B \rightarrow C$ alone holds.
  - $\{A\}^+$ with $F'$: $A \rightarrow A$ (trivial). Cannot derive $C$. Not extraneous.
  - $\{B\}^+$ with $F'$: $B \rightarrow B$ (trivial). Cannot derive $C$. Not extraneous.
- All other FDs have single-attribute LHS — no LHS reduction needed.

**1c. Remove redundant FDs:**

- Can we remove $AB \rightarrow C$? Check if $C$ is in $\{A,B\}^+$ without it. $\{A,B\}^+ = \{A,B\}$ — no. **Keep.**
- Can we remove $C \rightarrow D$? $\{C\}^+$ without it: $C \rightarrow C$. **Keep.**
- Can we remove $D \rightarrow E$? $\{D\}^+$ without it: $D \rightarrow D$. **Keep.**
- Can we remove $E \rightarrow F$? $\{E\}^+$ without it: $E \rightarrow E$. **Keep.**
- Can we remove $F \rightarrow A$? $\{F\}^+$ without it: $F \rightarrow F$. **Keep.**

$$
\boxed{ F_c = \{ AB \rightarrow C, \; C \rightarrow D, \; D \rightarrow E, \; E \rightarrow F, \; F \rightarrow A \} }
$$

### Step 2 — Group by LHS

| Group | LHS | RHS | Relation |
|-------|-----|-----|----------|
| 1 | $AB$ | $C$ | $R_1(A, B, C)$ |
| 2 | $C$ | $D$ | $R_2(C, D)$ |
| 3 | $D$ | $E$ | $R_3(D, E)$ |
| 4 | $E$ | $F$ | $R_4(E, F)$ |
| 5 | $F$ | $A$ | $R_5(F, A)$ |

### Step 3 — Find Candidate Key

**Try $B^+$:** $B \rightarrow B$ (nothing extra). Not a key.

**Try $A^+$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow A \quad \text{(trivial)} \\
A &\rightarrow F \quad \text{(via } F \rightarrow A \text{, reverse? No, FD is } F \rightarrow A \text{, not } A \rightarrow F) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — we need to find what attributes $A$ can derive using the **reverse** direction is not allowed. Let us compute $A^+$ using the given FDs:

$A^+ = \{A\}$. We need FDs where LHS is a subset of $\{A\}$ — only $F \rightarrow A$ has LHS $F$, not in our closure chain. So $A^+ = \{A\}$.

**Try $B, F$:** $B^+ = \{B\}$, then $B \cup F = \{B, F\}^+ = \{B, F, A\}$ (via $F \rightarrow A$). Then add $C \rightarrow \ldots$? No, $C \notin \{A, B, F\}$. So $(ABF)^+ = \{A, B, F\}$.

**Try $B, C$:** $\{B, C\}^+ = \{B, C, D\}$ (via $C \rightarrow D$), then $D \rightarrow E$: $\{B, C, D, E\}$, then $E \rightarrow F$: $\{B, C, D, E, F\}$, then $F \rightarrow A$: $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ ✅

**Candidate Key = $\{B, C\}$**

### Step 4 — Check Key Inclusion

No existing relation contains $\{B, C\}$ as a subset. → **Add a new relation** $R_6(B, C)$.

### Step 5 — Check for Subsumption

- $R_2(C, D) \subseteq R_3(D, E)$? No, $C \notin R_3$.
- $R_3(D, E) \subseteq R_4(E, F)$? No.
- $R_4(E, F) \subseteq R_5(F, A)$? No.
- $R_5(F, A) \subseteq R_1(A, B, C)$? No.

No subsumption.

### Final 3NF Decomposition

$$
\boxed{ R_1(A, B, C), \; R_2(C, D), \; R_3(D, E), \; R_4(E, F), \; R_5(F, A), \; R_6(B, C) }
$$

**Note:** $R_1$ is subsumed by $R_6 \cup$ check — actually $R_6(B,C)$ has only 2 attrs, $R_1(A,B,C)$ has 3. $R_6 \not\subseteq R_1$ as relation schemas (different attrs). All six relations are kept.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: 3NF vs BCNF

| **Property** | **3NF** | **BCNF** |
|--------------|---------|----------|
| Definition basis | FDs and prime attribute relaxation | FDs only (no prime attr exception) |
| Anomaly elimination | Transitive + partial | All FD-based anomalies |
| Always achievable | ✅ **Yes** (synthesis algorithm) | ⚠️ Not always (may need 4NF/5NF info) |
| Lossless join | ✅ Guaranteed | ✅ Guaranteed |
| Dependency preservation | ✅ **Guaranteed** | ❌ **Not always** |
| Strictness | Less strict | More strict |
| Subset relation | $\text{BCNF} \subset \text{3NF}$ | — |
| Typical use case | OLTP systems | Analytical edge cases |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Normalization Hierarchy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Unnormalized Relation] --> A1[1NF: Atomic Values]
    A1 --> A2[2NF: No Partial Dependencies]
    A2 --> A3[3NF: No Transitive Dependencies]
    A3 --> A4[BCNF: Every Determinant is Superkey]
    A3 --> A5[3NF Final: Dependency Preserved]
    A4 --> A6[4NF: No Multivalued Dependencies]
    A4 --> A7[5NF: No Join Dependencies]
    A5 -.Trade-off.-> A4
    
    style A0 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style A1 fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#fffacc,stroke:#cccc00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style A4 fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#003399,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style A5 fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style A6 fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style A7 fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#cc0066,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 3NF Synthesis Algorithm Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Input: Relation R and FD Set F] --> B1[Step 1: Compute Canonical Cover Fc]
    B1 --> B1a[Decompose RHS into Singletons]
    B1a --> B1b[Remove Extraneous LHS Attributes]
    B1b --> B1c[Eliminate Redundant FDs]
    B1c --> B2[Step 2: Group FDs by LHS]
    B2 --> B3[Step 3: Create Relation per Group]
    B3 --> B4[Step 4: Remove Subsumed Schemas]
    B4 --> B5[Step 5: Check Candidate Key Coverage]
    B5 --> B5a{Key Already in Some Relation?}
    B5a -->|No| B5b[Add Key Relation Rkey]
    B5a -->|Yes| B6[Output 3NF Schema]
    B5b --> B6
    
    style B0 fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B1 fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#006699,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B2 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B3 fill:#fffacc,stroke:#cccc00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B4 fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B5 fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#cc0066,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B6 fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Transitive Dependency Decomposition Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Original 2NF Table R X Y Z] --> C1{Transitive Y to Z?}
    C1 -->|Yes, Non-prime| C2[Split into Two Relations]
    C1 -->|No, Prime attr| C3[Keep as 3NF]
    C2 --> C2a[R1: X Y]
    C2 --> C2b[R2: Y Z]
    C2a -.Join on Y.-> C4[Reconstructed via Lossless Join]
    C2b -.Join on Y.-> C4
    C3 --> C5[Validated 3NF Schema]
    C4 --> C5
    
    style C0 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    C1{Transitive Y to Z?}
    style C1 fill:#fffacc,stroke:#cccc00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C3 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C5 fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 FD Closure Testing Flow for 3NF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[For each FD: X to A in F+] --> D1{Check Condition 1}
    D1 -->|A subset of X| D2[Trivial: Pass 3NF]
    D1 -->|No| D3{Check Condition 2}
    D3 -->|X is Superkey| D4[Superkey: Pass 3NF]
    D3 -->|No| D5{Check Condition 3}
    D5 -->|A is Prime Attribute| D6[Prime RHS: Pass 3NF]
    D5 -->|No| D7[FAIL: Violates 3NF]
    D7 --> D8[Decompose: Isolate X to A]
    D8 --> D9[Re-test Decomposed Relations]
    
    style D0 fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D4 fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D6 fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D7 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style D9 fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#006699,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Third Normal Form (3NF). How does it differ from Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **3NF Definition:** A relation $R$ is in Third Normal Form if it is in 2NF and no non-prime attribute is transitively dependent on any candidate key of $R$.

> **Equivalently:** For every FD $X \rightarrow A$ in $F^+$, either $X$ is a superkey, or $A \subseteq X$, or $A$ is a prime attribute.

> **Difference from BCNF:**
> - **3NF** allows an FD $X \rightarrow A$ even when $X$ is not a superkey, provided $A$ is a **prime attribute** (part of some candidate key).
> - **BCNF** is stricter — it disallows this exception. Every determinant must be a superkey.
> - Hence, every BCNF relation is in 3NF, but the converse is not always true.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a transitive dependency? Explain with an example why it causes update anomalies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** A transitive dependency exists in a relation $R$ when a non-prime attribute $Z$ is functionally dependent on another non-prime attribute $Y$, which in turn is functionally dependent on the candidate key $X$. Formally: $X \rightarrow Y$, $Y \rightarrow Z$, $Y \nrightarrow X$, and $Z \notin$ candidate key.

> **Example:**
> Consider `STUDENT(RegNo, Dept, HOD)` with FD: `RegNo → Dept` and `Dept → HOD`.
> - `RegNo → HOD` is transitive via `Dept`.

> **Update Anomaly:** If the HOD of a department changes, the new HOD's name must be updated in **every row** belonging to students of that department. Forgetting to update one row causes **inconsistent data** — different students in the same department showing different HODs.

> 3NF eliminates this by splitting the relation into `STUDENT(RegNo, Dept)` and `DEPARTMENT(Dept, HOD)`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the relation:
$$
R = \{ \text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}, \text{ProjectName}, \text{EmpDept}, \text{DeptHead} \}
$$
with FDs:
- $\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{ProjectName}, \text{EmpDept}$
- $\text{EmpID} \rightarrow \text{EmpDept}$
- $\text{EmpDept} \rightarrow \text{DeptHead}$

**Identify the highest normal form. If it is not in 3NF, decompose it into 3NF.**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **3NF Synthesis Algorithm** with suitable steps. Apply it to the above relation to obtain a dependency-preserving, lossless-join 3NF decomposition.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Identification and Decomposition [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Find Candidate Key:**

Compute $\{$ EmpID, ProjectID $\}^+$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\{ \text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID} \}^+ \\
&= \{ \text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}, \text{ProjectName}, \text{EmpDept} \} \quad \text{(using FD1)} \\
&\cup \{ \text{DeptHead} \} \quad \text{(using EmpDept → DeptHead)} \\
&= \{ \text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}, \text{ProjectName}, \text{EmpDept}, \text{DeptHead} \} \\
&= R \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Candidate Key:** $\{$ EmpID, ProjectID $\}$
**Prime attributes:** EmpID, ProjectID
**Non-prime attributes:** ProjectName, EmpDept, DeptHead

**Step 2 — Test for 3NF:**

Check each FD:
| FD | Is X superkey? | Is A ⊆ X? | Is A prime? | 3NF OK? |
|----|----------------|-----------|-------------|---------|
| EmpID, ProjectID → ProjectName | ✅ Yes | No | No (non-prime) | ✅ |
| EmpID, ProjectID → EmpDept | ✅ Yes | No | No (non-prime) | ✅ |
| EmpID → EmpDept | ❌ No | No | No (non-prime) | ❌ **Violates** |
| EmpDept → DeptHead | ❌ No | No | No (non-prime) | ❌ **Violates** |

**Violations:** Two transitive dependencies:
- $\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{EmpID} \rightarrow \text{EmpDept}$
- $\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{EmpDept} \rightarrow \text{DeptHead}$

**Highest NF: 2NF** (it is in 2NF since the only candidate key is composite and non-key attrs depend on the full key — but transitive FDs violate 3NF).

**Step 3 — Decompose into 3NF:**

- $R_1 = \{ \text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}, \text{ProjectName} \}$ (from FD1)
- $R_2 = \{ \text{EmpID}, \text{EmpDept} \}$ (from FD2)
- $R_3 = \{ \text{EmpDept}, \text{DeptHead} \}$ (from FD3)

**Step 4 — Verify Lossless Join (Heath's Theorem):**

For $(R_1, R_2)$: $R_1 \cap R_2 = \{ \text{EmpID} \}$. $\{ \text{EmpID} \}^+ \supseteq R_2$? $\{ \text{EmpID} \}^+ = \{ \text{EmpID}, \text{EmpDept} \} \supseteq R_2$ ✅

For $(R_1, R_3)$: $R_1 \cap R_3 = \emptyset$. ❌ Not lossless between $R_1$ and $R_3$ directly. But $R_1 \cap R_2$ is superkey of $R_2$, and $R_2 \cap R_3 = \{ \text{EmpDept} \}$ is superkey of $R_3$ (since $\{ \text{EmpDept} \}^+ = \{ \text{EmpDept}, \text{DeptHead} \} \supseteq R_3$). ✅ **Cascading lossless join holds.**

**Step 5 — Verify Dependency Preservation:**

All FDs are preserved in their respective relations. ✅

**Final 3NF Schema:** $R_1(\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}, \text{ProjectName})$, $R_2(\text{EmpID}, \text{EmpDept})$, $R_3(\text{EmpDept}, \text{DeptHead})$

#### Part (b) — Synthesis Algorithm Application [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute Canonical Cover $F_c$:**

Given $F = \{ \text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{ProjectName}; \; \text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{EmpDept}; \; \text{EmpID} \rightarrow \text{EmpDept}; \; \text{EmpDept} \rightarrow \text{DeptHead} \}$

Combine FDs with same LHS: $\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \{ \text{ProjectName}, \text{EmpDept} \}$

Split RHS: $\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{ProjectName}$ and $\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{EmpDept}$

Check if $\text{EmpID} \rightarrow \text{EmpDept}$ is redundant (since it follows from the combined FD): Yes, redundant. Remove.

**Check extraneous LHS:** For $\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{ProjectName}$, can we drop `ProjectID`? $\{\text{EmpID}\}^+ = \{\text{EmpID}, \text{EmpDept}, \text{DeptHead}\}$. Does not contain `ProjectName`. So `ProjectID` is not extraneous. Same for `EmpID` — cannot be dropped.

$$
\boxed{ F_c = \{ \text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{ProjectName}; \; \text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID} \rightarrow \text{EmpDept}; \; \text{EmpDept} \rightarrow \text{DeptHead} \} }
$$

**Step 2 — Group by LHS:**

- Group 1: $\{\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}\}$ → $R_1 = \{ \text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}, \text{ProjectName}, \text{EmpDept} \}$
- Group 2: $\{\text{EmpDept}\}$ → $R_2 = \{ \text{EmpDept}, \text{DeptHead} \}$

**Step 3 — Check Key Inclusion:** Candidate key is $\{$ EmpID, ProjectID $\}$, which is in $R_1$. ✅

**Step 4 — Subsumption Check:** $R_2 \subseteq R_1$? No (DeptHead $\notin R_1$). Keep both.

**Final 3NF Decomposition via Synthesis:**
$$
\boxed{ R_1(\text{EmpID}, \text{ProjectID}, \text{ProjectName}, \text{EmpDept}), \; R_2(\text{EmpDept}, \text{DeptHead}) }
$$

**Note:** The synthesis algorithm yields **fewer relations** (2) compared to the ad-hoc decomposition (3), demonstrating its efficiency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **transitive dependency**. Given a relation:
$$
R = \{ \text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}, \text{CourseName}, \text{FacultyID}, \text{FacultyDept} \}
$$
with FDs:
- $\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID} \rightarrow \text{FacultyID}$
- $\text{FacultyID} \rightarrow \text{FacultyDept}$

**Identify transitive dependencies and normalize to 3NF.**

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain how 3NF **differs from BCNF**. Show that a relation can be in 3NF but not in BCNF.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Transitive Dependency & 3NF Normalization [7 marks]

**Transitive Dependency Definition:** A functional dependency $X \rightarrow Z$ is transitive if there exists an attribute set $Y$ such that $X \rightarrow Y$, $Y \rightarrow Z$, and $Y \nrightarrow X$, with $Z$ being a non-prime attribute.

**Step 1 — Find Candidate Key:**

$\{\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}\}^+ = \{\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}, \text{FacultyID}, \text{FacultyDept}\} = R$ ✅

**Candidate Key:** $\{$ StudentID, CourseID $\}$

**Step 2 — Identify Transitive Dependency:**

$$
\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID} \rightarrow \text{FacultyID} \rightarrow \text{FacultyDept}
$$

The FD $\text{FacultyID} \rightarrow \text{FacultyDept}$ creates a **transitive dependency** where:
- LHS of the inner FD (`FacultyID`) is **not a superkey**.
- RHS (`FacultyDept`) is a **non-prime attribute**.

**❌ Violates 3NF.**

**Step 3 — Decompose to 3NF:**

- $R_1 = \{ \text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}, \text{FacultyID} \}$
- $R_2 = \{ \text{FacultyID}, \text{FacultyDept} \}$

**Step 4 — Lossless Join Check:**

$R_1 \cap R_2 = \{ \text{FacultyID} \}$. Is $\{ \text{FacultyID} \}^+ \supseteq R_2$?
$\{ \text{FacultyID} \}^+ = \{ \text{FacultyID}, \text{FacultyDept} \} = R_2$ ✅ **Lossless.**

**Step 5 — Dependency Preservation:** All FDs preserved. ✅

**Final 3NF Schema:**
$$
\boxed{ R_1(\text{StudentID}, \text{CourseID}, \text{FacultyID}), \quad R_2(\text{FacultyID}, \text{FacultyDept}) }
$$

#### Part (b) — 3NF vs BCNF [7 marks]

**Definition Recap:**
- **3NF:** Allows FD $X \rightarrow A$ if $A$ is a **prime attribute** (even if $X$ is not a superkey).
- **BCNF:** Does **not allow** this exception. $X$ must be a superkey for every non-trivial FD.

**Counter-Example Showing 3NF ⊄ BCNF:**

Consider relation $R = \{ \text{Student}, \text{Subject}, \text{Professor} \}$ with FDs:
1. $(\text{Student}, \text{Subject}) \rightarrow \text{Professor}$ — Each student has one professor per subject.
2. $\text{Professor} \rightarrow \text{Subject}$ — Each professor teaches only one subject.

**Candidate Keys:**
- $(\text{Student}, \text{Subject})$ — from FD1
- $(\text{Student}, \text{Professor})$ — from FD2 and FD1 (since Professor → Subject, so (Student, Professor) → (Student, Subject) → Professor)

**Prime Attributes:** Student, Subject, Professor (all three!)

**Test for 3NF:**
- FD1: LHS = (Student, Subject) is a superkey ✅
- FD2: LHS = Professor is **not** a superkey, RHS = Subject is a **prime attribute** ✅ → **Passes 3NF**

**Test for BCNF:**
- FD2: LHS = Professor is not a superkey ❌ → **Fails BCNF**

**Therefore, $R$ is in 3NF but NOT in BCNF.**

**Trade-off Insight:** If we decompose to BCNF:
- $R_1 = \{ \text{Student}, \text{Professor} \}$
- $R_2 = \{ \text{Professor}, \text{Subject} \}$

This is **lossless** but the FD `(Student, Subject) → Professor` is **not preserved** — it cannot be derived from FDs in $R_1$ and $R_2$ alone. Hence, BCNF sacrifices **dependency preservation**, while 3NF retains it.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in 3NF Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to verify 2NF first:** Many students jump directly to 3NF analysis without confirming the relation is in 2NF. A 2NF violation also disqualifies 3NF. **Always test 2NF → 3NF in sequence.**
> 
> 2. **Misidentifying prime vs non-prime attributes:** If a student incorrectly marks an attribute as prime or non-prime, all subsequent FD evaluations become wrong. **Always compute attribute closures and list candidate keys explicitly.**
> 
> 3. **Conflating 3NF with BCNF:** Saying "BCNF is the same as 3NF" costs 1–2 marks. **Clearly state the prime-attribute exception** in your 3NF definition.
> 
> 4. **Not verifying lossless join:** A decomposition without a lossless-join proof is incomplete. **Always cite Heath's Theorem** and compute the intersection closure.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring dependency preservation:** Synthesizing a 3NF schema but not verifying that all original FDs are representable loses marks.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the canonical cover computation:** In synthesis algorithm questions, students often skip the extraneous-attribute removal step. **Show the full canonical cover derivation.**
> 
> 7. **Confusing "transitive" with "partial" dependency:** Partial dependency = non-key attr depends on **part** of composite key. Transitive dependency = non-key attr depends on **another non-key attr**. Mixing these up is a frequent error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Third Normal Form (3NF):**

- **Core Definition:** $R$ is in 3NF if it is in 2NF and **no non-prime attribute** is transitively dependent on any **candidate key**.

- **Three-Condition Test:** For every FD $X \rightarrow A$:
  - (i) $A \subseteq X$ (trivial), **OR**
  - (ii) $X$ is a superkey, **OR**
  - (iii) $A$ is a **prime attribute**.

- **BCNF Difference:** 3NF allows condition (iii); BCNF does not. Hence **BCNF ⊂ 3NF**.

- **Transitive Dependency:** $X \rightarrow Y \rightarrow Z$, where $Y$ is non-superkey and $Z$ is non-prime.

- **Heath's Theorem (Lossless Join):** $(R_1 \cap R_2)$ must functionally determine $R_1$ or $R_2$.

- **Synthesis Algorithm (Bernstein):** 5 steps — Canonical Cover → Group by LHS → Create Relations → Remove Subsumed → Add Key Relation.

- **3NF is Always Achievable:** A **lossless-join, dependency-preserving** 3NF decomposition always exists (synthesis algorithm guarantees it).

- **3NF is the Industry Standard:** Used in OLTP systems (banking, inventory, CRM).

- **Hierarchy Reminder:** 1NF ⊂ 2NF ⊂ 3NF ⊂ ... (BCNF may not equal 3NF in membership, but every BCNF is in 3NF).

- **Prime Attribute:** An attribute that appears in **at least one** candidate key.

- **Non-Prime Attribute:** An attribute that does **not** appear in any candidate key.

- **Key Memory Trick:** "**3NF = No Transitives on Non-Primes**" — if a non-prime determines a non-prime, split it.

- **Common Exam Pattern:** Given relation + FDs → find CK → identify primes/non-primes → test 3NF → decompose → verify lossless & dependency-preserving.

- **Canonical Cover Steps:** Singleton RHS → Remove extraneous LHS → Remove redundant FDs. Repeat until stable.

- **Equi-join Reminder:** Lossless join means the natural join of $R_1$ and $R_2$ produces exactly the original relation $R$ — no extra, no missing tuples.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boyce Codd Normal Form (BCNF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Boyce Codd Normal Form (BCNF) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of BCNF > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept)** > A relational schema **R** is in **Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)** if and only if, for **every non-trivial functional dependency** of the form $X \rightarrow Y$ that holds in **R**, the left-hand side attribute set **X** must be a **superkey** of relation **R**. Mathematically, this can be expressed as: $$\forall \; X \rightarrow Y \; \text{(non-trivial FD in R)} : \; X^{+} \supseteq R$$ where $X^{+}$ denotes the **closure** of attribute set **X** under the given set of functional dependencies, and $R$ denotes the set of all attributes in the relation. ### 1.2 Terminology Refresher | Term | Definition | Example | |------|------------|---------| | **Superkey** | A set of attributes whose values uniquely identify a tuple in the relation | `{RollNo, CourseID}` | | **Candidate Key** | A *minimal* superkey (no proper subset is a superkey) | `{RollNo, CourseID}` (if no subset works) | | **Determinant** | The left-hand side (X) of a non-trivial FD | In `CourseID → Instructor`, CourseID is the determinant | | **Non-trivial FD** | An FD where the RHS is **not** a subset of the LHS | `RollNo → Name` (Name not in RollNo) | | **Trivial FD** | An FD where the RHS is always a subset of the LHS | `RollNo, Name → Name` | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Classroom Assignment" Intuition > [!NOTE] **Plain-English Analogy** > Imagine a school where each **Subject** is taught by exactly **one Professor** (a strict school rule). So `Subject → Professor`. Now suppose a **Student** enrolls in a subject, and we store records as `(Student, Subject, Professor)`. > > - A student taking "DBMS" must have Prof. "Sharma". > - Prof. Sharma teaches ONLY "DBMS". > > Here, `Professor → Subject` is a real-world rule. **BCNF** essentially says: *"If any non-key attribute (like Professor or Subject) determines another attribute, then that determining attribute must itself be powerful enough to uniquely identify an entire row."* > > If it can't, the table is mixing up two different real-world concepts and must be **split**. ### 1.4 BCNF vs. 3NF — The Critical Distinction > [!IMPORTANT] **BCNF = Stricter 3NF** > - **3NF** allows a non-superkey determinant **iff** the RHS is a **prime attribute** (part of some candidate key). > - **BCNF** has **no such exception** — *every* non-trivial FD's LHS must be a superkey. > - **Every BCNF relation is in 3NF, but NOT every 3NF relation is in BCNF.** This distinction is the **most frequently tested KTU question** on this topic. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Conditions for BCNF (Formal Logic) A relation schema **R** with functional dependency set **F** is in **BCNF** if and only if **both** of the following hold: 1. **Superkey Rule**: For every non-trivial FD $X \rightarrow Y \in F^{+}$, the attribute set $X$ is a **superkey** of $R$. 2. **No Anomalous Determinants**: There is no attribute (or set of attributes) other than a superkey that functionally determines any other attribute. ### 2.2 Why BCNF? — Eliminating All Remaining Anomalies BCNF was proposed by **Raymond F. Boyce** and **Edgar F. Codd** in 1974 as a stricter refinement of 3NF. It targets the rare but important cases where 3NF still permits: | Anomaly Type | Cause | Effect | |--------------|-------|--------| | **Update Anomaly** | Same info stored redundantly | Changing instructor of "DBMS" requires multiple updates | | **Insertion Anomaly** | Cannot insert new subject without a student | Data integrity compromised | | **Deletion Anomaly** | Deleting last student of a subject loses info about the subject | Information loss | ### 2.3 The BCNF Decomposition Algorithm (KTU High-Yield) Given a relation **R** with a set of functional dependencies **F**, the standard KTU-tested algorithm to decompose into BCNF is: > [!NOTE] **Algorithm: BCNF_Decomposition(R, F)** > 1. Compute $F^{+}$ (closure of F). > 2. If R is already in BCNF, return R. > 3. Else, find a non-trivial FD $X \rightarrow Y$ in $F^{+}$ that **violates BCNF** (i.e., X is not a superkey of R). > 4. Decompose R into two relations: > - $R_1 = X \cup Y$ > - $R_2 = R - (Y - X)$ > 5. Recursively apply the algorithm to $R_1$ (with projected FDs) and $R_2$ (with projected FDs). > 6. Return the union of all resulting relations. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Property / Condition | Mathematical Form | KTU Exam Use | |---|----------------------|-------------------|---------------| | 1 | BCNF Condition | $X \rightarrow Y \Rightarrow X \supseteq K$ for some candidate key K | Verifying BCNF | | 2 | Superkey Test | $X^{+} \supseteq R$ | Checking if LHS is superkey | | 3 | Lossless Join Test (Heath's Theorem) | $R \cap (R - R) \rightarrow (R - R) \;\vert\; \text{or} \;\vert\; R - R \rightarrow R$ | Verifying decomposition | | 4 | Dependency Preservation | $(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \ldots \cup F_n)^{+} = F^{+}$ | Checking if FDs preserved | | 5 | Attribute Closure | $X^{+} = X \cup \{A \;\vert\; X \rightarrow A \in F^{+}\}$ | Finding candidate keys | | 6 | Projected FD (for $R_i \subseteq R$) | $\pi_{R_i}(F) = \{X \rightarrow Y \;\vert\; X \subseteq R_i, Y \subseteq R_i, X \rightarrow Y \in F^{+}\}$ | Recursive decomposition | > [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Property** > The BCNF decomposition algorithm **always preserves lossless join**, but it **does NOT always preserve dependencies**. This is a *trade-off* that KTU frequently tests. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility BCNF is heavily used in: - **Banking Systems**: Account numbers must be superkeys for any FD like `AccountNo → Balance`. - **Hospital Information Systems**: `PatientID, TestCode → Result` (composite superkey). - **E-Commerce Databases**: `OrderID, ProductID → Quantity, Price`. - **Telecom Billing**: `PhoneNo, CallTimestamp → Duration`. In each case, **every determinant in the schema is a genuine, unique identifier**, eliminating logical redundancy at the schema design level itself. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples ### 3.1 Worked Example — The Classic Student-Subject-Professor Relation > [!NOTE] **Problem Setup** > Consider a relation **R(Student, Subject, Professor)** with the following real-world rules (FDs): > 1. **FD1**: $\{Student, Subject\} \rightarrow Professor$ — *"A student has exactly one professor for each subject."* > 2. **FD2**: $Professor \rightarrow Subject$ — *"Each professor teaches exactly one subject."* #### Step 1: Find the Candidate Keys Compute closures: $$\{Student, Subject\}^{+} = \{Student, Subject, Professor\} = R$$ $$\{Student, Professor\}^{+} = \{Student, Professor, Subject\} = R$$ $$\{Professor\}^{+} = \{Professor, Subject\} \neq R$$ Therefore, the **candidate keys** are: - $K_1 = \{Student, Subject\}$ - $K_2 = \{Student, Professor\}$ #### Step 2: Test BCNF Compliance Check each non-trivial FD: | FD | LHS | Is LHS a superkey? | BCNF Violation? | |----|-----|---------------------|-----------------| | $FD1$: {Student, Subject} → Professor | {Student, Subject} | ✅ Yes (Candidate Key) | No | | $FD2$: Professor → Subject | {Professor} | ❌ No (Not even prime) | **YES** | **Conclusion**: R is in **3NF** (because `Subject` is a prime attribute — it appears in $K_1$) but **violates BCNF** due to FD2. #### Step 3: Apply BCNF Decomposition Algorithm **Iteration 1**: Pick violating FD: $Professor \rightarrow Subject$. Decompose R: $$R_1 = X \cup Y = \{Professor\} \cup \{Subject\} = \{Professor, Subject\}$$ $$R_2 = R - (Y - X) = \{Student, Subject, Professor\} - (\{Subject\} - \{Professor\})$$ $$R_2 = \{Student, Subject, Professor\} - \{Subject\} = \{Student, Professor\}$$ #### Step 4: Verify Both Sub-Relations are in BCNF **Check R1(Professor, Subject)**: - FDs in R1: $Professor \rightarrow Subject$ holds. - Is `Professor` a superkey of R1? - $\{Professor\}^{+} = \{Professor, Subject\} = R_1$ ✅ - **R1 is in BCNF** ✅ **Check R2(Student, Professor)**: - FDs in R2: Only the key constraint (no non-trivial FDs other than trivial). - **R2 is trivially in BCNF** ✅ #### Step 5: Final Decomposed Schema $$\boxed{R_1(\underline{Professor}, Subject) \quad \text{and} \quad R_2(\underline{Student}, \underline{Professor})}$$ Both relations are in **BCNF**, the decomposition is **lossless**, but the FD $\{Student, Subject\} \rightarrow Professor$ is **NOT preserved** in this decomposition (it becomes a join dependency). ### 3.2 Second Worked Example — Library Books > [!NOTE] **Problem** > Relation **R(BookID, Title, AuthorID, Publisher)** with FDs: > - $FD1: BookID \rightarrow Title, AuthorID, Publisher$ > - $FD2: AuthorID \rightarrow Publisher$ (each author has one primary publisher) **Step 1: Find Candidate Keys** - $\{BookID\}^{+} = \{BookID, Title, AuthorID, Publisher\} = R$ ✅ - **Candidate Key**: `{BookID}` only. **Step 2: BCNF Check** | FD | LHS Superkey? | BCNF OK? | |----|---------------|----------| | $BookID \rightarrow Title, AuthorID, Publisher$ | Yes | ✅ | | $AuthorID \rightarrow Publisher$ | No | ❌ Violates BCNF | **Step 3: Decomposition using violating FD** $$R_1 = \{AuthorID, Publisher\}$$ $$R_2 = \{BookID, Title, AuthorID\}$$ **Step 4: Verification** - $R_1$: $\{AuthorID\}^{+} = \{AuthorID, Publisher\}$ — AuthorID is superkey → **BCNF** ✅ - $R_2$: $\{BookID\}^{+} = \{BookID, Title, AuthorID\}$ — BookID is superkey → **BCNF** ✅ $$\boxed{R_1(\underline{AuthorID}, Publisher), \quad R_2(\underline{BookID}, Title, \underline{AuthorID})}$$ ### 3.3 Summary of Decomposition Properties | Property | Example 1 (Student-Subject-Professor) | Example 2 (Library) | |----------|--------------------------------------|---------------------| | Lossless Join | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | Dependency Preserving | ❌ No (FD1 lost) | ✅ Yes | | All Relations in BCNF | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | > [!IMPORTANT] **Key Takeaway from Worked Examples** > - BCNF decomposition is **always lossless**. > - It is **not always dependency-preserving** — this is an *acceptable trade-off* in practice. > - When dependency preservation is mandatory, designers may opt to remain in **3NF** instead of BCNF. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Normal Form Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["UNNORMALIZED FORM
Raw data with repetitions"] --> B["FIRST NORMAL FORM 1NF
Atomic values only"] B --> C["SECOND NORMAL FORM 2NF
1NF plus no partial dependencies"] C --> D["THIRD NORMAL FORM 3NF
2NF plus no transitive dependencies"] D --> E["BOYCE CODD NORMAL FORM BCNF
3NF plus every determinant is a superkey"] E --> F["FOURTH NORMAL FORM 4NF
BCNF plus no multi-valued dependencies"] F --> G["FIFTH NORMAL FORM 5NF
4NF plus no join dependencies"] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#FF1493,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style F fill:#DA70D6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#BA55D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram** > BCNF sits between 3NF and 4NF. The arrow from D to E is highlighted because it is the *focus topic* of this module. The relationship is **strict containment**: BCNF ⊂ 3NF ⊂ 2NF ⊂ 1NF. ### 4.2 BCNF Decomposition Algorithm Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD P0["START: Relation R and FD set F"] --> P1["Compute F closure F plus"] P1 --> P2{"Is R in BCNF?
Check every non-trivial FD"} P2 -->|YES| P3["RETURN R
Already BCNF"] P2 -->|NO| P4["Pick a violating FD
X determines Y
X is not a superkey"] P4 --> P5["Decompose R into:
R1 equals X union Y
R2 equals R minus Y minus X"] P5 --> P6["Recurse on R1 with projected FDs"] P5 --> P7["Recurse on R2 with projected FDs"] P6 --> P8{"R1 in BCNF?"} P7 --> P9{"R2 in BCNF?"} P8 -->|NO| P4 P8 -->|YES| P10["Keep R1"] P9 -->|NO| P4 P9 -->|YES| P11["Keep R2"] P10 --> P12["RETURN R1 union R2
Final BCNF schema"] P11 --> P12 style P0 fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px style P3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px style P12 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px style P4 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Functional Dependency Decision Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["FD: X determines Y
in Relation R"] --> B{"Is FD trivial?
Y subset of X"} B -->|YES| C["IGNORE
Always holds"] B -->|NO| D{"Is X a superkey?
X plus equals R"} D -->|YES| E["BCNF COMPLIANT
No violation"] D -->|NO| F{"Is Y a prime
attribute in 3NF?"} F -->|YES| G["3NF OK
But BCNF VIOLATION"] F -->|NO| H["3NF VIOLATION
BCNF ALSO VIOLATED"] G --> I["DECOMPOSE
using BCNF algorithm"] H --> I style C fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] **How to Read This Diagram** > - **Green boxes**: No action needed, schema is fine. > - **Yellow box**: Relation is in 3NF but violates BCNF (the classic KTU case). > - **Red box**: Violates both 3NF and BCNF. > - **Blue box**: Apply the BCNF decomposition algorithm from Section 4.2. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q:** Define Boyce-Codd Normal Form. How is it different from 3NF? **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** A relation schema R is in **Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)** if for every non-trivial functional dependency $X \rightarrow Y$ holding in R, **X must be a superkey** of R. **Difference from 3NF (1 mark):** | Aspect | 3NF | BCNF | |--------|-----|------| | Allowance for prime attributes | Permits non-superkey determinant if RHS is prime attribute | **No exception** — every determinant must be a superkey | | Strictness | Looser | Stricter | | Relationship | $BCNF \subset 3NF$ | $3NF \supset BCNF$ | **Conclusion:** Every BCNF relation is in 3NF, but the converse is not always true. --- #### Question 2 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q:** State the conditions for a relation to be in BCNF. Is BCNF decomposition always dependency-preserving? Justify. **Model Answer:** **Conditions (2 marks):** 1. The relation must be in 3NF (atomic values, no partial or transitive dependencies). 2. For every non-trivial FD $X \rightarrow Y$ in R, X must be a **superkey**. **Dependency Preservation (1 mark):** **No**, BCNF decomposition is **not always dependency-preserving**. For example, in the relation `R(Student, Subject, Professor)` with FDs `{Student, Subject} → Professor` and `Professor → Subject`, the BCNF decomposition into `R1(Professor, Subject)` and `R2(Student, Professor)` **cannot enforce** the FD `{Student, Subject} → Professor` without a join. However, BCNF decomposition is **always lossless**. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Q:** Consider the relation `R(A, B, C, D, E, F)` with the following functional dependencies: $$AB \rightarrow C, \quad C \rightarrow D, \quad D \rightarrow E, \quad E \rightarrow F, \quad F \rightarrow B$$ **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Find all the candidate keys of R and determine the highest normal form that R satisfies. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Decompose R into BCNF. Is the decomposition lossless and dependency-preserving? --- ##### Part (a) Model Solution — Finding Keys and Normal Form **Step 1: Compute Attribute Closures** Attributes on LHS of FDs: {A, B, C, D, E, F}. Compute closures: $\{A\}^{+}$: A → (no FD starts with A) → **A**. Not a key. $\{A, B\}^{+}$: A,B → C (FD1) → {A,B,C} → D (FD2) → {A,B,C,D} → E (FD3) → {A,B,C,D,E} → F (FD4) → {A,B,C,D,E,F} ✅ Therefore, **{A, B} is a superkey**. No subset of {A, B} alone is a superkey (we checked A and B individually and they don't work). Hence **{A, B} is a candidate key**. **Other single attributes:** - $\{C\}^{+} = \{C,D,E,F,B\}$ → missing A → not key - $\{D\}^{+} = \{D,E,F,B\}$ → missing A, C → not key - $\{E\}^{+} = \{E,F,B\}$ → not key - $\{F\}^{+} = \{F,B\}$ → not key **Conclusion:** **{A, B} is the only candidate key.** **Step 2: Identify Prime Attributes** - Prime attribute: **A** and **B** (only attributes in candidate key). - Non-prime: **C, D, E, F**. **Step 3: Test Normal Forms** | FD | LHS Superkey? | RHS Prime? | 2NF/3NF/BCNF Status | |----|---------------|------------|---------------------| | $AB \rightarrow C$ | Yes | - | ✅ BCNF | | $C \rightarrow D$ | No | No (D non-prime) | ❌ 3NF Violation | | $D \rightarrow E$ | No | No | ❌ 3NF Violation | | $E \rightarrow F$ | No | No | ❌ 3NF Violation | | $F \rightarrow B$ | No | **Yes** (B is prime) | ⚠️ 3NF OK, BCNF Violation | **Highest Normal Form: 1NF** (multiple transitive dependencies). --- ##### Part (b) Model Solution — BCNF Decomposition **Iteration 1:** Pick violating FD $C \rightarrow D$. - $R_1 = C \cup D = \{C, D\}$ - $R_2 = R - (D - C) = \{A,B,C,E,F\}$ **Check R1(C,D):** Only FD is $C \rightarrow D$. C is superkey of R1. ✅ **BCNF** **Check R2(A,B,C,E,F):** Project FDs onto R2: - $AB \rightarrow C$ - $E \rightarrow F$ - $F \rightarrow B$ (still in R2) **Iteration 2:** Pick violating FD $E \rightarrow F$ in R2. - $R_{2a} = \{E, F\}$ - $R_{2b} = \{A, B, C, E\}$ **Check R2a(E,F):** $E \rightarrow F$, E is superkey. ✅ **BCNF** **Check R2b(A,B,C,E):** Project FDs: $AB \rightarrow C$. (E and F no longer together). Is {A,B} superkey of R2b? $\{A,B\}^{+} = \{A,B,C\}$. E is **not determined**. So {A,B} is superkey of R2b ✅. **BCNF**. **Final BCNF Decomposition:** $$\boxed{R_1(C, D), \quad R_{2a}(E, F), \quad R_{2b}(A, B, C, E)}$$ **Properties:** - ✅ **Lossless Join** (always guaranteed by BCNF algorithm using Heath's theorem) - ❌ **Dependency Preservation:** FD $F \rightarrow B$ is **lost** (F is in R2a, B is in R2b — no single relation contains both). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** Given the relation `R(EmpID, ProjectID, DeptID, DeptName, Hours)` with FDs: $$EmpID, ProjectID \rightarrow Hours, \quad ProjectID \rightarrow DeptID, \quad DeptID \rightarrow DeptName$$ **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Identify the candidate keys and determine the normal form of R. Justify your answer. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Apply BCNF decomposition algorithm. Verify each resulting relation is in BCNF and comment on dependency preservation. --- ##### Part (a) Model Solution **Step 1: Find Candidate Keys** $\{EmpID, ProjectID\}^{+} = \{EmpID, ProjectID, Hours, DeptID, DeptName\} = R$ ✅ Check subsets: - $\{EmpID\}^{+} = \{EmpID\}$ — No FD starts with EmpID. - $\{ProjectID\}^{+} = \{ProjectID, DeptID, DeptName\}$ — Missing EmpID, Hours. So **{EmpID, ProjectID}** is the **only candidate key**. **Prime attributes:** EmpID, ProjectID. **Non-prime attributes:** DeptID, DeptName, Hours. **Step 2: Normal Form Check** | FD | LHS Superkey? | Violation | |----|---------------|-----------| | $\{EmpID, ProjectID\} \rightarrow Hours$ | Yes | ✅ BCNF OK | | $ProjectID \rightarrow DeptID$ | No | ❌ BCNF Violation (and 3NF — DeptID is non-prime) | | $DeptID \rightarrow DeptName$ | No | ❌ BCNF Violation (and 3NF — DeptName is non-prime) | **Highest Normal Form: 1NF** (multiple transitive dependencies exist). --- ##### Part (b) Model Solution — BCNF Decomposition **Iteration 1:** Violating FD: $ProjectID \rightarrow DeptID$. - $R_1 = \{ProjectID, DeptID\}$ - $R_2 = R - (DeptID - ProjectID) = \{EmpID, ProjectID, DeptName, Hours\}$ **Check R1(ProjectID, DeptID):** - FDs: $ProjectID \rightarrow DeptID$. ProjectID is superkey of R1. ✅ **BCNF** **Check R2(EmpID, ProjectID, DeptName, Hours):** - FDs: $\{EmpID, ProjectID\} \rightarrow Hours$ (still valid) - $DeptID$ is removed, so $DeptID \rightarrow DeptName$ no longer applies. $\{EmpID, ProjectID\}^{+} = \{EmpID, ProjectID, Hours\}$ — Not all of R2 (DeptName missing). But check if there is any other FD: **No**. So {EmpID, ProjectID} is superkey of R2. ✅ **BCNF**. **Iteration 2:** Check R2 again — no other violating FDs. **Final BCNF Schema:** $$\boxed{R_1(\underline{ProjectID}, DeptID), \quad R_2(\underline{EmpID}, \underline{ProjectID}, DeptName, Hours)}$$ **Dependency Preservation Analysis:** - $ProjectID \rightarrow DeptID$ ✅ preserved in R1 - $DeptID \rightarrow DeptName$ ❌ **LOST** (DeptID is in R1, DeptName is in R2) **Conclusion:** Decomposition is **lossless but not dependency-preserving**. To enforce the lost FD, the application layer must perform a join — costly at runtime. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks** > 1. **Confusing BCNF with 3NF**: Many students write "3NF and BCNF are the same." They are NOT. BCNF is strictly stricter. Examiner deducts **1–2 marks** immediately. > 2. **Forgetting to verify candidate keys**: Before declaring a violation, students must PROVE the LHS is not a superkey. Always compute attribute closure. > 3. **Ignoring dependency preservation**: KTU specifically asks about this trade-off. Simply decomposing without commenting on preservation loses **2 marks**. > 4. **Wrong decomposition formula**: $R_2 = R - (Y - X)$, NOT $R_2 = R - Y$. The intersection X must remain in both relations to guarantee lossless join. > 5. **Not showing recursive steps**: After first decomposition, you MUST verify each sub-relation. Stopping after one iteration loses **3 marks**. > 6. **Writing "lossless" without showing the proof**: State which Heath's theorem condition is satisfied: $R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_1$ or $R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_2$. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist** - ✅ **BCNF Definition**: For every non-trivial FD $X \rightarrow Y$, X is a **superkey** of R. - ✅ **BCNF ⊂ 3NF ⊂ 2NF ⊂ 1NF** (strict containment at each level). - ✅ **Every BCNF relation is in 3NF**, but **NOT vice versa**. - ✅ **Algorithm**: Find violating FD $X \rightarrow Y$ → Decompose into $X \cup Y$ and $R - (Y - X)$ → Recurse. - ✅ **Lossless Join**: Always guaranteed by BCNF decomposition (by Heath's theorem). - ⚠️ **Dependency Preservation**: **NOT always guaranteed** — this is the key trade-off. - ✅ **3NF allows prime attribute exceptions**; BCNF does **not**. - ✅ Always compute **attribute closures** to verify superkey status. - ✅ Common KTU marks distribution: Part A = 2 × 3 = 6 marks, Part B = 1 × 14 = 14 marks (with internal choice). - ✅ Use **Heath's Theorem** to prove lossless join in viva questions. - ✅ Real-world examples: Library systems, hospital records, banking, e-commerce — know at least one.

topics.title: Lossless Join Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lossless Join Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A decomposition of a relation schema $R$ into relation schemas $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$ is called a **Lossless Join Decomposition** (also called *non-additive join decomposition*) if and only if for every legal instance $r$ of $R$, the natural join of its projections on the decomposed schemas produces the **exact original relation** — i.e., no spurious (extra) tuples and no missing tuples are introduced.

Mathematically, the decomposition $(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n)$ of $R$ is **lossless** if and only if:

$$\pi_{R_1}(r) \bowtie \pi_{R_2}(r) \bowtie \cdots \bowtie \pi_{R_n}(r) = r$$

for every relation state $r$ that is a legal instance satisfying the given set of functional dependencies $F$. Equivalently, no spurious tuples are generated, meaning:

$$r = \pi_{R_1}(r) \bowtie \pi_{R_2}(r) \bowtie \cdots \bowtie \pi_{R_n}(r)$$

The converse — that joining produces *more* tuples than the original — is called a **Lossy (or Additive) Decomposition**, which is strictly undesirable in relational database design.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Photocopy & Shuffle" Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition Builder):**
> Imagine a class register (a single big sheet) containing columns: **Roll No, Name, Subject, Marks, Teacher**. The principal tears this sheet into two smaller sheets:
> - **Sheet A** = Roll No, Name, Subject, Marks
> - **Sheet B** = Subject, Teacher
>
> Now, if the teacher later glues them back together using the common column **Subject**, the rows should match **exactly one-to-one** with the original — every student gets back exactly one teacher, and no "ghost" students appear. This perfect reconstruction = **Lossless Join**.
>
> But suppose the principal tore it differently:
> - **Sheet A** = Roll No, Name, Subject
> - **Sheet B** = Subject, Marks
>
> Joining back on Subject might now wrongly pair a student's name with the marks of *another student studying the same subject* — producing **spurious tuples**. This = **Lossy Decomposition**.

The crux is the **common attribute (Subject)** acting as a "glue" — and the strength of that glue is determined by **functional dependencies**.

---

## 1.3 Why Lossless Join Decomposition Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Why this is critical):**
> The entire motivation for **Database Normalization** is to split large, anomaly-prone tables into smaller, well-structured ones. However, splitting is pointless (and harmful) if we cannot **reliably reconstruct** the original data. Lossless join is the **non-negotiable property** every good decomposition must satisfy.

The three classical "goodness" criteria for any decomposition are:
1. **Lossless Join** (no spurious tuples) — *Mandatory, never compromised.*
2. **Dependency Preservation** (all FDs remain enforceable locally) — *Highly desirable.*
3. **Lack of Redundancy** (achieved via Normal Forms like 3NF, BCNF) — *Design goal.*

If (1) fails, the database produces **incorrect query results** — the worst possible failure mode.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Formal Tests for Lossless Join Decomposition

### Test 1 — Binary Decomposition Theorem (Heath's Theorem Variant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heath's Theorem for Binary Decompositions:**
> Let $R$ be a relation schema with a set of functional dependencies $F$. A decomposition of $R$ into $R_1$ and $R_2$ is **lossless** if and only if at least one of the following FDs holds in $F^+$:
>
> $$R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_1 \quad \text{or} \quad R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_2$$

Where:
- $R_1 \cap R_2$ is the set of **common attributes** shared between the two decomposed schemas.
- The arrow $\rightarrow$ denotes a functional dependency in the closure $F^+$.

In simpler terms: **the common attributes must functionally determine at least one of the two decomposed schemas**. The shared column must act as a "primary key" for at least one side.

### Test 2 — General n-ary Decomposition (Chase Test / Tableau Method)

When decomposing into more than two relations, we use the **Chase Test**, which is the universal algorithmic verification. It operates on a **chase tableau** (a matrix) initialized with symbolic variables, then iteratively applies FDs until either:
- A row becomes entirely composed of distinguished variables (subscripted `a`'s) → **Lossless**.
- No further changes occur and no row is all distinguished → **Lossy**.

### Test 3 — Sufficient Condition Using Common Attributes

If $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1$ **OR** $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_2$ holds in $F^+$, the join is guaranteed lossless. This is a *sufficient* condition; the converse is not always necessary for non-binary decompositions.

---

## 2.2 Why Lossy Decompositions Generate Spurious Tuples

> [!NOTE]
> **The Spurious Tuple Mechanism:**
> When we take the natural join $\pi_{R_1}(r) \bowtie \pi_{R_2}(r)$, the join equates tuples that share equal values on $R_1 \cap R_2$. If the common attributes do **not** functionally determine either side, then multiple distinct tuples on each side can match — producing **Cartesian-like combinations** that never existed in $r$.

If $|r| = n$ tuples and the join produces $m > n$ tuples, then $(m - n)$ **spurious (phantom) tuples** have been manufactured, corrupting query results.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Reference Table for Board Examinations:**

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Lossless Join Definition | $\pi_{R_1}(r) \bowtie \pi_{R_2}(r) = r$ | Must hold for **every** legal instance $r$ |
| Binary Lossless Condition (Heath) | $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1$  OR  $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_2$ | Equivalent to: common attrs are a **superkey** of at least one $R_i$ |
| General Test (n-ary) | **Chase Test** on tableau | Only algorithmic method that always works |
| Lossy Decomposition | $\pi_{R_1}(r) \bowtie \pi_{R_2}(r) \supset r$ | Strict superset — extra tuples appear |
| Spurious Tuples Count | $\vert \pi_{R_1}(r) \bowtie \pi_{R_2}(r) \vert - \vert r \vert$ | Difference = number of phantom rows |
| Attribute Membership | $R_1 \cap R_2 \subseteq R_1$ and $R_1 \cap R_2 \subseteq R_2$ | Always true by decomposition |
| Lossless + Dependency Preservation | Both must hold for "ideal" decomposition | Often a trade-off exists in BCNF |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> In real database systems (Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL), lossless join is the bedrock of:
> - **Schema Refactoring** during application migrations.
> - **Horizontal/Vertical Partitioning** for distributed databases (e.g., sharding in Cassandra, MongoDB).
> - **Data Warehousing** — star/snowflake schemas rely on lossless joins between fact and dimension tables.
> - **ETL Pipelines** — incorrect joins in Spark/Hadoop produce duplicate analytical rows.

---

## 2.4 Chase Test Algorithm — Stepwise Logic

The Chase Test is the **gold-standard algorithmic verification** mandated in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

**Step-by-step logic of the Chase:**

1. **Construct the Chase Tableau:** Create an $n \times m$ matrix, where $n$ = number of decomposed relations (rows) and $m$ = number of attributes in $R$ (columns).
2. **Initialize Cells:** For each cell $(i, j)$:
   - If attribute $A_j \in R_i$ → write $a_j$ (distinguished variable).
   - Otherwise → write $b_{ij}$ (non-distinguished variable).
3. **Apply FDs Iteratively:** For each FD $X \rightarrow Y$ in $F$:
   - Find two rows $t_1, t_2$ where the symbols on $X$ are **identical**.
   - For each attribute $A \in Y$: if either cell has a distinguished variable $a_k$, set **both** cells to that $a_k$. Otherwise, replace both with one of the non-distinguished symbols.
4. **Termination Check:** Repeat until no cell changes.
5. **Verdict:** If **any row** is composed entirely of distinguished variables $\{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_m\}$, the decomposition is **Lossless**. Otherwise, it is **Lossy**.

The distinguished variables $\{a_j\}$ represent "original, untouched" symbols, while $b_{ij}$ represents "potentially spurious" symbols. A fully distinguished row means *that one relation alone can recover the entire original tuple* — the gold standard.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Binary Decomposition Verification (Heath's Test)

**Problem Setup:**
Given relation schema $R(A, B, C, D, E)$ with FDs:
$$F = \{ A \rightarrow BC, \; CD \rightarrow E, \; B \rightarrow D, \; E \rightarrow A \}$$

Decompose into:
$$R_1(A, B, C, D) \quad \text{and} \quad R_2(A, E)$$

**Step 1 — Compute the Common Attributes:**
$$R_1 \cap R_2 = \{ A \}$$

**Step 2 — Check Functional Dependency from Common Attributes:**

We need to verify if either $A \rightarrow R_1$ or $A \rightarrow R_2$ holds in $F^+$.

- Check $A \rightarrow R_2$: Since $A \rightarrow BC$ (given) and we need $A \rightarrow E$. Compute $A^+$:
  - $A^+ = \{A\}$
  - Add $B, C$ via $A \rightarrow BC$: $A^+ = \{A, B, C\}$
  - Add $D$ via $B \rightarrow D$: $A^+ = \{A, B, C, D\}$
  - Does $E \in A^+$? No. So $A \rightarrow E$ does **not** hold.

- Check $A \rightarrow R_1$: $R_1 = \{A, B, C, D\}$. From above, $A^+ = \{A, B, C, D\}$. So $A \rightarrow ABCD$ holds!

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**
Since $(R_1 \cap R_2) = \{A\}$ functionally determines $R_1$, by Heath's theorem the decomposition is **Lossless**.

$$\boxed{\text{Decomposition } (R_1, R_2) \text{ is Lossless Join.}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Chase Test for n-ary Decomposition
