# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the terms *physical record* and *logical record*. Explain why the distinction between them is essential in database design.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

A **logical record** is a collection of related data items treated as a single unit from the *user's* perspective — typically a tuple in a relation. A **physical record** (or **block**) is the unit of data transfer between main memory and secondary storage — it is determined by the storage hardware and operating system.

The distinction is essential because:
1. The **logical view** is what applications and queries operate on (declarative).
2. The **physical view** determines **I/O cost**, **buffer pool behaviour**, and **storage efficiency**.
3. **Decoupling** allows the DBMS to reorganize physical storage (e.g., defragment, reblock) without altering the logical schema or affecting applications — this is the principle of **physical data independence**.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the *blocking factor* of a file? State its formula for fixed-length unspanned records.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

The **blocking factor** ($bfr$) of a file is the **average number of logical records stored in one physical record (block)**. It measures how efficiently a block is packed with logical data.

For **fixed-length, unspanned** records of size $R$ bytes stored in a block of size $B$ bytes:
$$bfr = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{R} \right\rfloor$$

This is the **maximum number of *whole* logical records** that can fit into a single block without splitting any record across block boundaries.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]` — **(Choice 1)**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **physical and logical records** in a database system. Differentiate between **spanned** and **unspanned** record organization with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A file has $r = 30{,}000$ fixed-length logical records of size $R = 180$ bytes each. The disk block size is $B = 2400$ bytes. Compute the **blocking factor**, **number of blocks**, **wasted bytes per block**, and **space utilization ratio** for both *unspanned* and *spanned* organization. Comment on which is more efficient and why. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Physical vs. Logical Records** *(3 marks)*

A **logical record** is the unit of data as perceived by the application — a single tuple containing the values of all attributes of one entity. For example, in a `STUDENT` relation, one student's complete row (roll number, name, age, branch, marks) forms one logical record.

A **physical record** (or **block**) is the *smallest unit of data transfer* between main memory and disk. The DBMS fetches or writes **entire blocks**, never individual tuples. A block is the unit of I/O and is constrained by hardware geometry (sector size, page size).

The DBMS **buffer manager** maps logical record references to physical block addresses. The **physical data independence** principle guarantees that changes to physical record layout do not affect the logical schema or application programs.

**Spanned vs. Unspanned Organization** *(4 marks)*

In **unspanned** organization, a logical record is stored entirely within one block. If a record is too large to fit in the remaining space of a block, it is moved to the **next block**, leaving the leftover space unused (internal fragmentation). The blocking factor is:
$$bfr_{\text{unspanned}} = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{R} \right\rfloor$$

```
+-------------------+-------------------+-------------------+
| Block 1           | Block 2           | Block 3           |
| R1 | R2 | R3 | ** | R4 | R5 | R6 | ** | R7 | R8 | ... |
+-------------------+-------------------+-------------------+
** = unused space (wasted)
```

In **spanned** organization, a record that does not fit in the remaining space is **split** into a head (stored at the end of the current block) and a tail (stored at the beginning of the next block). A **pointer field** in the first block locates the tail. The blocking factor is:
$$bfr_{\text{spanned}} = \left\lceil \frac{B}{R} \right\rceil$$

```
+-------------------+-------------------+-------------------+
| Block 1           | Block 2           | Block 3           |
| R1 | R2 | R3a|--->| R3b | R4 | R5 | R6a|--->| R6b | R7 |... |
+-------------------+-------------------+-------------------+
                       ^ pointer
```

| Property | Unspanned | Spanned |
|---|---|---|
| Internal fragmentation | Up to $R-1$ bytes per block | Nearly zero |
| Can handle $R > B$? | No | Yes |
| Implementation complexity | Simple | Requires pointer logic |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Unspanned Organization**

$$bfr_u = \left\lfloor \frac{2400}{180} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 13.33 \right\rfloor = 13 \text{ records/block}$$

$$b_u = \left\lceil \frac{30{,}000}{13} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2307.69 \right\rceil = 2308 \text{ blocks}$$

$$W_u = 2400 - (13 \times 180) = 2400 - 2340 = 60 \text{ bytes/block}$$

$$U_u = \frac{13 \times 180}{2400} = \frac{2340}{2400} = 0.975 = 97.5\%$$

**Spanned Organization**

$$bfr_s = \left\lceil \frac{2400}{180} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 13.33 \right\rceil = 14 \text{ records/block}$$

(Records may be split, so we *can* pack 14 records, with the 14th spanning into the next block.)

$$b_s = \left\lceil \frac{30{,}000}{14} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2142.86 \right\rceil = 2143 \text{ blocks}$$

$$W_s = 2400 - (14 \times 180) + P_{ptr} = 2400 - 2520 + P_{ptr}$$

Since the raw space is insufficient, the 14th record must span, and a pointer of $P_{ptr} \approx 8$ bytes is added. The effective utilization is:

$$U_s = \frac{30{,}000 \times 180}{2143 \times 2400} = \frac{5{,}400{,}000}{5{,}143{,}200} \approx 1.0498$$

Wait — this exceeds 1, which is impossible. Let us recompute: the issue is that the spanned organization allows up to $\lceil 13.33 \rceil = 14$ records per block **on average** when spanning, but in practice the maximum whole records that *can start* in a block is 13; the 14th's head occupies the trailing 60 bytes. So strictly, the "logical records that begin" in a block is 14, but they overlap with the next block.

The correct comparison is:
- **Unspanned**: 2308 blocks × 2400 B = **5,539,200 bytes** on disk.
- **Spanned**: 2143 blocks × 2400 B = **5,143,200 bytes** on disk.
- **Savings**: ~396,000 bytes (≈ 7.15%).

**Comment**: Spanned organization is **more space-efficient** (7.15% less disk space) and uses **fewer I/O blocks** (2143 vs. 2308), which translates to lower scan time. However, it incurs **pointer overhead** and **fragment-reassembly cost** on every read. For this dataset, since $R = 180 < B/2$, the unspanned strategy already achieves 97.5% utilization, so the **complexity of spanning is rarely justified** for small records — the unspanned approach is preferred in practice.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]` — **(Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A)**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **storage hierarchy** of a computer system. Discuss how this hierarchy influences the design of physical and logical records in a database. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A sequential file contains $r = 100{,}000$ fixed-length records of $R = 250$ bytes each. The disk parameters are: block size $B = 2048$ bytes, average seek time $t_s = 8$ ms, rotational latency $t_{rl} = 4.5$ ms, and transfer rate $R_{tr} = 50$ MB/s. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
> 1. Blocking factor and number of blocks.
> 2. Wasted space per block and total wasted space.
> 3. Total file size on disk.
> 4. Time to read one block.
> 5. Total time to sequentially read the entire file.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Storage Hierarchy** *(4 marks for diagram + 2 marks for explanation)*

```
        +-----------+
        |  Register |  ←  ~1 ns,  bytes
        +-----------+
        |   Cache   |  ←  ~10 ns, KB
        +-----------+
        |    RAM    |  ←  ~100 ns, GB  ← Buffer Pool lives here
        +-----------+
        |   SSD     |  ←  ~100 µs, TB  ← Database files live here
        +-----------+
        |   HDD     |  ←  ~10 ms, TB   ← Database files may live here
        +-----------+
        |   Tape    |  ←  seconds, PB  ← Archival backups
        +-----------+
        Speed  ↑           ↓  Capacity
        Cost   ↑           ↓
```

Each level is **faster, smaller, and more expensive** per byte than the one below it. The DBMS exploits this hierarchy by maintaining a **buffer pool** in RAM that caches recently/frequently used blocks from disk.

**Influence on Record Design** *(1 mark)*

- **Logical records** are sized for the application (small enough to fit in RAM comfortably for query processing).
- **Physical records** are sized to match the disk's natural transfer unit (sector / page) — typically 4–16 KB — to maximize sequential I/O throughput.
- The DBMS's **buffer manager** swaps physical blocks in and out of RAM in response to query demand, hiding disk latency from applications that only see logical records.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Blocking Factor and Number of Blocks** *(1.5 marks)*

$$bfr = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2048}{250} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 8.192 \right\rfloor = 8 \text{ records/block}$$

$$b = \left\lceil \frac{r}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{100{,}000}{8} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 12{,}500 \right\rceil = 12{,}500 \text{ blocks}$$

**2. Wasted Space per Block and Total** *(1.5 marks)*

$$W_{block} = B - (bfr \cdot R) = 2048 - (8 \times 250) = 2048 - 2000 = 48 \text{ bytes/block}$$

$$W_{total} = b \cdot W_{block} = 12{,}500 \times 48 = 600{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 600 \text{ KB}$$

**3. Total File Size on Disk** *(1 mark)*

$$S = b \cdot B = 12{,}500 \times 2048 = 25{,}600{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 25.6 \text{ MB}$$

**4. Time to Read One Block** *(1.5 marks)*

$$t_{bt} = \frac{B}{R_{tr}} = \frac{2048 \text{ B}}{50 \times 10^6 \text{ B/s}} = 4.096 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.04096 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{block} = t_s + t_{rl} + t_{bt} = 8 + 4.5 + 0.04096 = 12.54096 \text{ ms} \approx 12.54 \text{ ms}$$

**5. Total Sequential Read Time** *(1.5 marks)*

$$T_{scan} = b \cdot T_{block} = 12{,}500 \times 12.54096 = 156{,}762 \text{ ms} \approx 156.76 \text{ seconds} \approx 2.61 \text{ minutes}$$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Evaluations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ with $\lceil \cdot \rceil$**: Students often compute $bfr = \lceil B/R \rceil$ for unspanned records. This is **wrong** — you cannot fit a partial record. Use **floor** for unspanned. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the ceiling for the final block**: When computing $b = r/bfr$, students sometimes write $b = r / bfr$ as a plain division and lose a mark when the result is not an integer. **Always use $\lceil r/bfr \rceil$.** **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Mixing up wasted space per block vs. total wasted space**: Both quantities are different; examiners ask for one or both. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Incorrect units in I/O calculations**: $t_s$ is in **ms**, $t_{bt} = B / R_{tr}$ must be computed carefully with consistent units (1 MB = $10^6$ B for transfer-rate convention; 1 MB = $2^{20}$ B for binary). **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Ignoring that $t_{bt}$ is usually negligible compared to $t_s$ and $t_{rl}$**: Some approximations set $t_{bt} \approx 0$ for hand calculations — be consistent with the question's given data. **Loss: 0.5–1 mark.**
>
> 6. **Forgetting to state the *unspanned* assumption**: Always begin the solution with "Assuming fixed-length, unspanned, blocked organization…" to anchor the formula choice. **Loss: 0.5 mark.**
>
> 7. **Drawing a wrong spanning diagram**: A spanning diagram must show the **head and tail** with a **pointer** — not a duplicate record. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Logical record** = application-level unit (a tuple/row). **Physical record / block** = disk-transfer unit (a page/sector).
- **Blocking factor** $bfr = \lfloor B / R \rfloor$ for **unspanned**; $bfr = \lceil B / R \rceil$ for **spanned** (where the record can be split).
- **Number of blocks** $b = \lceil r / bfr \rceil$ — **always** ceiling.
- **Wasted space per block** $W = B - bfr \cdot R$, with $0 \leq W < R$ for unspanned.
- **Total file size on disk** $S = b \cdot B$.
- **Space utilization** $U = (bfr \cdot R) / B$, expressed as a fraction.
- **Average I/O time per block** $T_{block} = t_s + t_{rl} + t_{bt}$, where $t_{bt} = B / R_{tr}$.
- **Total scan time** $T_{scan} = b \cdot T_{block}$.
- **Unspanned** is **simpler but wastes** up to $R-1$ bytes per block; **spanned** is **more space-efficient** but needs **pointer logic**.
- **Variable-length records** use a **slot directory** inside each block.
- **Doubling block size halves I/O blocks and scan time** (until diminishing returns from internal fragmentation).
- **Modern RDBMS** use 4 KB, 8 KB, or 16 KB blocks; **OLTP** prefers smaller, **OLAP** prefers larger.
- Always **declare the blocking strategy** at the start of your KTU answer.
- The **principle of physical data independence** allows reblocking without changing the logical schema.
- **Inter-record gap (IRG)** is a legacy concept from magnetic tape and old disks — modern systems pack blocks contiguously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blocking Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Blocking Factor # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of Database Management Systems (DBMS) and disk storage architecture, the **Blocking Factor (bfr)** — also referred to as the *records per block* or *blocking factor of a file* — is formally defined as: > **Blocking Factor (bfr):** The maximum number of logical database records (tuples) of a given fixed size that can be packed and stored contiguously within a single physical disk block (or page) of the underlying storage device. It is a **fundamental storage parameter** in **File Organization** that bridges the gap between the logical record size (as defined by the database schema) and the physical block size (as defined by the operating system and disk hardware). The blocking factor determines how efficiently secondary storage is utilized and directly influences the cost of disk I/O operations, query optimization, and buffer management. Mathematically, it represents the integer ratio of the usable block capacity to the record footprint: $$bfr = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R} \right\rfloor$$ where $B$ is the **block size (in bytes)** and $R$ is the **fixed record size (in bytes)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Blocking Factor is a core building block in Module 3 (Disk Storage, File Organization, and Indexing). Mastery of this concept is mandatory for solving file sizing problems, calculating I/O cost, and understanding primary/secondary index construction. Expect direct formula-based questions in Part A and numerical problems in Part B. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡 Imagine you are a **warehouse manager** tasked with shipping fragile items (database records) using standardized cardboard cartons (disk blocks). Each carton has a fixed internal capacity — say, it can safely hold exactly **4 items** without exceeding the carton's volume limit or risking damage. - The **carton capacity** is analogous to the **disk block size ($B$)**. - The **size of one fragile item** is analogous to the **record size ($R$)**. - The **number of items that fit in one carton** is the **blocking factor ($bfr$)**. If each item takes up exactly 1/4th of the carton's space, you can pack **4 items per carton** ($bfr = 4$). You would *never* try to squeeze a 5th item because the carton physically cannot accommodate it without breaking or overflowing — this is exactly why we use the **floor function** in the formula: you can only fit *whole, intact* records into a block. Now, if the warehouse has **10,000 items** to ship, you would need: $$b = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{Total Records}}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{10{,}000}{4} \right\rceil = 2{,}500 \text{ cartons (blocks)}$$ This simple "packing problem" is the essence of the Blocking Factor in DBMS — it governs how many complete records fit in one physical read/write unit of the disk. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics | Term | Symbol | Standard Unit | Description | |---|---|---|---| | Disk Block / Page Size | $B$ | **bytes** (commonly 4 KB, 8 KB, 16 KB) | The fixed-size physical storage unit managed by the OS and DBMS buffer manager. | | Record Size | $R$ | **bytes** | The total size of one fixed-length database record (sum of all attribute sizes + overhead). | | Blocking Factor | $bfr$ | **records per block** (unitless) | The number of whole records that fit in one block. | | Number of Records | $r$ | **records** (unitless) | The total number of records (tuples) in the file. | | Number of Blocks | $b$ | **blocks** (unitless) | The total number of blocks required to store the file. | | File Size | — | **bytes** | Total disk space occupied by the file ($b \times B$). | > [!NOTE] > **Default KTU Assumption:** Unless stated otherwise in an exam problem, KTU 2024 scheme questions assume **fixed-length records** and **unspanned organization** (records do not cross block boundaries). Always check the problem statement carefully for the "spanned vs. unspanned" condition. --- ## 1.4 Why Blocking Factor Matters in DBMS The blocking factor is not just a storage arithmetic curiosity — it is a **critical performance lever**: 1. **I/O Efficiency:** Disk I/O operates at the *block* level, not the record level. A higher $bfr$ means more records fetched per I/O operation, reducing the number of slow disk accesses. 2. **Buffer Pool Optimization:** The DBMS buffer manager loads entire blocks into memory. $bfr$ directly determines how many records are processed per buffer frame. 3. **Index Design:** The depth of B+ trees, the fan-out of multi-level indexes, and the size of index entries all depend on $bfr$. 4. **Query Cost Estimation:** The query optimizer uses $bfr$ to estimate the number of block accesses for sequential scans, range queries, and join operations. 5. **Storage Utilization:** The ratio $\dfrac{bfr \times R}{B}$ gives the **space utilization factor** — a key metric for storage efficiency. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "How" and "Why" ### Step 1: Determine the Block Size ($B$) The **block size** is dictated by the operating system, file system, and the DBMS configuration. Common values are **4 KB, 8 KB, 16 KB, or 32 KB**. In KTU exam problems, this is typically **given as a fixed constant** (e.g., $B = 4096$ bytes). The *why*: The OS reads/writes in fixed-size chunks to amortize the seek and rotational latency costs of the disk. ### Step 2: Compute the Record Size ($R$) For a **fixed-length** record with attributes $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$: $$R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{size}(A_i) + \text{record overhead}$$ The record overhead typically includes pointers, null bitmaps, or tuple headers (commonly **4 to 8 bytes** per record in practice). The *why*: Records are the logical units defined by the schema; their size determines the packing density. ### Step 3: Compute the Blocking Factor ($bfr$) Divide the block size by the record size and **floor** the result: $$bfr = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R} \right\rfloor$$ The *why* for the floor: A **partial record** cannot be stored as a complete, addressable entity in a block (in the unspanned case). So we discard the leftover space. ### Step 4: Compute the Number of Blocks for the File ($b$) For a file containing $r$ records: $$b = \left\lceil \dfrac{r}{bfr} \right\rceil$$ The *why* for the ceiling: Even if only one partial record remains, a full block must be allocated to hold it. ### Step 5: Compute Storage Utilization ($\eta$) The fraction of the last block (and total file) that is actually filled with record data: $$\eta = \dfrac{bfr \times R}{B} \quad \text{(per-block utilization)}$$ $$\text{Overall Utilization} = \dfrac{r \times R}{b \times B}$$ --- ## 2.2 Spanned vs. Unspanned Records — A Critical Distinction | Property | Unspanned (Default) | Spanned | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | A record must be stored entirely within one block; it **cannot cross** block boundaries. | A record **may span** across two or more blocks using pointer chains. | | **Blocking Factor** | $bfr = \lfloor B / R \rfloor$ | $bfr = \lfloor B / R \rfloor$ (same formula, but leftover space is *not* wasted in the same way) | | **Storage Waste** | Up to $(B - 1)$ bytes wasted per block (the leftover tail). | Minimal waste — leftover bytes can hold the start of the next record. | | **Access Complexity** | Simple: one block read retrieves a complete record. | Complex: must follow pointer chain to reconstruct a record spanning blocks. | | **KTU 2024 Default** | ✅ **Yes** — unless explicitly stated. | Only when problem says "spanned" or "records may span blocks." | > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Point:** In KTU exams, the *overwhelming majority* of questions assume **unspanned** records. If the problem does not mention "spanned," do **not** apply the spanned logic. Misreading this distinction is a guaranteed mark-loser. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋 | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $bfr = \lfloor B / R \rfloor$ | Blocking factor (unspanned) | records/block | | 2 | $b = \lceil r / bfr \rceil$ | Number of blocks for the file | blocks | | 3 | $R = \sum \text{attr sizes} + \text{overhead}$ | Record size | bytes | | 4 | $\text{File Size} = b \times B$ | Total file storage | bytes | | 5 | $\eta = (bfr \times R) / B$ | Block space utilization | fraction (0 to 1) | | 6 | $b = \lceil r \times R / B \rceil$ | Direct formula (combines 1 & 2) | blocks | | 7 | $\text{Waste per block} = B - (bfr \times R)$ | Wasted bytes per block | bytes | | 8 | $\text{Total Waste} = b \times B - r \times R$ | Wasted bytes in entire file | bytes | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The blocking factor concept is not confined to textbook exercises — it has **direct, measurable impact** in production systems: - **Database Tuning (Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL):** DBAs tune the **block size** (called "page size" in InnoDB, "db_block_size" in Oracle) to optimize $bfr$ for the dominant record size. OLTP systems (small records) benefit from smaller blocks; OLAP systems (large records, sequential scans) benefit from larger blocks. - **Data Warehousing (Snowflake, Redshift, BigQuery):** Columnar storage engines use **very large blocks** (often 1 MB or more) to maximize the blocking factor for analytical queries that read millions of records at once. - **Embedded Systems & IoT:** In resource-constrained environments, minimizing waste from low blocking factors directly impacts flash memory endurance and cost. - **Hadoop HDFS:** Default block size is **128 MB** — a massive $B$ designed to maximize $bfr$ for the enormous records processed in batch jobs. # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Comprehensive Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Style):** > Consider a file of **fixed-length records** used to store employee data. Each record has the following attributes: > - `EmpID` (integer, **4 bytes**) > - `Name` (char(30), **30 bytes**) > - `Department` (char(20), **20 bytes**) > - `Salary` (float, **8 bytes**) > > The disk block size is **$B = 1024$ bytes**. The file contains **$r = 5000$ records**. > Records are **unspanned**. > > **Compute:** > (a) The record size $R$. > (b) The blocking factor $bfr$. > (c) The number of blocks $b$ required to store the file. > (d) The block space utilization $\eta$. > (e) The total wasted bytes in the file. --- ### Part (a): Record Size $R$ The record size is the sum of all attribute sizes. For this problem, we assume no additional tuple header overhead (a standard KTU simplification): $$R = \text{size}(\text{EmpID}) + \text{size}(\text{Name}) + \text{size}(\text{Department}) + \text{size}(\text{Salary})$$ $$R = 4 + 30 + 20 + 8$$ $$\boxed{R = 62 \text{ bytes}}$$ --- ### Part (b): Blocking Factor $bfr$ We apply the core formula. The blocking factor is the integer number of 62-byte records that fit in a 1024-byte block: $$bfr = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1024}{62} \right\rfloor$$ Performing the division: $$\dfrac{1024}{62} = 16.5161...$$ Applying the floor function (discarding the fractional part): $$\boxed{bfr = 16 \text{ records per block}}$$ **Interpretation:** We can pack exactly 16 complete employee records into one 1024-byte block. The 17th record would not fit (it would require $16 + 1 = 17$ records × 62 bytes = 1054 bytes > 1024 bytes). --- ### Part (c): Number of Blocks $b$ We divide the total number of records by the blocking factor and apply the ceiling function (since the last partial block still needs a full allocation): $$b = \left\lceil \dfrac{r}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{5000}{16} \right\rceil$$ Performing the division: $$\dfrac{5000}{16} = 312.5$$ Applying the ceiling function (rounding up to the next whole block): $$\boxed{b = 313 \text{ blocks}}$$ **Verification:** 312 full blocks × 16 records/block = 4992 records. The remaining 5000 − 4992 = **8 records** go into the 313th block, which is only half-full (8 × 62 = 496 bytes out of 1024 bytes). --- ### Part (d): Block Space Utilization $\eta$ We compute the fraction of each block that is filled with actual record data: $$\eta = \dfrac{bfr \times R}{B} = \dfrac{16 \times 62}{1024} = \dfrac{992}{1024}$$ $$\boxed{\eta \approx 0.9688 \text{ or } 96.88\%}$$ **Interpretation:** Each block is 96.88% utilized, wasting only 32 bytes per block (1024 − 992 = 32 bytes). This is a **high-utilization** scenario, typical when $B$ is a near-multiple of $R$. --- ### Part (e): Total Wasted Bytes in the File The wasted space comes from two sources: 1. The unused tail bytes in every full block: $B - (bfr \times R) = 1024 - 992 = 32$ bytes/block. 2. The unused space in the last (partially filled) block: $B - (8 \times 62) = 1024 - 496 = 528$ bytes. **Total Waste:** $$\text{Total Waste} = (b - 1) \times 32 + 528$$ $$\text{Total Waste} = 312 \times 32 + 528 = 9984 + 528$$ $$\boxed{\text{Total Waste} = 10{,}512 \text{ bytes}}$$ **Alternative Cross-Check:** $$\text{Total Waste} = b \times B - r \times R = 313 \times 1024 - 5000 \times 62 = 320{,}512 - 310{,}000 = 10{,}512 \text{ bytes} \quad \checkmark$$ Both methods agree, confirming the calculation. --- ## 3.2 General Derivation: From Raw Parameters to Full File Characterization Given: $B, R, r$ (block size, record size, total records), all unspanned. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Blocking Factor} \quad & bfr = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R} \right\rfloor \\[10pt] \text{Step 2: Number of Blocks} \quad & b = \left\lceil \dfrac{r}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{r}{\lfloor B/R \rfloor} \right\rceil \\[10pt] \text{Step 3: Direct Formula} \quad & b = \left\lceil \dfrac{r \times R}{B} \right\rceil \\[10pt] \text{Step 4: Utilization} \quad & \eta = \dfrac{bfr \times R}{B} \\[10pt] \text{Step 5: Waste} \quad & W = b \times B - r \times R \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **The Direct Formula (Step 3) is a powerful shortcut.** It combines Steps 1 and 2 into a single computation, bypassing the need to compute $bfr$ explicitly. KTU students who memorize this form save valuable time in the exam hall. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Formula to Use? | Given Information | Required Output | Recommended Formula | |---|---|---| | $B, R$ | $bfr$ | $bfr = \lfloor B / R \rfloor$ | | $r, bfr$ | $b$ | $b = \lceil r / bfr \rceil$ | | $B, R, r$ | $b$ (direct) | $b = \lceil r \times R / B \rceil$ | | $B, R, bfr$ | $\eta$ | $\eta = (bfr \times R) / B$ | | $b, B, r, R$ | $W$ | $W = b \times B - r \times R$ | | $B, R, b$ | $r_{\max}$ | $r_{\max} = b \times bfr = b \times \lfloor B / R \rfloor$ | # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Packing Pipeline The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end logical flow** of how raw records are transformed into disk blocks using the blocking factor concept: ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Schema Definition
Attributes and Types"] --> B["Compute Record Size R
Sum of attribute sizes"] B --> C["Block Size B
Fixed by OS or DBMS"] C --> D{"Division B / R"} D --> E["Apply Floor Function
Discard fractional part"] E --> F["Blocking Factor bfr
Records per block"] F --> G["File of r Records
Logical collection"] G --> H{"r mod bfr = 0?"} H -- "Yes Exact Fit" --> I["b = r / bfr blocks
No waste in last block"] H -- "No Remainder" --> J["b = floor r/bfr + 1 blocks
Last block partially filled"] I --> K["File Stored on Disk
b blocks allocated"] J --> K K --> L["Space Utilization eta
Computed per block"] ``` --- ## 4.2 Physical Layout Schematic: Records Packed into Blocks The following diagram shows the **internal layout** of three consecutive disk blocks under the unspanned record assumption, with $bfr = 3$ records per block: ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Block1["Block 1 B bytes"] R1A["Record 1
R bytes"] R2A["Record 2
R bytes"] R3A["Record 3
R bytes"] W1["Wasted Space
B minus 3R bytes"] end subgraph Block2["Block 2 B bytes"] R4A["Record 4
R bytes"] R5A["Record 5
R bytes"] R6A["Record 6
R bytes"] W2["Wasted Space
B minus 3R bytes"] end subgraph Block3["Block 3 Last Partial"] R7A["Record 7
R bytes"] R8A["Record 8
R bytes"] W3["Empty Space
B minus 2R bytes"] end Block1 --> Block2 Block2 --> Block3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** In this example, $bfr = 3$ (so 3 records per block), and the file has $r = 8$ records. The first two blocks are full (3 records each = 6 records), and the third block holds the remaining 2 records with significant unused space. Total blocks $b = \lceil 8/3 \rceil = 3$. --- ## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Spanned vs. Unspanned ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Problem Statement Given"] --> Q1{"Does the problem
mention spanned
or pointer chains?"} Q1 -- "No" --> US["Apply Unspanned Logic
bfr equals floor B/R
Record cannot cross blocks"] Q1 -- "Yes" --> S["Apply Spanned Logic
Leftover bytes hold start of
next record via pointer"] US --> Out["Standard Formula
b equals ceil r times R over B"] S --> Out Out --> End["Compute Utilization
and Waste"] ``` # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define Blocking Factor. How is it calculated from the block size and record size?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Blocking Factor (bfr)** of a file is defined as the number of logical records of a file that can be stored in one physical disk block. It is a key parameter in file organization that determines the packing density of records into blocks. **Calculation (2 Marks):** The blocking factor is calculated as the integer (floor) ratio of the block size to the record size: $$bfr = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R} \right\rfloor$$ where $B$ is the block size in bytes and $R$ is the fixed record size in bytes. The floor function is used because only whole, complete records can be stored in a block under the unspanned record organization assumption. **Significance (1 Mark):** A higher blocking factor means more records are read/written per I/O operation, improving disk access efficiency and reducing query execution time. --- ### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: What is the difference between spanned and unspanned record organization? How does it affect the blocking factor?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Unspanned Organization (1.5 Marks):** In unspanned record organization, each record is stored entirely within a single disk block. A record is not allowed to cross block boundaries. If a record is too large to fit in the remaining space of a block, the remaining space is left unused (wasted). The blocking factor under this scheme is $bfr = \lfloor B / R \rfloor$. **Spanned Organization (1.5 Marks):** In spanned record organization, a record is allowed to span across two or more consecutive blocks. The remaining bytes at the end of one block hold the beginning portion of the next record, with a pointer linking to the continuation in the next block. This eliminates internal fragmentation and maximizes storage utilization, at the cost of increased access complexity (multiple block reads + pointer following to reconstruct a single record). **Effect on Blocking Factor:** The formula $bfr = \lfloor B / R \rfloor$ remains numerically identical, but in the spanned case, the *leftover space* is not wasted — it is used to hold the start of the next record, effectively making the effective blocking factor slightly higher in practice. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with an **internal choice**. The standard structure is two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **A file uses fixed-length records. Each record has the following schema:** - `StudentID`: integer → **4 bytes** - `StudentName`: char(40) → **40 bytes** - `Branch`: char(10) → **10 bytes** - `CGPA`: float → **4 bytes** - `RecordPointer`: **6 bytes** (logical pointer for chaining) **Given:** Block size $B = 512$ bytes. Total records $r = 3000$. Records are **unspanned**. **Solve the following:** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the blocking factor $bfr$ and the total number of disk blocks $b$ required to store the file. **(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the space utilization $\eta$ and the total number of wasted bytes in the file. Also, determine how many records are stored in the last (partially filled) block. --- #### **Model Solution for Part (a)** **Step 1: Compute Record Size $R$** Sum of all attribute sizes: $$R = 4 + 40 + 10 + 4 + 6 = 64 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2: Compute Blocking Factor $bfr$** $$bfr = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{512}{64} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 8.0 \rfloor = 8 \text{ records/block}$$ *(In this case, the division is exact, so there is no waste in a full block.)* **Step 3: Compute Number of Blocks $b$** $$b = \left\lceil \dfrac{r}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{3000}{8} \right\rceil = \lceil 375.0 \rceil = 375 \text{ blocks}$$ **Result:** $bfr = 8$ records/block; $b = 375$ blocks. --- #### **Model Solution for Part (b)** **Step 1: Compute Space Utilization $\eta$** Since $bfr \times R = 8 \times 64 = 512 = B$ (exact fit): $$\eta = \dfrac{bfr \times R}{B} = \dfrac{512}{512} = 1.0 \text{ or } 100\%$$ **Step 2: Compute Records in the Last Block** Since $r = 3000$ and $3000 / 8 = 375$ (exact), the last block is also completely full. The remainder is: $$r \mod bfr = 3000 \mod 8 = 0$$ So all 375 blocks are completely full, with **8 records in the last block**. **Step 3: Compute Total Wasted Bytes** $$\text{Total Waste} = b \times B - r \times R = 375 \times 512 - 3000 \times 64 = 192{,}000 - 192{,}000 = 0 \text{ bytes}$$ **Result:** $\eta = 100\%$; Last block has 8 records; Total waste = 0 bytes. --- ### **Question B-B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **A library database file uses fixed-length records with the following fields:** - `BookID`: integer → **4 bytes** - `Title`: char(50) → **50 bytes** - `Author`: char(30) → **30 bytes** - `YearPublished`: short integer → **2 bytes** - `AvailabilityFlag`: boolean → **1 byte** **Given:** Block size $B = 4096$ bytes. Total records $r = 10{,}000$. Records are **unspanned**. **Solve the following:** **(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the record size $R$, the blocking factor $bfr$, and the number of blocks $b$ needed to store the file. **(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the block space utilization $\eta$, the wasted bytes per full block, and the total wasted bytes in the entire file. --- #### **Model Solution for Part (a)** **Step 1: Record Size $R$** $$R = 4 + 50 + 30 + 2 + 1 = 87 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2: Blocking Factor $bfr$** $$bfr = \left\lfloor \dfrac{4096}{87} \right\rfloor$$ Performing the division: $4096 / 87 = 47.080...$ $$\boxed{bfr = 47 \text{ records/block}}$$ **Step 3: Number of Blocks $b$** $$b = \left\lceil \dfrac{10{,}000}{47} \right\rceil$$ Performing the division: $10{,}000 / 47 = 212.765...$ $$\boxed{b = 213 \text{ blocks}}$$ **Verification of last block:** 212 full blocks × 47 records = 9964 records. Remaining: $10{,}000 - 9964 = 36$ records in the 213th block. --- #### **Model Solution for Part (b)** **Step 1: Space Utilization $\eta$** $$\eta = \dfrac{bfr \times R}{B} = \dfrac{47 \times 87}{4096} = \dfrac{4089}{4096}$$ $$\boxed{\eta \approx 0.9983 \text{ or } 99.83\%}$$ **Step 2: Wasted Bytes per Full Block** $$\text{Waste per block} = B - (bfr \times R) = 4096 - 4089 = 7 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 3: Total Wasted Bytes** The 212 full blocks each waste 7 bytes. The last block (block 213) contains 36 records: $$\text{Used in last block} = 36 \times 87 = 3132 \text{ bytes}$$ $$\text{Waste in last block} = 4096 - 3132 = 964 \text{ bytes}$$ $$\text{Total Waste} = (212 \times 7) + 964 = 1484 + 964 = 2448 \text{ bytes}$$ **Cross-check using unified formula:** $$\text{Total Waste} = b \times B - r \times R = 213 \times 4096 - 10{,}000 \times 87$$ $$= 872{,}448 - 870{,}000 = 2448 \text{ bytes} \quad \checkmark$$ **Result:** $\eta \approx 99.83\%$; Waste per full block = 7 bytes; Total waste = **2,448 bytes**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Blocking Factor Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting the Floor Function (–1 to –2 Marks):** Students often write $bfr = B / R$ directly (e.g., 16.51) instead of applying the floor to get 16. The blocking factor **must be a whole number** of records — you cannot store a fractional record. > > 2. **Forgetting the Ceiling for $b$ (–1 to –2 Marks):** Similarly, computing $b = r / bfr$ directly (e.g., 312.5) instead of $\lceil 312.5 \rceil = 313$. A partial last block still requires a full block allocation. > > 3. **Ignoring Record Overhead (–1 Mark):** Many KTU problems include a "record pointer" or "tuple header" field. Students who omit these auxiliary fields get an incorrect $R$, which cascades into wrong $bfr$ and $b$. > > 4. **Confusing Spanned and Unspanned (–2 to –3 Marks):** If the problem says "spanned," the blocking factor formula changes conceptually (leftover space is used). If you apply unspanned logic to a spanned problem, you may get full marks for the formula but lose marks for interpretation. > > 5. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 Mark):** Mixing KB and bytes. If $B = 4$ KB, write $B = 4096$ bytes *explicitly* in your solution. Examiners check for unit clarity. > > 6. **Not Showing Intermediate Steps (–1 Mark):** KTU expects to see $B/R$ evaluated, the floor applied, and the result stated. Jumping directly to the answer $bfr = 16$ without showing $\lfloor 1024/62 \rfloor$ is risky. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅ - **Blocking Factor ($bfr$):** Number of fixed-length records that fit in one disk block. Formula: $bfr = \lfloor B / R \rfloor$. - **Number of Blocks ($b$):** Total blocks needed to store $r$ records. Formula: $b = \lceil r / bfr \rceil = \lceil r \times R / B \rceil$. - **Direct Shortcut:** $b = \lceil r \times R / B \rceil$ — combines $bfr$ and $b$ into one calculation. - **Record Size ($R$):** Sum of all attribute sizes **plus any tuple header or pointer overhead**. Always read the problem schema carefully. - **Block Size ($B$):** Given in the problem (e.g., 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192 bytes). Always convert KB to bytes explicitly. - **Spanned vs. Unspanned:** KTU default is **unspanned**. A record cannot cross block boundaries unless explicitly stated otherwise. - **Space Utilization ($\eta$):** $\eta = (bfr \times R) / B$. Ranges from slightly above 0 to 1.0. Values above 95% are typical for well-matched schemas. - **Waste:** Per-block waste = $B - (bfr \times R)$. Total file waste = $b \times B - r \times R$. - **I/O Cost Insight:** A higher $bfr$ means more records per disk read, directly reducing the number of block accesses ($b$) and improving query performance. - **Index Linkage:** The blocking factor determines the number of index entries per block in a multi-level index — a critical connection for Module 3's indexing topics. - **Last Block Rule:** The last block is often only partially filled. Always check $r \mod bfr$ to determine the exact record count in the final block. - **Verification Habit:** Cross-check your answer using two methods (e.g., $b = \lceil r / bfr \rceil$ *and* $b = \lceil r \times R / B \rceil$) to ensure arithmetic consistency.

topics.title: Pinned and Unpinned Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pinned and Unpinned Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

In the context of **Database Management Systems (DBMS)**, **Pinned and Unpinned Organization** refers to the file/page organization strategy that classifies records (or pages) into two operational states based on their *mobility* and *access status*:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pinned Record/Page:** A record (or page) is said to be *pinned* when it has been retrieved by an application program (typically via a cursor or iterator) and its physical address is currently being referenced by an ongoing transaction. The record is therefore **locked at its current disk address** and cannot be physically relocated, compacted, or overwritten until it is explicitly *unpinned*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unpinned Record/Page:** A record that is *not currently referenced* by any active transaction. Such records **may be freely relocated** during internal page reorganization, slot compaction, garbage collection, or buffer pool eviction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Bookmark" Model

Imagine a large library where books are arranged on shelves:

- **Pinned Records** = A book currently lying open on a student's desk with a bookmark on a specific page. The librarian **cannot move, re-shelve, or discard** that book because the student may return to read it again from the exact same shelf position.
- **Unpinned Records** = Books sitting quietly on the shelf. The librarian is free to rearrange, consolidate, or remove them whenever shelf-space needs reorganization.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as the librarian's re-shelving policy is **transparent to the borrower who holds the book** (the borrower always returns to their own desk), the DBMS ensures that any program holding a *pinned* record sees **stable, unchanging physical pointers (RIDs / TIDs)** even while background reorganization moves unpinned records around.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Default Page / Block size | **4 KB – 16 KB** | $B$ |
| Pointer / RID size | **4 – 8 bytes** | $P$ |
| Slot directory overhead | **2 – 4 bytes per slot** | $S$ |
| Average record size | **100 – 500 bytes** | $R$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Pinning Exists — The Fundamental Problem

When the DBMS stores records on **fixed-size disk pages**, the records inside a page have **physical offsets** that form their *Record Identifier (RID)*:

$$\text{RID} = \langle \text{PageID}, \text{SlotNumber} \rangle$$

If the DBMS performs **internal page reorganization** (for example, to reclaim space after deletions), it may physically move a record to a different slot within the same page. If an application program is still holding a pointer to the **old slot**, that pointer becomes a **dangling reference**, leading to data corruption.

To prevent this, the DBMS introduces the **pin/unpin abstraction**.

## 2.2 Operational Lifecycle of a Pin

```
APPLICATION LAYER                    BUFFER / DISK LAYER
==================                   ====================
  GET_NEXT(record) ────────────────► Pin page in buffer pool
  read(record.field)               (page locked in memory,
   ... manipulations ...            RID pointer valid)
  COMMIT / UNPIN ─────────────────► Release page lock
                                    (page eligible for
                                     eviction / relocation)
```

### 2.3 Granularity Levels of Pinning

| Granularity | What gets pinned | Used in |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Page-level** | Entire disk page | Buffer Pool Manager |
| **Record-level** | Individual record slot | Slotted Page Organization |
| **Tuple-level** | Logical tuple (heap files) | Open-Access Table scans |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU-board exam staples** for Module-3 File Organization problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Records per Page (blocking factor)** | $bfr = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R} \right\rfloor$ | $B$ = page size, $R$ = record size |
| 2 | **Number of Pages** | $n = \left\lceil \dfrac{r}{bfr} \right\rceil$ | $r$ = total number of records |
| 3 | **Space Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{bfr \cdot R}{B}$ | Fraction $\in [0,1]$ |
| 4 | **Pin Count** | $P_c$ | Integer counter per page |
| 5 | **Eviction Condition** | $P_c = 0 \Rightarrow \text{page is unpinned (evictable)}$ | Boolean rule |
| 6 | **Slotted Page Entry Size** | $E = S + P$ | $S$ = slot length, $P$ = pointer size |
| 7 | **Max Slots per Page** | $s_{\max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{E} \right\rfloor$ | Upper bound on record slots |
| 8 | **Free Space Fraction** | $f = \dfrac{B - (bfr \cdot R)}{B}$ | Used during reorganization |
| 9 | **Compaction Gain (theoretical)** | $\Delta = \dfrac{b_{\text{new}} - b_{\text{old}}}{b_{\text{old}}}$ | $b$ = occupied slots before/after |
| 10 | **Pointer Stability Guarantee** | $\text{RID}_{\text{pinned}} = \text{constant}$ while pin holds | Hard invariant |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, **absolute value** must be written as `\vert x \vert` — the vertical bar `|` character is **reserved** as the table-column separator and will break the table layout.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OLTP Transaction Systems:** Pinning prevents torn-writes and lost-update anomalies when multiple transactions touch the same page concurrently.
- **Recovery Subsystems (ARIES, Write-Ahead Logging):** Pinned dirty pages must be flushed **before** their corresponding log records hit stable storage — a cornerstone of the *STEAL / NO-FORCE* policy.
- **In-Memory Databases (Redis, SAP HANA):** Pinning maps to *reference counting* on memory objects.
- **Operating Systems:** Equivalent concept = **pinned kernel pages** that cannot be swapped out.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Number of Pinned vs. Unpinned Slots per Page

We want to determine how many slots a slotted page can hold simultaneously, given that each slot entry consumes $E = S + P$ bytes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total page size available} &= B \\
\text{Each slot consumes} &= E = S + P \\
\text{Hence maximum slot count} &= \left\lfloor \frac{B}{E} \right\rfloor \\
\therefore s_{\max} &= \left\lfloor \frac{B}{S + P} \right\rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:** Let $B = 4096$ bytes, $S = 4$ bytes (slot length field), $P = 6$ bytes (pointer + status bit).

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{\max} &= \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{4 + 6} \right\rfloor \\
         &= \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{10} \right\rfloor \\
         &= 409 \text{ slots}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pinning Overhead in the Buffer Pool

Each pinned page carries an extra metadata structure called the **pin-control block (PCB)**. For a buffer pool of $N$ frames:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory for PCB} &= N \times \text{sizeof(PCB)} \\
\text{sizeof(PCB)} &= 4\,(\text{pageID}) + 4\,(\text{pin\_count}) + 4\,(\text{dirty\_bit}) + 4\,(\text{ref\_bit}) \\
&= 16 \text{ bytes} \\
\therefore \text{PCB overhead} &= 16\,N \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $N = 1024$ frames: $16 \times 1024 = 16\,384$ bytes $= 16$ KB of pure metadata — negligible compared to GB-scale buffer pools.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Probability That a Random Page is Pinned

Assume a workload where each of $T$ concurrent transactions holds on average $k$ pinned pages, and the DBMS owns $N$ buffer frames.

$$
p_{\text{pinned}} = \frac{T \cdot k}{N}
$$

When $p_{\text{pinned}} \to 1$, the buffer pool is **saturated** — every replacement request must either wait (blocking the transaction) or force a victim-page flush. KTU examiners love asking: *"At what value of $T$ does the buffer pool become fully pinned?"* — Answer: $T = N/k$.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pinned vs. Unpinned

| Property | **Pinned** | **Unpinned** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mobility | **IMMOVABLE** | Movable (compaction, reorg) |
| Evictable from buffer? | ❌ NO | ✅ YES |
| RID stable? | ✅ YES | ⚠️ Only via indirection |
| Eligible for write-back? | Only when $P_c = 0$ | Immediately when dirty |
| Memory cost | Holds 1 pin-count unit | Holds 0 pin-count units |
| Concurrency | May block other transactions on same page | Free to be replaced |
| Use case | Active transaction holds record pointer | Reorganizable background slot |

## 3.5 Algorithmic Pin/Unpin Sequence

```text
PIN(pageID):
    1. Locate frame for pageID in buffer pool (hash lookup).
    2. IF frame not in memory THEN
           Load page from disk into a free/evictable frame.
       END IF
    3. Increment pin_count[frame] by 1.
    4. Return pointer to frame.

UNPIN(pageID, dirty_flag):
    1. Locate frame for pageID.
    2. Decrement pin_count[frame] by 1.
    3. IF dirty_flag = TRUE THEN
           Set dirty_bit[frame] = TRUE.
       END IF
    4. IF pin_count[frame] = 0 THEN
           Add frame to LRU/MRU replacement list.
       END IF.
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Slotted Page Architecture (Pinned vs. Unpinned)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PG[Disk Page - 4 KB]
        HDR["PAGE HEADER"]
        SD["SLOT DIRECTORY"]
        R1["Record A - PINNED"]
        R2["Record B - UNPINNED"]
        R3["Record C - PINNED"]
        R4["Record D - UNPINNED"]
        FRE["FREE SPACE POOL"]
    end

    HDR --- SD
    SD --- R1
    SD --- R2
    SD --- R3
    SD --- R4
    SD --- FRE

    APP["Application Program via Cursor"] -- GET_NEXT --> R1
    APP -- HOLDING --> R1

    BG["Background Reorganizer"] -. MOVE .-> R2
    BG -. MOVE .-> R4
    BG -. SKIP .-x R1
    BG -. SKIP .-x R3
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Buffer Pool Pin/Unpin State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Free: Page loaded from disk

    Free --> Pinned: pin_count = 1
    Pinned --> Pinned: pin_count++
    Pinned --> PinnedDirty: write occurs

    PinnedDirty --> Pinned: dirty bit persists
    PinnedDirty --> UnpinnedDirty: pin_count = 0

    Pinned --> UnpinnedClean: pin_count = 0
    UnpinnedClean --> Free: Frame reused

    UnpinnedDirty --> UnpinnedClean: Flushed to disk
    UnpinnedDirty --> Free: Forced write-back and reuse

    Free --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of Pin Management

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    USER["User Transaction T1"]
    APPL["Application Program Module"]
    API["DBMS API Layer pin_page / unpin_page"]
    BUF["Buffer Pool Manager"]
    HASH["Hash Table pageID to frame mapping"]
    REPL["Replacement Policy LRU or CLOCK"]
    DISK["Stable Storage Disk"]

    USER --> APPL
    APPL -- READ or WRITE --> API
    API -- pin request --> BUF
    BUF --> HASH
    BUF -- victim selection --> REPL
    REPL -- candidate frame --> BUF
    BUF -- flush dirty frame --> DISK
    BUF -- read page --> DISK
    BUF -- unpin response --> API
    API --> APPL
    APPL --> USER
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Timeline of Pin Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T as Transaction
    participant M as Buffer Manager
    participant D as Disk

    T->>M: OPEN_CURSOR(table)
    M->>D: READ_PAGE(pageID = 7)
    D-->>M: Page contents
    M-->>T: Cursor handle (page pinned)

    T->>T: Read record slot 3
    Note over T: Slot 3 is now PINNED

    T->>M: GET_NEXT()
    M-->>T: Slot 5 (also pinned)

    T->>M: UNPIN(slot 3)
    Note over M: pin_count decremented

    T->>M: COMMIT
    M->>D: FLUSH page 7 (if dirty)
    D-->>M: ACK
    M->>M: UNPIN final slots
    M-->>T: COMMIT successful
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> Differentiate between **pinned** and **unpinned** records in the context of slotted page organization.

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

A **pinned record** is one that has been retrieved by an application program via a cursor/iterator and is currently being referenced by an active transaction. Its Record Identifier (RID) must remain stable — therefore the record **cannot be physically relocated, compacted, or overwritten**. An **unpinned record** is not currently held by any application program and is therefore **freely eligible for relocation** during internal page reorganization, slot compaction, or garbage collection. Pinning preserves pointer validity while permitting background reorganization of unused slots.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> What is a *pin count*? State the condition under which a page becomes eligible for buffer-pool replacement.

**Model Answer (≈ 70 words):**

The **pin count** $P_c$ of a page is an integer counter maintained in the buffer pool's frame-control block that records how many active transactions or cursors currently hold a reference to that page. A page becomes **eligible for replacement (eviction)** if and only if $P_c = 0$ — that is, **no transaction is currently pinning it**. Additionally, if the page is *dirty*, its contents must be flushed to disk before the frame can be reused for another page.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following **KTU ESE Module-3 internal choice** convention, **both Question A and Question B are provided**. The student answers **only ONE**.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neatly labeled diagram, explain the **slotted page organization** and clearly mark the regions where *pinned* and *unpinned* records would reside. Describe what happens when the system performs **page reorganization**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Consider a disk page of size **$B = 8192$ bytes**. Each record is **$R = 200$ bytes**, each slot entry takes **$E = 12$ bytes**, and the page header occupies **$H = 64$ bytes**. Compute:
  1. The blocking factor $bfr$.
  2. The maximum number of slots the slot directory can index.
  3. The free-space fraction if only 30 records are currently stored.
  4. The pin-related overhead if the buffer pool has $N = 500$ frames. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Slotted Page Diagram + Reorganization Explanation**

A **slotted page** divides the fixed-size page into three logical regions:

```
+-------------------------------------------------------+
| PAGE HEADER  (pageID, LSN, free-space pointer)  64 B  |
+-------------------------------------------------------+
| SLOT DIRECTORY (Slot 0, Slot 1, ... Slot n-1)         |
|  Each entry = (offset-to-record, record-length, pin-bit)
+-------------------------------------------------------+
| RECORD 0  | RECORD 1  | ... | RECORD k-1 | FREE SPACE |
+-------------------------------------------------------+
```

- The **slot directory grows from the top** (just below the header).
- **Records grow from the bottom**.
- Each slot entry contains a **pin-bit**: `1 ⇒ pinned`, `0 ⇒ unpinned`.

**During page reorganization (compaction):**

1. The reorganizer scans the slot directory.
2. **Pinned records** are left untouched — their physical slot offset is preserved.
3. **Unpinned records** are copied contiguously toward the bottom of the page, reclaiming fragmented gaps.
4. The slot directory is updated; a new free-space pointer is computed.
5. The new free-space fraction is announced to the buffer manager.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

Given: $B = 8192$, $R = 200$, $E = 12$, $H = 64$, $N = 500$.

**(i) Blocking factor:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
bfr &= \left\lfloor \frac{B - H}{R + E} \right\rfloor \\
    &= \left\lfloor \frac{8192 - 64}{200 + 12} \right\rfloor \\
    &= \left\lfloor \frac{8128}{212} \right\rfloor \\
    &= 38 \text{ records per page}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum slots:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{\max} &= \left\lfloor \frac{B - H}{E} \right\rfloor \\
         &= \left\lfloor \frac{8192 - 64}{12} \right\rfloor \\
         &= \left\lfloor \frac{8128}{12} \right\rfloor \\
         &= 677 \text{ slots}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Free-space fraction (with 30 records stored):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Used space} &= 30 \times (R + E) = 30 \times 212 = 6360 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Free space} &= (B - H) - 6360 = 8128 - 6360 = 1768 \text{ bytes} \\
f &= \frac{1768}{8192} \approx 0.2158 = 21.58\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Pin-related overhead:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PCB size} &= 4 + 4 + 4 + 4 = 16 \text{ bytes/frame} \\
\text{Total overhead} &= 16 \times 500 = 8000 \text{ bytes} = 7.8125 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **buffer-pool pinning** in a DBMS. Discuss how the pin count evolves through the typical lifecycle of a transaction (open cursor → fetch → update → commit). **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A DBMS buffer pool contains **$N = 200$ frames**. Suppose **$T = 50$ transactions** are concurrent, each pinning on average **$k = 3$ pages**. Compute:
  1. The expected number of pinned frames.
  2. The probability that a randomly chosen frame is pinned.
  3. The smallest value of $T$ that would cause **buffer-pool saturation**.
  4. Comment on the implication of saturation for transaction throughput. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Buffer-Pool Pinning Lifecycle**

| Phase | Action | Pin-count effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `OPEN_CURSOR` | Buffer manager reads the target page into a free frame | Pin count = 1 |
| `FETCH` | Cursor returns record to application | Pin count unchanged |
| `UPDATE` | Application modifies record; dirty bit set | Pin count unchanged (still held) |
| `COMMIT` | Log records flushed; page written back | Pin count decremented; frame evictable |

**Critical insight:** Until the application explicitly *closes* the cursor or the transaction *commits/aborts*, the page remains **pinned** — the buffer manager **cannot evict it** even under memory pressure. This is why long-running cursors are a notorious source of performance problems in production DBMSs.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

Given: $N = 200$, $T = 50$, $k = 3$.

**(i) Expected pinned frames:**
$$
E[\text{pinned}] = T \cdot k = 50 \times 3 = 150 \text{ frames}
$$

**(ii) Probability a frame is pinned:**
$$
p_{\text{pinned}} = \frac{E[\text{pinned}]}{N} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75 = 75\%
$$

**(iii) Saturation threshold:**
$$
T_{\text{sat}} = \frac{N}{k} = \frac{200}{3} \approx 66.67 \Rightarrow T_{\text{sat}} = 67 \text{ transactions}
$$

**(iv) Throughput implication:** Beyond $T = 67$, every new pin request either **forces a synchronous disk flush** of an unpinned dirty page or **blocks the requesting transaction** until a frame is released. Net effect: transaction throughput **collapses** (thrashing), average response time **skyrockets**, and the DBMS may enter a **livelock** condition.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing "pinning" with "locking":** Pinning is a *physical buffer-pool* concept (prevents page eviction). Locking is a *logical concurrency* concept (prevents concurrent access). Examiners **deduct 1 mark** if you mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract the page-header size** before computing blocking factor — yields inflated $bfr$.
> 3. **Stating "$P_c = 0$ ⇒ page is dirty":** Wrong. $P_c = 0$ means evictable; the dirty-bit is *orthogonal*.
> 4. **Using `R` instead of `R + E`** in the blocking-factor formula — slot overhead is *always* present.
> 5. **Skipping the labeled diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts — KTU typically allocates **2 marks** for the diagram alone.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Pinned record/page** = currently held by an active cursor/transaction → **immovable** → **not evictable**.
- ✅ **Unpinned record/page** = no active reference → **reorganizable** → **evictable candidate**.
- ✅ **Pin-count** is the integer that governs eviction eligibility: **$P_c = 0$** is the only safe eviction state.
- ✅ **Pin-unpin overhead** in the buffer pool is typically **16 bytes per frame** — negligible at GB scale.
- ✅ **RID stability** is the central invariant of pinning — application pointers remain valid until the pin is released.
- ✅ **Blocking factor** $bfr = \lfloor (B - H) / (R + E) \rfloor$ — remember header AND slot overhead.
- ✅ **Buffer-pool saturation** occurs at $T = N/k$ — beyond this, throughput collapses.
- ✅ Pinning is a **physical / buffer-pool concept**; locking is a **logical / concurrency concept** — never interchange them in answers.
- ✅ Slotted pages **grow records from the bottom** and **grow the slot directory from the top**, meeting in the middle — this is the source of all fragmentation calculations.
- ✅ Reorganization walks the slot directory, **skips pinned entries**, and **compacts only unpinned entries**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heap Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heap Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Heap File** (also known as an *Unordered File* or *Pile File*) is the simplest, most primitive file organization technique used in a Database Management System (DBMS) where **records are inserted and stored at the end of the file in the order in which they arrive, with no specific ordering, indexing, or clustering applied to the data**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A heap file is a sequence of *variable-length* or *fixed-length* records, where the physical arrangement of records on disk bears no logical relation to the values stored within the records themselves. The only ordering is the *insertion sequence* — i.e., chronological order of arrival.

The relationship between a logical record (a row in a table) and its physical location (a byte offset on disk) is managed by the **File Manager / Storage Manager** layer of the DBMS architecture, which sits below the Buffer Manager and above the Disk Manager in the System R / KTU reference architecture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Dump Box" Model

Imagine a **cluttered return box** at a school library:

- Students throw returned books into a **single big bin**, with no sorting, no cataloging, and no order.
- If you want a specific book, you must **rummage through the entire box from top to bottom** (a *full table scan*).
- When a new book comes in, the librarian simply **drops it on top of the pile** if there's room, or **starts a new pile** if the current one is full.
- The system is **chaotic but fast for inserting** and **painfully slow for searching**.

This is precisely how a heap file operates at the disk level — **O(1) insert, O(N) search**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Boundary |
|---|---|
| **Page Size** | **4 KB, 8 KB, or 16 KB** (PostgreSQL default = 8 KB) |
| **Record Slot State** | Occupied (1) or Free (0) |
| **Block Header Size** | Typically **16 – 64 bytes** per page |
| **Full Page Threshold** | > 90% utilization triggers overflow page |
| **Max Records per Page** | = ⌊(Page Size − Header) / Record Size⌋ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO3, KTU 2024):** Heap files are the *default storage structure* used when a table is created **without** an explicit `INDEX` or `CLUSTER` / `ORDER BY` clause. For example, in SQL: `CREATE TABLE Student ( ... );` → backing file is a heap.

---

## 1.4 Visualizing the Heap File Conceptually

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  HEAP FILE (No Order)                        │
│                                                              │
│   ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐     │
│   │  Page 0  │──│  Page 1  │──│  Page 2  │──│  Page 3  │──▶  │ (more)
│   │          │  │          │  │          │  │          │     │
│   │ [R1][R4] │  │ [R2][R5] │  │ [R3][   ]│  │ [R6][R7] │     │
│   │ [  ][  ] │  │ [R8][  ] │  │ [  ][  ] │  │ [  ][  ] │     │
│   └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘     │
│        ▲                                                      │
│        │                                                      │
│   Free Space                                          Record IDs │
│   Tracking                                              R1, R2...│
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Heap File Page

A single page (block) within a heap file is internally divided into three logical regions:

1. **Page Header** — Contains metadata: number of records, free space pointer, page ID, LSN (Log Sequence Number) for crash recovery, and a slot directory.
2. **Record Storage Area** — Where the actual data records (tuples) are physically laid out as a contiguous byte stream.
3. **Slot Directory (or Record Offset Array)** — A small array of `(offset, length)` pairs, one entry per record, indexed by *slot ID*. Each slot is marked as *occupied* or *free*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slot Directory Insight (CO3):** The slot directory enables *efficient deletion* without re-packing the page. A deleted record's slot is simply flagged as *free*, leaving a "hole" that gets reused during the next insert.

## 2.2 The Three Classical Heap File Implementations

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **three** distinct implementations, each progressively more sophisticated:

### Implementation 1 — Linked List of Pages (Simplest)

- The DBMS maintains a **header page** that stores pointers (RIDs) to the first data page and the last data page in the file.
- All data pages are linked in a **singly or doubly linked list** via `next_page_id` and `prev_page_id` fields.
- **Insertion Algorithm:** Walk the list from head, find the first page with free space, insert. If none, append a new page.
- **Cost of Insert:** O(N) for full scan + O(1) for append.
- **Cost of Search (Equality):** O(N) — must scan every page.
- **Drawback:** Header page is a *single point of failure* and a *concurrency bottleneck* under heavy insert load.

### Implementation 2 — Directory of Pages (Used by Oracle, PostgreSQL early versions)

- The DBMS maintains a **directory** (an array of page pointers) stored in the file's header/data dictionary.
- The directory is itself stored in one or more pages, but is **cached in memory** for fast access.
- **Insertion Algorithm:** Check directory for a page with free space using cached bitmap, insert directly. If full, allocate a new page and update directory.
- **Cost of Insert:** O(1) *amortized* with in-memory directory cache.
- **Cost of Search:** O(N).
- **Advantage:** Eliminates the linked-list traversal; the directory acts like a coarse-grained index.

### Implementation 3 — Bitmap / Free-Space Map (Modern, Used by PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB)

- A **bitmap** (or set of bitmaps) tracks which pages have free space.
- One bit per page: `1` = has free space, `0` = full.
- Optionally, a finer-grained **Free Space Map (FSM)** tracks the *exact percentage* of free space per page.
- **Cost of Insert:** O(1) — find first `1` bit, use that page.
- **Advantage:** Trivially fast page selection; easily supports *bulk inserts* in parallel.

## 2.3 Operations Cost Analysis (Big-O Cheat Sheet)

| Operation | Time Complexity | Disk I/O Cost | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Insert** | O(1) amortized | 1 page write | Append at end; reused free slot |
| **Equality Search** | O(N) | N page reads | Full table scan required |
| **Range Search** | O(N) | N page reads | No ordering advantage |
| **Delete** | O(1) at storage | 1 page write | Logical tombstone possible |
| **Update (no size change)** | O(1) | 1 page write | In-place rewrite |
| **Update (size change)** | O(1) | 2 page writes | Delete + insert |
| **Sequential Scan** | O(N) | N page reads | Cache-friendly due to contiguity |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> Use the following formulas in any KTU numerical problem on heap file capacity planning.

$$
\text{Max Records per Page} = \left\lfloor \frac{P - H}{R} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
\text{Total Pages} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{\text{Max Records per Page}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
\text{File Size (bytes)} = N \times R + \text{Directory Overhead} + \text{Free Space}
$$

$$
\text{Space Utilization} = \frac{N \times R}{P \times \text{Total Pages}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Avg. Search Cost (Linear Scan)} = \frac{N \times R}{2P} \text{ pages}
$$

$$
\text{Insert Cost (Linked List)} = \left\lfloor \frac{N \times R}{2P} \right\rfloor + 1 \text{ pages}
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Page size in bytes (**typically 4096**)
- $H$ = Page header size in bytes (**typically 64**)
- $R$ = Record size in bytes
- $N$ = Total number of records in the file

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Heap files are the **default backing store** for almost every relational table created without an index. In production systems:
- **Data Warehousing:** Staging tables are heap files because bulk inserts dominate; search happens later via columnar indexes.
- **OLTP Audit Logs:** Append-only audit tables use heap files for maximum write throughput.
- **IoT Time-Series Buffers:** In-memory heap buffers (e.g., PostgreSQL's `shared_buffers`) before flushing to disk in sorted runs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Capacity Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU-Style 3-mark question):**
> A heap file uses **4 KB pages** with a **64-byte page header**. Each record is **200 bytes** long. If the table holds **50,000 records**, calculate: (a) Max records per page, (b) Total pages required, (c) Total file size in MB, (d) Space utilization.

### Step 1 — Compute Max Records per Page

$$
\text{Max Records per Page} = \left\lfloor \frac{P - H}{R} \right\rfloor
$$

Substituting the values $P = 4096$ bytes, $H = 64$ bytes, $R = 200$ bytes:

$$
\text{Max Records per Page} = \left\lfloor \frac{4096 - 64}{200} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
= \left\lfloor \frac{4032}{200} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 20.16 \right\rfloor = 20 \text{ records}
$$

> **Logic:** Each page can hold 20 full records; the remaining 32 bytes are unused (wasted due to rounding).

### Step 2 — Compute Total Pages

$$
\text{Total Pages} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{\text{Max Records per Page}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
= \left\lceil \frac{50000}{20} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2500 \right\rceil = 2500 \text{ pages}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total File Size

$$
\text{File Size (bytes)} = \text{Total Pages} \times P
$$

$$
= 2500 \times 4096 = 10{,}240{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

Converting to megabytes:

$$
\text{File Size (MB)} = \frac{10{,}240{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 9.77 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Space Utilization

$$
\text{Utilization} = \frac{N \times R}{P \times \text{Total Pages}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \frac{50000 \times 200}{4096 \times 2500} \times 100\% = \frac{10{,}000{,}000}{10{,}240{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\approx 97.66\%
$$

> **Insight:** The 2.34% slack is the cumulative wasted space from the last page of each block (32 bytes per page × 2500 = 80 KB total).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Average Search Cost via Linear Scan

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Given the file in Example 1, find the **average number of pages accessed** for a successful equality search assuming records are uniformly distributed.

The average cost of a successful linear search is the cost of probing through *half* the file on average:

$$
C_{\text{avg}} = \frac{N \times R}{2 \times P} = \frac{50000 \times 200}{2 \times 4096}
$$

$$
= \frac{10{,}000{,}000}{8192} \approx 1220.7 \approx \mathbf{1221 \text{ pages}}
$$

> **Why?** Since records are unordered, a search for any record has a uniform probability of being at any position. The expected position is the middle, requiring approximately $N/2$ comparisons.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Insertion Cost in Linked-List Implementation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** With the same 4 KB page / 200 byte record setup, what is the **expected number of pages read** to find a free slot when inserting a new record (linked-list implementation, no in-memory directory)?

$$
C_{\text{insert}} = \left\lfloor \frac{N \times R}{2P} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

$$
= \left\lfloor \frac{50000 \times 200}{2 \times 4096} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 1220.7 \right\rfloor + 1 = 1221 \text{ pages}
$$

> **Logic:** In the linked-list variant, we must *walk* the list to find the first page with free space. On average, this is roughly half the file.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Heap vs. Sorted vs. Hashed)

| Property | **Heap File** | **Sorted File** | **Hashed File** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Insert Cost** | O(1) ✓ | O(log N) | O(1) avg, O(N) worst |
| **Equality Search** | O(N) ✗ | O(log N) | O(1) avg ✓ |
| **Range Search** | O(N) ✗ | O(log N + k) ✓ | O(N) ✗ |
| **Sequential Scan** | O(N), fast | O(N), ordered | O(N), scattered ✗ |
| **Storage Overhead** | Minimal ✓ | Low–Medium | High (directory) |
| **Crash Recovery** | Easy ✓ | Medium | Hard (rehash) |
| **Best Use Case** | Bulk load, audit | Range queries, reports | Point lookups, joins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The trade-off is formalized as: **Heap = fast writes, slow reads**. Choosing heap is justified when the workload is *write-heavy with occasional full scans* (e.g., log buffers, ETL staging).

---

## 3.5 Record Deletion — The "Tombstone" Mechanism

When a record is deleted from a heap file, the DBMS does **not** immediately compact the page (which would require reading and rewriting the entire page). Instead, it marks the slot as *free* in the slot directory.

### Pseudocode (Logical, Not for Coding Submission)

```
DELETE(RID):
    1. Locate page p = RID.page_id
    2. Read page p into buffer
    3. Set slot[RID.slot_num].occupied = 0
    4. Decrement p.num_records
    5. Mark p as dirty
    6. (Optional) Append to free-space map
```

> **Vacuum / Reclaim:** Periodically, a background process (*VACUUM* in PostgreSQL, *OPTIMIZE TABLE* in MySQL) compacts pages by rewriting them with active records, returning space to the OS.

    subgraph PageBlock["PAGE STRUCTURE (4 KB)"]
        HDR["Page Header (64 B)\nLSN | PageID | FreeSpacePtr | NumRecords"]
        SLOT["Slot Directory\n[(offset, length, occupied)]\n... up to 20 slots ..."]
        REC["Record Storage Area\n[ R1 ][ R2 ][ R3 ] ... [ R20 ]\n[ 32 bytes FREE SPACE ]"]
    end

        D3["[3] -> Page 3 (25% full)"]
    end

    DIR -. pointer .-> P0["Page 0"]

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Insert / Delete Lifecycle on a Heap File

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INSERT new record R"] --> B{"Directory cache hit?\n(Free space map)"}
    B -->|"Yes (Page P has free slot)"| C["Read Page P into buffer"]
    B -->|"No (all pages full)"| D["Allocate new Page P_new\nAppend to directory"]
    D --> C
    C --> E["Find first FREE slot in slot dir\nMark as occupied"]
    E --> F["Write record at offset\nUpdate free-space pointer"]
    F --> G["Mark page dirty\nFlush to disk"]
    G --> H["Return RID (page_id, slot_num)"]

    I["DELETE record R via RID"] --> J["Read page containing R"]
    J --> K["Set slot[RID.slot_num].occupied = 0\nDecrement num_records"]
    K --> L["Update free-space map\nMark page dirty"]
    L --> M["(Optional background)\nVACUUM reclaims space"]

    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style H fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style I fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style M fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
```

    B --> B2["Search: O(N)"]

    D --> D2["Search: O(1) avg"]
