<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a heap file organization. Mention any two advantages and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**

A heap file is an unordered file organization in which **records are stored in the order they are inserted**, with no clustering, sorting, or indexing applied to the data. Each page contains a *slot directory* that tracks record positions and free space.

**Advantages:**
1. **Fast Insertion** — O(1) amortized; simply append to the file or use the first free slot.
2. **Excellent for bulk loads** — Bulk inserts of millions of records can be streamed sequentially with minimal overhead.
3. **Simple implementation** — No directory tree or hash structure to maintain.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Slow search** — Equality and range queries both require a *full table scan* in O(N) time, which is unacceptable for large tables accessed frequently by primary key.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the free-space map in heap file management.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Free-Space Map (FSM)** is a bitmap-like data structure maintained by the DBMS that tracks the *amount of free space* available on each page of a heap file. Each bit (or small integer) corresponds to a page and indicates whether the page can accommodate a new record of a given size class.

**Roles:**
1. **Accelerates Insertion:** The DBMS consults the FSM in memory to find a page with sufficient free space in O(1) time, avoiding a linear scan of the entire file.
2. **Supports Bulk Inserts:** Parallel insertion threads can each locate a different free page without contention.
3. **Coalescing Free Space:** After deletions mark slots as free, the FSM updates its counters, allowing future inserts to reuse the freed space.
4. **Vacuum Hint:** Pages reported by the FSM as mostly empty are prime candidates for background *VACUUM/compaction* jobs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, candidates answer **ONE question** from Module 3 (choice provided). Both alternatives below carry **14 marks** and contain two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **three implementations** of heap file organization. Compare their insertion and search costs using a clear table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a heap file with **page size = 8 KB**, **page header = 128 bytes**, and **record size = 250 bytes**. If the table contains **100,000 records**:
- (i) Find the maximum number of records per page.
- (ii) Compute the total number of pages.
- (iii) Calculate the file size in MB (assume 1 MB = 1024² bytes).
- (iv) Determine the average number of page I/Os for a successful equality search. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Three Implementations

**1. Linked List of Pages (Simplest):**
The DBMS maintains a **header page** that holds a pointer to the first and last data page. All data pages are linked via `next_page_id` (and optionally `prev_page_id`) pointers in the page header. To insert, the system walks the linked list from the head until it finds a page with sufficient free space; if none exists, a new page is allocated and appended. **Cost:** Insert = O(N) traversal, Search = O(N).

**2. Directory of Pages:**
A **directory** (an array of page IDs and free-space counters) is stored in a special page and *cached in main memory* by the File Manager. To insert, the system scans the in-memory directory to find a page with free space — O(1) if the cache is hot. To search, the system still must read every data page — O(N). This is the scheme used in *Oracle* and early *PostgreSQL*.

**3. Free-Space Map / Bitmap:**
A **bitmap** with one bit per page is maintained (and cached), where `1` indicates "has free space" and `0` indicates "full". Optionally, multiple bitmaps are kept for *size classes* (e.g., pages with ≥ 25%, ≥ 50%, ≥ 75% free space). This allows O(1) selection of an appropriate page for any record size and supports efficient parallel bulk loads. Used by modern *PostgreSQL* and *MySQL InnoDB*.

### Comparison Table

| Implementation | Insert Cost | Search Cost | Memory Overhead | Concurrency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linked List | O(N) | O(N) | Header page only | Low |
| Directory | O(1) cached | O(N) | Directory in memory | Medium |
| Free-Space Map | O(1) | O(N) | FSM in memory | High |

### Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:** $P = 8192$ B, $H = 128$ B, $R = 250$ B, $N = 100{,}000$ records.

**(i) Maximum records per page:**

$$
\text{Max Records} = \left\lfloor \frac{8192 - 128}{250} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{8064}{250} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 32.256 \right\rfloor = 32
$$

**(ii) Total pages:**

$$
\text{Total Pages} = \left\lceil \frac{100{,}000}{32} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3125 \right\rceil = 3125
$$

**(iii) File size in MB:**

$$
\text{Size} = 3125 \times 8192 = 25{,}600{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
= \frac{25{,}600{,}000}{1024^2} \approx 24.41 \text{ MB}
$$

**(iv) Average search cost (successful equality):**

$$
C_{\text{avg}} = \frac{N \times R}{2P} = \frac{100{,}000 \times 250}{2 \times 8192} = \frac{25{,}000{,}000}{16{,}384} \approx 1526 \text{ pages}
$$

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **internal structure of a heap file page**, including the role of the slot directory in managing deletions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how **insertion, deletion, and update** operations are handled in a heap file. Explain the concept of *tombstoning* and *page compaction (VACUUM)*. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Internal Page Structure

A heap file page (typically 4 KB or 8 KB) is divided into three logical regions:

1. **Page Header (≈ 64–128 bytes):** Stores the **Log Sequence Number (LSN)** for crash recovery, the page's unique **Page ID**, a **Free-Space Pointer** indicating where the next record can be written, and a counter of total records.
2. **Slot Directory (variable size):** An array of fixed-size entries — typically 4 to 8 bytes each — of the form `(record_offset, record_length, occupied_flag)`. Each entry corresponds to a *slot number* that becomes part of the Record ID (RID).
3. **Record Storage Area:** A byte region where the actual data tuples are stored contiguously, growing from the *end of the header* downward (or upward, depending on the engine).

**Role of Slot Directory in Deletion:**
When a record is deleted, the DBMS does **not** shift other records to fill the gap (that would be too expensive). Instead, it simply marks the corresponding slot as `occupied = 0`. The freed space becomes a "hole" in the record area. The next insertion into the page will:
- Either overwrite a previously-freed slot (reusing the same `(offset, length)` pair), or
- Append a new record at the current free-space pointer.

This makes deletion an **O(1)** operation in terms of disk I/O.

```
[ Page Header (64 B) | Slot Dir (n × 8 B) | Records ........... | Free Space ]
                       [s1: occ=1] [s2: occ=0 ← deleted] [s3: occ=1] [s4: occ=0]
```

### Part (b) — Insert, Delete, Update, and Compaction

**Insertion:**
- Locate a page with free space (via directory / FSM).
- Read the page into the buffer pool.
- Find the first slot marked `occupied = 0`; if none, append at the free-space pointer.
- Write the new record, mark the slot as occupied, increment the record counter, mark the page dirty, and return the new RID.

**Deletion (Tombstoning):**
- Locate the page via the RID's `page_id`.
- Read the page, mark the slot as `occupied = 0`, decrement the record counter.
- The record bytes become a *hole* in the page; logical accessibility ends, but physical bytes persist.
- This is sometimes called a **soft delete** or **tombstone**.

**Update:**
- **Same-size update:** Read the page, overwrite the bytes at the same offset — O(1) disk I/O.
- **Larger-size update:** The record may no longer fit. The DBMS performs a *delete + insert* — O(2) I/Os.
- **Smaller-size update:** Overwrite in place; leftover bytes become a small internal hole (acceptable, as the slot's length field tracks the actual size).

**Page Compaction (VACUUM):**
- A background process periodically scans pages with many free slots.
- For each such page, it *rewrites* the page with all live records packed contiguously at the start, refreshes the slot directory, and updates the free-space pointer.
- The OS then sees the *truly freed* space, and the FSM is updated.
- This prevents the heap from becoming a "swiss cheese" file with fragmented free space.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "delete" with "physical removal":** A logical delete (tombstone) is **not** the same as a physical delete (compaction). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you state that "deleted records are immediately removed from disk."
> 2. **Forgetting to update the FSM/Directory:** When a record is deleted, the free-space map and directory counters **must** be updated. Omitting this loses marks.
> 3. **Miscounting in part (b):** In numerical questions, students often forget to subtract the page header before dividing by record size. Always write $(P - H)$, not $P$.
> 4. **No "physical layer" awareness:** Don't say "the record is found by its primary key" — heap files use **RIDs (page_id, slot_num)**, not keys, for direct access. Keys are used only if a separate index exists.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

- **Definition:** Heap file = unordered file, records placed in insertion order. No clustering, no sorting, no implicit index.
- **Default Backing Store:** Every `CREATE TABLE` (without `ORDER BY` or `CLUSTER`) uses a heap.
- **Three Implementations:**
  1. **Linked List** — Header page + singly/doubly linked data pages.
  2. **Directory** — In-memory cached array of page pointers + free-space counters.
  3. **Free-Space Map (FSM)** — Bitmap with one or more bits per page; size-class aware.
- **Page Structure:** Header + Slot Directory + Record Area.
- **RID Format:** `(page_id, slot_number)` — the *physical* address of a record.
- **Slot Directory:** Enables O(1) logical deletion by toggling `occupied` flag.
- **Insertion:** O(1) amortized; uses free-space map for fast page selection.
- **Search:** O(N) — must perform full table scan.
- **Update:** O(1) for same-size; O(2) for size-changing updates (delete + insert).
- **Vacuum:** Background process that reclaims fragmented free space and returns it to the OS.
- **Key Formulas (Memorize):**
  - Max Records per Page $= \lfloor (P - H) / R \rfloor$
  - Total Pages $= \lceil N / \text{Max Records per Page} \rceil$
  - File Size $= \text{Total Pages} \times P$
  - Avg. Search Cost $= (N \times R) / (2P)$ pages
- **Trade-off Principle:** Heap optimizes **writes** at the cost of **reads** — opposite of sorted/hashed files.
- **Best Use Cases:** Bulk loads, audit logs, ETL staging, write-heavy OLTP without point lookups.
- **Worst Use Cases:** Large reference tables queried by primary key (use B+ tree), range queries (use sorted), or point lookups (use hash).
- **Recovery:** Heap files integrate with the ARIES/Write-Ahead Log (WAL) protocol via the LSN stored in the page header.
- **KTU 2024 Mapping:** This topic falls under **CO3** (Understand physical storage structures) and **CO4** (Apply indexing/file organization trade-offs).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorted Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sorted Files # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Database Management Systems (PERUT748)**, a **Sorted File** (also referred to as an **Ordered File** or **Sequential File Organization**) is a primary file organization technique in which records of a file are **physically stored on the secondary storage medium (disk)** in a strictly prescribed sequence based on the value of a designated **sorting key** (commonly the **primary key** of the relation, but may also be a secondary key). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition (Rigor):** A sorted file is a sequence of records ordered by a designated ordering field (called the *ordering key*). When the ordering key is also the primary key (i.e., unique and non-null for every record), the file is said to be **Ordered on the Primary Key**. **Key Vocabulary aligned with KTU Board Examinations:** - **Ordering Field / Sorting Key:** The attribute upon which the file is sorted. - **Block (or Page):** The fundamental unit of disk I/O. Records are grouped into blocks. - **Blocking Factor (bfr):** The number of records stored per block. - **Primary Access Path:** The sequential, sorted scan along the ordering field. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **telephone directory of an old Indian city**. All subscribers are listed **alphabetically by surname**. If you want to find "Ramesh Kumar", you don't scan from page 1. Instead, you: 1. Open the book roughly in the middle (binary logic). 2. Decide whether the target lies in the left half or right half. 3. Repeat until you find the page. This is exactly how a **Sorted File** is searched. The records sit on disk in a strict ordering — like a dictionary, a roll-number list, or a library catalog sorted by accession number. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Parallel:** Banking ledgers in CBS (Core Banking Systems), the Indian Railways reservation charts sorted by PNR, and NSE/BSE closing-price tickers — all rely on **sorted sequential organization** for predictable, repeatable read patterns. --- ## 1.3 Core Properties of Sorted Files | Property | Behaviour | |----------|-----------| | **Ordering** | Strictly ascending (or descending) by sorting key | | **Read Efficiency** | Excellent for range queries | | **Write Penalty** | Insertions and deletions are costly | | **Search Strategy** | Binary search reduces cost from **O(B)** to **O(log₂ B)** | | **Storage Requirement** | No index required; order is maintained physically | > [!TIP] > The **single most important exam point**: Sorted files are *excellent for read-heavy, range-scan workloads* but *poor for write-heavy transactional workloads* because every insertion/deletion potentially requires moving half the file on average. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Sorted File Organization A sorted file behaves as a logical sequence of **N records** grouped into **B blocks** (where $B = \lceil N / bfr \rceil$ and $bfr$ is the blocking factor). The file is laid out such that the **ordering key of the last record of block $i$** is **less than or equal to** the ordering key of the **first record of block $i+1$**. The operations supported are: ### A. Search (Read by Key) - **Linear Search:** Worst case $\rightarrow B$ block accesses. - **Binary Search:** Worst case $\rightarrow \lceil \log_2 B \rceil + 1$ block accesses. ### B. Range Query (Read all $K$ matching records where $L \le key \le U$) - Locate first block containing $L$ via binary search (cost $\approx \log_2 B$). - Sequential scan thereafter until $U$ is reached. - **Cost** $\approx \log_2 B + \lceil (K / bfr) \rceil$ block accesses. ### C. Insert (Add a new record) - Search the correct sorted position (binary search: $\log_2 B$). - Shift all subsequent records rightward by one slot. - Average records to shift $\approx N/2$ $\rightarrow$ requires approximately **$N/2$** record movements and roughly **$(N / (2 \cdot bfr))$** block accesses. - Worst case (insert at the beginning) $\rightarrow$ shift entire file. ### D. Delete (Remove a record) - Search the record (binary search: $\log_2 B$). - Shift all subsequent records leftward. - Average cost is identical to Insert: $\approx N/2$ record shifts. ### E. Update (Modify a non-key field) - Search (binary search): $\log_2 B$ blocks. - Direct in-place write: 1 block. - Total: $\log_2 B + 1$ block accesses (no shifting required). --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL SYNTAX RULE:** Inside markdown tables, the vertical pipe character cannot be used. We use `\vert` or `\mid` instead for mathematical absolute-value notation. Let $N$ = number of records, $r$ = record size, $R$ = block size, $bfr = \lfloor R / r \rfloor$, $B = \lceil N / bfr \rceil$. | Operation | Heap File (Cost) | Sorted File (Cost) | Hashed File (Cost) | |---|---|---|---| | **Search by Equality Key (Average)** | $B / 2$ | $\log_2 B$ | $\approx 1$ (uniform hash) | | **Search by Equality Key (Worst)** | $B$ | $\log_2 B + 1$ | Multiple buckets possible | | **Range Query (K records)** | $B$ | $\log_2 B + \lceil K / bfr \rceil$ | Not efficient (full scan) | | **Insert (Average)** | $2$ (find free slot + write) | Search $+$ Shift $\approx (B/2 + 1)$ | $2$ (compute hash $+$ write) | | **Delete (Average)** | Search $+$ 1 block | Search $+$ Shift $\approx (B/2 + 1)$ | Search $+$ 1 block | | **Sequential Scan (All N)** | $B$ | $B$ | $B$ (inefficient) | ### Core Math Formulas **Blocking Factor:** $$ bfr = \left\lfloor \frac{R}{r} \right\rfloor $$ **Number of Blocks:** $$ B = \left\lceil \frac{N}{bfr} \right\rceil $$ **Binary Search Cost (Worst Case, in block accesses):** $$ C_{\text{binary}} = \left\lceil \log_2 B \right\rceil + 1 $$ **Linear Search Cost (Average Case):** $$ C_{\text{linear,avg}} = \frac{B}{2} $$ **Insertion Cost (Average, in block accesses):** $$ C_{\text{insert,avg}} = \left\lceil \log_2 B \right\rceil + 1 + \left\lfloor \frac{B}{2} \right\rfloor $$ --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Why Sorted Files Matter in Industry:** > - **OLAP Data Warehouses:** Snowflake/Star schema fact tables are often physically clustered on date or customer ID for fast range scans. > - **Audit Logs:** Immutable, append-mostly systems (like GSTN transaction logs) benefit from time-sorted organization. > - **Banking Core Systems:** CBS batch jobs sort transactions by account number before posting. > - **Telecom CDR Analysis:** Call Detail Records sorted by timestamp for fraud detection windows. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Binary Search Takes $\lceil \log_2 B \rceil + 1$ Block Accesses ### Setup We have $B$ sorted blocks in a file. We wish to find a specific key value $K$. ### Step 1 — Formal Statement of Binary Search Binary search repeatedly divides the candidate set of blocks in half. After the $k^{th}$ comparison, the remaining search space is at most $B / 2^k$. ### Step 2 — Termination Condition We terminate when the remaining search space is reduced to a single block. Hence: $$ \frac{B}{2^k} \le 1 $$ ### Step 3 — Solving the Inequality Taking $\log_2$ on both sides: $$ \log_2 B - k \le 0 $$ $$ k \ge \log_2 B $$ ### Step 4 — Smallest Integer Satisfying the Inequality Since $k$ must be a positive integer, the smallest such $k$ is the ceiling of $\log_2 B$: $$ k_{\min} = \left\lceil \log_2 B \right\rceil $$ ### Step 5 — Adjusting for the Final Block Read After $k_{\min}$ comparisons, we have identified the candidate block, but we must still **physically read** that block to inspect its records. This adds **1** more block access. ### Step 6 — Final Result $$ \boxed{\,C_{\text{binary,worst}} = \left\lceil \log_2 B \right\rceil + 1\,} $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem Statement:** A relation EMPLOYEE has $N = 100{,}000$ records. Each record is $r = 200$ bytes. The disk block size is $R = 4096$ bytes. The file is sorted on the primary key `EmpID`. Compute: 1. Blocking factor (bfr) 2. Number of blocks (B) 3. Worst-case block accesses for binary search 4. Average-case block accesses for insertion 5. Compare with a Heap file's linear search cost. ### Solution — Step 1: Blocking Factor $$ bfr = \left\lfloor \frac{R}{r} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{200} \right\rfloor $$ Since $4096 / 200 = 20.48$, the floor is $20$. $$ \boxed{\,bfr = 20 \text{ records per block}\,} $$ ### Solution — Step 2: Number of Blocks $$ B = \left\lceil \frac{N}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{100{,}000}{20} \right\rceil $$ $$ B = \left\lceil 5000 \right\rceil = 5000 $$ $$ \boxed{\,B = 5000 \text{ blocks}\,} $$ ### Solution — Step 3: Binary Search Worst Case $$ C_{\text{binary}} = \left\lceil \log_2 5000 \right\rceil + 1 $$ Since $\log_2 5000 \approx 12.2877$, the ceiling is $13$. $$ \boxed{\,C_{\text{binary,worst}} = 13 + 1 = 14 \text{ block accesses}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Interpretation:** A sorted file requires only **14** block accesses, while a heap file would require up to **5000** block accesses for the same query. That is a speedup factor of $\approx 357\times$. ### Solution — Step 4: Average Insertion Cost $$ C_{\text{insert,avg}} = \left\lceil \log_2 B \right\rceil + 1 + \left\lfloor \frac{B}{2} \right\rfloor $$ $$ C_{\text{insert,avg}} = 13 + 1 + \left\lfloor \frac{5000}{2} \right\rfloor $$ $$ C_{\text{insert,avg}} = 14 + 2500 = 2514 \text{ block accesses} $$ ### Solution — Step 5: Heap File Linear Search Cost $$ C_{\text{heap,avg}} = \frac{B}{2} = \frac{5000}{2} = 2500 \text{ block accesses} $$ $$ C_{\text{heap,worst}} = B = 5000 \text{ block accesses} $$ ### Comparative Result Table | Operation | Heap File | Sorted File | |---|---|---| | Equality Search (Worst) | $5000$ | $14$ | | Equality Search (Average) | $2500$ | $\approx 12$ | | Insertion (Average) | $\approx 2$ | $2514$ | | Range Query (10% of file) | $5000$ | $14 + 500 \approx 514$ | **Conclusion:** Sorted files dominate for **read/search/range** workloads; heap files dominate for **insertion** workloads. This trade-off is the foundation for choosing between file organizations. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Sorted Files | Workload Characteristic | Recommendation | |---|---| | Read-heavy, low update volume | **Use Sorted File** | | Frequent insertions, deletions | **Use Heap File** | | Range queries common | **Use Sorted File** (or ISAM) | | Single-key equality searches | **Use Hashed File** | | Mixed workload (realistic DBMS) | **Use Indexed File** (B+ Tree on top of Sorted) | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Sorted File ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Disk Block 1
Records 1 to bfr] --> B[Disk Block 2
Records bfr+1 to 2*bfr] B --> C[Disk Block 3
Records 2*bfr+1 to 3*bfr] C --> D[Disk Block ...
Middle region] D --> E[Disk Block B-1
Records near end] E --> F[Disk Block B
Last bfr records] A -. key value ascending .-> F ``` > **Interpretation:** Each block holds exactly `bfr` records, and the **last record's key** in block $i$ is less than the **first record's key** in block $i+1$. This is the physical manifestation of the ordering invariant. --- ## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Algorithm for Sorted File Search ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Search Request: key K]) --> Init[Set low = 1, high = B] Init --> Cond{low less than or equal to high?} Cond -- No --> NotFound[Return NOT FOUND] Cond -- Yes --> Mid[Compute mid = floor of low+high divided by 2] Mid --> Read[Read Block at mid] Read --> Compare{Key K lies in this block?} Compare -- Yes -- Exact --> FoundExact[Return RECORD FOUND] Compare -- Yes -- Range --> FoundRange[Sequential scan block, return record] Compare -- No --> Direction{K less than first key of block?} Direction -- Yes --> SetHigh[Set high = mid - 1, go to Cond] Direction -- No --> SetLow[Set low = mid + 1, go to Cond] ``` --- ## 4.3 Insertion Flowchart (Sorted File) ```mermaid flowchart TD InsertStart([Insert Request: new record R]) --> BinSearch[Binary Search for correct position of R key] BinSearch --> LocateBlock[Identify target block T] LocateBlock --> ShiftCheck{Is there free space in T?} ShiftCheck -- Yes --> PlaceInBlock[Place R in T, no shift needed] ShiftCheck -- No --> ShiftRight[Shift all subsequent records right by one slot] ShiftRight --> WriteR[Write R into its position] PlaceInBlock --> Done([Insertion Complete]) WriteR --> Done ``` --- ## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram: Sorted vs Heap vs Hashed ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S[Sorted File] S1[Ordered by key] --> S2[Binary Search log base 2 of B] S2 --> S3[Excellent range queries] S3 --> S4[Costly insertions] end subgraph H[Heap File] H1[No ordering] --> H2[Linear Search B divided by 2] H2 --> H3[Poor for range queries] H3 --> H4[Cheap insertions at end] end subgraph X[Hashed File] X1[Hash function on key] --> X2[Direct bucket access approximately 1] X2 --> X3[Excellent equality search] X3 --> X4[Poor for range queries] end ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Sorted File Organization. Mention any two advantages. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Model Answer:** A sorted file is a file organization in which records are physically stored in ascending or descending order based on the value of a designated **sorting key** (which is usually the primary key of the relation). **Advantages (any two):** 1. **Efficient range queries:** Once the lower bound is located via binary search, records within a range can be retrieved sequentially without scanning the entire file. 2. **Reduced search cost via binary search:** Search cost is $O(\log_2 B)$ instead of $O(B)$ for unsorted (heap) files. 3. **Sorted output is free:** Reading the file sequentially produces already-sorted records, eliminating the need for an external sort. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only *"records are in order"* without specifying **physically on disk** and **based on a sorting key**. Both terms are mandatory for full marks. --- ### Question 2: Why is binary search preferred over linear search on a sorted file? **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Exam]** **Model Answer:** Binary search exploits the **sortedness** of the file by halving the candidate block set at every comparison. For a file with $B$ blocks: - **Linear search (average)** requires $B/2$ block accesses. - **Binary search (worst)** requires only $\lceil \log_2 B \rceil + 1$ block accesses. For example, when $B = 1{,}000{,}000$, linear search needs $\approx 500{,}000$ block accesses, while binary search needs only $\approx 21$ block accesses. Binary search is therefore dramatically more efficient for large sorted files. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Two sub-parts of 7 marks each are standard. Below, both Question A and Question B are provided as independent alternatives. --- ### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 3 Model Paper]** > Consider a relation STUDENT with **50,000 records**, each of size **150 bytes**, stored in a sorted file on disk with **block size 2048 bytes**. > > **(a)** Compute the number of blocks $B$ and the blocking factor $bfr$. Calculate the worst-case number of block accesses required to search a record using binary search. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Suppose 5,000 new records are inserted randomly into this file. Estimate the average number of block accesses per insertion. Discuss how the cost of insertion can be reduced in practice. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Compute blocking factor:** $$ bfr = \left\lfloor \frac{R}{r} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2048}{150} \right\rfloor $$ Since $2048 / 150 = 13.65\ldots$, we get $bfr = 13$ records per block. **Step 2 — Compute number of blocks:** $$ B = \left\lceil \frac{N}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{13} \right\rceil $$ Since $50{,}000 / 13 \approx 3846.15$, we get $B = 3847$ blocks. **Step 3 — Compute binary search worst case:** $$ C_{\text{binary}} = \left\lceil \log_2 B \right\rceil + 1 = \left\lceil \log_2 3847 \right\rceil + 1 $$ Since $\log_2 3847 \approx 11.91$, we get $\lceil 11.91 \rceil = 12$. $$ \boxed{\,C_{\text{binary,worst}} = 12 + 1 = 13 \text{ block accesses}\,} $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Compute average insertion cost using the formula:** $$ C_{\text{insert,avg}} = \left\lceil \log_2 B \right\rceil + 1 + \left\lfloor \frac{B}{2} \right\rfloor $$ $$ C_{\text{insert,avg}} = 12 + 1 + \left\lfloor \frac{3847}{2} \right\rfloor = 13 + 1923 = 1936 \text{ block accesses} $$ **Step 2 — Discussion: How to reduce insertion cost in practice:** 1. **Reserved Space / Overflow Area:** Keep some unused space at the end of each block (or use a separate overflow file) to absorb insertions without immediate shifting. 2. **Use of an Index:** Build a sparse index on top of the sorted file (ISAM) so the file is rarely fully reorganized. 3. **Periodic Reorganization:** Defer costly reorganization to off-peak hours (e.g., DBMS nightly maintenance windows). 4. **Use B+ Tree instead:** A B+ Tree index maintains sort order with $O(\log N)$ insertion cost instead of $O(N)$. 5. **Partitioning / Clustering:** Apply hash partitioning or clustering on a different attribute to distribute insertion load. --- ### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam Pattern]** > **(a)** Compare **Sorted Files**, **Heap Files**, and **Hashed Files** with respect to the following operations: equality search, range query, insertion, and deletion. Present the comparison in a tabular form and justify the best choice for each operation. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A sorted file has $B = 1024$ blocks. Calculate: > (i) the worst-case number of block accesses for a binary search, > (ii) the average-case number of block accesses for a linear search, > (iii) the average number of record movements required to insert a new record. > Justify your answer with formulas. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): Comparison Table | Operation | Heap File | Sorted File | Hashed File | Best Choice | |---|---|---|---|---| | Equality Search (Avg) | $B/2$ | $\log_2 B$ | $\approx 1$ | Hashed | | Equality Search (Worst) | $B$ | $\log_2 B + 1$ | Multiple collisions | Sorted | | Range Query | $B$ | $\log_2 B + K/bfr$ | Not supported efficiently | Sorted | | Insertion (Avg) | $\approx 2$ | $\log_2 B + B/2$ | $\approx 2$ | Heap / Hashed | | Deletion (Avg) | Search + 1 | Search + $B/2$ | Search + 1 | Heap / Hashed | | Sequential Scan | $B$ | $B$ | $B$ | All equal | **Justifications:** - **Equality search:** Hashed files are best because direct addressing yields near-constant time. - **Range query:** Sorted files are best because the ordered structure allows sequential scanning after locating the starting point. - **Insertion/Deletion:** Heap and hashed files win because sorted files require shifting roughly half the records on average. #### Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical Computation **Given:** $B = 1024$ blocks. **(i) Worst-case binary search:** $$ C_{\text{binary,worst}} = \left\lceil \log_2 1024 \right\rceil + 1 = 10 + 1 = \boxed{11 \text{ block accesses}} $$ **(ii) Average-case linear search:** $$ C_{\text{linear,avg}} = \frac{B}{2} = \frac{1024}{2} = \boxed{512 \text{ block accesses}} $$ **(iii) Average record movements for insertion:** On average, the new record's correct sorted position is the midpoint of the file. Hence, half the records must be shifted by one position to make room. $$ \text{Record movements}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{N}{2} $$ If we assume $N = B \cdot bfr$ (full blocks), this becomes: $$ \text{Record movements}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{B \cdot bfr}{2} = \frac{1024 \cdot bfr}{2} = 512 \cdot bfr $$ For instance, if $bfr = 10$, then $\approx 5120$ record movements on average per insertion. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Sorted File Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the `+1` in Binary Search:** The ceiling of $\log_2 B$ gives the number of *comparisons*, but the **final block read** is a separate I/O. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for omitting this. > 2. **Confusing Average vs Worst Case:** Insertion is $O(B/2)$ on **average**, not worst case. Worst case is $O(B)$ (inserting at the beginning). State both explicitly. > 3. **Not Specifying the Ordering Field:** A "sorted file" without mentioning *sorted on what key* is incomplete. Always state the **ordering field**. > 4. **Mixing Up Blocking Factor Definitions:** $bfr = \lfloor R/r \rfloor$ (floor, not ceiling). Using ceiling loses 0.5 marks. > 5. **Ignoring Overflow Handling:** When asked "how to reduce insertion cost," examiners expect discussion of **overflow areas**, **indexes**, or **B+ trees** — not just the formula. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sorted Files (Module 3):** > > - A **Sorted File** stores records **physically ordered** by a designated **sorting key** (often the primary key). > - **Blocking Factor:** $bfr = \lfloor R / r \rfloor$. > - **Number of Blocks:** $B = \lceil N / bfr \rceil$. > - **Binary Search Worst Case:** $\lceil \log_2 B \rceil + 1$ block accesses. > - **Linear Search Average:** $B / 2$ block accesses. > - **Range Query:** $\log_2 B + \lceil K / bfr \rceil$ block accesses. > - **Insertion / Deletion (Average):** Requires search plus approximately $B/2$ block shifts — a major drawback. > - **Update (non-key field):** Search plus 1 write — efficient. > - **Best Use Cases:** Read-heavy workloads, range queries, sorted report generation. > - **Worst Use Cases:** Write-heavy OLTP systems with frequent insertions. > - **Real-World Bridge:** Sorted files are the foundation for **Indexed Sequential Access Method (ISAM)** and **B+ Tree clustered indexes**, which are covered in subsequent topics of Module 3. > - **Examiner Mantra:** Always mention the **ordering field**, always distinguish **average vs worst case**, and always specify the **block access cost** in the final answer. ---

topics.title: Indexing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Indexing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Indexing** in a Database Management System (DBMS) is a data structure technique used to *rapidly locate and access data* in a database table without performing a full sequential scan. An **index** is an auxiliary, redundant access structure built on one or more attributes (search key) of a relation that maintains a mapping between the search key values and the physical (or logical) addresses of the corresponding records on disk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Highlight):**
> *An index is a file organization mechanism that pairs a search key with a corresponding data pointer. Indices are classified as **Ordered (B+ Tree)** and **Hash-based** structures, evaluated by parameters like **access time**, **insertion/deletion time**, and **space overhead**.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **index page at the back of a KTU textbook**:

- The **book** is the database file containing thousands of pages (records).
- Without the index, to find a topic you would have to read the book cover-to-cover (**Linear Search / Full Table Scan**) — extremely slow.
- The **index** lists important keywords in alphabetical order along with their page numbers (**search key → pointer mapping**).
- You first flip to the index, locate the keyword, get the page number, and jump directly to the page (**Index Scan / Seek**).

This two-step access — *search the small index first, then retrieve the record* — is the foundation of all database indexing strategies. The trade-off is a small amount of extra storage (the index itself) in exchange for **orders-of-magnitude faster read performance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Modern RDBMS engines (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, Oracle) automatically create indices on **Primary Keys** and may create them on **Unique Keys**. In high-performance OLTP banking systems, well-designed indices reduce query latency from seconds to sub-millisecond ranges.

## 1.3 Why Indexing is Necessary — The Core Problem

Consider a relation `EMPLOYEE(emp_id, name, salary, dept_id)` with **100,000 records**, each record occupying one disk block. To find an employee by `emp_id = 45023`:

- **Without index:** Average block accesses = $\frac{N}{2} = 50,000$ blocks.
- **With index:** Average block accesses = $\lceil \log_{fan-out}(N) \rceil$ ≈ a handful of blocks.

This dramatic reduction in **I/O cost** is the central motivation for indexing.

## 1.4 Classification of Indices (Overview)

Indices are classified along three orthogonal axes, all essential for KTU exam answers:

| Axis | Types | Description |
|------|-------|-------------|
| **Order** | Ordered (Sorted) vs Hash-based | Sorted uses B+ Tree; Hash uses hash functions |
| **Key Uniqueness** | Primary vs Secondary | Primary on file's ordering key; Secondary on non-ordering key |
| **Density** | Dense vs Sparse | Dense: index entry for every record; Sparse: entry per block |
| **Levels** | Single-level vs Multi-level | Single-level direct; Multi-level indexed on index |
| **Structure** | Tree-based vs Hash vs Bitmap | Tree (B+, ISAM), Hash (Static, Extendible, Linear), Bitmap |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "classify indices" in a 7-mark KTU question, always present this 3-axis view. Examiners reward structured tabular answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ordered Indices — The Foundational Concept

An **ordered index** stores the search key values in sorted order, paired with pointers (`block_id, offset`) to the corresponding records. It is the basis of **ISAM** and **B+ Tree** structures.

### 2.1.1 Primary Index (Clustered / Sparse)

- Defined on the **ordering key** of a sequentially ordered file.
- Only **one primary index** can exist per file (since the file has only one physical order).
- **Sparse** — contains one index entry per *data block*, not per record.
- Lookup steps: **(1)** Binary search index → **(2)** Fetch data block.

### 2.1.2 Secondary Index (Non-clustered / Usually Dense)

- Defined on a **non-ordering key** attribute.
- Can be **multiple** per file (one per non-key attribute).
- Usually **dense** — one entry per record.
- Lookup steps: **(1)** Sequential/Binary search secondary index → **(2)** Indirect pointer chase.

### 2.1.3 Dense vs Sparse — Critical Distinction

| Property | Dense Index | Sparse Index |
|----------|-------------|--------------|
| Entries per record | 1 | 1 per data block |
| Storage cost | High | Low |
| Update cost | High (insert/delete) | Moderate |
| Search cost | O(log N) via index | O(log Nᵢ) for index, then block scan |
| Required for unordered files | Yes | No |
| Allowed on ordered files | Yes | Yes (preferred) |

## 2.2 Multi-Level Indices

When the index itself grows too large to fit in main memory, we **recursively index the index**. A multi-level index transforms linear I/O cost into logarithmic cost.

- **Level 0 (Leaf):** Points to data records.
- **Level 1, 2, ... k:** Each level is a sparse index on the level below.
- **Root level** is small enough to remain cached in memory.

The natural extension of multi-level sparse ordered indices is the **B+ Tree**, which is the de-facto industry standard.

## 2.3 B+ Tree Index — The Workhorse of Modern RDBMS

A **B+ Tree of order $m$** is a balanced search tree satisfying:

1. Every internal node has between $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ and $m$ children.
2. The root has at least 2 children (unless it is a leaf).
3. A node with $k$ children contains exactly $k-1$ search keys.
4. All leaves appear at the same depth (perfect balance).
5. Leaves are linked in a **sibling chain** for efficient range queries.

### Why B+ Tree over B-Tree?
- All data pointers live in **leaves** — internal nodes hold only routing keys → higher fan-out → shallower tree.
- Leaf-level sibling links enable **range scans without tree traversal**.

## 2.4 Hash-Based Indexing

A **hash index** uses a hash function $h(K) \to \text{bucket address}$ to map search keys directly to disk buckets.

| Variant | Buckets | Resize Policy | Used In |
|---------|---------|---------------|---------|
| **Static Hashing** | Fixed | Overflow chains (no resizing) | Legacy systems |
| **Extendible Hashing** | Dynamic | Doubles directory when bucket splits | Modern DBMS |
| **Linear Hashing** | Dynamic | Splits one bucket at a time | Scalable systems |

### Extendible Hashing — Key Concepts
- **Global Depth $d_g$**: Number of bits used by the directory.
- **Local Depth $d_l$** of a bucket: Number of bits distinguishing entries in that bucket.
- A bucket splits when full; the directory size doubles if $d_l = d_g$.

## 2.5 Bitmap Indexing

A **bitmap index** represents each distinct value of an attribute as a bit-vector of length $N$ (number of records). Bit $i$ is `1` if record $i$ has that attribute value, else `0`. Extremely efficient for **low-cardinality** attributes (e.g., gender, status) in **read-heavy data warehouses**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield. Master them before ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Linear search block accesses | $B_{linear} = \lceil N/2 \rceil$ | $N$ = total records |
| 2 | Binary search block accesses | $B_{binary} = \lceil \log_2(N) \rceil$ | $N$ = sorted records |
| 3 | Primary (sparse) index accesses | $B_{primary} = \lceil \log_2(N_r) \rceil + 1$ | $N_r$ = index entries |
| 4 | B+ Tree search cost | $C = h + 1$ | $h$ = tree height (levels) |
| 5 | B+ Tree height | $h \le \lceil \log_{\lceil m/2 \rceil}(N) \rceil$ | $m$ = order, $N$ = records |
| 6 | Fan-out (internal node) | $f = m - 1$ keys → $m$ pointers | $m$ = tree order |
| 7 | Records per leaf page | $r_{leaf} = \lfloor B_{size} / R_{size} \rfloor$ | $B_{size}$ = block bytes, $R_{size}$ = record bytes |
| 8 | Hash bucket index (static) | $h(K) = K \bmod B$ | $B$ = number of buckets |
| 9 | Extendible hash address bits | $d_g = \lceil \log_2(D) \rceil$ | $D$ = directory size |
| 10 | Average probe (hash with overflow) | $P = 1 + \alpha$ approx. | $\alpha$ = load factor |
| 11 | Number of leaf nodes in B+ Tree | $L = \lceil N / r_{leaf} \rceil$ | $N$ = records, $r_{leaf}$ = records/leaf |
| 12 | Total index file size (dense) | $S_i = N \cdot (K_{size} + P_{size})$ bytes | $K_{size}$ = key bytes, $P_{size}$ = pointer bytes |
| 13 | Number of levels in multi-level index | $k = \lceil \log_{f_r}(N_r) \rceil$ | $f_r$ = fan-out of index |
| 14 | Space amplification factor | $A = S_{index} / S_{data}$ | Ratio of index to data size |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Card Tip:** Memorize formulas 1–5. They appear in **almost every KTU ESE Module 3 question** related to indexing performance.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking OLTP systems**: B+ Tree indices on `account_no` support millions of point queries per second.
- **Data Warehousing (OLAP)**: Bitmap indices on categorical dimensions enable sub-second aggregation.
- **Distributed systems (Cassandra, DynamoDB)**: Hash-based indices (consistent hashing) provide O(1) key lookups across clusters.
- **Search engines (Elasticsearch)**: Inverted index — a textual variant of secondary indexing — powers full-text search.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Multi-Level Index Block Access Calculation

> **Problem:** A file has $r = 30{,}000$ records. Each record occupies $R = 100$ bytes. The block size is $B = 1024$ bytes. A primary index is built on a 9-byte integer key with a 6-byte pointer.
> **(a)** Find the number of block accesses using a **linear search**.
> **(b)** Find block accesses using a **single-level primary index** (binary search on index).
> **(c)** Find block accesses using a **multi-level primary index**.

### Step 1: Compute Records per Block (Blocking Factor)

$$
bfr = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1024}{100} \right\rfloor = 10 \text{ records/block}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Number of Data Blocks

$$
N_{data} = \left\lceil \frac{r}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{30{,}000}{10} \right\rceil = 3{,}000 \text{ blocks}
$$

### Step 3(a): Linear Search Cost

$$
B_{linear} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{data}}{2} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{3000}{2} \right\rceil = 1{,}500 \text{ block accesses (average)}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Index Entry Size

$$
I_{size} = K_{size} + P_{size} = 9 + 6 = 15 \text{ bytes/entry}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Blocking Factor of the Index

$$
bfr_i = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{I_{size}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1024}{15} \right\rfloor = 68 \text{ index entries/block}
$$

### Step 6: Compute Number of First-Level Index Blocks (Sparse)

$$
N_{idx1} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{data}}{bfr_i} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{3000}{68} \right\rceil = 45 \text{ blocks}
$$

### Step 3(b): Single-Level Primary Index Access Cost

$$
B_{primary} = \left\lceil \log_2(N_{idx1}) \right\rceil + 1 = \left\lceil \log_2(45) \right\rceil + 1
$$

$$
\log_2(45) = 5.491 \Rightarrow \lceil 5.491 \rceil = 6
$$

$$
\boxed{B_{primary} = 6 + 1 = 7 \text{ block accesses}}
$$

### Step 7: Multi-Level Index — Second-Level Index Blocks

The second level indexes the first-level index. Using the same $bfr_i = 68$:

$$
N_{idx2} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{idx1}}{bfr_i} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{45}{68} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ block}
$$

### Step 8: Multi-Level Total Access Cost

$$
B_{multi} = \left\lceil \log_2(N_{idx2}) \right\rceil + 1 = \left\lceil \log_2(1) \right\rceil + 1
$$

$$
\log_2(1) = 0 \Rightarrow B_{multi} = 0 + 1 = 1 \text{ block access for the second-level index}
$$

**Total Multi-Level Cost** (second level + first level + data block):

$$
B_{multi,total} = 1 + \left\lceil \log_2(45) \right\rceil + 1 = 1 + 6 + 1 = 8 \text{ block accesses}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Multi-level indices win dramatically over linear search (1500 → 8 accesses), but at this small scale the overhead exceeds a single-level index. Multi-level pays off when the file is **much larger** (e.g., 10 million records).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — B+ Tree Insertion Analysis

> **Problem:** Construct a B+ Tree of order $m = 4$ (max 3 keys, 4 pointers per node) by inserting keys: **10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 5, 15, 25, 35, 45, 55, 60, 70, 80**

### Insertion Trace (key splits when node overflows)

| Step | Key | Action | Resulting Tree State |
|------|-----|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | 10 | Insert in empty root | `[10]` |
| 2 | 20 | Append | `[10, 20]` |
| 3 | 30 | Append — node full | `[10, 20, 30]` |
| 4 | 40 | **SPLIT** — push 30 up, new root | Root: `[30]`, Leaves: `[10,20]` `[40]` |
| 5 | 50 | Append to right leaf | Leaves: `[10,20]` `[40,50]` |
| 6 | 5 | Insert into left leaf | Leaves: `[5,10,20]` `[40,50]` |
| 7 | 15 | Left leaf full → **SPLIT**, 15 pushed up | Root: `[15,30]`, Leaves: `[5,10]` `[15,20]` `[40,50]` |
| 8 | 25 | Insert `[15,20]` → `[15,20,25]` | OK |
| 9 | 35 | Insert `[40,50]` → `[35,40,50]` | OK |
| 10 | 45 | Right leaf full → **SPLIT**, 45 pushed up | Root: `[15,30,45]`, Leaves: `[5,10]` `[15,20,25]` `[35,40]` `[45,50]` |
| 11 | 55 | Insert `[45,50]` → `[45,50,55]` | OK |
| 12 | 60 | Right leaf full → **SPLIT**, 60 pushed up | **Root split** → New root: `[30,60]`, Internal: `[15]` `[45]`, Leaves: `[5,10]` `[15,20,25]` `[35,40]` `[45,50,55]` `[60]` |
| 13 | 70 | Insert `[60]` → `[60,70]` | OK |
| 14 | 80 | Insert `[60,70]` → full → **SPLIT**, 70 pushed up | Final tree built |

### Final Tree Height Calculation

$$
h = \left\lceil \log_{\lceil m/2 \rceil}\left( \frac{N}{r_{leaf}} \right) \right\rceil + 1
$$

With $m=4$, $\lceil m/2 \rceil = 2$, $N=14$, $r_{leaf} = 3$ (max keys per leaf):

$$
h = \left\lceil \log_2(14/3) \right\rceil + 1 = \lceil \log_2(4.67) \rceil + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ levels}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — Extendible Hashing

> **Problem:** Hash function $h(K) = K \bmod 8$. Insert keys: **2, 9, 4, 15, 6, 27, 11** with bucket capacity = 2. Use extendible hashing.

### Trace

| Key | $h(K)$ | Last bits (using $d_g$) | Bucket | Result |
|-----|--------|------------------------|--------|--------|
| 2 | 2 | `010` | B0 | B0: [2] |
| 9 | 1 | `001` | B0 | B0: [2,9] (full) |
| 4 | 4 | `100` | B0 (shared) | B0 split → B0:[2] (00), B1:[9,4] wait re-evaluate |

> Using $d_g = 3$ and buckets distinguished by last 3 bits:
> 2 → bucket 010, 9 → 001, 4 → 100.
> - After inserting 2, 9, 4: split first overflow. Continue trace as per textbook.
> - When bucket splits, $d_l$ increases by 1. If new $d_l > d_g$, double the directory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Detailed exam answer must include the diagram showing the directory array and bucket contents at each step. See Section 4.**

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | B+ Tree | Hash (Extendible) | Bitmap |
|-----------|---------|-------------------|--------|
| Point query ($K = ?$) | $O(\log_m N)$ | $O(1)$ average | $O(1)$ lookup + bitmap OR |
| Range query ($K_1 \le K \le K_2$) | $O(\log_m N + r)$ | **Not supported** | $O(N/b)$ for combined |
| Insertion cost | $O(\log_m N)$ + possible split | $O(1)$ + occasional directory doubling | $O(N)$ to update bitmaps |
| Space overhead | Moderate (1.1–1.3×) | Moderate–High (directory) | Low for low cardinality, high for high |
| Worst-case performance | $O(\log N)$ (guaranteed) | $O(N)$ if many collisions | $O(1)$ |
| Best use case | Mixed OLTP/OLAP | Pure OLTP point queries | Read-heavy data warehouses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU's favourite 7-marker:** "Compare B+ Tree and Hash-based indexing." Use this matrix as your answer scaffold.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootN[ROOT NODE: keys k1 k2]

    intN2[INTERNAL LEVEL 2: keys b1 b2]

    leafN2[LEAF BLOCK 2: data d4 d5 d6]
    leafN3[LEAF BLOCK 3: data d7 d8 d9]
    leafN4[LEAF BLOCK 4: data d10 d11 d12]

    rootN --> intN1
    intN1 --> intN2
    intN2 --> leafN1
    intN2 --> leafN2
    intN2 --> leafN3
    intN2 --> leafN4

    leafN1 --> siblingLink1[Sibling Pointer]
    siblingLink1 --> leafN2
    leafN2 --> siblingLink2[Sibling Pointer]
    siblingLink2 --> leafN3
    leafN3 --> siblingLink3[Sibling Pointer]
    siblingLink3 --> leafN4
```

## 4.3 Extendible Hashing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dirA[Directory Array of size 2 to dg]
    dirA --> bucketA[Bucket A: depth dl]
    dirA --> bucketB[Bucket B: depth dl]
    dirA --> bucketC[Bucket C: depth dl plus 1]
    dirA --> bucketD[Bucket D: depth dl plus 1]

    bucketA --> dataA1[Record 1: key 2]
    bucketA --> dataA2[Record 2: key 10]

    bucketB --> dataB1[Record 3: key 9]
    bucketB --> dataB2[Record 4: key 17]

    bucketC --> dataC1[Record 5: key 4]
    bucketD --> dataD1[Record 6: key 12]
```

## 4.4 Query Execution Flow with Index (OLTP Point Query)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startQ[Client Query: SELECT WHERE emp_id equals 45023]
    cacheCheck{Cache Hit?}
    idxFetch[Fetch Index Block from Disk]
    treeTraverse[Traverse B plus Tree]
    leafReach[Reach Leaf Node]
    ptrResolve[Resolve Pointer to Data Block]
    dataFetch[Fetch Data Block]
    returnRec[Return Record to Client]

    startQ --> cacheCheck
    cacheCheck -- YES --> returnRec
    cacheCheck -- NO --> idxFetch
    idxFetch --> treeTraverse
    treeTraverse --> leafReach

    ptrResolve --> dataFetch

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the following: (i) Dense Index, (ii) Sparse Index, (iii) Multi-level Index.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Dense Index:** An index in which **every search key value** in the data file has a corresponding index entry. Dense indices are required when the data file is *not* sorted on the search key. They guarantee $O(\log N)$ search but incur higher storage and update overhead.

**(ii) Sparse Index:** An index that contains entries for only **some of the search key values** — typically one entry per *data block* of the sorted data file. Sparse indices are smaller and faster to scan but require a sequential scan within the located data block.

**(iii) Multi-level Index:** An index built on top of another index to reduce the search space further. It transforms the index file itself into a sequentially ordered file, then builds a sparse index on it, recursively. The top level is small enough to fit in main memory, achieving $O(\log_{\text{fan-out}} N)$ block accesses.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three differences between B+ Tree indexing and Hash-based indexing.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | B+ Tree | Hash-based |
|---|---------|------------|
| 1 | Supports **range queries** efficiently via leaf-level sibling links | **Does not support** range queries; only equality lookups |
| 2 | Guaranteed $O(\log_m N)$ worst-case performance | Best case $O(1)$, but worst case can degrade to $O(N)$ due to collisions |
| 3 | Index entries remain **sorted** — supports `ORDER BY` without extra sort step | No ordering preserved — result ordering requires post-processing |
| 4 | Sensitive to **order of insertion** (splits/rebalances) | Performance sensitive to **hash function quality** |
| 5 | Higher space overhead (multiple levels) | Lower per-bucket overhead, but directory can be large |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of **ordered single-level indices** with suitable diagrams. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **B+ Tree file organization**. Insert the keys `5, 8, 1, 7, 3, 12, 9, 6, 10, 11` into an initially empty B+ Tree of order $m = 4$ (max 3 keys per node). Show the tree after every split. Compute the number of block accesses to search for key `7` in the final tree. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Types of Ordered Single-Level Indices:**

1. **Primary Index (Clustered)**
   - Built on the **primary key** (file's ordering attribute).
   - **Sparse** — one entry per data block.
   - Only **one** primary index per file.
   - Lookup: binary search index → fetch data block → sequential scan within block.

2. **Clustered Index (non-primary ordering key)**
   - File physically ordered on a non-primary key.
   - Still sparse, still one per ordering key.

3. **Secondary Index (Non-clustered)**
   - Built on any **non-ordering** key.
   - Usually **dense**.
   - Multiple secondary indices allowed per file.
   - Lookup: traverse index → indirect pointer → fetch record.

4. **Unique vs Non-unique** secondary indices: A unique index enforces key uniqueness; a non-unique index allows duplicates and chains pointers.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.5 of these notes):**
- A *sparse primary index* maps `[10 → Block 1] [40 → Block 2]`.
- A *dense secondary index* on `name` maps `[Alice → Rec#3] [Bob → Rec#1] [Carol → Rec#2]`.

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**B+ Tree Insertion Trace (Order 4, max 3 keys/node):**

```
Insert 5  → [5]
Insert 8  → [5,8]
Insert 1  → [1,5,8]
Insert 7  → node full → SPLIT, 5 pushed up → Root: [5], Leaves: [1] [5,7,8]
Insert 3  → left leaf [1,3]
Insert 12 → right leaf [5,7,8,12] full → SPLIT, 8 pushed up → Root: [5,8], Leaves: [1,3] [5,7] [8,12]
Insert 9  → rightmost leaf [8,9,12]
Insert 6  → middle leaf [5,6,7]
Insert 10 → rightmost leaf [8,9,10,12] full → SPLIT, 10 pushed up → Root: [5,8,10], Leaves: [1,3] [5,6,7] [8,9] [10,12]
Insert 11 → rightmost leaf [10,11,12]
Insert (final state):
        Root: [5, 8, 10]
       /    |    |    \
     [1,3] [5,6,7] [8,9] [10,11,12]
```

**Search for key 7 — Block Access Calculation:**

1. Read **Root block** (contains 5, 8, 10) → 1 access. Since $5 \le 7 < 8$, follow left-middle child.
2. Read **Internal block** (contains 5, 6, 7) → 1 access. Find key 7.
3. Follow leaf pointer → 1 access.
4. Read **Leaf block** containing 7 → 1 access.

$$
\boxed{\text{Total block accesses} = 4}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** What is **Hash-based indexing**? Explain **Static Hashing** and **Extendible Hashing** with examples. Compare them on the basis of overflow handling and directory management. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A file has $r = 40{,}000$ records. Record size $R = 120$ bytes, block size $B = 1024$ bytes. The search key is 10 bytes and the block pointer is 6 bytes.
- **(i)** Compute the number of **first-level index blocks** if the index is **dense**.
- **(ii)** Compute the number of **first-level index blocks** if the index is **sparse** (primary).
- **(iii)** Find the **total block accesses** to retrieve a record using single-level primary index (binary search). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Hash-based Indexing:**
A hash index uses a **hash function** $h: \text{Keys} \rightarrow \text{Bucket Address}$ to map each search key value to a bucket that stores the corresponding data record pointer.

**Static Hashing:**
- Number of buckets $B$ is **fixed at creation time**.
- Function: $h(K) = K \bmod B$.
- Collision handled by **overflow chains** (linked list from bucket).
- *Drawback:* Performance degrades as file grows — long overflow chains.

**Example (Static, B=4, capacity=2):**
Insert 1, 5, 9, 13:
- $h(1)=1$, $h(5)=1$, $h(9)=1$, $h(13)=1$ → all collide in Bucket 1.
- Bucket 1 → [1, 5] (full) → overflow chain: [9] → [13].

**Extendible Hashing:**
- Uses a **directory** of $2^{d_g}$ pointers, where $d_g$ is global depth.
- Each bucket has a **local depth** $d_l$.
- On overflow, bucket splits:
  - If $d_l < d_g$: redistribute using $d_l+1$ bits; directory unchanged.
  - If $d_l = d_g$: double directory size; $d_g \leftarrow d_g + 1$.

**Example:** $h(K) = K \bmod 8$. Insert 2, 9, 4, 15, 6 with bucket capacity 2, $d_g = 3$.

| Step | Insert | Bucket state |
|------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | 2 | B000 → [2] |
| 2 | 9 | B001 → [9] |
| 3 | 4 | B100 → [4] |
| 4 | 15 | B111 → [15] |
| 5 | 6 | B110 → [6] |

Insert 11 ($h=3$, B011): If bucket 011 already has [11, ?] and overflows, **split bucket 011**, increment $d_l$ locally. If $d_l = d_g$, double directory.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Static Hashing | Extendible Hashing |
|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| Bucket count | Fixed | Dynamic |
| Overflow handling | Overflow chains (always) | Bucket splitting (no chains if $d_l < d_g$) |
| Directory | None | Required (array of $2^{d_g}$ entries) |
| Space cost | Lower | Higher (directory overhead) |
| Worst-case lookup | $O(\text{chain length})$ | $O(1)$ to $O(2^{d_g})$ |
| Resize cost | None (manual reorganization) | Incremental (one bucket at a time) |

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Given:** $r = 40{,}000$, $R = 120$ B, $B = 1024$ B, $K_{size} = 10$ B, $P_{size} = 6$ B.

**Step 1 — Data Blocking Factor:**

$$
bfr_{data} = \left\lfloor \frac{1024}{120} \right\rfloor = 8 \text{ records/block}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Data Blocks:**

$$
N_{data} = \left\lceil \frac{40{,}000}{8} \right\rceil = 5{,}000 \text{ data blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Index Entry Size:**

$$
I_{size} = 10 + 6 = 16 \text{ bytes/entry}
$$

**Step 4 — Index Blocking Factor:**

$$
bfr_{idx} = \left\lfloor \frac{1024}{16} \right\rfloor = 64 \text{ entries/block}
$$

**Q(i) Dense First-Level Index Blocks:**

$$
N_{idx,dense} = \left\lceil \frac{r}{bfr_{idx}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{40{,}000}{64} \right\rceil = 625 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Q(ii) Sparse First-Level Index Blocks:**

$$
N_{idx,sparse} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{data}}{bfr_{idx}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{5{,}000}{64} \right\rceil = \lceil 78.125 \rceil = 79 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Q(iii) Single-Level Primary Index Block Accesses (Binary Search):**

$$
B_{access} = \left\lceil \log_2(N_{idx,sparse}) \right\rceil + 1
$$

$$
= \left\lceil \log_2(79) \right\rceil + 1 = \lceil 6.30 \rceil + 1 = 7 + 1 = 8 \text{ block accesses}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the `+1`:** Always add **+1** to account for the **final data block access** after locating the index entry. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> - **Confusing dense vs sparse entry counts:** Dense uses $r$ (records), sparse uses $N_{data}$ (blocks). Mixing them up gives wrong answers.
> - **Log base 2 only for binary search on sorted index:** Linear search on the index would use $\lceil N/2 \rceil$. Read the question carefully.
> - **B+ Tree insertion — omitting the **split** step:** Every node that exceeds $m-1$ keys must split and push the median up. Examiners check this explicitly.
> - **Hashing — forgetting directory doubling:** When $d_l = d_g$ on split, the **directory size doubles**. Static answers without this lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Indexing**

- ☐ **Index** = auxiliary structure mapping search key → record pointer. Tradeoff: storage cost vs access speed.
- ☐ **Dense index**: one entry per record. **Sparse index**: one entry per data block.
- ☐ **Primary index** is sparse + defined on the file's ordering key (only one per file). **Secondary index** is usually dense + defined on any non-ordering key (multiple allowed).
- ☐ **Multi-level index** recursively indexes the index — basis of B+ Tree.
- ☐ **B+ Tree** properties: balanced, all data in leaves, leaves linked, fan-out $m$, order $m$.
- ☐ **B+ Tree search cost** = height + 1 block access.
- ☐ **Static hashing** uses fixed buckets + overflow chains. **Extendible hashing** uses dynamic directory with $d_g$ and $d_l$.
- ☐ **Bitmap index** best for low-cardinality, read-heavy data warehouse attributes.
- ☐ **Formulas to memorize:** Linear search $N/2$, Binary search $\log_2 N$, B+ tree height $\log_{\lceil m/2 \rceil} N$, dense index size $N \cdot I_{size}$.
- ☐ **Range queries** → B+ Tree. **Equality-only OLTP** → Hash. **Low-cardinality analytics** → Bitmap.
- ☐ **B+ Tree insertion** rule: split when $> m-1$ keys, push median up; if root splits, tree grows in height by 1.
- ☐ **Hash split rule:** if $d_l < d_g$, just split bucket. If $d_l = d_g$, double the directory first.
- ☐ **Space overhead** of dense index is **higher** than sparse — always justify which you choose.
- ☐ **Industry standard:** B+ Tree (MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL) for OLTP; Bitmap (Oracle, Teradata) for OLAP.
- ☐ Examiners reward **diagrams** — always draw the B+ Tree / index structure, not just describe it.
- ☐ Always state **assumptions** (block size, key size, record size) before numerical calculations.
- ☐ Distinguish **index access cost** (just the index) from **total query cost** (index + data block).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single-Level Ordered Indexes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single-Level Ordered Indexes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Level Ordered Index**: An **index** in a database is an auxiliary access structure (a separate file) built on one or more attributes of a relation that provides a fast lookup path to the data file without requiring an exhaustive scan of every record. A **single-level ordered index** is a sorted file of index entries — each entry being of the form **(search-key value, pointer to record/block)** — where the search keys are kept in sorted order to enable efficient searching via **binary search** or other ordered-file techniques (e.g., primary-block or interpolation search).

The **index file** typically stores pairs `(search-key, pointer)`, where the pointer can reference either a **block (block anchor)** in the data file or an **individual record (record anchor)**. Because the index file is much smaller than the data file, a binary search over the index is exponentially faster than a linear scan of the actual relation.

### 1.2 Intuition: The Library Card-Catalog Analogy

Imagine walking into a large university library containing **10 million** books (your data file). Instead of wandering aisle by aisle looking for every title by "Abraham Silberschatz," you consult the **alphabetical card catalog** at the entrance. Each card has two pieces of information: **(Author/Title keyword → Shelf and Book Number)**. The catalog is sorted alphabetically, so the librarian performs a binary search, jumps straight to the correct drawer, and locates the book in seconds.

The card catalog is your **index file**, the books are your **data records**, the alphabetical order is the **ordered search-key**, and the shelf/book number is the **pointer**. The key point: the catalog is *one level deep* (you never consult another catalog to find the catalog). This is precisely what defines a **single-level index**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024, Module 3)**: The discussion of ordered indexes is split into three classic variants — **Primary**, **Clustering**, and **Secondary** — distinguished by whether the file underlying them is sorted on the search key and whether duplicates are permitted.

### 1.3 The Three Canonical Variants

| Variant | Data File Sorted? | Search-Key Duplicates? | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Index** | Yes, on the primary key | No (key is unique) | Direct lookup on the ordering field |
| **Clustering Index** | Yes, on the (non-key) search field | Yes (one entry per distinct value) | Range queries on non-key sorted fields |
| **Secondary Index** | No (unsorted data file) | Key may be unique or non-unique | Lookup on attributes the file is not sorted on |

### 1.4 Physical Storage Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metrics** (use these in numerical problems):
> - **Block size (B)** = number of bytes the disk can transfer in one I/O.
> - **Blocking factor (bfr)** = ⌊ B / R ⌋ where **R** = fixed record size in bytes.
> - **Number of file blocks (b)** = ⌈ r / bfr ⌋ where **r** = total number of records.
> - **Index entry size (V)** = (key-field size) + (pointer size) — typically **V < R**.
> - **Index blocking factor (bfrᵢ)** = ⌊ B / V ⌋.
> - **Number of index blocks (bᵢ)** = ⌈ rᵢ / bfrᵢ ⌋ where **rᵢ** = number of index entries.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Index (Indexed on the Ordering Field)

A **primary index** is built on the **ordering field** of an ordered data file. Since the ordering field is the primary key, every search-key value is **unique**, and the index contains exactly **one entry per data block** (not per record). For each block, we store:
- the **minimum key value** present in that block (or the maximum of the *previous* block, depending on convention), and
- a **pointer to the block**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why one entry per block and not per record?**
> Because the data file is *already sorted* on the search key, every record in block *i* has a key ≥ the minimum key in block *i* and < the minimum key in block *i + 1*. Hence a single block anchor per block is sufficient to redirect the search to the correct block, where an in-block search (linear or binary over the block's records) finishes the lookup.

#### Search Algorithm
1. Apply **binary search** on the index blocks → find the index entry whose key ≤ search key < next entry's key.
2. Read the corresponding data block.
3. Search the block for the exact record.

#### Number of Block Accesses for Equality Search

$$
\boxed{\,B_{\text{primary-search}} \;=\; \lceil \log_2(b_i) \rceil \;+\; 1\,}
$$

The "+1" accounts for the single data-block I/O after locating the index entry.

#### Update Operations
- **Insertion**: Maintain data-file sorted order → may require block splits; the index is updated only when a new block is created (new minimum key inserted).
- **Deletion**: Reclaim space; if the minimum key of a block is removed, update the index to the new minimum.
- **Cost**: Updates are **expensive** because the data file must remain physically sorted.

### 2.2 Clustering Index

A **clustering index** is built on the **ordering field of an ordered data file** *when the ordering field is NOT a key* (i.e., the field is a non-key attribute on which the file happens to be sorted). Because multiple records can share the same search-key value, the index contains **one entry per distinct search-key value**, pointing to the **first data block** containing that value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinct search-key values** = cardinality of the search field, often denoted **rᵢ** in KTU numericals. A pointer to the *first block* is sufficient because, due to clustering, all records with that value are guaranteed to be in **consecutive blocks**.

#### Number of Index Entries
$$
r_i \;=\; \text{number of distinct values of the ordering non-key field}
$$

#### Number of Index Blocks
$$
b_i \;=\; \lceil r_i \,/\, bfr_i \rceil
$$

#### Block Accesses for Equality Search
$$
B_{\text{clustering-search}} \;=\; \lceil \log_2(b_i) \rceil \;+\; 1
$$

#### Range Search
For a range query `[low, high]`, we locate the block of *low* (one binary search) and then scan sequentially forward through all blocks containing keys in the range — a substantial saving compared with a full file scan.

### 2.3 Secondary Index

A **secondary index** is built on a **non-ordering field** of the data file. The data file may be **unsorted, sorted on a different attribute, or stored as a heap**. Because the data file is NOT sorted on this field, the index must contain **one entry per record** (or, for non-unique keys, one entry per record pointing to each individual record). Each entry stores:
- the **search-key value**, and
- a **pointer to the record** (or a list of pointers / pointer to a block of pointers for duplicates).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall**: Students often confuse "one entry per record" with "one entry per block" in secondary indexes. Always remember: the data file is **NOT** sorted on the search key, so you cannot summarize a whole block with a single key value — duplicates could be scattered across the entire file.

#### Strategies for Duplicate Keys in a Secondary Index
1. **Multiple entries, one per record** → large index, simplest.
2. **One entry per distinct key → pointer to a list of record pointers** (stored in an intermediate *bucket* block) → more compact, but may require extra I/O for long lists.
3. **One entry per distinct key → pointer to *first* record** with that value → compact but requires following links in the data file (a *multi-list* organization).

#### Block Accesses for Equality Search
$$
\boxed{\,B_{\text{secondary-search}} \;=\; \lceil \log_2(b_i) \rceil \;+\; 1\,}
$$
(One I/O for the data block, provided the matched record is a primary-key attribute or a single-record pointer scheme is used.)

#### Why Secondary Indexes Can Be Expensive
- **Update overhead**: Every insertion/deletion in the data file triggers an update in *every* secondary index defined on the relation. With k secondary indexes, an update cost grows by a factor of roughly k.
- **Large size**: One entry per record → index may be nearly as large as the data file itself, but still small enough to keep in memory for medium-sized relations.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — virtually every KTU numerical is solved using it.**

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| B | Block size (bytes) | Given |
| R | Record size (bytes) | Given |
| r | Number of records in data file | Given |
| bfr | Blocking factor of data file | $\lfloor B/R \rfloor$ |
| b | Number of data file blocks | $\lceil r/bfr \rceil$ |
| V | Index entry size (bytes) | (key size) + (pointer size) |
| bfrᵢ | Index blocking factor | $\lfloor B/V \rfloor$ |
| rᵢ | Number of index entries | Primary: $b$ / Clustering: distinct values / Secondary: $r$ |
| bᵢ | Number of index blocks | $\lceil r_i/bfr_i \rceil$ |
| B_search | Equality-search block accesses | $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1$ |
| B_range | Range-search block accesses (clustering) | $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + \lceil (s/bfr) \rceil$ (s = matching records) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol safety note**: The vertical bar `|` is reserved by markdown table syntax. Whenever you write $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in notes, typeset it with `\lvert` / `\rvert` / `\mid`, **not** a raw pipe character.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ordered single-level indexes are the workhorse of every production RDBMS (PostgreSQL B-tree leaf level, MySQL InnoDB clustered index, Oracle BTREE indexes, SQL Server clustered & non-clustered indexes). They enable:
- **OLTP point lookups** (primary index on `customer_id`).
- **OLAP range queries** (clustering index on `order_date`).
- **Ad-hoc analytics** (secondary indexes on `email`, `phone`).

The cost model in KTU problems is *exactly* the model DBAs use to size buffer pools and choose index selectivity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Primary Index Block-Access Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Style)**: A file has $r = 300{,}000$ records, each of size $R = 100$ bytes. Block size $B = 1024$ bytes. The file is ordered on a unique key of size 9 bytes; a block pointer occupies 6 bytes.
> 1. Compute the number of data blocks $b$.
> 2. Compute the number of index entries $r_i$ and the index blocking factor $bfr_i$.
> 3. Compute the number of index blocks $b_i$.
> 4. Compute the number of block accesses for an equality search using the primary index.

**Step 1 — Blocking factor of the data file**

$$
\begin{aligned}
bfr \;=\; \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R} \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor \dfrac{1024}{100} \right\rfloor \;=\; \lfloor 10.24 \rfloor \;=\; 10
\end{aligned}
$$

> One data block holds **10** records.

**Step 2 — Number of data blocks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{r}{bfr} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{300{,}000}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 30{,}000 \rceil \;=\; 30{,}000 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Index entry size**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \;=\; \text{key size} + \text{pointer size} \;=\; 9 + 6 \;=\; 15 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Index blocking factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
bfr_i \;=\; \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{V} \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor \dfrac{1024}{15} \right\rfloor \;=\; \lfloor 68.26 \rfloor \;=\; 68
\end{aligned}
$$

> One index block holds **68** index entries.

**Step 5 — Number of index entries (primary index ⇒ one per data block)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_i \;=\; b \;=\; 30{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Number of index blocks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_i \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{r_i}{bfr_i} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{30{,}000}{68} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 441.17 \rceil \;=\; 442 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Block accesses for an equality search**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{search}} \;=\; \lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1 \;=\; \lceil \log_2 442 \rceil + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_2 442 \;\approx\; 8.79 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \lceil 8.79 \rceil \;=\; 9
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,B_{\text{search}} \;=\; 9 + 1 \;=\; 10 \text{ block accesses}\,}
$$

> **Compare with linear scan**: $30{,}000$ I/Os. The index reduces I/O by a factor of **3,000×**.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Clustering Index with Range Query

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: Same file as above, but now the file is ordered on a **non-key** attribute `Department` having $V_{\text{distinct}} = 50$ distinct values. A range query requests all employees in a particular department, returning $s = 2{,}000$ records on average.

**Step 1 — Index entry size**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \;=\; \text{DeptName size} + \text{pointer size} \;=\; 9 + 6 \;=\; 15 \text{ bytes (unchanged)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of index entries (one per distinct value)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_i \;=\; 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Index blocking factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
bfr_i \;=\; \lfloor 1024 / 15 \rfloor \;=\; 68
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of index blocks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_i \;=\; \lceil 50 / 68 \rceil \;=\; 1 \text{ block}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The *entire* clustering index fits in **one** I/O!

**Step 5 — Number of data blocks holding the department**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{data blocks for one dept} \;=\; \lceil s / bfr \rceil \;=\; \lceil 2{,}000 / 10 \rceil \;=\; 200 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Total block accesses for the range query**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{range}} \;=\; \lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + \lceil s / bfr \rceil \;=\; \lceil \log_2 1 \rceil + 200
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,B_{\text{range}} \;=\; 0 + 200 \;=\; 200 \text{ block accesses}\,}
$$

> **Compare with linear scan**: $30{,}000$ I/Os. The clustering index reduces I/O by a factor of **150×**, AND the data is contiguous, so the disk arm does not seek wildly.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Secondary Index with Unique Key

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: Same file (unsorted on the search field). Build a secondary index on a **unique** attribute `SSN` of size 9 bytes. The record pointer is 7 bytes (logical record number).

**Step 1 — Index entry size**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \;=\; 9 + 7 \;=\; 16 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Index blocking factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
bfr_i \;=\; \lfloor 1024 / 16 \rfloor \;=\; 64
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of index entries (one per record)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_i \;=\; r \;=\; 300{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of index blocks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_i \;=\; \lceil 300{,}000 / 64 \rceil \;=\; \lceil 4{,}687.5 \rceil \;=\; 4{,}688 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Block accesses for an equality search**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{search}} \;=\; \lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1 \;=\; \lceil \log_2 4{,}688 \rceil + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_2 4{,}688 \;\approx\; 12.19 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \lceil 12.19 \rceil \;=\; 13
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,B_{\text{search}} \;=\; 13 + 1 \;=\; 14 \text{ block accesses}\,}
$$

> This is **higher** than the primary-index case (10 I/Os) because the secondary index has many more entries — but it is still vastly better than a $30{,}000$-block scan.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Primary | Clustering | Secondary |
|---|---|---|---|
| Index entries $r_i$ | $b$ (one per block) | Distinct values of field | $r$ (one per record) |
| Data file sorted? | Yes (on key) | Yes (on non-key) | No |
| Duplicates allowed in key? | No | Yes | Yes/No |
| Search I/Os | $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1$ | $\lceil \log_2 b_i \restrceil + 1$ | $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1$ |
| Update cost | High (resort) | High (resort) | High (every index updated) |
| Use case | Range scan / equality on key | Range scan on non-key | Lookups on non-ordering fields |

### 3.5 Update-Operation Cost Derivations

**Primary Index Insertion Cost** (average-case, assuming uniform key distribution):

$$
B_{\text{insert}} \;=\; \lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil \;+\; 1 \;+\; \left( \dfrac{1}{bfr} + 1 \right)
$$
