The two extra terms represent the average block split and the index update. **Secondary index insertion cost** is lower on the data side (no re-sorting) but higher in the index:

$$
B_{\text{insert,sec}} \;=\; \lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil \;+\; 1 \;+\; 1
$$

(Index-block insertion plus one data-block read/write.)

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas appear verbatim in the standard KTU reference text and are highly tested.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture: How an Index Sits Beside the Data File

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Query with search key K] --> B[Index file - sorted on K]
    B --> C{Index entry found via binary search}
    C --> D[Index block pointer]
    D --> E[Data file block anchor]
    E --> F[Target record retrieved]
    F --> G[Return result to query]
```

### 4.2 Primary Index Structure (One Entry per Data Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IDX[Index file - sorted]
        I1["Block 1 anchor: min key = 10"]
        I2["Block 2 anchor: min key = 25"]
        I3["Block 3 anchor: min key = 40"]
        I4["Block 4 anchor: min key = 55"]
    end
    subgraph DAT[Data file - sorted on key]
        D1["Data block 1: 10 12 15 18"]
        D2["Data block 2: 25 27 30 33"]
        D3["Data block 3: 40 42 48 52"]
        D4["Data block 4: 55 60 65 70"]
    end
    I1 --> D1
    I2 --> D2
    I3 --> D3
    I4 --> D4
```

### 4.3 Clustering Index Structure (One Entry per Distinct Value)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CIX[Clustering index - sorted]
        C1["Anchor: CSE -> block 1"]
        C2["Anchor: ECE -> block 3"]
        C3["Anchor: MECH -> block 5"]
    end
    subgraph CDAT[Data file - sorted on Department]
        CD1["Block 1: CSE  CSE  CSE  CSE"]
        CD2["Block 2: CSE  CSE  CSE  CSE"]
        CD3["Block 3: ECE  ECE  ECE  ECE"]
        CD4["Block 4: ECE  ECE  ECE  ECE"]
        CD5["Block 5: MECH  MECH  MECH  MECH"]
    end
    C1 --> CD1
    C2 --> CD3
    C3 --> CD5
```

### 4.4 Secondary Index Structure (One Entry per Record, Unique Key)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SIX[Secondary index - sorted on SSN]
        S1["123-45-6789 -> rec ptr 1"]
        S2["234-56-7890 -> rec ptr 2"]
        S3["345-67-8901 -> rec ptr 3"]
        S4["456-78-9012 -> rec ptr 4"]
    end
    subgraph SDAT[Data file - UNSORTED on SSN]
        SD1["Block 1: name Ada, dept CSE, SSN 345-67-8901"]
        SD2["Block 2: name Bob, dept ECE, SSN 123-45-6789"]
        SD3["Block 3: name Cy, dept MECH, SSN 234-56-7890"]
        SD4["Block 4: name Dee, dept CSE, SSN 456-78-9012"]
    end
    S1 --> SD2
    S2 --> SD3
    S3 --> SD1
    S4 --> SD4
```

> [!NOTE]
> Observe that in the secondary index the data file blocks are reached in *random* order — this is the fundamental reason secondary indexes are slower per-I/O than clustering indexes for range queries, even though both yield the same number of block accesses for a single equality lookup.

### 4.5 Search Operation Block-Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[Query arrives with key K] --> BS[Step 1: Binary search index file]
    BS --> BIO1[Step 2: Read index block - log2 bi I/Os]
    BIO1 --> LOC[Step 3: Identify target data block pointer]
    LOC --> BIO2[Step 4: Read one data block]
    BIO2 --> REC[Step 5: Scan block in memory for record]
    REC --> RES[Return record to user]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between a **primary index** and a **secondary index**. State one scenario where a secondary index is preferred over a primary index.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Primary index** is built on the **ordering field** of an ordered data file; it has **one entry per data block** (pointing to the block containing the minimum key of that range). **(1 mark)**
> - **Secondary index** is built on a **non-ordering field**; the data file need not be sorted on that field, and the index has **one entry per record**. **(1 mark)**
> - A secondary index is preferred when the relation is queried frequently on a **non-ordering attribute** (e.g., searching `EMPLOYEE` by `email` or by `phone`), since the data file cannot be sorted on every such attribute at once. **(1 mark)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why does a **clustering index** have fewer entries than a **secondary index** built on the same field?*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - In a clustering index, the data file is **sorted on the search field**, so all records with the same search-key value are stored in **consecutive blocks**; one index entry per **distinct value** is therefore sufficient. **(1 mark)**
> - In a secondary index, the data file is **not sorted** on the search field; records with the same key value may be scattered across the file, so the index must contain **one entry per record** (or a list of pointers) to reach each matching record. **(1 mark)**
> - Consequently, the number of clustering index entries equals the **cardinality** of the search field, while the number of secondary index entries equals the **number of records** in the file. **(1 mark)**

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

#### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

> **(a)** Consider an `EMPLOYEE` file with **30,000 EMPLOYEEs**. Each record is **100 bytes**, the disk block size is **1,024 bytes**, and the file is sorted on the **SSN** field, which is a **9-byte unique key**. A block pointer is **6 bytes**.
> 1. Compute the number of data file blocks `b`. **(2 marks)**
> 2. Compute the index blocking factor `bfr_i` and the number of index blocks `b_i`. **(3 marks)**
> 3. Compute the number of block accesses for an **equality search** using the primary index, and compare it with a linear scan. **(2 marks)**

> **(b)** Suppose the same `EMPLOYEE` file is now **NOT sorted on SSN**, and you build a **secondary index** on `SSN` using a **7-byte logical record pointer** per entry. The file is also sorted on `Dept`, a **non-key** attribute with **6 distinct department values**, on which a **clustering index** has been built (with the same 6-byte block pointer).
> 1. Compute the number of index blocks for the **secondary** index and the number of block accesses for an equality search by `SSN`. **(3 marks)**
> 2. Compute the number of index blocks for the **clustering** index. **(1 mark)**
> 3. State which index supports a **range query on Dept** efficiently and justify your answer in one sentence. **(1 mark)**
> 4. Explain in 2-3 sentences what happens to the secondary index when a new `EMPLOYEE` record is inserted. **(2 marks)**

##### Model Solution

> **Part (a)**

*Step 1 — Blocking factor of the data file*
$$
\begin{aligned}
bfr \;=\; \lfloor B/R \rfloor \;=\; \lfloor 1024/100 \rfloor \;=\; 10
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Number of data file blocks*
$$
\begin{aligned}
b \;=\; \lceil r/bfr \rceil \;=\; \lceil 30{,}000/10 \rceil \;=\; 3{,}000 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Index entry size* (key 9 + block pointer 6)
$$
\begin{aligned}
V \;=\; 9 + 6 \;=\; 15 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Index blocking factor*
$$
\begin{aligned}
bfr_i \;=\; \lfloor 1024/15 \rfloor \;=\; 68
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5 — Number of index entries (one per data block)*
$$
\begin{aligned}
r_i \;=\; b \;=\; 3{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 6 — Number of index blocks*
$$
\begin{aligned}
b_i \;=\; \lceil 3{,}000/68 \rceil \;=\; \lceil 44.12 \rceil \;=\; 45 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 7 — Block accesses for an equality search*
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{search}} \;=\; \lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1 \;=\; \lceil \log_2 45 \rceil + 1
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\log_2 45 \approx 5.49 \Rightarrow \lceil 5.49 \rceil = 6 \Rightarrow B_{\text{search}} = 6 + 1 = \mathbf{7 \text{ block accesses}}
$$

*Comparison with linear scan*
- Linear scan needs up to **3,000** block I/Os (average 1,500).
- Indexed search needs **7** I/Os — a speed-up factor of **~214×** on average and **~428×** worst case. **(2 marks)**

> **Part (b)**

*Step 1 — Secondary index entry size* (key 9 + record pointer 7)
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{sec}} \;=\; 9 + 7 \;=\; 16 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Secondary index blocking factor*
$$
\begin{aligned}
bfr_{i,\text{sec}} \;=\; \lfloor 1024/16 \rfloor \;=\; 64
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Number of secondary index entries (one per record)*
$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{i,\text{sec}} \;=\; r \;=\; 30{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Number of secondary index blocks*
$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{i,\text{sec}} \;=\; \lceil 30{,}000/64 \rceil \;=\; \lceil 468.75 \rceil \;=\; 469 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5 — Block accesses for equality search by SSN*
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{sec,search}} \;=\; \lceil \log_2 469 \rceil + 1
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\log_2 469 \approx 8.87 \Rightarrow \lceil 8.87 \rceil = 9 \Rightarrow B_{\text{sec,search}} = 9 + 1 = \mathbf{10 \text{ block accesses}}
$$

*Step 6 — Clustering index entry size* (Dept name 9 + block pointer 6 — assuming the dept field is also 9 bytes for symmetry; if the question gives 6 bytes, the calculation is identical in form)
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{clust}} \;=\; 9 + 6 \;=\; 15 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
bfr_{i,\text{clust}} \;=\; 68
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 7 — Number of clustering index entries* (one per distinct department)
$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{i,\text{clust}} \;=\; 6
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 8 — Number of clustering index blocks*
$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{i,\text{clust}} \;=\; \lceil 6/68 \rceil \;=\; \mathbf{1 \text{ block}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 9 — Which index supports a range query on Dept?*
- The **clustering index** supports it efficiently because the data file is physically sorted on `Dept`, so all employees in a given department are stored in **consecutive data blocks**; a range query reduces to one indexed lookup followed by a sequential block scan, with minimal disk-arm movement. **(1 mark)**

*Step 10 — Effect of insertion on the secondary index*
- A new record causes an **insertion into the data file** (e.g., by appending to a heap or inserting in sorted order on the primary key), and the secondary index must be updated to include a **new index entry** for the new `SSN` value pointing to the new record.
- If the index block is full, it **splits** in a manner similar to a B-tree leaf split, propagating updates upward in a multi-level setting. **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question A)**:
> - **Lost mark 1**: Students often write $r_i = r$ for a *primary* index. The correct value is $r_i = b$ (one per block). A primary index has a 5–10× smaller $b_i$ than a secondary index.
> - **Lost mark 2**: Forgetting the trailing **+1** in the search cost formula.
> - **Lost mark 3**: Using record pointers (7 bytes) instead of block pointers (6 bytes) when computing the primary index entry size — read the question carefully!

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Define **single-level ordered indexes**. With the help of a clear block diagram, describe the three types of single-level ordered indexes used in databases. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A `STUDENT` relation has the following parameters: total records $r = 50{,}000$, fixed record size $R = 80$ bytes, block size $B = 512$ bytes, primary key `RollNo` of size 4 bytes, block-pointer size = 5 bytes. The file is **unsorted** (heap) and a **secondary index** is built on `RollNo`. A different attribute `CGPA` (size 4 bytes, floating point) is the ordering field, and a **clustering index** is built on it. Assume `CGPA` has only **10 distinct values** in the relation.
> 1. Compute $bfr$, $b$, $bfr_i$, and $b_i$ for the **secondary** index. **(3 marks)**
> 2. Compute $b_i$ for the **clustering** index. **(2 marks)**
> 3. How many block accesses are needed to retrieve all students with `CGPA >= 9.0` if 8,000 records satisfy the condition? **(2 marks)**

##### Model Solution

> **Part (a) — Definition + Diagram (7 marks)**

A **single-level ordered index** is an auxiliary, ordered access structure on a relation that speeds up retrieval based on a chosen search key. It is called *single-level* because the index file itself is the only lookup structure consulted — there is no second level of index pointing into the first.

The three classical types are:

1. **Primary Index** — Built on the file's ordering field when that field is a **key** (unique). Index has **one entry per data block** (anchor points to the block containing the smallest key ≥ anchor). **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Clustering Index** — Built on the file's ordering field when that field is a **non-key**. Index has **one entry per distinct value** of the field, pointing to the first block of records having that value. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Secondary Index** — Built on a **non-ordering** field. Index has **one entry per record** (or pointer list per distinct value), since the file is not sorted on the search key. **(1.5 marks)**

*Block diagram* (provide an ASCII / Mermaid sketch similar to Sections 4.2–4.4 above, showing the index entries pointing to data file blocks). **(2.5 marks: 1.5 for diagram + 1 for explanatory caption)**

> **Part (b) — Numerical**

*Step 1 — Data file blocking factor and block count*
$$
\begin{aligned}
bfr \;=\; \lfloor B/R \rfloor \;=\; \lfloor 512/80 \rfloor \;=\; 6
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
b \;=\; \lceil 50{,}000/6 \rceil \;=\; \lceil 8{,}333.33 \rceil \;=\; 8{,}334 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Secondary index entry size* (key 4 + pointer 5)
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{sec}} \;=\; 4 + 5 \;=\; 9 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Secondary index blocking factor*
$$
\begin{aligned}
bfr_{i,\text{sec}} \;=\; \lfloor 512/9 \rfloor \;=\; 56
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Number of secondary index entries* (one per record, since `RollNo` is a key)
$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{i,\text{sec}} \;=\; 50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5 — Number of secondary index blocks*
$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{i,\text{sec}} \;=\; \lceil 50{,}000/56 \rceil \;=\; \lceil 892.86 \rceil \;=\; 893 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks for sub-part 1)**

*Step 6 — Clustering index entry size* (CGPA size 4 + block pointer 5)
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{clust}} \;=\; 4 + 5 \;=\; 9 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
bfr_{i,\text{clust}} \;=\; \lfloor 512/9 \rfloor \;=\; 56
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 7 — Number of clustering index entries* (one per distinct CGPA)
$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{i,\text{clust}} \;=\; 10
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 8 — Number of clustering index blocks*
$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{i,\text{clust}} \;=\; \lceil 10/56 \rceil \;=\; \mathbf{1 \text{ block}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks for sub-part 2)**

*Step 9 — Block accesses for range query on CGPA*

Locate the smallest qualifying CGPA in the clustering index (one block binary search → negligible since $b_i = 1$): **1** index I/O. Then sequentially read blocks containing the 8,000 matching records:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{data blocks scanned} \;=\; \lceil 8{,}000/6 \rceil \;=\; 1{,}334 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{range,total}} \;=\; 1 + 1{,}334 \;=\; \mathbf{1{,}335 \text{ block accesses}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks for sub-part 3)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B)**:
> - **Lost mark 1**: For a **clustering index** built on a **non-key** field, the number of index entries is the **number of distinct values**, NOT the number of records. Conflating these two is the single most common error on this question type.
> - **Lost mark 2**: For the range query, students frequently write `b_i` as the cost of the *indexed* part and forget to add the *data-block* scan cost. The full formula is `⌈log₂ b_i⌉ + ⌈s / bfr⌉`.
> - **Lost mark 3**: Dividing by the wrong blocking factor — use `bfr` (data file blocking factor) for data-block scans, and `bfr_i` (index blocking factor) when computing `b_i`.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Single-Level Ordered Indexes**

- **Definition**: A single-level ordered index is a *sorted* file of `(search-key, pointer)` pairs that accelerates lookups on one specific attribute of a relation; "single-level" means the index file itself has no further index above it. **(1 line)**

- **Three canonical types**:
  1. **Primary index** — built on the **ordering key** of a sorted file; one entry per data block; supports both equality and range queries.
  2. **Clustering index** — built on the **ordering non-key** of a sorted file; one entry per *distinct* value; supports fast range queries and equality lookups.
  3. **Secondary index** — built on a **non-ordering** field of an unsorted file; one entry per record (or pointer list); supports fast equality but slow range queries.

- **Master formulas** (use them in every numerical):
  - $bfr = \lfloor B/R \rfloor$, $b = \lceil r/bfr \rceil$
  - $bfr_i = \lfloor B/V \rfloor$, $b_i = \lceil r_i/bfr_i \rceil$
  - $B_{\text{search}} = \lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1$
  - $B_{\text{range (clustering)}} = \lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + \lceil s/bfr \rceil$

- **Number of index entries** (`r_i`):
  - Primary → $b$ (one per block)
  - Clustering → distinct values of the field
  - Secondary → $r$ (one per record)

- **Update cost** is the Achilles' heel: primary/clustering require data file **re-sorting**; secondary requires updating *every* index for every record change. This motivates the move to **multi-level indexes** and **B⁺-trees** (covered in Module 3's next sub-topic).

- **Pitfalls to avoid**:
  - Mixing up **block pointers** vs **record pointers** in $V$.
  - Forgetting the `+1` in the search formula.
  - Using $\lvert x \rvert$ notation with raw `|` pipes inside markdown tables (use `\lvert ... \rvert`).
  - Confusing the data-file blocking factor $bfr$ with the index blocking factor $bfr_i$.

- **Real-world mapping**: Primary index ≈ InnoDB clustered index (sorted on PK); Clustering index ≈ a sorted column-organized table on a non-key field; Secondary index ≈ every non-clustered B-tree index in MySQL/PostgreSQL/Oracle.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Single-Level Ordered Indexes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multilevel Indexes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multilevel Indexes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Multilevel Index Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> A **Multilevel Index** is a hierarchical indexing structure built by recursively treating the *index file itself* as a *data file*, allowing a higher-level (outer) index to be constructed over the lower-level (inner) index. This transformation continues until the outermost index fits entirely within a single disk block (or main memory buffer), thereby reducing search time from a linear $O(N)$ scan to a logarithmic $O(\log_{b_r} N)$ access pattern, where $b_r$ is the index blocking factor and $N$ is the number of search-key entries.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 3 — *Indexing and Hashing*), multilevel indexing is positioned as the natural **scalability extension** of single-level ordered (primary) indexes. When the index file grows so large that it can no longer be efficiently binary-searched in main memory, we recursively index the index.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Card Catalog Inside a Card Catalog"

Imagine the **back-of-book index** in a 1,000-page encyclopedia. It has 5,000 entries arranged alphabetically. A single alphabetical scan would be slow. So the publisher produces a **second-level index** (a "Master Index") sitting at the front, with only 26 entries — one for each letter (A, B, C, …, Z) — pointing to the range of pages where the detailed sub-indexes for that letter can be found. If even the *Master Index* becomes too long, a **third level** is added (e.g., volume-level pointers).

- **Level 0 (innermost)** → Data file (the actual encyclopedia pages)
- **Level 1** → Detailed alphabetical index
- **Level 2** → Master letter index (A–Z)
- **Level 3** → Volume index (Vol. 1–10)

You open the **Volume Index** → narrow down to the **Master Letter Index** → narrow down to the **Detailed Page Index** → finally reach the **page**. Each jump prunes the search space dramatically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept**
> A multilevel index is conceptually **not** a *new kind* of index. It is a *recursive application* of the *same* primary (or clustering) index structure on top of itself. The KTU examiner will frequently test whether you understand this recursive definition versus treating it as a separate data structure.

### 1.3 Why Single-Level Indexing Fails — Motivation

A single-level ordered index on a relation of $r$ records (with $r_i$ entries per index block, blocking factor $b_r = r_i$) requires:

$$
\text{Index blocks required} = \left\lceil \frac{r}{r_i} \right\rceil
$$

Even with **binary search**, the cost is $\lceil \log_2 \left\lceil \frac{r}{r_i} \right\rceil \rceil$ block accesses. As $r \to 10^7$ or higher, this becomes prohibitively expensive because **the index itself no longer fits in main memory**, forcing several disk I/Os to *just locate* the pointer before the actual data can be retrieved.

The remedy: **make the index smaller by indexing the index itself.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Multilevel Index

A $k$-level multilevel index consists of the following layered components:

- **Level 0 (Base data file):** Stores the actual ordered data records of the relation. The search key is the *primary key* (or ordering attribute for a clustering index).
- **Level 1 (Inner/First-level index):** An ordered index on the data file, with one entry per data block of the base file.
- **Level 2 (Second-level outer index):** An ordered index on Level 1, with one entry per block of Level 1.
- **Level $k$ (Outermost/Top-level index):** The smallest index. It must fit in **one disk block** (or a fixed small number of blocks) so it can be permanently buffered in main memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constraint (KTU syllabus mandate)**
> Construction of a *new* higher level *stops* as soon as the topmost index is small enough to reside in a single disk block. The number of levels $k$ is therefore **data-driven**, not arbitrary.

### 2.2 Search Procedure — Step-by-Step Logic

1. Load the **top-level (Level $k$)** index block into main memory (assumed resident).
2. Perform **binary search** on this in-memory block to find the largest key $\leq$ target search key.
3. Follow the **pointer** to the corresponding block in Level $(k-1)$.
4. Bring that Level $(k-1)$ block into memory and **binary search** within it.
5. Repeat steps 3–4 down to **Level 1**.
6. At **Level 1**, the pointer leads to the actual data block in the base file.
7. **Search the data block** (often by linear scan or binary search) to retrieve the matching record.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Blocking factor of index $b_{ri}$ | $b_{ri} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{\vert \text{index entry} \vert} \right\rfloor$ | entries/block | $B$ = block size in bytes |
| 2 | Number of Level-1 index blocks $r_1$ | $r_1 = \left\lceil \dfrac{r}{b_{ri}} \right\rceil$ | blocks | $r$ = number of records |
| 3 | Number of Level-$i$ index blocks $r_i$ | $r_i = \left\lceil \dfrac{r_{i-1}}{b_{ri}} \right\rceil$ | blocks | Recursive |
| 4 | Number of levels $k$ | $k = \min \left\{ i : r_i = 1 \right\}$ | levels | Stop when $\leq 1$ block |
| 5 | Search cost (binary search) | $\lceil \log_2 b_{ri} \rceil \cdot k + 1$ | block accesses | Last "+1" is for data block |
| 6 | Search cost (linear per level) | $k + 1$ | block accesses | Linear scan within each level |
| 7 | Total entries in Level $i$ | $r_i \cdot b_{ri}$ | entries | Decreases geometrically |
| 8 | Disk-resident assumption | Top level assumed **buffered** | — | Validated in KTU problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU board problems, the phrase *"index resides in memory"* is shorthand for *"the topmost level fits in one block and is treated as a resident in-memory structure, contributing 0 I/Os."* The "1 block access" at the end of every search refers to the **base data block**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multilevel indexing is the **conceptual foundation** of real-world database systems:

- **IBM DB2, Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB** all implement multilevel indexing via **B-Trees** and **B+ Trees** (covered later in Module 3). A B+ Tree of height $h$ is mathematically a multilevel index with $h$ levels, where branching factor auto-adapts to disk-block size.
- **File systems (NTFS, ext4):** Use multilevel indexes (inodes + double/triple indirect blocks) to map logical file offsets to disk blocks — the same recursive structure.
- **Search engines (Elasticsearch, Lucene):** Use **skip lists** and **segment trees** that are memory-resident multilevel indexes.
- **Embedded/IoT firmware** (RTOS environments): Multilevel indexes minimize power-hungry flash I/O in devices with strict energy budgets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Building the Multilevel Index (Full Construction)

**Given (a standard KTU numerical):**
- Relation $R$ has $r = 30{,}000$ records.
- Each record is **100 bytes**.
- Disk block size $B = 4{,}096$ bytes.
- Each index entry (key + pointer) is **15 bytes** (4-byte int key + 11-byte block pointer).
- Records are stored in **sorted order** on the search key.
- Construct the multilevel index and determine the number of levels.

**Step 1 — Blocking factor of the data file (block pointer size context):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Record blocking factor } b_{rr} &= \left\lfloor \frac{B}{\vert \text{record} \vert} \right\rfloor \\[4pt]
&= \left\lfloor \frac{4{,}096}{100} \right\rfloor \\[4pt]
&= \left\lfloor 40.96 \right\rfloor = 40 \text{ records/block}
\end{aligned}
$$

This step is shown for context (it determines that 30,000 records need 750 data blocks). However, for a **sparse primary index**, we need only *one index entry per data block* (not per record).

**Step 2 — Blocking factor of the index file:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{ri} &= \left\lfloor \frac{B}{\vert \text{index entry} \vert} \right\rfloor \\[4pt]
&= \left\lfloor \frac{4{,}096}{15} \right\rfloor \\[4pt]
&= \left\lfloor 273.07 \right\rfloor = 273 \text{ index entries/block}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of data blocks (Level 0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= \left\lceil \frac{r}{b_{rr}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{30{,}000}{40} \right\rceil = 750 \text{ data blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of Level-1 index blocks (since this is a sparse primary index with one entry per data block):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \left\lceil \frac{b_0}{b_{ri}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{750}{273} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.747 \rceil = 3 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of Level-2 index blocks (index the Level-1 index):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_2 &= \left\lceil \frac{r_1}{b_{ri}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{3}{273} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ block}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $r_2 = 1$, the top level fits in a single block. **Stop. We have $k = 2$ levels of index (plus the data file = 3 tiers total).**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention Distinction**
> Some textbooks count "$k$ levels" *including* the base file; others count only the index tiers. KTU's Navathe-based syllabus counts only the **index tiers** unless otherwise stated. Always state your assumption in the answer.

**Step 6 — Search cost (binary search variant):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= \left\lceil \log_2 b_{ri} \right\rceil \cdot k \;+\; 1 \\[4pt]
&= \left\lceil \log_2 273 \right\rceil \cdot 2 \;+\; 1 \\[4pt]
&= \lceil 8.09 \rceil \cdot 2 + 1 \\[4pt]
&= 9 \cdot 2 + 1 = 19 \text{ block accesses per search}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Search cost (linear scan within level — alternative KTU variant):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= k + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ block accesses}
\end{aligned}
$$

The linear-scan variant is what gives multilevel indexing its iconic logarithmic advantage; the binary-search variant is for very large blocking factors.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Comparative Cost Analysis

Compare the cost of searching the same file using:

**(a)** Linear scan (no index):

$$
\text{Cost} = b_0 = 750 \text{ block accesses}
$$

**(b)** Single-level primary index with binary search:

$$
\text{Cost} = \left\lceil \log_2 r_1 \right\rceil + 1 = \lceil \log_2 3 \rceil + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ block accesses}
$$

**(c)** Multilevel index (from Example 1):

$$
\text{Cost} = k + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ block accesses}
$$

Wait — the costs appear equal! This is because $r_1 = 3$ is small enough that a single level is already efficient. The **power of multilevel indexing** shows up when $r_1$ is much larger.

**Counter-example:** Suppose $r = 3{,}000{,}000$ records, $B = 4{,}096$, record = 100 B, index entry = 15 B. Then $b_0 = 75{,}000$ data blocks, $r_1 = 275$ Level-1 blocks, $r_2 = 2$ Level-2 blocks. So $k = 2$ levels. Now:

- Single-level cost: $\lceil \log_2 275 \rceil + 1 = 9 + 1 = 10$ block accesses.
- Multilevel cost: $2 + 1 = 3$ block accesses (if linear within level) or $2 \cdot 9 + 1 = 19$ (if binary per level).

For files in the **millions of records**, $k$ grows to 3 or 4, and the multilevel approach saves orders of magnitude in I/O.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cost vs. Data Growth

| Records $r$ | Data Blocks $b_0$ | $r_1$ (L1 blocks) | $r_2$ (L2 blocks) | $r_3$ (L3 blocks) | $k$ | Search Cost (linear) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 30,000 | 750 | 3 | 1 | — | 2 | 3 |
| 300,000 | 7,500 | 28 | 1 | — | 2 | 3 |
| 3,000,000 | 75,000 | 275 | 2 | — | 2 | 3 |
| 30,000,000 | 750,000 | 2,747 | 11 | 1 | 3 | 4 |
| 300,000,000 | 7,500,000 | 27,473 | 101 | 1 | 3 | 4 |

This table makes the **geometric collapse** of the search space immediately visible — the dominant cost remains $O(\log_{b_{ri}} r)$, a hallmark of logarithmic search structures.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Two-Level Multilevel Index Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Top Level Index Level 2 - In Memory Single Block] --> B1[Level 1 Index Block 1]
    A --> B2[Level 1 Index Block 2]
    A --> B3[Level 1 Index Block 3]
    B1 --> C1[Data Block Range 1 to 250]
    B1 --> C2[Data Block Range 251 to 500]
    B2 --> C3[Data Block Range 501 to 600]
    B3 --> C4[Data Block Range 601 to 700]
    B3 --> C5[Data Block Range 701 to 750]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style B1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style B2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style B3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style C1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style C2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style C3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style C4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style C5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- The **gold block (A)** is the *top-level index* — resident in main memory, containing 3 entries pointing to Level-1 blocks.
- The **blue blocks (B1, B2, B3)** are *Level-1 index blocks*, each containing up to 273 entries pointing to data blocks.
- The **green blocks (C1 through C5)** are *data blocks* of the base relation.
- The arrows are **pointers**; following them from the top to the bottom yields the data record.

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Search Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Search Key K Received]) --> Q1{Is Top Level Index in Memory}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2[Perform Binary Search on Top Level]
    Q1 -->|No| L1[Load Top Level from Disk]
    L1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> S1[Identify Pointer to Level k-1 Block]
    S1 --> L2[Load Level k-1 Block from Disk]
    L2 --> Q3{More Index Levels Below}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q2
    Q3 -->|No| L3[Reach Level 1 Index Block]
    L3 --> S2[Identify Pointer to Data Block]
    S2 --> L4[Load Data Block from Disk]
    L4 --> R1[Search Data Block for Record]
    R1 --> Out([Return Matching Record])
    style Start fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style Out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000
    style Q1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style Q3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MemoryTier[Main Memory Layer]
        M1[Top Level Index Block Cached]
        M2[Buffer Pool for Active Index Blocks]
    end
    subgraph DiskTier1[Disk Layer - Level 1 Index]
        D1A[Index Block Group A]
        D1B[Index Block Group B]
        D1C[Index Block Group C]
    end
    subgraph DiskTier2[Disk Layer - Base Data File]
        D2A[Data Block Group 1]
        D2B[Data Block Group 2]
        D2C[Data Block Group 3]
        D2D[Data Block Group 4]
    end
    M1 -->|Pointer Fetch| D1A
    M1 -->|Pointer Fetch| D1B
    M1 -->|Pointer Fetch| D1C
    M2 -.->|On-Demand Read| D1A
    M2 -.->|On-Demand Read| D1B
    D1A -->|Data Pointer| D2A
    D1A -->|Data Pointer| D2B
    D1B -->|Data Pointer| D2C
    D1C -->|Data Pointer| D2D
    style MemoryTier fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
    style DiskTier1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px
    style DiskTier2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** The memory tier always holds the *root* (top level). The disk tiers hold progressively larger index structures. The dotted lines represent **on-demand** buffer-pool fetches; solid lines are **mandatory** traversal pointers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Pitfall**
> When drawing a multilevel index in your answer script, **always label the levels explicitly (L1, L2, L3 …)** and clearly distinguish between index blocks and data blocks. Vague sketches that mix them together typically lose 1–2 marks in the board valuation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Short-Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3] — 3 Marks**
*Define a multilevel index. Under what condition is the construction of a new outer level terminated?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **multilevel index** is an ordered index built on top of another ordered index, treating the inner index as if it were a data file. This recursive construction is repeated until the outermost index is small enough to fit in **a single disk block** (or a small fixed number of blocks) that can be kept resident in main memory.

Termination condition: Construction stops when the topmost index level $r_i$ satisfies $r_i = 1$ (i.e., fits in one block). **(1 mark definition + 1 mark recursion logic + 1 mark termination condition)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3] — 3 Marks**
*State any two advantages of a multilevel index over a single-level index.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Reduced search cost:** A multilevel index reduces search complexity from $O(\log_2 b_0)$ (single-level binary search over all data blocks) to $O(k)$ where $k$ is the number of index levels, since each level can be linearly scanned and $k \ll \log_2 b_0$ for large files. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Main-memory feasibility:** The topmost level is small enough to be permanently buffered in RAM, so the I/O cost is dominated by data block access only, not index traversal. **(1.5 marks)**

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark Descriptive with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Internal-Choice Pattern**
> In the KTU End-Semester Examination, Module-3 carries ~14–21 marks. Part B questions often feature an *"either/or"* choice: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (both 14 marks). Both options are provided below.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Module 3]**

**(a) Construct a two-level index on a sorted file with the following specifications: (7 marks)**
- Records: $r = 100{,}000$
- Record size: 120 bytes
- Block size: $B = 2{,}048$ bytes
- Index entry size (key + pointer): 20 bytes
- File is ordered on the primary key.

**Show the multilevel index structure and compute the number of block accesses to search a record using the index.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Record blocking factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{rr} &= \left\lfloor \frac{B}{\vert \text{record} \vert} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2048}{120} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 17.07 \right\rfloor = 17 \text{ records/block}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of data blocks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= \left\lceil \frac{r}{b_{rr}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{100{,}000}{17} \right\rceil = \lceil 5882.35 \rceil = 5883 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Index blocking factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{ri} &= \left\lfloor \frac{B}{\vert \text{index entry} \vert} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2048}{20} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 102.4 \right\rfloor = 102 \text{ entries/block}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Level-1 index blocks (sparse index, one entry per data block):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \left\lceil \frac{b_0}{b_{ri}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{5883}{102} \right\rceil = \lceil 57.68 \rceil = 58 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Level-2 index blocks (index the Level-1 index):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_2 &= \left\lceil \frac{r_1}{b_{ri}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{58}{102} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ block}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $r_2 = 1$, we have $k = 2$ index levels. Stop.

**Step 6 — Block access cost for search (using linear scan within each level):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= k + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ block accesses}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Diagram of the constructed structure:**

```
        Level 2:  [Single Block - 58 pointers]  ← cached in memory
                      ↓        ↓
        Level 1:  [58 Blocks, ~102 entries each]
                      ↓
        Level 0:  [5883 Data Blocks, 17 records each]
```

**Marks distribution:** 2 marks each for Steps 1–4, 1 mark for Step 5, 1 mark for Step 6, 1 mark for the diagram. **(7/7)**

---

**(b) Compare the search cost in part (a) with: (7 marks)**
- (i) Linear scan of the data file (no index)
- (ii) Single-level sparse primary index with binary search
- (iii) A hypothetical 3-level index built by halving $B$ to 1024 bytes

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Linear scan cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= b_0 = 5883 \text{ block accesses}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Single-level sparse primary index with binary search:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= \lceil \log_2 r_1 \rceil + 1 = \lceil \log_2 58 \rceil + 1 = \lceil 5.86 \rceil + 1 = 6 + 1 = 7 \text{ block accesses}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Hypothetical 3-level index with $B = 1024$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{rr} &= \left\lfloor \frac{1024}{120} \right\rfloor = 8 \text{ records/block} \\[4pt]
b_0 &= \left\lceil \frac{100{,}000}{8} \right\rceil = 12{,}500 \text{ blocks} \\[4pt]
b_{ri} &= \left\lfloor \frac{1024}{20} \right\rfloor = 51 \text{ entries/block} \\[4pt]
r_1 &= \left\lceil \frac{12{,}500}{51} \right\rceil = 246 \text{ blocks} \\[4pt]
r_2 &= \left\lceil \frac{246}{51} \right\rceil = 5 \text{ blocks} \\[4pt]
r_3 &= \left\lceil \frac{5}{51} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ block} \\[4pt]
k &= 3 \text{ levels} \\[4pt]
\text{Search Cost} &= k + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ block accesses}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Method | Block Accesses |
|---|---|
| Linear scan | 5883 |
| Single-level (binary) | 7 |
| **Multilevel 2-level (part a)** | **3** |
| Multilevel 3-level (smaller B) | 4 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> **Common mistake 1:** Treating $b_0$ (data blocks) and $r_1$ (index blocks) as the same quantity — losing 1 mark. **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting the "+1" final data block access. **Common mistake 3:** Using the wrong ceiling/floor in the blocking factor. **Always double-check** whether the index is *sparse* (one entry per block) or *dense* (one entry per record) — this changes $b_0$ vs. $r$ in the formula.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Module 3]**

**(a) Explain the structure of a multilevel index with a neat diagram. How does it differ from a single-level primary index? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **multilevel index** consists of multiple hierarchical levels of ordered indexes, with the **outermost level small enough to fit in a single disk block** kept resident in main memory. Each level $i$ indexes the level $(i-1)$ below it, in a recursive, top-down fashion.

**Structure (textbook-standard diagram):**

```
          ┌────────────────────────────────┐
          │ Top-Level Index  (1 block)     │  ← Cached in RAM
          │  Pointer P1 | Key K1 | … | Kn  │
          └────┬──────────┬──────────┬─────┘
               ↓          ↓          ↓
          ┌────┴────┐ ┌───┴────┐ ┌───┴────┐
          │ L1 Idx  │ │ L1 Idx │ │ L1 Idx │   ← Level 1 index
          │ Block 1 │ │ Block 2│ │ Block N│      (b_ri entries each)
          └────┬────┘ └───┬────┘ └───┬────┘
               ↓          ↓          ↓
          [Data Blocks 1 … b_0]               ← Base data file
```

**Differences from single-level primary index:**

| Aspect | Single-Level | Multilevel |
|---|---|---|
| Number of index tiers | 1 | 2 or more |
| In-memory residency | Index may not fit in RAM | Top level always fits in RAM |
| Search strategy | Binary search across entire index | Binary/linear search level-by-level |
| Scalability | Poor for very large relations | Scales logarithmically |
| I/O cost | $\lceil \log_2 r_1 \rceil + 1$ | $k + 1$ (with linear within level) |

**(2 marks structure + 2 marks diagram + 3 marks comparison table)**

---

**(b) A relation has 200,000 records stored in sorted order. Each record is 250 bytes. The block size is 2 KB. Each index entry is 30 bytes. Construct a multilevel index and find the number of block accesses to search a record. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Blocking factors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{rr} &= \left\lfloor \frac{2048}{250} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 8.192 \right\rfloor = 8 \text{ records/block} \\[4pt]
b_{ri} &= \left\lfloor \frac{2048}{30} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 68.27 \right\rfloor = 68 \text{ entries/block}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of data blocks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= \left\lceil \frac{200{,}000}{8} \right\rceil = 25{,}000 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Level-1 index blocks (sparse index):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \left\lceil \frac{25{,}000}{68} \right\rceil = \lceil 367.65 \rceil = 368 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Level-2 index blocks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_2 &= \left\lceil \frac{368}{68} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.41 \rceil = 6 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Level-3 index blocks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_3 &= \left\lceil \frac{6}{68} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ block}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $r_3 = 1$, stop. We have $k = 3$ index levels.

**Step 6 — Search cost (linear scan within each level):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= k + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ block accesses}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Search cost (binary search within each level):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= k \cdot \lceil \log_2 b_{ri} \rceil + 1 = 3 \cdot \lceil \log_2 68 \rceil + 1 = 3 \cdot 7 + 1 = 22 \text{ block accesses}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final structure summary:**

| Level | Number of Blocks | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Level 0 (Data) | 25,000 | 8 records each |
| Level 1 | 368 | Sparse index, 68 entries/block |
| Level 2 | 6 | Index on L1 |
| Level 3 (Top) | 1 | Resident in RAM |

**(Marks: 1 per Steps 1–4, 0.5 for Step 5, 0.5 each for Steps 6 and 7, 1 for the summary table = 7/7)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Recap**
> 1. **Don't confuse sparse vs. dense index** in the cost calculation. A *dense* primary index has $r$ entries (one per record), not $b_0$.
> 2. **Always state the assumption** that the top level is memory-resident. Skipping this costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **The "+1" data block access** is mandatory — examiners explicitly check for it.
> 4. **For Part B, present the diagram** — even a hand-drawn one. Skipping the diagram costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Avoid $\vert x \vert$ notation inside markdown tables** — KTU expects the explicit `abs(x)` or word "absolute value" in tables.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for KTU 2024 ESE preparation)**

- ✅ A **multilevel index** = a *primary (or clustering) index* built recursively on top of itself.
- ✅ Construction **terminates** when the top level fits in **one block** ($r_k = 1$).
- ✅ **Top level is memory-resident** — it contributes **0 disk I/Os** to the search cost.
- ✅ **Sparse index assumption** for primary index → one entry per *data block*; for dense, one per *record*.
- ✅ **Two key formulas to memorize:**
  - Search cost (linear within level) = $k + 1$
  - Search cost (binary within level) = $k \cdot \lceil \log_2 b_{ri} \rceil + 1$
- ✅ **Blocking factor** $b_{ri} = \lfloor B / \vert \text{index entry} \vert \rfloor$.
- ✅ **Recursion for level counts:** $r_i = \lceil r_{i-1} / b_{ri} \rceil$.
- ✅ Multilevel index **cost grows logarithmically** with file size — this is the central advantage.
- ✅ **Real-world analog:** Library catalog, file-system inode tables, B+ Trees of height $h$.
- ✅ **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Construct a multilevel index for the given relation and find the number of block accesses" — always provide the *full* construction (level-by-level block count) + a labeled diagram.
- ✅ The **last +1** in the search cost formula represents the final data block access — *never forget it*.
- ✅ Multilevel indexes are **distinct from B-Trees and B+ Trees** (Module 3, later topics) — but the **conceptual lineage is identical**; B-Trees are *dynamic* multilevel indexes that handle inserts/deletes via splitting/merging.
- ✅ The total entries across all index levels **decrease geometrically** (e.g., 25,000 → 368 → 6 → 1) — a signature of logarithmic search.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** When the relation is *unsorted*, you cannot build a multilevel *primary* index — you must use a *clustering* or *secondary* index, which has different cost formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Indexing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Indexing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Dynamic Indexing?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Dynamic Indexing* refers to indexing structures in a Database Management System that **automatically reorganize, grow, and shrink** themselves in response to insertions and deletions of records, while preserving efficient search performance (typically $O(\log_d n)$ where $d$ is the order and $n$ is the number of keys). The principal examples are **B-Trees**, **B+ Trees**, **Extendible Hashing**, and **Linear Hashing**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Why Do We Need Dynamic Indexing?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Magic Filing Cabinet**
>
> Imagine a small office library. Initially, the librarian creates a single index card for every 100 books and stores them in a drawer (**static index**). Over a year, **10,000 new books** arrive. The drawer overflows. The librarian must now:
> 1. Manually re-shuffle every card.
> 2. Buy a **second drawer** and create a master index for the drawers.
> 3. Possibly split full drawers into two.
>
> This is exactly what **ISAM (Indexed Sequential Access Method)** requires — costly periodic reorganization.
>
> A **dynamic index** behaves like a *self-expanding filing cabinet*: whenever a drawer gets full, it **automatically splits** in two, and a higher-level index card is created to track the new drawer. The librarian (DBA) does nothing manually. **B-Trees and B+ Trees** are precisely such self-balancing indexing structures.

### 1.3 The Core Problem: Limitations of Static Indexing

Static indexes (e.g., ISAM) suffer from three fundamental issues:

| Limitation | Description |
|---|---|
| **Overflow Chains** | When a leaf page fills up, new records are appended in overflow pages, degrading search cost from $O(\log n)$ to $O(n)$ in the worst case. |
| **Costly Reorganization** | Periodic reorganization locks the file, halting queries for hours. |
| **Fixed Tree Height** | Tree height grows only after full reorganization, causing unpredictable performance. |

Dynamic indexing eliminates these problems by enforcing a self-balancing invariant.

---

## 1.4 The Two Principal Dynamic Index Structures (KTU Focus)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes two structures under Module 3:**
>
> 1. **B-Tree (Balanced Tree)** of order $m$ — every node can have up to $m$ children and $m-1$ keys.
> 2. **B+ Tree** of order $m$ — a variant where all data pointers live exclusively in the leaf nodes, which are also linked sequentially.
>
> Hash-based dynamic methods (Extendible / Linear Hashing) are typically covered in Module 4.

### 1.5 Key Vocabulary (KTU Standard)

- **Order ($m$):** Maximum number of children a node can have.
- **Degree ($d$):** Minimum number of children for non-root internal nodes (commonly $d = \lceil m/2 \rceil$).
- **Root:** Topmost node; may have as few as 2 children (or 1 if only one key exists).
- **Leaf:** Bottom-level node. In **B+ Tree**, leaves are linked.
- **Split Operation:** When a node overflows (exceeds $m-1$ keys), it is split into two, and the median key is **promoted** to the parent.
- **Merge / Redistribution Operation:** When a node underflows (drops below the minimum key count), keys are borrowed from siblings or merged.
- **Fan-Out ($f$):** The *average* number of children per internal node; determines the tree's effective height.
- **Fill Factor:** The fraction of node space actually used. Default target is roughly **69%** in practice due to splitting.
- **Search Key:** The attribute on which the index is built.
- **Pointer / Block Address:** The disk address of a child node or a data record.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The B-Tree of Order $m$ — Definition and Invariants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A B-Tree of order $m$ is a multiway search tree where every node $x$ satisfies:**
>
> 1. Every node has at most $m$ children.
> 2. Every **non-root** internal node has at least $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ children.
> 3. The root has at least 2 children (unless it is a leaf).
> 4. A node with $n$ keys stores $n+1$ pointers to children (or null pointers if leaf).
> 5. All leaf nodes appear at the **same level** — this is what makes the tree "balanced."
> 6. Keys within a node are kept in **sorted order**.

### 2.2 B+ Tree of Order $m$ — Key Differences from B-Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **B+ Tree is the de-facto standard for disk-based DBMS indexes (used by InnoDB, PostgreSQL, Oracle B-tree indexes).**
>
> Differences:
>
> 1. **All data records (or record pointers) live only in leaf nodes.** Internal nodes store only routing keys.
> 2. **Leaves are linked** in a doubly/singly linked list — enables fast **range queries**.
> 3. A leaf with $n$ keys stores exactly $n$ search-key-to-record-pointer pairs.
> 4. An internal node with $n$ keys has exactly $n+1$ children, where key $K_i$ routes to the subtree containing keys in the range $(K_i, K_{i+1})$.
> 5. **Insertion / Deletion rules apply only at the leaf level**, simplifying the algorithm.

### 2.3 Operations Walk-Through

#### 2.3.1 Searching a Key in a B+ Tree
- Start at root.
- At each internal node, do a binary search over the $n$ keys.
- Descend to the appropriate child.
- At leaf, scan keys left-to-right.

**Cost:** $O(\log_d n)$ disk accesses, where $d$ is the minimum degree (degree of fan-out).

#### 2.3.2 Insertion in a B+ Tree
1. Find the target leaf using search.
2. Insert key in sorted order.
3. **If leaf overflows** (more than $m-1$ keys):
   - Split leaf into two leaves.
   - Copy the **smallest key of the right half** up to the parent as a routing key.
4. **If parent overflows**, recursively split and propagate upward.
5. **If root splits**, create a new root (tree height increases by 1).

#### 2.3.3 Deletion from a B+ Tree
1. Find and remove the key from the leaf.
2. **If leaf underflows** (fewer than $\lceil (m-1)/2 \rceil$ keys):
   - Try **redistribution** (borrow) from a sibling.
   - Else, **merge** leaf with sibling and pull down the separator key from parent.
3. Recursively rebalance upward.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE numerical/model questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximum keys per node | $m - 1$ | For order $m$ |
| 2 | Minimum keys in non-root leaf | $\lceil (m-1)/2 \rceil$ | B+ Tree |
| 3 | Minimum children of non-root internal node | $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ | Both B & B+ |
| 4 | Maximum children per node | $m$ | Order |
| 5 | Tree height (worst case) | $h \le 1 + \log_{\lceil m/2 \rceil} \bigl(\tfrac{N+1}{2}\bigr)$ | $N$ = number of keys |
| 6 | Maximum number of keys in tree of height $h$ | $N_{\max} = (2 \cdot \lceil m/2 \rceil^{h} - 1) \cdot (\text{keys per leaf})$ | B+ Tree |
| 7 | Number of disk accesses for search | $h$ | One per level |
| 8 | Number of disk accesses for range query $[a,b]$ | $h + \lceil (b-a)/(\text{keys per leaf}) \rceil$ | B+ Tree with linked leaves |
| 9 | Effective fan-out | $f \approx \lceil m/2 \rceil$ | Typical after splits |
| 10 | Space utilization (long-run average) | $\approx \ln 2 \approx 69.3\%$ | Theoretical steady-state |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In all equations above, vertical bars are typeset as `\lceil` / `\rceil` or `\vert`. **Never** use the literal pipe character `|` inside the markdown table, as it breaks the column separator.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OLTP Databases** — InnoDB, PostgreSQL B-tree indexes use B+ Trees for primary keys and secondary indexes.
- **Filesystems** — NTFS, ReiserFS, HFS+, ext4 all use **B+ Tree variants** (called B-trees in their codebases).
- **Search Engines** — Lucene / Elasticsearch postings lists use B-Tree variants for term dictionaries.
- **Embedded / Real-Time Systems** — Predictable $O(\log_d n)$ search latency is critical; dynamic indexing delivers this.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Worst-Case Height of a B+ Tree of Order $m$ with $N$ Search Keys

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the maximum possible height $h$ of a B+ Tree of order $m$ holding $N$ keys. This is a classic KTU 14-mark sub-question.

### Step 1 — Define the Minimum Degree

For a B+ Tree of order $m$, the minimum number of children at the root is 2, and at every other internal node it is $\lceil m/2 \rceil$.

$$
d_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{m}{2} \right\rceil
$$

### Step 2 — Upper Bound the Number of Keys at the Lowest Internal Level

The internal nodes just above the leaves form a B-Tree (with possibly fewer children at the root). The minimum number of children at this level (excluding the root case) is $d_{\min}$.

At depth 1 (children of the root): minimum children $\;=\; d_{\min}$.

### Step 3 — Recurse Up the Tree

At depth $i$ (counting root as depth 0), the minimum number of nodes is:

$$
n_{\text{nodes}}(i) \ge d_{\min}^{i}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Node Count to Leaf Count

Each internal node at depth $i$ has at least $d_{\min}$ children, and the deepest internal level holds the parents of all leaves.

For a tree of height $h$ (root at level 0, leaves at level $h$):

$$
\text{Number of leaves} \;\ge\; d_{\min}^{h}
$$

### Step 5 — Account for Minimum Keys per Leaf

Each leaf holds at least $\lceil (m-1)/2 \rceil$ keys. Call this quantity $L_{\min}$.

$$
N \;\ge\; L_{\min} \cdot d_{\min}^{h}
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $h$

$$
d_{\min}^{h} \;\le\; \frac{N}{L_{\min}}
$$

$$
h \cdot \log d_{\min} \;\le\; \log\!\left(\frac{N}{L_{\min}}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;h \;\le\; \frac{\log(N \,/\, L_{\min})}{\log d_{\min}} \;=\; \frac{\log N - \log L_{\min}}{\log \lceil m/2 \rceil}\;}
$$

### Step 7 — Interpretation (Why It Matters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For $m = 100$ (typical for disk pages of size 4 KB holding 100 keys), $d_{\min} = 50$. A tree of **1 billion** keys has:
>
> $$
> h \;\le\; \frac{\log_{10}(10^{9})}{\log_{10}(50)} \;\approx\; \frac{9}{1.699} \;\approx\; 5.3
> $$
>
> So **at most 6 disk accesses** to find any record. This is why B+ Trees dominate database indexing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Disk Block Accesses for a Range Query

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Compute the I/O cost of retrieving all records whose keys lie in $[a, b]$.

### Step 1 — Locate the Left Boundary

Search from the root to the leaf containing $a$. This costs **$h$ disk accesses**, where $h$ is the tree height.

$$
T_1 = h
$$

### Step 2 — Traverse the Linked Leaves

Let $\text{keys\_per\_leaf} = k_{\ell}$. The number of leaves spanned by the interval $[a,b]$ is:

$$
T_2 = \left\lceil \frac{b - a + 1}{k_{\ell}} \right\rceil
$$

### Step 3 — Total Cost

$$
\boxed{\;T_{\text{range}} \;=\; h \;+\; \left\lceil \frac{b - a + 1}{k_{\ell}} \right\rceil\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without leaf linking** (as in plain B-Tree), the cost would be $h \cdot T_2$ — far worse. This is the key reason B+ Trees dominate.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — B-Tree vs B+ Tree vs ISAM

| Property | B-Tree | B+ Tree | ISAM (Static) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data pointers in internal nodes | Yes | No | Yes |
| Leaf nodes linked | No | Yes | No |
| Search cost | $O(h)$ | $O(h)$ | $O(h)$ but degrades |
| Range query | $O(h + r \cdot h)$ | $O(h + r)$ | Slow due to overflow chains |
| Insert/Delete cost | Moderate | Moderate | Periodic full reorg |
| Space utilization | $\sim 69\%$ | $\sim 69\%$ | High but rigid |
| Used in production | Rare | **Default everywhere** | Legacy |

In the formula above, $h$ denotes the tree height and $r$ the number of result records.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** Insert the keys $\{10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90\}$ in this order into a B+ Tree of order $m=4$ (so each leaf holds at most 3 keys).

**Insertion Trace:**

1. Insert **10, 20, 30** → Leaf: `[10, 20, 30]`.
2. Insert **40** → Leaf overflows (4 keys). **Split:** Left `[10, 20]`, Right `[30, 40]`. Copy up `30` to root.
   - Root: `[30]`; Leaves: `[10,20] | [30,40]`.
3. Insert **50, 60** → Right leaf becomes `[30,40,50,60]` → overflow. **Split:** `[30,40] | [50,60]`. Copy up `50`.
   - Root: `[30, 50]`; Leaves: `[10,20] | [30,40] | [50,60]`.
4. Insert **70, 80, 90** → similarly splits and propagates.

**Final Tree Structure (after all inserts):**

$$
\text{Root: } [40, 70]
$$

Leaves (linked list): `[10,20,30] ↔ [40,50,60] ↔ [70,80,90]`

**Search cost for key 80:** 2 disk accesses (root → middle leaf) — efficient and predictable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are rendered as Mermaid `flowchart` blocks (KTU board-exam friendly and reproducible in plain Markdown). Node IDs are alphanumeric only; labels contain plain uppercase text to avoid rendering conflicts.

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — B+ Tree of Order 4 After the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["ROOT : 40 , 70"]
    p1["INTERNAL : 20 , 30"]
    p2["INTERNAL : 50 , 60"]
    p3["INTERNAL : 80 , 90"]
    leafA["LEAF 1 : 10"]
    leafB["LEAF 2 : 20 , 30"]
    leafC["LEAF 3 : 40 , 50"]
    leafD["LEAF 4 : 60"]
    leafE["LEAF 5 : 70 , 80"]
    leafF["LEAF 6 : 90"]

    root --> p1
    root --> p2
    root --> p3
    p1 --> leafA
    p1 --> leafB
    p1 --> leafC
    p2 --> leafD
    p2 --> leafE
    p3 --> leafF

    leafA -. NEXT .-> leafB
    leafB -. NEXT .-> leafC
    leafC -. NEXT .-> leafD
    leafD -. NEXT .-> leafE
    leafE -. NEXT .-> leafF
```

**Legend:**
- Solid arrows: parent-to-child pointer (tree links).
- Dotted arrows labelled `NEXT`: the linked-list of leaves that enables fast range queries.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — B-Tree Insertion with Split (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input["INSERT NEW KEY K"]
    search["SEARCH FROM ROOT TO LEAF"]
    place["PLACE K IN TARGET LEAF"]
    check{"LEAF HAS MORE THAN M MINUS 1 KEYS ?"}
    done["INSERT COMPLETE NO OVERFLOW"]
    splitLeaf["SPLIT LEAF INTO L1 AND L2"]
    promote["COPY MEDIAN KEY UP TO PARENT"]
    parentCheck{"PARENT OVERFLOWS ?"}
    done2["INSERT COMPLETE"]
    splitInternal["SPLIT INTERNAL NODE"]
    newRoot["CREATE NEW ROOT TREE HEIGHT PLUS 1"]

    input --> search
    search --> place
    place --> check
    check -- NO --> done
    check -- YES --> splitLeaf
    splitLeaf --> promote
    promote --> parentCheck
    parentCheck -- NO --> done2
    parentCheck -- YES --> splitInternal
    splitInternal --> newRoot
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Static (ISAM) vs Dynamic (B+ Tree) Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STATIC["STATIC INDEX ISAM"]
        s1["FIXED PRIMARY AREA"]
        s2["OVERFLOW PAGES GROW OVER TIME"]
        s3["PERIODIC FULL REORGANIZATION NEEDED"]
    end

    subgraph DYNAMIC["DYNAMIC INDEX B PLUS TREE"]
        d1["EVERY NODE SELF BALANCING"]
        d2["SPLIT AND MERGE ON OVERFLOW OR UNDERFLOW"]
        d3["LOG N PERFORMANCE GUARANTEED AT ALL TIMES"]
    end

    STATIC -- MIGRATION TO --> DYNAMIC
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Deletion Underflow & Rebalancing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    del["DELETE KEY K"]
    find["LOCATE LEAF CONTAINING K"]
    rem["REMOVE K FROM LEAF"]
    uf{"LEAF FEWER THAN MIN KEYS ?"}
    rb["TRY REDISTRIBUTE BORROW FROM SIBLING"]
    merge["MERGE WITH SIBLING AND PULL DOWN SEPARATOR"]
    pup{"PARENT NOW UNDERFLOWING ?"}
    fin["DELETE COMPLETE TREE REBALANCED"]
    rootShrink["ROOT HAD ONE CHILD REMOVE ROOT LEVEL"]

    del --> find --> rem --> uf
    uf -- NO --> fin
    uf -- YES --> rb
    rb -- SUCCESS --> fin
    rb -- FAIL --> merge --> pup
    pup -- NO --> fin
    pup -- YES --> rootShrink --> fin
```
