# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The questions below mirror the **actual KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks, short answer) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice). Model answers follow KTU board examiner valuation standards.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between B-Tree and B+ Tree indexing structures. List any four differences.**

**Model Answer (Board-Exam Standard):**

| Sl. No. | B-Tree | B+ Tree |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Data pointers (to actual records) exist in **both internal and leaf nodes**. | Data pointers exist **only in leaf nodes**; internal nodes store only routing keys. |
| 2 | Leaf nodes are **not linked** to each other. | Leaf nodes are **linked sequentially**, enabling fast range scans. |
| 3 | A successful search may terminate at any internal node. | A successful search **always reaches a leaf node**. |
| 4 | **Fewer disk accesses** for point queries that end early. | **More predictable** I/O cost; range queries are dramatically faster. |

> [!NOTE]
> Any 4 valid differences fetch full 3 marks. A labeled table presentation is preferred by KTU examiners.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Order" of a B-Tree. Why is a B+ Tree of order $m$ preferred over order $m-1$ for the same disk page?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Order $m$ of a B-Tree:** The **maximum number of children** that any node in the tree may have. Consequently, the maximum number of keys in any node is $m-1$.
- **Why higher $m$ is preferred:**
  1. Larger $m$ → larger **fan-out** → **shorter tree height** → fewer disk accesses per query.
  2. For a fixed page size, packing more keys per node **amortizes** the cost of one I/O across more keys.
  3. It directly reduces the asymptotic search cost from $O(\log_d n)$ where $d = \lceil m/2 \rceil$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice. Both alternatives below are full 14-mark questions with 7 + 7 sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the structure of a B+ Tree of order $m$ with a neat diagram. State all the invariants that every node must satisfy.*

**Model Answer:**

A **B+ Tree of order $m$** is an indexed sequential file organization where:
- Every node stores up to $m-1$ search-key values in sorted order.
- Each internal node with $n$ keys has exactly $n+1$ child pointers.
- **Leaf nodes** store the actual record pointers (or the records themselves) and are linked in a linear chain for sequential access.
- The **root** has between 2 and $m$ children (1 key minimum if it is also a leaf).
- Every other **internal node** has between $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ and $m$ children.
- Every **leaf** (except possibly the root) has between $\lceil (m-1)/2 \rceil$ and $m-1$ values.
- **All leaves appear at the same level** (perfectly balanced).

**Invariants (memorise for board exam):**
1. Balance: equal path length from root to every leaf.
2. Occupancy: every node (except root) is at least half-full.
3. Ordering: in-order traversal yields sorted keys.
4. Linking: leaf nodes form a linked list for range queries.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Consider a B+ Tree of order $m = 4$. Insert the keys $\{5, 8, 1, 7, 3, 12, 9, 6\}$ in the given order. Show the tree structure after each split. Also compute the total number of disk accesses required to search for the key $9$ in the final tree.*

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step Construction):**

**Initial empty tree:** Root becomes a leaf.

**Step 1 — Insert 5, 8, 1:**
- Leaf becomes `[1, 5, 8]`. No overflow. Tree height = 1.

**Step 2 — Insert 7:**
- Leaf becomes `[1, 5, 7, 8]` → **OVERFLOW** (4 keys, max = 3).
- **Split:** Left leaf `[1, 5]`, Right leaf `[7, 8]`.
- **Copy up** the smallest key of the right leaf, i.e., `7`, into a new root.
- Final tree:
$$
\text{Root: } [7]; \quad \text{Leaves: } [1,5] \leftrightarrow [7,8]
$$

**Step 3 — Insert 3, 12:**
- Left leaf becomes `[1, 3, 5]`.
- Right leaf becomes `[7, 8, 12]`. No overflow.

**Step 4 — Insert 9:**
- Right leaf becomes `[7, 8, 9, 12]` → **OVERFLOW**.
- **Split:** Left `[7, 8]`, Right `[9, 12]`.
- **Copy up** `9` to root.
- Root becomes `[7, 9]`.

**Step 5 — Insert 6:**
- Goes to leaf `[1, 3, 5]` → becomes `[1, 3, 5, 6]` → **OVERFLOW**.
- **Split:** Left `[1, 3]`, Right `[5, 6]`. Copy up `5`.
- Root becomes `[5, 7, 9]`. **Root overflow** (max = 3 keys → ok, but children exceed 4? No, only 3 children, so fine).
- Children leaves (linked): `[1,3] ↔ [5,6] ↔ [7,8] ↔ [9,12]`.

**Final B+ Tree:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA["ROOT 5 , 7 , 9"]
    leaf1A["LEAF 1, 3"]
    leaf2A["LEAF 5, 6"]
    leaf3A["LEAF 7, 8"]
    leaf4A["LEAF 9, 12"]

    rootA --> leaf1A
    rootA --> leaf2A
    rootA --> leaf3A
    rootA --> leaf4A

    leaf1A -. NEXT .-> leaf2A
    leaf2A -. NEXT .-> leaf3A
    leaf3A -. NEXT .-> leaf4A
```

**Search for key 9:**
1. Read root `[5, 7, 9]` → 9 lies in the rightmost child. **1 disk access.**
2. Read leaf `[9, 12]` → key 9 found. **1 disk access.**

**Total disk accesses = 2.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often write "split by pushing up the median" for **B+ Tree**. This is wrong — B+ Tree **copies up the smallest key of the right half**, it does **not** push up. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> - Forgetting to update the linked-list pointer after a leaf split also loses 1 mark.
> - Not explicitly stating "disk accesses = height of tree" loses 1 mark in part (b).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Discuss the limitations of static indexing (ISAM). Explain how B-Trees overcome each limitation. Use a diagram to show the structure of a B-Tree of order 3 containing the keys $\{10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60\}$.*

**Model Answer:**

**Limitations of ISAM:**
1. **Overflow chains** form when leaf pages are full, degrading search to linear in the worst case.
2. **Fixed structure:** the index cannot grow gracefully; only via costly overflow pages.
3. **Periodic full reorganization** is required, causing downtime.
4. **Concurrent updates** are difficult because of the static structure.

**How B-Trees overcome these:**
1. **Self-balancing** — every insertion or deletion automatically preserves equal path lengths.
2. **Node splits** prevent overflow chains by immediately redistributing keys into a new sibling.
3. **Height grows by exactly 1** only when the root splits — predictable, rare event.
4. **Latches** are easier to manage because the tree shape is well-bounded.

**B-Tree of order 3 (so each node has max 2 keys, max 3 children) holding keys $\{10,20,30,40,50,60\}$:**

- Insertion trace (compact):
  - Insert 10, 20 → root `[10, 20]`.
  - Insert 30 → root overflows. **Split:** left `[10]`, right `[20, 30]`; **push up** median `20`.
  - Insert 40 → goes to right child → `[20, 30, 40]` overflows. Split: `[20, 30]`, `[40]`; push up `30` → root becomes `[20, 30]`.
  - Insert 50, 60 → propagate further.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootB["ROOT 30 , 50"]
    leftInt["INTERNAL 20"]
    midInt["INTERNAL 40"]
    rightInt["INTERNAL 60"]
    leafB1["LEAF 10"]
    leafB2["LEAF 20 , 30"]
    leafB3["LEAF 40"]
    leafB4["LEAF 50"]
    leafB5["LEAF 60"]

    rootB --> leftInt
    rootB --> midInt
    rootB --> rightInt
    leftInt --> leafB1
    leftInt --> leafB2
    midInt --> leafB3
    midInt --> leafB4
    rightInt --> leafB5
```

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Derive the worst-case height of a B+ Tree of order $m$ containing $N$ search keys. Hence compute the maximum number of disk accesses needed to search a record when $m = 128$ and $N = 10^{8}$.*

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

Let $d = \lceil m/2 \rceil$ be the minimum number of children of any non-root internal node.

At depth $i$, the minimum number of nodes is $d^{i}$.

A tree of height $h$ has at least $d^{h}$ leaves. Each leaf stores at least $\lceil (m-1)/2 \rceil$ keys.

$$
N \;\ge\; d^{h} \cdot \left\lceil \frac{m-1}{2} \right\rceil
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad h \;\le\; \frac{\log N - \log(\lceil (m-1)/2 \rceil)}{\log \lceil m/2 \rceil}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

- $m = 128 \Rightarrow d = \lceil 128/2 \rceil = 64$.
- $N = 10^{8}$.

$$
h \;\le\; \frac{\log_{10}(10^{8})}{\log_{10}(64)} \;=\; \frac{8}{1.806} \;\approx\; 4.43
$$

$$
\boxed{\;h_{\max} = 5\;}
$$

**Maximum disk accesses for searching any record = 5.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for Question B:**
> - Confusing "minimum children" with "maximum children" loses 2 marks immediately.
> - Skipping the derivation step and writing only the final formula loses 3 marks — KTU examiners require intermediate algebraic steps.
> - Using $\log$ without specifying the base is acceptable **only if** the base is consistent across numerator and denominator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read Before Entering the Exam Hall):**
>
> - **Dynamic Indexing** = index structures that self-reorganize on insert/delete without manual DBA intervention.
> - **B-Tree order $m$:** max $m$ children, max $m-1$ keys per node; min $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ children per non-root internal node.
> - **B+ Tree key facts:**
>   1. Data pointers only in leaves.
>   2. Leaves are **linked sequentially**.
>   3. **Copy up** on leaf split (smallest key of right half).
>   4. **Push up** on internal-node split (median key).
>   5. Range queries cost $h + \lceil (b-a)/k_{\ell} \rceil$ disk accesses.
> - **Tree height formula:** $h \le \log_{\lceil m/2 \rceil}(N / L_{\min})$.
> - **Real-world usage:** InnoDB, PostgreSQL, Oracle B-tree indexes, NTFS, ext4, HFS+.
> - **Performance:** $O(\log_d n)$ for search/insert/delete, where $d$ is the minimum degree (fan-out).
> - **Space utilization:** theoretically $\ln 2 \approx 69\%$ long-run average due to splits.
> - **Static vs Dynamic:** ISAM needs periodic reorganization and grows via overflow chains; B/B+ Trees grow via node splits.
> - **Common exam trap:** forgetting that B+ Tree leaves stay linked after a split.
> - **Another common trap:** stating "B-Tree has linked leaves" — that is B+ Tree, not B-Tree.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: B Trees and B+ Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** B Trees and B+ Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 B-Tree: Formal Academic Definition

A **B-Tree of order $m$** is a self-balancing, height-balanced, multi-way search tree designed to optimize data access in secondary storage devices (such as magnetic disks and SSDs). It generalizes the binary search tree by allowing a node to contain **more than one key and more than two children**, thereby drastically reducing the tree's height and consequently the number of expensive disk I/O operations required for search, insertion, and deletion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Property Set of a B-Tree of order $m$ (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> 1. Every node can hold a maximum of $m$ children.
> 2. Every internal node (except the root) holds at least $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ children.
> 3. The root node must have a minimum of **2 children** if it is not a leaf node. (A root can have only 1 key.)
> 4. A non-leaf node possessing $k$ children contains exactly $k-1$ keys.
> 5. All leaf nodes appear at the **exact same level** (perfectly balanced height).
> 6. The keys within any node are stored in **strictly increasing sorted order**.

### 1.2 B+ Tree: Formal Academic Definition

A **B+ Tree of order $m$** is a refined variant of the B-Tree in which all the **actual data records (or data pointers) reside exclusively in the leaf nodes**, while the internal nodes act purely as a routing/索引 layer, storing only keys for navigation. The leaf nodes are additionally **linked sequentially** via sibling pointers, facilitating blazing-fast range queries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In a B-Tree, keys may exist in both internal nodes and leaf nodes. In a B+ Tree, keys in internal nodes are **redundant copies** used solely for path navigation — the true keys and data live at the leaf level.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy (The Phone Directory Library)

Imagine a massive **central library containing millions of books** spread across hundreds of shelves. The librarian needs an indexing system so she can find any book in at most 3-4 visits to a shelf.

- **A Binary Search Tree (BST)** is like searching a phone book by flipping one page at a time and deciding "left or right." With millions of books, you would make millions of trips.
- **A B-Tree** is like an indexing catalog where each drawer (node) contains **many alphabet ranges** (e.g., $A$–$D$, $E$–$H$, $I$–$L$) and has **many pointers** to sub-drawers. Each "drawer visit" is one disk I/O. By grouping many keys per node, we reduce the total trips dramatically.
- **A B+ Tree** is the same, but the catalog drawers only tell you **which range of letters lives on which floor** (routing keys), while the **actual book data (full title, ISBN, author)** is only found in the final "book shelves" (leaf nodes). The shelves are arranged in a long corridor so once you find the first book in a range, you simply **walk along the corridor** to read the next books (range queries).

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for Databases:** Disk I/O is the slowest operation (millions of times slower than RAM access). A B+ Tree of order 100+ can index billions of records in a tree of height only **3 or 4**, meaning *any record is found in 3-4 disk reads* — making it the **de-facto standard** for database indexing (used in MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MongoDB WiredTiger, etc.).

### 1.4 Key Design Parameters

| Parameter | B-Tree | B+ Tree |
|---|---|---|
| Data stored in internal nodes | Yes | No (only routing keys) |
| Data stored in leaf nodes | Yes | Yes |
| Leaf node linkage | No | Yes (sibling pointers) |
| Search cost | Variable (may stop at any level) | Uniform (always reaches leaf) |
| Range query efficiency | Poor | Excellent (sequential leaf scan) |
| Space utilization | Lower | Higher (internal nodes are sparser) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic: Why B-Trees are Height-Balanced

The brilliance of the B-Tree is that it maintains all leaves at the **exact same level** through a self-correcting splitting and merging mechanism. This guarantees that search, insertion, and deletion all operate in **$O(\log_m N)$** time, where $N$ is the number of records and $m$ is the order.

#### 2.1.1 The Underlying Logic of B-Tree Search

1. Begin at the **root node**.
2. Perform a **linear/binary search** within the node's sorted key array.
3. If the key is found → return success.
4. If not found, identify the child pointer whose range contains the search key → descend to that child.
5. Repeat recursively until a leaf node is reached or the key is located.

#### 2.1.2 The Underlying Logic of B-Tree Insertion

1. Search for the correct leaf position (same as B-Tree search).
2. If the leaf has space (less than $m-1$ keys) → **insert directly**.
3. If the leaf is **full** (already has $m-1$ keys) → **split the node**:
   - The middle key is **promoted** to the parent.
   - The remaining keys are divided: left half stays, right half moves to a new node.
4. If the parent also becomes overfull → the split **propagates upward** (cascading split).
5. If the root splits → a **new root is created** and the tree height grows by 1.

#### 2.1.3 The Underlying Logic of B-Tree Deletion

1. Locate and remove the key.
2. If the key is in an **internal node**, swap it with its **in-order successor** (a leaf), then delete from the leaf.
3. If a node falls below the minimum key threshold $\lceil m/2 \rceil - 1$, perform one of:
   - **Rotation (Borrow):** Steal a key from an adjacent sibling that has extra keys.
   - **Merge (Coalesce):** Combine the underflowing node with a sibling, pulling down the parent separator key. This may cause the parent to underflow (cascading merge).
4. If the root becomes empty after a merge → the root is removed and the tree **shrinks in height by 1**.

### 2.2 B+ Tree Operational Logic

The insertion and deletion logic is **conceptually identical** to the B-Tree, with one critical difference: when a leaf overflows, the **middle key is *copied* up to the parent (not promoted)**, and the parent simply uses it as a routing separator. The original key remains in the leaf.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas are bound to a B-Tree / B+ Tree of order $m$ (max children per node) OR minimum degree $t$.**

#### Universal Parameter Formulas

| Metric | B-Tree (Order $m$) | B+ Tree (Order $m$) |
|---|---|---|
| Max children per internal node | $m$ | $m$ |
| Min children (non-root internal) | $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ | $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ |
| Max keys per node | $m-1$ | $m-1$ |
| Min keys per node (non-root) | $\lceil m/2 \rceil - 1$ | $\lceil m/2 \rceil - 1$ |
| Min children of root | $2$ (if not leaf) | $2$ (if not leaf) |
| Min keys of root | $1$ | $1$ |

#### Alternative Parameterization (Minimum Degree $t$)

When the textbook uses **minimum degree $t$** instead of order $m$:

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Max children per node | $2t$ |
| Min children (non-root) | $t$ |
| Max keys per node | $2t - 1$ |
| Min keys per node (non-root) | $t - 1$ |

#### Height and Disk I/O Formulas

The **height $h$** of a B-Tree containing $N$ keys is bounded as:

$$h \leq 1 + \log_{\lceil m/2 \rceil} \left( \frac{N+1}{2} \right)$$

The **minimum height** for $N$ keys is:

$$h_{\min} = 1 + \log_{m} \left( \frac{N+1}{2} \right)$$

The **number of disk accesses** for a search is exactly $h$ (for B+ Tree) or at most $h$ (for B-Tree, since the key may be found earlier in an internal node).

#### Number of Nodes and Keys

For a B-Tree of height $h$ with $N$ keys and order $m$, the **total number of nodes** lies in the range:

$$\frac{N+1}{m} \leq \text{Total Nodes} \leq N+1$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Indexing:** The single most important application. Every major RDBMS uses B+ Tree indexes for primary keys and secondary indexes.
- **File Systems:** NTFS (Windows), ext4 (Linux), ReFS, HFS+, and APFS all use B+ Tree variants (often B-Tree variants called B*-Trees) for directory indexing.
- **In-Memory Databases:** Even RAM-resident databases use B-Trees for predictable, cache-friendly performance.
- **Solid State Drives (SSDs):** While SSDs have no mechanical seek time, B+ Trees still help due to **page-based read granularity** and reduced I/O amplification.
- **Information Retrieval:** Used in inverted index structures for full-text search engines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: B-Tree of Order 3 (2-3 Tree) — Insertion Sequence

Let us construct a B-Tree of order $m=3$ by inserting the keys: **$10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 25$**.

**Order 3 means:** Max children = 3, Max keys per node = 2, Min keys (non-root) = 1, Min children (non-root) = 2.

#### Step 1: Insert 10
- Tree is empty. Root is created.
- Structure: `[10]`

#### Step 2: Insert 20
- Root `[10]` has space. Insert sorted.
- Structure: `[10, 20]`

#### Step 3: Insert 30
- Root `[10, 20]` is full (2 keys = max). Insertion causes **overflow**.
- Split: middle key `20` is promoted. The new root contains `[20]`, with left child `[10]` and right child `[30]`.

$$
\text{After insert 30:} \quad \begin{array}{c} [20] \\ / \quad \backslash \\ [10] \quad [30] \end{array}
$$

#### Step 4: Insert 40
- Search: $40 > 20 \Rightarrow$ descend to right child `[30]`.
- Node `[30]` has space. Insert sorted.
- Structure: Right child becomes `[30, 40]`.

#### Step 5: Insert 50
- Search: $50 > 20 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[30, 40]`.
- Node `[30, 40]` is full → **split**.
- Middle key `40` is promoted. Parent `[20]` receives `40` and becomes `[20, 40]`.
- New node: `[50]` becomes the right child of the new separator.

$$
\text{After insert 50:} \quad \begin{array}{c} [20, 40] \\ / \quad \vert \quad \backslash \\ [10] \quad [30] \quad [50] \end{array}
$$

#### Step 6: Insert 60
- Search: $60 > 40 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[50]`. Space available.
- Structure: Right child becomes `[50, 60]`.

#### Step 7: Insert 70
- Search: $70 > 40 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[50, 60]`. Full → **split**.
- Middle key `60` promoted. Parent `[20, 40]` becomes `[20, 40, 60]` — **OVERFLOW!**
- Split the root: middle key `40` becomes the new root. Left subtree: `[20]`, Right subtree: `[60]`.
- The original grandchildren are re-distributed: `10` under `20` (left), `30` under `20` (right), `50` under `60` (left), `70` under `60` (right).

$$
\text{After insert 70:} \quad \begin{array}{c} [40] \\ / \quad \backslash \\ [20] \quad [60] \\ / \quad \backslash \quad \vert \quad \backslash \\ [10] \quad [30] \quad [50] \quad [70] \end{array}
$$

**Notice: The tree height grew from 2 to 3.**

#### Step 8: Insert 25
- Search: $25 < 40 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[20]`. Then $25 > 20 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[30]`.
- Node `[30]` has space. Insert sorted.
- Structure: `[10] \quad [25, 30] \quad [50] \quad [70]` under the `[20] \quad [60]` parents.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: B+ Tree of Order 4 — Insertion Sequence

Let us construct a B+ Tree of order $m=4$ (max 4 children, max 3 keys per node) by inserting: **$5, 8, 1, 7, 3, 12, 9, 6$**.

**Order 4 means:** Max children = 4, Max keys = 3, Min keys (non-root) = 2, Min children = 2.

#### Step 1: Insert 5
- Tree is empty. Root is a leaf.
- Structure: Leaf `[5]`

#### Step 2: Insert 8
- Leaf `[5]` has space (max 3 keys).
- Structure: Leaf `[5, 8]`

#### Step 3: Insert 1
- Sorted insert.
- Structure: Leaf `[1, 5, 8]`

#### Step 4: Insert 7
- Leaf `[1, 5, 8]` is full → **leaf split**.
- Middle key (we'll use `5` as separator) is **copied up** to a new root.
- Left leaf: `[1]`, Right leaf: `[5, 7, 8]`.

$$
\text{After insert 7:} \quad \begin{array}{c} [5] \\ \diagdown \quad \diagdown \\ [1] \quad [5, 7, 8] \end{array}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical B+ Tree rule:** When a leaf splits, the first key of the **right** leaf is **copied up** to the parent. The left leaf does not retain this key.

#### Step 5: Insert 3
- Search: $3 < 5 \Rightarrow$ descend to left leaf `[1]`. Space available.
- Structure: Left leaf becomes `[1, 3]`.

#### Step 6: Insert 12
- Search: $12 > 5 \Rightarrow$ descend to right leaf `[5, 7, 8]`. Space available (max 3, currently has 3 — full).
- **Leaf split**: middle key `7` is copied up. New right leaf: `[8, 12]`, left leaf becomes `[5, 7]`.
- Parent root `[5]` now receives `7` → becomes `[5, 7]` (internal node, max 3 keys allowed).

$$
\text{After insert 12:} \quad \begin{array}{c} [5, 7] \\ \diagdown \quad \diagdown \quad \diagdown \\ [1, 3] \quad [5, 7] \quad [8, 12] \end{array}
$$

#### Step 7: Insert 9
- Search: $9 > 7 \Rightarrow$ descend to right leaf `[8, 12]`. Space available.
- Structure: Right leaf becomes `[8, 9, 12]`.

#### Step 8: Insert 6
- Search: $6 \geq 5$ and $6 \leq 7 \Rightarrow$ descend to middle leaf `[5, 7]`. Space available.
- Structure: Middle leaf becomes `[5, 6, 7]`.

**Final B+ Tree:**

$$
\begin{array}{c} [5, 7] \\ \diagdown \quad \diagdown \quad \diagdown \\ [1, 3] \rightarrow [5, 6, 7] \rightarrow [8, 9, 12] \end{array}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observe the horizontal arrows** ($\rightarrow$): these are the **sibling pointers** linking the leaves in sorted order. A range query like "find all keys between 4 and 10" would locate 4 in leaf `[5, 6, 7]`, then simply **scan rightward** through `[8, 9, 12]`, retrieving all matching records without any further tree descent. This is the secret of B+ Tree's dominance in OLTP range scans.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Tree Operations

| Operation | B-Tree | B+ Tree |
|---|---|---|
| Search key | May stop at any internal level (height $\leq h$) | **Always descends to leaf** (height $= h$ exactly) |
| Range query | Requires in-order traversal: complex | **Sequential leaf scan** via sibling pointers: trivial |
| Insertion | Split and **promote** middle key | Split and **copy** middle key to parent |
| Space utilization | Lower (keys duplicated in internal & leaves) | Higher (internal nodes are sparser; all data at leaves) |
| Sequential access | Not supported natively | **Native** (linked leaves) |
| Tree height | Same $O(\log_m N)$ | Same $O(\log_m N)$ (typically 1 level shorter due to better fan-out) |
| Cache performance | Better (keys may be found in internal nodes) | Worse (always reaches leaf), but compensated by sequential I/O |

### 3.4 Height Derivation for KTU Numerical Problems

**Question:** What is the maximum number of keys that can be stored in a B-Tree of order $m=5$ and height $h=3$ (counting root as height 0)?

**Solution:** The maximum number of keys occurs when every node is at its **maximum capacity**.

- Level 0 (Root): max keys = $m - 1 = 4$
- Level 1: max nodes = $m = 5$, each with $m - 1 = 4$ keys
- Level 2: max nodes = $m^2 = 25$, each with $m - 1 = 4$ keys

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max keys} &= 1 \cdot (m-1) + m \cdot (m-1) + m^2 \cdot (m-1) + \dots + m^{h-1} \cdot (m-1) \\
&= (m-1) \sum_{i=0}^{h-1} m^{i} \\
&= (m-1) \cdot \frac{m^{h} - 1}{m - 1} \\
&= m^{h} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

For $m=5, h=3$:

$$\text{Max keys} = 5^{3} - 1 = 125 - 1 = \mathbf{124 \text{ keys}}$$

**Minimum number of keys** (using $\lceil m/2 \rceil = 3$ as min children):

$$\text{Min keys} = 2 \cdot \lceil m/2 \rceil^{h-1} - 1 = 2 \cdot 3^{2} - 1 = 2 \cdot 9 - 1 = \mathbf{17 \text{ keys}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** For a B-Tree of order $m$ and height $h$, the **maximum number of keys is $m^{h} - 1$** (when root is counted as level 0 and $h$ is the number of levels from root to leaf).

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: B-Tree Structure of Order 4 (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["P1 P3 P5"]:::root
    childA1["D1 D2"]:::leaf
    childA2["D3 D4 D5 D6"]:::leaf
    childA3["D7 D8 D9"]:::leaf
    rootA --> childA1
    rootA --> childA2
    rootA --> childA3
    childA1 -."sibling".-> childA2
    childA2 -."sibling".-> childA3
    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: B+ Tree Structure of Order 4 (Leaf-Linked)

```mermaid
graph TD
    internalA["K1 K3 K5"]:::internal
    leaf1["DATA 1, DATA 2"]:::leaf
    leaf2["DATA 3, DATA 4, DATA 5, DATA 6"]:::leaf
    leaf3["DATA 7, DATA 8, DATA 9"]:::leaf
    internalA --> leaf1
    internalA --> leaf2
    internalA --> leaf3
    leaf1 ===> leaf2
    leaf2 ===> leaf3
    classDef internal fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Disk Block: Root Node] --> B{Key in range?}
    B -- Less than K1 --> C[Disk Block: Child Pointer P0]
    B -- Between K1 and K2 --> D[Disk Block: Child Pointer P1]
    B -- Greater than K2 --> E[Disk Block: Child Pointer P2]
    C --> F{Leaf Reached?}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -- Yes B+ Tree --> G[Sequential Leaf Scan]
    F -- Yes B-Tree --> H[Check Internal Nodes for Key]
    G --> I[Return Records / Data]
    H --> I
    classDef disk fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    class A,C,D,E disk
    class B,F decision
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Insertion Flow (Splitting Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Insert Key K] --> search[Search for Leaf Position]
    search --> checkfull{Leaf Full?}
    checkfull -- No --> directins[Insert Sorted in Leaf]
    checkfull -- Yes --> split[Split Leaf: Middle Key Promoted/Copied]
    split --> checkparent{Parent Full?}
    checkparent -- No --> updparent[Update Parent Pointer]
    checkparent -- Yes --> cascadesplit[Cascade Split Upward]
    cascadesplit --> checkroot{Root Split?}
    checkroot -- Yes --> newroot[Create New Root, Height Increases]
    checkroot -- No --> updparent
    directins --> done[Done]
    updparent --> done
    newroot --> done
    classDef process fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef decision fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    class start,search,split,cascadesplit,newroot process
    class checkfull,checkparent,checkroot decision
```

### 4.5 Visual Comparison: B-Tree vs B+ Tree Node Anatomy

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BTREE["B-Tree Node: Keys AND Data Together"]
        k1[K1 with Data]:::ktype
        k2[K2 with Data]:::ktype
        k3[K3 with Data]:::ktype
    end
    subgraph BPTREE["B+ Tree Internal Node: Keys ONLY for Routing"]
        rk1[K1 Routing]:::rtype
        rk2[K2 Routing]:::rtype
        rk3[K3 Routing]:::rtype
    end
    subgraph BPLEAF["B+ Tree Leaf Node: Full Data Records"]
        lk1[Data K1]:::ltype
        lk2[Data K2]:::ltype
        lk3[Data K3]:::ltype
        lk4[Data K4]:::ltype
    end
    classDef ktype fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rtype fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef ltype fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** Notice how the B+ Tree internal nodes are **"slimmer"** — they contain only routing keys, no data payloads. This means a single disk block can hold **many more routing keys**, resulting in a **higher fan-out** and a **shorter, more I/O-efficient tree**. The trade-off is that every search must descend to the leaf, but this is a *constant-time penalty* that is dwarfed by the savings in tree height.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Define a B-Tree of order $m$. List any four properties of a B-Tree of order 5.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** A B-Tree of order $m$ is a self-balancing, height-balanced multi-way search tree in which every node can contain a maximum of $m$ children and $m-1$ keys, with all leaf nodes appearing at the same level, designed to minimize disk I/O operations for large-scale data access.

> **Properties of B-Tree of order 5 (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. Every internal node (except root) has at least $\lceil 5/2 \rceil = 3$ children and at most $5$ children.
> 2. The root has at least $2$ children (if non-leaf) and at most $5$ children.
> 3. A node with $k$ children contains exactly $k-1$ keys.
> 4. All leaf nodes appear at the **same level** (perfectly balanced).
> 5. The keys within a node are stored in **sorted increasing order**.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**Distinguish between a B-Tree and a B+ Tree. Mention any three differences.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Sl. No. | B-Tree | B+ Tree |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Data records can be stored in **both internal nodes and leaf nodes**. | Data records are stored **only in leaf nodes**; internal nodes contain only routing keys. |
| 2 | Leaf nodes are **not linked** to each other. | Leaf nodes are **linked sequentially** via sibling pointers, enabling fast range queries. |
| 3 | On leaf split, the middle key is **promoted** (removed) to the parent. | On leaf split, the middle key is **copied** (retained) to the parent. |
| 4 | Search may terminate at any internal level. | Search always reaches the leaf level (uniform cost). |
| 5 | Less efficient for range queries. | Highly efficient for range queries via sequential leaf scan. |

> **Mnemonic:** *"B-Tree Buries keys in branches; B+ Tree keeps them only at the Bottom."*

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**A.** **(a)** Construct a **B-Tree of order 5** by inserting the following keys in the given order: **$3, 14, 7, 1, 8, 17, 11, 24, 25, 4, 5, 9, 10, 12, 20$**. Show the tree structure after every split. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Delete the keys **$17$ and $24$** from the resulting B-Tree. Show the tree structure after each deletion, clearly indicating rotation or merging operations. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution for Part A(a):**

**Order 5:** Max children = 5, Max keys = 4, Min keys (non-root) = 2, Min children (non-root) = 3.

We track the tree state after each insert that causes a structural change.

| Step | Inserted Key | Action | Resulting Tree (textual) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | Direct insert into empty root | `[3]` |
| 2 | 14 | Space available | `[3, 14]` |
| 3 | 7 | Space available | `[3, 7, 14]` |
| 4 | 1 | Space available | `[1, 3, 7, 14]` |
| 5 | 8 | **Overflow**: Split. Middle key `7` promoted. New root `[7]`. Left child `[1, 3]`, right child `[8, 14]`. | Root `[7]`; leaves `[1, 3]` and `[8, 14]` |
| 6 | 17 | $17 > 7 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[8, 14]`. Insert sorted. | Leaf `[8, 14, 17]` |
| 7 | 11 | $11 > 7 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[8, 14, 17]`. Insert sorted. | Leaf `[8, 11, 14, 17]` (full) |
| 8 | 24 | $24 > 7 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[8, 11, 14, 17]`. **Overflow**: Split. Middle key `14` promoted. Parent root `[7]` becomes `[7, 14]`. Left leaf `[8, 11]`, right leaf `[17, 24]`. | Root `[7, 14]`; leaves `[1, 3]`, `[8, 11]`, `[17, 24]` |
| 9 | 25 | $25 > 14 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[17, 24]`. Insert. | Leaf `[17, 24, 25]` |
| 10 | 4 | $4 < 7 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[1, 3]`. Insert. | Leaf `[1, 3, 4]` |
| 11 | 5 | $5 < 7 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[1, 3, 4]`. Insert. | Leaf `[1, 3, 4, 5]` (full) |
| 12 | 9 | $7 \leq 9 \leq 14 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[8, 11]`. Insert. | Leaf `[8, 9, 11]` |
| 13 | 10 | $7 \leq 10 \leq 14 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[8, 9, 11]`. Insert. | Leaf `[8, 9, 10, 11]` (full) |
| 14 | 12 | Descend to `[8, 9, 10, 11]`. **Overflow**: Split. Middle key `10` promoted. Parent `[7, 14]` becomes `[7, 10, 14]`. Leaves become `[8, 9]` and `[11, 12]`. | Root `[7, 10, 14]`; children `[1, 3, 4, 5]`, `[8, 9]`, `[11, 12]`, `[17, 24, 25]` |
| 15 | 20 | $20 > 14 \Rightarrow$ descend to `[17, 24, 25]`. Insert. | Leaf `[17, 20, 24, 25]` (full) |

**Final B-Tree after all 15 insertions:**

$$
\begin{array}{c} [7, 10, 14] \\ / \quad \vert \quad \vert \quad \backslash \\ [1, 3, 4, 5] \quad [8, 9] \quad [11, 12] \quad [17, 20, 24, 25] \end{array}
$$

##### **Model Solution for Part A(b): Deletion of 17 and 24**

**Starting from the final tree above:**

**Delete 17:**
- Locate 17 in leaf `[17, 20, 24, 25]`.
- 17 is in a leaf, so simply remove it.
- Leaf becomes `[20, 24, 25]`. This still satisfies the min-keys constraint ($\lceil 5/2 \rceil - 1 = 2$ keys minimum; we have 3).
- No restructuring needed.

**Intermediate tree after deleting 17:**

$$
\begin{array}{c} [7, 10, 14] \\ / \quad \vert \quad \vert \quad \backslash \\ [1, 3, 4, 5] \quad [8, 9] \quad [11, 12] \quad [20, 24, 25] \end{array}
$$

**Delete 24:**
- Locate 24 in leaf `[20, 24, 25]`. Remove it.
- Leaf becomes `[20, 25]`. This is exactly at the **minimum threshold** of 2 keys. Still valid.
- No restructuring needed.

**Final tree after deleting both keys:**

$$
\begin{array}{c} [7, 10, 14] \\ / \quad \vert \quad \vert \quad \backslash \\ [1, 3, 4, 5] \quad [8, 9] \quad [11, 12] \quad [20, 25] \end{array}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to Examiner:** In this specific instance, the deletions did not require rotation or merging because the leaf still retained the minimum number of keys. If the question had used a different deletion sequence (e.g., deleting 20 after 17), then a **merge** would have been necessary because the leaf would fall below 2 keys. Award **full 7 marks** for the part (a) sequence and **partial 3 marks** for part (b) if the student correctly identifies that no restructuring is required and **full marks** if they also describe what *would* happen in a cascade scenario.

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted]**

**B.** **(a)** Explain the **structure of a B+ Tree of order 4** with a neat diagram. Discuss how **insertion** is carried out in a B+ Tree with an example of inserting keys: **$10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90$**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **maximum number of keys** that can be indexed by a B+ Tree of order 5 with height 3 (root at level 0)? Derive the formula. Also, find the **minimum number of keys** that the same tree can hold. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution for Part B(a):**

**B+ Tree of Order 4 — Structure (2 Marks):**
- Max children per internal node = 4; Max keys per internal node = 3.
- Min children (non-root internal) = $\lceil 4/2 \rceil = 2$; Min keys (non-root) = 2.
- Root must have at least 2 children if not a leaf.
- All data records are stored **exclusively in leaf nodes**.
- Leaf nodes are **linked sequentially** via sibling pointers.
- Internal nodes contain only **routing keys** that direct the search to the correct child.

**Insertion Sequence (5 Marks):**

**Order 4:** Max 3 keys per node, Max 4 children.

| Step | Key | Action | Tree State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | Direct insert, root = leaf | Leaf `[10]` |
| 2 | 20 | Insert in leaf | Leaf `[10, 20]` |
| 3 | 30 | Insert in leaf (now full) | Leaf `[10, 20, 30]` |
| 4 | 40 | **Leaf split**. Middle key `20` **copied up** to new root. Left leaf `[10]`, right leaf `[20, 30, 40]`. | Root `[20]`; leaves `[10]`, `[20, 30, 40]` |
| 5 | 50 | $50 > 20 \Rightarrow$ right leaf. Insert. | Right leaf `[20, 30, 40, 50]` (full) |
| 6 | 60 | Right leaf full → **Split**. Middle key `40` copied up. Parent `[20]` becomes `[20, 40]`. Leaves: `[20, 30]` and `[40, 50, 60]`. | Root `[20, 40]`; leaves `[10]`, `[20, 30]`, `[40, 50, 60]` |
| 7 | 70 | $70 > 40 \Rightarrow$ rightmost leaf. Insert. | Rightmost leaf `[40, 50, 60, 70]` (full) |
| 8 | 80 | Rightmost leaf full → **Split**. Middle key `60` copied up. Parent `[20, 40]` becomes `[20, 40, 60]` (full at 3 keys). Leaves: `[40, 50]` and `[60, 70, 80]`. | Root `[20, 40, 60]`; leaves `[10]`, `[20, 30]`, `[40, 50]`, `[60, 70, 80]` |
| 9 | 90 | $90 > 60 \Rightarrow$ rightmost leaf. Insert. | Rightmost leaf `[60, 70, 80, 90]` (full) |

**Final B+ Tree (Order 4):**

$$
\begin{array}{c} [20, 40, 60] \\ \diagdown \quad \diagdown \quad \diagdown \quad \diagdown \\ [10] \rightarrow [20, 30] \rightarrow [40, 50] \rightarrow [60, 70, 80, 90] \end{array}
$$

##### **Model Solution for Part B(b): Maximum and Minimum Keys**

**Given:** B+ Tree of order $m = 5$, height $h = 3$ (root at level 0, leaves at level 3).

**Maximum Keys Formula Derivation (4 Marks):**

When the tree is **fully packed** with maximum fan-out at every level:

- **Level 0 (Root):** 1 node, each with max $m - 1 = 4$ keys $\Rightarrow$ contributes $1 \cdot 4 = 4$ keys.
- **Level 1:** Max $m = 5$ children of root, each with max $4$ keys $\Rightarrow 5 \cdot 4 = 20$ keys.
- **Level 2 (Leaves):** Max $m^2 = 25$ leaf nodes. Each leaf can hold at most $m - 1 = 4$ keys (assuming leaf capacity = internal node capacity) $\Rightarrow 25 \cdot 4 = 100$ keys.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max Keys} &= \sum_{i=0}^{h-1} m^{i} \cdot (m-1) \\
&= (m-1) \cdot \left( m^{0} + m^{1} + m^{2} \right) \\
&= (m-1) \cdot \frac{m^{h} - 1}{m - 1} \\
&= m^{h} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $m = 5$ and $h = 3$:

$$\text{Max Keys} = 5^{3} - 1 = 125 - 1 = \mathbf{124 \text{ keys}}$$

**Minimum Keys Formula Derivation (3 Marks):**

When the tree is **as sparse as possible** while still maintaining the B+ Tree invariants:

- **Root:** Minimum 1 key (since root is allowed to have as few as 1 key, i.e., 2 children).
- **Level 1:** Each node must have at least $\lceil m/2 \rceil = 3$ children. The root has 2 children (minimum), so level 1 has 2 nodes, each with min 2 keys $\Rightarrow 2 \cdot 2 = 4$ keys.
- **Level 2 (Leaves):** Each internal node has 3 children, so total leaves = $2 \cdot 3 = 6$ leaves. Each leaf must have at least $\lceil m/2 \rceil - 1 = 2$ keys.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min Keys} &= 1 + 2 \cdot 2 + 6 \cdot 2 \\
&= 1 + 4 + 12 \\
&= \mathbf{17 \text{ keys}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "order $m$" vs "minimum degree $t$":** Some textbooks define B-Tree of minimum degree $t$ (with max $2t$ children), while KTU often uses order $m$ (max $m$ children). Students lose **2-3 marks** by mixing these conventions. **Always state your convention** at the start of your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the leaf-link in B+ Tree diagrams:** Examiners deduct **1 mark** if sibling pointers are omitted in the final B+ Tree drawing.
> 3. **Promotion vs. Copy confusion:** In B-Tree, middle key is **promoted (moved up)**. In B+ Tree, middle key is **copied up (retained in leaf)**. This is a **favourite 2-mark question** and a common error zone.
> 4. **Missing the underflow logic in deletion:** Students often only show "removal" but forget to show the **rotation or merge** when the node falls below minimum occupancy. This costs **up to 3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 5. **Wrong level counting for height:** If the question says "height 3", clarify whether the root is at level 0 or level 1. KTU convention: **root at level 0**, leaves at level $h$. Mixing this up invalidates the entire numerical derivation.
> 6. **Forgetting to show the tree after EACH split in insertion sequences:** KTU expects a tree diagram after every split operation, not just the final tree. Missing intermediate trees costs **2-3 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

#### Core Definitions
- ☐ **B-Tree of order $m$:** Multi-way self-balancing search tree; max $m$ children, max $m-1$ keys per node; all leaves at same level.
- ☐ **B+ Tree of order $m$:** Variant of B-Tree; data only in leaves; leaves linked sequentially; internal nodes hold only routing keys.

#### Critical Properties (B-Tree)
- ☐ Max keys per node = $m - 1$
- ☐ Min keys per non-root node = $\lceil m/2 \rceil - 1$
- ☐ Root min keys = $1$ (min children = $2$ if non-leaf)
- ☐ Keys in internal node: $k$ children $\Leftrightarrow$ $k-1$ keys

#### Critical Properties (B+ Tree)
- ☐ Internal node keys are **copies** used for routing only
- ☐ Leaf splits → middle key **copied** to parent (not promoted)
- ☐ Leaf node order: typically $L$ (different from internal order $m$)
- ☐ Sibling pointers between leaves enable **$O(\log N + k)$** range queries

#### Operation Time Complexities
- ☐ Search: $O(\log_m N)$
- ☐ Insert: $O(\log_m N)$ — includes possible cascading splits
- ☐ Delete: $O(\log_m N)$ — includes possible cascading merges/rotations
- ☐ Range Query (B+ Tree only): $O(\log_m N + k)$ where $k$ is the result size

#### Master Formulas
- ☐ Max keys in tree of order $m$ and height $h$: $\boxed{m^{h} - 1}$
- ☐ Min keys in tree of order $m$ and height $h$: $2 \cdot \lceil m/2 \rceil^{h-1} - 1$
- ☐ Disk I/O for B+ Tree search: exactly $h$ (root read + descend to leaf)
- ☐ Disk I/O for B-Tree search: at most $h$

#### Real-World Use Cases
- ☐ **RDBMS Indexes:** MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server
- ☐ **NoSQL Stores:** MongoDB WiredTiger, Cassandra
- ☐ **File Systems:** NTFS, ext4, HFS+, APFS
- ☐ **Information Retrieval:** Inverted indexes in search engines

#### Examiner Pet Topics (High Probability)
- ☐ Insertion sequence with **splitting cascade** up to root (height increase)
- ☐ Deletion with **rotation/borrow** from sibling
- ☐ **Max/min keys numerical** for given order and height
- ☐ **Comparison table** between B-Tree and B+ Tree (favourite 3-marker)
- ☐ **Range query efficiency** argument (why B+ Tree wins)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transaction States and Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transaction States and Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Transaction

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Database Management Systems (PERUT748)**, a **transaction** is formally defined as a **logical unit of database processing** that includes one or more database access operations (read/write), bounded by clearly demarcated boundary statements.

A transaction is a sequence of operations performed on a database that transforms it from one **consistent state** to another consistent state, while preserving the integrity of the data.

The standard ISO/IEC SQL boundary statements that demarcate a transaction are:

- `BEGIN TRANSACTION` (or `START TRANSACTION`) — Marks the beginning.
- `COMMIT` — Marks the successful end.
- `ROLLBACK` (or `ABORT`) — Marks the unsuccessful end.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> A transaction is a unit of program execution that accesses and possibly updates various data items. It is bounded by begin and end statements. The transaction must execute atomically — either all its operations complete successfully (commit) or none of them have any effect (abort/rollback).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The ATM Cash Withdrawal Analogy**

Imagine you walk up to an **ATM (Automated Teller Machine)** to withdraw ₹5,000. The bank has to perform several sub-operations to fulfil this single request:

1. **Verify PIN** (Read operation on customer record)
2. **Check account balance** (Read operation on balance field)
3. **Deduct ₹5,000 from account balance** (Write operation on balance field)
4. **Dispense cash from the machine** (Hardware/External operation)
5. **Update the transaction log** (Write operation on log file)
6. **Print receipt** (Output operation)

Now consider this scenario: What if **Step 3 succeeds** (₹5,000 is deducted from your account) but **Step 4 fails** (the ATM runs out of cash)? You have lost money from your account without actually receiving it. This is a **disaster** — the database is now in an **inconsistent state**.

To prevent this, the entire sequence must be treated as a **single atomic unit**:

- Either **all 6 steps succeed** → `COMMIT` (the transaction is permanent).
- Or **any step fails** → `ROLLBACK` (the entire sequence is undone, as if it never happened).

This atomic bundle of operations is precisely what a **database transaction** represents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A transaction is the database equivalent of a "**do-everything-or-do-nothing**" promise. It is the safety net that prevents partial updates from corrupting the database.

---

## 1.3 The Six States of a Transaction (High-Level Preview)

According to the standard KTU/Elmasri-Navathe model, every transaction passes through a well-defined **state lifecycle**. The six canonical states are:

| # | State Name | Status | Meaning |
|---|------------|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Active** | In-progress | Transaction is currently executing its operations. |
| 2 | **Partially Committed** | Final phase | All operations executed; awaiting final commit confirmation. |
| 3 | **Committed** | Successful | Transaction completed successfully; changes are permanent. |
| 4 | **Failed** | Discovered failure | Normal execution can no longer proceed. |
| 5 | **Aborted** | Rolled back | Transaction has been rolled back; DB restored to pre-transaction state. |
| 6 | **Terminated** | Final cleanup | Transaction has ended (either committed or aborted) and resources released. |

These states are the foundation of the **Transaction Recovery Subsystem**, which the KTU syllabus covers extensively under Module 4.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The distinction between **Partially Committed** and **Committed** is one of the most frequently tested concepts. Examiners often ask: *"At which state is the transaction considered truly permanent?"* The answer is **Committed**, not Partially Committed.

---

## 1.4 Transaction Operations (High-Level Preview)

A transaction is composed of primitive operations that act on database items. The two core types of operations are:

- **READ_ITEM(X)** — Reads the value of database item `X` from the database into a local program variable.
- **WRITE_ITEM(X)** — Writes the value of program variable `X` back into the database item `X`.

In addition to these data operations, transactions have **control operations**:
- `COMMIT` — Make all changes permanent.
- `ROLLBACK` / `ABORT` — Undo all changes.
- `SAVEPOINT` — Mark an intermediate point within a transaction for partial rollback.
- `BEGIN_TRANSACTION` — Start a new transaction.

These are explored in depth in Section 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete Transaction State Lifecycle

Every transaction undergoes a deterministic sequence of state transitions. The state transition model is a **deterministic finite automaton (DFA)** as far as the legal state paths are concerned.

### 2.1.1 State 1: ACTIVE State

- **Definition:** This is the **initial state** of every transaction, immediately after the `BEGIN_TRANSACTION` statement is issued.
- **Behaviour:** The transaction is actively reading and writing database items.
- **Possible Transitions:**
  - → **Partially Committed** (when the final statement of the transaction is executed)
  - → **Failed** (when a normal execution can no longer proceed)

```text
ACTIVE STATE CHARACTERISTICS:
─────────────────────────────────────────
• Transaction is executing its operations
• Buffer modifications are held in volatile memory
• Other transactions may read UNCOMMITTED data (if isolation is weak)
• Locks may be held on data items
• Write-ahead log entries are being generated
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Active" matters:** In the Active state, all changes are **tentative**. The database on disk has not yet been modified permanently. Changes exist only in the **main memory buffer** of the DBMS.

---

### 2.1.2 State 2: PARTIALLY COMMITTED State

- **Definition:** Reached immediately after the **final statement** of the transaction has been executed, but **before** the `COMMIT` operation has completed its physical log-writing.
- **Behaviour:** The DBMS is in the process of writing the transaction's log records to **stable storage** (disk) to ensure recoverability.
- **Possible Transitions:**
  - → **Committed** (when the COMMIT operation successfully writes all log records to stable storage)
  - → **Failed** (if a system crash occurs during the commit process, before log records are fully written)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:**
> The **Partially Committed** state is a **transient state**. It exists only for the brief duration required to flush all log records from the volatile log buffer to the non-volatile (stable) storage. A crash during this window leaves the transaction in an ambiguous state, which is why ARIES-style recovery algorithms use **redo/undo logs** to handle this.

---

### 2.1.3 State 3: COMMITTED State

- **Definition:** Reached after the transaction has successfully completed all its operations AND all log records have been physically written to stable storage.
- **Behaviour:** The transaction's effects are now **permanent** and **durable** on the database.
- **Possible Transitions:**
  - → **Terminated** (after the system releases all resources and locks held by the transaction)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "D" in ACID:** The **Committed** state is the embodiment of the **Durability** property of ACID. Once a transaction reaches this state, its changes MUST survive any subsequent system crash.

---

### 2.1.4 State 4: FAILED State

- **Definition:** Reached when the system detects that **normal execution of the transaction can no longer proceed**.
- **Common Triggers for Failure:**
  - Logical error (e.g., division by zero, constraint violation)
  - System error (e.g., deadlock detected, timeout)
  - Arithmetic overflow
  - Insufficient resources
- **Possible Transitions:**
  - → **Aborted** (the transaction is rolled back; the database is restored to its state before the transaction began)

---

### 2.1.5 State 5: ABORTED State

- **Definition:** Reached after the transaction has been **rolled back**. All changes made by the transaction have been **undone** using the system log.
- **Behaviour:** The database is restored to its **consistent state** as it existed before the transaction started.
- **Possible Transitions:**
  - → **Terminated** (after rollback is complete and the system releases all held resources)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note on Restart:** In some systems, after aborting, the transaction may be **restarted** automatically by the system (a process called **transaction restart**). However, from the perspective of the state diagram, the original transaction instance has terminated.

---

### 2.1.6 State 6: TERMINATED State

- **Definition:** The **final state** of a transaction. The transaction has either committed (success) or aborted (failure), and all resources (locks, memory buffers) have been released.
- **Behaviour:** The transaction leaves the system. No further transitions are possible.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Transaction Operations

### 2.2.1 READ_ITEM(X) Operation

The READ operation fetches the value of a database item `X` from the database into a local program variable.

**Formal Steps:**

1. Find the **address** of the disk block containing item `X`.
2. Copy that disk block into a **memory buffer** (if not already present in the buffer).
3. Copy the value of `X` from the buffer into the **program variable** declared in the transaction.

**Notation:**

$$
\text{READ\_ITEM}(X) \rightarrow \text{local\_var} := X_{\text{value}}
$$

**Conceptual Justification:** Reading a single field does not require reading the entire file; only the relevant block is brought into the buffer. Multiple reads of the same item within the same transaction will be served from the buffer (with possible cache invalidation in concurrent settings).

---

### 2.2.2 WRITE_ITEM(X) Operation

The WRITE operation sets the value of database item `X` in the database to a new value, supplied by the program variable.

**Formal Steps:**

1. Find the **address** of the disk block containing item `X`.
2. Copy that disk block into a **memory buffer** (if not already present).
3. **Modify the value** of `X` in the buffer to the new value.
4. **Defer writing** the buffer back to disk until the transaction commits (or until the buffer is needed for another item).

**Notation:**

$$
\text{WRITE\_ITEM}(X) \leftarrow \text{local\_var} := X_{\text{new\_value}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defer-Write Optimisation:** The DBMS does NOT write modified buffers back to disk immediately. It is far more efficient to batch these updates and flush them to disk either at commit time or when the buffer is needed for a different block. This is governed by the **Steal / No-Steal** and **Force / No-Force** policies covered in recovery modules.

---

### 2.2.3 COMMIT Operation

**COMMIT** signals the **successful end** of a transaction. It must make all updates of the transaction **permanent** in the database.

**What happens during COMMIT:**

1. All updates made by the transaction are **flushed to stable storage** (or a commit log record is written, depending on the policy).
2. A `[COMMIT, T]` log record is written to **stable storage** before any other actions.
3. The transaction enters the **Committed** state, then **Terminated** state.
4. All **locks** held by the transaction are released.

> [!NOTE]
> **Write-Ahead Log (WAL) Rule:** Before any data block is written to the database on stable storage, the corresponding log record must already have been written to stable storage. This guarantees recoverability.

---

### 2.2.4 ROLLBACK (ABORT) Operation

**ROLLBACK** signals the **unsuccessful end** of a transaction. All updates made by the transaction must be **undone**, and the database must be restored to its state before the transaction began.

**What happens during ROLLBACK:**

1. The system consults the **transaction log** in reverse chronological order.
2. For each write made by the transaction, the **old value** (before-image / UNDO log) is restored.
3. A `[ABORT, T]` log record is appended to the log on stable storage.
4. The transaction enters the **Aborted** state, then **Terminated** state.
5. All **locks** held by the transaction are released.

---

### 2.2.5 SAVEPOINT Operation

A **savepoint** is a named marker within a transaction that allows **partial rollback**.

**Usage in SQL:**

```sql
SAVEPOINT sp1;
UPDATE account SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 'A';
-- If something goes wrong here, we can rollback ONLY to sp1
ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT sp1;
-- The transaction is still active; we can continue
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Savepoints do not end the transaction. After `ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT`, the transaction remains **Active**, allowing further operations.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The following tables use `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### Table 2.3.A: Transaction State Transitions Summary

| Current State | Trigger Event | Next State |
|---------------|---------------|------------|
| **(none)** | `BEGIN_TRANSACTION` issued | **Active** |
| Active | Final statement executed | Partially Committed |
| Active | Logical/System error detected | Failed |
| Partially Committed | All log records written to stable storage | Committed |
| Partially Committed | System crash before log flush completes | Failed |
| Committed | Resources released by system | Terminated |
| Failed | System performs ROLLBACK | Aborted |
| Aborted | System releases resources | Terminated |

### Table 2.3.B: ACID Properties Reference

| Property | Full Name | Guarantee Provided |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|
| **A** | **Atomicity** | All-or-nothing execution |
| **C** | **Consistency** | DB moves from one valid state to another |
| **I** | **Isolation** | Concurrent transactions appear serial |
| **D** | **Durability** | Committed changes survive crashes |

### Table 2.3.C: Operations Summary

| Operation | Symbol | Effect on DB |
|-----------|--------|--------------|
| `READ_ITEM(X)` | $R_i(X)$ | Reads $X$ into local variable |
| `WRITE_ITEM(X)` | $W_i(X)$ | Writes new value to $X$ in buffer |
| `COMMIT` | $C_i$ | Permanent; log flushed |
| `ROLLBACK` | $A_i$ | Undo all updates |
| `SAVEPOINT` | $S_i$ | Named checkpoint for partial rollback |

### Table 2.3.D: Buffer & Stable Storage Metrics

| Metric | Definition | Typical Value |
|--------|------------|----------------|
| $t_B$ | Time to read/write one disk block | $\mathbf{10^{-3}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-2}}$ seconds |
| $t_{commit}$ | Time to commit (log flush) | Depends on $t_B$ count |
| $N_{log}$ | Number of log blocks to flush at commit | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for $n$ write operations |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The transaction model underpins virtually every mission-critical data system in the world:

- **Banking & Financial Systems:** Core banking, stock trading platforms, payment gateways (UPI, SWIFT) all rely on ACID transactions to prevent money duplication or loss.
- **E-Commerce:** Order processing, inventory updates, and payment capture must be atomic.
- **Healthcare Systems:** Patient records, prescription updates, and insurance claims require absolute data integrity.
- **Aviation & Reservation Systems:** Airline, hotel, and railway reservation systems depend on transactions to prevent overbooking.
- **Telecommunications:** Call Detail Record (CDR) processing and billing aggregation use transaction semantics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Big Tech Note:** Modern distributed systems (Google Spanner, CockroachDB) have extended the classical transaction model to **distributed transactions** using protocols like **Two-Phase Commit (2PC)** and **Paxos Commit**, but the foundational state and operations remain identical to what is taught in this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: State × Operation

The following matrix analyses the legality and effect of each operation at each state of a transaction.

| State \ Operation | READ | WRITE | COMMIT | ROLLBACK | SAVEPOINT |
|------------------|------|-------|--------|----------|-----------|
| **Active** | ✓ Legal | ✓ Legal | ✓ Legal (early commit) | ✓ Legal (rollback to start) | ✓ Legal |
| **Partially Committed** | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal | ✓ Completes commit | ✗ Illegal (transient state) | ✗ Illegal |
| **Committed** | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal (already committed) | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal |
| **Failed** | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal (must abort) | ✓ Legal (system initiates) | ✗ Illegal |
| **Aborted** | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal (already aborted) | ✗ Illegal |
| **Terminated** | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal | ✗ Illegal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners often present a scenario like *"A transaction is in the Partially Committed state. Can it issue a ROLLBACK?"* The correct answer is **NO** — the Partially Committed state is transient and awaits either successful log flush (→ Committed) or system crash (→ Failed). User-issued operations are not permitted in this state.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: The Log-Based Commit Protocol

The COMMIT operation is the most safety-critical operation in transaction processing. Its protocol is derived from the requirement that **no committed transaction's effects may be lost** (Durability) and **no aborted transaction's effects may remain** (Atomicity).

### Step 1: Trace of a Transaction

Consider transaction $T_i$ with the following operations:

$$
T_i : \text{read}(A), \text{write}(A), \text{read}(B), \text{write}(B), \text{commit}
$$

### Step 2: The Log Record Format

For each write operation, a log record of the form is generated:

$$
[T_i, \text{ID}, \text{old\_value}, \text{new\_value}]
$$

where ID is the data item being written.

### Step 3: The Commit Protocol

**Step 3.1:** When the transaction issues `COMMIT`, the DBMS first **forces** all log records belonging to $T_i$ from the volatile log buffer to the **stable log storage**. This is called the **force-write at commit** step.

$$
\text{flush}(\text{LogBuffer}) \rightarrow \text{StableStorage}
$$

**Step 3.2:** A special commit log record $[T_i, \text{COMMIT}]$ is appended to the log in stable storage.

$$
\text{append}([T_i, \text{COMMIT}]) \rightarrow \text{StableLog}
$$

**Step 3.3:** The transaction state transitions to **Committed**, and the commit log record is the **point of finality**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Order Matters:** The COMMIT log record must be the **last** log record written for that transaction in stable storage. This guarantees that the moment we see a COMMIT record in the log, all prior log records (including write records) are also in stable storage.

### Step 4: Why This Works (The "Why" Behind WAL)

Suppose a system crash occurs **after** the COMMIT log record is written to stable storage but **before** the actual data blocks are flushed to disk.

- On **recovery**, the recovery manager reads the log.
- It finds the $[T_i, \text{COMMIT}]$ record for $T_i$.
- It performs a **REDO** of all write operations of $T_i$, reapplying them to the database.
- This restores the committed effects — Durability is preserved.

Conversely, if the crash occurs **before** the COMMIT log record is written:

- The recovery manager finds **no COMMIT record** for $T_i$.
- It performs an **UNDO** of all write operations of $T_i$, restoring the old values.
- Atomicity is preserved.

### Step 5: The Four Recovery Scenarios

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Scenario A: Crash before commit record on stable storage} &\Rightarrow \text{UNDO} \;\; T_i \\
\text{Scenario B: Crash after commit record, before data flush} &\Rightarrow \text{REDO} \;\; T_i \\
\text{Scenario C: Crash after commit record AND data flush} &\Rightarrow \text{NO ACTION} \\
\text{Scenario D: No crash, normal termination} &\Rightarrow \text{Flush data, release locks}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Question Pattern:** *"Explain how the Write-Ahead Log (WAL) protocol ensures Atomicity and Durability in the presence of system failures."* The four-scenario analysis above is the model answer framework.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough: SAVEPOINT Mechanics

Savepoints introduce a **stack-based checkpointing** mechanism within an active transaction.

### Step 1: Establish Savepoint

When the DBMS processes `SAVEPOINT sp1`:

$$
\text{SavepointStack.push}(\text{sp1}, \text{currentLogPosition})
$$

The current position in the log is recorded.

### Step 2: Execute More Operations

Subsequent operations are appended to the log:

$$
[T_i, X_1, \text{old}_1, \text{new}_1], \;\; [T_i, X_2, \text{old}_2, \text{new}_2], \;\; \ldots
$$

### Step 3: Rollback to Savepoint

When `ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT sp1` is issued:

$$
\text{currentLogPosition} = \text{sp1.logPosition}
$$

The DBMS walks the log **backwards** from the current position to the savepoint's log position, applying **UNDO** for each write operation it encounters.

### Step 4: The Savepoint Stack After Rollback

The savepoint `sp1` is **preserved** (not destroyed), but any savepoints established **after** `sp1` are **destroyed**.

$$
\text{After ROLLBACK TO sp1: sp1 exists, sp2 is destroyed}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why savepoints are preserved:** This allows the application to rollback to the same savepoint multiple times if needed. Only savepoints **after** the rollback target are removed.

### Step 5: The Transaction Remains Active

After `ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT`, the transaction is still in the **Active** state. The application can issue further operations, commit, or rollback the entire transaction.

---

## 3.4 Quantitative Analysis: Buffer Pinning and Disk I/O

The READ and WRITE operations are governed by the **buffer management** subsystem. Here is the analytical breakdown:

### Step 1: Block Location

The DBMS maintains a directory mapping data item $X$ to its disk block address:

$$
\text{BlockMap}[X] = \langle \text{BlockID}, \text{Offset} \rangle
$$

### Step 2: Buffer Pool Lookup

The DBMS checks if the block containing $X$ is already in the **buffer pool** (in main memory):

$$
\text{BufferPool.contains}(\text{BlockID}) \rightarrow \text{Boolean}
$$

### Step 3: Buffer Hit vs. Miss

**Buffer Hit (In Memory):**

$$
\text{Time} = t_{\text{memory}} \approx \mathbf{10^{-8}} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Buffer Miss (Disk Read Required):**

$$
\text{Time} = t_{\text{disk}} = t_{\text{seek}} + t_{\text{latency}} + t_{\text{transfer}} \approx \mathbf{10^{-3}} \text{ to } \mathbf{10^{-2}} \text{ seconds}
$$

The **Buffer Hit Ratio** is defined as:

$$
H = \frac{\text{Number of buffer hits}}{\text{Total number of block accesses}}
$$

The **Average Access Time** is:

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = H \cdot t_{\text{memory}} + (1 - H) \cdot t_{\text{disk}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The transaction commit protocol is designed to minimise the number of disk writes per transaction. By batching log writes and using force-at-commit, the protocol achieves $O(1)$ commits per block instead of $O(n)$ per data item.

---

## 3.5 The Transaction Schedule Notation

In the formal study of transactions, a **schedule** is a sequence of operations from one or more transactions that preserves the order of operations within each individual transaction.

Given two transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: r_1(A), \; w_1(A), \; c_1 \\
T_2 &: r_2(B), \; w_2(B), \; c_2
\end{aligned}
$$

A **valid schedule** $S$ must interleave these operations such that:

$$
\text{If } op_1 \text{ precedes } op_2 \text{ in } T_i, \text{ then } op_1 \text{ precedes } op_2 \text{ in } S
$$

For example, a valid serial schedule is:

$$
S_{\text{serial}} : r_1(A), \; w_1(A), \; c_1, \; r_2(B), \; w_2(B), \; c_2
$$

A valid non-serial (interleaved) schedule is:

$$
S_{\text{interleaved}} : r_1(A), \; r_2(B), \; w_1(A), \; w_2(B), \; c_1, \; c_2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Schedules are the foundation of **serializability theory** and **concurrency control**, which build directly on the transaction state model. The schedule notation $r_i(X)$, $w_i(X)$, $c_i$, $a_i$ for read, write, commit, and abort of transaction $T_i$ on item $X$ is a high-frequency KTU exam symbol.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transaction State Transition Diagram (Mermaid)

The following state diagram maps every legal transition in the lifecycle of a transaction. This is the **most important diagram** in Module 4 for KTU exams.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> txnActive : BEGIN_TRANSACTION
    txnActive --> txnPartialCommit : Final statement executed
    txnActive --> txnFailed : Logical or system error
    txnPartialCommit --> txnCommitted : COMMIT log written to stable storage
    txnPartialCommit --> txnFailed : System crash during commit
    txnFailed --> txnAborted : ROLLBACK executed by system
    txnAborted --> txnTerminated : Resources released
    txnCommitted --> txnTerminated : Resources released
    txnTerminated --> [*] : Transaction ended
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - The `[*]` symbol denotes the entry/exit point.
> - `txnActive` is the **only** state from which a user transaction can issue further operations.
> - `txnPartialCommit` is a **transient** state that exists for the log-flush duration.
> - The path `txnActive → txnFailed → txnAborted → txnTerminated` is the **failure path**.
> - The path `txnActive → txnPartialCommit → txnCommitted → txnTerminated` is the **success path**.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Transaction Processing

The following diagram maps the interaction between the **Transaction Manager**, **Log Manager**, **Buffer Manager**, and **Recovery Manager** during a typical transaction lifecycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Application] --> B[Transaction Manager TM]
    B --> C[Log Manager LM]
    B --> D[Buffer Manager BM]
    D --> E[Stable Storage Disk]
    C --> E
    B --> F[Lock Manager]
    B --> G[Recovery Manager RM]
    G --> E
    G --> D
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#f4d03f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#bb8fce,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Explanation:**
> - **Transaction Manager (TM):** Coordinates the begin, commit, and rollback of transactions.
> - **Log Manager (LM):** Maintains the write-ahead log on stable storage.
> - **Buffer Manager (BM):** Manages the in-memory buffer pool where modified data resides.
> - **Lock Manager:** Handles concurrency control (covered later in the module).
> - **Recovery Manager (RM):** On crash recovery, uses the log to perform REDO/UNDO operations.

---

## 4.3 Operation Sequence Diagram (Mermaid)

This sequence diagram shows the chronological interaction of operations during a successful transaction.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User App
    participant TM as Transaction Manager
    participant LM as Log Manager
    participant BM as Buffer Manager
    participant DB as Stable Database
    U->>TM: BEGIN_TRANSACTION
    TM->>U: Transaction started (ACTIVE state)
    U->>BM: READ_ITEM(A)
    BM->>DB: Fetch block containing A
    DB-->>BM: Block returned
    BM-->>U: Value of A
    U->>BM: WRITE_ITEM(A) := A - 100
    BM-->>U: Buffer updated
    Note over U,LM: Write log entry [T, A, old, new]
    LM->>DB: Flush log record (WAL)
    U->>TM: COMMIT
    TM->>LM: Force-write all log records
    LM->>DB: Write COMMIT record
    LM-->>TM: Commit acknowledged
    TM-->>U: Transaction COMMITTED
    U->>TM: BEGIN_TRANSACTION or End
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Sequence:** Note the critical **WAL (Write-Ahead Log)** step where the log record is flushed to stable storage **before** any data block modification. This is the cornerstone of the recovery mechanism.

---

## 4.4 Failure Path Diagram (Mermaid)

This flowchart illustrates what happens when a transaction fails midway.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[ACTIVE Transaction] --> Op1[Execute Read Write operations]
    Op1 --> Check{Error detected?}
    Check -- No --> Continue[Continue execution]
    Continue --> Op1
    Check -- Yes --> Failed[Transition to FAILED state]
    Failed --> Rollback[System initiates ROLLBACK]
    Rollback --> Undo[UNDO all writes using log]
    Undo --> Aborted[Transition to ABORTED state]
    Aborted --> Release[Release all locks and buffers]
    Release --> Terminated[Transition to TERMINATED state]
    Terminated --> End[Transaction ended]
    style Start fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Failed fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Aborted fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Terminated fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style End fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern:** Part A questions are short-answer type. They test direct conceptual recall. Each carries **3 marks**. Time allocation: ~5 minutes per question.

---

### Question 5.1.A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a transaction in the context of a Database Management System. List the various states a transaction can be in.**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A transaction is a **logical unit of database processing** that consists of a sequence of database operations (such as read, write, update, or delete) executed as a single, indivisible unit of work. It is bounded by the statements `BEGIN_TRANSACTION` and either `COMMIT` (for successful completion) or `ROLLBACK` (for failure). The transaction transforms the database from one consistent state to another.

**States (1 mark):**
The various states of a transaction are:

1. **Active**
2. **Partially Committed**
3. **Committed**
4. **Failed**
5. **Aborted**
6. **Terminated**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students confuse "Partially Committed" with "Committed." These are distinct. "Partially Committed" is the transient state after the last operation but before the commit log is fully written to stable storage. "Committed" is the state after the log write is confirmed. **Losing 1 mark** for this confusion is very common.

---

### Question 5.1.A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the difference between the COMMIT and ROLLBACK operations in a transaction. What is the role of the system log in supporting these operations?**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**COMMIT (1.5 marks):**
- The `COMMIT` operation signals the **successful end** of a transaction.
- It makes all updates of the transaction **permanent** in the database.
- A `[COMMIT, T]` log record is written to stable storage.
- After commit, the transaction cannot be undone by the system.

**ROLLBACK (1 mark):**
- The `ROLLBACK` (or `ABORT`) operation signals the **unsuccessful end** of a transaction.
- It **undoes** all updates made by the transaction, restoring the database to its state before the transaction began.
- An `[ABORT, T]` log record is written to the log.

**Role of System Log (0.5 mark):**
The system log records every read/write operation of every transaction. During rollback, the log provides the **old values (before-images)** needed to undo the changes. This is the foundation of the **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** protocol.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to mention that the system log must be on **stable storage** (non-volatile disk), not on volatile memory. Stating only "the system log" without specifying "stable storage" may result in **partial marks deduction** in strict valuation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is worth **14 marks** with an internal choice (you answer either Choice A or Choice B). Sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically **7 marks each**. Provide a clear, structured answer with diagrams where applicable.

---

### Part B Question 5.2.A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question A: With the help of a state transition diagram, explain the various states a transaction goes through during its execution. Also, explain the role of the system log in ensuring the atomicity and durability of transactions.** **(14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): Transaction State Diagram and State Explanations **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A transaction in a DBMS goes through a sequence of well-defined states during its lifetime. The state transition diagram is as follows:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> stateActive : BEGIN_TRANSACTION
    stateActive --> statePartial : Final statement executed
    stateActive --> stateFailed : Logical or system error
    statePartial --> stateCommitted : Log flushed to stable storage
    statePartial --> stateFailed : System crash during commit
    stateFailed --> stateAborted : System rollback
    stateAborted --> stateTerminated : Resources released
    stateCommitted --> stateTerminated : Resources released
    stateTerminated --> [*] : Transaction ended
```

**Explanation of Each State:**

1. **Active State:** The transaction begins here. All read/write operations are executed. The transaction holds locks and maintains a local copy of values in memory.

2. **Partially Committed State:** Reached after the final operation is executed. The DBMS is in the process of writing the log records to stable storage.

3. **Committed State:** All log records have been written successfully. Changes are now permanent and durable.

4. **Failed State:** Normal execution can no longer continue due to a logical error (e.g., division by zero) or a system error (e.g., deadlock).

5. **Aborted State:** The transaction has been rolled back. All changes are undone using the log, and the database is restored to its pre-transaction state.

6. **Terminated State:** The transaction has ended (either committed or aborted). All resources (locks, memory buffers) have been released.

#### Part (b): Role of System Log in Atomicity and Durability **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **system log** is a critical component of the DBMS that records every operation performed by every transaction. It is stored on **stable storage (non-volatile disk)** and follows the **Write-Ahead Log (WAL) protocol**, which states: *Before any data block is modified on stable storage, the corresponding log record must first be written to stable storage.*

**Role in Atomicity:**
- When a transaction needs to be rolled back (e.g., due to a failure), the DBMS consults the log in **reverse chronological order**.
- For each write operation, the log provides the **old value (before-image)**.
