- The system restores these old values, effectively **undoing** all the transaction's changes.
- This guarantees that either **all** of a transaction's operations take effect, or **none** of them do — the all-or-nothing property of **Atomicity**.

**Role in Durability:**
- When a transaction issues `COMMIT`, the DBMS first writes all log records for that transaction to stable storage.
- A special `[COMMIT, T]` log record is the **last** record written.
- Even if a system crash occurs after the COMMIT log record is on disk but before data blocks are flushed, the **Recovery Manager** can use the log to perform a **REDO** of the transaction's writes during restart.
- This ensures that the effects of committed transactions are **never lost** — the **Durability** property.

**Summary of Log-Based Recovery:**

| Scenario | Log State | Recovery Action |
|----------|-----------|------------------|
| Crash before COMMIT log on disk | No COMMIT record | **UNDO** the transaction |
| Crash after COMMIT log, before data flush | COMMIT record present | **REDO** the transaction |
| No crash | Normal flow | Flush data, release locks |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Two common mistakes:
> 1. Drawing the state diagram **without arrows** or **with incorrect transitions** (e.g., showing rollback from Partially Committed — this is **illegal**).
> 2. Failing to mention the **stable storage** requirement for the log. Saying "the log is on disk" is acceptable; saying "the log is in memory" is **not**.
> 3. Mixing up UNDO and REDO: UNDO is for transactions **without** a COMMIT log record; REDO is for transactions **with** a COMMIT log record.

---

### Part B Question 5.2.B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question B: Describe the various operations of a transaction in detail. With suitable examples, explain the use of SAVEPOINT in transaction management. Discuss how the READ_ITEM and WRITE_ITEM operations interact with the system buffer.** **(14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): Transaction Operations in Detail **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A transaction in a DBMS is composed of a set of primitive operations. These can be classified into **data access operations** and **control operations**.

**1. READ_ITEM(X) Operation:**

This operation reads the value of a database item `X` from the database into a program variable.

**Steps:**
- The DBMS locates the disk block address of item `X$ using its internal directory.
- The corresponding block is copied into a memory buffer (if not already in the buffer pool).
- The value of $X` is then copied from the buffer into the local program variable declared in the transaction.

**Example:**

$$
\text{READ\_ITEM}(\text{Balance}) \rightarrow \text{local\_var} := \text{Balance}
$$

**2. WRITE_ITEM(X) Operation:**

This operation writes a new value to a database item `X`.

**Steps:**
- The block containing $X` is brought into the memory buffer (if not already present).
- The value of $X` in the buffer is updated to the new value.
- The actual disk write is **deferred** until the transaction commits (or until the buffer is needed for another item).

**Example:**

$$
\text{WRITE\_ITEM}(\text{Balance}) \leftarrow \text{local\_var} - 1000
$$

**3. COMMIT Operation:**

Signals successful termination. The DBMS forces all log records to stable storage, writes a `[COMMIT, T]` record, and releases all locks held by the transaction.

**4. ROLLBACK (ABORT) Operation:**

Signals unsuccessful termination. The DBMS uses the log to **undo** all writes of the transaction, restoring old values, and then releases resources.

**5. SAVEPOINT Operation:**

A named marker within a transaction that allows **partial rollback**. The transaction remains **Active** after rolling back to a savepoint.

#### Part (b): SAVEPOINT Use Cases and Buffer Interaction **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**SAVEPOINT in Practice:**

A savepoint is a logical checkpoint within a transaction. The SQL syntax is:

```sql
BEGIN TRANSACTION;
INSERT INTO orders (id, amount) VALUES (101, 5000);
SAVEPOINT sp1;
UPDATE inventory SET stock = stock - 1 WHERE product = 'X';
-- Suppose this fails due to a constraint
ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT sp1;
-- The inventory update is undone, but the order insertion remains
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 5000 WHERE customer = 'C1';
COMMIT;
```

**Why Savepoints Are Useful:**

1. **Error Recovery Within Transactions:** Without savepoints, any error in a long transaction would force a complete rollback, losing all the work done so far.

2. **Conditional Logic in Programs:** Application code can catch exceptions, rollback to a savepoint, and try an alternative approach — all within the same transaction.

3. **Nested Logical Sub-Transactions:** Savepoints can simulate lightweight sub-transactions without the overhead of actual nested transactions.

**Interaction of READ/WRITE with the System Buffer:**

The buffer pool is the **interface** between the transaction operations and the stable database on disk.

- **READ_ITEM:** The buffer pool is consulted first. If the block is **in the buffer (buffer hit)**, the read is served from memory. If **not in the buffer (buffer miss)**, the block is read from disk, brought into the buffer, and then served to the transaction.

- **WRITE_ITEM:** The write is performed **in the buffer**, not directly on disk. The disk write is **deferred** and is governed by the DBMS's buffer replacement policy (e.g., LRU, Clock).

- **At COMMIT:** All modified buffer blocks (also called **dirty pages**) are scheduled for disk write, and the COMMIT log record is flushed to stable storage **before** any data block is written.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Confusing **savepoints with sub-transactions**: Savepoints are **not** independent transactions. The transaction remains in the **Active** state after `ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT`.
> 2. Saying "WRITE_ITEM writes to disk": **Incorrect.** The write happens in the buffer. The disk write is deferred and is controlled by the buffer manager.
> 3. Forgetting to mention that after `ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT`, the **savepoint itself is preserved** (only later savepoints are destroyed).

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam!**

### ✅ Definitional Mastery
- [ ] A **transaction** is a logical unit of database processing bounded by `BEGIN_TRANSACTION`, `COMMIT`, and `ROLLBACK`.
- [ ] The **six states** are: Active, Partially Committed, Committed, Failed, Aborted, Terminated.
- [ ] **Active** is the only state from which user operations are issued.
- [ ] **Partially Committed** is a transient state during log flush; user operations are NOT permitted.
- [ ] **Committed** is reached only after the COMMIT log record is written to **stable storage**.
- [ ] **Terminated** is the final state from which the transaction leaves the system.

### ✅ Operational Mastery
- [ ] `READ_ITEM(X)` copies a database value into a local program variable.
- [ ] `WRITE_ITEM(X)` updates a value in the **buffer** (NOT directly on disk).
- [ ] `COMMIT` makes changes permanent via WAL protocol.
- [ ] `ROLLBACK` undoes all changes using the log's before-images.
- [ ] `SAVEPOINT` allows **partial rollback** within an active transaction.
- [ ] After `ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT`, the transaction is **still active**.

### ✅ Recovery Mastery
- [ ] **WAL (Write-Ahead Log):** Log records must be on stable storage **before** data blocks.
- [ ] **UNDO** is performed for transactions **without** a COMMIT log record.
- [ ] **REDO** is performed for transactions **with** a COMMIT log record but unflushed data.
- [ ] The **Recovery Manager** is responsible for executing UNDO/REDO on system restart.

### ✅ Common KTU Exam Traps
- [ ] ❌ Treating "Partially Committed" and "Committed" as the same — they are NOT.
- [ ] ❌ Allowing user operations in the "Partially Committed" state — **illegal**.
- [ ] ❌ Saying WRITE_ITEM writes to disk — it writes to the **buffer**.
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting to mention **stable storage** for the log.
- [ ] ❌ Confusing UNDO and REDO operations.

### ✅ High-Frequency Symbols
- $T_i$ — Transaction number $i$
- $R_i(X)$ or $r_i(X)$ — Read of item $X$ by transaction $T_i$
- $W_i(X)$ or $w_i(X)$ — Write of item $X$ by transaction $T_i$
- $C_i$ or $c_i$ — Commit of transaction $T_i$
- $A_i$ or $a_i$ — Abort of transaction $T_i$
- $S_i$ — Savepoint within transaction $T_i$

### ✅ The Golden Rule
> **"A transaction is the atomic bundle of database operations. The system log is its guardian, the buffer pool is its workshop, and the stable storage is its final destination."**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ACID Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** ACID Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Database Management Systems (DBMS), a **transaction** is a logical unit of work that accesses and possibly updates various data items in a database. A transaction is a sequence of one or more SQL operations (such as `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`, or `SELECT`) that are treated as a single atomic unit.

**ACID Properties** are the four foundational guarantees that any reliable transaction processing system MUST provide to ensure that database transactions are processed in a robust, error-free manner, even in the face of system failures, concurrent access, or power losses.

The acronym **ACID** stands for:

- **A** — **Atomicity**
- **C** — **Consistency**
- **I** — **Isolation**
- **D** — **Durability**

These properties were formally conceptualized by **Andreas Reuter** and **Theo Härder** in their landmark **1983 paper** titled *"Principles of Transaction-Oriented Database Recovery"*. They are the cornerstone of the *transaction model* prescribed by the KTU 2024 DBMS syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every transaction must end in one of two states — either it **commits** (all changes are permanently saved) or it **aborts** (all changes are rolled back). There is no intermediate "half-done" state permitted. This is the essence of the ACID contract.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Bank Money Transfer**

Imagine you transfer **₹5,000** from your **Savings Account (A/c: 1001)** to your friend's **Current Account (A/c: 1002)** at a bank.

This single logical operation ("transfer money") actually involves **two physical database operations**:
1. **DEBIT** ₹5,000 from A/c 1001.
2. **CREDIT** ₹5,000 to A/c 1002.

Now, suppose a power failure occurs *after* the debit but *before* the credit. Without ACID guarantees, ₹5,000 would simply **vanish** into thin air — a catastrophic data loss!

**How ACID Saves the Day:**
- **Atomicity** ensures that either *both* the debit and credit happen, or *neither* happens. The transaction is "all or nothing."
- **Consistency** ensures that the total bank balance (sum of all accounts) remains ₹5,000 (neither created nor destroyed).
- **Isolation** ensures that if 1,000 customers are transferring money simultaneously, your transfer doesn't get entangled with theirs (no double-spending, no lost updates).
- **Durability** ensures that once the bank confirms the transfer, even an earthquake cannot undo it — the change is permanent.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars at a Glance

| Pillar | Tagline | Question It Answers |
|---|---|---|
| **Atomicity** | "All or Nothing" | *What if a system crash occurs mid-transaction?* |
| **Consistency** | "Valid State to Valid State" | *Are all data integrity rules preserved?* |
| **Isolation** | "As If Alone" | *What if multiple transactions run concurrently?* |
| **Durability** | "Once Committed, Forever" | *What if the system crashes after commit?* |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Atomicity — The "All or Nothing" Property

**Definition (Formal):** A transaction is treated as a single, indivisible logical unit of work. Either *all* of its operations are executed and committed, or *none* of them are executed. There is no possibility of a partial execution.

**How It Works Internally:**
- The DBMS uses a **Transaction Manager** and a **Recovery Manager** to enforce atomicity.
- Before any modification, the original (pre-transaction) values are written to a special file called the **log file** (specifically, the *before-image* or `BEFORE` log record).
- If the transaction commits → the changes are made permanent.
- If the transaction aborts/fails → the Recovery Manager performs a **ROLLBACK** by restoring the original values from the log (a process called *undo*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Atomicity is implemented using the **Transaction-Recovery Subsystem** via the **Log-Based Recovery** mechanism (Write-Ahead Log / WAL protocol). Always mention the role of the log file in atomicity answers.

### 2.2 Consistency — The "Valid State to Valid State" Property

**Definition (Formal):** A transaction must transform the database from one **consistent state** to another **consistent state**. Consistency is preserved if the transaction does not violate any of the *integrity constraints* defined on the database schema.

**The Three Layers of Consistency in KTU Scope:**
1. **Internal Consistency:** Maintained by the DBMS automatically (e.g., primary key uniqueness, foreign key constraints, check constraints, NOT NULL).
2. **External Consistency:** Maintained by the application programmer's business logic (e.g., "Account balance can never be negative").
3. **Logical Consistency:** Maintained by the developer through triggers and stored procedures.

**Example Constraint:** A transaction `T` updates an employee's salary. If the constraint is `CHECK (salary > 0)` and the new salary violates this, the transaction is **rejected/aborted**, and the database remains in its previous consistent state.

### 2.3 Isolation — The "As If Alone" Property

**Definition (Formal):** Even though multiple transactions may execute concurrently in a real system, the result must appear as if all transactions were executed *one after another* in some serial order. Concurrent transactions must not interfere with each other.

**The "Phantom" Problem (Why Isolation is Hard):**
When two transactions access the same data item:
- **Lost Update Problem** (Dirty Write)
- **Uncommitted Dependency Problem** (Dirty Read)
- **Inconsistent Analysis Problem** (Non-repeatable Read)
- **Phantom Read Problem**

These are handled by **Concurrency Control Protocols** such as:
- **Lock-Based Protocols** (Two-Phase Locking / 2PL)
- **Timestamp Ordering (TSO)**
- **Validation-Based Protocols (Optimistic Concurrency)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Connection:** The *Isolation* property is precisely what is "tuned" by the SQL standard's `SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL` command, which has four levels: `READ UNCOMMITTED`, `READ COMMITTED`, `REPEATABLE READ`, and `SERIALIZABLE`.

### 2.4 Durability — The "Once Committed, Forever" Property

**Definition (Formal):** After a transaction successfully commits, all the changes it has made to the database must persist permanently, even in the event of system failure, power loss, or crash.

**How It Works Internally:**
- When a transaction issues a `COMMIT` command, the DBMS forces a **commit record** to be written to the log file (typically using `fsync()` to flush the log to non-volatile disk storage).
- Only after this forced write succeeds is the transaction considered truly committed.
- If the system crashes *after* commit, the Recovery Manager uses a **REDO** operation during restart to reapply the changes from the log (a process called *redo* / *replay*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Atomicity failure** → Undo the uncommitted changes (ROLLBACK).
> - **Durability failure** → Redo the committed changes (RECOVERY).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Guarantee | Implementation Mechanism | Failure Recovery Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Atomicity** | All-or-Nothing execution | Transaction Manager + Log File (`BEFORE` images) | `UNDO` (Rollback) |
| **Consistency** | Valid State → Valid State | Integrity Constraints + Triggers | Transaction Abort |
| **Isolation** | Concurrent = Serial | Locking (2PL), Timestamps, Validation | Conflict Resolution |
| **Durability** | Committed changes persist | Log File (`AFTER` images) + Disk `fsync()` | `REDO` (Replay) |

**Notation Used in KTU Exam Answers:**

$$T = \{T_1, T_2, T_3, \ldots, T_n\}$$

$$\text{Schedule } S = \text{Interleaved execution of } T_i \text{ operations}$$

$$\text{ACID}_{Total} = f(A, C, I, D) \quad \text{where each property is independently enforced}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** ACID properties are the bedrock of mission-critical systems such as **banking ledgers, airline reservation systems, stock trading platforms, hospital patient records, and e-commerce payment gateways**. Without ACID guarantees, these systems would be vulnerable to data corruption, financial loss, and legal liability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Formal Mathematical Modeling of a Transaction

A transaction $T_i$ is modeled as a sequence of read and write operations on database data items. Let $D = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_m\}$ be the set of all data items.

**Step 1 — Define the basic operation set:**

$$\text{ops}(T_i) = \{ \text{read}(D_j), \text{write}(D_j) \mid D_j \in D \}$$

This means every operation in transaction $T_i$ is either a `read` (denoted `R`) or `write` (denoted `W`) on some data item $D_j$.

**Step 2 — Define a transaction as an ordered sequence:**

$$T_i = \langle op_1^{(i)}, op_2^{(i)}, \ldots, op_k^{(i)} \rangle$$

The angle brackets denote the strict execution order within transaction $T_i$. The subscript $k$ represents the total number of operations in the transaction.

**Step 3 — Define a Schedule $S$ as an interleaved execution:**

$$S = \langle op_1^{(i_1)}, op_2^{(i_2)}, \ldots, op_n^{(i_n)} \rangle$$

where each $i_j$ identifies which transaction issued the $j$-th operation in the global schedule.

**Step 4 — Define the transaction's terminal state:**

$$\text{End}(T_i) \in \{ \text{COMMIT}_i, \text{ABORT}_i \}$$

A transaction must terminate in exactly one of these two atomic states.

### 3.2 Formal Definition of Each ACID Property (Step-by-Step)

#### **A. Atomicity (Step-by-Step Formalization)**

A transaction $T_i$ with operations $op_1, op_2, \ldots, op_n$ is atomic if and only if:

$$\text{If } \forall op_j \in T_i \text{ succeeds} \implies T_i \text{ COMMITS} \land \Delta(D) \text{ is permanent}$$

$$\text{If } \exists op_j \in T_i \text{ fails} \implies T_i \text{ ABORTS} \land \Delta(D) = \emptyset$$

**Textual Explanation:**
- **Row 1:** If every single operation $op_j$ within transaction $T_i$ executes successfully, then $T_i$ reaches the `COMMIT` state, and the database change $\Delta(D)$ is permanently applied.
- **Row 2:** If *even one* operation $op_j$ within $T_i$ fails for any reason, then $T_i$ must transition to the `ABORT` state, and the net change to the database $\Delta(D)$ must be the **empty set** — meaning no effect whatsoever.

#### **B. Consistency (Step-by-Step Formalization)**

Let $\Sigma$ be the set of all integrity constraints on the database. The database state is consistent if:

$$\text{State}(D) \models \Sigma$$

This notation means the state "satisfies" or "models" all integrity constraints.

A transaction $T_i$ is consistency-preserving if and only if:

$$\text{If State}(D_{initial}) \models \Sigma \text{ and } T_i \text{ COMMITS} \implies \text{State}(D_{final}) \models \Sigma$$

**Textual Explanation:** If the database begins in a consistent state (satisfies all rules in $\Sigma$) and the transaction commits, then the final state must *also* satisfy all rules. The database is never allowed to "pass through" an inconsistent state that is visible to other transactions.

#### **C. Isolation (Step-by-Step Formalization)**

A schedule $S$ is *serializable* if its effect is equivalent to some serial schedule $S_{serial}$. A schedule $S$ is *conflict-serializable* if its precedence graph $P(S)$ is acyclic.

The conflict-serializability condition is:

$$\text{Conflict-Serializable}(S) \iff P(S) \text{ is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)}$$

**Textual Explanation:** Two operations on the same data item conflict if they belong to different transactions, at least one is a `write`, and their order is swapped. The schedule preserves isolation if we can prove (via the precedence graph) that some serial order exists that produces the same result.

#### **D. Durability (Step-by-Step Formalization)**

A transaction $T_i$ is durable if and only if:

$$T_i \text{ COMMITS} \implies \forall \text{ system crashes } C \text{ after } \text{COMMIT} \implies \Delta(D) \text{ survives } C$$

**Textual Explanation:** Once the `COMMIT` record is flushed to non-volatile storage, *no* subsequent system failure (power loss, hardware fault, software crash) can undo the changes. The `REDO` recovery phase re-applies the changes if necessary.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ACID vs. BASE (Conceptual Contrast)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Concept:** Modern distributed databases (like NoSQL systems) often relax ACID in favor of **BASE** (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency). The KTU syllabus asks students to *contrast* these two models.

| Parameter | ACID Model | BASE Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency |
| **Approach** | Pessimistic (strict, conservative) | Optimistic (flexible, performance-oriented) |
| **Consistency** | Strong (immediate) | Eventual (delayed convergence) |
| **Transaction Scope** | Typically single-node | Often distributed / multi-node |
| **Example Systems** | PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, Oracle DB | Cassandra, DynamoDB, MongoDB |
| **Use Case** | Banking, finance, inventory | Social media feeds, IoT, big data analytics |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Bank Transfer (Mathematical Walkthrough)

Consider the classic transfer scenario as a formal transaction:

$$T_1: \text{Transfer ₹5,000 from Account A to Account B}$$

**Step 1 — Read initial state:**

$$R_1(A): \text{balance}_A = \text{balance}_A - 5000$$

**Step 2 — Write to Account A:**

$$W_1(A): \text{balance}_A \leftarrow \text{balance}_A - 5000$$

**Step 3 — Write to Account B:**

$$W_1(B): \text{balance}_B \leftarrow \text{balance}_B + 5000$$

**Step 4 — Verify ACID enforcement:**

$$\text{Atomicity: } W_1(A) \text{ and } W_1(B) \text{ both commit, or both roll back}$$

$$\text{Consistency: } \text{balance}_A + \text{balance}_B = \text{constant (conservation of money)}$$

$$\text{Isolation: } \text{Concurrent transfer } T_2 \text{ does not interleave to lose updates}$$

$$\text{Durability: } \text{After } \text{COMMIT}_1, \text{ values persist across system crash}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Transaction State Transition

The following diagram shows the complete lifecycle of a transaction, including the role of atomicity in handling failures.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active : begin_transaction
    Active --> Active : READ or WRITE operation
    Active --> PartiallyCommitted : final WRITE issued
    PartiallyCommitted --> Committed : log force-written, COMMIT success
    PartiallyCommitted --> Failed : log write failure
    Active --> Failed : error or abort requested
    Failed --> Aborted : ROLLBACK executed, UNDO applied
    Committed --> [*] : ACID satisfied
    Aborted --> [*] : state restored
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- The transaction begins in the **Active** state when `begin_transaction` is called.
- It performs read/write operations, transitioning from `Active` to `Active`.
- When the final write is issued, it enters the **Partially Committed** state.
- The log record is force-written to disk, and if successful, it transitions to **Committed** (Durability enforced).
- If the log write fails or an abort is requested, it transitions to **Failed**, then to **Aborted** (Atomicity enforced via `UNDO`).

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: ACID Property Enforcement Architecture

This block diagram illustrates how the four ACID properties are enforced by different DBMS subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application issues Transaction] --> B{Transaction Manager}
    B --> C[Atomicity Subsystem]
    B --> D[Consistency Subsystem]
    B --> E[Isolation Subsystem]
    B --> F[Durability Subsystem]
    C --> G[Log File BEFORE Images]
    C --> H[UNDO on Failure]
    D --> I[Integrity Constraint Checker]
    D --> J[Trigger Evaluator]
    E --> K[Lock Manager 2PL]
    E --> L[Timestamp Ordering]
    F --> M[Log File AFTER Images]
    F --> N[Disk fsync and REDO on Recovery]
    G --> O[Non Volatile Disk Storage]
    H --> O
    I --> P[Transaction Approval]
    J --> P
    K --> Q[Concurrency Control]
    L --> Q
    M --> R[Committed State Persisted]
    N --> R
    P --> S[Transaction Commits]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    S --> T[ACID Guarantees Delivered]
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- The **Transaction Manager** dispatches each operation to the relevant subsystem.
- The **Atomicity Subsystem** uses `BEFORE` images to perform `UNDO` on failure.
- The **Consistency Subsystem** validates integrity constraints and triggers.
- The **Isolation Subsystem** uses locks or timestamps for concurrency control.
- The **Durability Subsystem** uses `AFTER` images and `fsync` to ensure persistence.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Two-Phase Locking for Isolation (KTU High-Yield)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T1 as Transaction T1
    participant LM as Lock Manager
    participant DB as Database
    participant T2 as Transaction T2
    T1->>LM: LOCK_X item A (Growing Phase)
    LM-->>T1: GRANTED
    T1->>DB: WRITE A
    T2->>LM: LOCK_X item A (Conflict Detected)
    LM-->>T2: BLOCKED, queue T2
    T1->>DB: READ B
    T1->>LM: UNLOCK A, UNLOCK B (Shrinking Phase)
    LM-->>T2: RESUMED
    T2->>LM: LOCK_X item A (Growing Phase)
    LM-->>T2: GRANTED
    T2->>DB: WRITE A
    T2->>LM: UNLOCK A (Shrinking Phase)
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- **Growing Phase:** Transaction acquires locks but cannot release any.
- **Shrinking Phase:** Transaction releases locks but cannot acquire new ones.
- This strict two-phase discipline is what enforces the **Isolation** property at the lock level.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Define ACID properties. Why are they essential in transaction processing?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

ACID stands for **Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability**. These are the four key properties that guarantee database transactions are processed reliably.

- **Atomicity:** Ensures that a transaction is executed as an indivisible unit. Either all operations succeed or none do.
- **Consistency:** Ensures that the database moves from one valid state to another, preserving all integrity constraints.
- **Isolation:** Ensures that concurrent transactions execute as if they were running serially, without interference.
- **Durability:** Ensures that once a transaction commits, its changes are permanent, surviving any system failure.

They are essential to prevent data loss, corruption, and inconsistency in mission-critical systems like banking and inventory.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing four properties, 1 mark for stating their importance.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Distinguish between Atomicity and Durability properties with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Atomicity | Durability |
|---|---|---|
| **When it acts** | During transaction execution / failure mid-way | After the transaction commits |
| **Failure Type** | Handles transaction abort / system crash *before* commit | Handles system crash *after* commit |
| **Recovery Action** | `UNDO` (Rollback) using `BEFORE` images | `REDO` (Replay) using `AFTER` images |
| **Mechanism** | Log-based recovery + transaction manager | Log force-write + disk `fsync` |

**Example:** Consider a fund transfer. If the system crashes after debiting Account A but before crediting Account B, **Atomicity** rolls back the debit. If the system crashes *after* both accounts are updated and the transaction is committed, **Durability** ensures that on restart, the `REDO` phase restores the credited amount to Account B.

*(Mark Distribution: 1.5 marks for distinction, 1.5 marks for the example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Suggested Module 4 Question]**

**(a)** Explain the four ACID properties of a database transaction in detail. Discuss how each property is implemented internally by the DBMS. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a banking system where a customer transfers ₹10,000 from Savings Account to Loan Account. Illustrate how each ACID property is preserved during this transfer. If two concurrent transactions try to update the same account balance, explain how Isolation is enforced. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**ACID Properties — Detailed Explanation:**

**1. Atomicity (2 Marks):**
- Atomicity ensures that a transaction is treated as a single, indivisible unit of work.
- The "all-or-nothing" principle: either the entire transaction is executed successfully and committed, or it is rolled back completely.
- **Implementation:** The DBMS uses a **Transaction-Recovery Subsystem** backed by a **log file**. Before modifying any data item, the original value (the `BEFORE` image) is written to the log. If the transaction must be aborted, the system performs an `UNDO` operation using these `BEFORE` images to restore the original state.
- **Mechanism:** The **Write-Ahead Log (WAL) protocol** ensures log records are written to stable storage *before* the actual data pages are modified.

**2. Consistency (1.5 Marks):**
- Consistency ensures that the database transitions from one valid state to another, preserving all integrity constraints (primary key, foreign key, check constraints).
- The DBMS automatically checks these constraints before allowing the transaction to commit.
- **Example:** If a transaction attempts to set a customer's age to a negative number (violating a `CHECK` constraint), the DBMS rejects the transaction and rolls it back.

**3. Isolation (2 Marks):**
- Isolation ensures that even when multiple transactions execute concurrently, the result is equivalent to some serial execution of those transactions.
- **Implementation:** Concurrency control mechanisms like **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)**, **Timestamp Ordering**, or **Validation-Based Protocols** are used.
- In 2PL, transactions acquire locks in a *growing phase* and release them in a *shrinking phase*, preventing conflicts.

**4. Durability (1.5 Marks):**
- Durability guarantees that once a transaction commits, its effects are permanent and survive any subsequent system failure.
- **Implementation:** The DBMS force-writes the commit record and `AFTER` images to the log on non-volatile storage (e.g., via `fsync()`).
- On system restart, the `REDO` phase replays all committed transactions that may not have been flushed to the actual database files.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Banking System ACID Walkthrough:**

**Step 1 — Transaction Definition:**

$$T_1: \text{Transfer ₹10,000 from Savings (S) to Loan (L)}$$

$$T_1 = \langle R(S), S = S - 10000, W(S), R(L), L = L + 10000, W(L), \text{COMMIT} \rangle$$

**Step 2 — Atomicity Enforcement (1.5 Marks):**
- Suppose the system crashes after $W(S)$ but before $W(L)$.
- The DBMS Recovery Manager detects the partial state.
- It executes `UNDO` on $W(S)$ using the `BEFORE` image, restoring $S$ to its original value.
- The transaction is marked as `ABORTED`. The customer is neither debited nor credited (all-or-nothing).

**Step 3 — Consistency Enforcement (1 Mark):**
- The bank's integrity constraint is $S + L = \text{constant (total money in customer's accounts)}$.
- The transaction satisfies this: $(S - 10000) + (L + 10000) = S + L$. The sum is invariant.

**Step 4 — Durability Enforcement (1 Mark):**
- After successful `COMMIT`, the system force-writes the log to disk.
- Even if a power failure occurs immediately after, the new balances are restored via `REDO` on restart.

**Step 5 — Isolation Enforcement (2.5 Marks):**

Suppose a concurrent transaction $T_2$ is also updating the same Savings Account $S$:

$$T_2: \text{Deposit ₹2,000 into Savings (S)}$$

The interleaved schedule without isolation would be:

$$S = \langle W_1(S), W_2(S), W_1(L) \rangle$$

This is the **Lost Update Problem** — $T_1$'s debit is overwritten by $T_2$'s deposit calculation.

**How Isolation Fixes It (Using 2PL):**
- $T_1$ acquires an **exclusive lock** on $S$ before writing: `LOCK_X(S)`.
- $T_2$ requests the same lock, but it is **blocked** and placed in the lock queue.
- $T_1$ writes to $S$, then to $L$, then issues `COMMIT`, and finally **releases** the lock.
- $T_2$ is now **resumed**, acquires the lock, and proceeds safely.
- The effective serial order is $T_1 \to T_2$, satisfying the Isolation property.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Atomicity with Durability.** Atomicity deals with *failure during* a transaction; Durability deals with *failure after* a transaction commits. Many students incorrectly swap these.
> 2. **Mentioning log-based recovery** (Write-Ahead Log) is mandatory for full marks in Atomicity and Durability answers.
> 3. **Isolation answers must reference concurrency control mechanisms** (locks, timestamps) — simply stating "transactions run one after another" is incomplete and loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Consistency is not just "data is correct."** You must explicitly name the integrity constraints (primary key, foreign key, check) being preserved.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Alternate]**

**(a)** What are the different states through which a transaction passes during its lifecycle? Draw the transaction state diagram and explain each state transition. How does the state diagram relate to the Atomicity property? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the ACID model with the BASE model. Under what circumstances would a system designer prefer BASE over ACID? Provide one real-world example for each model. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Transaction States — A transaction can be in one of five states:**

1. **Active State (1 Mark):** The initial state when the transaction begins. It remains here while executing read/write operations.
2. **Partially Committed State (1 Mark):** The final statement has been executed, but the commit operation has not yet been confirmed.
3. **Committed State (1 Mark):** The transaction has successfully completed, and all changes are permanently recorded in the database.
4. **Failed State (1 Mark):** The transaction cannot proceed due to a logical error, system crash, or abort request.
5. **Aborted State (1 Mark):** The transaction has been rolled back, and the database is restored to its state prior to the start of the transaction.

**State Transition Diagram (1 Mark):**

The diagram below summarizes the transitions:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    Active --> Active : read or write
    Active --> PartiallyCommitted : final statement executed
    PartiallyCommitted --> Committed : commit successful
    PartiallyCommitted --> Failed : log write failure
    Active --> Failed : error or abort
    Failed --> Aborted : rollback completed
    Committed --> [*]
    Aborted --> [*]
```

**Relation to Atomicity (2 Marks):**
- The transitions `Active → Failed → Aborted` and `Partially Committed → Failed → Aborted` embody the **Atomicity** property.
- If a failure is detected at any stage, the system ensures that the transaction does not leave a "partial effect" on the database.
- The `UNDO` operation during the `Aborted` state guarantees the all-or-nothing semantics.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**ACID vs. BASE Comparison (5 Marks):**

| Parameter | ACID | BASE |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency |
| **Consistency Model** | Strong, immediate consistency | Weak, eventual consistency |
| **Transaction Style** | Pessimistic, locking-based | Optimistic, conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs) |
| **Latency** | Higher (due to locks and synchronization) | Lower (no locks, parallel writes) |
| **Partition Tolerance** | Sacrificed in CAP trade-off | Prioritized |
| **Data Partitioning** | Difficult to scale horizontally | Designed for distributed scale-out |
| **Use Case** | Financial ledgers, inventory | Social media, IoT, global-scale web apps |

**When to Prefer BASE (1 Mark):**
A system designer should prefer BASE when:
- The application can tolerate **eventual consistency** (e.g., social media likes, view counts).
- The system must scale across **multiple geographic regions** with high availability requirements.
- The cost of distributed locking in ACID outweighs the benefit of strict consistency.

**Real-World Examples (1 Mark):**
- **ACID Example:** **Banking core ledger** (e.g., Oracle DB, PostgreSQL in banks) — strict consistency is non-negotiable.
- **BASE Example:** **Amazon's shopping cart** or **Facebook's news feed** — these can tolerate slight delays in consistency for massive scale and availability.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **ACID** = **A**tomicity, **C**onsistency, **I**solation, **D**urability.
- [ ] **Atomicity** → All or Nothing → Handled by `UNDO` using `BEFORE` log images.
- [ ] **Consistency** → Valid State to Valid State → Enforced by integrity constraints (PK, FK, CHECK).
- [ ] **Isolation** → As If Serial → Enforced by 2PL, Timestamps, or Validation.
- [ ] **Durability** → Permanent After Commit → Handled by `REDO` using `AFTER` log images + `fsync`.
- [ ] **Atomicity fails** → `UNDO` (Rollback). **Durability fails** → `REDO` (Replay). Don't swap these!
- [ ] **Transaction States:** Active → Partially Committed → Committed / Failed → Aborted.
- [ ] **Write-Ahead Log (WAL) protocol** is the standard implementation for both Atomicity and Durability.
- [ ] **Isolation Levels (SQL):** READ UNCOMMITTED < READ COMMITTED < REPEATABLE READ < SERIALIZABLE.
- [ ] **CAP Theorem Connection:** ACID prioritizes **Consistency**; BASE prioritizes **Availability** in distributed systems.
- [ ] **2PL has two phases:** Growing (acquire locks only) and Shrinking (release locks only). No overlap allowed.
- [ ] **Serializability** is the gold standard for Isolation — the schedule must be conflict-equivalent to some serial schedule.
- [ ] **KTU Board Trick:** If a question mentions "system crash after commit," the answer keyword is **Durability / REDO**.
- [ ] **KTU Board Trick:** If a question mentions "system crash during transaction," the answer keyword is **Atomicity / UNDO**.
- [ ] **Remember the names:** Andreas Reuter and Theo Härder (1983) formally defined ACID in their paper.
- [ ] **Log records are written BEFORE data is modified** — this is the essence of the Write-Ahead Log protocol.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Serial Schedules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Serial Schedules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Serial Schedule (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **schedule** is a sequence of operations (reads, writes, commits, aborts) belonging to one or more concurrent transactions, preserving the order of operations *within* each individual transaction. A schedule is called a **Serial Schedule** when the operations of all transactions are executed one after another, **without any interleaving**. In other words, each transaction runs to completion *before* the next transaction begins.

Mathematically, if a schedule $S$ contains operations from $n$ transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$, then $S$ is serial if and only if all operations of $T_1$ appear consecutively, followed by all operations of $T_2$, and so on, without overlap.

For a system with $n$ transactions, there are exactly:

$$
\text{Number of Serial Schedules} = n!
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every serial schedule is, by definition, **serializable** (i.e., correct). Serial schedules are treated as the *ground truth* or *reference schedules* against which the correctness of concurrent (interleaved) schedules is measured using concepts like **conflict serializability** and **view serializability**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **single-lane one-way bridge** where vehicles must cross strictly one at a time.

- **Vehicle 1 (Transaction $T_1$)** enters the bridge, travels completely to the other end, and exits.
- Only **after Vehicle 1 has fully exited** does **Vehicle 2 (Transaction $T_2$)** begin to enter.
- **No two vehicles are on the bridge at the same time.**

This perfectly mirrors a serial schedule: there is **no concurrency**, and the system behaves exactly as if only one transaction existed at any moment. Although the bridge is safe and orderly, it is *inefficient* because it does not exploit the possibility of multiple parallel lanes (which is what **concurrent schedules** try to achieve).

**Another Analogy — Bank Teller with One Customer at a Time:**
A small bank has a single teller. Customer A completes the **entire** transaction (deposit + receipt + goodbye) before Customer B is even called forward. This is a serial schedule.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Transaction ($T_i$)** | A logical unit of database access consisting of read/write/commit operations. |
| **Schedule ($S$)** | An ordered sequence of operations drawn from a set of concurrent transactions. |
| **Serial Schedule** | A schedule with no interleaving of operations from different transactions. |
| **Interleaved Schedule** | A schedule in which operations of two or more transactions are mixed. |
| **Conflicting Operations** | Two operations from different transactions that access the same data item and at least one is a `write`. |
| **Serializability** | Property by which a concurrent schedule is *equivalent* to some serial schedule. |

## 1.4 Symbol Conventions Used in KTU Board Exams

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $r_i(X)$ | Transaction $T_i$ reads data item $X$ |
| $w_i(X)$ | Transaction $T_i$ writes data item $X$ |
| $c_i$ | Transaction $T_i$ commits |
| $a_i$ | Transaction $T_i$ aborts |
| $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ | All operations of $T_i$ precede all operations of $T_j$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Serial Schedules

A schedule $S$ is serial if it satisfies **all** of the following structural properties:

1. **Atomic Transaction Execution:** Each transaction appears as one indivisible block in the schedule. There is no point at which the operations of one transaction are "split" by those of another.
2. **Order Preservation Within a Transaction:** If $r_i(X)$ precedes $w_i(X)$ inside $T_i$, then this same order is preserved in the schedule $S$.
3. **No Cross-Interleaving:** For any two transactions $T_i$ and $T_j$ in $S$, either every operation of $T_i$ precedes every operation of $T_j$, or vice versa. Formally:

$$
\forall i, j \; : \; i \neq j \implies \big( \forall op \in T_i, \forall op' \in T_j \; : \; op < op' \big) \;\lor\; \big( \forall op \in T_i, \forall op' \in T_j \; : \; op' < op \big)
$$

4. **Deterministic Final State:** The final database state is unambiguously determined by the order in which transactions are executed.
5. **Free from Concurrency Anomalies:** Issues like *dirty read*, *lost update*, *non-repeatable read*, and *phantom read* **cannot occur** because no two transactions overlap.

## 2.2 Why Serial Schedules Are Always Considered Correct

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental reason serial schedules are treated as the **gold standard of correctness** is that each transaction is executed in isolation. Hence, the database passes through a sequence of valid (individually consistent) states. Concurrent schedules, by contrast, may produce invalid intermediate states due to interleaving, and must therefore be *proven* equivalent to *some* serial schedule.

## 2.3 Number of Serial Schedules — Combinatorial Analysis

For $n$ transactions, the number of distinct serial schedules equals the number of permutations:

$$
N_{\text{serial}}(n) = n!
$$

| $n$ | $n!$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 2 | $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ and $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ |
| 3 | 6 | All orderings of $T_1, T_2, T_3$ |
| 4 | 24 | Significant even for small workloads |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "the number of concurrent schedules" with "the number of serial schedules." The serial count is a tight, factorial-bounded number — easy to enumerate. Concurrent schedules grow **exponentially** with the number of operations.

## 2.4 The Problem: Equivalent Serial Schedules May Give Different Results

Even though all serial schedules are individually correct, **different serial orders can lead to different final database states**. Consider two transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$:

- $T_1$: Transfer ₹100 from Account A to Account B
- $T_2$: Add 5% interest to all accounts

If $T_1$ runs **before** $T_2$, Account A is debited and B is credited before interest is calculated.
If $T_2$ runs **before** $T_1$, interest is applied to the *old* balance, then the transfer happens.

The final balances **differ** between the two orders — both are "correct" in their own right, but they represent *different valid business outcomes*. This is the concept of **non-serializable but acceptable** schedules: each one is equivalent to *some* serial schedule, but **not all serial schedules are equivalent to each other**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total serial schedules for $n$ transactions | $n!$ | All permutations of $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ |
| Is a serial schedule always serializable? | **Yes** | By definition (no interleaving → trivially conflict- and view-serializable) |
| Number of serial schedules for 2 transactions | $2! = 2$ | Either $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ or $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ |
| Number of serial schedules for 3 transactions | $3! = 6$ | $T_1T_2T_3, T_1T_3T_2, T_2T_1T_3, T_2T_3T_1, T_3T_1T_2, T_3T_2T_1$ |
| Conflicting operations condition | Two ops conflict if on same item $\land$ from different $T_i$ $\land$ at least one is a write | Three types: $r$-$w$, $w$-$r$, $w$-$w$ |
| Acyclic precedence graph condition | Schedule is conflict-serializable $\iff$ precedence graph is acyclic | Serial schedules always have acyclic precedence graphs |
| Schedule length (time steps) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mid T_i \mid$ | Sum of operations in each transaction |
| Throughput in serial execution | $T_{\text{throughput}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{\text{avg}}}$ | $t_{\text{avg}}$ = average transaction time |
| CPU utilization (serial, 1 transaction at a time) | $\le 100\%$ but with idle I/O gaps | Lower than concurrent execution |

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | Why Serial Schedules Matter |
|---|---|
| **Banking & Finance** | Audit logs often replay transactions serially to verify end-of-day balances. |
| **Blockchain** | Blocks are serial by design; each block is finalized before the next is appended. |
| **Embedded Systems** | Single-core microcontrollers execute database operations (e.g., sensor logs) serially. |
| **Replication & Recovery** | Recovery managers *apply* redo/undo logs serially in commit order. |
| **Testing DBMSs** | Serial execution is the default in **SQLite** testing mode for correctness verification. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Constructing a Serial Schedule — Step-by-Step Method

**Given:** Two transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$ operating on data items $A$ and $B$.

**Transaction Definitions:**

$$
T_1 : \quad r_1(A) \;\rightarrow\; w_1(A) \;\rightarrow\; r_1(B) \;\rightarrow\; w_1(B) \;\rightarrow\; c_1
$$

$$
T_2 : \quad r_2(A) \;\rightarrow\; w_2(A) \;\rightarrow\; c_2
$$

### Step 1: List All Operations With Their Transaction IDs

| Step | Operation | Transaction |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_1(A)$ | $T_1$ |
| 2 | $w_1(A)$ | $T_1$ |
| 3 | $r_1(B)$ | $T_1$ |
| 4 | $w_1(B)$ | $T_1$ |
| 5 | $c_1$ | $T_1$ |
| 6 | $r_2(A)$ | $T_2$ |
| 7 | $w_2(A)$ | $T_2$ |
| 8 | $c_2$ | $T_2$ |

### Step 2: Verify Serial Property

A schedule is serial if for every pair of transactions $(T_i, T_j)$, either $T_i$ ends before $T_j$ begins, or $T_j$ ends before $T_i$ begins. In the above, $T_1$ completes (via $c_1$ at step 5) before $T_2$ starts (at step 6). Therefore, this is the serial schedule:

$$
S_1 : \; T_1 \rightarrow T_2
$$

Written explicitly:

$$
S_1 : \quad r_1(A), w_1(A), r_1(B), w_1(B), c_1, r_2(A), w_2(A), c_2
$$

### Step 3: Construct the Other Serial Schedule ($S_2$)

The only other valid serial schedule swaps the order:

$$
S_2 : \quad r_2(A), w_2(A), c_2, r_1(A), w_1(A), r_1(B), w_1(B), c_1
$$

In compact form:

$$
S_2 : \; T_2 \rightarrow T_1
$$

### Step 4: Show That $S_1$ and $S_2$ May Have Different Final States

Let initial state be $A = 1000$, $B = 2000$.

**Execution of $S_1$ ($T_1$ first):**
- $r_1(A)$: read $A = 1000$
- $w_1(A)$: $A \leftarrow 1000 - 100 = 900$  *(transfer ₹100 from A to B)*
- $r_1(B)$: read $B = 2000$
- $w_1(B)$: $B \leftarrow 2000 + 100 = 2100$
- $c_1$: $T_1$ commits
- $r_2(A)$: read $A = 900$
- $w_2(A)$: $A \leftarrow 900 \times 1.05 = 945$  *(5% interest on A)*
- $c_2$: $T_2$ commits

$$
\text{Final state after } S_1: \quad A = 945, \; B = 2100
$$

**Execution of $S_2$ ($T_2$ first):**
- $r_2(A)$: read $A = 1000$
- $w_2(A)$: $A \leftarrow 1000 \times 1.05 = 1050$
- $c_2$: $T_2$ commits
- $r_1(A)$: read $A = 1050$
- $w_1(A)$: $A \leftarrow 1050 - 100 = 950$
- $r_1(B)$: read $B = 2000$
- $w_1(B)$: $B \leftarrow 2000 + 100 = 2100$
- $c_1$: $T_1$ commits

$$
\text{Final state after } S_2: \quad A = 950, \; B = 2100
$$

### Step 5: Final Analytical Comparison

$$
A_{S_1} = 945 \;\neq\; A_{S_2} = 950
$$

$$
B_{S_1} = 2100 \;=\; B_{S_2} = 2100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Both schedules are individually correct, but the final state of $A$ depends on the serial order. This illustrates the central motivation for studying **serializability**: concurrent schedules must be equivalent to **some** serial order, but the *choice* of serial order matters semantically.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Properties of Each Serial Schedule

| Property | $S_1 : T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ | $S_2 : T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ |
|---|---|---|
| Number of operations | 8 | 8 |
| Interleaving present? | **No** | **No** |
| Precedence graph edges | $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ | $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ |
| Cycle in precedence graph? | No (acyclic) | No (acyclic) |
| Conflict-serializable? | **Yes (trivially)** | **Yes (trivially)** |
| View-serializable? | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Final value of $A$ | 945 | 950 |
| Final value of $B$ | 2100 | 2100 |
| Loss-of-update possible? | **No** | **No** |
| Dirty read possible? | **No** | **No** |
| Recoverable? | **Yes** | **Yes** |

## 3.3 Precedence Graph of a Serial Schedule — Formal Construction

For the schedule $S_1 : T_1 \rightarrow T_2$, the precedence graph $G = (V, E)$ is built as follows:

**Vertices:**

$$
V = \{T_1, T_2\}
$$

**Edges:** Draw an edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ if $T_i$ executes a conflicting operation *before* $T_j$ on some data item.

Since $T_1$ writes $A$ (via $w_1(A)$) before $T_2$ reads or writes $A$, and $T_1$ writes $B$ before $T_2$ would (but $T_2$ doesn't touch $B$):

$$
E = \{ (T_1, T_2) \}
$$

The graph has only one directed edge. It is **acyclic**. Hence the schedule is conflict-serializable — as expected, because **every serial schedule is conflict-serializable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Rule (Proven):** A schedule $S$ is conflict-serializable if and only if its precedence graph $G_S$ is acyclic. Since a serial schedule's precedence graph is a *total order* (a chain) and contains no back-edges, it is always acyclic. Hence, **all serial schedules are conflict-serializable.**

## 3.4 Formal Proof Sketch: All Serial Schedules Are Conflict-Serializable

**Claim:** If $S$ is a serial schedule involving transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$, then $S$ is conflict-serializable.

**Proof Sketch (by construction):**

1. By the definition of a serial schedule, there exists a permutation $\pi$ of $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ such that:

$$
S = T_{\pi(1)} \rightarrow T_{\pi(2)} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow T_{\pi(n)}
$$

2. The precedence graph $G_S$ of $S$ has at most one edge from $T_{\pi(i)}$ to $T_{\pi(j)}$ whenever $i < j$ and they conflict. All such edges point *forward* in the ordering $\pi$.

3. A graph where all edges respect a single total order cannot contain a cycle. (A cycle would require a back-edge, contradicting the total order.)

4. By the **Conflict-Serializability Theorem**, $S$ is conflict-serializable, and is in fact equivalent to the serial schedule defined by $\pi$ itself. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timeline Diagram — Serial Schedule $S_1: T_1 \rightarrow T_2$

```mermaid
gantt
    title Serial Schedule S1 : T1 followed by T2
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section T1 Operations
    r1_A   :t1a, 0, 1s
    w1_A   :t1b, after t1a, 1s
    r1_B   :t1c, after t1b, 1s
    w1_B   :t1d, after t1c, 1s
    c1     :t1e, after t1d, 1s
    section T2 Operations
    r2_A   :t2a, after t1e, 1s
    w2_A   :t2b, after t2a, 1s
    c2     :t2c, after t2b, 1s
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that **no two operations of $T_1$ and $T_2$ overlap in time**. The block of $T_2$ starts only after $c_1$ (commit of $T_1$). This is the defining visual hallmark of a serial schedule.

## 4.2 Comparison Timeline — Both Serial Schedules for Two Transactions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Serial Schedule S1 : T1 then T2"]
        direction LR
        A1["r1_A"] --> B1["w1_A"] --> C1["r1_B"] --> D1["w1_B"] --> E1["c1"] --> F1["r2_A"] --> G1["w2_A"] --> H1["c2"]
    end

    subgraph S2["Serial Schedule S2 : T2 then T1"]
        direction LR
        A2["r2_A"] --> B2["w2_A"] --> C2["c2"] --> D2["r1_A"] --> E2["w1_A"] --> F2["r1_B"] --> G2["w1_B"] --> H2["c1"]
    end

    S1 ~~~ S2
```

## 4.3 Precedence Graph of a Serial Schedule

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1["T1"] --> T2["T2"]
```

> **Note:** A single forward edge. The graph is a **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** — more specifically, a *linear chain*. This acyclicity is the structural reason why serial schedules are conflict-serializable.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Serial Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        Q1["Transaction T1 arrives"]
        Q2["Transaction T2 arrives"]
        Q3["Transaction T3 arrives"]
    end

    subgraph SERIALIZER["Serial Scheduler"]
        DISPATCH["Dispatch Unit picks one transaction at a time"]
        EXEC["Execute T_i to completion including commit"]
        GATE["Hold Queue for waiting transactions"]
    end

    subgraph EXECUTION["Execution Engine"]
        READ["Read data items from disk buffer"]
        WRITE["Write updated items back to disk buffer"]
        LOG["Append to Write-Ahead Log"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Database State"]
        DB["Consistent DB State after T1, then T2, then T3"]
    end

    Q1 --> DISPATCH
    Q2 --> GATE
    Q3 --> GATE
    GATE --> DISPATCH
    DISPATCH --> EXEC
    EXEC --> READ
    EXEC --> WRITE
    EXEC --> LOG
    READ --> DB
    WRITE --> DB
    LOG --> DB
```

> **Reading the architecture:** Even though multiple transactions arrive, the **Dispatch Unit** enforces a strict one-at-a-time policy. $T_2$ and $T_3$ wait in the **Hold Queue** until $T_1$ finishes, then $T_2$ runs, then $T_3$. This is the **system-level implementation** of a serial schedule.

## 4.5 State-Transition Diagram of a Transaction in a Serial Schedule

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    Active --> PartiallyCommitted : last operation executed
    PartiallyCommitted --> Committed : commit succeeded
    Committed --> [*]
    Active --> Aborted : rollback triggered
    Aborted --> [*]
```

> **In a serial schedule:** Once a transaction moves from `Active` to `PartiallyCommitted`, the next transaction in the queue is allowed to start. No transaction ever sees another in a `PartiallyCommitted` state — eliminating cascading aborts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a serial schedule. Why is every serial schedule considered correct?**

**Model Answer:**

A **serial schedule** is a schedule in which the operations of all transactions are executed one after another, with no interleaving. Each transaction runs to completion before the next transaction begins.

Every serial schedule is considered correct because:

1. Each transaction executes in **isolation** — no other transaction's operations interfere.
2. The database passes through a sequence of **valid intermediate states** since every individual transaction is assumed to maintain consistency.
3. There are no conflicts arising from concurrent access (no lost update, no dirty read, no non-repeatable read).
4. By definition, a serial schedule is trivially **conflict-serializable** and **view-serializable**.

Hence, serial schedules form the **reference standard** of correctness against which concurrent schedules are evaluated.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: How many serial schedules are possible for 3 transactions? List all of them.**

**Model Answer:**

The number of serial schedules for $n$ transactions is $n!$.

For $n = 3$:

$$
3! = 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 6
$$

All six serial schedules are:

1. $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3$
2. $T_1 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_2$
3. $T_2 \rightarrow T_1 \rightarrow T_3$
4. $T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_1$
5. $T_3 \rightarrow T_1 \rightarrow T_2$
6. $T_3 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: Consider the following two transactions operating on data items A and B:**

$$
T_1 : \; r_1(A), \; w_1(A), \; r_1(B), \; w_1(B)
$$

$$
T_2 : \; r_2(A), \; w_2(A)
$$

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** [7 Marks] List all possible **serial schedules** of $T_1$ and $T_2$. Show the precedence graph for each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Assume initial values $A = 500$ and $B = 700$. For each serial schedule, compute the final values of $A$ and $B$, where:
- $T_1$ performs: $A \leftarrow A - 50$ and $B \leftarrow B + 50$ *(transfer of ₹50 from A to B)*.
- $T_2$ performs: $A \leftarrow A \times 1.10$ *(10% interest on A)*.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

Since there are 2 transactions, the number of serial schedules is $2! = 2$.

**Serial Schedule 1: $S_1 : T_1 \rightarrow T_2$**

$$
S_1 : \quad r_1(A), w_1(A), r_1(B), w_1(B), c_1, r_2(A), w_2(A), c_2
$$

Precedence graph:

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1A["T1"] --> T2A["T2"]
```

Edge: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ (because $T_1$ writes $A$ before $T_2$ reads/writes $A$).

**Serial Schedule 2: $S_2 : T_2 \rightarrow T_1$**

$$
S_2 : \quad r_2(A), w_2(A), c_2, r_1(A), w_1(A), r_1(B), w_1(B), c_1
$$

Precedence graph:

```mermaid
graph LR
    T2B["T2"] --> T1B["T1"]
```

Edge: $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ (because $T_2$ writes $A$ before $T_1$ reads/writes $A$).

Both precedence graphs are **acyclic** (single edge in a chain), confirming conflict-serializability.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Initial State:** $A = 500$, $B = 700$.

**Execution of $S_1$ ($T_1$ first, then $T_2$):**

Step 1: $r_1(A)$ → reads $A = 500$
Step 2: $w_1(A)$ → $A = 500 - 50 = 450$
Step 3: $r_1(B)$ → reads $B = 700$
Step 4: $w_1(B)$ → $B = 700 + 50 = 750$
Step 5: $c_1$ → $T_1$ commits
Step 6: $r_2(A)$ → reads $A = 450$
Step 7: $w_2(A)$ → $A = 450 \times 1.10 = 495$
Step 8: $c_2$ → $T_2$ commits

$$
\boxed{\text{Final state after } S_1: \; A = 495, \; B = 750}
$$

**Execution of $S_2$ ($T_2$ first, then $T_1$):**

Step 1: $r_2(A)$ → reads $A = 500$
Step 2: $w_2(A)$ → $A = 500 \times 1.10 = 550$
Step 3: $c_2$ → $T_2$ commits
Step 4: $r_1(A)$ → reads $A = 550$
Step 5: $w_1(A)$ → $A = 550 - 50 = 500$
Step 6: $r_1(B)$ → reads $B = 700$
Step 7: $w_1(B)$ → $B = 700 + 50 = 750$
Step 8: $c_1$ → $T_1$ commits

$$
\boxed{\text{Final state after } S_2: \; A = 500, \; B = 750}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Schedule | Final $A$ | Final $B$ |
|---|---|---|
| $S_1 : T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ | **495** | 750 |
| $S_2 : T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ | **500** | 750 |

The value of $A$ differs (495 vs 500), while $B$ remains the same (750) because $T_2$ does not modify $B$. This demonstrates that different serial schedules can lead to different database states.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q:**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of a serial schedule with a suitable example involving two transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$. Use the operations $r_1(X), w_1(X), r_2(Y), w_2(Y)$ and show the two possible serial schedules.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **precedence graph** method. Draw the precedence graph for the serial schedule $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ and prove that the schedule is conflict-serializable.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:**
A **serial schedule** is one in which the operations of one transaction are executed completely before the operations of any other transaction begin. There is no interleaving of operations between transactions.

**Example Transactions:**

$$
T_1 : \quad r_1(X), \; w_1(X)
$$

$$
T_2 : \quad r_2(Y), \; w_2(Y)
$$

**Two possible serial schedules:**

**Schedule 1: $S_1 : T_1 \rightarrow T_2$**

$$
S_1 : \quad r_1(X), w_1(X), c_1, r_2(Y), w_2(Y), c_2
$$

Here, all of $T_1$'s operations precede all of $T_2$'s operations.

**Schedule 2: $S_2 : T_2 \rightarrow T_1$**

$$
S_2 : \quad r_2(Y), w_2(Y), c_2, r_1(X), w_1(X), c_1
$$

Here, all of $T_2$'s operations precede all of $T_1$'s operations.

**Illustration:**

```mermaid
gantt
    title S1: T1 then T2
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section T1
    r1_X  :s1a, 0, 1s
    w1_X  :s1b, after s1a, 1s
    c1    :s1c, after s1b, 1s
    section T2
    r2_Y  :s1d, after s1c, 1s
    w2_Y  :s1e, after s1d, 1s
    c2    :s1f, after s1e, 1s
```

> In the diagram, the block of $T_2$ begins only after $c_1$ (commit of $T_1$) — this is the hallmark of a serial schedule.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Precedence Graph Method — Overview:**

A precedence graph $G = (V, E)$ is a directed graph used to test whether a given schedule is conflict-serializable.

- **Vertices $V$:** Each transaction $T_i$ in the schedule.
- **Edges $E$:** A directed edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ is added if $T_i$ executes a **conflicting operation** on some data item *before* $T_j$.

**Two operations conflict if all three hold:**

1. They belong to **different** transactions.
2. They access the **same data item**.
3. At least one of them is a **write** operation.

**The Theorem:**
> A schedule $S$ is conflict-serializable **if and only if** its precedence graph $G_S$ is **acyclic**.

**Applying to $S_1 : T_1 \rightarrow T_2$:**

- $T_1$ writes $X$ before $T_2$ touches anything.
- $T_2$ writes $Y$ — $T_1$ doesn't touch $Y$.

Edges: Since the only shared data items do not exist (T1 touches X, T2 touches Y), there are **no conflicts** between $T_1$ and $T_2$. The precedence graph is:

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1N["T1"] -.-> T2N["T2"]
```

(For didactic purposes, the chain edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ represents the serial ordering — strictly the precedence graph is empty since no conflicts exist; either way, the graph is acyclic.)

**Proof of Conflict-Serializability for $S_1$:**

1. The graph $G_{S_1}$ has no back-edges, no self-loops, and no cycles.
2. By the **Conflict-Serializability Theorem**, a schedule is conflict-serializable iff its precedence graph is a DAG.
3. Since $G_{S_1}$ is a DAG, $S_1$ is conflict-serializable.
4. **Equivalent serial schedule:** $S_1$ is itself a serial schedule, equivalent to $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$.

**General Result:** All serial schedules are trivially conflict-serializable, because they induce a total order on the transactions, which is necessarily acyclic. $\blacksquare$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "serial" with "sequential":** A *serial* schedule in DBMS has a strict technical meaning — no interleaving. Students often use it loosely to mean "ordered" or "sequential operations of one transaction."
> 2. **Forgetting to list ALL serial schedules:** KTU examiners expect **all $n!$ permutations** when asked. Missing even one results in a -2 to -3 mark deduction.
> 3. **Not drawing the precedence graph correctly:** Use the *conflict* rule (same item, different transactions, at least one write). Drawing a generic arrow between $T_1$ and $T_2$ without justification loses marks.
> 4. **Assuming all serial schedules give the same output:** This is a **frequent conceptual error**. Different serial orders can yield different final states, as demonstrated in our worked example.
> 5. **Skipping the commit operation $c_i$:** Many schedules in KTU papers end with `c_i`. Omitting it in the schedule notation is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Mixing up "conflict-serializable" and "serial":** A schedule can be conflict-serializable without being serial. The converse (serial implies conflict-serializable) is what examiners test.
> 7. **Arithmetic errors in part (b) of numerical questions:** The valuation step (e.g., interest calculation, transfer) is worth 2-3 marks. Show every step clearly.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Serial Schedules**
>
> - **Definition:** A schedule where transactions execute one after another, **no interleaving** between them.
> - **Gold Standard:** Serial schedules are the *reference* for correctness; concurrent schedules are judged by their equivalence to **some** serial schedule.
> - **Counting:** Number of serial schedules for $n$ transactions = $\mathbf{n!}$
> - **Always Correct:** Every serial schedule is **conflict-serializable** *and* **view-serializable** by definition.
> - **Precedence Graph:** A serial schedule produces a precedence graph that is a *linear chain* (total order) — always acyclic.
> - **Different Outcomes:** Different serial orders can lead to **different final database states** — both are individually correct, but represent different business outcomes.
> - **Conflict Rule:** Two operations conflict only if (a) different transactions, (b) same data item, (c) at least one is a write.
> - **Properties Preserved:** No lost update, no dirty read, no non-repeatable read, no phantom read, no cascading aborts.
> - **Drawbacks:** Low throughput, poor resource utilization, no parallelism, longer response time for individual users.
> - **Common Examples:** Banking audit logs, blockchain append-only ledgers, single-threaded SQLite, log-based recovery.
> - **Equation to Memorize:** $N_{\text{serial}}(n) = n!$ — frequently tested in Part A (3 marks).
> - **Notation:** $r_i(X)$, $w_i(X)$, $c_i$, $a_i$ — write neatly in KTU answer sheets; messy notation is a hidden mark-deductor.
> - **Linkage:** Serial schedules are the **baseline**; the next sub-topics — *conflict serializability*, *view serializability*, and *recoverability* — all build on the concept of serial schedules as the correctness benchmark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concurrent and Serializable Schedules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concurrent and Serializable Schedules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Schedule?

In DBMS, a **schedule** is a chronological sequence of execution of concurrent transactions that interleaves the operations of those transactions in some order, while preserving the order of operations within each individual transaction.

A schedule `S` over a set of transactions `T = {T1, T2, ..., Tn}` is a sequence of operations drawn from the union of the operations of all transactions, such that:
- The order of operations within each individual transaction is preserved.
- The sequence represents a possible real-time execution order observed by the DBMS scheduler.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A schedule is a list of actions (read, write, commit, abort) from a set of transactions. The order of actions within any single transaction must remain the same as in the original transaction, but operations of different transactions can be interleaved.

## 1.2 Types of Schedules

### 1.2.1 Serial Schedule
A schedule in which transactions are executed one after the other, without any interleaving of operations from different transactions. The next transaction begins only after the previous one fully completes.

### 1.2.2 Concurrent (Interleaved) Schedule
A schedule where operations from multiple transactions are interleaved (mixed) in time. This allows better **CPU and disk utilization**, since one transaction can use the CPU while another waits for I/O.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need concurrent schedules?**
> - Improved throughput and resource utilization.
> - Reduced average response time.
> - A long transaction does not block short ones.
> - However, concurrency introduces problems: lost update, dirty read, non-repeatable read, phantom read (covered in detail elsewhere in Module 4).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Shared Whiteboard

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a whiteboard in a hostel corridor shared by two students, A and B.
> - A **serial schedule** means A writes everything, then erases, then B writes. No one is ever confused.
> - A **concurrent schedule** means A writes "T = 25" and steps back; B sees the old value, calculates, and writes "T = 30" on top. Now A returns and reads the board — the value is *not* what A expected.
> - **Serializability** is the property that guarantees the *final result* of a concurrent execution is *identical* to some serial execution. It is like saying: "Even though you both wrote at the same time, the final answer on the board is the same as if one of you had gone first."

## 1.4 Formal Classification of Schedules (KTU Hierarchy)

| Classification | Property | Strength |
|---|---|---|
| Serial | No interleaving | Strongest correctness |
| Conflict Serializable | Equivalent to some serial schedule via conflict swaps | Strong |
| View Serializable | Equivalent via view equivalence (weaker than conflict) | Weaker |
| Recoverable | No transaction commits before the one it depends on | For recovery |
| Cascadeless | No transaction reads uncommitted data of another | For recovery |
| Strict | No transaction reads/writes a value written by an uncommitted transaction | Strongest recovery property |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Notations Used

Let a transaction `Ti` perform operations on data item `X`:
- `ri(X)` — Transaction `Ti` **reads** data item `X`.
- `wi(X)` — Transaction `Ti` **writes** new value to `X`.
- `ci` — Transaction `Ti` **commits**.
- `ai` — Transaction `Ti` **aborts**.

Each transaction is assumed to begin with an implicit `START Ti`.

## 2.2 Serializability — The Central Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Serializability** is the property of a concurrent schedule that guarantees it produces the same effect (on the database and on the values read by transactions) as some serial execution of the same transactions.

There are two principal forms defined in the KTU syllabus:
1. **Conflict Serializability** — based on conflict-equivalence of schedules.
2. **View Serializability** — based on view-equivalence of schedules.

## 2.3 Conflict Operations

Two operations of a schedule `S` are said to **conflict** if and only if **ALL** of the following hold:

- They belong to **different** transactions.
- They access the **same data item** `X`.
- **At least one** of them is a `write` operation.

The four types of conflicting operation pairs on item `X`:

| Pair | Conflict? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `ri(X)` and `rj(X)` | **No** | Reads do not interfere with each other |
| `ri(X)` and `wj(X)` | **Yes** | Order matters: write after read vs. read after write produces different values |
| `wi(X)` and `rj(X)` | **Yes** | Order matters: read sees different value depending on position |
| `wi(X)` and `wj(X)` | **Yes** | Last write wins; order matters |

## 2.4 Conflict Equivalent Schedules

Two schedules `S` and `S'` are **conflict equivalent** if:
- They involve the same set of transactions and the same set of operations.
- They order every pair of conflicting operations of the same data item in the **same way**.

## 2.5 Conflict Serializable Schedule

A schedule `S` is **conflict serializable** if it is conflict equivalent to some serial schedule of the same `n` transactions.

> [!NOTE]
> Conflict serializability is **sufficient but not necessary** for correctness. Some schedules that are not conflict serializable may still be view serializable (and hence correct).

## 2.6 Precedence Graph (Serialization Graph)

A **precedence graph** `P(S)` is a directed graph constructed from a schedule `S` as follows:
- **Nodes:** One node for each committed transaction `Ti` in `S`.
- **Edges:** A directed edge `Ti → Tj` exists if and only if there exists a data item `X` such that in `S`:
  - `Ti` executes `write(X)` **before** `Tj` executes `read(X)`, **OR**
  - `Ti` executes `read(X)` **before** `Tj` executes `write(X)`, **OR**
  - `Ti` executes `write(X)` **before** `Tj` executes `write(X)`.

### Serializability Theorem (KTU Result)
> A schedule `S` is conflict serializable **if and only if** its precedence graph `P(S)` is **acyclic**.

If the precedence graph contains a **cycle**, the schedule is **not** conflict serializable, and the cycle is typically a clue to a concurrency anomaly.

## 2.7 View Serializability

Two schedules `S` and `S'` are **view equivalent** if:
- Same initial reads: for every data item `X`, if `Ti` reads the initial value of `X` in `S`, then `Ti` must also read the initial value of `X` in `S'`.
- Same read-from relationships: for every `X`, if `Ti` reads `X` from `Tj` in `S`, then `Ti` must read `X` from `Tj` in `S'`.
- Same final writes: for every `X`, the transaction that performs the final write to `X` in `S` is the same as in `S'`.

> A schedule is **view serializable** if it is view equivalent to some serial schedule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conflict Serializable ⊂ View Serializable.** Every conflict-serializable schedule is view-serializable, but the reverse is **not** true. Some view-serializable schedules are not conflict-serializable — these almost always involve **blind writes** (writes without a prior read in the same transaction).

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Rule |
|---|---|
| Schedule `S` | Sequence of operations from `n` transactions preserving intra-transaction order |
| Serial Schedule | No interleaving: all ops of `T1`, then all of `T2`, ... |
| Conflict on `X` | Different transactions, same item `X`, at least one is a write |
| Conflict Equivalence | Same conflict order for every conflicting pair |
| Conflict Serializable | Conflict equivalent to **some** serial schedule |
| Precedence Graph Edge | `Ti → Tj` if `Ti` accesses `X` before `Tj` with a conflict |
| Test (Conflict) | Precedence graph is **acyclic** $\Leftrightarrow$ `S` is conflict serializable |
| View Equivalent | Same initial reads, same read-from, same final writes |
| View Serializable | View equivalent to **some** serial schedule |
| Test (View) | NP-complete in general — KTU does not require algorithmic test |
| Blind Write | `wi(X)` without a prior `ri(X)` in `Ti` |
| Hierarchy | Conflict Serializable $\subset$ View Serializable $\subset$ All Schedules |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

Concurrent transaction scheduling is the cornerstone of:
- **Online Banking Systems** — thousands of simultaneous balance updates must not produce lost updates.
- **E-commerce Inventory** — two customers reserving the last unit must see consistent stock.
- **Airline Reservation Systems** — seat booking under heavy load.
- **Distributed Databases** — Google's Spanner, Amazon Aurora rely on serializability-based concurrency control.
- **Stock Trading Platforms** — strict serializability (or even stronger: linearizability) is required for regulatory compliance.
