- A transaction holds the lock for the duration of its execution, and releases it (either implicitly at commit/rollback or explicitly) according to a specific protocol.

The two primitive lock modes are:

| Lock Mode | Symbol | Allows Concurrent Reads? | Allows Concurrent Writes? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Shared Lock (S)** | `LS` | Yes | No |
| **Exclusive Lock (X)** | `LX` | No | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Locking is a *pessimistic* approach, meaning conflicts are assumed to be likely, and locks prevent them. This contrasts with *optimistic* methods (timestamp ordering, validation) where transactions proceed without locks and conflicts are detected at commit time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **public restroom in a busy railway station**. The door has two possible states:

- **Unlocked + Unoccupied** → Anyone can enter (multiple readers possible).
- **Locked from inside** → The person inside has exclusive access; no one else can enter (writer exclusivity).
- **"Engaged" sign with a see-through glass** → Multiple people can see inside (read), but only one can lock from inside to use it (write).

The restroom door is the **lock**, the people wanting to enter are the **transactions**, and the station manager is the **Lock Manager** enforcing the rules. Without this mechanism, two people entering simultaneously would lead to a "conflict" — a state the database never wants.

Similarly, in a DBMS, a **Shared (S) lock** is like a "read-only" sticker on a file in a library: many students can read it together, but no one can edit it. An **Exclusive (X) lock** is like a student taking the file to a private room: no one else can read or write it until it is returned.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Locking Matters:** Without locking, concurrent transactions can violate the **ACID** properties (especially Isolation and Consistency), leading to problems like the *lost update problem*, *dirty read*, *non-repeatable read*, and *phantom read*.

## 1.3 Why Pessimistic Locking?

In high-contention systems (e.g., banking, e-commerce flash sales, airline reservations), the probability of transaction conflict is high. **Locking** is preferred because:

- It provides **deterministic conflict resolution**.
- It guarantees **serializability** when combined with a correct protocol such as **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)**.
- It is well-supported by commercial DBMS engines (Oracle, MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL, SQL Server).
- It allows fine-grained control at the row, page, or table level.

## 1.4 Standard Lock Granularity Metrics

The standard metrics used in lock granularity are:

- **Lock Granularity (g):** The size of the data item on which a lock is held. Smaller granularity (row) → higher concurrency but more overhead. Larger granularity (table) → lower concurrency but less overhead.
- **Lock Manager Overhead (L):** Typically expressed as the number of locks held × memory cost per lock.
- **Coarse-grained vs Fine-grained trade-off boundary:** **Multi-level Locking** (Intent Locks) is the KTU-approved solution to balance this trade-off.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Engineering Insight:** Locking is the *backbone* of transaction processing in RDBMS. Every SQL statement (`SELECT … FOR UPDATE`, `UPDATE`, `INSERT`, `DELETE`) implicitly or explicitly interacts with the lock manager.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lock Types — The Complete KTU Taxonomy

### 2.1.1 Binary Locks
- A simple lock with two states: **locked (1)** and **unlocked (0)**.
- Limited utility; cannot distinguish between read and write access.

### 2.1.2 Shared / Exclusive Locks (Read/Write Locks)
This is the most fundamental KTU-required lock model.

- **Shared Lock (S):** A transaction $T_i$ requests a shared lock on data item $Q$ before *reading* $Q$.
- **Exclusive Lock (X):** A transaction $T_i$ requests an exclusive lock on data item $Q$ before *writing* $Q$.

### 2.1.3 Intention Locks (Used in Multi-Granularity Locking)
Used when a transaction locks a node at one level (e.g., a table) and implicitly signals intent to lock at a finer level (e.g., a row within that table).

- **IS (Intention Shared):** Intends to acquire S locks at finer granularity.
- **IX (Intention Exclusive):** Intends to acquire X locks at finer granularity.
- **SIX (Shared + Intention Exclusive):** Holds S on the current node and intends to acquire X on some child node.

### 2.1.4 Update Locks
- Acquired by a transaction that intends to *modify* a data item but has not yet read it. Prevents lock upgrade deadlocks.

### 2.1.5 Schema Locks, Bulk Update Locks, etc. (advanced, not always in KTU Module 4)

## 2.2 Lock Compatibility Matrix

A transaction $T_i$ requests a lock of mode $M$ on item $Q$. The request is granted **iff** the requested mode is compatible with the strongest mode already held on $Q$ by another transaction.

$$
\text{Compatible}(M_{\text{requested}}, M_{\text{held}}) = \text{True} \iff M_{\text{requested}} \text{ and } M_{\text{held}} \text{ can coexist}
$$

**Compatibility Matrix (KTU standard):**

| Held \ Requested | **S** | **X** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **S** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **X** | ❌ No | ❌ No |

For multi-granularity with intention locks:

| Held \ Requested | **IS** | **IX** | **S** | **SIX** | **X** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **IS** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **IX** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **S** | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **SIX** | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **X** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |

## 2.3 The Two-Phase Locking Protocol (2PL)

**Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** is a concurrency control protocol that guarantees **conflict-serializability**. It enforces the rule that every transaction handles its locks in two distinct, non-overlapping phases:

1. **Growing Phase:** The transaction may acquire locks, but cannot release any lock.
2. **Shrinking Phase:** The transaction may release locks, but cannot acquire any new lock.

**Lock Point:** The instant in time when a transaction acquires its *final* lock (the boundary between the growing and shrinking phases).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Key Point:** A transaction is said to follow the 2PL protocol if *all* lock acquisitions precede *all* lock releases. Mathematically, if $T_i$ holds lock $L_a$ at time $t_a$ and $L_b$ at time $t_b$, and $T_i$ releases $L_a$ at $t'_a$ and $L_b$ at $t'_b$, then either $t_a < t_b < t'_b < t'_a$ or $t_a < t'_a < t_b < t'_b$ must hold (i.e., no interleaved acquire-release pairs).

### 2.3.1 Variants of 2PL

| Variant | Restriction | Avoids | Drawback |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Basic 2PL** | Two phases, as above | Inconsistency from interleaving | Cascading rollbacks possible |
| **Conservative 2PL (Static 2PL)** | Locks acquired *before* execution starts | Deadlock | Starvation + low concurrency |
| **Strict 2PL** | All X-locks held until commit/abort | Cascading aborts | Slightly more holding time |
| **Rigorous 2PL** | *All* locks (S and X) held until commit/abort | Cascading aborts + easy recovery | Reduces concurrency |

## 2.4 Deadlock in Locking Systems

A **deadlock** occurs when two or more transactions are permanently blocked, each waiting for a lock held by another transaction in the set.

**Example (Classic Cycle):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: \text{lock-X}(A) \rightarrow \text{wait for lock on } B \\
T_2 &: \text{lock-X}(B) \rightarrow \text{wait for lock on } A
\end{aligned}
$$

This creates a *wait-for cycle*: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_1$.

### 2.4.1 Deadlock Handling Strategies

| Strategy | Description | KTU Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Prevention** | Pre-declare all locks (Conservative 2PL); use timestamps | Restricted lock requests |
| **Detection** | Build a **Wait-For Graph (WFG)**, check for cycles periodically | $O(V+E)$ algorithm |
| **Avoidance** | Use a partial ordering of resources; request resources in order | Banker's algorithm-like |
| **Recovery** | Victim selection: abort the cheapest transaction (youngest, fewest locks) | Starvation possible |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Lock Compatibility | $S \leftrightarrow S$ allowed; any pair with $X$ denied | $N \ge 2$ concurrent transactions |
| 2PL Condition | $t_{\text{acquire}} < t_{\text{release}}$ partition | Lock Point $t_p$ is unique |
| Wait-For Graph | Cycle $\Rightarrow$ Deadlock | $|V| \le n$ active transactions |
| Lock Granularity Cost | $C_{\text{total}} = n_r \cdot c_r + n_p \cdot c_p + n_t \cdot c_t$ | $c_r < c_p < c_t$ |
| Deadlock Probability (approx.) | $P_d \approx 1 - e^{- \lambda \cdot t_h}$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $t_h$ = holding time |
| Starvation Resolution | Use FIFO or wait-die / wound-wait schemes | Aging counter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Locking is used in *every* production DBMS. Multi-granularity locking (IS/IX/SIX) is what allows MySQL InnoDB to do **row-level locking** efficiently, and SQL Server to support **lock escalation** heuristics. The trade-offs studied here directly influence real-world **deadlock detection intervals** in Oracle (typically every 3 seconds) and **lock timeout** values in PostgreSQL.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Proof That 2PL Guarantees Conflict-Serializability

**Theorem:** If every transaction in a schedule follows the 2PL protocol, then the schedule is conflict-serializable.

**Proof Outline (Step-by-Step Derivation):**

**Step 1: Define a precedence graph.**
For a schedule $S$, construct a precedence graph $P = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of committed transactions and an edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ exists if $T_i$ and $T_j$ conflict and $T_i$ accesses the conflicting data item earlier.

**Step 2: Show that 2PL implies no cycle in the precedence graph.**
Assume for contradiction that 2PL allows a cycle:

$$
T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow T_n \rightarrow T_1
$$

**Step 3: Analyze the lock point of each transaction.**
Let $\text{LP}(T_k)$ be the lock point of transaction $T_k$. The lock point is the moment when $T_k$ acquires its last lock.

**Step 4: Use the conflict edges to derive a time-ordering contradiction.**

Consider the cycle $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$. By definition of conflict:

- $T_1$ accessed data item $Q$ (with a lock) *before* $T_2$ accessed $Q$.
- Therefore, $T_1$ must have held its lock on $Q$ until $T_2$ requested it.
- Hence, $T_1$ cannot release its lock on $Q$ before $T_2$ acquires it.

**Step 5: Establish the chain of lock points.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LP}(T_1) &< \text{LP}(T_2) \quad \text{(because $T_1$ must wait for $T_2$'s release after $T_2$ acquires)} \\
\text{LP}(T_2) &< \text{LP}(T_3) \\
&\vdots \\
\text{LP}(T_n) &< \text{LP}(T_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Combine the inequalities.**

$$
\text{LP}(T_1) < \text{LP}(T_2) < \cdots < \text{LP}(T_n) < \text{LP}(T_1)
$$

This gives $\text{LP}(T_1) < \text{LP}(T_1)$, a **contradiction**.

**Step 7: Conclusion.**
Since the assumption of a cycle leads to a contradiction, the precedence graph of any 2PL schedule must be acyclic. Therefore, **every 2PL schedule is conflict-serializable**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Lock-Compatibility Decision Logic

**Step 1: Define the lock request function.**

$$
\text{Request}(T_i, Q, M) = \text{grant} \iff \forall T_j \text{ holding a lock on } Q, \text{Compatible}(M, \text{Mode}(T_j, Q))
$$

**Step 2: For S/X compatibility.**

$$
\text{Compatible}(M_1, M_2) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } (M_1, M_2) \in \{(S,S), (S,\text{null}), (\text{null},S)\} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3: The lock manager maintains a lock table.**

Each entry is a tuple: $(Q, \text{lock list}, \text{waiting queue})$.

**Step 4: Example walk-through.**

Suppose $Q = A$ is in state unlocked. Now we evaluate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 \text{ requests } \text{lock-S}(A) &: \text{grant (no holders)} \\
T_2 \text{ requests } \text{lock-S}(A) &: \text{grant (compatible with $T_1$'s S-lock)} \\
T_3 \text{ requests } \text{lock-X}(A) &: \text{DENY (conflict with both S-locks)} \\
T_1 \text{ releases } \text{lock-S}(A) &: \text{$T_3$ still waiting, $T_2$ holds} \\
T_2 \text{ releases } \text{lock-S}(A) &: \text{now $T_3$ can be granted}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When 2PL Variant Should Be Used

| Scenario | Recommended Variant | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Banking system (high consistency) | Strict 2PL | Avoids cascading aborts; clean recovery |
| Read-heavy reporting DB | Rigorous 2PL | All locks held to commit ensures consistent snapshot |
| Real-time scheduling system | Basic 2PL | Maximizes concurrency; tolerates cascading aborts |
| Long analytical queries | Conservative 2PL | Pre-declared locks prevent deadlock in read-mostly loads |
| Mixed OLTP workload | Strict 2PL with row-level + intent locks | Best balance |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Building the Wait-For Graph

**Step 1: Vertices** = active (uncommitted) transactions $\{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4\}$.

**Step 2: Directed edge** $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ is added if $T_i$ is currently waiting for a lock held by $T_j$.

**Step 3: Example scenario.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: \text{holds } X\text{-lock on } A; \text{waiting for } B \text{ (held by } T_2) \\
T_2 &: \text{holds } X\text{-lock on } B; \text{waiting for } C \text{ (held by } T_3) \\
T_3 &: \text{holds } X\text{-lock on } C; \text{waiting for } A \text{ (held by } T_1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Resulting WFG edges.**

$$
T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_1
$$

**Step 5: Cycle detection** (Depth-First Search) finds the cycle. **Deadlock confirmed.**

**Step 6: Recovery** — DBMS picks a *victim* (e.g., $T_1$, the one with the lowest cost-to-rollback) and aborts it, releasing all its locks and breaking the cycle.

## 3.5 Derivation: Conservative 2PL Deadlock-Freeness

In Conservative 2PL, a transaction acquires **all** its locks *before* executing the first read or write.

**Claim:** Conservative 2PL is deadlock-free.

**Proof:**

- Suppose a deadlock exists among $T_1, \ldots, T_n$.
- Then each $T_i$ is waiting for a lock held by some $T_j$.
- But $T_i$ acquired *all* of its locks upfront, so it is *not* holding any lock that $T_j$ is waiting for.
- The only way $T_j$ waits is if it failed to acquire a lock at the start.
- But the lock manager only grants locks to a transaction if the lock is free; otherwise, the transaction does not start.
- Therefore, no two transactions can be in a circular wait state. $\blacksquare$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lock Compatibility Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transaction Ti requests lock M on data item Q] --> B{Check lock table for Q}
    B --> C{Any conflicting lock held?}
    C -- No --> D[Grant Lock to Ti]
    C -- Yes --> E[Add Ti to waiting queue for Q]
    E --> F[Block Ti; resume when holder commits or rolls back]
    D --> G[Update lock table entry: list now includes Ti mode M]
    G --> H[Allow Ti to proceed with read or write on Q]
    F --> I[When prior holder releases lock]
    I --> J[Re-evaluate waiting queue: grant if compatible]
```

## 4.2 Two-Phase Locking (2PL) Protocol State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Growing
    Growing --> Growing: Acquire new lock S or X
    Growing --> LockPoint: Acquire final required lock
    LockPoint --> Shrinking: First lock released
    Shrinking --> Shrinking: Release more locks
    Shrinking --> Committed: All locks released and transaction commits
    Shrinking --> Aborted: Rollback triggered
    Committed --> [*]
    Aborted --> [*]
    note right of LockPoint: Lock Point = boundary\nbetween growing and shrinking
    note right of Shrinking: NO new lock acquisitions\nallowed in this phase
```

## 4.3 Multi-Granularity Locking (IS / IX / S / SIX / X) — Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    DB[Database Level] --> T1[Table A]
    DB --> T2[Table B]
    T1 --> P1[Page A1]
    T1 --> P2[Page A2]
    T2 --> P3[Page B1]
    P1 --> R1[Row R1]
    P1 --> R2[Row R2]
    P2 --> R3[Row R3]
    P3 --> R4[Row R4]

    style DB fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P1 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style P2 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style P3 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style R1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style R2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style R3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style R4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** A transaction wanting to update a single row must first acquire an `IX` lock on the database, then `IX` on the table, then `IX` on the page, and finally `X` on the row. The *intention* locks propagate downward, signaling the intent to lock children.

## 4.4 Wait-For Graph with Deadlock Cycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1((T1)) -- waiting for B held by --> T2((T2))
    T2((T2)) -- waiting for C held by --> T3((T3))
    T3((T3)) -- waiting for A held by --> T1((T1))

    style T1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T2 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T3 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:** The cycle `T1 → T2 → T3 → T1` indicates a deadlock. The lock manager will select a victim (often the one with the lowest rollback cost) and abort it to break the cycle.

## 4.5 Block-Level Architecture: Lock Manager Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    TXN[Transaction Manager] --> LM[Lock Manager]
    LM --> LT[Lock Table]
    LM --> WFG[Wait For Graph Builder]
    LM --> DD[Deadlock Detector]
    LM --> LR[Lock Request Queue]
    LT --> SHM[Shared Hash Map: data item to lock list]
    WFG --> DD
    DD --> VS[Victim Selector]
    DD --> TXN
    VS --> TXN
    LR --> LM
```

**Function of each block:**

| Block | Role |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Transaction Manager** | Issues lock/unlock requests on behalf of transactions |
| **Lock Manager** | Central arbiter; grants/denies lock requests |
| **Lock Table** | Data structure mapping data items to current holders and waiters |
| **Wait-For Graph Builder** | Maintains directed edges between waiting/blocked transactions |
| **Deadlock Detector** | Runs DFS / cycle detection on WFG periodically |
| **Victim Selector** | Chooses cheapest-to-abort transaction for rollback |
| **Lock Request Queue** | FIFO or priority queue of pending lock requests |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a lock. What is a shared lock and an exclusive lock? Give an example.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

A **lock** is a mechanism used by a DBMS to control concurrent access to a data item. It ensures that multiple transactions can access the same data item without violating the consistency of the database.

- **Shared Lock (S):** Allows a transaction to *read* a data item. Multiple transactions can hold shared locks on the same data item simultaneously, but no transaction holding a shared lock can write to it.
- **Exclusive Lock (X):** Allows a transaction to both *read and write* a data item. Only one transaction can hold an exclusive lock on a data item at a time, and no other lock (S or X) can coexist with it.

**Example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: \text{lock-S}(A) \rightarrow \text{read}(A) \\
T_2 &: \text{lock-S}(A) \rightarrow \text{read}(A) \quad \text{(allowed concurrently)} \\
T_3 &: \text{lock-X}(A) \rightarrow \text{write}(A) \quad \text{(must wait for $T_1, T_2$ to release)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Q2. What is the Two-Phase Locking (2PL) protocol? List any two disadvantages.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Two-Phase Locking (2PL) protocol** is a concurrency control technique where every transaction is divided into two distinct phases:

1. **Growing Phase:** The transaction acquires all required locks. No locks are released.
2. **Shrinking Phase:** The transaction releases locks. No new locks can be acquired.

The moment the last lock is acquired is called the **lock point**. 2PL guarantees **conflict-serializability**.

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Deadlocks:** 2PL can lead to deadlocks, where two or more transactions wait indefinitely for each other to release locks. (3 marks) / (also: cascading aborts, reduced concurrency, possible starvation)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

#### Q3. (a) Explain the Two-Phase Locking (2PL) protocol with suitable example. Why does it guarantee serializability? (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
2PL is a protocol that ensures that in any transaction, all lock acquisitions precede all lock releases.

**Phases:**

- **Growing Phase:** The transaction acquires locks (S or X) on data items it needs. It may not release any lock during this phase.
- **Shrinking Phase:** The transaction releases locks. It may not acquire any new lock during this phase.

**Example Schedule:**

Consider two transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$ on data items $A$ and $B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: \text{lock-S}(A) \rightarrow \text{read}(A) \rightarrow \text{lock-X}(B) \rightarrow \text{write}(B) \rightarrow \text{unlock}(A) \rightarrow \text{unlock}(B) \\
T_2 &: \text{lock-S}(B) \rightarrow \text{read}(B) \rightarrow \text{lock-X}(A) \rightarrow \text{write}(A) \rightarrow \text{unlock}(B) \rightarrow \text{unlock}(A)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analysis of $T_1$:** Acquires `lock-S(A)` and `lock-X(B)` (Growing), then unlocks `A` and `B` (Shrinking). ✓ Follows 2PL.

**Why 2PL Guarantees Serializability:**

The precedence graph of any 2PL schedule is acyclic. Suppose, for contradiction, a cycle exists: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow T_n \rightarrow T_1$. Each edge implies the source transaction accessed a data item first and held its lock until the destination acquired its lock. Thus, the lock point of the source is before the lock point of the destination. This chain gives:

$$
\text{LP}(T_1) < \text{LP}(T_2) < \cdots < \text{LP}(T_n) < \text{LP}(T_1)
$$

This is a contradiction. Hence, no cycle exists, and the schedule is **conflict-serializable**.

#### Q3. (b) Explain the different variants of 2PL. How does Strict 2PL avoid cascading rollbacks? (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Variants of 2PL:**

1. **Basic 2PL:** Two phases as defined above. Allows cascading rollbacks.
2. **Conservative (Static) 2PL:** Transaction acquires **all** locks before execution begins. Avoids deadlock but reduces concurrency.
3. **Strict 2PL:** All **exclusive (X)** locks must be held until the transaction **commits or aborts**. Shared locks may be released earlier.
4. **Rigorous 2PL:** **All** locks (S and X) must be held until commit/abort.

**How Strict 2PL Avoids Cascading Rollbacks:**

A **cascading rollback** occurs when an uncommitted transaction's updates are read by another transaction, and the first transaction then aborts, forcing the second to also abort. In Strict 2PL:

- An exclusive lock is held until commit/abort.
- Therefore, no other transaction can read or write the locked item.
- Hence, no transaction reads uncommitted data.
- If $T_1$ aborts, no other transaction was dependent on its writes, so no cascading abort is needed. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

#### Q4. (a) What is a deadlock? Explain deadlock detection using Wait-For Graph with a suitable example. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Deadlock:**
A **deadlock** is a state in which two or more transactions are blocked indefinitely, each waiting for a lock held by another transaction in the same set.

**Wait-For Graph (WFG):**

- **Vertices:** Active transactions.
- **Directed Edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$:** Exists iff $T_i$ is waiting for a lock currently held by $T_j$.
- **Cycle in WFG $\Rightarrow$ Deadlock exists.**

**Example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: \text{lock-X}(A) \rightarrow \text{read}(A) \rightarrow \text{request lock-X}(B) \rightarrow \text{(waits, $B$ held by $T_2$)} \\
T_2 &: \text{lock-X}(B) \rightarrow \text{read}(B) \rightarrow \text{request lock-X}(A) \rightarrow \text{(waits, $A$ held by $T_1$)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**WFG Construction:**

- Edge $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ (since $T_1$ waits for $B$, held by $T_2$).
- Edge $T_2 \rightarrow T_1$ (since $T_2$ waits for $A$, held by $T_1$).

**Cycle Detected:** $T_1 \leftrightarrow T_2$ → **Deadlock confirmed.**

**Recovery:** The DBMS chooses a **victim** (e.g., $T_2$, the one with the lowest cost to roll back) and aborts it. This breaks the cycle, releases $T_2$'s locks, and allows $T_1$ to proceed.

**Deadlock Detection Algorithm:** Periodically (e.g., every few seconds) the system runs a DFS on the WFG. If a back edge is found, a cycle is reported. The complexity is $O(V + E)$.

#### Q4. (b) Explain lock compatibility matrix for S and X locks. Show with a schedule how a request for X-lock is denied while S-locks are held. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]**

**Model Solution:**

**Lock Compatibility Matrix:**

| Held \ Requested | **S** | **X** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **S** | Yes | No |
| **X** | No | No |

**Rule:** A new lock request $M$ on data item $Q$ is granted **iff** the requested mode is compatible with the strongest lock currently held on $Q$.

**Schedule Demonstration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time} \; t_0 &: T_1 \text{ requests lock-S}(A) \rightarrow \text{GRANTED (no holder)} \\
\text{Time} \; t_1 &: T_2 \text{ requests lock-S}(A) \rightarrow \text{GRANTED (compatible with $T_1$'s S)} \\
\text{Time} \; t_2 &: T_3 \text{ requests lock-X}(A) \rightarrow \text{DENIED (conflict with two S-locks)} \\
\text{Time} \; t_3 &: T_3 \text{ is BLOCKED and added to waiting queue} \\
\text{Time} \; t_4 &: T_1 \text{ releases lock-S}(A) \rightarrow T_3 \text{ still waiting (one S-holder remains)} \\
\text{Time} \; t_5 &: T_2 \text{ releases lock-S}(A) \rightarrow T_3 \text{ is now granted lock-X}(A) \\
\text{Time} \; t_6 &: T_3 \text{ performs write}(A) \text{ and commits; releases lock-X}(A)
\end{aligned}
$$

This demonstrates that **X-locks must wait for all S-locks to be released** before being granted, and **multiple S-locks can coexist**. The lock manager enforces this via the compatibility matrix. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing lock point with commit/rollback:** The lock point is when the *last* lock is acquired, *not* when the transaction commits. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if this is stated incorrectly.
> 2. **Forgetting to label the Growing and Shrinking phases:** When drawing the 2PL timeline, the phases **must** be clearly demarcated with a vertical line at the lock point.
> 3. **Ignoring cascading aborts:** If asked *why* 2PL is not enough, students forget to mention **cascading rollbacks**. Mention it explicitly.
> 4. **Wrong direction of WFG edge:** The edge $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ means $T_i$ **waits for** $T_j$, *not* the other way around. Many students invert this. KTU examiners will cut marks.
> 5. **Incomplete compatibility matrix:** When asked about IS/IX/SIX, students often write only the S/X matrix. Always include all 5 modes if the question touches multi-granularity.
> 6. **Not distinguishing Strict 2PL from Rigorous 2PL:** Strict holds *only* X-locks until commit; Rigorous holds *all* locks. Conflating them costs a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lock** = a synchronization variable associated with a data item.
- **Shared (S)** lock = read-only access, multiple allowed.
- **Exclusive (X)** lock = read + write, only one allowed.
- **Lock Manager** = central authority; consults the lock table.
- **Compatibility Matrix** = grid showing which lock-mode pairs can coexist.
- **Two-Phase Locking (2PL):**
  - **Growing Phase** → acquire only.
  - **Shrinking Phase** → release only.
  - **Lock Point** = transition moment.
  - Guarantees **conflict-serializability**.
- **Strict 2PL** → all X-locks held until commit/abort → no cascading rollbacks.
- **Rigorous 2PL** → all locks (S + X) held until commit/abort.
- **Conservative 2PL** → all locks pre-acquired → deadlock-free but reduced concurrency.
- **Deadlock** = circular wait, detected via **Wait-For Graph** cycle.
- **Deadlock Recovery** = select victim (cheapest to roll back) and abort.
- **Multi-granularity Locks:** IS, IX, S, SIX, X — used together with **intention locks** to signal descendant-level locking.
- **Intention locks** traverse the tree top-down (DB → Table → Page → Row).
- **Starvation** prevention → FIFO waiting queue, aging counter, or wound-wait scheme.
- **Engineering trade-off:** finer granularity = more concurrency, higher overhead; coarser = less concurrency, less overhead.
- **Real-world DBs:** MySQL InnoDB (row-level + intent locks), Oracle (row-level + deadlocks detected every 3s), PostgreSQL (no lock escalation, uses MVCC + 2PL hybrid).
- **KTU Board Hot Topics:** 2PL phases, lock compatibility matrix, wait-for graph, deadlock detection/recovery, strict vs basic 2PL.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two-Phase Locking and Its Variations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Two-Phase Locking and Its Variations # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** is a **concurrency control protocol** used in Database Management Systems that guarantees **serializability** of transactions. A transaction following the 2PL protocol must acquire all the locks it needs before it begins to release any of them, dividing its execution into two distinct, non-overlapping phases: a **Growing Phase** (where locks are only acquired) and a **Shrinking Phase** (where locks are only released). In the formal mathematical sense, for a transaction $T_i$, the set of all locks held by $T_i$ at any time $t$ is denoted as $L_i(t)$. The 2PL condition states that there must exist a **Lock Point** $t_p$ in the schedule such that: - For all $t < t_p$: $L_i(t) \subseteq L_i(t+1)$ (the set of locks is monotonically non-decreasing — Growing Phase). - For all $t > t_p$: $L_i(t) \supseteq L_i(t+1)$ (the set of locks is monotonically non-increasing — Shrinking Phase). The **Lock Point** is the exact moment in time when the transaction has acquired its final (last) lock, immediately before it releases its first lock. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Two-Phase Locking ensures **Conflict-Serializability**. However, it does **not** prevent deadlocks and is **susceptible to cascading aborts**, which directly motivates the study of its **variations** (Conservative, Strict, and Rigorous 2PL). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are moving furniture into a new house. The process naturally splits into two phases: 1. **Growing Phase (Packing & Loading):** You can keep picking up more boxes and items, but you cannot drop any off yet. Your hands keep getting "fuller" with more boxes. 2. **Shrinking Phase (Unloading & Placing):** The instant you put down (release) the first box, you can no longer pick up another. You can only put boxes down from here on. The **exact moment you set down your first box** is your *Lock Point* — the highest count of boxes/holds you will ever have at any single point. In DBMS terms, each "box" is a **lock** on a data item, and a transaction that follows these two non-overlapping rules is guaranteed to be serializable. > [!TIP] > A transaction is in the **Growing Phase** if it can acquire new locks but cannot release any. It enters the **Shrinking Phase** the moment it releases its first lock, after which it cannot acquire any new locks. ## 1.3 Why 2PL is Necessary Without 2PL, a transaction might release a lock on item $A$, and then later re-acquire a lock on $A$ after it has read another conflicting item $B$. This interleaving breaks the precedence graph's acyclicity, leading to a **non-serializable schedule** (lost updates, uncommitted dependency, inconsistent analysis). 2PL rigidly prevents this by enforcing the monotonicity of the lock set. --- ## 1.4 Variations of 2PL — At a Glance The basic 2PL has three important variations, each designed to overcome a specific weakness: | Variation | Key Rule | Solves | |---|---|---| | **Basic 2PL** | Grow → Shrink (one transition) | Conflict-Serializability | | **Conservative 2PL** | Lock all items **before** start; grow = ∅ | **Deadlock** prevention | | **Strict 2PL** | All **exclusive (write) locks** held until commit/abort | **Cascading Aborts** (for writes) | | **Rigorous 2PL** | All locks (read + write) held until commit/abort | Cascading Aborts (fully) + recoverable | > [!WARNING] > **Strict 2PL ≠ Rigorous 2PL.** In *Strict 2PL*, **only write locks** are held till commit. In *Rigorous 2PL*, **both read and write locks** are held till commit. This is a frequent KTU exam trap. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of Basic 2PL A transaction $T$ obeying 2PL passes through the following state transitions: ### Phase 1 — Growing Phase - The transaction may **acquire** any number of locks (`Shared` or `Exclusive`) on any data items. - It **cannot release** any lock during this phase. - New locks can be acquired at any time before the lock point. ### Lock Point ($t_p$) - The instant in time when the lock set $L(t)$ reaches its **maximum cardinality**. - Mathematically: $t_p = \arg\max_t \vert L(t) \vert$ ### Phase 2 — Shrinking Phase - The transaction may **release** locks, one or more at a time. - It **cannot acquire** any *new* lock during this phase. - Existing locks cannot be re-acquired if they were released earlier in this phase. ## 2.2 Lock Compatibility Matrix Two locks on the **same data item** issued by different transactions are compatible only if they do not conflict. This is the central reference table for 2PL: | Lock Held $\downarrow$ \ Lock Requested $\rightarrow$ | **Shared (S) / Read** | **Exclusive (X) / Write** | |---|---|---| | **Shared (S) / Read** | ✔ Compatible | ✘ Conflict | | **Exclusive (X) / Write** | ✘ Conflict | ✘ Conflict | Symbolically, the compatibility function $\mathcal{C}(L_a, L_b)$ returns **1** if the pair is compatible and **0** otherwise. ## 2.3 Lock Conversion (Upgrade / Downgrade) A powerful extension of 2PL allows a transaction to **convert** an existing lock: - **Lock Upgrade:** Converting a held `S-lock` to an `X-lock`. This is treated as a *new lock acquisition* and therefore is allowed **only in the Growing Phase**. - **Lock Downgrade:** Converting a held `X-lock` to an `S-lock`. This is treated as a *lock release* and therefore is allowed **only in the Shrinking Phase**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** Attempting an upgrade in the Shrinking Phase or a downgrade in the Growing Phase **violates 2PL** and may lead to non-serializable schedules. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | |---|---|---| | 1 | 2PL Growing condition | $L(t) \subseteq L(t+1)$ for $t < t_p$ | | 2 | 2PL Shrinking condition | $L(t) \supseteq L(t+1)$ for $t > t_p$ | | 3 | Lock point | $t_p = \arg\max_t \vert L(t) \vert$ | | 4 | Conservative 2PL | $L(\text{start}) = $ all required locks; No growing | | 5 | Strict 2PL | All $X$-locks released only at commit / abort | | 6 | Rigorous 2PL | All $S$-locks + $X$-locks released only at commit / abort | | 7 | Read–Read compat | $S$ + $S$ → ✔ | | 8 | Read–Write conflict | $S$ + $X$ → ✘ | | 9 | Write–Read conflict | $X$ + $S$ → ✘ | | 10 | Write–Write conflict | $X$ + $X$ → ✘ | ## 2.5 Why the Variations Matter — Real-World Engineering Utility In real-world DBMS (e.g., **PostgreSQL, Oracle, MySQL InnoDB**), the choice of 2PL variant directly affects: - **Throughput** (transactions per second) - **Recovery time** (cascading aborts slow down recovery) - **Deadlock frequency** (conservative 2PL eliminates them at the cost of knowing all items in advance) The **Rigorous 2PL** is conceptually the simplest and is closely related to the **S2PL** (Strict 2PL) protocol used in practice for write-heavy workloads. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Proof of 2PL Correctness (Conflict-Serializability) ### Claim Every schedule produced by a set of transactions following 2PL is **conflict-serializable**. ### Step 1 — Define the Precedence Relation For two transactions $T_i$ and $T_j$ in schedule $S$, we write $T_i \rightarrow T_j$ if there exists a data item $Q$ such that: - $T_i$ executes `read(Q)` or `write(Q)` *before* - $T_j$ executes `write(Q)` (or `read(Q)` in case of read–write conflict). ### Step 2 — Assume for Contradiction that the Precedence Graph is Cyclic Suppose there is a cycle: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow T_n \rightarrow T_1$. This implies $T_1$ accessed some data item $Q_a$ in a conflicting manner with $T_2$, and so on, until $T_n$ accessed a data item $Q_z$ conflicting with $T_1$. ### Step 3 — Analyze Lock Acquisition Order Without loss of generality, consider the conflicting edge $T_i \rightarrow T_{i+1}$ on item $Q_k$. Under 2PL: - $T_i$ must acquire its lock on $Q_k$ **before** releasing it. - $T_{i+1}$ must acquire its lock on $Q_k$ **after** $T_i$ acquires, and **before** $T_i$ releases. Therefore, the **lock point** of $T_i$ must occur **before** the lock point of $T_{i+1}$: $$ t_p(T_i) < t_p(T_{i+1}) $$ ### Step 4 — Chain the Inequalities From the cycle $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow T_n \rightarrow T_1$, we obtain: $$ \begin{aligned} t_p(T_1) &< t_p(T_2) \\ t_p(T_2) &< t_p(T_3) \\ &\;\;\vdots \\ t_p(T_n) &< t_p(T_1) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Derive a Contradiction Chaining all inequalities: $$ t_p(T_1) < t_p(T_2) < \dots < t_p(T_n) < t_p(T_1) $$ This is a strict inequality cycle, which is **impossible** in a total order of real numbers. Hence, no cycle can exist. ### Step 6 — Conclude The precedence graph is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**. Therefore, the schedule is **conflict-serializable**. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Properties of 2PL Variants | Property | Basic 2PL | Conservative 2PL | Strict 2PL | Rigorous 2PL | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Conflict-Serializable?** | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes | | **Deadlock-Free?** | ✘ No | ✔ Yes | ✘ No | ✘ No | | **Avoids Cascading Aborts?** | ✘ No | ✘ No | ✔ Partially (writes only) | ✔ Fully | | **Recoverable?** | ✔ (if so designed) | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes | | **Growing Phase Length** | Flexible | **Zero** (predeclared) | Flexible | Flexible | | **Shrinking Phase** | Begins at first release | **Never shrinks** (releases all at end) | Only after commit (X-locks) | Only at commit (all locks) | | **Lock release for S-locks** | Any time after lock point | Only at commit | Any time after lock point | Only at commit | | **Lock release for X-locks** | Any time after lock point | Only at commit | **Only at commit / abort** | **Only at commit / abort** | | **Implementation Cost** | Low | High (requires *a priori* knowledge) | Medium | High | | **Used in Practice** | Academic baseline | Rare | **Common** (InnoDB) | Academic / specific DBs | ## 3.3 Worked Example: Cascading Abort in Basic 2PL Consider the following schedule $S$ under basic 2PL: | Time | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ | |---|---|---|---| | $t_1$ | `lock-S(A)` | | | | $t_2$ | `read(A)` | | | | $t_3$ | `unlock(A)` | | | | $t_4$ | | `lock-S(A)` | | | $t_5$ | | `read(A)` | | | $t_6$ | | `lock-S(B)` | | | $t_7$ | | `read(B)` | | | $t_8$ | | `unlock(A)` | | | $t_9$ | | `write(B)` → upgrade | | | $t_{10}$ | | `commit` | | | $t_{11}$ | | | `lock-X(B)` | | $t_{12}$ | | | `read(B)` | | $t_{13}$ | | | `commit` | **Cascading abort problem:** If $T_1$ fails to commit *after* $T_2$ has read the uncommitted value of $A$, then $T_2$ must also abort. This is a **dirty read**. Strict 2PL would prevent this by holding $T_1$'s $X$-lock on $A$ until $T_1$ commits. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 State Transition Diagram of Basic 2PL The following Mermaid diagram models the lifecycle of a transaction obeying basic 2PL: ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> StartState StartState --> GrowingPhase : BEGIN TRANSACTION GrowingPhase : Acquires S / X locks GrowingPhase : No lock release allowed GrowingPhase --> LockPointState : Last lock acquired LockPointState : t_p = arg max |L(t)| LockPointState --> ShrinkingPhase : First lock released ShrinkingPhase : Releases S / X locks ShrinkingPhase : No new lock acquisitions ShrinkingPhase --> CommitState : All locks released + COMMIT ShrinkingPhase --> AbortState : ROLLBACK triggered CommitState --> [*] AbortState --> [*] ``` ## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — 2PL Variants ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph V0 [Basic 2PL] direction LR B_Grow[Growing Phase
Acquire locks] --> B_LP[Lock Point] B_LP --> B_Shrink[Shrinking Phase
Release locks freely] end subgraph V1 [Conservative 2PL] direction LR C_Pre[Pre-declare ALL locks
at start] --> C_Hold[Hold all locks] C_Hold --> C_End[Release all at commit / abort] end subgraph V2 [Strict 2PL] direction LR S_Grow[Growing Phase
Acquire S / X locks] --> S_LP[Lock Point] S_LP --> S_Shrink_S[Release S-locks freely] S_LP --> S_Hold_X[Hold X-locks until commit] S_Shrink_S --> S_Commit S_Hold_X --> S_Commit[COMMIT / ABORT] end subgraph V3 [Rigorous 2PL] direction LR R_Grow[Growing Phase
Acquire S / X locks] --> R_LP[Lock Point] R_LP --> R_Hold[Hold ALL locks S and X] R_Hold --> R_End[Release all at COMMIT / ABORT] end ``` ## 4.3 Lock Compatibility Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Transaction T_i requests LOCK on item Q] --> B{Item Q already locked?} B -- No --> C[Grant lock immediately] B -- Yes --> D{Is the request compatible?} D -- Yes --> C D -- No --> E{Is requestor willing to wait?} E -- Yes --> F[Add to wait queue for Q] E -- No --> G[Abort / error] F --> H[Lock released by holder] H --> I[Wake up and grant lock to next waiter] ``` ## 4.4 Cascading Abort Visualization (Basic 2PL Failure) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant T1 as T1 participant DB as Database participant T2 as T2 participant T3 as T3 T1->>DB: lock-S(A), read(A) T1->>DB: unlock(A) (enters shrinking phase) T2->>DB: lock-S(A), read(A) -- DIRTY READ T2->>DB: write(B) T1-->>DB: FAILURE / ABORT Note over T2: Must ABORT (cascading abort) T2-->>T3: Propagated failure Note over T3: T3 may also need to abort ``` > [!NOTE] > In **Strict 2PL**, $T_1$ would have **held its $X$-lock on $A$ until commit**, preventing $T_2$ from reading uncommitted data and eliminating the cascading abort chain. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the Two-Phase Locking (2PL) protocol. Explain the two phases with an example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Two-Phase Locking is a concurrency control protocol that ensures **conflict-serializability**. A transaction following 2PL is divided into two phases: 1. **Growing Phase:** The transaction acquires locks but does not release any. 2. **Shrinking Phase:** The transaction releases locks but cannot acquire any new ones. **Example:** Consider transaction $T$: - `lock-S(A)`, `read(A)` — *Growing* - `lock-X(B)`, `read(B)`, `write(B)` — *Growing* (lock point reached here) - `unlock(A)`, `unlock(B)` — *Shrinking* - `commit` The transition from *Growing* to *Shrinking* happens at the **lock point**, i.e., the moment just before the first unlock. This protocol guarantees serializability but does **not** guarantee freedom from deadlocks. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between Strict 2PL and Rigorous 2PL.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Strict 2PL | Rigorous 2PL | |---|---|---| | **Shared (S) locks** | Can be released after lock point | Held until commit / abort | | **Exclusive (X) locks** | Held until commit / abort | Held until commit / abort | | **Cascading aborts** | Avoided for writes only | Avoided completely | | **Recoverability** | Yes | Yes | Thus, Rigorous 2PL is a **stricter** version of Strict 2PL. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4] #### Part (a) [7 Marks] **Explain the Two-Phase Locking protocol. Prove that 2PL ensures conflict-serializability.** **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 marks):** The Two-Phase Locking (2PL) protocol is a locking scheme where each transaction issues lock and unlock requests in two distinct phases: - **Growing Phase:** New locks may be acquired, but none may be released. - **Shrinking Phase:** Existing locks may be released, but no new locks may be acquired. - The point of transition is the **Lock Point** $t_p$. **Proof of Conflict-Serializability (5 marks):** Assume, for contradiction, that the precedence graph $P(S)$ of a 2PL schedule $S$ contains a cycle $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow T_n \rightarrow T_1$. For each edge $T_i \rightarrow T_{i+1}$, the two transactions conflict on some data item $Q$. Under 2PL: - $T_i$ must hold its conflicting lock on $Q$ until $T_{i+1}$ acquires its lock. - Therefore, $T_i$'s **lock point** must occur strictly before $T_{i+1}$'s lock point. Chaining along the cycle: $$ t_p(T_1) < t_p(T_2) < t_p(T_3) < \dots < t_p(T_n) < t_p(T_1) $$ This is a strict total order cycle, which is impossible in $\mathbb{R}$. Contradiction. Therefore, the precedence graph is a **DAG**, and $S$ is conflict-serializable. $\blacksquare$ #### Part (b) [7 Marks] **Describe the Conservative, Strict, and Rigorous variations of 2PL. Compare their properties with respect to deadlocks and cascading aborts.** **Model Solution:** **Conservative 2PL (2.3 marks):** A transaction must acquire **all** locks it will ever need **before it begins execution**. It has an empty Growing Phase. Because no transaction ever holds a lock while waiting for another, **deadlocks are impossible**. However, it is often impractical because transactions rarely know all required items in advance. **Strict 2PL (2.3 marks):** A transaction must hold **all exclusive (write) locks** until it **commits or aborts**. Shared (read) locks may be released after the lock point. This prevents **dirty writes** and hence eliminates cascading aborts caused by write–read conflicts on uncommitted writes. **Rigorous 2PL (2.4 marks):** A transaction must hold **all locks (both S and X)** until it commits or aborts. This is the strictest variant. It completely eliminates cascading aborts and produces highly recoverable schedules, at the cost of reduced concurrency. **Comparison Table:** | Property | Conservative | Strict | Rigorous | |---|---|---|---| | Deadlock-free | ✔ Yes | ✘ No | ✘ No | | Cascading-abort-free | ✔ Yes | ✔ For writes | ✔ Fully | | Pre-declaration needed | ✔ Yes | ✘ No | ✘ No | --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 — Alternative] #### Part (a) [7 Marks] **Consider the following two transactions. Show a schedule that follows 2PL but results in a deadlock. Also show how Conservative 2PL would have avoided it.** | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | |---|---| | `read(A)` | `read(B)` | | `write(B)` | `write(A)` | **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — 2PL schedule leading to deadlock (4 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Time } t_1: T_1 \to \text{lock-S}(A); \text{read}(A) \\ &\text{Time } t_2: T_2 \to \text{lock-S}(B); \text{read}(B) \\ &\text{Time } t_3: T_1 \to \text{lock-X}(B) \;\; \rightarrow \textbf{BLOCKED} \;(B \text{ held by } T_2) \\ &\text{Time } t_4: T_2 \to \text{lock-X}(A) \;\; \rightarrow \textbf{BLOCKED} \;(A \text{ held by } T_1) \\ &\Rightarrow \text{Circular wait} \Rightarrow \textbf{DEADLOCK} \end{aligned} $$ The system would detect the deadlock and abort one transaction (e.g., $T_2$ rolled back as victim), wasting work. **Step 2 — Conservative 2PL avoidance (3 marks):** Under Conservative 2PL, both $T_1$ and $T_2$ would attempt to lock **both $A$ and $B$** at the very start: $$ T_1: \text{lock-S}(A), \text{lock-X}(B) \quad \text{vs} \quad T_2: \text{lock-S}(B), \text{lock-X}(A) $$ The first transaction to arrive would acquire **both** locks. The second would be **immediately blocked** before executing any step, since the conservative protocol prevents holding a lock while waiting for another. This ensures **no deadlock** can ever arise. **(7 marks)** #### Part (b) [7 Marks] **Illustrate with a schedule how basic 2PL can lead to a cascading abort, and explain how Strict 2PL prevents it.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Schedule with cascading abort under basic 2PL (4 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1: &\; T_1 \to \text{lock-X}(A),\; \text{write}(A) \\ t_2: &\; T_1 \to \text{unlock}(A) \quad \text{(enters shrinking phase)} \\ t_3: &\; T_2 \to \text{lock-S}(A),\; \text{read}(A) \quad \text{(DIRTY READ of uncommitted value)} \\ t_4: &\; T_2 \to \text{write}(B),\; \text{unlock}(A) \\ t_5: &\; T_2 \to \text{commit} \\ t_6: &\; T_1 \to \textbf{ABORT (failure)} \quad \Rightarrow T_2 \text{ must also be rolled back} \end{aligned} $$ $T_2$ read $A$ **before $T_1$ committed**, so $T_2$'s commit is invalid. The abort of $T_1$ forces $T_2$ to abort too — a classic **cascading abort**. **Step 2 — How Strict 2PL prevents this (3 marks):** Under Strict 2PL, $T_1$ would **not release its $X$-lock on $A$** at $t_2$. It would hold it until $T_1$ either commits or aborts. Therefore at $t_3$, $T_2$'s `lock-S(A)` request would **block**. $T_2$ would only proceed **after** $T_1$ commits successfully, ensuring it never reads uncommitted data. Cascading aborts are **eliminated** for write–read conflicts. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Strict vs Rigorous 2PL:** Many students write "Strict 2PL holds all locks till commit" — this is **wrong**. Strict 2PL holds **only exclusive (write) locks** till commit. Rigorous holds **all** locks. > 2. **Forgetting the lock point:** 2PL is not "acquire all → release all". There is a valid intermediate state where locks are held and partial releases are made — but only **after the lock point**. > 3. **Conflating 2PL with Serializability:** 2PL is a *sufficient* condition for serializability, not a *necessary* one. A non-2PL schedule may still be serializable. Examiners love this distinction. > 4. **Neglecting the proof:** KTU often awards partial credit (2–3 marks out of 7) for the proof that 2PL ensures serializability. Skipping the contradiction step is a common loss. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **2PL Rule:** *Grow* first (acquire locks), then *Shrink* (release locks) — no overlap. - ⭐ **Lock Point:** The moment the lock set is maximum; transition between phases. - ⭐ **2PL ⇒ Conflict-Serializable** (proven by contradiction on precedence graph). - ⭐ **2PL ≠ Deadlock-Free**: Basic 2PL can deadlock. - ⭐ **Conservative 2PL** ⇒ Pre-declares ALL locks → **No deadlock**, but needs *a priori* knowledge. - ⭐ **Strict 2PL** ⇒ Holds **X-locks** till commit → prevents cascading aborts on writes. - ⭐ **Rigorous 2PL** ⇒ Holds **S + X locks** till commit → fully prevents cascading aborts. - ⭐ **Lock Upgrade** (S → X) only in **Growing Phase**. - ⭐ **Lock Downgrade** (X → S) only in **Shrinking Phase**. - ⭐ **Compatibility:** Only **S + S** is allowed; all other pairs conflict. - ⭐ **Real-world use:** Strict 2PL is closest to **InnoDB** and **PostgreSQL** MVCC fallback. - ⭐ **Proof technique:** Contradiction via lock-point total ordering on a cycle. - ⭐ **Mnemonic:** *"Conservative = Pre-warn, Strict = Hold Writes, Rigorous = Hold Everything"*. ---

topics.title: System Log
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** System Log

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Log (Definition)**
> The **System Log** (also called the **Database Log** or **Transaction Log**) is a sequential, append-only file maintained by the DBMS on stable (non-volatile) storage that records every modification operation performed by transactions on the database. It serves as the authoritative historical record enabling **crash recovery**, **transaction atomicity**, and **durability** guarantees under the ARIES (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics) recovery framework.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus terminology, the system log is treated as the **single source of truth** for reconstructing the database state after a system failure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Bank Vault's Black Box Recorder**
> Imagine a bank vault where every transaction is recorded on a continuous roll of paper before any money is actually moved. This paper roll (the **System Log**) captures *who* (transaction ID), *what* they did (operation type), *what the balance was before* (before-image), and *what it became after* (after-image). If a robber crashes the vault door mid-transaction, the bank manager simply rewinds the paper to the last consistent point and replays valid operations to restore the correct balances.

- **Stable Storage** = The fireproof, waterproof tape archive where logs are stored
- **Before-Image (BFIM)** = Old value of the data item
- **After-Image (AFIM)** = New value of the data item
- **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)** = "Write on paper *before* you actually move the money"

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Standards

- **LSN (Log Sequence Number)**: A monotonically increasing integer assigned to every log record (typical metric: starts from **1** and increments by **1** per record).
- **Force-Writing**: The act of physically flushing a log page to stable storage.
- **Standard Log Block Size**: Typically **4 KB** to **8 KB** on commercial systems like PostgreSQL and Oracle.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: For KTU 2024, you are expected to know the **structure of log records**, the **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) protocol**, **deferred vs. immediate update techniques**, and **checkpointing mechanisms**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structure of a Log Record

Every entry in the system log follows a rigid tuple structure. The most common generalized form (used in ARIES) is:

$$\text{Log Record} = \langle LSN,\ Type,\ TID,\ prev\_LSN,\ undo\_next\_LSN,\ Data\_Item,\ BFIM,\ AFIM \rangle$$

Where:
- $LSN$ = Log Sequence Number (unique, monotonically increasing)
- $Type$ ∈ {BEGIN, UPDATE, COMMIT, ABORT, CHECKPOINT, COMPENSATION, END}
- $TID$ = Transaction Identifier
- $prev\_LSN$ = LSN of the previous log record for the same transaction
- $BFIM$ = Before-Image (old value)
- $AFIM$ = After-Image (new value)

### 2.2 Types of Log Records

The system log accommodates **6 distinct record types**, each serving a specific operational role:

1. **BEGIN [T]** — Marks the start of transaction $T$. Initialized when the transaction begins execution.
2. **UPDATE [T, X, old_val, new_val]** — Records that transaction $T$ changed data item $X$ from $old\_val$ to $new\_val$.
3. **COMMIT [T]** — Atomic declaration that transaction $T$ has successfully completed all operations.
4. **ABORT [T]** — Records that transaction $T$ has been rolled back; triggers undo propagation.
5. **CHECKPOINT** — Snapshot record of active transactions at a quiescent moment for recovery efficiency.
6. **COMPENSATION (CLR — Compensation Log Record)** — Synthetic record inserted during undo to record restoration actions.

### 2.3 The Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) Protocol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of WAL**
> *"Before any modified data block is flushed to disk, its corresponding log record MUST already be written to stable storage."*

This single invariant guarantees atomicity and durability.

### 2.4 Recovery Algorithm Variants

The KTU syllabus specifically emphasizes **three recovery paradigms**:

- **Deferred Update (No-Undo/Redo)**: All updates are first written to the log; the database itself is updated *only after* commit.
- **Immediate Update (Undo/Redo)**: Database is updated *during* transaction execution; log records both old and new values.
- **Immediate Update with No-Undo (Redo Only)**: Database updated during execution, but log only contains after-images.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Symbol** | **Formula or Rule** | **Application Domain** |
|:------------------------|:----------:|:-------------------|:-----------------------|
| Log Record Size | $S_{LR}$ | $S_{LR} = S_{header} + S_{BFIM} + S_{AFIM}$ | Disk space estimation |
| Number of Log Records | $N_{LR}$ | $N_{LR} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{UPDATE}_i + \text{OVERHEAD}_i)$ | Recovery cost |
| Checkpoint Frequency | $T_{CP}$ | $T_{CP} = \min(T_{min},\ t_{recovery})$ | Performance tuning |
| Undo Cost | $U(T)$ | $U(T) = \sum_{j \in T_{aborted}} \text{cost}(X_j)$ | Transaction abort |
| Redo Cost | $R(T)$ | $R(T) = \sum_{k \in T_{committed}} \text{cost}(X_k)$ | Crash recovery |
| WAL Constraint | $W_{forced}$ | $\text{Log\_flush}(L_i) \le \text{Data\_flush}(D_i)$ | Atomicity guarantee |
| Dirty Pages Table | $DPT$ | $DPT = \{P_i \vert \exists \text{ uncommitted write to } P_i\}$ | Recovery checkpoint |
| Active Transactions Table | $ATT$ | $ATT = \{T_j \vert T_j \text{ started but not ended}\}$ | Recovery checkpoint |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: The system log architecture is the foundational backbone of real-world systems including **Oracle's Redo Log**, **PostgreSQL's WAL files**, **MySQL's InnoDB Redo Logs**, and **Microsoft SQL Server's Transaction Log**. Every ACID-compliant DBMS in production today implements a variant of this structure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Log-Based Recovery Decision Logic

When the DBMS restarts after a crash, the recovery manager must classify each transaction as one of three categories. We derive the classification rule:

**Step 1: Identify log boundaries.**
At restart time, scan the log from the most recent checkpoint $C_k$ forward. Let $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$ be the transactions whose BEGIN appears in the scanned segment.

**Step 2: Trace the terminal event.**
For each transaction $T_i$, find its last log entry. There are only two terminal possibilities: COMMIT or ABORT. If neither exists, the transaction was *active* at crash time.

**Step 3: Apply the classification function.**
Let $f(T_i)$ be the classification function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(T_i) &= \begin{cases}
\text{WINNER (Redo required)} & \text{if } \exists \ \text{COMMIT}[T_i] \in \text{Log after } C_k \\
\text{LOSER (Undo required)} & \text{if } \exists \ \text{ABORT}[T_i] \in \text{Log after } C_k \\
\text{IN-DOUBT (Undo required)} & \text{if neither COMMIT nor ABORT found}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Justification of the rule.**
The reasoning is logically airtight:
- A committed transaction has *legally* modified the database; its changes must survive (Durability).
- An aborted transaction must have its changes reversed (Atomicity).
- A transaction with no terminal record crashed mid-execution; under the principle of *precaution*, it is conservatively treated as uncommitted and undone.

### 3.2 Worked Example: WAL Enforcement Derivation

**Problem Setup:** A transaction $T$ executes:
$$T: \text{READ}(A,\ t_1);\ A := A - 100;\ \text{WRITE}(A,\ t_2);\ \text{READ}(B,\ t_3);\ B := B + 100;\ \text{WRITE}(B,\ t_4);\ \text{COMMIT}$$

Assume $A$ resides on page $P_A$ and $B$ resides on page $P_B$. The DBMS buffer pool initially holds no dirty pages.

**Step 1: At $t_2$, transaction $T$ modifies $A$ from $A_0$ to $A_1$.**
The log record is written:
$$\text{UPDATE}[T,\ A,\ A_0,\ A_1]$$
Page $P_A$ is now dirty in the buffer.

**Step 2: Apply WAL constraint.**
$$\text{If } \text{flush}(P_A) \rightarrow \text{must have } \text{Log\_flush}(\text{UPDATE}[T,A,A_0,A_1]) \le \text{flush}(P_A)$$

**Step 3: At $t_4$, similar logic applies to $B$.**
Log record $\text{UPDATE}[T,\ B,\ B_0,\ B_1]$ must be on stable storage *before* page $P_B$ is flushed.

**Step 4: At COMMIT, log record $\text{COMMIT}[T]$ is force-written to disk.**

**Analytical Result**: The WAL protocol ensures that for *every* data page reaching disk, a recovery-capable log record has *already* reached disk. This is the formal proof of atomicity under crash scenarios.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Deferred vs. Immediate Update

| **Property** | **Deferred Update** | **Immediate Update** |
|:-------------|:-------------------|:---------------------|
| When DB is modified | Only after COMMIT | During transaction |
| Log contents | After-images only | Both before- and after-images |
| Recovery steps | REDO only | UNDO + REDO |
| Concurrency overhead | Higher (locks held longer) | Lower |
| Disk I/O during normal execution | Lower | Higher |
| Use case | Batch processing | Online Transaction Processing (OLTP) |
| Example system | Early IMS prototypes | Modern PostgreSQL, Oracle |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Idempotence of Redo Operation

**Theorem**: For any log record $L_i = \text{UPDATE}[T,\ X,\ v_{old},\ v_{new}]$, applying REDO multiple times yields the same result.

**Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State}_0 &\rightarrow \text{Apply REDO}(L_i) \rightarrow \text{State}_1 \text{ where } X = v_{new} \\
\text{State}_1 &\rightarrow \text{Apply REDO}(L_i) \rightarrow \text{State}_2 \text{ where } X = v_{new} \\
\text{Therefore: } \text{State}_1 &= \text{State}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning**: Since REDO sets $X := v_{new}$ unconditionally and $X$ already equals $v_{new}$ after the first application, subsequent applications are no-ops. This idempotence is *critical* because recovery may re-execute log records multiple times due to repeated crashes during recovery itself.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: System Log Architecture and Recovery Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Transaction T Begins] --> nodeB[BEGIN Log Record]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Read Data Item X]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Modify X in Buffer Pool]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Generate UPDATE Log Record]
    nodeE --> nodeF{WAL Check: Log on Disk?}
    nodeF -->|No| nodeG[Force-Write Log to Stable Storage]
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeH[Mark Page Dirty in Buffer]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI{All Operations Done?}
    nodeI -->|No| nodeC
    nodeI -->|Yes| nodeJ[COMMIT Log Record]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Force-Write COMMIT to Disk]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Transaction T Committed Successfully]

    nodeM[System Crash Detected] --> nodeN[Locate Last Checkpoint Ck]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Scan Log Forward from Ck]
    nodeO --> nodeP{Classify Transactions}
    nodeP -->|COMMIT Found| nodeQ[WINNER - Apply REDO]
    nodeP -->|ABORT Found| nodeR[LOSER - Apply UNDO]
    nodeP -->|No Terminal| nodeS[IN-DOUBT - Apply UNDO]
    nodeQ --> nodeT[Database State Restored]
    nodeR --> nodeT
    nodeS --> nodeT
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Log Manager Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Transaction Manager] -->|Issues WRITE op| nodeB[Log Manager]
    nodeB -->|Construct Log Record| nodeC[Log Buffer in RAM]
    nodeC -->|Force Write on Commit| nodeD[Stable Storage - Disk]
    nodeD -->|Read on Recovery| nodeE[Recovery Manager]
    nodeE -->|REDO List| nodeF[Winner Transactions]
    nodeE -->|UNDO List| nodeG[Loser Transactions]
    nodeF -->|Re-apply AFIM| nodeH[Database Buffer Pool]
    nodeG -->|Write CLR + Restore BFIM| nodeH
    nodeH -->|Flush Dirty Pages| nodeI[Database on Disk]
    nodeJ[Checkpoint Daemon] -->|Periodically| nodeB
    nodeJ -->|Snapshot ATT and DPT| nodeD
```

### 4.3 Log Record Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Event in DBMS] --> nodeB{Event Type}
    nodeB -->|Transaction Start| nodeC[Log: BEGIN T]
    nodeB -->|Data Modification| nodeD[Log: UPDATE T X old new]
    nodeB -->|Successful End| nodeE[Log: COMMIT T]
    nodeB -->|Unsuccessful End| nodeF[Log: ABORT T]
    nodeB -->|System Snapshot| nodeG[Log: CHECKPOINT]
    nodeB -->|Undo Operation| nodeH[Log: CLR T X new]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the System Log. List any four types of log records used in transaction processing.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **System Log** is a sequential, append-only file maintained on stable (non-volatile) storage that records all transaction operations and their effects, enabling crash recovery and atomicity guarantees. **(1 mark)**

The four types of log records are:
1. **BEGIN [T]** — Marks the start of transaction $T$ **(0.5 mark)**
2. **UPDATE [T, X, old_val, new_val]** — Records modification of data item $X$ **(0.5 mark)**
3. **COMMIT [T]** — Signals successful completion of $T$ **(0.5 mark)**
4. **ABORT [T]** — Signals termination of $T$ due to failure or explicit rollback **(0.5 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) protocol. Why is it essential for crash recovery?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**WAL Protocol Statement:** Before a modified data block is written from the buffer pool to the database on disk, the corresponding log record MUST have already been written to stable storage. **(2 marks)**

**Essentiality for Crash Recovery:** WAL ensures that for any data modification reflected on disk, a recovery-actionable log record is also available on disk. This guarantees that during recovery, the system can either REDO committed changes or UNDO uncommitted changes — the two fundamental recovery operations. Without WAL, the database could contain changes that have no corresponding log record, making atomic restoration impossible. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

*(a)* Explain the structure of a log record in detail. Discuss the role of the Log Sequence Number (LSN) and the `prev_LSN` field in transaction recovery. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* With a suitable example, describe the **Immediate Update** recovery technique using log records. How does it differ from the **Deferred Update** technique? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Structure of a Log Record:**

A generalized log record (used in ARIES-style systems) contains the following fields:

$$\text{Log Record} = \langle LSN,\ TID,\ prev\_LSN,\ Type,\ PageID,\ Data\_Item,\ BFIM,\ AFIM \rangle$$

- **LSN (Log Sequence Number)**: A unique monotonically increasing identifier assigned to every log record at the moment of creation. **(1 mark)**
- **TID (Transaction ID)**: Identifier of the transaction that generated this record. **(1 mark)**
- **prev_LSN**: Pointer to the LSN of the previous log record belonging to the same transaction, forming a per-transaction linked list. **(1 mark)**
- **Type**: BEGIN, UPDATE, COMMIT, ABORT, or COMPENSATION. **(1 mark)**
- **BFIM / AFIM**: Before-image and after-image of the modified data item. **(1 mark)**

**Role of LSN in Recovery:**
The LSN serves as the chronological anchor for the log. During recovery, the recovery manager scans logs in LSN order to reconstruct a serial history. Each data page in the buffer pool maintains a `pageLSN` field; only log records with LSN $\le$ `pageLSN` are guaranteed to have been applied. **(1 mark)**

**Role of prev_LSN:**
The `prev_LSN` field enables efficient backward traversal during UNDO operations. Instead of scanning the entire log to find a transaction's previous updates, the recovery manager simply follows the `prev_LSN` chain, dramatically reducing undo cost from $O(N)$ to $O(k)$ where $k$ is the number of updates per transaction. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Immediate Update Technique — Example:**

Consider transaction $T$ with initial values $A = 1000$, $B = 500$:

$$T: \text{READ}(A);\ A := A - 100;\ \text{WRITE}(A);\ \text{READ}(B);\ B := B + 100;\ \text{WRITE}(B);\ \text{COMMIT}$$

**Execution Trace with Immediate Update:**

| **Step** | **Action** | **Database State** | **Log Record** |
|:--------:|:-----------|:-------------------|:---------------|
| 1 | READ A | $A=1000$ | — |
| 2 | $A := A - 100$ | $A=900$ (buffer) | — |
| 3 | WRITE A | $A=900$ (DB + buffer) | UPDATE[T, A, 1000, 900] |
| 4 | READ B | $B=500$ | — |
| 5 | $B := B + 100$ | $B=600$ (buffer) | — |
| 6 | WRITE B | $B=600$ (DB + buffer) | UPDATE[T, B, 500, 600] |
| 7 | COMMIT | — | COMMIT[T] |

**(3 marks for the table and explanation)**

**Recovery Procedure for Immediate Update:**
On crash, the recovery manager:
1. Scans log backward from the end.
2. Identifies LOSER transactions (no COMMIT) → applies **UNDO** using before-images.
3. Identifies WINNER transactions (COMMIT found) → applies **REDO** using after-images from the last checkpoint.

**(2 marks for recovery procedure)**

**Difference from Deferred Update:**

| **Aspect** | **Immediate Update** | **Deferred Update** |
|:-----------|:--------------------|:--------------------|
| DB modification timing | During transaction | Only after COMMIT |
| Log contents | Before- and after-images | Only after-images |
| Recovery operations | UNDO + REDO | REDO only |

**(2 marks for comparison)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper]**

*(a)* Explain the **Checkpoint** mechanism in log-based recovery. Differentiate between **transaction-consistent checkpoints** and **action-consistent checkpoints**. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A transaction $T_1$ performs the following sequence of operations: $T_1$: READ(A), A := A × 2, WRITE(A), READ(B), B := B × 3, WRITE(B), COMMIT. Given initial values A = 100 and B = 50, write down the complete log sequence and describe the recovery process if a system crash occurs **immediately after the WRITE(A) operation**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Checkpoint Mechanism:**

A checkpoint is a synchronization point in the log that allows the recovery manager to bound the scope of recovery work. Instead of scanning the entire log from the beginning of time after every crash, the recovery starts from the most recent checkpoint. **(2 marks)**

**Transaction-Consistent Checkpoint:**

In this approach, the DBMS waits until **all currently active transactions have either committed or aborted** before writing the CHECKPOINT record. This ensures that at the checkpoint moment, no transaction is in-flight. The log contains a list of active transactions (ATT) at checkpoint time.

**Advantages:** Simpler recovery logic.
**Disadvantages:** May delay checkpoint execution significantly if long transactions are active. **(2 marks)**

**Action-Consistent Checkpoint (Fuzzy Checkpoint):**

In this approach, the DBMS does *not* wait for active transactions. Instead, it captures:
1. The **Active Transactions Table (ATT)** — list of currently running transactions.
2. The **Dirty Pages Table (DPT)** — list of buffer pages modified but not yet flushed.

This allows checkpoints to occur frequently without blocking the system. Modern systems like ARIES employ fuzzy checkpoints. **(2 marks)**

**Comparison Summary:**

| **Property** | **Transaction-Consistent** | **Action-Consistent** |
|:-------------|:---------------------------|:----------------------|
| Active transactions allowed | No (all completed) | Yes (in-flight allowed) |
| Checkpoint frequency | Lower | Higher |
| Recovery complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Used in | Older systems | Modern ARIES-based systems |

**(1 mark for comparison table)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Initial State:** $A = 100$, $B = 50$

**Complete Log Sequence:**

| **Step** | **Operation** | **Log Record** |
|:--------:|:-------------|:---------------|
| 1 | BEGIN | BEGIN[T₁] |
| 2 | READ(A) | — |
| 3 | $A := A \times 2$ | — |
| 4 | WRITE(A) | UPDATE[T₁, A, 100, 200] |
| *CRASH* | | |

**(3 marks for log sequence construction)**

**Recovery Process After Crash:**

Since the crash occurred *after* WRITE(A) but *before* COMMIT, transaction $T_1$ is classified as a **LOSER** (or IN-DOUBT) transaction.

**Step 1:** Identify terminal status of $T_1$.
- No COMMIT record found → $T_1$ is a LOSER. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Apply UNDO operation.
- Read the UPDATE[T₁, A, 100, 200] record backwards (using prev_LSN chain).
- Extract the before-image $BFIM = 100$.
- Restore $A := 100$ in the database. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Write a Compensation Log Record.
- Insert CLR[T₁, A, 100] into the log to record the undo action. This is idempotent and prevents re-undo during a subsequent crash. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Append ABORT record.
- Write ABORT[T₁] to finalize the rollback. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 5:** Write END record.
- Write END[T₁] to mark transaction lifecycle completion. **(0.5 mark)**

**Final Recovered State:** $A = 100$, $B = 50$ (unchanged), and $T_1$ is rolled back to its original logical starting point.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning**
> - **Common Mistake #1**: Students often write only the after-image (new value) in the log record, forgetting the before-image. Both are required for UNDO capability. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #2**: Confusing *CHECKPOINT* with *COMMIT*. A checkpoint is a *system-level* snapshot record; it does not commit any transaction.
> - **Common Mistake #3**: In recovery algorithm questions, students forget to write the **ABORT** and **END** log records after UNDO. The CLR must be followed by ABORT[T] and END[T] to formally close the transaction lifecycle.
> - **Common Mistake #4**: Students often state WAL as *"write log before writing data"* but omit the critical clause *"to STABLE STORAGE"*. The log must reach non-volatile storage, not merely the in-memory log buffer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **System Log** = Sequential, append-only file on stable storage recording all DB modifications.
- ✅ **Log Record Format**: `[LSN, prev_LSN, TID, Type, BFIM, AFIM]` (ARIES generalized form).
- ✅ **Log Record Types**: BEGIN, UPDATE, COMMIT, ABORT, CHECKPOINT, CLR (6 types).
- ✅ **WAL Rule**: *Log record MUST be on stable storage BEFORE its corresponding data page is flushed to disk.*
- ✅ **LSN**: Monotonically increasing; uniquely identifies each log record chronologically.
- ✅ **prev_LSN**: Enables backward traversal during UNDO without full log scan.
- ✅ **Three Recovery Paradigms**:
   - **Deferred Update** → REDO only
   - **Immediate Update** → UNDO + REDO
   - **Immediate Update (No-Undo)** → REDO only
- ✅ **Recovery Classification**:
   - **WINNER** (COMMIT found) → Apply REDO with AFIM
   - **LOSER** (ABORT found) → Apply UNDO with BFIM
   - **IN-DOUBT** (no terminal) → Apply UNDO conservatively
- ✅ **Checkpoint Types**: Transaction-consistent (waits for active Tx) vs. Action-consistent / Fuzzy (snapshots ATT + DPT).
- ✅ **CLR (Compensation Log Record)**: Synthetic log record inserted during UNDO to ensure idempotent recovery.
- ✅ **REDO is Idempotent**: Repeated application yields the same result (critical for nested crashes during recovery).
- ✅ **PageLSN Field**: Each data page stores the LSN of the latest log record applied to it.
- ✅ **Dirty Pages Table (DPT)**: Tracked at checkpoint time to identify uncommitted buffer modifications.
- ✅ **Active Transactions Table (ATT)**: Tracked at checkpoint time to identify in-flight transactions.

> **Final Exam Tip**: When asked to "explain recovery," always structure your answer in **three phases**: *(1) Analysis Phase* (scan log + classify transactions), *(2) Redo Phase* (reapply winners from checkpoint), *(3) Undo Phase* (reverse losers backward to beginning). KTU examiners award full marks only when all three phases are explicitly mentioned.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
