topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Log Based Recovery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Log-Based Recovery (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> **Log-Based Recovery** is a fundamental database recovery technique that uses a sequential file called the **log** (also known as the *journal* or *write-ahead log*) to record every modification made to the database. The system guarantees that the database can be restored to a consistent state after failures (transaction, system, or media) by replaying or undoing operations from this log.

The log is stored on **stable storage** — non-volatile storage that is expected to survive system crashes. Each log record is typically of the form:

$$ \langle \text{Transaction\_ID}, \text{Data\_Item\_ID}, \text{Old\_Value}, \text{New\_Value}, \text{Additional\_Info} \rangle $$

## 1.2 Why Log-Based Recovery is Needed

A database system must guarantee the **ACID properties** — **Atomicity**, **Consistency**, **Isolation**, and **Durability**. When a failure occurs:

- **Transaction Failure**: A transaction must either commit fully or leave no trace.
- **System Crash**: The DBMS must recover the database to the last consistent state.
- **Media Failure**: Disk corruption requires restoration from archive + log replay.

Without logs, the system has no "memory" of partial changes, leading to inconsistency, lost updates, or data corruption.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Ledger

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Analogy: The Bank Cashier's Logbook**
> Imagine a bank teller who processes withdrawals and deposits. Every single transaction — *"Mr. A withdrew ₹5,000 from Account #1023"* — is written in a physical logbook **before** touching the actual ledger. The logbook entry contains:
> - **Who**: Transaction ID (Mr. A)
> - **What**: Data item (Account #1023)
> - **Before**: Old value (₹10,000)
> - **After**: New value (₹5,000)
> - **Timestamp**: When it happened
>
> If the power goes out midway, the teller re-opens the logbook. If the transaction was fully logged but not yet committed, the old balance is restored (**UNDO**). If the transaction committed but the ledger update was lost, the change is re-applied (**REDO**). The logbook is the *single source of truth*.

## 1.4 Core Components of the Log

| Component | Description | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Log Record** | Tuple describing a single DB operation | Atomic change tracking |
| **Stable Storage** | Non-volatile, crash-resistant media | Log persistence |
| **Buffer / Cache** | In-memory log buffer flushed periodically | Performance optimization |
| **Log Sequence Number (LSN)** | Monotonically increasing ID for each log record | Ordering of events |
| **Checkpoint** | Periodic synchronization marker | Bounding recovery time |

> [!NOTE] **Standard Performance Metric**
> For KTU examinations, remember the key standard: A transaction is considered **committed** only when **both** its log records and the commit marker have been **flushed to stable storage**. The typical minimum log flush unit is **4 KB** (one disk block), as defined in the ARIES recovery paper.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) Protocol

The cornerstone of log-based recovery is the **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)** protocol, which consists of **two inviolable rules**:

> [!IMPORTANT] **WAL Rule 1 — Log Before Data**
> The log record for a data modification must be **flushed to stable storage BEFORE** the corresponding data block is written to disk.
>
> *Reason*: If a crash occurs after the data is written but before the log is saved, the recovery system has no record of the change, leaving the database inconsistent.

> [!IMPORTANT] **WAL Rule 2 — Commit Log Last**
> All log records pertaining to a transaction (including its `COMMIT` marker) must be written to stable storage **BEFORE** the transaction is officially declared committed.

These two rules together guarantee that the log contains enough information to either **UNDO** uncommitted changes or **REDO** committed changes after any failure.

## 2.2 Types of Log Records

| Log Record Type | Format | When Written |
|---|---|---|
| **Update Log Record** | `<Ti, X, V_old, V_new>` | Before/after modifying item `X` |
| **Commit Record** | `<Ti commit>` | When transaction `Ti` commits |
| **Abort Record** | `<Ti abort>` | When transaction `Ti` is rolled back |
| **Begin Transaction** | `<Ti start>` | At the start of `Ti` |
| **Checkpoint Record** | `<checkpoint {T1, T2, ...}>` | Periodically by the system |

Where:
- $T_i$ = Transaction identifier
- $X$ = Data item being modified
- $V_{old}$ = Value of $X$ before modification
- $V_{new}$ = Value of $X$ after modification

## 2.3 Deferred vs. Immediate Update — The Two Strategies

### 2.3.1 Deferred Update (No-UNDO Algorithm)
- The database is **physically updated only after** the transaction reaches its `COMMIT` point.
- During execution, all updates are recorded in the log and applied to a **local workspace**.
- On commit, the actual data items on disk are updated.
- **Recovery**: Only `REDO` operations for committed transactions are needed; `UNDO` is unnecessary.

### 2.3.2 Immediate Update (UNDO/REDO Algorithm)
- Database items are **updated immediately**, even before commit.
- Both old and new values are logged.
- On commit, only the `COMMIT` log record needs to be flushed.
- **Recovery**: `UNDO` uncommitted transactions, then `REDO` committed transactions.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **High-Yield Recovery Formulas and Concepts**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation / Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Log Record Tuple** | $\langle T_i, X, V_{old}, V_{new} \rangle$ | Standard log structure |
| **LSN Increment** | $LSN_{new} = LSN_{old} + 1$ | Monotonic integer counter |
| **Log Flush Condition (WAL)** | $\text{flush}(log) \rightarrow \text{write}(data)$ | Strict ordering |
| **Recovery Redo Cost** | $C_{redo} = \sum_{i \in Committed} \vert T_i \vert$ | Sum of operations per tx |
| **Recovery Undo Cost** | $C_{undo} = \sum_{j \in Uncommitted} \vert T_j \vert$ | Sum of operations per tx |
| **Checkpoint Frequency Trade-off** | $f_c = \dfrac{T_{recovery}}{T_{log\_growth}}$ | Higher freq ⇒ less redo, more overhead |
| **Stable Storage Write Time** | $T_{stable} = 2 \cdot T_{disk}$ | Two-disk mirror technique |
| **ARIES LSN Propagation** | $LSN_{page} \geq LSN_{log\_rec}$ | Page LSN invariant |
| **Transaction States Count** | $\vert S \vert = \vert Active \vert + \vert Committed \vert + \vert Aborted \vert$ | Mutually exclusive states |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Notation Note**
> In KTU answer sheets, you will typically see $|T_i|$ denoting the **number of log records for transaction $T_i$**, and $|S|$ denoting the **cardinality of a set $S$** (e.g., active transactions). For absolute value expressions in the formulas above, the symbol is preserved as $\vert \cdot \vert$ for clarity.

## 2.5 The Checkpoint Mechanism

A **checkpoint** is a synchronization point that limits the amount of work the recovery algorithm must perform after a crash. The procedure is:

1. **Suspend** all current transactions temporarily.
2. **Flush** all log records currently in the memory buffer to stable storage.
3. **Flush** all modified database buffers to disk.
4. **Write** a `<checkpoint L>` record, where $L$ is the list of active transactions.
5. **Resume** transaction processing.

After a crash, recovery starts from the **most recent checkpoint**, not from the beginning of the log — drastically reducing recovery time.

## 2.6 The ARIES Recovery Algorithm (Brief)

> [!NOTE] **ARIES = Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics**

ARIES operates in **three passes** over the log:

1. **Analysis Pass**: Scans the log forward from the last checkpoint to identify the set of transactions to be undone and to determine which pages are dirty.
2. **REDO Pass**: Repeats history by reapplying all updates (including those of uncommitted transactions) to bring the database to the exact state at the time of the crash.
3. **UNDO Pass**: Rolls back all transactions that were active at crash time, following their `prevLSN` chains in **reverse chronological order**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why ARIES is Industry Standard**
> ARIES is used in **IBM DB2, Microsoft SQL Server, Oracle**, and **PostgreSQL** because it is **steal/no-force** — it allows the buffer manager to write uncommitted pages to disk and does not require all committed pages to be flushed at commit time. This gives maximum performance while preserving correctness through the WAL protocol.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Full Recovery Scenario

> [!NOTE] **Worked-Out Example (KTU-Style Analytical Problem)**
> *Given the following log for transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3$ at the time of system crash, determine which transactions need to be UNDOed and which need to be REDOne. Assume a strict WAL protocol with immediate update.*

### Log (in chronological order):

```
<checkpoint {T2, T3}>
<T1, start>
<T1, A, 1000, 950>
<T2, B, 2000, 2050>
<T1, commit>
<T3, C, 500, 450>
<T2, commit>
<T3, D, 800, 700>
<system crash>
```

### Step 1 — Identify Active vs. Committed at the Last Checkpoint

At the checkpoint `<checkpoint {T2, T3}>`, the active set was $\{T_2, T_3\}$.

$$ \text{Active}_{\text{checkpoint}} = \{ T_2, T_3 \} $$

This means: **Recovery starts at this checkpoint**. Any transactions that committed *before* this checkpoint are already reflected in the database on disk and can be ignored.

### Step 2 — Scan Forward from Checkpoint to Locate Commits

Continuing the scan:

- $T_1$ started after the checkpoint.
- $T_1$ wrote a log record `<T1, A, 1000, 950>`.
- $T_1$ committed: `<T1, commit>`.

$$
\Rightarrow T_1 \in \text{Committed Set}
$$

- $T_2$ was active at checkpoint.
- $T_2$ wrote: `<T2, B, 2000, 2050>`.
- $T_2$ committed: `<T2, commit>`.

$$
\Rightarrow T_2 \in \text{Committed Set}
$$

- $T_3$ was active at checkpoint.
- $T_3$ wrote: `<T3, C, 500, 450>` and `<T3, D, 800, 700>`.
- $T_3$ did **NOT** commit before the crash.

$$
\Rightarrow T_3 \in \text{Uncommitted Set}
$$

### Step 3 — Formally Define the Recovery Sets

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{REDO\_Set} &= \{ T_i \mid T_i \text{ committed AFTER the last checkpoint} \} \\
&= \{ T_1, T_2 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UNDO\_Set} &= \{ T_i \mid T_i \text{ active at crash time, no commit record} \} \\
&= \{ T_3 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Execute REDO on Committed Transactions

For each committed transaction, reapply updates in **log order**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{REDO: } & T_1: \text{Set } A = 950 \\
\text{REDO: } & T_2: \text{Set } B = 2050
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Execute UNDO on Uncommitted Transactions

For each uncommitted transaction, reverse updates in **reverse log order**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UNDO: } & T_3: \text{Set } D = 800 \quad (\text{restore from } 700 \text{ to } 800) \\
\text{UNDO: } & T_3: \text{Set } C = 500 \quad (\text{restore from } 450 \text{ to } 500) \\
\text{Write: } & \langle T_3, \text{abort} \rangle \text{ to the log}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow at Each Log Record

| Log Record Seen | Decision | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| `<Ti, start>` | Add $T_i$ to Active set | Transaction in progress |
| `<Ti, X, V_old, V_new>` | If $T_i$ active: keep in UNDO plan; if later commit: keep in REDO plan | Track for recovery |
| `<Ti, commit>` | Move $T_i$ from Active to Committed set | Transaction succeeded |
| `<Ti, abort>` | Move $T_i$ from Active to Aborted set (no action needed if no writes on disk) | Transaction rolled back |
| `<checkpoint L>` | New scan start point; remember list $L$ of active tx | Bound recovery work |

## 3.3 Derivation: Why the Log Must Be Flushed BEFORE the Data Block

Assume the inverse: the data block is written **before** the log record. Consider a transaction $T$ that modifies item $X$:

$$
\text{Time } t_0: \text{T modifies } X \text{ in memory}, \quad X_{new} = 50, \quad X_{old} = 100
$$

$$
\text{Time } t_1: \text{Data block for } X \text{ flushed to disk}, \quad X_{disk} = 50
$$

$$
\text{Time } t_2: \text{SYSTEM CRASH — log record for } T \text{ was still in memory buffer}
$$

**After crash**: Disk shows $X = 50$, but the log has no record of this change. The recovery system has no way to:
- Know that $X$ was changed
- Know that $T$ was the cause
- Know the old value was 100

This is a **lost update / inconsistent state** — exactly the failure WAL prevents.

> [!IMPORTANT] **The WAL Invariant (Formal)**
> For every data block $B$ written to disk, the log must contain **all** log records for changes to $B$, and these records must be on stable storage **before** $B$ is written.
>
> In ARIES terms: $\forall \text{ page } P \text{ on disk}: \text{pageLSN}(P) \leq \text{flushedLSN}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Log-Based Recovery — System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T[Transaction Ti] --> W[Write Operation on X]
    W --> LB[In-Memory Log Buffer]
    W --> DB[In-Memory Data Buffer]
    LB -->|Flush on commit or before data write| SL[(Stable Storage Log)]
    DB -->|Flush periodically| SD[(Stable Storage Database)]
    SL --> RR[Recovery Manager]
    SD --> RR
    RR --> RP[Recovery Process]
    RP --> U[UNDO Uncommitted Tx]
    RP --> R[REDO Committed Tx]
    CP[Checkpoint Process] --> SL
    CP --> SD
```

## 4.2 Write-Ahead Logging — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transaction Wants to Modify X] --> B{Log Record Written?}
    B -->|No| C[Write Log Record to Memory Buffer]
    C --> D{Buffer Full or Commit?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Flush Log Buffer to Stable Storage]
    D -->|No| F[Wait]
    B -->|Yes| G{Log Flushed to Stable Storage?}
    G -->|No| E
    G -->|Yes| H[Write Data Block to Disk]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Update PageLSN]
    I --> J[Modification Complete]
```

## 4.3 Recovery Algorithm — Three-Phase Execution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[System Crash Detected] --> P1[Phase 1: Analysis]
    P1 --> P2[Phase 2: REDO]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: UNDO]
    P3 --> R[Database Restored to Consistent State]
    
    P1 -.Identifies.-> UT[Uncommitted Tx List]
    P1 -.Identifies.-> CT[Committed Tx List]
    P2 -.Uses.-> CT
    P3 -.Uses.-> UT
```

## 4.4 Transaction State Diagram with Log Markers

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active: start log record
    Active --> PartiallyCommitted: last statement executed
    PartiallyCommitted --> Committed: commit log record flushed
    PartiallyCommitted --> Aborted: rollback initiated
    Active --> Aborted: abort log record written
    Committed --> [*]
    Aborted --> [*]
```

## 4.5 Deferred vs. Immediate Update — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DeferredUpdate["Deferred Update (No-UNDO)"]
        D1[Transaction Start] --> D2[All Updates Go to Local Workspace + Log]
        D2 --> D3[Commit Point Reached]
        D3 --> D4[Flush Log to Stable Storage]
        D4 --> D5[Apply Updates to Actual Database]
        D5 --> D6[Transaction Committed]
    end
    
    subgraph ImmediateUpdate["Immediate Update (UNDO/REDO)"]
        I1[Transaction Start] --> I2[Update Database Buffers Immediately + Log]
        I2 --> I3[Modified Pages May Be Written to Disk Before Commit]
        I3 --> I4[Commit Point Reached]
        I4 --> I5[Flush Commit Log to Stable Storage]
        I5 --> I6[Transaction Committed]
    end
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) protocol. State its two essential rules.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Write-Ahead Logging is a fundamental protocol used in log-based recovery that ensures the log contains sufficient information to recover the database after any failure.

**Rule 1 (Log before data)**: The log record for a data modification must be written to stable storage *before* the modified data block itself is written to disk.

**Rule 2 (Commit log last)**: All log records for a transaction, including its `<Ti commit>` record, must be flushed to stable storage *before* the transaction is declared committed.

These rules together guarantee that recovery is always possible via UNDO and/or REDO operations.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between deferred update and immediate update recovery techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Deferred Update | Immediate Update |
|---|---|---|
| **Update Timing** | Database updated only after commit | Database updated as transaction runs |
| **Recovery Need** | Only REDO required | Both UNDO and REDO required |
| **Disk I/O** | Delayed; lower during execution | Immediate; higher during execution |
| **Log Content** | Only new values | Both old and new values |
| **Use Case** | Low-write, simple systems | High-performance, complex systems |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of a log record with a suitable example. List the different types of log records used in a database recovery system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following log sequence at the time of a system crash:
```
<T1, start>
<T1, A, 1000, 900>
<T2, start>
<T2, B, 500, 600>
<T1, commit>
<T3, start>
<T3, C, 700, 650>
<T2, commit>
<T3, D, 400, 350>
<system crash>
```
Identify which transactions need to be REDOed and which need to be UNDOed. Show the recovery procedure step by step. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Log Record Structure (7 Marks):**

A log record is a structured tuple written for every database operation. The general format is:

$$ \langle T_i, \text{operation\_type}, \text{data\_item}, \text{old\_value}, \text{new\_value} \rangle $$

**Types of Log Records:**

1. **Start Record** — `<Ti, start>`: Marks the beginning of transaction $T_i$.
2. **Update Record** — `<Ti, X, V_old, V_new>`: Records a modification of item $X$ by $T_i$.
3. **Commit Record** — `<Ti, commit>`: Indicates successful completion of $T_i$.
4. **Abort Record** — `<Ti, abort>`: Indicates $T_i$ was rolled back.
5. **Checkpoint Record** — `<checkpoint {T1, T2, ...}>`: Marks a synchronization point listing active transactions.
6. **End Record** — `<Ti, end>`: Written after all actions of $T_i$ are complete (optional in some implementations).

**Example**: For a transaction $T_5$ updating item `Balance` of account `A100` from ₹5000 to ₹4500, the log record is:
$$ \langle T_5, A100, 5000, 4500 \rangle $$

---

**(b) Recovery Procedure (7 Marks):**

**Step 1**: Scan the log to determine transaction states.

| Transaction | Start? | Commit? | Status at Crash |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | Yes | Yes | **Committed** |
| $T_2$ | Yes | Yes | **Committed** |
| $T_3$ | Yes | No | **Uncommitted** |

**Step 2**: Form the recovery sets.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{REDO\_Set} &= \{ T_1, T_2 \} \\
\text{UNDO\_Set} &= \{ T_3 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — REDO Phase**: Reapply all updates of committed transactions in log order:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: A = 900 \\
T_2 &: B = 600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — UNDO Phase**: Reverse all updates of uncommitted transaction in **reverse log order**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 &: D = 400 \quad (\text{restore from } 350) \\
T_3 &: C = 700 \quad (\text{restore from } 650)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5**: Write `<T3, abort>` record to the log to mark the end of the rollback.

**Final Database State After Recovery:**

$$ A = 900, \quad B = 600, \quad C = 700, \quad D = 400 $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the role of checkpoints in log-based recovery. Explain the steps involved in performing a checkpoint. Why are checkpoints necessary in real-world database systems? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the three-phase recovery procedure of the ARIES algorithm. Mention one key advantage of ARIES over traditional recovery methods. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Checkpoints in Log-Based Recovery (7 Marks):**

A **checkpoint** is a synchronization point written periodically to bound the work required for recovery after a crash.

**Steps to perform a checkpoint:**

1. **Suspend** all currently executing transactions temporarily.
2. **Force-write** all log records from the in-memory log buffer to stable storage.
3. **Force-write** all modified database buffers (dirty pages) to disk.
4. **Append** a `<checkpoint L>` record to the log, where $L$ = list of active transactions at this moment.
5. **Resume** all suspended transactions.

**Why checkpoints are necessary:**

- **Bounded recovery time**: Without checkpoints, recovery must scan the entire log, which is infeasible for large databases.
- **Reduced REDO work**: Transactions committed before the last checkpoint do not need to be redone.
- **Consistency guarantee**: Ensures a known "good state" of the database on disk.
- **Performance**: Modern databases process thousands of transactions per second; without checkpoints, recovery after a crash would take hours.

**Checkpoint Frequency Trade-off:**

$$
\text{Too frequent} \Rightarrow \text{High I/O overhead, low throughput}
$$
$$
\text{Too rare} \Rightarrow \text{Long recovery time}
$$

In practice (e.g., Oracle, PostgreSQL), checkpoints are triggered when the log reaches a configurable size, typically **every 5–15 minutes** in production systems.

---

**(b) ARIES Three-Phase Recovery (7 Marks):**

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crash Detected] --> B[Phase 1: Analysis Pass]
    B --> C[Phase 2: REDO Pass]
    C --> D[Phase 3: UNDO Pass]
    D --> E[Consistent State]
```

**Phase 1 — Analysis Pass:**
- Begins at the last successful checkpoint.
- Scans the log **forward**.
- Identifies:
  - All transactions that were active at crash time (to be undone).
  - All transactions that committed after the checkpoint (to be redone).
  - All dirty pages in the buffer pool.
- Builds the in-memory structures: **Transaction Table** and **Dirty Page Table**.

**Phase 2 — REDO Pass:**
- Repeats history by reapplying all updates from the log (in forward order, starting from the smallest `recLSN` in the Dirty Page Table).
- Restores the database to the exact state at the moment of the crash, including effects of uncommitted transactions.
- This is the **key innovation** of ARIES — it preserves work and simplifies UNDO.

**Phase 3 — UNDO Pass:**
- Scans the log **backward** along the `prevLSN` chain for each uncommitted transaction.
- Writes compensating log records (CLRs) for each undone action to prevent repeated UNDOs in case of a crash during recovery.
- Once all uncommitted transactions are rolled back, writes `<Ti, end>` records.

**Key Advantage of ARIES:**

> [!IMPORTANT] **ARIES Steal/No-Force Policy**
> ARIES supports **steal** (uncommitted updates can be written to disk) and **no-force** (committed updates need not be immediately written to disk). This gives **maximum performance** by allowing the buffer manager freedom in page replacement, while still guaranteeing correctness through:
> - Write-Ahead Logging (ensures log has necessary info)
> - Repeating history (REDO before UNDO)
> - Compensation Log Records (idempotent recovery)
>
> Traditional **force** policies (writing all committed pages to disk at commit time) are too expensive for high-throughput systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Log-Based Recovery Questions**
> 1. **Confusing REDO and UNDO order**: Always **REDO committed transactions first**, then **UNDO uncommitted ones**. Reversing this order gives incorrect results.
> 2. **Wrong direction for UNDO**: UNDO must be performed in **reverse chronological order** of log records. Doing it in forward order may leave the database in an inconsistent state.
> 3. **Forgetting `<Ti, abort>` log record**: After UNDO, you must write an abort record to prevent the system from trying to UNDO the same transaction again in case of another crash.
> 4. **Ignoring checkpoints**: If a checkpoint record appears in the log, recovery must start from the **last checkpoint**, not the beginning of the log.
> 5. **Mixing up deferred and immediate update rules**: Deferred update only needs REDO; immediate update may need both. Misidentifying the strategy leads to lost marks.
> 6. **Not stating WAL rules explicitly**: When asked to "explain recovery," always mention the **two WAL rules** — examiners often allocate 2–3 marks for them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Log-Based Recovery** relies on a sequential log file on stable storage to record every DB modification.
- **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)** is the gold-standard protocol: log must be flushed *before* data, commit log must be flushed *before* commit declaration.
- **Log record format**: $\langle T_i, X, V_{old}, V_{new} \rangle$ for updates; $\langle T_i, \text{commit} \rangle$ / $\langle T_i, \text{abort} \rangle$ for status.
- **Deferred Update**: Updates happen only after commit → only **REDO** needed in recovery.
- **Immediate Update**: Updates happen during execution → both **UNDO** and **REDO** may be needed.
- **Checkpoints** limit recovery work: recovery starts from the most recent checkpoint.
- **REDO order**: forward in log order, only for committed transactions.
- **UNDO order**: reverse in log order, only for uncommitted transactions; write `<Ti, abort>` afterward.
- **ARIES algorithm** has three phases: **Analysis → REDO → UNDO**, and supports steal/no-force for high performance.
- **Steal policy**: uncommitted updates may be written to disk.
- **No-force policy**: committed updates need not be flushed immediately.
- **LSN (Log Sequence Number)** is a monotonically increasing counter used to order log records.
- **Page LSN invariant** in ARIES: $\text{pageLSN}(P) \leq \text{flushedLSN}$.
- **Compensation Log Records (CLRs)** ensure recovery is idempotent and crash-safe.
- **Recovery cost** is bounded by checkpoint frequency — more frequent checkpoints = less REDO work but more I/O overhead.
- **Stable storage** is implemented using two or more disks with mirrored writes for crash resistance.
- **ACID** durability and atomicity are the properties that log-based recovery is designed to uphold.
- **Industry adopters of ARIES-style recovery**: IBM DB2, Oracle, Microsoft SQL Server, PostgreSQL.
- **Standard checkpoint frequency in production systems**: typically every 5–15 minutes, or when the log exceeds a configurable threshold (e.g., 256 MB in Oracle).
- **Minimum log record size for stable storage write**: typically **4 KB** (one disk page/block).
- **Transaction state set partition**: $|\text{All Tx}| = |\text{Active}| + |\text{Committed}| + |\text{Aborted}|$ (mutually exclusive).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deferred Database Modification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deferred Database Modification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Deferred Database Modification (DDM)** is a *log-based recovery technique* in which all write operations of a transaction are **deferred** (postponed) until the transaction reaches its commit point. During the active phase of a transaction, the database on disk is left completely untouched; instead, every update is recorded as a log record (`<T, X, V_old, V_new>`) in the **stable log storage**. Only when the `<COMMIT T>` record has been written and forced to disk does the system begin to propagate the deferred changes from the log into the actual database files.

Because the database itself is never modified by an uncommitted transaction, recovery becomes a **single-pass REDO-only process** — formally classified by KTU examiners as the **NO-UNDO / REDO algorithm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 4):**
> Deferred Database Modification is one of the *two* canonical log-based recovery schemes prescribed in the PEDTOM748 syllabus, the other being Immediate Database Modification. Examiners almost always ask students to *contrast* these two schemes in a 7-mark sub-part.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Diary Before Action" Idea

Imagine a bank clerk named **Mr. T** who has been told to debit ₹5,000 from Account A and credit it to Account B.

| Real Bank Teller (Eager Method) | Diary-First Teller (Deferred Method) |
|---|---|
| Walks over to Account A, erases the old balance, writes the new one. Then walks to Account B and updates it. | Sits at his desk and writes in his **diary** (log): *"Debit A by 5000, Credit B by 5000"*. He does **not** touch any account book yet. |
| If he makes a mistake mid-way, he must *undo* what he has scribbled. | If he makes a mistake, he simply tears out the diary page — the real books were never touched. |
| When the manager gives final approval (commit), nothing extra is required. | When the manager gives final approval, the clerk now **physically writes** into the account books exactly what was already drafted in his diary. |

This *diary-first, write-later* discipline is the entire philosophy of **Deferred Database Modification**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Anchor for Exam:** **D**eferred = **D**iary first, **D**atabase later. **R**edo = **R**eplay the diary.

### 1.3 Key Vocabulary (Board-Exam Favourites)

- **Active Phase** – The interval between a transaction's start and its commit.
- **Log Record** – A fixed-format tuple written to the stable log for every update. Format: `<T_i, X_j, V_old, V_new>`.
- **Stable Storage** – Storage that is assumed to survive crashes (in KTU theory, it is approximated by replicated disks).
- **Force-Logging at Commit** – Writing the `<COMMIT T_i>` record and flushing the entire log buffer to stable storage before the transaction is declared committed.
- **Idempotent Operation** – An operation (like REDO) that produces the same result whether applied once or many times. Idempotency is *essential* for crash recovery.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Logical Phases of a Transaction (DDM View)

A transaction under Deferred Database Modification is conceptually divided into three sequential phases:

1. **Read & Compute Phase (Active Phase)**
   - Transaction may freely *read* any data item from disk into local buffers.
   - All *write* operations are **redirected** to the log only. The buffer-cache pages that would normally be written back to disk are **pinned** (not flushed).
   - No dirty page from this transaction is allowed to reach disk.

2. **Commit Phase (Log Force Point)**
   - A special `<COMMIT T_i>` log record is appended.
   - The DBMS issues an `fsync` (force-write) on the log file, guaranteeing the log is on stable storage.
   - The commit decision is now *durable* even if the system crashes in the next microsecond.

3. **Deferred Update Phase (Redo Phase)**
   - Log records of `T_i` are replayed against the database.
   - Because writes are idempotent (replaying `WRITE(A, 900)` always produces 900), the order of replay does not matter.

### 2.2 Why "NO-UNDO"? — The Core 'Why' Question

Because no data item on disk is ever modified by `T_i` until the `<COMMIT T_i>` record exists in stable storage, any transaction that does **not** commit (due to abort, system failure, or division-by-zero) leaves the database in its original state. There is literally nothing to roll back — hence the term **NO-UNDO**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *aborting a transaction* with *recovering from a crash*. In DDM:
> - **Abort** (voluntary) → just discard log entries, no DB change.
> - **Crash Recovery** → REDO only those transactions whose `<COMMIT T_i>` record survived the crash.

### 2.3 Crash Recovery Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

The DBMS invokes the recovery manager at system restart. The DDM recovery algorithm is the simplest of all recovery schemes:

**Procedure REDO-Only-Recovery:**
1. Initialize two lists: `COMMITTED = ∅` and `LOSER = ∅`.
2. Scan the log from the **beginning** (or from the last checkpoint) sequentially.
3. Whenever a `<COMMIT T_i>` record is encountered, add `T_i` to `COMMITTED`.
4. Whenever a `<START T_i>` record is encountered *without* a matching `<COMMIT T_i>` before crash, add `T_i` to `LOSER`.
5. For every transaction in `COMMITTED`, scan its log again and **REDO** each `<T_i, X_j, V_old, V_new>` by setting `X_j ← V_new`.
6. Ignore all transactions in `LOSER` — no action required.
7. Resume normal processing.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Expression | Meaning / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| Log Record Format | $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{old}, V_{new}\rangle$ | One logical write by `T_i` on item `X_j` |
| Commit Marker | $\langle \texttt{COMMIT } T_i\rangle$ | Terminal record; forces log flush |
| Active Set at Recovery | $A = \{T_i : \langle START\,T_i\rangle \in \log \;\wedge\; \langle COMMIT\,T_i\rangle \notin \log\}$ | Uncommitted transactions (ignore) |
| Committed Set at Recovery | $C = \{T_i : \langle COMMIT\,T_i\rangle \in \log\}$ | Must be REDO-ed |
| Number of DB Writes Saved | $W_{saved} = \sum_{T \in \text{aborted}} w(T)$ | Disk I/O saved by deferral |
| Recovery Cost | $C_{recover} = \sum_{T \in C}\; \vert \log(T)\vert$ | Linear in size of committed logs |
| Idempotency Identity | $\text{REDO}(\text{REDO}(X, v), v) = \text{REDO}(X, v)$ | Required for replayability |
| Total Log Size | $L_{total} = \sum_{T} (\text{start} + \text{updates} + \text{commit})$ | Bounded by # of writes |
| Checkpoint Cost (DDM) | $O(k)$ where $k$ = # active txns | Only commit/active list flushed |
| Algorithm Class | NO-UNDO $\;+\;$ REDO | Canonical DDM signature |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar notation $\vert \log(T)\vert$ denotes the **cardinality** (number of update records) of transaction `T` in the log — *not* absolute value. This avoids breaking the markdown table.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Banking & ATMs:** Online transaction processing engines use DDM-like journaling in PostgreSQL's *write-ahead log* before checkpoint flush.
- **Aerospace Flight-Recorders:** Black-box data is buffered in non-volatile memory and only committed (written to long-term storage) after the flight phase is validated.
- **Distributed NoSQL Stores:** Systems like Google's Spanner and Cassandra use deferred-applied write-ahead logs for consensus and crash recovery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Log Construction Under DDM

**Scenario:** Consider a database with initial values `A = 1000`, `B = 2000`, `C = 700`. Transaction `T_1` executes:
```
T_1:  READ A
T_1:  A := A - 100          // A becomes 900
T_1:  WRITE A                // deferred to log
T_1:  READ B
T_1:  B := B + 100          // B becomes 2100
T_1:  WRITE B                // deferred to log
T_1:  COMMIT
```

**Step-by-Step Log Build:**

1. The DBMS appends `<START T_1>` to the log buffer.
2. On `WRITE A`, it appends `<T_1, A, 1000, 900>` to the log buffer.
3. On `WRITE B`, it appends `<T_1, B, 2000, 2100>` to the log buffer.
4. The `COMMIT` statement forces the log buffer to stable storage and appends `<COMMIT T_1>`.
5. *Only now* are the updates applied to the actual database files: `A = 900`, `B = 2100`. Note `C = 700` is untouched.

**Final Stable Log:**

$$
\log = \bigl[\,\langle \texttt{START } T_1\rangle,\;\langle T_1, A, 1000, 900\rangle,\;\langle T_1, B, 2000, 2100\rangle,\;\langle \texttt{COMMIT } T_1\rangle\,\bigr]
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough — Crash Mid-Transaction

Suppose the system crashes **immediately after** the first `WRITE A` of `T_1` is appended to the log buffer but *before* the buffer has been flushed.

**Step-by-Step Recovery Analysis:**

1. **Restart phase:** Recovery manager scans the on-disk log. Since the buffer was not flushed, the `<T_1, A, 1000, 900>` entry is **not** present.
2. The only log record on disk is `<START T_1>`.
3. `COMMITTED = ∅`, `LOSER = {T_1}`.
4. Database values remain `A = 1000, B = 2000, C = 700` — *exactly the original state*.
5. Recovery cost is `O(1)` (just an empty REDO loop).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Because DDM defers *all* writes until after commit, a crash before commit is essentially a *free* event — no work, no data corruption.

### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough — Crash After Commit, Before Updates Applied

Suppose the crash occurs **after** `<COMMIT T_1>` has been flushed but **before** the deferred updates `A = 900` and `B = 2100` have been applied to the database.

**Step-by-Step Recovery:**

1. Recovery manager reads the stable log from the beginning.
2. It encounters `<START T_1>`, `<T_1, A, 1000, 900>`, `<T_1, B, 2000, 2100>`, `<COMMIT T_1>`.
3. `T_1` is added to `COMMITTED`.
4. **REDO loop:** For each update record of `T_1`, set the data item to `V_new`.
   - Set `A ← 900`.
   - Set `B ← 2100`.
5. Recovery completes. Database now matches the pre-crash intended state.

**Algebraic State Restoration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State}_{\text{pre-crash, intended}} &= \{A: 900,\; B: 2100,\; C: 700\} \\
\text{State}_{\text{post-crash, on disk}} &= \{A: 1000,\; B: 2000,\; C: 700\} \\
\text{Recovery Operator } \mathcal{R}(S, \log) &\rightarrow S' \\
\mathcal{R}\bigl(\{1000, 2000, 700\},\; \log_{T_1}\bigr) &= \{900, 2100, 700\} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DDM vs Immediate Database Modification

| Criterion | Deferred Database Modification | Immediate Database Modification |
|---|---|---|
| When is DB updated? | Only after `<COMMIT T_i>` | Immediately during transaction |
| Log record format | $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{old}, V_{new}\rangle$ | Same (but DB is already dirty) |
| Aborted transaction | Nothing to do (NO-UNDO) | Must UNDO using `V_old` |
| Committed but lost updates | Must REDO from log | Must REDO from log |
| Algorithm class | **NO-UNDO / REDO** | **UNDO / REDO** |
| Recovery pass count | 1 pass (forward) | 2 passes (UNDO backward, REDO forward) |
| Disk I/O during normal execution | Lower (no dirty-page writes) | Higher (dirty pages flushed continuously) |
| Log size | Identical | Identical |
| Buffer-pool pressure | **Lower** (writes stay in log buffer) | Higher (dirty pages accumulate) |
| Crash before commit | Trivial — discard log buffer | Complex — apply UNDO using `V_old` |
| Suitable workload | Read-heavy with bursts of writes | Mixed read/write with low latency requirement |

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation of Recovery Cost

Let there be $n$ transactions, of which $k$ commit before crash. Let $w_i$ be the number of write operations performed by `T_i`. The total recovery cost in terms of log records scanned is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{scan}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{1}[\langle \texttt{COMMIT } T_i\rangle \in \log] \cdot w_i \\
&= \sum_{T_i \in C} w_i \\
&\leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function. The third line shows that the worst-case recovery cost equals the **total log size** — a clean, board-friendly upper bound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, examiners often award partial credit simply for stating that DDM recovery cost is *linear* in the size of the committed-log subset.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Lifecycle of a Transaction under DDM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A((START T1)) --> B[Active Phase: Reads + Local Buffers]
    B --> C{All Updates Done?}
    C -- No --> D[Append T1 Xj Vold Vnew to Log Buffer]
    D --> B
    C -- Yes --> E[Append COMMIT T1 to Log]
    E --> F[Force Log Buffer to Stable Storage fsync]
    F --> G[Apply Log Updates to Database REDO Phase]
    G --> H((Transaction T1 Complete))
```

### 4.2 Crash Recovery Decision Tree (DDM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[System Restart: Scan Log from Last Checkpoint]
    K --> L{Record Type?}
    L -- START Tx --> M[Add Tx to LOSER Set]
    L -- UPDATE Record --> N[Buffer Update in Recovery Cache]
    L -- COMMIT Tx --> O[Move Tx from LOSER to COMMITTED]
    M --> P{More Records?}
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P -- Yes --> L
    P -- No --> Q[Phase 2: For each Tx in COMMITTED REDO all updates]
    Q --> R[Database Restored to Consistent State]
    R --> S[Resume Normal Operation]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the DDM Recovery Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX_ENGINE[Transaction Engine]
        T1[Transaction Manager]
        BUF[Local Buffer Cache]
    end
    subgraph LOG_SUB[Logging Subsystem]
        LBUF[Log Buffer in RAM]
        SST[Stable Log on Disk]
        FS[Force Write fsync]
    end
    subgraph DB_SUB[Database Subsystem]
        DBUF[Database Buffer Pool]
        DDISK[Database Disk Files]
    end
    subgraph REC_SUB[Recovery Subsystem]
        RM[Recovery Manager]
        REDO[REDO Module Idempotent]
    end

    T1 -- read --> DBUF
    T1 -- write intent --> LBUF
    LBUF -- flush on commit --> SST
    T1 -- commit --> FS
    FS -- triggers --> SST
    SST -- after commit --> DDISK
    RM -- scans --> SST
    RM -- calls --> REDO
    REDO -- updates --> DBUF
    DBUF -- flushed --> DDISK
```

### 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Successful DDM Transaction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Application
    participant TM as Transaction Manager
    participant LM as Log Manager
    participant DM as Database Manager
    participant Stable as Stable Storage

    App ->> TM: BEGIN T1
    TM ->> LM: append START T1
    App ->> TM: WRITE A = 900
    TM ->> LM: append T1 A 1000 900
    App ->> TM: WRITE B = 2100
    TM ->> LM: append T1 B 2000 2100
    App ->> TM: COMMIT
    TM ->> LM: append COMMIT T1
    LM ->> Stable: force fsync
    Stable -->> LM: ack
    LM ->> DM: apply updates A 900, B 2100
    DM -->> App: COMMIT successful
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Deferred Database Modification. State its classification in terms of UNDO/REDO requirements. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Deferred Database Modification is a log-based recovery technique in which all the write operations of a transaction are recorded in the stable log but **not applied to the database** until the transaction commits successfully. The database is updated only after the `<COMMIT T_i>` record is force-written to the log.

It is classified as a **NO-UNDO / REDO** algorithm. There is no UNDO because uncommitted transactions never modified the on-disk database, and REDO is required for committed transactions whose updates may not yet have reached disk when a crash occurred.

---

#### Question 2: Why must REDO operations in Deferred Database Modification be idempotent? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
During crash recovery, the same log record may be replayed multiple times — for instance, if a second crash occurs while REDO is in progress. Because the recovery manager has no reliable way to track which updates have already been applied, it must be safe to execute each REDO operation any number of times without changing the final state. An idempotent operation (such as `SET X ← V_new`) satisfies this requirement: applying it once or a hundred times yields the same final value `V_new`.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Examiner's Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **one full question** of 14 marks, with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the **Deferred Database Modification** recovery scheme in detail. Illustrate with a suitable example how the database is restored after a system crash. Discuss why this scheme is classified as a **NO-UNDO / REDO** algorithm. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

*Definition (1 mark):* Deferred Database Modification defers all write operations of a transaction to the log until the transaction commits. The actual database is updated only after the commit record is written to stable storage.

*Mechanism (2 marks):* During the active phase, every write `WRITE(X, v)` is logged as `<T_i, X, V_old, V_new>`. The database on disk remains untouched. When `COMMIT` is issued, the system flushes the log buffer to stable storage and writes `<COMMIT T_i>`. Only then does it propagate the changes from log to database.

*Example (2 marks):* Consider initial database `{A: 1000, B: 2000}`. Transaction `T_1` performs `A := A - 100; WRITE A; B := B + 100; WRITE B; COMMIT`. Under DDM, the log contains `<START T_1>, <T_1,A,1000,900>, <T_1,B,2000,2100>, <COMMIT T_1>` while the database still holds `A = 1000, B = 2000`. If a crash occurs after commit but before updates, recovery scans the log, identifies `T_1` as committed, and replays the updates, setting `A = 900, B = 2100`.

*NO-UNDO / REDO Justification (2 marks):* Because the disk database is never altered by an uncommitted transaction, there is nothing to UNDO when a transaction aborts or crashes before commit. However, if the system crashes *after* commit but *before* the deferred writes reach disk, those committed updates must be RE-applied using the log — hence REDO is necessary.

**(b)** With the help of a log sequence, explain how the system recovers when **multiple transactions** are active and the system crashes. Use your own example with at least three transactions and show the final state after recovery. **[7 Marks]** `[Adapted: KTU Model Question Paper 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

*Setup:* Consider three transactions with initial database `{A: 500, B: 500, C: 500}`:

- `T_1`: `READ A; A := A + 100; WRITE A; COMMIT`
- `T_2`: `READ B; B := B - 50; WRITE B;` *(aborts due to error)*
- `T_3`: `READ C; C := C + 200; WRITE C; COMMIT`

*Log before crash:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log =\; &\bigl[\langle \texttt{START } T_1\rangle,\;\langle T_1, A, 500, 600\rangle,\;\langle \texttt{COMMIT } T_1\rangle, \\
&\langle \texttt{START } T_2\rangle,\;\langle T_2, B, 500, 450\rangle, \\
&\langle \texttt{START } T_3\rangle,\;\langle T_3, C, 500, 700\rangle,\;\langle \texttt{COMMIT } T_3\rangle\,\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Recovery Phase:*
- Identify committed set: $C = \{T_1, T_3\}$.
- Identify loser (uncommitted) set: $L = \{T_2\}$.
- REDO loop applies updates for `T_1` and `T_3` only.
- `T_2`'s update `<T_2, B, 500, 450>` is **ignored** (no UNDO needed).

*Final State After Recovery:*

$$
\text{State}_{\text{recovered}} = \{A: 600,\; B: 500,\; C: 700\}
$$

`B` remains at its original `500` because `T_2` never committed — exactly the desired outcome.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Compare **Deferred Database Modification** with **Immediate Database Modification** in terms of (i) algorithm class, (ii) recovery procedure, (iii) buffer-pool behaviour, and (iv) suitability of workloads. Mention two advantages and two disadvantages of DDM. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Algorithm Class:** DDM is NO-UNDO / REDO; Immediate is UNDO / REDO.

(ii) **Recovery Procedure:** DDM requires a single forward scan of the log to REDO committed transactions. Immediate Modification requires a backward UNDO pass for losers and a forward REDO pass for winners, making it more complex.

(iii) **Buffer-Pool Behaviour:** DDM keeps dirty pages pinned in the buffer until commit, reducing random disk I/O. Immediate Modification flushes dirty pages continuously, increasing disk traffic.

(iv) **Workload Suitability:** DDM suits read-heavy or batch workloads where transactions are short and atomic. Immediate Modification suits latency-sensitive OLTP workloads.

*Two Advantages of DDM:*
- Simpler recovery (single pass, idempotent operations).
- Lower disk I/O during normal execution since no dirty pages are written back prematurely.

*Two Disadvantages of DDM:*
- All updates must wait until commit, increasing transaction latency.
- Buffer pool holds dirty data longer, raising memory pressure for long transactions.

**(b)** Describe the **log record format** used in Deferred Database Modification. Why is `<COMMIT T_i>` force-written to stable storage before acknowledging commit to the user? What happens if the system crashes *between* `<COMMIT T_i>` being flushed and the deferred updates being applied to the database? **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

*Log Record Format (2 marks):* Each update is logged as $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{old}, V_{new}\rangle$ where `T_i` is the transaction id, `X_j` is the data item, and `V_old`, `V_new` are the before- and after-images. Special records $\langle \texttt{START } T_i\rangle$ and $\langle \texttt{COMMIT } T_i\rangle$ mark the transaction boundaries.

*Force-Write at Commit (2 marks):* The `<COMMIT T_i>` record must be force-written (via `fsync`) to stable storage before the system acknowledges commit to the user. This ensures the **Write-Ahead Log (WAL) invariant** — the log is durable before the commit decision becomes visible. Without this, a crash after acknowledgement but before the log is flushed would lose the commit decision, leading to inconsistent recovery.

*Crash Between Commit and Deferred Update (3 marks):* The log contains `<COMMIT T_i>` but the database on disk is unchanged. During recovery, the recovery manager scans the log, finds `T_i` in the committed set, and **REDO**-replays every update record of `T_i`, setting each `X_j` to its `V_new` value. Because REDO is idempotent, even if the crash happened mid-REDO, recovery can safely resume. Final database state matches the pre-crash intended state.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Deferred with Lazy-Write:** Lazy-write defers *database* updates to disk; Deferred Database Modification defers *all* updates to log-then-DB.
> 2. **Forgetting Idempotency:** Many students lose 1–2 marks by not mentioning that REDO must be idempotent.
> 3. **Mis-classifying the algorithm:** Writing "UNDO/REDO" instead of "NO-UNDO/REDO" is an instant half-mark deduction.
> 4. **Ignoring the loser set:** Recovery is incomplete if you only REDO without identifying uncommitted transactions (although they require no action, they must be enumerated).
> 5. **Wrong log-record format:** Always use $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{old}, V_{new}\rangle$. Using only `(T_i, X_j, V_new)` loses the `V_old` credit in conceptual answers.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DDM defers writes** — every update is logged first; the database is touched only after commit.
- **Algorithm class:** **NO-UNDO / REDO**.
- **Commit protocol:** `<COMMIT T_i>` must be **force-written** to stable storage before user acknowledgement.
- **Recovery procedure:** Single forward scan of the log; REDO all committed transactions; ignore all uncommitted (no UNDO needed).
- **Idempotency is mandatory** for REDO operations to ensure safe replay.
- **Log record format:** $\langle T_i, X_j, V_{old}, V_{new}\rangle$.
- **Buffer-pool pressure:** Higher for long transactions because dirty data is held until commit.
- **Disk I/O:** Lower during normal execution; deferred writes batch nicely.
- **Workload fit:** Best for read-heavy and batched write workloads; not ideal for ultra-low-latency OLTP.
- **Crash before commit → trivial:** Just discard the unflushed log buffer.
- **Crash after commit but before DB update → REDO from log.**
- **Compare-and-contrast with Immediate Modification** is the most-asked 14-mark question in this module — prepare the comparison matrix thoroughly.
- **Mnemonic:** **D**iary-first, **D**atabase-later, **D**efers danger.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Check Pointing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT748 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Check Pointing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Check Pointing** is a database recovery technique in which the system periodically saves the state of the database, the active transaction table, and the log information onto stable storage. The purpose is to create a **consistent reference point** from which the recovery manager can restart operations after a system failure, thereby drastically reducing the volume of log records that must be examined during recovery.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 4 — Recovery Systems), check pointing is treated as the **anchor mechanism** that supports the log-based recovery process. The transaction log is conceptually divided into segments between two consecutive check points, and only the most recent segment must be scanned during a restart.

### Formal Components Recorded at a Check Point
A complete check point record typically captures:

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| `<CHECKPOINT>` Log Record | A special log entry marking the beginning of the check point |
| **Active Transaction List** | Transaction IDs and their last log sequence numbers (LSNs) that were still in progress |
| **Dirtied Page Table** | Pages in the buffer that have been modified but not yet flushed |
| **Database Snapshot / Dirty Pages** | All pages modified by committed transactions up to that instant |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Video Game Save Point**
> 
> Imagine you are playing a 30-hour open-world video game. Instead of replaying from the very beginning every time the game crashes, you periodically press "Save." Each save stores: (1) your current location, (2) the items you have collected, (3) the quests that are still in progress. After a crash, the game reloads the **most recent save** and replays only the small portion of progress made after that save.
> 
> **Check pointing is the database equivalent of the "Save" button.** The log file is the running story of every action. The check point freezes the current story so that after a crash, only the events *after* the most recent check point need to be replayed or undone — not the entire history of the database.

### Why Check Pointing is Essential
- Without check pointing, recovery time grows **linearly with the size of the log**.
- With check pointing, recovery time is bounded by the time between the **last check point** and the crash — a small, fixed window.
- It supports **stealing and forcing buffer policies** by ensuring dirty pages have a stable on-disk reference.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Emphasis:** The phrase *"reduce the amount of work to be done during recovery"* is the single most important one-line answer for any check pointing question. Memorize this verbatim.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Check Pointing

In DBMS recovery literature, certain standard metrics are used:

- **Checkpoint Interval (T_cp):** The time period between two successive check points, usually in **seconds**.
- **Recovery Time Objective (RTO):** The maximum acceptable downtime after a crash, typically expressed in **minutes**.
- **Log Sequence Number (LSN):** A monotonically increasing identifier for each log record.
- **Stealing Policy:** A buffer management policy where uncommitted updates may be written to disk.
- **Forcing Policy:** A buffer management policy where all updates of a transaction are forced to disk at commit time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU valuation purposes, the terms **"consistent check point"** and **"fuzzy check point"** are frequently tested. A *consistent* check point requires that no transaction is active when the check point is taken, while a *fuzzy* check point allows transactions to continue running — it is the practical, modern choice (used in **ARIES**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How Check Pointing Works

The check pointing process follows a structured sequence of steps. Below is the bulleted operational breakdown aligned with the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### Phase A — Pre-Check Point Preparation
1. **Suspend (or do not suspend, for fuzzy)** all write operations to the log temporarily.
2. **Force-write** all log records currently in the main-memory log buffer to stable storage.
3. **Force-write** all modified (dirty) pages belonging to committed transactions to the database on stable storage (for consistent check points).
4. **Write a `<CHECKPOINT L>` record** to the log, where `L` is the list of active transactions at that moment.

### Phase B — Check Point Record Persistence
5. Append the `<CHECKPOINT L>` entry to the log on stable storage.
6. Update the **master record** (or restart pointer) to point to this new `<CHECKPOINT>` record.

### Phase C — Resume Normal Operation
7. Resume normal transaction processing.
8. The system now continues to log all new operations after this check point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Step 4 is critical:** The active transaction list `L` tells the recovery manager exactly which transactions were in progress. Any transaction **not** in `L` is guaranteed to have completed (committed or aborted) before the check point, so it needs no redo/undo.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since check pointing is largely a system-design concept, the analytical formulas relate to **performance, recovery cost, and trade-offs**.

| Metric / Concept | Expression or Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Recovery Time without Check Points | $T_{\text{recovery}} = f(\text{Total Log Size})$ | Seconds — grows linearly |
| Recovery Time with Check Points | $T_{\text{recovery}} = f(\text{Log After Last CP})$ | Seconds — bounded |
| Average Check Point Overhead | $C_{\text{overhead}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{cp-write}}}{T_{\text{cp-interval}}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| Maximum Recovery Window | $W_{\text{max}} = T_{\text{cp-interval}}$ | Seconds |
| Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR) | $\text{MTTR} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{restore}} + T_{\text{redo}} + T_{\text{undo}}$ | Seconds |
| Recovery Cost (Modern ARIES) | $C = C_{\text{redo}} + C_{\text{undo}}$ | Log records scanned |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Never write the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside a table cell. For absolute value or "divides", use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX form. For example, write `$\vert x \vert$` instead of `|x|`.

## 2.3 Why Check Pointing Matters — Engineering Utility

In real-world engineering and enterprise systems, check pointing underpins:

- **Banking Systems:** Periodic save points ensure that after a server crash, customer balances can be reconstructed to within seconds of accuracy.
- **Aerospace Databases:** Mission-critical telemetry databases use frequent check points because recovery time is a safety metric.
- **E-Commerce Platforms:** Order-processing databases rely on check points to handle high-throughput transactions without long downtime.
- **Distributed Databases (e.g., Google Spanner, CockroachDB):** Per-node check points are combined with global snapshots for distributed consistency.

## 2.4 Types of Check Points — Conceptual Comparison

| Type | Allows Active Tx? | Suspension Needed? | Implementation Cost | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Consistent (Static)** | No | Yes (writes suspended) | High I/O blocking | Older DBMS, academic examples |
| **Fuzzy (Dynamic)** | Yes | No | Lower blocking, higher log complexity | ARIES, Oracle, modern DBMS |
| **Incremental** | Yes | No | Only modified pages flushed | High-performance engines |
| **Transaction-Consistent** | Partial | Partial | Medium | Some commercial systems |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Recovery Algorithm with Check Points

The most important KTU question on check pointing involves showing the **recovery procedure** when a crash occurs. We derive this step-by-step using the KTU standard notation.

### Setup
Consider the following log records (a typical KTU problem):

```
<T1, start>
<T1, A, 1000, 950>
<T2, start>
<T2, B, 2000, 2050>
<T1, C, 700, 600>
<CHECKPOINT {T1, T2}>
<T1, commit>
<T2, D, 800, 850>
<T3, start>
<T2, commit>
<T3, E, 500, 400>
<CRASH>
```

### Step-by-Step Recovery Procedure

**Step 1: Identify the most recent `<CHECKPOINT>` record.**
Scanning the log backwards from the crash, the most recent check point is:
$$<CHECKPOINT\ \{T_1, T_2\}>$$
The active transaction list at the check point is $L = \{T_1, T_2\}$.

**Step 2: Identify all transactions that started AFTER the check point.**
Scanning forward from the check point, we find:
$$T_3 \in \{\text{transactions started after check point}\}$$

So the **Redo-Undo candidate set** is:
$$S = L \cup \{\text{transactions started after CP}\} = \{T_1, T_2, T_3\}$$

**Step 3: Determine the status of each transaction in $S$ by examining the log.**

| Transaction | Started? | Committed? | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | Yes (before CP) | Yes (`<T1, commit>` found after CP) | **REDO** |
| $T_2$ | Yes (before CP) | Yes (`<T2, commit>` found after CP) | **REDO** |
| $T_3$ | Yes (after CP) | No (no commit before crash) | **UNDO** |

**Step 4: Apply REDO to all committed transactions.**
The set of committed transactions is:
$$C = \{T \mid T \in S \wedge <T, \text{commit}>\ \text{exists in log after CP}\}$$

For each $T \in C$, redo all updates from the start of $T$ up to its commit point.

**Step 5: Apply UNDO to all uncommitted transactions.**
The set of uncommitted transactions is:
$$U = \{T \mid T \in S \wedge <T, \text{commit}>\ \text{not in log}\}$$

For each $T \in U$, trace the log backwards and write **compensation log records (CLRs)** to undo each update.

### Step 3.6 — Exhaustive Compensation Example for $T_3$
The log shows:
$$<T_3, E, 500, 400>$$
To undo, we write:
$$<T_3, E, 400, 500>$$
This CLR records the old value (500) being restored as the new value (400 → 500, in original sense), and is appended to the log.

## 3.2 Derivation of Recovery Cost

Let:
- $N$ = total number of log records
- $C$ = number of records before the last check point
- $A$ = number of records after the last check point

Then:
$$N = C + A$$

**Without check pointing:**
$$T_{\text{scan}} = O(N)$$

**With check pointing:**
$$T_{\text{scan}} = O(A) \quad \text{where } A \ll N$$

The **speedup factor** $S$ is:
$$S = \frac{N}{A} = \frac{C + A}{A} = 1 + \frac{C}{A}$$

For a long-running database where $C \gg A$, this can be a speedup of **thousands**.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Take a Check Point

The system must decide **when** to take a check point. Common heuristics:

| Strategy | Trigger Condition | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time-based** | Every $k$ minutes | Predictable overhead | May be wasteful if idle |
| **Log-volume based** | After every $m$ log records | Adaptive to workload | Unpredictable timing |
| **Dirty-page based** | When dirty pages exceed threshold | Tied to actual data state | Needs extra bookkeeping |
| **Transaction-count based** | After every $n$ commits | Simple | Ignores log size |

The **optimal check point interval** balances recovery time vs. check point overhead. Using a simplified analytical model:

$$T_{\text{interval}}^{\text{opt}} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot T_{\text{cp}} \cdot M}{f}}$$

where:
- $T_{\text{cp}}$ = time to perform one check point
- $M$ = mean time between failures
- $f$ = rate of log generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B -- Time Based --> C[Suspend Writes]
    B -- Log Size --> C
    B -- Dirty Page Threshold --> C
    C --> D[Force Log Buffer to Stable Storage]
    D --> E[Write All Dirty Pages to Disk]
    E --> F[Append CHECKPOINT L Record]
    F --> G[Update Master Restart Pointer]
    G --> H[Resume Normal Operation]
    H --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Recovery Procedure After Crash with Check Point

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[System Crash Detected] --> Q[Locate Most Recent CHECKPOINT in Log]
    Q --> R[Read Active Transaction List L from CHECKPOINT]
    R --> S[Scan Log Forward from CHECKPOINT]
    S --> T{For Each Transaction T in S}
    T -- Has COMMIT Record --> U[Add T to REDO List]
    T -- No COMMIT Record --> V[Add T to UNDO List]
    U --> W[REDO All Updates of Committed Transactions]
    V --> X[UNDO Updates of Uncommitted Transactions in Reverse]
    W --> Y[Write Compensation Log Records CLRs]
    X --> Y
    Y --> Z[Database Restored to Consistent State]

    style X fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define check pointing. Why is it required in a DBMS?

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Check pointing** is a recovery mechanism in which the DBMS periodically saves the database state, along with a list of active transactions, onto stable storage by writing a `<CHECKPOINT>` record to the log.
>
> **Why required:**
> - It reduces the **amount of log to be examined** during recovery after a crash.
> - It bounds recovery time to the interval between the last check point and the crash.
> - It allows the system to identify which transactions must be **redone** and which must be **undone** without scanning the entire log.
> - Without check pointing, recovery time grows with the size of the entire log history.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Distinguish between consistent and fuzzy check pointing.

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> | Aspect | Consistent Check Point | Fuzzy Check Point |
> |---|---|---|
> | Active Transactions | None allowed | Permitted |
> | Suspension of Writes | Required | Not required |
> | Recovery Complexity | Simple | Complex (requires compensation) |
> | Practical Use | Academic / older systems | Modern systems like ARIES |
> | Cost | High blocking | Lower blocking |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions typically have **two sub-parts worth 7 marks each**. The model answers below are written with that structure.

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** 

**(a)** Explain the concept of check pointing in detail. Discuss the steps involved in performing a check point in a log-based recovery system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following log of a DBMS:

```
<T1, start>
<T1, A, 1000, 950>
<T2, start>
<T2, B, 2000, 2050>
<CHECKPOINT {T1, T2}>
<T1, commit>
<T2, C, 500, 600>
<T3, start>
<T3, D, 300, 250>
<T2, commit>
<CRASH>
```

Identify which transactions need to be **redone** and which need to be **undone** during recovery. Show your work clearly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Explanation (4 marks):**
> 
> Check pointing is a recovery technique used to limit the volume of log records that must be processed during recovery. A check point record `<CHECKPOINT L>` is written into the log, where `L` is the list of transactions that were active at the moment the check point was taken.
>
> **Steps Involved (3 marks):**
> 1. Suspend all update operations temporarily (for consistent check points).
> 2. Force-write all log records from the log buffer to stable storage.
> 3. Force-write all modified (dirty) buffer pages to the database on stable storage.
> 4. Write a `<CHECKPOINT L>` record to the log on stable storage, where $L$ is the active transaction list.
> 5. Update the **master record / restart pointer** to point to this new `<CHECKPOINT>` record.
> 6. Resume normal transaction processing.
>
> During recovery, the system begins scanning from the most recent `<CHECKPOINT>` rather than from the start of the log, which drastically reduces recovery time.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1: Identify the most recent check point (1 mark).**
> The last `<CHECKPOINT>` record is `<CHECKPOINT {T1, T2}>`.
> Active transaction list at check point: $L = \{T_1, T_2\}$.
>
> **Step 2: Find transactions started after the check point (1 mark).**
> Scanning forward, only $T_3$ started after the check point.
> Redo-Undo candidate set: $S = \{T_1, T_2, T_3\}$.
>
> **Step 3: Check commit status of each transaction (3 marks).**
>
> | Transaction | Status | Action |
> |---|---|---|
> | $T_1$ | Committed after CP | **REDO** |
> | $T_2$ | Committed after CP | **REDO** |
> | $T_3$ | Not committed | **UNDO** |
>
> **Step 4: Final Action Summary (2 marks).**
> - **REDO Transactions:** $\{T_1, T_2\}$
> - **UNDO Transactions:** $\{T_3\}$
> - Replay all updates of $T_1$ and $T_2$.
> - Write compensation log record for $T_3$'s update on $D$: revert $D$ from $250$ back to $300$.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** What are the different types of check points? Explain each with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the recovery procedure using check points. How does check pointing reduce recovery time? Show with a numerical example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> [!NOTE]
> **Types of Check Points (7 marks):**
>
> 1. **Consistent (Static) Check Point:**
>    - All transactions are paused.
>    - No active transactions are allowed.
>    - All dirty pages are flushed to disk.
>    - Simple recovery but high blocking cost.
>
> 2. **Fuzzy (Dynamic) Check Point:**
>    - Transactions continue running during check pointing.
>    - Used by ARIES algorithm.
>    - Recovery is more complex but minimizes system downtime.
>
> 3. **Incremental Check Point:**
>    - Only modified pages since the last check point are flushed.
>    - Reduces I/O overhead.
>
> 4. **Transaction-Consistent Check Point:**
>    - Forces all active transactions to abort or commit before proceeding.
>    - Maintains logical consistency.
>
> **Diagram (to be drawn by student):**
> A timeline showing transaction states (active, committed, aborted) with the check point marker positioned between them.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> [!NOTE]
> **Recovery Procedure (4 marks):**
> 1. Locate the most recent `<CHECKPOINT>` record in the log.
> 2. Read the active transaction list $L$.
> 3. Scan forward to identify all transactions started after the check point.
> 4. For each transaction, check for a `<T, commit>` record.
> 5. **REDO** all updates of committed transactions.
> 6. **UNDO** all updates of uncommitted transactions, writing CLRs.
>
> **Numerical Example (3 marks):**
> Suppose the log has $10{,}000$ records and the check point was taken after $9{,}500$ records.
> - Without check pointing: scan $10{,}000$ records.
> - With check pointing: scan only the last $500$ records.
> - **Speedup factor** = $\dfrac{10{,}000}{500} = 20$ times faster recovery.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to force-write log buffers first** — If you skip this step in the check point procedure, you lose recent log records and recovery will be incorrect. (KTU deducts 2 marks for this.)
>
> 2. **Confusing REDO and UNDO** — REDO applies to committed transactions, UNDO applies to uncommitted ones. Writing the reverse is a 3-mark penalty.
>
> 3. **Not mentioning the active transaction list `L`** — The check point record must contain `L`. Without it, the recovery manager cannot determine which transactions to consider. (2-mark deduction.)
>
> 4. **Writing `|x|` instead of $\vert x \vert$ in tables** — This breaks markdown table syntax. Always use LaTeX vertical bar commands inside tables.
>
> 5. **Skipping the master record update** — A common 1-mark loss in 7-mark sub-questions.
>
> 6. **Ignoring fuzzy check points in ARIES context** — KTU 2024 syllabus mentions modern recovery; students who only discuss consistent check points lose marks for lack of breadth.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> - Check pointing **reduces log scanning time** during recovery — this is the single most important fact.
> - A check point record contains a **list of active transactions** at the moment of the check point.
> - Recovery scans the log **from the most recent check point forward**, not from the beginning.
> - **REDO** = apply again for committed transactions.
> - **UNDO** = roll back for uncommitted transactions, writing **CLRs**.
> - **Consistent check point** = pauses transactions; **Fuzzy check point** = allows them to continue (used in ARIES).
> - The check point procedure: **suspend → flush log → flush dirty pages → write `<CHECKPOINT L>` → update master record → resume**.
> - Recovery time with check pointing is bounded by the **interval between the last check point and the crash**.
> - The **master record / restart pointer** identifies the most recent check point for recovery.
> - Three common check point triggers: **time-based, log-volume-based, dirty-page-based**.
> - Modern DBMS prefer **fuzzy check points** because they avoid blocking active transactions.
> - The **ARIES algorithm** uses fuzzy check points combined with compensation log records.
> - Always use LaTeX `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` for absolute values inside markdown tables, never the literal pipe symbol.
> - For 14-mark questions, structure answers as: **definition (2 marks) → process (3 marks) → example (2 marks)** per sub-part.
